Document 1 of 249

Readers write Letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write-letters-editor/docview/3171234796/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write+Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-02-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Self-serving
gambit
Re: A guide to the Ontario election's most dramatic
ridings, Feb. 24
On Thursday, thanks to Doug Ford, we will, in the middle of winter, vote for a new provincial government. This date overrides the fixed election date legislation which would have had us voting in June 2026.
This early election is a politically, self-serving gambit by the monied Ford campaign. Clearly a dead of the winter campaign handicaps challenging parties that are more dependent on door-knocking and lawn signs. The few signs erected in frozen terrain are buried in snow drifts. It is a risky business to have volunteers delivering election materials door to door. This is not democracy.
Identifying supporters and getting voters dependent on rides to vote will be challenging.
PC candidates failing to participate in all-candidate meetings and debates is an affront. Rather than addressing the human right to food, shelter and livable wages, Ford is campaigning as the best person to stand up to Donald Trump's tariffs, which is a stretch.
There is a sizeable element of craziness in Ford's pronouncement for tunnels under Highway 401. Ford broke a contract with LCBO one year early costing millions of dollars. Apparently, getting beer at the corner store is more important than putting money toward, let's say, health care. Then there is the $3 billion he is spending with $200 handouts to buy votes.
It's not pretty.
Ron Brydges, St. Catharines
Ford's a leader
Re: Mayors of Niagara's 3 largest cities endorse Ford, Jan. 31
I agree with the three Niagara mayors endorsing Doug Ford.
Right now we have very large, serious problems.
We need someone who is leading all the premiers in Canada.
He is a strong fighter.
Gail Williams, St. Catharines
Minority
is needed
I have to admit, I agree with the three Niagara mayors, Doug Ford is the best leader for these troubled times.
However, looking at his history of spontaneous decisions, some of which are ridiculous (tunnel under Highway 401) others reveal his obsession with booze. These things cost needlessly.
Then there was the Greenbelt. He didn't know he couldn't do that? A highway through farmland to save 20 minutes of travel time? Now he's buying back the 407 former Tory premier Mike Harris sold.
I guess what we have is an impulsive premier. This isn't a desirable attribute in any premier in these precarious times.
Now we're faced with a "snap" election to give Mr. Ford a larger majority. These elections are costly.
I believe we need a minority government at this time.
We need to focus on the "train wreck" our southern neighbour has become and perhaps we can find a way to encourage some U.S. doctors to come here.
Pete Morro, St. Catharines

Letters welcome
Send them to letters@ni-
agaradailies.
com or mail to PO Box 5031 Stn Main, St. Catharines, Ont., L2R 7T4. Letters should be 250 words or less, and may be edited for length and clarity. Please include your name, address and phone number for verification purposes.
CAPTION:

Document 2 of 249

Niagara politicians endorsement of premier concerning, says Mac prof

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/niagara-politicians-endorsement-premier/docview/3170778614/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Niagara+politicians+endorsement+of+premier+concerning%2C+says+Mac+prof&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Niagara-on-the-Lake Coun. Sandra O'Connor wanted to make it clear that a colleague endorsing Premier Doug Ford didn't mean the town has thrown its support behind a particular candidate.
After endorsements from Welland Mayor Frank Campion, St. Catharines Mayor Mat Siscoe and Niagara Falls Mayor Jim Diodati of Niagara Falls to support Ford in his quest to win another majority government, Niagara-on-the-Lake Deputy Lord Mayor Erwin Wiens publicly endorsed the premier during an event at Niagara District Airport.
"We look forward to working with you so much," said Wiens, prior to Ford making an announcement during a Feb. 3 campaign sweep in Niagara.
Prior to Ford's trip to the area, the three mayors issued a statement "recognizing that having Doug Ford's strong, stable leadership when it comes to negotiating with (U.S. President) Donald Trump is critical to ensuring Ontario's continued economic success."
O'Connor said she wanted to distinguish between a personal endorsement from a councillor and the individual who represents the community. She was concerned residents could misunderstand the endorsement process.
"(Wiens) is free to endorse anybody," said O'Connor. "I just think people will think the town is endorsing the premier. The town does not endorse anybody. It is a personal decision (by the councillor)."
During the election campaign a number of mayors have publicly endorsed Ford, including Oakville's Rob Burton, Brampton's Patrick Brown (a former provincial Tory leader) and Windsor's Drew Dilkens.
Peter Graefe, associate professor of political science at McMaster University, said there has been a flurry of municipal politicians endorsing candidates - particularly the Progressive Conservatives. He said they risk alienating the people who voted for them, especially at a time when politics is polarizing the electorate.
"It may affect their trust," said Graefe in an interview. "There could be some negative ramifications in the next municipal election."
He said it has been a tradition that municipal politics is free of party affiliations. Endorsements could compromise that political environment, he said.
The local endorsements have raised concerns among some Niagara-on-the-Lake residents who posted on social media they were upset about Wiens and the Niagara mayors endorsing the premier.
"Mayors shouldn't be endorsing any provincial or federal candidates," said Adam Russell Box. "Since they were voted into office by a (sic) array of people with different views."
Elena Marandi-Bonner said, "Our elected municipal politicians should be non-partisan and certainly not seen to favour certain candidates."
Graefe said these municipal endorsements could create in the minds of the community a sense there is some sort of quid pro quo involved.
He said there could be "benefits" of endorsing a candidate who will eventually win. But those endorsements could also have an effect on an opposing candidate, he said.
He pointed out Niagara Falls riding has been represented by NDP MPP Wayne Gates since 2014.
"Some politicians have long memories," said Graefe.
In Ontario, in general the Progressive Conservatives are usually strong in rural areas, while in the urban areas the NDP and Liberals are traditionally popular, he said.
It can happen that the Liberals and NDP ignore the rural areas, while the PCs could conceivably forget the more urban areas of the province, he said.
It could mean creating a wider political gulf in Ontario, said Graefe.
Now days until the Thursday election, Graefe said the NDP and Liberals have not had enough time to make an impact on voters.
"The NDP and Liberals have been weak on the ground game," he said.
Still, the Liberals have been polling a bit better as Ontarians start to vote in advance polls.
Nanos Research has the PCs provincially with the support of 45.2 per cent of decided voters, followed by the Liberals at 29.5 per cent, the NDP at 17.7 per cent and the Greens at 5.4 per cent.
Abacus Data has the PCs at 45 per cent, a five-point decline, while the Liberals are at 28 per cent, with the NDP at 21 per cent and the Greens at six per cent.
And the 338 Canada polling website has Niagara Centre, and St. Catharines leaning PC. Niagara West is projected to be a secured PC seat and Niagara Falls is as a toss-up between the PC and NDP candidates, according to 338 Canada.
Graefe said the strong support of the PCs in all four ridings in Niagara, including in Niagara Centre, indicates Ford has tapped into matters that are concerning to residents, including cross-border issues and tariffs, which will hit hard the area's manufacturing industries.
"These issues have been top of mind with the manufacturing plants in St. Catharines and Niagara Falls," he said. "Ford is drawing a high number of male manufacturing workers, and even winemakers."
CAPTION: Erwin Wiens, Niagara-on-the-Lake deputy lord mayor and councillor, left, endorsed Ontario Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford during his recent provincial election campaign visit to Niagara District Airport.Ray Spiteri Niagara Falls Review

CREDIT: Kevin Werner Reporter

Document 3 of 249

Tories failed on housing crisis, challengers argue: Liberal Kapur, NDP's Augustyn promote their parties' plans

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tories-failed-on-housing-crisis-challengers-argue/docview/3170771383/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tories+failed+on+housing+crisis%2C+challengers+argue%3A+Liberal+Kapur%2C+NDP%27s+Augustyn+promote+their+parties%27+plans&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Progressive Conservative government has been given a failing grade from local opposition candidates in Thursday's election as it pertains to Ontario's growing housing crisis.
During a poorly attended regionwide candidates forum, hosted by Niagara Association of Realtors (NAR) on Monday, the two contenders who did show conveyed how their political parties would deal with the housing attainability crisis, if elected.
Niagara Falls Liberal candidate Shafoli Kapur said her party's housing strategy is the "most ambitious" one ever proposed by a provincial party.
"We're going to leverage private-sector efficiency while ensuring public interest safeguards through structured partnerships and we're going to treat housing as a fundamental necessity rather than a speculative investment tool," she said during the forum held in a committee room at Gale Centre in Niagara Falls.
"The housing crisis in Niagara is not just about statistics. It's about people. Families striving for homeownership. Renters facing displacement and young individuals contemplating leaving the region due to unaffordable living costs. (Ontario Premier and PC Leader) Doug Ford's administration has failed to address these pressing issues. however, with bold and effective leadership, we have the opportunity to change course."
The Niagara Association of Realtors and a senior regional economist with Canada Mortgage and Housing Corp. presented the 2025 market outlook before opening the floor to candidates. Organizers said all Niagara candidates running for the four major parties were invited.
Only Shafoli, Niagara West NDP candidate Dave Augustyn, and St. Catharines Liberal candidate Robin McPherson, agreed to participate.
McPherson, however, cancelled just prior to the event. She sent a statement that was read at the end of the forum.
Augustyn said as a former 12-year mayor of Pelham, he understands housing issues.
"Everyone knows that we're in a housing crisis. Folks are spending more income on housing. Young people can't afford a home," he said.
"There are parents, grandparents worried about the kids getting out of the basement."
He said the Progressive Conservatives "failed" to help the situation.
"I think their Plan A was to bulldoze the Greenbelt and build big mansions on it, and when Plan A failed they didn't have a Plan B," said Augustyn.
"Housing starts are actually the lowest since the mid-'50s when you factor in population. Our population is growing through the roof and housing starts haven't."
Augustyn said an NDP government would provide grants and low-cost financing for non-market housing and for first-time homebuyers.
"I remember talking to some folks going door to door and they said, we bought our first house in 1980 when the government guaranteed the down payment and that was a great program then," he said.
"Let's bring that program back. Getting back into the business of building co-op housing and preferring apartments."
He said the NDP also supports bringing back rent controls, legalizing fourplexes and increasing density around transit hubs.
"We've also said that we need to make sure that those development charges are at the right level or reduce them. We'll actually reduce property taxes ... because we're going to be uploading many of the services that were downloaded."
Kapur said the Ford government has "consistently fallen short" of its housing target, thus increasing the affordability crisis.
She said the Liberals would "streamline" the approval process for new builds.
"We're going to simplify and expedite approvals for housing projects in high-demand areas like St. Catharines, Niagara Falls and Welland," she said.
"We're going to offer incentives to developers building affordable and rental housing. We're going to remove the Ontario Land Transfer Tax for first-time homebuyers, seniors downsizing and non-profit homebuilders, saving families an average of $13,500.
"We're going to scrap development charges on new homes under 3,000 square feet, potentially reducing home costs by up to $170,000."
She said her party's plan calls for a fund to assist municipalities cover infrastructure costs, promoting "responsible" development.
"We're going to introduce fair, phased-in rent control. We're going to resolve landlord-tenant disputes within two months and address the 53,000 (case) backlog at the landlord tenant board," said Kapur.
"We're going to implement an interest-free loan program to prevent evictions and homelessness. We're going to allocate $3.6 billion by consolidating ineffective provincial housing programs and eliminating wasteful spending."
CAPTION: Niagara West NDP candidate Dave Augustyn and Niagara Falls Liberal candidate Shafoli Kapur talk at a Monday candidates forum on housing-related issues hosted by the Niagara Association of Realtors at Gale Centre in Niagara Falls.Niagara West NDP candidate Dave AugustynNiagara Falls Liberal candidate Shafoli KapurJulie Jocsak St. Catharines Standard

CREDIT: Ray Spiteri Reporter

Document 4 of 249

Gates 'stooping too low' says Liberal challenger: Kapur acknowledges she made donations to PC and NDP candidates

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/gates-stooping-too-low-says-liberal-challenger/docview/3170771334/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Gates+%27stooping+too+low%27+says+Liberal+challenger%3A+Kapur+acknowledges+she+made+donations+to+PC+and+NDP+candidates&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With the provincial election just a days away, political mudslinging has made its way into the contest for the Niagara Falls riding.
According to a news release from NDP incumbent Wayne Gates, recent campaign finance returns from Elections Ontario showed Liberal candidate Shafoli Kapur donated $970 to the Progressive Conservative Party of Ontario less than a year ago.
The release said it "raises significant questions about the seriousness" of Kapur's candidacy.
Kapur, who resides and works in Toronto, was selected by the Liberal party to run in the riding that represents Niagara Falls, Niagara-on-the-Lake and Fort Erie.
"The Liberal candidate is not running a serious campaign. She was parachuted into Niagara Falls without any connection to the local community, and now we discover she donated nearly $1,000 to Doug Ford's Conservatives less than a year ago," Gates stated in the release.
"It's a political circus."
Gates said, as has been the case in recent years, the Niagara Falls riding is "shaping up" to be a two-way race between himself and his PC opponent - this time, Niagara Falls city Coun. Ruth-Ann Nieuwesteeg.
He said the Liberals received just eight per cent of the vote in Niagara Falls in the 2022 provincial election. Gates was re-elected with 48 per cent of the vote, while the PCs secured just less than 37 per cent.
In an interview, Kapur said while she has "all the respect" for Gates, bringing up her donation is "just stooping too low."
She said the donation had "nothing to do" with party politics.
"It was just a family connection who needed fundraising, and he was just doing a party and I donated to him," said Kapur.
"I do it to everybody - anybody who asks me for donations, if I know them, it's like a personal gesture, a loving gesture to somebody."
Kapur said she even donated to "somebody from the NDP" a few months back.
"He helped me with my son. He was a school board trustee in my ward, and he helped me with my child's situation in the school when we were going through a rough time, and I gave him money, too, when he was running," she said.
"It's not a big deal. I don't even look at party - for me, it's a human."
Kapur said she believes Gates making an issue about her donation means he's "very nervous."
"I don't stoop low. If you look at his social media, he's always punching at me and he's saying I'm not from the region - he needs to calm down, everybody knows that," she said.
"That's fine, if I'm not from the region. I'm a Canadian citizen, I can run from anywhere. Mr. Gates talks about him getting so much from the province - I've been talking to a lot of people in the community, and they say he takes credit for more things than he actually does."
CAPTION: After a mild-mannered YourTV debate earlier this month, the mudslinging has begun in a campaign that features, among the candidates, Niagara Falls NDP incumbent Wayne Gates and Liberal Shafoli Kapur.metroland file photo

Document 5 of 249

Comic Marc-Anthony Sinagoga on family life and being Doug Ford

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/comic-marc-anthony-sinagoga-on-family-life-being/docview/3170770807/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Comic+Marc-Anthony+Sinagoga+on+family+life+and+being+Doug+Ford&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Comedian Marc-Anthony Sinagoga won't say which way he leans politically, but he won't be heartbroken if Doug Ford is still the premier after Feb. 27.
Purely for the material.
Sinagoga's impression of Ford has served him well over the years, on stage and on social media. His take isn't that far from reality, just a bit more redneck and gangster, with an obsession for doughnuts. There's enough truth in it that Ford's camp has come to check him out.
Which has Sinagoga wondering if the premier is swiping his material.
"His team is always watching what I'm doing," says the Toronto comic, playing The Warehouse in St. Catharines on Feb. 28. "All of a sudden, I'll see one of his videos and he's making more jokes about his weight, he's making more jokes about bacon and doughnuts.
"I'm like, 'I was doing you before you were, man!' It's a weird thing but it's great."
Sinagoga is hoping to meet Ford some day, but admits he wouldn't know what to say if the moment arrives.
"I'd just be like, 'Let's take a photo, people would love it. Let me show up as you to one of your press conferences.'"
Which brings him back to the election. He isn't working on a Bonnie Crombie impression - it's Ford or bust.
"That's extremely selfish of me, but if I can squeeze more from this orange, that would be fantastic," he says. "Or this doughnut, whatever. Any more crème out of this Boston Crème, that would be great."
But for Sinagoga, politics is a sidebar for his current "Family Values" standup tour. The Toronto comic has been through some major life changes lately, including becoming a dad and owning a home.
Serious stuff. Except when he's on stage.
"You get a lot of the family respect now," he says. "Everyone's like, 'Oh, you're a man now, finally.' I'm like, 'I'm in my 30s here! I feel like I've been a man for 20 years already.'"
Throw in tales about his Italian upbringing, and there's plenty to relate to with Sinagoga's show. It's why, aside from the Ford material, he shies away from political material.
Doing shows in both the U.S. and Canada for the current tour, he knows it can be a touchy subject in the current climate.
"I find that, for the most part, people come to comedy shows to try to escape the day-to-day rhetoric of anything that's going on in politics," he says. "I try to stay away from it as well. A, because I don't feel like I'm the most educated individual to talk on politics, but also, I don't want to create problems.
"Sometimes I'll jab at it and be like, 'Hey, I'm the guy from the 51st state.' But I feel like with most things in mainstream news media, people are like, 'Oh, please don't talk about this unless you're going to make me die laughing about it.'"
Marc-Anthony Sinagoga plays The Warehouse at 11 Geneva St. for two shows Feb. 28, 7 and 9 p.m. The 7 p.m. show is sold out. For tickets go to warehouseconcerthall.com.
CAPTION: Marc-Anthony Sinagoga

CREDIT: John Law Reporter

Title: Comic Marc-Anthony Sinagoga on family life and being Doug Ford

Document 6 of 249

Readers write Letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write-letters-editor/docview/3169359828/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write+Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Fear of U.S.
is growing
Re: Trump threatens to slap 25 per cent tariffs on all auto imports, Feb. 18
I have shifted from thinking of Donald Trump as a cartoonish buffoon to actually being frightened of what he has in store for the U.S., Canada and the rest of the world.
His suggestions the U.S. could take Greenland, the Panama Canal and even Canada as his 51st state are a mockery of international law which makes it illegal for a world leader to even speculate about aggression against another nation.
And he has called Putin a "genius" for his invasion of Ukraine.
He has moved the U.S. from being a relatively calm influence for peace to a source of unease for the rest of the world.
David Fowler, Wainfleet
Living above
the crack house
Watching Donald Trump and rave about taking over other countries including Canada makes me recall watching a program in which late U.S. comedian Robin Williams said being the northern neighbour of the U.S. was like "living in an apartment above a crack house."
That was when U.S. leadership was not made up of a group of crooks led by a spoiled man-child.
We are being warned by several Canadian officials these wild provocations should not be taken lightly.
There are also reports a substantial number of Albertans, 18 per cent according to one poll, would look favourably upon a U.S. takeover.
The one positive to come out of this farce is Canadians are as united against this threat to our society as we were in two world wars, both of which we entered and fought in for years before the U.S. did.
Richard Murri, Niagara Falls
No-show
Tory candidate
Re: St. Catharines candidates share no Valentine's Day love for PC hopeful, Feb. 14
If a candidate can't bother to show up for a debate, will they show to represent their constituents at Queen's Park?
Again, another disappointment from St. Catharines PC candidate Sal Sorrento. I agree with Liberal candidate Robin McPherson, it shows a lack of respect and I'll add it's insulting.
As for GO train service in Niagara, there is no excuse there isn't daily rail service from Niagara to Toronto without having to switch in Burlington.
When I first moved here, I heard Niagara didn't receive an equal share of the pie because we didn't speak with one voice. We can't seem to move the needle to rectify that and we continue to spend a lot of money supporting 12 municipalities.
Why do we continue to put up with the lack of accountability we see from our elected, not appointed, politicians? Ask yourself if your life and the lives of those you care for have improved in the last seven years. Elect someone who will show up.
Catherine Livingston, St. Catharines

Readers write

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write/docview/3168997176/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Voting accordingly
Re: Tory candidate slammed for not showing up to debate health care, housing, Feb. 13
I was appalled to read Ontario Progressive Conservative candidates Bill Steele and Sal Sorento failed to appear at debate opportunities hosted by YourTV.
How dare you expect the job when you do not show up for the interview. As far as I'm concerned, your resumes are going straight in the trash.
I will vote for no one who thinks they are above answering tough questions from their constituents. Not showing for debates or answering media is troubling behaviour exhibited by PC candidates, provincially and federally.
Upon whose direction is this tactic being used? Our elected officials answer to us, the people.
I will vote accordingly.
Henry Vanderkant, Welland
Good for
the mayors
Re: Are the mayors kidding?, Letters, Feb. 13
A majority of the letters in this publication are very anti-Doug Ford and show a frustration with our Niagara big city mayors endorsing him. It is almost like some people are taking this endorsement personally.
I think the mayors endorsing Ford is a good thing. Good for them to take a stand. They know he is the best candidate to fight for jobs. They know he is the best candidate to support workers who will be impacted by tariffs.
Despite his many "warts," I think Ford is by far the best person to lead us through the next chaotic years while U.S. President Donald Trump is in power.
Steve Baechler, Niagara Falls
A master
of gimmicks
Re: Ford scores fourth political endorsement in Niagara,
Feb. 8
So, the mayors of St. Catharines, Niagara Falls and Welland endorse Doug Ford.
Ford's tenure as premier has been plagued by deficits and political gimmicks.
Some examples: buck a beer; blue licence plates and "free" licence renewals we will pay for through taxes to recoup lost revenue and alcohol in convenience and grocery stores (our PC government could have waited a year and avoided wasting huge amounts of money.)
Others examples include a $200 rebate cheques, costing taxpayers $3 billion; bike lanes with taxpayers paying to build them and paying to eliminate them; an early election despite more than a year to go in the premier's existing mandate and a 99-year lease for a private spa at Ontario Place, awarded without competitive bids.
To quote from an article by Ben Eisen and Jake Fuss from Fraser Institute: "... if the Ford government wants to get serious about economic growth and making Ontario a better place to live, work and invest, it should enact substantial and permanent tax relief instead of relying on gimmicks to disguise its high-tax approach to governance."
Dan Augustine, Wainfleet

Document 8 of 249

Diana Huson resigning from regional council: 'Life has a way of surprising us,' says Pelham representative

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/diana-huson-resigning-regional-council/docview/3168996853/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Diana+Huson+resigning+from+regional+council%3A+%27Life+has+a+way+of+surprising+us%2C%27+says+Pelham+representative&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: One of Niagara Region's most active councillors is resigning her seat.
Diana Huson, who represents Pelham and also serves as chair of planning and economic development, said she made "the difficult decision" that she will step down on March 15 and prioritize her family life.
"While serving my community in this role has been one of the most rewarding and fulfilling experiences, sometimes things change," Huson said in an emailed statement. "I've recently made some changes in my life, such as accepting a new job, that have made me reflect on how I spend my time.
"A little over three years ago, my father passed away due to prostate cancer. His absence is something I still have great difficulty talking about. It's been very difficult for my mother, who has been navigating life as a widow."
Huson said in an ideal world, the changes would align perfectly with the end of the council term, "but as we all know, life has a way of surprising us."
In her day job, Huson is director of marketing and communications at Vineland Research and Innovation Centre, which conducts research to improve the Canadian horticulture industry.
In a short phone interview, Huson said she can't reveal where she is headed next, other than it will be "in a couple of weeks."
"I don't know if I'm leaving politics for now or forever. Who knows, down the road, that door might open again."
Huson first ran for regional council in 2018 and upended Pelham's incumbent councillor, Brian Baty, and sitting mayor Dave Augustyn. Huson received 2,817 votes, 534 more than Baty. Augustyn finished third with 1,694.
Augustyn had entered the race at the last minute after Premier Doug Ford cancelled the first Niagara-wide election for regional chair hours before nominations closed.
"When I first put my name on the ballot in 2018," Huson said in her email, "I ran a campaign focused on values, the need for change, and Pelham having a strong voice at the council table. I was pleasantly surprised and overwhelmed by the level of support I received.
"I was re-elected in 2022 with nearly 70 per cent of the vote, leaving me feeling that perhaps I'm doing something right. I've been incredibly grateful for the opportunity."
In 2022, Huson received 3,461 votes, 2,435 more than runner-up Wally Braun's 1,026. Fred Sarvis finished third with 495 votes.
Regarding vacant regional council seats, municipal bylaws usually give the local council a choice of holding a byelection or appointing a replacement. If the representative is appointed, it's often the runner-up in the most recent election.
The next municipal election is on Oct. 26, 2026.
Pelham Mayor Marv Junkin did not immediately respond to a voice-mail message seeking comment.
Huson's first session at regional headquarters in 2018 was the first council meeting she had ever attended. Soon after, councillors elected her chair of the Region's planning and economic development committee, a position she now holds after being re-elected three times. Chairs serve two-year terms.
Huson also chaired the Region's women's advisory committee and played a vital role in helping Niagara Peninsula Conservation Authority recover from internal strife and mismanagement as the board's vice-chair and Pelham representative.
Huson's profile beyond the region includes serving as the first voting member from Niagara to serve on the Federation of Canadian Municipalities' board, which sets policy priorities for municipalities on a national level. FCM holds its board elections every year. She was elected to the FCM Ontario Caucus for four consecutive years.
Huson said the most rewarding experiences she had as a regional councillor were the ones closest to home.
"I've enthusiastically supported our small business community, attending new openings and celebrating milestones, often as a surprise," Huson said in her email. "I've also championed environmental conservation, rolling up my sleeves to help plant trees in parks, clean up trails or serving on our conservation authority."
Huson recently founded the local chapter of Equal Voice Canada, a national volunteer organization supporting women in politics by co-ordinating initiatives, events and programming that support learning and networking in this space. The official launch is scheduled for March 5.
Before politics, Huson worked in operations and governance at Brock University for 15 years.
CAPTION: Diana Huson is resigning her Pelham seat on Niagara Region council as of March 15.Metroland file photo

Document 9 of 249

Commuters in Niagara drive debate in election campaign: Candidates agree more needs to be done to accommodate growing population in region

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/commuters-niagara-drive-debate-election-campaign/docview/3168222632/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Commuters+in+Niagara+drive+debate+in+election+campaign%3A+Candidates+agree+more+needs+to+be+done+to+accommodate+growing+population+in+region&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-02-19&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Improving how Niagara residents travel - whether by highway, rail or air - has been a central local topic during Doug Ford's two regional visits this provincial election.
It's also on the minds of several local candidates across Niagara.
During a Jan. 31 campaign stop in Niagara Falls, the Ontario Progressive Conservative leader announced a re-elected PC government will widen the Queen Elizabeth Way between Burlington and St. Catharines.
During a Feb. 8 visit to Niagara District Airport, Ford said he's awaiting project details, including the cost, when it may begin and how long it will take.
"We'll make sure we contact you as soon as we find out," he said in response to a question from a Niagara Falls Review reporter.
"The commuters that go from Niagara into Toronto, I've been on that strip like a thousand times, it's absolutely jammed, so we're going to widen it and add more capacity."
Twinning Garden City Skyway to fight gridlock was also a stated priority of the last PC government.
In an emailed statement, Niagara Falls PC candidate Ruth-Ann Nieuwesteeg said widening the QEW and the recently announced Casablanca GO station in Grimsby will be a "game changer for our community."
"It will tackle gridlock and provide more convenient connections to jobs, housing and transit in our fast-growing community," she said.
Nieuwesteeg said the PC team is "dedicated" to working with municipal and federal partners to expand the airport.
Niagara Falls incumbent NDP candidate Wayne Gates said he has advocated for GO train service since his successful byelection campaign in 2014.
"We've increased GO train in the summer. We still need two-way, all-day GO all the way to Niagara Falls," he said. "Our area, it doesn't matter where you live ... is just growing rapidly and to attract those new, young families and tourists, we have to make sure we have two-way, all-day GO."
St. Catharines Liberal candidate Robin McPherson said further investments in GO is important.
"There are lots of people who are using a third of their income to just pay for their car," she said. "It's about making sure that people have options."
McPherson said many people want to hop on the GO train in Niagara but the current hours "don't make sense for the average person."
She said while there needs to be a balance between road and rail investments, statistics show expanding roadways doesn't alleviate traffic, it "just puts more cars on the roads."
McPherson also questioned how realistic widening the QEW is.
"We have agricultural lands that are running along the highway and then through St. Catharines, you have houses literally built right up against the highway," she said.
When asked when Niagara could expect all-day, two-way GO train service between Niagara and Toronto, Ford passed the question to then transportation minister Prabmeet Sarkaria, who said they've "made some historic progress on the Niagara line."
He said the government introduced 300 additional trips on the overall GO transit system this past summer - "the largest increase in over a decade."
"This region, particularly, has been the beneficiary of a lot of that investment and will continue," he said.
"Very soon we will be able to reduce the travel time from Niagara to Union Station by over 20 minutes. We're just in the testing and commissioning phase of that at Union Station, but once that is built - that specific portion of the rail line - people in this region can get to Union Station 20 minutes faster."
Ford also described expanding Niagara District Airport as "one of the highest priorities" in his destination strategy for Niagara.
"You have to get people here. If you have a great destination, you have to get them in through road, train, or by air," Ford said.
"We plan on really working hard with the federal government because that falls under their jurisdiction, but we're there to help invest and support expanding Niagara airport - it's absolutely critical."
The airport is owned by Niagara-on-the-Lake, Niagara Falls and St. Catharines.
Gates said he's also an advocate for expanding the airport, "provided we do the proper consultation with the residents that it's going to affect, obviously with more planes flying overhead."
He said there are questions related to the QEW widening project that need to be answered.
"What properties are you going to have to get rid of? Are we going to end up losing more farmland?"
McPherson said there's a "big" opportunity for the province to partner with municipalities and the federal government on GO train expansion and the airport.
"We have to be realistic that it is going to take a lot of money. It's not as simple as just saying we're going to build a new GO train station - the rail infrastructure has to go with it," she said.
"I have council friends who are in London and who are in Kitchener and they use their airport - we're not talking overseas flights, but within Canada, being able to move within Canada. It's something that could be really fantastic and not just for people who have their own private planes."
Mishka Balsom, Greater Niagara Chamber of Commerce chief executive officer, said investments in the airport and GO train service are top of mind for members.
"All of us are talking about the transportation between Toronto and Niagara and the challenges that they have," she said. "We have an economic asset in the Niagara District Airport and to make it a fully functioning airport, I think the time is right for it."
Balsom said Niagara is Canada's largest metropolitan area in North America with no commercial airport within 25 kilometres.
"It would attract a different traveller - more the conferences, more the commercial traveller," she said. "We also have to look at it from a Niagara perspective. This would create a lot of advantages for people living in Niagara, being able to just drive up to the airport and from there getting a flight to New York City or Montreal or Ottawa or Chicago."
Balsom said more regular GO train service between Niagara and Toronto is important for the growing region.
"When you look at the population forecast ... I think we hold a lot of potential," she said. "I regularly drive up to Burlington and hop on the train there, but if one could do this from Niagara, it would be a great advantage for sure."
CAPTION: Mishka Balsom, Greater Niagara Chamber of Commerce chief executive officer, said said more regular GO train service between Niagara and Toronto is important for the growing region. The previous PC government announced plans to expand Garden City Skyway in St. Catharines, and during a stop in Niagara this election campaign, the party announced, if re-elected, it would expand the QEW between Burlington and St. Catharines to alleviate congestion.metroland file photo

Document 10 of 249

Readers write Letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write-letters-editor/docview/3167861694/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write+Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-02-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: No special
treatment
Re: Canada's premiers meet with Trump advisers at White House, Feb. 12
Donald Trump continues to carry on about the weakness of the Canadian border. I suggest we provide him an up close and personal experience showing how strong our border actually is.
Canada hosts the G7 summit in June. Trump in all likelihood will attend.
Canada has one of the toughest borders in the world, meaning an American with a felony record typically has difficulty travelling here.
Trump is a convicted felon. Felons that want to travel to Canada can apply for special permission to enter.
There should be no special dispensation given to Trump in this instance.
He should have to go through the same procedures any other person with a similar criminal record would be required to follow.
If he refuses, Trump should be denied entry.
Larry Newton, Welland
Party of the
working class?
Re: Supporting Ford,
Letters, Feb. 7
The letter writer said "The PCs have become the party of the working class, building the economy and supporting good-paying jobs." I take issue with this.
Does this reader contend the only "working people" are those who have well-paid jobs, work in offices or in highly skilled, high-paying roles in construction and industry?
We have a huge population of genuine "working people" who have to work at one, two or more minimum- wage part-time jobs just to exist.
I would further draw attention to the fact that, though Doug Ford is gaining support from a number of unions, many unions have a vested interest in supporting the unprecedented push for new housing and highway construction. The majority of this construction is being done in the higher-priced market rather than affordable, subsidized or geared-to-income housing that would benefit more of the "working" population.
It is no coincidence Ford, already in possession of a majority, called this entirely unnecessary election before the RCMP concluded its investigation into the Greenbelt development scandal.
I would like to remind people they can look at municipal tax bills and rail about increases. Stop and think. Did you vote for the Progressive Conservative candidate in the last election because Ford promised to lower taxes?
Well, those tax breaks were due to downloading of services and cutbacks in transfer payments to lower levels of government. They were also due to the privatization of many services like sectors in health care we rely on.
I close by reminding voters of a story told by Tommy Douglas many decades ago. To paraphrase, it boiled down to: Working people voting for the conservative party is like mice electing cats to govern them.
It doesn't matter what colour the cats are or what they promise ... they are still cats.
Peter Bryan, Welland

Document 11 of 249

Hate growing toward Niagara newcomers, candidates agree: Conservative blasted for skipping debate

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/hate-growing-toward-niagara-newcomers-candidates/docview/3167861524/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Hate+growing+toward+Niagara+newcomers%2C+candidates+agree%3A+Conservative+blasted+for+skipping+debate&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-02-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There's too much hate towards immigrants and refugees in Niagara, say the Niagara Falls riding New Democrat and Liberal candidates running in the provincial election.
"We do have people that are racist in our community - we can't hide from it. We have to tell the truth," NDP incumbent Wayne Gates said during a YourTV Cogeco debate Friday afternoon. "We have to stop having any kind of racism."
Liberal Shafoli Kapur said hate is "growing" against immigrants across Ontario, "especially towards certain communities."
The candidates made their comments in response to a question from Bridges Niagara, formerly known as Niagara Folk Arts Multicultural Centre, which asked candidates what initiatives they would propose to better support newcomers integrate into the region's workforce and social fabric.
Kapur, who moved to Canada from India 17 years ago and has a Toronto business that helps immigrants settle in the country, said supporting immigrants and refugees "isn't just the right thing to do, it's also an investment in Niagara's future."
"They leave their homes and they come to this country with the hope to make this their new home and if they are going to face discrimination it's going to be very hard for them to integrate into the society," she said.
"I work very closely with immigrants (and) every single day I come across highly educated individuals having master's degrees from back home coming here only seeking a better lifestyle and they're coming here and driving Uber, they're working in Subway, they're working in a coffee shop. They are not getting the jobs that they have been trained for, they have the education for."
In framing its question, Bridges Niagara said immigrants and refugees are facing challenges when it comes to entering the workforce due to cuts to both provincial and federal English as a second language funding and employment supports.
It said there is also a "negative anti-immigrant sentiment growing in our province."
Gates said agencies such as Bridges Niagara, which offers volunteering opportunities to newcomers, need more funding.
"We should make sure that we make it easier to have internationally trained doctors be able to come to this country and be doctors, instead of making it harder," he said.
Gates and Kapur addressed a wide range of issues that included food insecurity, health care, transportation investments, governance and tourism during a nearly hour-long debate filmed by YourTV Cogeco in its Niagara Falls studio.
YourTV said PC candidate Ruth-Ann Nieuwesteeg, a Niagara Falls city councillor, did not respond to an invitation to participate in the debate. Green Party candidate Celia Taylor declined the invitation.
Both Gates and Kapur took notice of Nieuwesteeg's absence.
"If you want to be a representative of Niagara, your first job is to show up," said Gates. "But my local Conservative opponent is hiding from the public and the press.
"The Conservatives simply refuse to defend (Ontario PC Leader) Doug Ford's record on health care, on housing, on affordability in the province of Ontario. This Conservative candidate is unwilling to show up and defend that record.
"When you want this job - you want to take my job - you have an obligation to show up at a debate," said Gates, who has been an MPP since 2014.
Kapur said while she's not as well known as Gates or Nieuwesteeg, she was "brave" enough to participate "when the Conservative (candidate) chose not to even show up."
The debate will be posted to the TV station's website as of Sunday.
It will air on channels 700 and 100 on Epico Monday at 12:30 p.m. and Tuesday at 1 p.m. It will also air Saturday, Feb. 22, at 10:30 a.m.; Sunday, Feb. 23, at 1 p.m. and Tuesday, Feb. 25, at 2:30 p.m.
CAPTION: Niagara Falls NDP incumbent candidate Wayne Gates and Liberal candidate Shafoli Kapur participate in a YourTV debate Friday.Bob Tymczyszyn St. Catharines Standard

Readers write Letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write-letters-editor/docview/3166972007/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write+Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Action needed quickly
Re: Canada has less than 30 days to satisfy Donald Trump's border demands. Here's what it plans to do, Feb. 4
I see an endless stream of RCMP, OPP, Niagara Parks Police and Black Hawk helicopters going along Niagara Parkway.
I do not see any way this huge expenditure is accomplishing much, other than depleting our treasury.
Last time Donald Trump was elected president he said Mexico would pay for his border wall.
Mexico was not duped by him. We are being duped by him now, we are paying for his border wall.
Why are we not demanding the U.S. secure its border and prevent drugs and illegal guns from getting into Canada? I would venture most of the fentanyl coming into Canada is from our southern neighbours and pretty well 100 per cent of the guns are from south of the border. The harm we cause them is minuscule whereas the damage to Canada, from them, is enormous.
For the longest time Canada has been an exporter of raw materials while importing more expensive finished products, often made from material they got from us at discount prices. For instance, we should be keeping oil for ourselves and our future needs.
Why do we need anything from the U.S.? We have become lazy, depending on the States and others to make what we could produce ourselves.
We grow tomatoes and can't make our own ketchup? Heinz and French say they use Canadian tomatoes and make the product in Canada. But profits still go elsewhere. This applies to so many products.
Most scientists say a price or tax is needed on carbon dioxide to prevent our planet becoming lethal to life. I am a retired scientist and am distraught by the Canadian public being brainwashed by a political slogan to ignore the once-in-a-century climate disasters that happen routinely.
Canada will become a destination for people who want to escape. Our natural resources, such as fresh water, will be increasingly lacking in the rest of the world.
We must increase our military capabilities to prevent other predatory nations taking us over to attain these resources, including our neighbour to the south.
This all needs to happen quickly or Canada will soon cease to exist.
Joe Szabo, Niagara Falls
Seat at the table
Re: 'Strong' mayors lose,
Letters, Feb. 5
Unifor, LiUNA, International Brotherhood of Electrical Workers (IBEW) and Ontario Professional Fire Fighters Association must all be losers, too, since they have all lined up behind Doug Ford.
Unless we elect a member of government locally, we'll always be missing out on having a real voice and a vote.
Why do you think Douglas Memorial Hospital and the Fort Erie casino were closed?
The choice is clear. I'm tired of being on the outside looking in.
Marvin Warner, Niagara Falls

Document 13 of 249

Candidates share no Valentine's love for PC hopeful

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/candidates-share-no-valentines-love-pc-hopeful/docview/3166972000/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Candidates+share+no+Valentine%27s+love+for+PC+hopeful&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There was no love for the governing Progressive Conservative Party of Ontario as St. Catharines' provincial candidates faced off during a Valentine's Day debate.
Incumbent NDP MPP Jennie Stevens and Liberal challenger Robin McPherson laid out how their parties would tackle issues differently from the Doug Ford government's "failures and fumbles."
The pair addressed a wide range of issues that included tourism, governance, health care and homelessness in the debate hosted by YourTV Cogeco in its Niagara Falls studio Friday.
YourTV said PC candidate Sal Sorrento declined an invitation to participate.
"Unfortunately, our Conservative candidate wasn't here and that is distressing on so many levels because it means there's a lack of respect, in my opinion," McPherson said.
"They should be here. They should be answering the questions."
Stevens speculated Sorrento didn't participate because he's "afraid to tell you all the failures and fumbles that the Ford government has done."
The candidates answered questions posed by moderator Anthony Luongo for almost an hour.
When asked about improving tourism, both agreed that Niagara needs better GO service than what it's getting.
"We have these wonderful announcements about GO stations, but what we really need is a combined concerted effort to actually bring the trains here regularly so that when visitors do come to Niagara, it's convenient for them," McPherson said.
She said there needs to be a partnership between the province and federal government on the issue. The GO train to Niagara is now sharing lines with transport and product delivery trains.
"What we need to do is make sure that there's a dedicated line, and that's something that we can work with the federal government to achieve."
Stevens said she's been advocating for a two-way, all-day GO train from the province for seven years.
She said the NDP, under the leadership of Marit Stiles, will make sure all-day GO train service is accessible to the whole region.
"Residents across Ontario have the advantage to be able to come to our area of the Niagara Region and visit our local wineries," she said.
"And how do they get here? Well, from Toronto, you can't get on an all-day, everyday GO train. We need to make sure that that line is put in place."
Stevens said it's important people can commute back and forth from Toronto, so small businesses within the community can thrive.
Among the 11 questions of large and smaller scope was whether either of the candidates would change Niagara's governance model, which "many feel has been studied and debated for far too long."
McPherson, a St. Catharines city councillor, said things need to change.
"We have great city councillors, we have great regional councillors, but the overall system is not working," she said. "We're not able to solve the problems because we have these two levels of government."
She said when Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie was mayor of Mississauga, she was supportive of the removal of Peel Region and wants to look at Niagara's governance model to see what is the solution.
"Do I know what the perfect solution is? I don't. Is it one Niagara? It could be. Is it four cities? It could be. What we need to do is we need to get access to the reports that the Ford government has, the studies that have been done, to make them public so that we can come up with what I think is the best way: a Niagara-focused and a Niagara-based solution."
Stevens said the Ford government took away Niagara voters' opportunity to vote for their regional chair because that's the "kind of government we're dealing with" right now.
She said the city, region and province must work together to get a perspective on what is better for everyone.
"Is it one Niagara? Could it be more cities? I think it's working fine," said Stevens, a former city councillor.
She said Ontario needs a government that stops downloading on to local municipalities and making property taxes soar.
"It's so important the provincial government understands that municipalities can no longer afford your downloading."
The St. Catharines riding debate will be available to watch at yourtv.tv/niagara on Sunday.
CAPTION: NDP incumbent Jennie Stevens, left, and Liberal challenger Robin McPherson participate in a St. Catharines riding debate hosted by YourTV Cogeco in its Niagara Falls studio Friday.Karena Walter St. Catharines Standard

Document 14 of 249

Tory slammed for not showing up to debate health care and housing

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/tory-slammed-not-showing-up-debate-health-care/docview/3166471572/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Tory+slammed+for+not+showing+up+to+debate+health+care+and+housing&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Only two Niagara Centre candidates participated in a wide-ranging debate hosted by YourTV, tackling topics that included health care and housing affordability - even, at one point, nuclear power.
For just less than an hour, NDP incumbent Jeff Burch and Liberal candidate Damien O'Brien answered more than a dozen questions about what their parties would do, if elected to form the next provincial government.
But considerable focus was directed at who wasn't at the table: Progressive Conservative candidate and Port Colborne Mayor Bill Steele, who YourTV said declined its invitation to participate.
The strikes against Steele began early, with Burch saying the PC Party candidate "did not have the courage or the respect for the voters of Niagara Centre to show up," and only continued as questions focused on family doctors, emergency room wait times, the Welland hospital emergency department and the shuttering of the Port Colborne urgent-care centre.
"I can't believe at this point in time, the Conservative candidate would not be here as the urgent-care centre is threatening to close in his community. This is the No. 1 issue in this riding," said Burch.
Burch reiterated his party's promise to keep the urgent-care centre open.
O'Brien said the Liberals are committed to creating two new medical schools and doubling medical student spots and residency positions to address growing health-care concerns.
"(Doug Ford) is not a premier who has any plans to tackle health care and the fact that his candidate is not here right now to address his leader's failed leadership, I think, speaks volumes about how little the Tories care about health care," said O'Brien.
Housing affordability and homelessness came up frequently, with a series of questions from debate moderator Anthony Luongo and local organizations including The Hope Centre and South Niagara Chambers of Commerce.
O'Brien said Ontario needs more affordable housing. He said the Liberals plan to eliminate the land-transfer tax for first-time home buyers, seniors who are downsizing and non-profit home builders, and his party would scrap development charges for new middle-class housing.
"Ford slashed the money for municipalities trying to build affordable housing and when given the choice, he chose his billion-dollar boondoggle, putting beer in corner scores, rather than building 8,000 new affordable housing units."
Burch said as there are about 80,000 unhoused people across the province that must be supported, there remains a "mess that is the Landlord (and) Tenant Board," which is under supported.
"The government has to get back in the business of building housing for those who need it," he said.
With the provincial government freezing per-student post-secondary education funding, both candidates promised to increase funding for students, with Burch advocating for additional mental health supports.
A former student leader at Brock University, O'Brien said students are struggling under Ford, with institutions "crumbling under the cuts and the chaos."
There were only a few back-and-forth incidents, once when Burch pointed out O'Brien doesn't reside in Niagara - he lives and works in Hamilton - and when O'Brien expressed surprise when Burch mentioned the province needing to look at nuclear power as an electrical grid option.
After the debate, both candidates said they were pleased to have an opportunity to talk about Niagara Centre issues. Burch reiterated his disappointment Steele wasn't there representing the Progressive Conservatives.
"They don't trust their candidates, so how can you trust them to be an MPP?" Burch said.
Luongo said Green party candidate Natasha Bergen did not respond to an invitation to participate in the debate that was taped at YourTV's Niagara Falls studio Thursday and which will air on cable television next week and be posted online.
YourTV said it only invited candidates from parties holding at least one seat in the legislature. New Blue Party Jimmy Jackson and Ontario Party Darryl Weinberg were provided opportunity to submit pre-recorded messages.
CAPTION: Niagara Centre NDP candidate Jeff Burch, left, and the Liberals' Damien O'Brien participate in a YourTV debate to air next week.Bob Tymczyszyn St. Catharines Standard

CREDIT: Victoria Nicolaou Reporter

Readers write Letters to the editor

Full text: Do Tories
support Trump?
Re: Niagara municipal councillors feel the heat over Trump election pics, Feb. 7
I read this article with much concern. St. Catharines city Coun. Peter Secord said he was in the middle of a Conservative nomination campaign for the riding last fall and "conservative/republican views are similar."
Mr. Secord, does the Conservative party support privatized health care, deportation of immigrants, firing of government employees who did not vote for your party, agree with the tariffs Donald Trump is imposing on Canada, etc.? I think Canadians need to watch and listen to the provincial and federal Conservatives very closely and listen to what they are not saying.
Vera Woodland,

Are the
mayors kidding?
Re: Mayors of Niagara's 3 largest cities endorse Ford, Jan. 31
After reading the headline, my immediate reaction was the only big city mayor I knew about in Ontario was Olivia Chow in Toronto. Then I realized Doug Ford gave these three mayors special strong powers to deal with homeless problems.

It was less than three years ago when all we heard from Diodati was complaining that Ford's restrictive COVID policies were killing tourism in Niagara Falls.

Document 16 of 249

Marineland moving ahead with plan to sever its lands: Matter goes before Niagara Falls committee Tuesday

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/marineland-moving-ahead-with-plan-sever-lands/docview/3166080877/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Marineland+moving+ahead+with+plan+to+sever+its+lands%3A+Matter+goes+before+Niagara+Falls+committee+Tuesday&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Marineland is moving ahead with plans to separate its sprawling Niagara Falls property into four parcels of land.
Its land severance proposals will go before the city's committee of adjustment hearing next Tuesday.
The hearing, starting at 4 p.m. at city hall, will also deal with applications for a partial discharge of mortgage and to establish reciprocal blanket easements across all of Marineland's property on Portage Road and Stanley Avenue.
The Portage Road section is on the south side between Stanley and Norton Street. The Stanley portion is on the northeast corner of Stanley and Chippawa Parkway.
The lands are operated as the Marineland theme park with a range of marine mammals, land animal exhibits, amusement rides and ancillary uses.
The main portion of Marineland consists of park amusements including the marine and land animal exhibits and rides.
Its proposed severances pertain to two pieces of undeveloped land or lands used for ancillary purposes, said a city notice.
The proposed consent applications would allow the retained parcels of land, containing the theme park, to be separated from the rest of the property.
The city's notice said more information about Marineland's application will be made available during Tuesday's hearing.
Marineland formally announced it was for sale in 2023, hoping to find new ownership to "transition" the attraction into the future.
In a statement Wednesday, Marineland said the applications have nothing to do with the care it provides for its animals or its ongoing sale but rather is for "routine operating financing."
"Any loan is secured by a mortgage on the land and this severance is solely to create a sufficient parcel of land for security that does not contain any animals that would otherwise complicate the lenders' security," it said.
"The severance is intended to expire once the financing is repaid in the normal course. This is a routine matter unrelated to the future plans of the park."
Marineland said the application and severance "simply clarifies" the security for the lender so there is a partial discharge of its mortgage over parts of the property containing the animals.
"And then the security will be put in place over the part that doesn't contain animals that has been severed off."
People can send comments on the severance proposal to the city via email. They can also provide input by attending the hearing in person or online.
To participate remotely, people need to preregister with the secretary-treasurer at sanderson@niagarafalls.ca or by calling 905-356-7521, ext. 4281 before noon Tuesday.
Marineland was founded in 1961 by John Holer and he operated it until his death in 2018. His widow, Marie, took over the tourist attraction until her death last year.
Marineland's keeping of marine mammals has long been controversial. The park has been the site of numerous animal rights demonstrations over the years, with activists, former employees and marine biologists speaking out against the park.
The park has frequently found itself in court over allegations concerning the conditions and treatment of its animals.
Marineland has long maintained it treats its animals well. Its website said it has "a strong record" of providing for the welfare of its animals and "will continue to prioritize their health and well-being."
The attraction opened for a significantly scaled-back 63rd season late last June. Admission was vastly reduced, with all rides closed and much of the park off limits.
Last week, on its Instagram page, Marineland said on Feb. 5 it had euthanized Eos, a seven-year-old beluga whale plagued by multiple medical conditions.
Eos was the 18th beluga to die in captivity at Marineland since 2019. Three belugas sold to a Connecticut facility four years ago have also died.
Kiska, the last captive killer whale in Canada, died at the Niagara Falls park in March 2023. She had been there since 1979.
Marineland has also found itself in the crosshairs of the provincial election campaign.
This week, Niagara Falls NDP incumbent Wayne Gates said Marineland should be "repurposed," while Ontario's party leaders pledged to help the remaining animals at Marineland.
CAPTION: Marineland wants to divide its massive Niagara Falls property at Portage Road and Stanley Avenue into four parcels of land.Metroland file photo

CREDIT: Ray Spiteri Reporter

Readers write Letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write-letters-editor/docview/3165676682/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write+Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-02-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ford's fiscal follies
Re: Niagara's mayors disappoint, Feb. 4
I totally agree with Faye Suthons' letter regarding Niagara's big three mayors backing PC Leader Doug Ford.
One thing I disagree with is one mayor in particular, Niagara Falls Mayor Jim Diodati, praising Ford's business acumen. How can calling an unnecessary and premature election costing over $150 million be good business sense? Not to mention all Ford's other follies since being elected. He certainly has a great track record of blowing money.
I wish these mayors would keep their political preferences to themselves.
On top of this, Ford seems to be a self-appointed "Captain Canada." Unfortunately, it seems none of his cabinet ministers can speak up for fear of being booted out.
Don McIntee, Niagara Falls
A decades-old issue
Re: St. Catharines proposes to cut down on election sign 'litter,' Jan. 30
What an interesting article, given that decades ago - yes, decades ago - a volunteer community group went before city council to decry the change to the city bylaw allowing election signs on public property.
At that time, only Thorold and St. Catharines had a bylaw that permitted signs only on private property, with the permission of the owner of that property. Nothing on public property was allowed.
Several young councillors of the time argued it would not allow "businesses" to compete in elections. We citizen volunteers won, but had to keep at this for years and years.
Why? Because those persons who wanted to be elected blamed their throwing up signs anywhere at all (MTO property, city public lands) on their "overzealous volunteers." Sure, it was those little volunteers, not the candidates.
I recall this well, being one of the presenters to city council. In subsequent years, council appeared to relax and allowed signs anywhere you like. And we have come to this.
With two elections oncoming, the spread of cluttered signs is most annoying. No one seems to care, until now. This newspaper, to its credit, did cover this issue several times in subsequent elections. I was interviewed for those articles.
Can common sense prevail and people ask that legitimate candidates for elected office please think ethically about the landscape pollution that signs here, there and everywhere are meaningless and offensive?
Gail Benjafield, St. Catharines
Stop U.S. guns
and drugs
Re: Ford urges Ottawa to move
faster to satisfy U.S. border security demands, Feb. 7
While Canada continues to capitulate to all of U.S. President Donald Trump's border demands, why are our politicians not hitting back?
How about we force Trump to stop the flow of guns and drugs into Canada?
Jane Dejong, Welland
No permission
Re: Mayors of Niagara's 3 largest cities endorse Ford, Jan. 31
My Niagara Falls mayor, Jim Diodati, does not have my permission to endorse Doug Ford in the provincial election.
Jo Ann Worrall, Niagara Falls

Document 18 of 249

It's time to close Marineland permanently, MPP Gates says: 'Let's bring in something different that doesn't include animals'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/time-close-marineland-permanently-mpp-gates-says/docview/3165674290/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=It%27s+time+to+close+Marineland+permanently%2C+MPP+Gates+says%3A+%27Let%27s+bring+in+something+different+that+doesn%27t+include+animals%27&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-02-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Marineland should be permanently closed, says Niagara Falls MPP Wayne Gates.
"It's long past. The provincial and federal governments should make sure the animals at Marineland are taken care of, that they're safe, and that they're transported where they have to go. It should be repurposed," said Gates.
He commented on the Niagara Falls attraction Monday during a campaign stop and health-care announcement in Port Colborne with Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles.
Last week, on its Instagram page, Marineland said it had euthanized Eos, a seven-year-old beluga whale, on Feb. 5 after its "hard-fought battle with multiple medical conditions."
The brief statement from Marineland did not specify from what Eos was suffering.
Eos is the 18th beluga to die in captivity at Marineland since 2019. Three belugas sold to a Connecticut facility four years ago have also died.
Kiska, the last captive killer whale in Canada, died at the Niagara Falls park in March 2023. She had been there since 1979.
A reporter asked NDP Leader Marit Stiles if the park on Portage Road should be closed following the latest whale death.
Stiles said she remarked earlier Monday about the animals at Marineland.
"This has been going on far too long, it's outrageous, it's an embarrassment, I think, for Ontario," she said during a campaign stop in Toronto.
"We need to get these mammals out of that, or at least make sure that they're treated, at this point in their lives, humanely," she added.
"We cannot just continue to wait it out till they all die, it's really inhumane."
At the Port Colborne campaign stop, she deferred to Gates.
"It has served its purpose," said Gates. "Let's bring in something different that doesn't include animals. As we know, we've had a lot of our whales die. We've had other animals that have passed there. It's far past the time that they should move on and move on from the Marineland as we know it today."
Other political leaders also weighed in on the attraction Monday.
Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford said Monday the whale deaths were "terrible."
"It'd be great just to let them free, but what I understand, if you let them free in the ocean, they're done, they're dead," Ford said at a campaign stop in Oakville.
"So we're going to make sure that they still take care of these whales."
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said Ford's government failed the animals at the park.
"This is a heartbreaking situation and one that could be resolved with better inspections and better regulation," she said at a stop in Toronto," Crombie said.
And Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner said: "Ontario Greens would ensure that the remaining animals at Marineland are humanely relocated to appropriate accommodations that meet the health and well-being of the animals."
With files from The Canadian Press and John Law
CAPTION: Young visitors watch a beluga whale at Marineland last summer. Last week, the park announced it euthanized a seven-year-old beluga named Eos.marineland file photo

CREDIT: Dave Johnson Reporter With files from The Canadian Press and John Law
