Document 19 of 249

Former student sues Robert Land Academy for $3.5M: Claims include sexual assault and forcible confinement

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/former-student-sues-robert-land-academy-3-5m/docview/3165674073/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Former+student+sues+Robert+Land+Academy+for+%243.5M%3A+Claims+include+sexual+assault+and+forcible+confinement&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-02-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When he came across a trailer for a Netflix documentary featuring a woman haunted by her boarding school experience, it shook him to his core.
"I had to watch it," the man recalled.
"It was the last thing I wanted to watch, but I couldn't not watch it."
The harrowing stories recounted by former students in 2024's "The Program: Cons, Cults and Kidnapping" mirrored what the British Columbia resident said he experienced during his three years at Robert Land Academy on the outskirts of Wellandport in West Lincoln.
"Watching these people on TV gave me the courage to do what they're doing ... to be a voice," he said.
The former student at the Niagara private school recently launched a $3.5-million lawsuit against the institution for what he says was ongoing physical and sexual abuse that went ignored by administration.
Founded in 1978, Robert Land Academy provides a military-style school program for boys in grades 5 through 12 with an emphasis on academic remediation and university admission. Tuition and board is $64,000 per 10-month school term.
The complainant, referred to in court documents as B.C. as his identity is protected under a court-ordered publication ban, is seeking general damages for loss of education, opportunity and income related to physical and sexual assault, battery, forcible confinement and mental distress.
B.C. started at Robert Land Academy in the late 1990s. His parents believed its small class sizes and structured environment would match his learning style.
Instead, according to a statement of claim filed recently in Superior Court of Justice in Welland, he was physically abused by staff, and sexually assaulted by an older student who was his supervisor.
When he reported the sexual abuse to staff, the lawsuit alleges, he was interrogated, gaslit and blamed by school administrators.
According to the statement of claim, the abuse disclosure was not reported to police or his parents, and the student supervisor was not expelled.
"For decades, I have tried to forget the horrible things that happened to me at RLA," B.C. said.
"I felt scared, alone and depressed, and I am hoping to finally get some closure on this traumatic experience."
The complainant's lawyer, Alexi Wood of Toronto's St. Lawrence Barristers PC, said Robert Land Academy needs to be held accountable for failing to protect the students in its care.
"This lawsuit is about ensuring no other child faces abuse," she said.
The claims have not been proven in court, and a statement of defence has yet to be filed.
Wood said 30 lawsuits have been filed against RLA, the majority within the past year. This is the only RLA case her firm is involved with.
In an emailed statement, Robert Land Academy officials said "upon learning of the alleged incident involving one of the school's staff members, we immediately placed the individual on leave."
"Out of respect for the privacy of those involved and a fair and impartial process, we will not provide further comment as the legal investigation remains ongoing," said the statement attributed to headmaster Pete Stock.
"The safety and well-being of our students is our top priority and these alleged incidents that have been reported in the media do not reflect the values of the school, past or present."
Stock said the school is co-operating with an ongoing investigation by Family and Children's Services Niagara.
FACS would not comment on the matter.
"Due to privacy requirements in the Child, Youth and Family Services Act, we are unable to confirm whether FACS Niagara is involved with any specific investigation," said Brett Sweeney, the agency's communications director.
The academy's lawyer, Elizabeth Bowker of Stieber-Berlach LLP in Toronto, said Friday RLA remains steadfast in its commitment to provide a safe environment for its students.
"Robert Land Academy has been serving students and the community for almost half a century and is dedicated to cultivating well-rounded young men through holistic education, with a focus on academic excellence, active living, values-driven principles and positive habits and routines," she said in an emailed statement.
According to RLA's website, programming is geared toward students who struggle in the traditional public school system for a variety of reasons including academics, behaviour, attitude and other problems.
Although not affiliated with the Canadian Armed Forces, the campus and internal structure follow a traditional military model.
The campus itself replicates a 19th-century British fort, that includes military-style barracks where students sleep. Students wear uniforms and are assigned military ranks and titles.
The website said the vast majority of students experience a dramatic improvement in academic performance within the first semester, and 100 per cent of graduates who have applied are accepted into university or college.
Meanwhile, B.C. said he has tried to build a life for himself since his days at the academy, but has struggled with feelings of shame and guilt.
"I just tried to push it down for years with unhealthy ways - issues with drugs and alcohol and depression and suicidal thoughts," he said.
Now, he said, he wants to "slay the dragon."
His relationship with his parents is better these days and he is buoyed by the support he's received from friends and family.
He maintains the lawsuit has "nothing to do with money," rather it's about accountability and raising awareness of childhood trauma.
He hopes by sharing his story he can help others.
"It's time to talk about it ... don't keep it buried inside," he said. "People love you. Get some help if you're struggling."
CAPTION: Robert Land Academy in West Lincoln faces a $3.5M lawsuit.Julie Jocsak St. Catharines Standard

Document 20 of 249

Readers write Letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write-letters-editor/docview/3165306915/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write+Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-02-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Recalibrating
relationships
Re: With threat of a U.S. trade war hanging in the air, Niagara produce is out there, Feb. 3
Ontario and Canada are recalibrating foreign relationships and none too soon. Recent events have highlighted some interesting precedents.
For example, PC Leader Doug Ford's decision to boycott American liquor in the face of U.S. tariffs, since delayed, demonstrates the value of maintaining LCBO as the largest purveyor of alcohol in Ontario. How unlikely is that, when in the past discussions were underway to privatize the service?
Ontario will cancel the Starlink contract should tariffs be imposed. Access to government services is increasingly delivered through the internet. Perhaps Queen's Park needs to review the RFP (request for proposal) to ensure high-speed internet is delivered by a Canadian-based provider and ideally use made in Canada ground stations.
How else could Ontarians be confident any data shared with the government would be secure and not siphoned off as in the takeover of the U.S. federal payment system?
More than ever, it is time to buy local. The Niagara publications provide a welcome service and help residents locate foodstuffs typically thought of as out of season. Buckle up and strap in as some are fond of saying, the next couple of years will undoubtably surprise us all.
Robert Romanuk, Niagara Falls
Effective combo
Re: Trudeau says U.S. tariffs will be paused for 30 days, Feb. 3
The good cop, bad cop combination of Alberta's Danielle Smith and Ontario's Doug Ford brought results.
Smith's diplomacy and Ford's hardball resulted in the one-month delay on Donald Trump's tariffs.
The result is real and new efforts on border immigration and issues from the federal government are overdue.
Fentanyl and illegal immigration are the triggers but all drugs and guns and human trafficking will get more attention.
Even cigarette smuggling will get more. This will improve border security in both directions.
The best part is Trump will wait 30 days to see if Canada keeps the promises Trudeau and the premiers have made. Trump has done us a favour, at least on those issues.
Bruce Timms, St. Catharines
Will mayors be
eating crow?
Re: Mayors of Niagara's 3 largest cities endorse Ford, Jan. 31
The fact these three mayors believe their opinions and endorsements will influence voters is an insult to our intelligence.
By now, I can't believe anything anyone does changes the minds of voters.
Bowing down to kiss the ring of the person who called this expensive and unneeded election is a disturbing trend toward American-style politics.
There is still the possibility if enough people are fed up with the expensive boondoggles, these three mayors will have to eat crow when a new Liberal or NDP government assumes power.
That's why in the past smart mayors shut up and let the voters decide. Opinions are like belly buttons. Everybody has one.
Richard Murri, Niagara Falls

Document 21 of 249

Stiles vows to restore urgent care in south Niagara: MPP Jeff Burch takes shots at Tory rival Bill Steele

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/stiles-vows-restore-urgent-care-south-niagara/docview/3165304557/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Stiles+vows+to+restore+urgent+care+in+south+Niagara%3A+MPP+Jeff+Burch+takes+shots+at+Tory+rival+Bill+Steele&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-02-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Marit Stiles says she will restore urgent-care health services in Port Colborne and Fort Erie and reopen a staffed, full-service hospital in Welland with a 24/7 emergency department if elected premier this month.
The Ontario NDP leader made her campaign pledge Monday at Port Colborne's H.H. Knoll Lakeview Park, with the hospital in the background.
"Health care should never be a privilege of geography. It should never depend on where you live, how far you can drive, or whether the government of the day sees your community as a priority," Stiles said, adding that has happened in Niagara for too long.
"One by one, services have been stripped away. Hospitals have been downgraded. Emergency rooms have been shuttered, and urgent care hours have been slashed."
In front of at least a dozen supporters, some holding signs calling for 24/7 urgent care, Stiles said the changes have caused stress for people who need health care, and it's not what Ontarians deserve.
"Let's call it what it is, a deliberate choice by consecutive Liberal and Conservative governments. Decisions made far away from the people that are right here who rely on these hospitals every single day.
"Doug Ford's Conservatives took things even further. They gutted our after-hours urgent care in Port Colborne and Fort Erie, and they stripped emergency services from Welland. While that was happening, Conservative candidates in Niagara stood by in silence. Well, we didn't, and we will not. We see you. We hear you. I can tell you the Ontario NDP is on your side."
Stiles was joined in Port Colborne by Niagara Centre MPP Jeff Burch, Niagara Falls MPP Wayne Gates, St. Catharines MPP Jennie Stevens and Niagara West candidate Dave Augustyn.
Stiles said the election is about people, not politics.
She said families are struggling to buy expensive groceries while coping with high housing costs. Stiles said getting health care shouldn't mean long drives, more gas, taking time off work and paying for parking for families already stretched thin.
Asked about a proposal that would see space at the hospital used as a primary care clinic for Lockview Medical Group through a leasing arrangement with Niagara Health, Stiles deferred to Burch, whose riding takes in Port Colborne, Welland, Thorold and south St. Catharines.
Burch said urgent and primary care services are needed in the community.
"Some time ago, a proposal came forward that the PC candidate (Port Colborne Mayor Bill Steele) running against me approved of, including the closing of this urgent-care centre permanently in 2028," Burch said.
"Yes, they're talking about bringing a primary health care team in, but as I said when I started, this shouldn't be a choice between an urgent care centre and family doctors. That's a false choice; it's not either-or. People need both in this community."
The plan to close urgent care is part of Niagara Health's broader strategy to transition to a three-hospital system across the region, with the new south Niagara Falls hospital to open in 2028.
The new hospital is expected to replace urgent-care services. However, the plan has sparked debate over accessibility and travel times for residents, particularly those in Port Colborne and surrounding areas.
Steele on Monday said no one should question his commitment to local health care.
"Nobody has worked harder for health than I have. My wife has been a nurse for 37 years and has worked in the urgent care," he said.
"The three centres of excellence is a good plan. We've heard from doctors and retired surgeons in favour of what Niagara Health is putting in place. It's the way to go."
The primary health care team proposed by Lockview Medical Group is one way to keep people out of urgent-care centres and emergency departments, by offering more primary care and access to other health specialists, said Steele.
He said Lockview Medical Group plans to have a walk-in clinic and become an urgent-care centre, offering more than is now at the Sugarloaf Street site.
Steele said it took an election being called for Burch to come out and speak about the proposed plan in Port Colborne.
When talking about health care, Stiles had St. Catharines resident Debbie McGregor share her personal experience with the health-care system.
McGregor recounted her time seeking care for her mother, who was undergoing treatment for stomach cancer in 2017 and the subsequent challenges.
"We spent 300 days travelling back and forth to Hamilton, spending money on gas and taking time off work, and then we can't forget these parking ticket receipts. My daughter and I figured we have already spent at least $7,500 in parking," she said.
"Access to health care should never depend on how far you can drive," she said.
CAPTION: Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles said she will restore urgent-care health services in Port Colborne and Fort Erie during a stop in Niagara Monday.MPP Jennie Stevens, left, and Niagara West candidate Dave Augustyn, right, listen as Niagara Centre MPP Jeff Burch talks about health care alongside and NDP Leader Marit Stiles.Dave Johnson Welland Tribune

Document 22 of 249

Readers write Letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write-letters-editor/docview/3164932973/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write+Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-02-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Precarious positions
Re: Mayors of Niagara's 3 largest cities endorse Ford, Jan. 31
I was disappointed to see the mayors endorse PC Leader Doug Ford. Two of the two largest industries in Niagara - education and health - have been decimated by his policies.
Health care is on the radar of most Ontarians and many have seen the impact of his policies on school boards, but it seems the public is not as aware to concerns about the post-secondary system.
Two institutions employ 1,000s of Niagarans. Brock University's contribution to the vitality of the region is nearly half a billion dollars and that does not include the community partnerships it supports.
Both Niagara College and Brock are in precarious economic positions, not because the federal government restricted international study visas but because the PCs have choked funding since they came into power.
Brock is additionally threatened by the decision of the PCs to license the for-profit Niagara Falls University.
To side with Ford is a myopic and ridiculous decision when his government is bleeding dry our region's major industries and threatening our future.
Dan Malleck, St. Catharines
Votes matter
Re: Secord takes Region to task over development charges, Jan. 27

A majority of Niagara regional councillors recently voted in a "tribunal exercising quasi-judicial powers" to change the classification of two buildings from commercial to industrial. This means the lessor and developer do not pay charges for infrastructure.
You and I pay it instead. Or we will, as part of our next tax bill.
This is similar to last year's decision to bill taxpayers for development costs for high-end homes (also affordable homes, which is more acceptable). Last year's decision contributed to the nine per cent plus tax increase we face this year.
The year before it was a 14 per cent increase and taxpayers were responsible to pay for development fees for all types of new housing.
Some councillors opposed passing on development costs to taxpayers but not enough of them. In the current case, St. Catharines city Coun. Peter Secord subsequently requested some changes. Unfortunately he cannot vote on regional matters.
On the positive side, taxpayers are able to vote for councillors in the next Niagara regional council election. Please vote for those who oppose the transfer of these development fees to you and me.
Joan Worthington, St. Catharines
PM Carney?
Re: Mayors of Niagara's 3 largest cities endorse Ford, Jan. 31
Fine. I'm willing to sacrifice my province for my country.
If putting Doug Ford back in power with a minority (sorry Dougie, but that's what it should be) means history holds true, our country will be saved by a strong Liberal government in Ottawa as they take on the madman south of the border.
Prime Minister Mark Carney has a nice ring to it, don't you think? Go Canada, buy Canadian!
Janice Parker-Elcich, Welland

Document 23 of 249

Ford pledges $35 million for Shaw Festival theatre during second Niagara campaign stop: Tories promise to invest $50M to purchase H-135 helicopters to detect more illegal activity at border crossings

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-pledges-35-million-shaw-festival-theatre/docview/3164932463/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+pledges+%2435+million+for+Shaw+Festival+theatre+during+second+Niagara+campaign+stop%3A+Tories+promise+to+invest+%2450M+to+purchase+H-135+helicopters+to+detect+more+illegal+activity+at+border+crossings&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-02-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario government is investing $35 million into Shaw Festival's Royal George Theatre, Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford announced Saturday during his second Niagara stop in as many weeks during the provincial election campaign.
"I was just told we just gave $35 million for a theatre and that's going to be continuing investments into heritage," he said during a stop at Niagara District Airport in Niagara-on-the-Lake.
Ford made the announcement when asked how the province's Destination Niagara vision will impact Niagara-on-the-Lake and what his party's plan is to preserve heritage.
"I think we always have to protect heritage," he said, pointing to the theatre announcement.
Last year, Shaw Festival revealed that unless funding for major upgrades could be secured, the 2025 season would be the final one for the deteriorating 109-year-old Royal George Theatre.
Niagara-on-the-Lake Lord Mayor Gary Zalepa, who was at Ford's campaign stop, welcomed the announcement, adding it's something officials have been asking for.
"The Royal George is scheduled to close because of structural problems with the theatre," he said.
"That being closed on our main street would be a massive blow to our tourism area and our Queen Street. We've been waiting to hear something positive about that. It's very exciting to hear that."
In an interview, Niagara Falls NDP incumbent candidate Wayne Gates said he's been fighting for provincial funding for Royal George Theatre for more than a year.
He delivered a letter to - and spoke on a number of occasions with - then minister of tourism, culture and sport highlighting how important the project is for Niagara-on-the-Lake and how residents and donors had come together to raise millions of dollars towards the rebuilding effort.
"It's another example ... why it's important to have good MPPs that will take on important issues locally," said Gates.
He said he hopes the funding will come to fruition and it's not just a campaign promise as Ford made the comments in response to a question and not through a formal announcement.
"I firmly believe that the money probably should have come to the Shaw and Niagara-on-the-Lake months ago. I can't speak for Premier Ford and why he decided to say that - sometimes when he goes off the top of his head, these type of things get said," he said, noting Shaw officials were surprised by Ford's comments.
"But it's something that we've been working on for a long time."
Ford also detailed his party's plan to secure Ontario's borders, including new helicopters and additional technology to detect, disrupt and deter illegal cross-border activity.
These measures build on the province's Operation Deterrence initiative, launched earlier this year in response to U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff threat against Canada and Mexico unless those two countries did more to prevent fentanyl and illegal crossings into America.
Ford announced that a re-elected PC government will invest $50 million to expand Ontario Provincial Police's Joint-Air Support Unit, with two additional H-135 helicopters to support Niagara Regional Police and Windsor Police Service with increased patrols, security and enforcement at key border entry points.
Niagara is home to four international bridge crossings with the U.S.
"This gives them the tools they need to enhance Operation Deterrence and increase patrols and enforcement at two key border crossings, so we can detect more illegal activity and put more criminals behind bars," said Ford.
The Joint-Air Support Unit was created by the PC government through a $134- million investment in last year's budget, with new police helicopters to help police in Ottawa, Toronto, Durham, Halton and Peel fight auto theft and combat organized crime.
Ford said since Operation Deterrence was launched, 200 dedicated OPP officers have conducted 10,000 hours of "focused patrol to disrupt and deter illegal cross-border activity."
"We're already seeing real, tangible results. Operation Deterrence has seized 21 illegal firearms in the short period already and 624 kilograms of cocaine. It has recovered seven stolen vehicles and intercepted eight illegal border crossers," he said.
"In total, over 566 charges have been laid - each one making Ontario and U.S. communities safer and more secure. This is welcome news and it's exactly the kind of action our American allies need to see."
Despite that, Ford said there's more Ontario can do to protect borders and communities.
Aside from the additional provincial commitments, Ford urged the federal government to introduce "strict and severe" mandatory minimum sentences for criminals convicted of drug trafficking.
Ford said a re-elected PC government will ensure landlords are "held liable for knowingly housing illicit drug production and trafficking sites."
"Ontario will work with the federal government to ensure the RCMP, regional and local law enforcement have the resources they need to address money laundering and other financial crimes by cartels and gangs," he said.
Ford also urged the federal government to increase inspections of all point-of-origin containers at shipping yards, ports, rail yards and airports from China, which he said is the frequent source of fentanyl and other illicit drugs "plaguing Canadian and American communities."
Last Friday, Ford visited the Walker Construction building in Niagara Falls, where he announced a re-elected PC government will widen the Queen Elizabeth Way between Burlington and St. Catharines to deal with congestion.
CAPTION: Ontario Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford made his second visit to Niagara in as many weeks during the provincial election campaign Saturday, stopping at Niagara District Airport in Niagara-on-the-Lake.Ray Spiteri Niagara Falls Review

CREDIT: Ray Spiteri Reporter

Readers write letters To the editor

Full text: Mayors miss
the mark
Re: Mayors of Niagara's 3 largest cities endorse Ford, Jan. 31
It would be helpful if the three Niagara mayors would focus on policy, rather than personalities, when commenting on provincial politics.
What they should have been addressing in the context of the provincial election is the issue of homelessness, a curse which seems to have no light at the end of the tunnel. Many mayors hope to use the infamous notwithstanding clause of the Charter on this, and it just moves the homeless around.
Virtually no rent-geared-to-income housing has been built in Ontario since new projects were abruptly terminated by the newly minted government of former premier Mike Harris in 1995.
This is the issue our mayors need to draw attention to in this election, which should include uploading the costs of social housing from municipalities to the provincial government.
John Bacher, St. Catharines
A proud
Canadian
Re: Trump postpones threatened tariffs for 30 days, Feb. 3
I was born and raised in Canada, both my parents were born here.
I am Canadian, and I do not care if Donald Trump thinks we all want to be American. He can go you-know-where. We all need to stand up and let each and every one of our politicians know how we feel.
Gage O. Norton, Welland
Election
not needed
Re: Niagara's mayors
disappoint, Letters, Feb. 4
Niagara's three mayors are playing directly into the provincial Progressive Conservatives' hands - that is, keeping the status quo.
Doug Ford already has a majority government. He insults our intelligence by saying he needs a mandate to take on Donald Trump's tariffs. All the other provincial premiers are doing so without calling new elections.
This election is not necessary. Do your job, Doug.
Giving the Ford government an even bigger majority will let him run roughshod over any remaining safeguards for the environment.
It will allow him to drastically enlarge his agenda, which is to satisfy his rich friends, continue starving out health care and education, and pave over the Greenbelt.
Barbara Chambers,
St. Catharines
Mayors' move
disappointing
Re: 'Strong' mayors lose,
Letters, Feb. 5
Although I'm not at all surprised about these mayors backing Ford, I must say it's deeply disappointing to see them do so.
I always understood mayors were supposed to be non-partisan. Perhaps I was wrong.
Clearly the call for change in this country isn't happening, or at least it's not expected from these three.
Catherine Livingston,
St. Catharines

Document 25 of 249

Councillors feel heat over Trump celebration photos

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/councillors-feel-heat-over-trump-celebration/docview/3164582911/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Councillors+feel+heat+over+Trump+celebration+photos&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-02-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: While regional councillors are mulling Thursday's announcement of the Region's four-point plan to help Niagara businesses deal with U.S. President Donald Trump's threatened tariffs, two of its own were in a more celebratory mood on U.S. election night.
St. Catharines regional Coun. Sal Sorrento, along with Peter Secord, a fellow St. Catharines city councillor, posed for photographs while grinning in a group shot at a St. Catharines bar during what appears to be a celebration of Donald Trump's victory on Nov. 5. The photo, which was posted on Twitter, now called X, was captioned, "We did it!!!! The Ultimate Group Shot!!!!"
Sorrento is on leave from council to run in the Feb. 27 provincial election for the Progressive Conservative Party in St. Catharines.
In an emailed statement from the Ontario Progressive Conservative Party, Sorrento didn't clarify what he was doing at the gathering.
He said the event was not a Trump victory party, and denied he was a Trump fan.
"I'm not a supporter of Trump, so I will work tirelessly to protect St. Catharines' families and workers and always put our community, and Ontario, first," Sorrento's statement read.
Secord said residents shouldn't assume he supports the U.S. president because he happened to appear in a celebratory photo on U.S. election night.
Secord said he stopped at the bar to watch the election coverage with a group of Conservative supporters.
"I was in the middle of the nomination campaign for the local riding at the time and Conservative/ Republican views are similar, as you know," said Secord, who previously served on St. Catharines city council and ran for mayor in 2014.
"As for the Region and its plans, these are all reactionary. As a business owner that imports U.S. goods, I fail to see how these measures will help a lot of others, but at least we are taking a stand."
On Monday in Etobicoke, a hot mic caught Sorrento's party leader, Premier Doug Ford, saying he wanted Donald Trump to win the U.S. presidential election.
"On election day, was I happy this guy won?" Ford said. "One hundred per cent I was. Then the guy pulled out the knife and f - - - ing yanked it in me."
A day later, Ford walked the comments back, telling reporters: "Well, you know something, I always believe in working with anyone.
"On the hot mic, yeah, he makes me swear. I think he makes a lot of people swear in this country," Ford said while insisting he believed Trump would somehow be less provocative in his second term.
At Thursday's council meeting, Niagara Region's chief administrative officer, Ron Tripp, outlined the local upper-tier government's response to possible tariffs.
Tripp said the Region is committed to ensuring Niagara remains resilient regardless of Trump's final decision on a trade war that would place a 25 per cent tariff on Canadian goods and devastate the economy.
Tripp said the top priority is protecting the organization, residents, businesses, employees and community partners relying on regional services and programs. The four-point plan includes:
Finding opportunities to reduce the Region's exposure to potential tariffs and additional costs.
Supporting local businesses with resources from Niagara Economic Development.
A public "Buy Local" campaign with municipal partners that promotes Niagara businesses.
Additional government relations efforts on the Niagara-U.S. partnership.
Tripp said in 2023, 72 per cent of Niagara's total exports went to the U.S. while more than 54 per cent of total imports came from south of the border.
"We realize that this is a significant number and poses a very real risk to our local businesses," Tripp said. "We also recognize that despite these circumstances, the U.S. is our neighbour and economic ally. The reality is that many Niagara businesses and communities, including the Niagara Region, have friends, family and employees that have a close relationship with our U.S. neighbours."
Asked about Sorrento and Secord, Regional Chair Jim Bradley said each member of council "is responsible for his or her own actions in public and can answer for those actions."
One of Sorrento's political rivals in the St. Catharines riding, New Democrat and two-term incumbent Jennie Stevens, said she wasn't surprised to see the Sorrento post, which has been making the rounds on social media.
"His support of Trump is in line with Doug Ford's hot mic moment," Stevens said. "He called this election under the guise that he needed to scare us, yet he and some of his own candidates openly have supported Trump's presidency. It's behaviour that should maybe make voters pause and ask, 'Is this the person of integrity that we want and trust to lead our community?' It's clear that there's a conflict of interest there.
"In 2024, for a Canadian politician to support someone who is ready to destroy our job market and destroy our small businesses, not only here in Niagara, but across Ontario, is a very shameful message. It really is."
CAPTION: Niagara regional councillors Sal Sorrento and Peter Secord, second and third from left, pose for a photo at a party in a St. Catharines bar on the November night Donald Trump was elected U.S. president.Regional Coun. Sal Sorrento, second from left, poses for a photo at a party on U.S. election night in a St. Catharines bar.Jeff Tyler Smith X photos

Document 26 of 249

Readers write

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write/docview/3164229600/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-02-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: CBC needed more than ever
Re: CBC programming used to be adventurous. Why has it bowed to lazy ideas and copycat shows?, Feb. 1
John Semley's critical take on the CBC seems to overlook the purpose of this long-standing Canadian institution. Mr. Semley takes glee in bashing "Family Feud Canada," apparently for its lack of "weirdness," a quality he seems to think would make the broadcaster somehow more vital.
Let us recall the purpose of the CBC: an institution founded in 1936 to thwart the drowning of a young Canada, with its mere population of 11 million, by a juggernaut America already dwarfing our young nation with an abundance of radio stations and ready to swallow an easily targeted market.
Today, the CBC works diligently to take on a universe awash with thousands of broadcasts in multiple platforms to, again, ensure that as the eyes, ears and voice of a nation some semblance of national identity is sustained. How more critical than ever is this task given the ever more belligerent intentions of our U.S. neighbour. That talk of defunding can even be contemplated at this time borders on treasonous.
Appealing to the "Dougs and Peggy-Sues of Saskatoon" may be beneath the refined tastes of Mr. Semley, but like any viable network, getting eyeballs in front of screens is the lifeblood of the business, even for a taxpayer-funded enterprise.
"Family Feud Canada" may be a mundane formula, but it is ready-made for Canadian humour and the amazing diversity of its far-flung population. Families from Tofino to Come-by-Chance face off in an arena managed by an authentically Canadian Gerry Dee. This is us, and is exactly what we need in a humourless, unfriendly world.
John Brucato, Niagara Falls
Mayors right
to support Ford
Re: Niagara's mayors
disappoint, Letters, Feb. 4
I support the mayors of Niagara's three biggest cities endorsing Doug Ford and the PC Party of Ontario. The reason is obvious. The PCs have become the party of the working class, building Ontario's economy and supporting good-paying jobs.
The same can be said for the federal Conservative party under Pierre Poilievre.
The Liberal and NDP parties at the provincial and federal level are obsessed with identity politics, taxing us to death and far left ideology instead of issues important to us like jobs, the economy, public safety and affordability.
If you don't agree then why have several big workers' unions endorsed Ford's PC party? They know the PC party will continue to support large infrastructure projects like highways and bridges, ink EV battery deals and fight U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff threats to protect Ontario workers.
No leader is perfect and I don't like the timing of this election. However, because I want to continue to build a strong Ontario economy, I support the PCs. Many workers' unions agree.
Brook Blanchfield,
Niagara Falls

Full text: Niagara's 'strong'
mayors are losers
Re: Mayors of Niagara's 3 largest cities endorse Ford, Jan. 31
Everyone knows Doug Ford didn't need a mandate to lead the fight against Donald Trump.
You know it, he knows it, even our local mayors know it.
He has a mandate. His party had a majority in the legislature.
There were no threats to the Ford Conservatives' power. They had it made.
No, Doug Ford didn't need a mandate, he just needed an excuse.
He went from being a strong voice against the guy in the White House to just another greedy politician.
No Captain Canada, just "What's in it for me?"
And yet there they are, our local mayors, shoulder to shoulder with Ford on the newspaper's front page.
What a sham.
These are the same guys who were happy to become "strong" mayors, thanks to Ford, happy to bypass their elected councils and make decisions all on their own.
And now it's time for them to pay the piper. So there they are, endorsing the premier and his phoney excuse for an election.
Hey, fellas, you know what you look like? You don't look like strong mayors, ready to stand up for your constituents, you look like a bunch of losers.
Ernie Armstrong, St. Catharines
Paving it over
Re: Doug Ford announces plan to widen QEW between Burlington and St. Catharines, Feb 1
Doug (Pave-it-all-and-they-will-come) Ford seems hell bent on plowing under and paving over as much farmland and environmentally sensitive hectarage in the province as humanly possible.
He has delusions of grandeur and fancies creating a legacy for himself with an unwanted bypass, the exorbitantly expensive twinning of a skyway, the unnecessary Highway 413, removing bicycle lanes and requiring provincial pre-approval for new ones and his attempted sleight of hand by suggesting the tunnelling beneath a highway.
Truly, if even a portion of his ideas come to fruition the level of environmental degradation will be on a scale we have not seen in generations, if ever.
This hyperfocus on "development" will create unprecedented pressure on our already faltering health-care system.
His true legacy will be an increasingly unlivable Ontario with macadam to the horizon, clouded in exhaust fumes and devoid of the scientifically proven health benefits of nature. It's time for leadership that focuses on creating sustainable growth in harmony with maintaining, better yet, enhancing livable environs.
Roger Demers, Welland
Polling discounted
Re: Ford's opponents offer more than he'll admit as he counts on voters to sleepwalk to the polls, Jan. 31
I read an article in the newspaper where a poll was mentioned.
I've been of voting age for almost 60 years and don't recall ever being contacted for an actual poll. I guess maybe you need to support a specific political party.
I simply vote for the candidate I feel will do the best job.
Don McIntee, Niagara Falls

Document 28 of 249

Readers write Letters To The Editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write-letters-editor/docview/3163148968/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write+Letters+To+The+Editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-02-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Election comes with a price
With everything that is going on in our province now and what could come soon, we are spending about $190 million on the whim of our premier for an early election.
Yes, we would have had to spend this in 18 months, but right now it is only a political move.
The last time a premier did this it backfired. I hope it backfires this time.
That money, or part of it, could have gone toward health care, the homeless, child care and other areas that would help the people right now.
This is a political move that shows a total disrespect for the people of Ontario.
John Hunt, Niagara Falls
Niagara's mayors disappoint
Welland Mayor Frank Campion, Niagara Falls Mayor Jim Diodati and St. Catharines Mayor Mat Siscoe have announced their combined support for Premier Doug Ford.
This I find is deeply disturbing because they all have power and that can translate into the power to persuade.
It's especially upsetting when you realize the track record of Ford, who is still being investigated by the RCMP regarding the Greenbelt, and what he has done to bring down our precious health care. Remember his promise of no more hallway medicine?
Think about it - why should these three politicians be able to do this?
Faye Suthons, Wainfleet
Time to step up on affordable
housing
Recent articles in the St. Catharines Standard triggered by the debate over the possible use of the Canadian Charter's notwithstanding clause against homeless encampments have sparked a helpful discussion on this stain on our society. A recent article identified the problem of the limited supply of rent-geared-to-income accommodation, but put too much blame on the slowness of people moving out of this protected shelter. The big problem with RGI housing, and the core of the homelessness disaster, is the fact so little of it has been built since 1995. Then it was axed by the Ontario government under premier Mike Harris, with substantial penalties being paid to providers who had signed contracts to build 17,000 more RGI shelter units. While RGI accommodation is still in deep freeze in Niagara, the City of Toronto has taken the lead in reviving this needed housing, taking advantage of newly minted federal programs. One big project is under construction at the former Honest Ed's retail complex. It benefitted from a special $20-million grant from the federal government. Currently, advocates of more RGI housing have their efforts focused on a City of St. Catharines-owned property on Geneva Street. This is a good idea, but construction would get speeded up if similar help was made by the federal and provincial governments, as what was achieved in the Honest Ed's redevelopment.
John Bacher, St. Catharines

Document 29 of 249

City must consult Indigenous communities in encampment talks, Vanderklis says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/city-must-consult-indigenous-communities/docview/3162729388/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=City+must+consult+Indigenous+communities+in+encampment+talks%2C+Vanderklis+says&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A lack of St. Catharines leadership consultation with Indigenous communities pertaining to potential use of the notwithstanding clause to clear encampments is disappointing, says Sean Vanderklis.
The Niagara Regional Native Centre (NRNC) president said the city did not discuss with it a move he said could disproportionately harm unhoused Indigenous people, who make up about 30 per cent of St. Catharines' homeless population.
Vanderklis said a memorandum of understanding between St. Catharines and the Indigenous community signed in 2018 by then-mayor Walter Sendzik requires co-operation from the city to work with Indigenous people on matters identified in the Truth and Reconciliation calls to action.
He said under the MOU there is at bare minimum a duty to consult with Indigenous people, government and organizations.
"Part of the concern we have is oftentimes unless things are explicitly about Indigenous people, we're often overlooked."
St. Catharines Mayor Mat Siscoe is one of 13 Ontario Big City Mayors who called on the Doug Ford government to use the notwithstanding clause to override a previous court decision blocking municipalities from removing encampments if no shelter spaces exist for the people displaced.
Siscoe, in an interview, said the city's intentions by using the notwithstanding clause is not to tear down all encampments, but rather to remove only those near schools, parks and people's backyards.
"I was elected by the Indigenous community and by the community at large ... for my judgment on these issues and to have to stop every time there's a decision to be made and double back to all the communities that might want to be consulted, I don't think that's a reasonable way to try and do things," he said.
"We've been straightforward from the beginning. We need to be able to regulate public use of land, and we need to be able to put regulations in place to where and when encampments can go," he said. "That's not an issue that's community-specific; that affects the entirety of St. Catharines.
"We don't launch into consultations with individual groups on issues that have ramifications for the entirety of the city."
Siscoe said the city is working to strengthen the MOU with NRNC, as the two parties are set to update the document.
"The truth is, I've had a chance to chat with the folks at the native centre over the last couple of months, and this isn't an issue they've raised with me," he said.
Vanderklis said Indigenous people are considered sovereign, and a lack of consultation on matters like the notwithstanding clause has damaging effects.
"We do have Indigenous people there who do not identify as citizens, they might identify as residents but, ultimately, they are sovereign per their Indigenous background," he said.
During a recent regional council meeting, politicians, including Siscoe, heard from several Niagarans upset at councillors for considering the use of the notwithstanding clause to remove encampments.
Councillors, ultimately, moved a motion that Niagara Region is "opposed" to the use of the notwithstanding clause, affirming its support for Section 7 of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms.
Rin Simon, a St. Catharines resident, echoed Vanderklis's concerns when they addressed councillors, criticizing them for a lack of understanding of Indigenous people's inherent rights.
"Indigenous people do not seek rights through a Canadian constitution, through Section 35, that's not where our rights come from," Simon said.
"The Canadian government, the federal government, the provincial government, the municipality or the Crown do not get to determine what our rights are. Our inherent rights trump your constitutional rights, if I, as an Indigenous person on my own land, wish to go and set up an encampment ... anywhere in my territory, that is fully within my rights, and you have no recourse to prevent me from doing so."
Vanderklis said through an inability to consult Indigenous communities, governments continue to reinforce their inability to understand, recognize or include Indigenous people in matters affecting them.
"There is a natural distrust among Indigenous people with levels of government ... we have historically not been listened to, agreements we've signed have not been honoured, but us at the Niagara Regional Native Centre are attempting to move past that," he said.
"We're attempting to build these relationships and do the heavy work now, so future generations don't have to do it every time we're overlooked, every time we're not considered."
With files from Bill Sawchuk
CAPTION: Indigenous people make up about 30 per cent of St. Catharines' homeless population.Niagara Regional Native Centre president Sean Vanderklis.Julie Jocsak St. Catharines Standard

CREDIT: Matthew P. Barker Reporter With files from Bill Sawchuk

Document 30 of 249

Niagara's big 3 mayors back Ford: 'It's not a good time to experiment,' Diodati says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/niagaras-big-3-mayors-back-ford/docview/3162729274/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Niagara%27s+big+3+mayors+back+Ford%3A+%27It%27s+not+a+good+time+to+experiment%2C%27+Diodati+says&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford has scored a hat trick in Niagara.
Mayors in the region's three most populous cities - St. Catharines, Niagara Falls and Welland - are publicly endorsing the Progressive Conservative leader in the Feb. 27 election.
Niagara Falls Mayor Jim Diodati, who said last week he was weighing his options, also backed Ford in the 2022 provincial election.
"(Ford) showed his leadership abilities during COVID, an unprecedented time in our history, and I thought his leadership was outstanding," Diodati said in an interview Friday morning. "With what's going on south of the border, the tariff threats and the kind of mercurial president that we're dealing with right now, this premier has got solid business background, he's got extensive American business experience running his own businesses.
"It's not a good time to experiment with someone who hasn't been tried during stressful times. We need a steady hand at the helm to take us into the turbulent waters that are waiting."
During a Ford campaign stop in Niagara Falls Friday afternoon, St. Catharines Mayor Mat Siscoe said his decision to endorse Ford was "relatively simple."
"I've been in office now for two years and over that time we've identified areas where we need help from the province and the reality is Premier Ford and his government have consistently delivered," he said.
For example, Siscoe said "we identified chronic underfunding in the homelessness prevention dollars from the province going back decades. Premier Ford raised that funding by over 80 per cent."
He said in St. Catharines alone, almost 9,000 jobs are dependent on exports to the U.S.
"The possibility of a trade war (with the United States) jeopardizes that partnership and the premier, on this file, has led the charge not just for St. Catharines, not just for Niagara and Ontario, but for the country of Canada and has clearly demonstrated his commitment to standing up for our workers."
Frank Campion said as Welland mayor, the PC government under Ford has been "fabulous to work with."
"They respond and I think that's the key element - they actually answer the phone and get back to you as soon as they can," he said. "They're investing wisely - they're not just throwing money around, they're actually dedicating money to areas that really need them."
Siscoe and Campion were PC candidates in the St. Catharines and then-Welland ridings, respectively, during the 2014 provincial election. Both finished second.
Diodati told The Review he received some blowback for supporting Ford in 2022 - but for the mayor, it's not about the political party but who he thinks would be best for his city.
"I don't take endorsements lightly," he said.
"I base it on the leader and the times. Each time is different and each time is unique and it's not partisan."
Diodati said Ford is "extremely accessible, he listens and he's responsive with action."
"He always takes my call or he always gets back to me if he's not available," said Diodati. "He called me at Christmastime to say, 'Hey, mayor, I hope you can help me.' I said, 'Yeah, what do you need, premier?' "
Diodati said Ford told him a Niagara Falls resident had called him about a rat problem in his townhouse.
"I said, how did this come to light? He said, 'He texted me and then I phoned him and he explained what was going on and I told him I'm going to reach out to the mayor.' These are the kind of stories nobody knows about because that's the kind of guy he is. He doesn't do it for some kind of publicity stunt. He actually calls people back."
Ford thanked the mayors for their endorsement, calling them "three incredible champions for this region."
"They believe in growing the region, building, creating more homes, creating more jobs, more opportunities for families right across this region and I'm blessed to be able to work with them day in and day out."
Ford began his Friday in Hamilton to meet with health-care workers represented by the Service Employees International Union. He then travelled to St. Catharines to meet with workers at Steelcon and Heddle Shipyards. In Niagara Falls, he met with workers at the Walker Construction building on Brown Road. He ended the day meeting with workers from the International Brotherhood of Electrical Workers Construction Council of Ontario.
CAPTION: Niagara Falls Mayor Jim Diodati, left, St. Catharines Mayor Mat Siscoe, middle, and Welland Mayor Frank Campion, right, have all endorsed Ontario Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford, front. Niagara Falls Mayor Jim Diodati, left, supports Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford because he is "extremely accessible, he listens and he's responsive with action."Bob Tymczyszyn St. Catharines StandardBob Tymczyszyn St. Catharines Standard

Document 31 of 249

Ford announces proposal to widen QEW in Niagara: Premier says work on highway would employ thousands in region

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-announces-proposal-widen-qew-niagara/docview/3162112151/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+announces+proposal+to+widen+QEW+in+Niagara%3A+Premier+says+work+on+highway+would+employ+thousands+in+region&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford says a re-elected Progressive Conservative government will widen the Queen Elizabeth Way between Burlington and St. Catharines.
During a campaign stop in Niagara Falls Friday, Ontario's premier announced he would add $22 billion to his already $200-billion plan to build more infrastructure across the province.
Ford was joined at the Walker Construction building on Brown Road by Niagara Falls Mayor Jim Diodati, St. Catharines Mayor Mat Siscoe and Welland Mayor Frank Campion, who all endorsed another Ford term.
The premier was also joined by his four Niagara PC candidates - Ruth-Ann Niewesteeg of Niagara Falls, Sal Sorrento of St. Catharines, Bill Steele of Niagara Centre and incumbent Sam Oosterhoff of Niagara West.
"From Day 1, our government has been hard at work on the largest, most ambitious plan to build in Canadian history," said Ford.
"Whether it's the largest expansion of public transit in North America, getting to work on Highway 413, building the Bradford Bypass or expanding the Garden City Skyway right here in the Niagara region."
He said a re-elected PC government will "ramp up our plan to build."
"We're not only going to invest more, we're going to invest faster. We'll prioritize shovel-ready projects that can get people to work quickly. Here in Niagara, that includes widening the QEW between Burlington and St. Catharines."
He described the project as a "big one" that will "keep thousands of local construction workers and skilled trades people working and help drivers get out of gridlock."
"These are important investments in infrastructure - just as important, they're important investments in jobs. They're paycheques, they're confidence and they're peace of mind for tens of thousands of families."
Ford was asked about the province's destination strategy for Niagara, which could include changing a deal dictating how Niagara Falls casinos are run and potentially encourage more casinos to open in the city.
"Maybe," he said, when asked if a re-elected PC government would consider new Niagara casinos.
Ford elaborated more, however, about the overall strategy.
"We want to create a destination, not just one spot, but the whole region," he said. "The wine sector, the wine tours are second to none. I had some friends call me up in the summer and say, 'Boy, these wine tours are incredible, they're packed.'"
Ford said it's important to look at other entertainment options in Niagara, not just Niagara Falls.
"We're going to change this whole region with the support of the local mayors and, hopefully, the federal government. We're going to have an opportunity to create a destination and create the environment for companies to come here and invest and thrive and prosper and grow."
Ford invited Diodati to expand on the strategy, which the mayor said doesn't just focus on gaming.
"They're going to use gaming as one offering on the buffet to expand tourism, to leverage tourism," said Diodati.
"Unfortunately, in Niagara Falls, we've got stagnant. We haven't had a new hotel room in almost 17 years, our numbers have stayed the same for a long time and places like Nashville have been more innovative and they've done a better job at marketing and promoting."
He said the Destination Niagara strategy looks at the region's wineries and theatres, expansion options for Niagara District Airport, expanding GO train frequency between Niagara and Toronto, a visitor transportation system to connect GO to the tourism core, and expanding highway infrastructure.
"As it is now, we're the largest population in Canada without regular, daily scheduled flights," said Diodati about the airport in Niagara-on-the-Lake, which is owned by the town along with the cities of Niagara Falls and St. Catharines.
"They're going to take a macro approach to everything. The whole idea is the high tide will bring up the boats. We're going to shine up that gem and once again, Niagara Falls will sparkle."
CAPTION: Doug Ford, in Niagara Falls Friday, announces a plan widen the Queen Elizabeth Way between Burlington and St. Catharines.Bob Tymczyszyn St. Catharines Standard

Document 32 of 249

Readers write letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write-letters-editor/docview/3162111619/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A grocery list
of Doug Ford's mistakes
Doug Ford is handing us the golden opportunity to boot him and his cabinet out of office. We have ample reason to do so.
He has screwed up health care by trying to privatize it; tried to sell the Greenbelt to developers who are creating "un"affordable housing; closed supervised drug sites; closed driver's licence offices; cancelled licence plate fees that could have provided millions of dollars for health care; arbitrarily closed Ontario Science Centre, I am certain with an eye to helping his developer buddies build yet another megatower on the site.
He also instituted "strong mayor" powers which negate powers of the local councils we, the public, elect and goes against the very basic right of individuals in a democratic society; and has desecrated Ontario Place and Toronto Islands with landfill to create more mega-space for his luxury spa, which is foreign-owned and unaffordable by working families trying to exist.
And, Ford is also planning on twinning the Garden City Skyway; building a tunnel under Toronto; creating another mega-highway through delicate Greenbelt and specific-crop farmland north of Toronto when the objective of trying to save the planet from warming is to remove vehicles.
He also removed bicycle lanes in critical areas of Toronto's downtown, and decreed that the province has to give approval for installing new bike lanes, which raises a question about areas of significant tourist traffic such as Niagara and its promotion of the wine route, which is attractive for cycling.
Ford also legislated wine and beer in corner stores and grocery stores, and broke a contract with a multi-million-dollar penalty, when it would have expired next year anyway.
Now he thinks he's our saviour by loudly bulldozing his way to U.S. President Donald Trump regarding the tariff situation between Canada and the U.S.A.
Enough, already. If we give him a further four-year mandate, that is precisely four years too much.
If you have any money left, put it where your mouth is - get out and vote.
Helen Zimmerman,
Vineland
Held hostage
by Niagara
governance model
Re: 'If that means we don't have a job, so be it': Niagara Falls pressing Queen's Park for governance reform, Jan. 23
A unanimous vote by Niagara Fall city councillors in favour of provincial intervention to change our governance structure in Niagara.
When will other lower-tier councils speak up in favour of change and when will the province do what is right and step in to resolve the issue it identified years ago?
Taxpayers continue to feel like they are being held hostage .
Allen Mckay,

Document 33 of 249

letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters-editor/docview/3161713350/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford a
carefree spender
Premier Doug Ford has become very adept at spending taxpayers' money in ways which do not present themselves as wise.
The hurriedly closed Ontario Science Centre has led to all kinds of questions as to how the situation was allowed to develop with an announcement that in a week the institution was to be closed. The new centre destined for Ontario Place will only add to congestion issues.
Ford broke a deal with the breweries that own The Beer Store that still had a year to run. That cost about $612 million for us.
The provincial government has cheques for taxpayers and their families who filed tax returns in 2023 at a cost of $3 billion of taxpayers' money. At one end of the useful scale, that windfall could allow free hospital parking for people whose plight has enough uncertainty and concern.
The provincial government does not foot the bill for hospital equipment. At $5 million for an up and running MRI machine, what could be better than us buying it for ourselves by donating the cheques to our local hospital authority.
Alcohol at the corner store and the handout do not address compelling electorate issues, of which there are many.
Ford has called a provincial election. That will, too, be a significant cost to taxpayers. One wonders how many more seats he needs so as to feel he is acting on behalf of all Ontarians. Not a perspective he has reflected in many of his decisions.
Mind you, with U.S. President Donald Trump's position, there is reason for much concern. Has Ford shown that he is the right person for the job?
Ross Smith, Niagara Falls
Review Ford's
record, then vote
Premier Doug Ford has called a snap election desiring a huge "historic" majority mandate, sounding a little Trumpish.
Millions more taxpayers' dollars wasted, already having a majority but believing U.S. President Donald Trump will care.
Basically, self-proclaimed "Captain Canada" Ford said NDP Leader Marit Stiles could not negotiate with Trump, to which I disagree strongly.
Please recall his track record: Greenbelt scandal, decline of health care, booze so important, downfall of Service Ontario, Ontario Place, etc.
Now billions spent by sending $200 cheques to try to buy votes.
Mine is donated to Environmental Defence Canada, fighting the needless Highway 413 paving over prime farmlands in an insecure food world.
Instead of crowing American tariffs will bring job losses, work now, Ford, to secure new market partnerships to make Canada independent rather than pandering to Trump.
If the NDP wins, there is a promise of a new Welland hospital. That promise alone, and the reckless record of Ford, will make you vote wisely.
Faye Suthons, Wainfleet

Document 34 of 249

Three candidates on leave from council in bid to become MPP: Nieuwesteeg, Steele, Sorrento taking unpaid absences while McPherson to donate pay

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/three-candidates-on-leave-council-bid-become-mpp/docview/3161713280/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Three+candidates+on+leave+from+council+in+bid+to+become+MPP%3A+Nieuwesteeg%2C+Steele%2C+Sorrento+taking+unpaid+absences+while+McPherson+to+donate+pay&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Four of the more than dozen Niagara candidates so far confirmed to be running in the Feb. 27 provincial election are city and regional councillors - one of them a mayor.
While there is no requirement for them to step away from their local duties during the month-long campaign, three of them will be.
Niagara Falls city Coun. Ruth-Ann Nieuwesteeg, who is the Progressive Conservative candidate in the Niagara Falls riding, said she will be taking an unpaid leave of absence to "focus my sole attention on winning so that I can be a strong voice in the Doug Ford government."
Port Colborne Mayor Bill Steele, who is the Progressive Conservative candidate in the Niagara Centre riding, said he's also taking an unpaid leave. Ward 4 Coun. Ron Bodner, a former mayor of the city, will serve in his absence.
"It's only fair, I'm going to be out campaigning," said Steele. "I'm legally still the mayor, but I've decided to take an unpaid leave.
"I suppose if some type of emergency was to happen, I've got to be part of that ... but as far as meetings and that type of stuff, I think it's only fair that I do an unpaid leave of absence."
St. Catharines Progressive Conservative candidate Sal Sorrento said he will be taking an unpaid leave from his role as a regional councillor.
Like Nieuwesteeg, Sorrento said he will be doing so to focus all of his attention on winning so that he can "be a strong voice in Doug Ford's government."
St. Catharines city Coun. Robin McPherson, the Liberal candidate in that riding, said she will remain active in her role as a St. Patrick's ward councillor.
"As a part-time councillor, I have always balanced my time between my council duties and my professional career," she said in a news release regarding her decision.
"This experience has prepared me to ensure I can fulfil my commitment to the residents of St. Patrick's ward during this campaign."
To prioritize her public service responsibilities, however, McPherson said she has chosen to take a leave of absence from her business during the election campaign.
"I know the time commitment it takes to run a campaign, and while I will remain engaged in council, I may not be as responsive to residents as I usually am," she said.
"In the spirit of transparency and fairness, I will be donating my council salary to charity for the duration of the campaign."
McPherson did not specify to which charity she would be donating her salary.
The release said McPherson's decision not to take a leave from city council is "rooted in her promise" to represent the residents of her ward "to the best of her abilities."
She acknowledges the trust placed in her by the community and is committed to ensuring her constituents remain supported, even during the demands of the election period, it said.
Niagara Falls city clerk Bill Matson said there have been past examples in his city for which candidates who were councillors have and have not decided to take a leave while seeking higher levels of government.
If any of Nieuwesteeg, Steele, Sorrento or McPherson win their riding, their respective councils would have to declare their seat vacant and decide how to fill the vacancy as they would not be allowed to serve as both a councillor or mayor and MPP.
That could be done either through a byelection or by appointing an eligible citizen.
Matson said in Nieuwesteeg's case, council would have 60 days to decide how they would fill that vacancy.
Traditionally, council has appointed the runner-up in the previous at-large municipal election.
For example, when longtime local politician Wayne Thomson resigned last September, council appointed Chris Dabrowski, a former councillor who had finished ninth in the race for one of eight city councillor spots.
"There's always the argument that there's a great deal of cost savings by not having a byelection and just appointing a runner-up," said Matson.
"It's the most easily argued case. The electorate have shown their support for that person and shown their confidence in voting for that person who may have just fallen short of getting elected, so it's usually a pretty good fit."
CAPTION: Ruth-Ann Nieuwesteeg is the Progressive Conservative candidate in the Niagara Falls riding.Port Colborne Mayor Bill Steele will take an unpaid leave of absence while running in Niagara Centre.St. Catharines Regional Coun. Sal Sorrento will take an unpaid leave of absence while running for the Progressive Conservatives.St. Catharines city Coun. Robin McPherson will remain active in her role as St. Patrick's ward councillor.

CREDIT: Ray Spiteri Reporter

Document 35 of 249

Ontario wine officials 'concerned' about the impact of U.S. tariffs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-wine-officials-concerned-about-impact-u-s/docview/3161712746/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+wine+officials+%27concerned%27+about+the+impact+of+U.S.+tariffs&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As U.S. President Donald Trump's government threatens to impose 25 per cent tariffs on all Canadian goods, the local wine industry is bracing for a dramatic change in how it does business.
"We are concerned about the tariffs," said Debbie Zimmerman, chief executive officer for the Grape Growers of Ontario.
If Trump follows through with imposing the tariffs starting Saturday, Zimmerman is expecting Ontario Premier Doug Ford to fight back and protect not just the province's wine industry, but its entire economy.
"There are lots of ways to fight it, especially by investing more in the wine industry," said Zimmerman.
Another way is through legislative change, such as putting "Ontario" on wine labels. And there is also investing in the wine tourism business, as well as expanding markets globally, such as in Asia and Europe.
Zimmerman said Ford has already opened up other locations such as grocery, convenience and big box stores to wines and other alcoholic beverages to the benefit of the sector.
"This is an opportunity to build further the industry," she said.
Zimmerman said she is also putting her faith in Ford, who she said has become "Captain Canada," willing to protect and invest in the Ontario economy.
"We support him 100 per cent," she said.
Ford has told LCBO to clear U.S. alcohol from its shelves if Trump imposes those sweeping tariffs.
"I've sent a direction to the LCBO that if these tariffs come to clear off every bit of U.S. alcohol off the shelves. Let's start promoting more Ontario-made wines, and vodka, the spirits. That's what we need to do," he previously said during remarks at the Rural Ontario Municipal Association's annual general meeting and conference.
LCBO did not respond to a request for comment.
Wine Growers Canada's 2019 report "Canada's Wine Economy - Growth and Innovation Through Global Challenges" revealed it is a $11.6-billion nationwide industry that has experienced "double-digit percentage growth" since 2011.
Wine tourism has poured nearly $2 billion into the Canadian economy, with 4.16 million wine-related tourists visiting the country each year.
The Observatory of Economic Complexity, in a profile of "Wine in Canada," showed that in 2022 the industry exported $84.6 million in wine, making it the 26th largest exporter of wine in the world. The main destination of the wine was to the United States at $68.3 million, China at $7.13 million, the United Kingdom at $1.67 million, South Korea at $1.36 million and Japan at $1.04 million.
The fastest growing markets for wine between 2021 and 2022 were to the United States at $6.29 million, Singapore at $393,000 and the United Kingdom at $377,000.
In 2022, Canada imported $2.17 billion of wine primarily from France at $550 million, the United States at $484 million, Italy at $458 million, Australia at $154 million and Spain at $126 million.
Jamie Slingerland of Pillitteri Estates Winery said in a recent interview the potential of 25 per cent tariffs on wine will force the company to adapt.
"There has always been a conscious understanding to export our wines," he said. "It's how you navigate that path and develop your customer base."
After being hit hard by COVID-19, climate change and other external forces, Slingerland said wineries have managed to adapt to a changing market.
Pillitteri's wines, particularly its icewines, have found a place in Asia, China and Europe, along with various parts of the United States.
Donald Ziraldo, co-founder of Inniskillin Winery in the 1970s, who helped to create the VQA wine program, said in a recent interview Canada needs to fight Trump with his tariff program.
"We need to open up the Canadian barriers," said Ziraldo, who founded Ziraldo Wines in Niagara-on-the-Lake after leaving Inniskillin following its sale to Constellation Brands in 2006.
Ziraldo said there are overly restrictive barriers that prevent interprovincial trade of wine among Canadian provinces.
"It would be a great marketing tool if we can get the LCBO to put, say, British Columbia wine in its stores and groceries," said Ziraldo.
Until 2019, Ontario had strict limits on how much alcohol one could bring into the province. The previous limit was nine litres of wine, three litres of spirits and 24.6 litres of beer. Those limits were lifted in 2019, but it only impacted alcohol use for personal consumption.
If Ontario residents want to order liquor for sale in the province, they have to order it through LCBO. Direct shipment of alcohol from other provinces to consumers is not allowed.
There has been some movement. British Columbia wineries can now directly ship wine to consumers in Alberta after an interprovincial deal was finalized in 2024. The agreement is for one year and will then be reviewed.
Ziraldo also pointed to expanding Canada's market to other countries and, echoing Zimmerman, promoting the country's wines.
"We have great wines, and we should let everybody know about it," said Ziraldo.
CAPTION: Ontario wineries could be dealing with another impact on their sales if U.S. President Donald Trump imposes sweeping tariffs on their product.Kevin Werner Metroland

CREDIT: Kevin Werner Reporter
