Document 36 of 249

Meet Niagara's Ontario election candidates - so far: Three of 16 spots for the four major parties remained open Wednesday

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/meet-niagaras-ontario-election-candidates-so-far/docview/3161406271/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Meet+Niagara%27s+Ontario+election+candidates+-+so+far%3A+Three+of+16+spots+for+the+four+major+parties+remained+open+Wednesday&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-01-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The candidates board in Niagara is filling in as the Ontario a provincial election campaign officially kicked off Wednesday after Premier Doug Ford triggered a snap election.
Voters in Niagara will head to the polls on Feb. 27 and the four major parties - for the most part - have chosen their candidates in St. Catharines, Niagara Falls, Niagara Centre and Niagara West ridings.
In the St. Catharines riding (population of 114,782 according to 2021 census), incumbent New Democrat Jennie Stevens will be challenged by Progressive Conservative and Niagara Region Coun. Sal Sorrento, Liberal and St. Catharines city Coun. Robin McPherson and Stephen Vincelette-Smith of the Greens.
Stevens first won the riding in 2018, ending Liberal Jim Bradley's 41-year run as the city's representative at Queen's Park.
In the Niagara Falls riding, which also encompasses Niagara-on-the-Lake and Fort Erie (total population of 146,404), incumbent New Democrat Wayne Gates will be challenged by Progressive Conservative and Niagara Falls city Coun. Ruth-Ann Nieuwesteeg and Celia Taylor of the Greens.
The Liberals have not yet chosen a candidate.
Gates first won the riding in a 2014 byelection after the resignation of Liberal Kim Craitor. Gates won the seat in a general election later that year and again in 2018 and 2022.
In the Niagara Centre riding, which encompasses Welland, Port Colborne, Thorold and part of south St. Catharines (total population of 119,799), incumbent New Democrat Jeff Burch will be challenged by Progressive Conservative and Port Colborne Mayor Bill Steele.
The Liberals and Greens have not yet chosen candidates.
Burch first won a seat at Queen's Park in 2018 and was returned in 2022 with just 850 votes more than the second place finisher, Progressive Conservative candidate Fred Davies, the narrowest margin in Niagara's four ridings.
In the Niagara West riding, which encompasses Pelham, Wainfleet, Lincoln, West Lincoln, Grimsby and part of west St. Catharines (total population of 96,779), incumbent Progressive Conservative Sam Oosterhoff will be challenged by New Democrat and former Pelham mayor Dave Augustyn, Liberal Shauna Boyle and Mark Harrison of the Greens.
Oosterhoff first won the riding in a 2016 byelection as a 19-year-old and was the youngest MPP ever elected. He has been re-elected twice since then. In June, Oosterhoff became the youngest cabinet minister in Ontario history after Ford named him associate minister of energy-intensive industries.

Document 37 of 249

Niagara awarded $4M to curb homelessness: Region moving forward with supportive housing strategy

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/niagara-awarded-4m-curb-homelessness/docview/3160793090/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Niagara+awarded+%244M+to+curb+homelessness%3A+Region+moving+forward+with+supportive+housing+strategy&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Niagara's efforts to combat homelessness have been awarded more than $4 million over the next two years from the federal government.
At Niagara Region headquarters in Thorold Tuesday, St. Catharines MP Chris Bittle, parliamentary secretary to Housing, Infrastructure and Communities Minister Nathaniel Erskine-Smith, spoke about the government's Unsheltered Homelessness and Encampments Initiative (UHEI) to fund solutions to the growing homelessness crisis.
"Addressing homelessness is one of the most pressing issues facing our community, and it's a responsibility all orders of government share," Bittle said, speaking directly to Niagara's problem.
"It's something we all see, if we go to parks, go downtown, it's something that confronts us.
"This investment will have a tangible impact and will support initiatives such as renovating shelters to increase capacity, creating transitional housing options and expanding outreach services to connect individuals with the support they need," he said.
Ottawa last week said it would be awarding nearly $90 million to 10 Ontario municipalities that promise to match the federal funding.
"This," said Bittle, "is a $250-million federal program that should, if you have matching partners, become a $500-million program. Unfortunately, the Province of Ontario did not sign onto this program."
Bittle voiced his frustration with the Doug Ford government.
"Premier Ford says there's a housing crisis, but when we look at provincial action, I don't see that urgency, unfortunately," he said.
Bittle said addressing homelessness requires sustained dedication, compassion and innovation from all levels of government.
Region Chair Jim Bradley said there are many challenges with which the area's unhoused population contends.
"Those experiencing homelessness in many cases are also battling mental-health issues and addictions and, given these complexities, there will never be a one-size-fits-all solution," he said.
"Rather, solving the homelessness crisis depends on offering a wide range of supports and services, from mental health supports to addiction services to supportive housing and emergency shelters.
"As we've been told many times, the only true long-term solution to homelessness is housing itself and that means everyone has access to a stable, affordable home."
Under the funding initiative, municipalities are required to have a community encampment response plan detailing supports available to unhoused people.
"A key principle the federal government asked (for) was a human rights-based approach to exiting people from encampments," Cathy Cousins, the Region's director of homelessness services and community engagement, said after Tuesday's announcement.
As a result of "fantastic outcomes" with pilot projects aimed at housing people who had been living outside, Cousins said how the Region addresses encampments will go unchanged.
Cousins said the largest components of the federal funding initiative, for the Region, are development of a permanent shelter strategy and supportive housing master plan, and enhancing outreach.
"We have smaller programs, including (there being) some money put aside to support individuals exiting encampments and going straight into housing to help with maybe first and last month's rent or other supplies they may need to make that transition successful," she said.
Henri Koning, Niagara's community services commissioner, speaking after the announcement said funding allows the Region to narrow in on long-term housing requirements.
"We're moving forward with a supportive housing strategy in Niagara and identifying what housing is required, where and at what level, and starting the process of identifying how do we move to outcomes on that," she said.
"This funding helps us to move that along, so in terms of us looking at meeting not just needs today, but also looking at moving forward, how do we address emerging needs in the future."
CAPTION: "Addressing homelessness is one of the most pressing issues facing our community," says St. Catharines MP Chris Bittle. "It's something that confronts us."Julie Jocsak St. Catharines Standard

CREDIT: Matthew P. Barker Reporter

Subject: Federal funding; Shelters; Homeless people; Initiatives

Grimsby's GO station on track for construction: As election looms, province announces building contract will be awarded in spring

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/grimsbys-go-station-on-track-construction/docview/3160792049/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Grimsby%27s+GO+station+on+track+for+construction%3A+As+election+looms%2C+province+announces+building+contract+will+be+awarded+in+spring&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With Ontario's voters headed to the polls next month, the Doug Ford government has announced the long-awaited Casablanca GO station in Grimsby is moving ahead.
"This contract will be awarded in the spring and will bring us one step closer to getting shovels in the ground," said Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria, who made the announcement at Grimsby town hall Tuesday morning.
"Grimsby GO will be a game-changer for communities in the region. It will help ease gridlock and connect more people to good jobs and homes and new opportunities in one of Ontario's fastest growing regions."
Asked when construction might begin, Sarkaria did not offer any timelines.
"We're going to move as quickly as we can to get shovels in the ground because we know how important it is to this region," he said.
Asked if the announcement was linked to the expected election call, Sarkaria dodged the question.
"What I can tell you is this government, like no other, has put money into public transit," Sakaria said.
He said the Grimsby GO station is part of the province's $70-billion plan to expand transit across Ontario.
Kinga Surma, Ontario's minister of infrastructure, was also there for the announcement.
"(The request for proposals announced today) now allows my ministry to truly have a mandate to negotiate with landowners within the area so we can bring a transit-oriented community here at Grimsby," said Surma.
She said expanded GO train service could result in as many as 250,000 new homes along the GO rail system.
"We are embarking on the largest transit expansion in Ontario's history," said Niagara West MPP Sam Oosterhoff. "It's the largest transit expansion in North America."
Niagara Region Chair Jim Bradley said it is welcome news.
"People rely on a robust transit system that services not only all of Niagara, but Niagara and beyond," Bradley said.
Grimsby Mayor Jeff Jordan said the town has worked hard on the GO file, and it's good to see movement on the part of the province.
"It's really exciting and (we'll) see if we can move forward with a station in the very near future," he said.
Frank Miele, Grimsby's economic development officer, said a GO train station in town would result in a massive boost to the local economy in terms of new commercial and residential development.
"Four billion dollars of development over the next five to 10 years," Miele said. "We calculated the amount of development that would take place as a result of the GO transit stop."
For his part, Dave Augustyn, the NDP candidate in Niagara West, said the announcement is not as impressive as it might seem.
"This is a reannouncement of a reannouncement of a promise," Augustyn said. "There's nothing concrete here."
The Niagara Region's website said the province announced in 2016 the extension of Lakeshore West GO trains to Niagara with stops in Grimsby, St. Catharines and Niagara Falls.
CAPTION: Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria announces the province is moving ahead with the long-awaited Casablanca GO station in Grimsby.Mark Newman Metroland

Document 39 of 249

Truckers pulled over in response to Trump threats: Project targets commercial haulers headed for border crossings

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/truckers-pulled-over-response-trump-threats/docview/3160791996/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Truckers+pulled+over+in+response+to+Trump+threats%3A+Project+targets+commercial+haulers+headed+for+border+crossings&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's efforts to boost Ontario's border security amid U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff threats played out near Niagara's international crossings over the weekend.
Several transport trucks could be seen stopped by Ontario Ministry of Transportation vehicles to the side of the Fort Erie-bound Queen Elizabeth Way, heading toward the Peace Bridge Saturday.
Commercial vehicles were also seen pulled over on the Canadian side of the Queenston-Lewiston Bridge in Niagara-on-the-Lake.
A similar scene played out on Sunday.
The MTO did not provide specifics, including how many transport trucks were stopped and what ensued, but a spokesperson did link the blitz to the province's recently launched Operation Deterrence program.
"We have zero tolerance for bad actors in Ontario," MTO's Tanya Blazina said in an emailed statement.
"We prioritize the safety of our citizens and have introduced various pieces of legislation to ensure we punish bad actors who undermine our systems and ensure people can thrive as they build their lives in our communities.
"Ontario has recently launched Operation Deterrence, a comprehensive preparedness and planning framework aimed at enhancing security at international borders - and addressing criminal activities affecting citizens on both sides of the border."
The initiative includes the deployment of transportation enforcement officers to inspect commercial vehicles, Blazina said.
"Additionally, our transportation enforcement officers continue to focus on the safe operations of commercial vehicles on our roads - both at truck inspection stations and on patrol, ensuring drivers are operating vehicles safely."
Trump threatened 25 per cent tariffs on Canadian and Mexican products following his November election victory unless America's neighbours strengthened their borders with the U.S. to curb illegal immigration and fentanyl smuggling.
Ford then announced the province would enhance security on the frontier.
"Ontario is stepping up with Operation Deterrence to crack down on illegal border crossings and illegal guns and drugs," Ford said earlier this month, imploring Ottawa to also do more.
"A more co-ordinated Team Canada approach that includes more boots on the ground is the only way to detect, deter and disrupt illegal activity and ensure the safety and security of Canadian and American communities."
Operation Deterrence includes 200 Ontario Provincial Police officers who are working in partnership with Royal Canadian Mounted Police.
In the past several weeks, the OPP has provided more than 6,000 hours of focused patrols to deter, detect and disrupt illegal border activity using fixed-wing aircraft, helicopters, remotely piloted aircraft, boats, off-road vehicles, patrol vehicles and foot patrol, the province said.
As well, it said, "Operation Deterrence activity will be enhanced through the use of the emergency response team, front-line officers and additional specialty resources, including canine units, commercial motor vehicle inspections and criminal investigators."
The initiative is above and beyond what was already being done by the Canada Border Services Agency at Ontario's 14 official U.S. border crossings.
The Canadian government in December announced a $1.3-billion border security plan.
With his riding of Niagara Falls, Niagara-on-the-Lake and Fort Erie being home to four border crossings, MPP Wayne Gates said his constituents are "uniquely threatened" by tariff threats.
"I think we need a strong response that protects Canadian interests, but do it without harming everyday people who rely on the border crossings as part of our daily lives," Gates said.
"We have people who come to Niagara to experience our wineries, the falls - thousands of local jobs rely on an efficient border processing and the close nature of our communities like Fort Erie, Buffalo, where families literally live on both sides.
"The border shouldn't be slowed down for political purposes. We need to ensure that the workers at the border have the tools they need to do their jobs, that they're certainly full staffed. Their work is critical to border security and it needs to be supported."
With files from Toronto Star
CAPTION: Commercial truckers headed to the Peace Bridge at Fort Erie on the weekend were pulled over by Ontario Transportation Ministry officials as part ofOperation Deterrence, initiated in response to U.S. President Donald Trump's tariff threats.Toronto Star file photo

Document 40 of 249

Homeless policies 'an unprecedented failure,' says NDP's Burch: 'What we need is adequate housing and wraparound supports,' MPP argues

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/homeless-policies-unprecedented-failure-says-ndps/docview/3160302985/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Homeless+policies+%27an+unprecedented+failure%2C%27+says+NDP%27s+Burch%3A+%27What+we+need+is+adequate+housing+and+wraparound+supports%2C%27+MPP+argues&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-01-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Jeff Burch says he's frustrated with the Ontario housing minister's response to Niagara's growing homelessness crisis.
The Niagara Centre MPP in December sent a letter to Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra that highlighted Richard Tremblay, a Welland man eking out an existence in his car, with his dog, after losing his home due to health and work issues.
Burch, the NDP's housing critic, outlined problems many unhoused individuals experience while waiting for subsidized housing.
In his response, Calandra sympathized and recognized "the challenges many Ontarians face in finding safe and stable housing," but fell short of talking about tangible solutions the government can offer to get more social housing units built in the province.
Burch called it a "form letter response," and scolded Calandra's answers, voicing frustration with what he said is a lack of movement on social housing starts.
"It's very frustrating to get a response from a minister that's so bureaucratic and follows this government's trend or tendency to blame everything on municipal governments or pass the buck," he said.
Burch added this is disappointing, especially for Tremblay.
"What we need is adequate housing and wraparound supports for folks to get themselves out of the situation that they're in," he said.
In his letter, Calandra pushed accountability for "community housing" to lower-tier governments, he said.
The responsibility for social housing in Ontario shifted from the federal government to provincial in 1985 and was later downloaded to lower-tier municipalities in 1999.
Since then, wait-lists for social housing have gotten longer, specifically in the Niagara region. About 12,000 people, like Tremblay, are on the chronological wait-list.
"Wait-times only continue to climb, as they vary across the region because each city has different turnover and a different number of units," said Janette McKay, Niagara Region housing services supervisor.
"It's going to range anywhere from five to over 20 years."
The region, through multiple programs including a rent supplement program, funds about 8,300 units across Niagara.
"People don't move out of housing units anymore, certainly not at the rate that they used to," McKay said.
"So, it's supply and demand and we can't make something out of nothing."
The turnover rate for Niagara Regional Housing is not what it was even five years ago. More people are staying longer because of higher market rents, said Cameron Banach, NRH chief executive officer.
"The last six or seven years, the region has got its fair share of new builds with stacking funds, whether with the federal or provincial governments, and have put new units on the market," he said.
"There's a consolidated housing master plan dealing with the shortage of housing in Niagara going to council in May. It's a 25-year projection of what's needed and an actual financial modelling of how we could actually achieve that."
Banach said more rent-geared-to-income housing is needed, especially for vulnerable populations, as "there is nowhere for them to go."
"If you're homeless, we've put on in the last two years another 120 or 130 shelter beds to address the situation, even though shelter is not the answer," he said. "But it's a temporary stopgap, at least you have somewhere to go."
In his letter, Calandra said through partnerships with municipalities and Indigenous groups, the provincial government provides "unprecedented support to ensure vulnerable people in Ontario have access to safe and appropriate housing."
Burch said Queen's Park bureaucrats are "talking themselves into a corner" when it comes to the amount of help provided to people experiencing homelessness.
"On the one hand, the only thing that's unprecedented is the number of homeless people that there are in Ontario," he said. "If they're spending an unprecedented amount of money and there is an unprecedented amount of homeless people at the same time, then this government has policies that are an unprecedented failure."
CAPTION: Niagara Centre MPP Jeff Burch is critical of a response from Ontario Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra, after sending him a letter in December about an unhoused Welland man.

CREDIT: Matthew P. Barker Reporter

Readers write Letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write-letters-editor/docview/3159971397/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write+Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-01-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Unnecessary
election
Re: Doug Ford to call
an Ontario election for Feb. 27, Jan. 24
Why do some politicians think it's important we know what they think of other candidates who aren't in their party? Cutting up, trying to degrade an opponent is small-minded.
I have a news flash: I don't care what your opinion of another party's candidate is.
If you want my vote, I would like to know what you are going to do to make my life better since my vote will make yours better.
I have never missed voting since I was 21.
I have worked hard all my life, for Canadian companies and places, starting at age nine working on my grandparents' farm.
I am a Zoomer whose CPP and OAS won't pay for a two-bedroom apartment in St. Catharines.
You want my vote? Give hard-working, well-deserving Zoomers and beyond a chance at a decent life. We would appreciate at least a cost-of-living income. Then we wouldn't have to go to food banks, etc.
And, Doug Ford, it is isn't necessary to call an early election. It might backfire. Don't waste my tax dollars on things like that.
You're already wasting them by telling us about the highways you want to build and doubling the Garden City Skyway without plans to widen the connecting QEW that I'm aware of. So we go from six lanes to 12 then back to six. Duh!
Use our tax dollars wisely. No unnecessary elections, tell us how you will help make our lives better and forget degrading others.
S.A. Timco, St. Catharines
Furl the flag
Re: Donald Trump pushes his tariff threats and '51st state' taunts against Canada on the world stage, Jan. 23
Dear fellow Canadians,
A flag is a powerful national symbol. I ask, urge and beg you to remove any foreign stars and bars flags you may be flying or own. The leader of that country does not want to be our friend.
He wants to be our ruler.
The only legitimate place for that flag in our country is at the American embassy. Please, please, please ...
Doreen M. Peever,
St. Catharines
MAID survey
an opportunity
Whether you are for or against medical assistance in dying, now is your chance to convey your thoughts to our federal government.
Health Canada's survey covers several aspects of MAID, but the goal is to assess support for advance directives. That means the possibility of a choice for people who may not be competent when closer to imminent death. Providing MAID in response to an advance request is a criminal offence at this time.
Use the online link, canada.ca/en/health-canada/programs/consultation-advance-requests-medical-assistance-dying.html.
The deadline to respond is Feb. 14. Health Canada will publish a report on key findings in the spring.
Joan Worthington,
St. Catharines

St. Catharines, Ont., L2R 7T4. Letters should be 250 words or less, and may be edited for length and clarity. Please include your name, address and phone number for verification purposes.

Document 42 of 249

With election call coming, some Niagara nominee slots still open: All four incumbents will run again with election expected on Feb. 27

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/with-election-call-coming-some-niagara-nominee/docview/3159624153/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=With+election+call+coming%2C+some+Niagara+nominee+slots+still+open%3A+All+four+incumbents+will+run+again+with+election+expected+on+Feb.+27&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-01-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With a hasty provincial election on the near horizon, there are still a few candidate slots to be filled in Niagara.
On Friday, Ontario Premier Doug Ford confirmed he plans to call a snap election on Wednesday, saying he needs a "strong mandate" to tackle the threat of 25 per cent tariffs from U.S. President Donald Trump.
Calling the election on Jan. 29 would mean Ontarians will be voting Feb. 27, well before the June 2026 fixed election date.
In Niagara, all the incumbents have been nominated to run again and over the past few months, challenger slots have been filling up. However, as of Friday, there still remained a half dozen slots across Niagara for the four major parties - Progressive Conservative, Liberal, NDP and Green.
Only in Niagara West, where PC MPP Sam Oosterhoff is again running, are all the slots filled.
Oosterhoff first won the riding in a 2016 byelection as a 19-year-old and was the youngest MPP ever elected. In June, Oosterhoff became the youngest cabinet minister in Ontario history after Ford named him associate minister of energy-intensive industries.
Challenging Oosterhoff will be former Pelham mayor Dave Augustyn, who is running under the NDP banner. On Jan. 7, the Liberals announced community leader and advocate Shauna Boyle would be running. For the Greens, the candidate is Mark Harrison.
This contrasts with Niagara Falls, where Wayne Gates, who hopes to regain his seat for the NDP, is so far the only officially listed candidate.
Gates first won the riding in a 2014 byelection after the resignation of Liberal Kim Craitor. Gates held the seat in a general election later that year and again in 2018.
Gates retained the seat in 2022 with a 5,946-vote margin, receiving 24,608 votes and 48.1 per cent of ballots cast. His top challenger, Progressive Conservative Bob Gale, received 18,662 votes and 36.5 per cent of the ballots.
In a news release Friday, Gates said he is ready to continue working for Niagara, despite the "unnecessary election."
"Ford has mused about calling an election for over a year now and is taking advantage of a crisis to put himself ahead," he said. "All parties have committed to working together to combat the threat of Trump's tariffs, but Ford has refused to call us back to the (legislature) despite the fact we have only been there for seven weeks in the last six months."
In St. Catharines, all but one slot has been filled to go up against NDP incumbent Jennie Stevens. Stevens first won the riding in 2018, ending Liberal Jim Bradley's 41-year run as the city's representative at Queen's Park.
Running for the Liberals is city Coun. Robin McPherson, who represents Ward 4. The Green Party candidate is Stephen Vincelette-Smith.
And in Niagara Centre, NDP incumbent Jeff Burch hopes to retain the seat he first won in 2018. He was returned in 2022 with just 850 votes more than second place finisher, PC candidate Fred Davies, the narrowest margin in Niagara's four ridings.
In a statement, Burch touted his commitment to fighting for health care in the riding and vowed to continue to do so.
"I am fighting to restore, protect, and expand our local health-care services," Burch said.
"With the increasing pressure and overwhelming demand on our health-care system, it is crucial that we have a fully-serviced Welland Hospital and a 24/7 Port Colborne Urgent Care Centre."
As of Friday, the only listed challenger is current Port Colborne mayor Bill Steele, who was nominated to run under the PC banner in December.
With files by Bill Sawchuk
CAPTION: Niagara's incumbents Niagara West PC MPP Sam Oosterhoff, Niagara Falls NDP MPP Wayne Gates, Niagara Centre NDP MPP Jeff Burch and St. Catharines NDP MPP Jennie Stevnens.Metroland file photos

CREDIT: Mike Zettel Reporter With files Bill Sawchuk

Document 43 of 249

Ford to call snap election next week: Premier says he needs 'big mandate' for Feb. 27 vote as he vows to fight Trump tariffs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-call-snap-election-next-week/docview/3159623473/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+to+call+snap+election+next+week%3A+Premier+says+he+needs+%27big+mandate%27+for+Feb.+27+vote+as+he+vows+to+fight+Trump+tariffs&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-01-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Doug Ford insists he's not rushing into a snap Feb. 27 Ontario election to get in front of a federal vote expected this spring.
Ontarians traditionally prefer different parties in power at Queen's Park and in Ottawa at the same time, so some Progressive Conservative strategists have privately expressed concern about their provincial electoral prospects if, as polls suggest, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre is triumphant.
Asked Friday if he were "scared" of a Poilievre victory, Ford said: "No, not whatsoever."
Ford plans to call a snap election Wednesday. The Feb. 27 election date is more than a year before the June 2026 fixed election date.
Pressed on whether he was scrambling to get ahead of potential federal spending cuts should the Conservatives succeed Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's Liberals, the Ontario Tory leader shook his head.
"My job - when we hopefully get a big mandate - is going to be standing up to any federal government that wants to cut health care from Ontario, cut jobs from Ontario, to tax people from Ontario," Ford said at a news conference in Brampton.
Last weekend in Mississauga, Poilievre bumped into provincial Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie at a community event where they had a constructive conversation after the federal Tory chief warmly greeted her.
"Pierre mentioned to Bonnie that he is a fan of her housing plan," said a senior Liberal strategist, speaking confidentially in order to recount details of the impromptu chat.
That's a reference to Crombie's plan to make housing more affordable by scrapping the land-transfer tax for first-time homebuyers and seniors who are downsizing, eliminating development charges on new homes, and interest-free loans for renters facing financial emergencies that could result in evictions.
"Look, these are two politicians who are focusing their plans on what people need - they couldn't be more different than Ford and Trudeau. Makes sense that they would be talking about it," said the provincial Liberal.
The federal Tory leader's office would not comment on the discussion. There is no love lost between the national Conservatives and the PCs, who work closely with Trudeau's Liberals.
Poilievre's "axe the tax" mantra is one reason he is leading in polls against Trudeau.
Crombie was one of the first prominent Liberals to distance herself from the consumer price on carbon, a stance now adopted by would-be federal Grit leaders such as Mark Carney and Chrystia Freeland.
Ford stressed the main rationale for the early vote is U.S. President Donald Trump's threatened 25 per cent levies on Canadian products as of Feb. 1.
"I'll be seeing the lieutenant governor on Tuesday (for the issuing of the writs Wednesday). We need a mandate from the people to fight against Donald Trump's tariffs, the attack, he's coming against our families, our businesses, our communities," he said.
The Tories already have a majority at Queen's Park and an election, which will cost between $150 million and $175 million, was not supposed to be held until June 2026.
"With a strong mandate, we will be able to fight with Donald Trump to make sure we stop the tariffs and make sure that we give certainty to the people of Ontario," said Ford, adding it may take "tens of billions of dollars" in stimulus funding to keep the province's economy afloat.
Aside from the Trump tariffs and a Poilievre victory, the provincial Tories are worried about the ongoing RCMP investigation into the $8.28-billion Greenbelt scandal and would like to strike the Liberals when they may be too busy with the March 9 federal leadership race to help Crombie.
Even though he was at a government announcement in Brampton, Ford struck a fiercely partisan tone, repeatedly attacking Crombie and NDP Leader Marit Stiles. "Imagine Bonnie Crombie or Marit Stiles sitting across from President Trump, negotiating a deal, it would be an absolute disaster, and God help us if that ever happens," he said dismissively, noting he will go to Washington twice during the campaign on previously planned trips to lobby U.S. officials.
No meeting between Ford and Trump is expected and the PC campaign will have to foot the tab for his February jaunts stateside.
Rival leaders accused him of being a political opportunist by triggering an election at a time of uncertainty.
"He thinks he can con Ontarians with an early election to escape his record - but we won't let him," said Stiles.
"Doug Ford has been on the side of wealthy insiders while life for working people has gotten harder and more expensive. How can we trust him to stand up against Donald Trump and his billionaires when he can't deliver on making life more affordable, getting people a family doctor, or building more homes?" said the New Democratic leader.
Crombie, who has yet to disclose which Mississauga riding she will run in, said "with the looming threat of tariffs and the challenges facing our province, Ford has chosen to prioritize his own political survival over the people he was elected to serve."
"He's not a leader - he's a coward," said the Liberal leader, a former Mississauga mayor.
"Doug Ford can attack me all he wants. But as premier, I wouldn't run from my responsibilities. This snap election isn't about Trump or tariffs, it's about Doug Ford scrambling to outrun the RCMP investigation into his Greenbelt scandal," she added.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner said "Doug Ford is putting his job before your job" when Trump tariffs could throw 500,000 Ontarians out of work.
"All the things that a provincial government should do - build affordable homes, make sure we have doctors and nurses, safe schools - Doug Ford has walked away from," said Schreiner.
Upon dissolution next Wednesday, there are 79 Tories, including Speaker Ted Arnott, 28 New Democrats, nine Liberals, six Independents and two Greens in the 124-member house.
CAPTION: Rival leaders accused Premier Doug Ford of being a political opportunist by triggering an election at a time of uncertainty.Chris Young the canadian press

Document 44 of 249

MPP urges buy-local strategy in the face of possible tariffs: Wayne Gates calls for 'Team Ontario' approach as Trump is sworn in for second term

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/mpp-urges-buy-local-strategy-face-possible/docview/3157388821/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=MPP+urges+buy-local+strategy+in+the+face+of+possible+tariffs%3A+Wayne+Gates+calls+for+%27Team+Ontario%27+approach+as+Trump+is+sworn+in+for+second+term&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-01-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Niagara Falls MPP Wayne Gates is encouraging his constituents to buy local as Donald Trump commences another term as president of the United States.
"We're investing in our neighbours, we're investing in our community - we're investing in our future," the New Democrat said of turning attentions to purchasing domestic.
During his inauguration Monday, Trump confirmed he will impose tariffs on foreign goods.
Gates said sweeping tariffs would impact key industries in Ontario, including auto manufacturing, energy, forestry and agriculture, and put as many as 1.5 million Canadian jobs at risk.
There have been reports about the Canadian federal government levying counter-tariffs on American goods if Trump follows through on his threat.
Gates said Niagara residents can also make a statement.
"You can go to a small business that you know is supplying stuff that's from Ontario. The breweries are a good example, the wineries," he said.
"If you're making a bigger purchase, you can take a look at where the car is made. There are cars that are made right here in Ontario.
"My favourite thing to do, when they're up and running, I like going to the farmers markets - buy local fruits, support the local farmers, support their families."
Gates said it may take a little more work for shoppers, but it's "worth it to stick up for your province and your country."
"I think that's one thing that's come out of this. It's waking up Canadians to say, 'We've got a pretty good country here,' and we've been very loyal to the Americans for a long time."
He said Canada stood strong with America after the 9/11 terror attacks and just recently sent water bombers to California in the battle to contain wildfires devastating large areas of Los Angeles.
"I think the Americans are seeing that and seeing that we are a good friend. We respect the Americans, but an attack against us unnecessarily is really going to get Ontarians and Canadians, I think, coming together that we haven't seen in a long time ago, probably since maybe World War Two."
Gates also called on Premier Doug Ford to stop "dousing gasoline" on rumours he will call a snap provincial election.
Instead, he said Ford should work with border communities and leaders across Ontario, like those in the riding Gates represents, to protect jobs and the economy.
"Ontario cannot afford to take its eye off the ball. What I hear from my communities along the border and from our leaders is that this is an existential threat that requires an all-hands-on-deck approach," said Gates.
"I don't care if you're Conservative, Liberal, NDP, Green or independent - we must work together across party lines to protect Ontarian and Canadian jobs, independence and sovereignty.
"In the middle of an affordability crisis, when taxes and families using food banks are up to historic levels, spending hundreds of millions of dollars on an election is something we can't afford right now."
He said calling an election would play into the hands of people who "want to divide us."
"How about instead of calling an unnecessary election for political gain, Ford recalls the legislature so we can work together to stand up united against Trump's tariffs?"
The Ontario legislature is on recess until March 3.
Last Thursday, Ford offered the clearest indication yet of an early provincial election.
Addressing a national response to tariff threats, Ford suggested he would require a new mandate before spending "tens of billions of dollars" on economic stimulus packages for impacted industries and workers.
Gates said a "Team Ontario" and "Team Canada" approach would show "we're all united" and that "we're going to do everything we can to protect Canadian jobs, Canadian companies."
"(Ford) says he needs a mandate if he's going to invest billions of dollars in keeping our economy going - he's got a majority government, it makes no sense (to call an election)."
Gates said it has become especially important Ontario take a lead since Prime Minister Justin Trudeau prorogued Parliament until March 24 and announced he will resign as party leader.
CAPTION: Niagara Falls MPP Wayne Gatessays U.S. tariffs will impact key industries in Ontario.

Document 45 of 249

Unity or 'American carnage': What tone will Trump take in second inaugural address?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/unity-american-carnage-what-tone-will-trump-take/docview/3157113807/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Unity+or+%27American+carnage%27%3A+What+tone+will+Trump+take+in+second+inaugural+address%3F&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-01-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: John F. Kennedy inspired Americans with the words, "Ask not what your country can do for you - ask what you can do for your country." Franklin D. Roosevelt assured them there's "nothing to fear but fear itself."
Abraham Lincoln sought to heal a nation divided by civil war by calling for "malice toward none" in a speech that would later be carved into the wall of the Lincoln Memorial.
These inaugural speeches have become part of the cultural fabric of America and are cited constantly in calls for patriotism, unity and peace.
President-elect Donald Trump's first speech as president in 2017 painted a bleak picture of what he called "American carnage." As Trump returns to the White House on Monday, the world is watching to see whether the Republican leader will take a different approach.
Not all inaugural speeches stand the test of time, said Aaron Kall, the director of debate for the University of Michigan. They do, however, set the tone for the four years of an administration.
Eight years ago, Trump's speech described a dwindling nation riddled with crime and drugs and fiercely criticized the policies of his predecessors. The approximately 16-minute speech was peppered with isolationist "America First" rhetoric.
At its conclusion, former president George W. Bush reportedly called it weird.
"It wasn't very well received just because it was very dark in tone, about crime and immigration," said Kall, an expert in speeches and politics. "And you want something a bit more uplifting and rallying."
Not much in Trump's messaging has changed in the years since. He campaigned on claims that violent migrants were crossing the border in droves, and on tackling crime and saving the economy. He repeatedly called the U.S. a nation in decline.
Since the election, he's pushed both isolationism and American imperialism. He threatened to impose 25 per cent tariffs on Canada and Mexico, initially saying the duties were linked to border security. He later connected the proposed levies on Canada to trade disparities and defence funding.
Trump mused about using "economic force" to make Canada the 51st state. He wouldn't rule out using the military to take over Greenland and the Panama Canal. Despite these comments, Kall said he believes a different kind of speech is in store when the Republican leader gives his second inaugural address on Monday.
"It doesn't mean everything will change, but there's some reasons to believe it could be a little different," he said.
Trump has a lot more confidence going into this speech, Kall said. He won the popular vote and Republicans now control both the House and the Senate. Trump has raised a lot more money for the celebration and his transition is rolling out more smoothly than in 2017.
More importantly, he's done this before.
"I think it could be more confident, forward-looking and optimistic because he's more confident," Kall said.
Kall said he's watching to see if Trump follows historical norms by thanking outgoing President Joe Biden and Vice-President Kamala Harris.
There are many reasons to be skeptical about the idea of Trump embracing unity as he returns to America's highest office, Kall added. But a lot has happened since Trump's first term, including the assassination attempt last summer.
Inauguration addresses are typically much shorter and less detailed than the State of the Union, another important speech usually given by presidents once a year, but this one should still provide some details about priorities in Trump's policy agenda.
Trump has pledged to sign an executive order Monday to put his tariff plan in place. Canadian officials are preparing a response but it remains unclear what the president-elect will ultimately do.
"This will kind of be the calm before the storm," Kall said of the speech.
"And once it's all over, then things will be back to a very rapid pace. So, this may be the last calming portion of the administration."
Here are some of the Canadians expected to be in Washington Monday:
Alberta Premier Danielle Smith: Smith has emerged as main Canadian voice amid the tariff threat, regularly appearing on U.S. news stations and recently travelling to Trump's Florida Mar-a-Lago resort to meet with the president-elect. But the premier caused controversy in Canada last week after refusing to sign off on a joint statement with other premiers and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau that said every countermeasure was on the table to push back any proposed duties.
Industry Minister François-Philippe Champagne: As co-lead of the federal government's Team Canada strategy, the cabinet minister has been connecting with American politicians and businesses since early 2024. Champagne declined to join the Liberal leadership race, saying he is focused on ensuring Ottawa is ready to protect Canadian industry from the trade threats posed by Trump.
Trade Minister Mary Ng: The other co-lead of Team Canada, Ng has also spent the past year connecting with Americans to promote the Canada-U.S. relationship. Last week, Ng was in New Jersey and New York where she met with business leaders and state government officials.
Newfoundland and Labrador Premier Andrew Furey: Furey is among the Canadian leaders who criticized Trump's comments about making Canada the 51st state and using "economic force." Furey, whose province is an oil producer, has said Canada should make sure the U.S. is aware of its critical reliance on Canadian energy but not pull back on those exports just yet.
Ontario Energy Minister Stephen Lecce and Trade Minister Vic Fedeli: Lecce and Fedeli will likely be headed to D.C. with Premier Doug Ford's "Fortress Am-Can" proposal in hand. Ontario's strategy to counter Trump's tariff threat has been pushing for a deeper relationship between Ontario and the United States, particularly around energy security. Ontario officials estimate the tariffs could cost half a million jobs.
Quebec International Relations Minister Martine Biron: Biron is headed south on what the Quebec government calls a "mission to Washington," during which she'll stress Quebec's shared interests with the U.S. She's due to attend an inauguration day reception hosted by Canada's ambassador to the U.S., Kirsten Hillman.
Liberal MP John McKay: McKay is co-chair Canada-United States Inter-Parliamentary Group and has met many American legislators over the years. McKay, who has been an MP since 1997, has said he won't run for office again.
With files from
The Associated Press
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: U.S. president-elect Donald Trump mused about using "economic force" to make Canada the 51st state.Alex Brandon The Canadian Press file photo

Document 46 of 249

Will fleeing U.S. residents head for Niagara border?: Immigration lawyer hears from those 'fearful' of Trump return

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/will-fleeing-u-s-residents-head-niagara-border/docview/3156766882/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Will+fleeing+U.S.+residents+head+for+Niagara+border%3F%3A+Immigration+lawyer+hears+from+those+%27fearful%27+of+Trump+return&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-01-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Niagara immigration lawyer Jonathon Jurmain says he's receiving calls from Americans "disillusioned" with Donald Trump's return to the White House.
And he's not alone.
A Vancouver-based immigration law firm has been getting so many inquiries it has created a website called "Trumpugees.ca."
"I think they're all profoundly depressed and troubled and fearful of the future, or just sick of it all and want to get out," said Jurmain, a 27-year Thorold professional.
"Mostly I've been getting contacted from relatively well-heeled individuals because they're the ones who are capable of just picking up and (leaving)."
Although he does not want to turn away business, Jurmain said he wants to be honest with potential clients.
"There are only so many doors to walk through and I'm not going to take money from people if that door is going to be real hard to open, if not impossible," he said.
"If you want to come in through legitimate immigration avenues into Canada, now is a particularly tough time to be doing that."
Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC) processes immigration applications in the country.
Jurmain said points are awarded based on particular qualifications when determining immigration applications, including age, language, education and work experience.
"Age is a big one. Most of the people contacting me are older and you start losing points under express entry at age 30 - so if you are 50, you're at zero."
He said some people think they can just purchase a home in Canada and live there, but there is a prohibition on the purchase of residential property by anybody who is not a citizen or a permanent resident.
There are exceptions, but they involve getting some kind of status such as work and study permits.
Jurmain said Canada's immigration economic stream is premised upon working.
"You combine all this somewhat difficult hurdle with the fact they (IRCC) have raised the pass mark very high ... it is difficult even for qualified people to qualify right now," he said.
"You really, in a lot of situations, need employment already established in Canada. And the government just announced they're going to reduce the number of immigrants by like 100,000 (this year)."
Jurmain said the chances of success are greater for younger Americans.
"The younger you are, the more likely this could occur because that opens up jobs and if you're an American citizen, especially, you have a way to bypass some of the (hurdles) that most other people have to go through to get jobs in Canada."
What about when, or if, Trump carries out his promise of mass deportations of undocumented immigrants? Is Niagara - a border region with four international crossings - ready for a possible influx of asylum seekers?
Jurmain said it's "extremely difficult" to predict what's going to happen, but there's a chance people with family members in Canada may try to come.
"Obviously, we have a major refugee crossing border in Fort Erie and we have our own refugee process," he said.
Fort Erie Multicultural Centre offers several settlement services for newcomers to Canada. One of them is the Peace Bridge Newcomer Centre, which is a refugee reception centre at the Peace Bridge in Fort Erie.
The centre provides hospitality for refugee claimants while they are being processed for their eligibility by Canada Border Services Agency, said the multicultural centre's website.
Claimants receive information, orientation and referrals regarding next steps in the Canadian refugee claim process and, as needed, assistance with transportation options and housing referrals
Jurmain said people with family members in Canada who "for whatever reason just didn't come (before) are probably going to come."
"That would include people without status, but it will also include people with Temporary Protected Status in the U.S. if Trump does what we wants to do, which is deport all those people and just eliminate TPS status because there's a lot of people with that," he said.
"All those people who technically have status in the U.S., but could have their status ripped away, there will probably be a great number of those with family in Canada who could make claims."
However, Jurmain said Canada and the U.S. have tightened their Safe Third Country agreement, which prevents any refugee claimant from making a claim in Canada unless the individuals meets an exception.
"The exceptions are almost exclusively based on having a family member of a particular degree of (consequence) with status in Canada. Unaccompanied minors are also an exception," he said.
"But another major exception was if you were able to enter Canada not through a normal border crossing, the Safe Third Country did not apply to you. That's why we had Roxham Road, with the many tens of thousands of people entering Canada via Roxham Road."
That exception has been closed.
Roxham Road was a well-travelled unofficial border crossing - between Champlain, N.Y., and Saint-Bernard-de-Lacolle, Que. - used by asylum seekers hoping to enter Canada.
"Now, the only exception outside of the ones in the agreement are if you get into Canada and you can stay under the radar for 14 days, then you can make a claim," said Jurmain.
"That's going to be a significant hurdle for a lot of people, especially if (the Canadian government) does what was just promised, which is to spend ($1.3 billion) on border enforcement," he said.
"It's going to be a lot harder to do that, to just come across, stay under the radar for two weeks and then make a claim because if you can't do that, then you can't make a claim. It's going to prevent all these people - only ones with family in Canada have access to claims."
Since Trump said he will impose a 25 per cent tariff on all Canadian and Mexican imports the day he takes office, unless those countries curb the flow of drugs and migrants across their borders, the Canadian federal and Ontario provincial governments have made several announcements about ways they plan to strengthen the border.
For example, the Canadian government announced it is investing $1.3 billion to bolster security at the border and strengthen the country's immigration system.
The Ontario government announced it will beef up security along its border with the U.S. The move, dubbed Operation Deterrence, will see some 200 Ontario Provincial Police officers focused on boosting border security. The OPP has been increasing patrols along the vast border using airplanes, helicopters, drones, boats and patrol vehicles.
Syed Hussan, executive director for Migrant Workers Alliance for Change, said his organization's assessment is there will not be a "massive wave" of people coming to Canada from United States.
"We've been talking to a lot of migrant justice groups in the U.S. and we've been asking them what they are hearing - just so we could be prepared to support people," he said.
"People are saying they will move to places like California where there are way more support systems than, for example, Canada."
Migrant Workers Alliance for Change is a coalition aiming to improve working and living conditions for live-in caregivers, seasonal agricultural workers and other temporary foreign workers.
"Generally speaking, people who are in the United States, when they're deported, return to the U.S. That's because they have families in the U.S., they have jobs in the U.S., they have communities in the U.S., they've been there for a long time," said Hussan.
"Packing up and moving to another country, even back to their home countries, is not an option that people want, much less coming to Canada."
He said it's "much harder" to get a cash job in Canada, as well as health care, education and a driver's licence than it is in much of the U.S.
"Canada is incredibly difficult to get basic supports as an undocumented person. It's not easy to come to Canada. It's also not easy to stay if you're undocumented. It's also not easy to apply for refugee status," said Hussan.
"If you are entering Canada - you can enter via a land crossing on a visa and then apply for an asylum claim, which is also quite difficult. Coming here on foot, also quite difficult."
He said between January and September, 13,000 to 17,000 people crossing from Canada to the U.S. were detained by U.S. border officers.
"On the other hand, people coming from the U.S. into Canada, (Canada Border Services Agency) isn't able to give an exact number, but it's certainly hovering between 2,000 and 3,000," said Hussan.
"The majority of outflow of migrants is from Canada to the U.S., not the other way around. This is why President Trump has been raising the spectre of the 25 per cent tariffs in part talking about migrants coming from Canada into the U.S."
He said his group organizes for migrants in Canada and is more concerned about Canadian policy that will see 2.3 million migrants "kicked out of the country" in the next two years.
"Trump is saying that he will kick out one million per year, so in fact Canada is going higher than Trump because, remember, the population of the U.S. is 10 times higher than Canada, so two million here is as if it was 20 million there," said Hussan.
"What we are most concerned about is not what will Trump do, who is part of the right-wing Republican party, but rather what the centre-left, self-described pro-immigrant prime minister of Canada is doing and what that will do to the flow of people in either direction."
He said the Liberal government, "despite its many challenges today," can return to its "principles" of putting forward pro-worker policies.
"You shouldn't kick 2.3 million people out of the country. The Liberals promised regularization, which is PR for undocumented people - they've not delivered it. They promised family work permits for our migrant workers - they've not delivered it. They promised to put in place a permanent residency program for domestic care workers - they've not delivered it."
Stefan Dolgert, associate professor of political science at Brock University, said during Trump's first term as president, Canada was more open to accepting immigrants, but public opinion has shifted "dramatically" in recent years.
"It does concern me that immigrants are being blamed in Canada for a lot of things that are not really mainly immigrant problems," he said, pointing to increased crime and the housing crisis.
Dolgert is an immigrant himself, originally from the U.S. and now a dual citizen of America and Canada.
"The majority of Canadians now feel that immigration has been too much, so I don't think you're going to see politicians of any party really embracing the possible opportunities (of people leaving the U.S. for Canada)," he said.
Georganne Burke, chapter lead of Republicans Overseas in Canada, said she believes Trump will follow through on his promise to deport illegal criminals.
"He's not deporting everybody. He's going to deport criminals, people who have criminal records who are in the United States illegally, people who have committed crimes in the United States and are illegally there or legally there and should have been deported long ago," she said.
"Do we want those people to come into Canada? I don't really think they're the ideal person that we're looking for."
Burke is a dual U.S-Canadian citizen and a seasoned veteran of political campaigns in both the U.S. and Canada. She is also senior vice-president of Pathway Group, a government relations consulting firm.
Burke said Niagara officials should "pressure" the Canadian federal government to "ensure the border is going to be carefully monitored."
"That people are not just going to be allowed to come because they say, 'I'm a refugee from the United States.' No, you've been deported from the United States, deported back to where you came from, so that's where you need to go," she said.
"The job of Niagara's regional government and local city government is to make sure that the people who control what happens at the border - that their federal representatives and their provincial representatives who do have some input into immigration - be aware that Niagara Falls is not going to put up with being made a target for illegal immigrants to come in."
Hugo Chesshire, director of policy and government relations with Greater Niagara Chamber of Commerce, said his agency is part of a cross-border coalition focused on "thinning" the border between Canada and the U.S.
"One of the policy goals of the Trump government seems to be that he wants that border to be thicker and ... Canada did promise substantial investment in border security," he said.
"Is this going to mean more delays at the border? More inspections? More technology? It is definitely a concern if the border gets thicker, if it becomes harder to go into Canada from the U.S. and vice versa. That is going to impact tourism traffic as we saw during the pandemic with the ArriveCan app. That had an impact on tourism because it was an additional layer of security."
Niagara Falls Tourism president Janice Thomson said tourism and border officials have worked hard over the years to ensure "tourism and the free flow of goods and services is maintained as a priority."
While there have been recent announcements by the Canadian and Ontario governments around increased security at the border, Thomson said she's hopeful it won't negatively impact people crossing Niagara's international bridges.
"Let's hope that we maintain those friendly borders and the open and safe and secure crossings that have been put in place," she said. "There are new initiatives being taken on the Canadian side and it's all positive in terms of making sure that we can welcome people safely."
Thomson said tourism officials were "gratified" to see the destination attract 3.2 million U.S. visitors last year.
"We need to remain focused on serving those people that are travelling."
But whatever the future holds on tourism, tariffs, immigration or border security, Trump will begin to determine our fates and whether Canada needs to react following his Monday inauguration.
CAPTION: The Rainbow Bridge between Niagara Falls, Ont., and Niagara Falls, N.Y.Jonathon Jurmain is an immigration lawyer with Jurmain Law Office in Thorold.Roxham Road was a well-travelled unofficial border crossing In Quebec used by asylum seekers hoping to enter Canada.A rally is held outside Niagara Falls city hall in March 2023 in a show of support for thousands of asylum seekers who had been placed in hotels in the municipality by Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada.Syed Hussan, executive director for Migrant Workers Alliance for Change says "it's not easy to come to Canada. It's also not easy to stay if you're undocumented." Readers write Letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write-letters-editor/docview/3155987976/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write+Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Unreal GO promises
Re: Ontario NDP leader commits to Grimsby GO station, double track to Niagara Falls, Jan. 10
The other day, Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles, with Niagara Falls MPP Wayne Gates and others, staged a photo op promising all-day GO trains to Niagara if she becomes premier.
Not a chance.
Former premier Dalton McGuinty made the same promise in 2010 and Bob Rae did so before. It's not happening.
Premier Doug Ford has increased the weekday service to six trains, the same as CN when it had passenger trains.
Shortly, the Stoney Creek station will open, while the region is spending $32 million on the west St. Catharines GO project.
Progress is happening, but a fact needs to be pointed out: we do not have passenger numbers to justify all-day service while Hamilton to Oshawa, Barrie and Stouffville do.
Service will not happen today, but it is coming.
Kevin Argue, St. Catharines
We're seeking
sacrificial lambs
Re: Here's who's running to replace Justin Trudeau. We're tracking all the contenders for Liberal party leader, Jan. 14
Remember all you Liberal leadership candidates what your goal is as the temporarily anointed prime minister of Canada.
Should you succeed and lead your party into the next election, your role is to be a greater sacrificial lamb than Kim Campbell was when she replaced a fleeing Brian Mulroney for the then-Progressive Conservatives.
No party has suffered a greater loss. Work on it.
Ron St. Louis, Welland
Why is Trudeau
so hated?
Re: Newsflash: Justin Trudeau has not created a crisis. Politics in Ottawa are working as they should, Jan. 14.
So the Justin Trudeau bashers have got what they desire - he is stepping down.
I've been wondering what it was that made his detractors so angry. Does it go back to his standing up to the outlaws who occupied our nation's capital during the COVID-19 crisis?
It seemed impossible to get a clear answer as to why they despised him so. I think it was largely a bandwagon effect: everybody else hates him, so I'd better hop on board.
Were they aware of his many accomplishments or did they even care? Canada is better off economically than the other G7 nations. He took us through the pandemic effectively. He began reconciliation with our Indigenous Peoples and initiated affordable child care, better gun control, he made a start at fighting the impending climate disasters.
Dental care and pharmacare have been initiated for some and will be expanded. Canada has an excellent reputation internationally.
P.P. is his main political opponent - this could be Pierre Poilievre or maybe the Pied Piper, who asks us to follow his mantra of "Axe the tax; Canada is broken; Axe the tax; Canada is broken."
We don't know where he will lead us because he won't tell us. Oh, Canada.
David Fowler, Wainfleet

Document 48 of 249

Much more money needed to help homeless, New Democrat MPPs say: 'How can you walk past people sleeping on concrete and feel good?': Stevens

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/much-more-money-needed-help-homeless-new-democrat/docview/3155986167/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Much+more+money+needed+to+help+homeless%2C+New+Democrat+MPPs+say%3A+%27How+can+you+walk+past+people+sleeping+on+concrete+and+feel+good%3F%27%3A+Stevens&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Niagara's New Democrat MPPs are calling for immediate action after a new Association of Municipalities of Ontario (AMO) report warns homelessness will get much worse in the next decade unless significant steps are taken to address the issue.
They claim the Conservative government's efforts so far fall short.
The report, released Thursday, said more than 80,000 Ontarians were unhoused in 2024 - an increase of about 25 per cent in just two years.
Without significant intervention, that number could grow to nearly 300,000 people by 2035, it said.
AMO recommends an increase in investments in the unhoused by $11 billion during the next 10 years to boost the supply of affordable housing, improve transitional and supportive services and enhance prevention programs.
The organization's report said a further investment of $2 billion over eight years could eliminate most encampments.
The full report is on AMO's website.
St. Catharines MPP Jennie Stevens called it the first provincewide report of its kind, which brings together contributions from 47 service managers across Ontario. "It really shed a light on the state of the homelessness crisis in Ontario," she said. "It really magnifies it and raises an eyebrow."
Stevens said the crisis has placed "immense pressure" on municipalities, who footed the bill for 51 per cent of the $4.1 billion provided for homelessness initiatives in Ontario by all levels of government in 2024, according to the report.
"They're trying to do it all with small pockets of money given to them, in comparison to what the (Premier Doug) Ford government has. (It has) deep pockets," she said.
And when municipalities are collectively spending billions of dollars to address homelessness issues, Stevens said, "they can't focus on parks, roads, sewers, infrastructure."
Niagara Centre MPP Jeff Burch, the NDP's municipal affairs critic, said the crisis is the result of decades of the provincial government abdicating responsibility for housing.
"It's gotten worse and worse, so now property taxes are funding what should be a provincial responsibility," he said. "It puts more pressure on things like rent prices and availability of affordable housing when you've got property taxes on top of everything else getting more expensive."
Niagara Falls MPP Wayne Gates said Ford has "allowed this crisis to develop under his watch. We need to build more into affordable housing, pass real rent control and provide municipalities and social service organizations with the funding and resources they need to help people in crisis," he added.
Niagara West Tory MPP Sam Oosterhoff said his government "has made the largest investment in affordable housing and anti-homelessness programs in Ontario's history and has taken decisive action to support municipalities in dealing with homeless encampments across the province.
"We have heard loud and clear from the people of Ontario that they want their parks and public spaces back," he said. "Encampments are a public safety concern and not a solution to homelessness. We believe that people experiencing homelessness should have access to proper support and stable housing."
Emma Testani, press secretary for Housing Minister Paul Calandra, said the provincial government is calling on the federal government to pay its "fair share" and take responsibility for the consequences of its policies that have escalated the number of individuals facing homelessness.
"Mayors have asked us for help in managing this complex issue and that is why over the next three years we're investing a record $3 billion in affordable housing, anti-homelessness and emergency shelter supports," she said. "Just last month, we announced an additional targeted investment of $75 million to help connect those (individuals) living in encampments with needed housing."
Oosterhoff said $62 million will be spent over three years in Niagara through the Homelessness Prevention Program to support Niagara Region's initiatives - and will increase funding for the region by 86 per cent.
"This increase almost doubles provincial funding for supportive housing in Niagara and is part of our plan to address the housing crisis across the province," Oosterhoff said.
But Stevens said the investments pale in comparison to the $11-billion to $13-billion boost during the next decade recommended by AMO. "When you read this report and you see 81,000 people experienced homelessness in Ontario, how can you walk past people sleeping on concrete and feel good?" she said.
Burch said the province has "failed on housing, there's no question about that.
"There are more homeless folks than ever before and their entire approach to housing has been a complete failure," he said.
CAPTION: This encampment is set up along a berm near railway tracks along Bridge Street in Niagara Falls.St. Catharines NDP MPP Jennie Stevensmetroland file photo
