Document 49 of 249

Is Niagara ready for the second coming of Trump?: Local experts cautiously weigh in on an unpredictable economic future

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/is-niagara-ready-second-coming-trump/docview/3155986009/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+Niagara+ready+for+the+second+coming+of+Trump%3F%3A+Local+experts+cautiously+weigh+in+on+an+unpredictable+economic+future&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Serious threat or negotiating tactic?
Plotting to overtake a neighbouring country and NATO ally, or is he simply trolling?
There's a lot of uncertainty about how U.S. president-elect Donald Trump's second term may impact Niagara, including his threat to impose sweeping 25 per cent tariffs on goods and services coming from Canada and Mexico.
That second term starts Monday, with Trump's inauguration as 47th president of the United States in a ceremony in Washington, D.C.
Should Niagara be worried? The experts say yes, but cautiously.
More than 500 businesses in Niagara exported $5.9 billion in goods to the U.S in 2023, based on information from Statistics Canada's international trade division.
What would Trump's sweeping tariffs mean for a border region so deeply entrenched in trade with Canada's closest ally?
On another front, is Niagara ready for a possible influx of asylum seekers when, or if, Trump carries out his promise of mass deportations of undocumented immigrants?
The Niagara Falls Review reached out to business leaders, academics and immigration/refugee experts during the past several weeks to better understand how Trump's promises - both during his most recent successful presidential campaign and during the two eventful months since winning the election - may affect Niagara's economy and border.
While many agreed to be interviewed, others didn't feel comfortable speaking on the record about speculation, or feared their comments may fan the flames during such volatile and fluid times.
The times are so volatile and fluid it can be difficult to keep up with the fast-paced news cycle that churns out stories that seem to up the ante almost hourly about the state of the relationship between Canada and the U.S.
Some who agreed to interviews acknowledged trying to walk a fine line between discussing issues more generally and not wanting to provide politically-driven analysis of current affairs.
"Often (Trump) does mean what he says and does what he says, but he's also a true businessperson and he knows the value of a bluff and he knows how to negotiate and get what he wants, so it is hard to tell exactly how to respond to this," said Hugo Chesshire, director of policy and government relations with Greater Niagara Chamber of Commerce.
Said Blayne Haggart, associate professor of political science at Brock University: "At this point, it is just a threat. He has a tendency and history of making threats and then backing down or modifying them based on what he sees as appeasement.
"If I was betting, I would say something is going to happen on the first day - what that's going to be, we don't quite know yet."
Jonathan Jurmain, a Niagara immigration lawyer for more than 25 years added: "It is extremely difficult, in my opinion, to try to predict what's going to happen.
"Obviously, if he wants to deport two million people, there are going to be a lot of people who are going to want to come to Canada, far more than I'm sure we can absorb easily. However, Canada has recently, even before the (Nov. 5 U.S.) election, kind of bolstered its walls and its boundaries that are going to make it much more difficult for any of these people to get over here."
It all started when a Nov. 25 social media post by Trump said he will impose a 25 per cent tariff on all Canadian and Mexican imports the day he takes office, unless those countries curb the flow of drugs and migrants across their borders.
The post set off an international firestorm.
That fire is still burning. In fact, it's spreading.
In the weeks since, Trump has repeatedly suggested Canada become America's 51st state and referred to Canada's prime minister as a governor.
Many people initially took it as a joke after Prime Minister Justin Trudeau flew to Trump's Mar-a-Lago home days after the president-elect's tariff threat.
But Trump has since escalated his American expansionism rhetoric by saying he would consider using "economic force" - ruling out military force - to lay claim to Canada, while not ruling out using military force to take over the Panama Canal and Greenland.
Despite national and provincial leaders insisting Canada will never become America's newest state, they have taken Trump's tariff threat more seriously.
The Canadian government announced Dec. 16 it is investing $1.3 billion to bolster security at the border and strengthen the country's immigration system. That same day, Canada's premiers gathered to discuss ways to address Trump's tariff threat.
An embattled Trudeau - facing both internal and external pressures - announced Jan. 6 he was proroguing Parliament until March 24 and will resign as prime minister after a decade in power, and after 12 years as Liberal leader, once his party selects a new flag-bearer.
The Ontario government announced Jan. 7 it will beef up security along its border with the U.S. The move, dubbed Operation Deterrence, will see some 200 Ontario Provincial Police officers focused on boosting border security. The OPP has been increasing patrols along the vast border using airplanes, helicopters, drones, boats and patrol vehicles.
The following day, Ontario Premier Doug Ford said he wants to better integrate energy systems with the U.S. to avert a trade war, pitching a "Fortress Am-Can" alliance fuelled by domestic energy production.
But not before Ford previously threatened to cutoff electricity from Ontario to homes in several states.
There are also reports Canada is readying counter-tariffs on a list of American products should Trump follow through on his threat, while also not ruling out cutting off energy exports to the U.S.
Alberta Premier Danielle Smith visited Trump at his Florida home this past weekend as a guest of Canadian entrepreneur and television personality Kevin O'Leary.
Smith said she received no indication from Trump he plans to back down on implementing hefty tariffs on Canadian products. But she said cutting off oil and gas exports to the south in retaliation isn't feasible.
Foreign Affairs Minister Mélanie Joly is scheduled to visit Washington this week to press the incoming U.S. administration not to impose tariffs on Canada.
Tomorrow, Part 2: How Trump's second term could impact Niagara's economy and trade
Saturday, Part 3: How Trump's second term could impact Niagara's border and immigration

Document 50 of 249

The gentrification of Queenston Street: 'No one can afford rent anymore,' one neighbour worries

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/gentrification-queenston-street/docview/3155545987/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+gentrification+of+Queenston+Street%3A+%27No+one+can+afford+rent+anymore%2C%27+one+neighbour+worries&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-01-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Derelict boarded up buildings, redevelopment projects and high rents are some signs worrying residents in St. Catharines' Queenston Street neighbourhood.
Terri-Lynn Van Blaricum, 35, a single mother of four on Ontario Works who grew up in the area and is renting her childhood home on Vine Street from her father, said fears of rising rents have many people, including herself, worried about where they will live if the area becomes gentrified.
"With other people in the neighbourhood, who I know struggle on assistance, no one can afford rent anymore ... I got lucky because of my father, but other people, they don't have that," she said.
Worries abound for Van Blaricum as she could face a similar fate as her neighbours. Her aging father is set to retire.
"It's coming to the point where he must retire, and his finances are going to be different," she said.
"Eventually, I'm going to be stuck in the same situation as everybody else and with this (possible gentrification) coming now, it makes it worse," she said.
Gentrification is a process that sees the character of a poorer neighbourhood changed as more affluent people move in, displacing residents through higher-priced housing and new businesses.
The Queenston neighbourhood on the edge of the city's downtown has traditionally been a bastion of working-class families, but over decades has become home to low-income individuals and families, older adults on fixed incomes and people on government assistance.
One large area of the neighbourhood likely to undergo change in the coming years is the former St. Catharines General Hospital property, part of Merritton ward, which was purchased by a Kitchener-based real estate developer.
The 4.86-hectare site was bought by Vive Development Corp. on Nov. 4, 2024.
Vive chief development officer Stephen Litt did not respond to interview requests for this story, but in an interview with The Standard in early November said the plan is to use the property for much-needed rental units.
He added the company wants to have a planning application into the city by February and start construction as early as this year, but estimated the development could take 10 years to complete.
Syl Roach, a Queenston Neighbourhood Association housing committee member, said historically the neighbourhood has been affordable.
"Gentrification, of course, is a business plan for corporate investors, for the buying and replacing or renovating of cheap real estate, which is what we must call those boarded up houses in the Queenston Street area," she said.
"It has displaced not only low-income tenants, but also small businesses."
Roach sees this displacement of residents as a way to get rid of people perceived as less desirable community members, shuffling them off to other neighbourhoods, if not becoming homeless.
"We're seeing it now. When the more affluent population comes in with different needs, the first thing they do is they want the neighbourhood cleaned up," she said.
"They don't want to see the poor people and they don't want to see the problems that they live with, but these are vulnerable people who deserve more than being pushed out of their homes, and that's what it is."
Roach said this is no more evident than by a petition that came from highrise residents at 7 Gale Cres., imploring Premier Doug Ford to use the notwithstanding clause to deal with homelessness.
"It wasn't about the people, it was about cleaning up the neighbourhood. My goal is to see rent-geared-to-income (RGI) housing for our most vulnerable people first," she said.
"We know gentrification is coming, let's be proactive."
Paul Miller, Westminster United Church minister and Start Me Up Niagara board chair, said what he sees, despite rents increasing across the board, is Queenston remaining a more affordable place for working families in St. Catharines than other neighbourhoods.
"It's still possible for a working family to manage to find an affordable place to live there," he said. "But it's under a lot of stress, and then you layer on top of that all the social problems."
Miller said what is happening is not unique to St. Catharines. The Queenston neighbourhood is a "microcosm" encapsulating what is occurring in many of Ontario's larger cities.
"You'd start off moving to Queenston, you'd rent, you'd buy a house and then you'd move on," he said.
"The city is full of people who grew up there, whose families got established there. If we lost that, I don't know where people in St. Catharines would go to live."
Michael Ripmeester, a Brock University geography and tourism studies professor, said gentrification can occur in two specific, yet completely different, ways.
One is the gradual growing popularity of a neighbourhood attracting people and eventually developers, he said, or there can be a less nuanced approach.
"We've seen cities working to jump-start the process by offering developers incentives to build condos in areas where neighbourhoods are in some trouble, or they're economically down and it works in some cases where the positive attributes take," he said.
What happens in that case, Ripmeester said, is people living in the area because of cheaper rents are displaced.
That's an issue for him, as it poses a problem for following or studying people from gentrified areas moving to their next destination.
"I know of no studies able to track where people displaced by gentrification end up," he said.
"That's a problem, in the sense we don't know where they're going and whether they're able to find housing or whether this pushes them into homelessness."
This impacts many aspects of people's livelihoods, Ripmeester said, including their sense of belonging.
"(What) happens to people who are displaced, not only are they displaced from their homes, but they're displaced from their communities."
Ripmeester equated the process to a diaspora - a scattering of people, in this case - from their neighbourhood as they search for adequate and affordable housing.
"What you see is people looking and saying, 'Well, where can I find a place that I can live?' So, it may not even be in the same city," he said.
"It may mean moving from St. Catharines to Wainfleet, Fort Erie or other places where the rents might be a little lower."
With the ongoing housing crisis, Ripmeester said, what is needed more than the gentrification of the Queenston neighbourhood is to "provide housing for people who need it most."
"Housing people can afford or that can be subsidized," he said.
"If we look at other places, Finland is a good example, where they said ... 'we will build housing for the people who need it,' and that ... certainly helped the homelessness problem in Helsinki."
Van Blaricum said many neighbourhood characteristics she grew up with are at risk of being lost.
Van Blaricum said the neighbourhood is "close knit" and fears that will be lost.
"It's like when a big business moves into the mom-and-pop restaurant, you don't have the nice people looking out for each other," she said.
"I know if I left my door unlocked, I could trust my neighbour to lock my door, that's how much I trust these people around me."
With files from
Karena Walter
CAPTION: Syl Roach worries gentrification will displace many people living in the Queenston Street neighbourhood of St. Catharines as buildings are being boarded up, redeveloped or demolished.Bob Tymczyszyn St. Catharines Standard

CREDIT: Matthew P. Barker Reporter With files from Karena Walter

Document 51 of 249

Welland fairgrounds sold for $40M to housing developer: Niagara Regional Agricultural Society president remains mum on sale to Mountainview

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/welland-fairgrounds-sold-40m-housing-developer/docview/3155545374/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Welland+fairgrounds+sold+for+%2440M+to+housing+developer%3A+Niagara+Regional+Agricultural+Society+president+remains+mum+on+sale+to+Mountainview&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-01-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Welland's fairgrounds have a new owner after the Niagara Regional Agricultural Society sold the property for nearly $40 million to Mountainview Fairgrounds Developments Inc. last November.
The 21-hectare parcel of land at 1100 Niagara St. on Welland's border with Thorold has been in NRAS' hands since 1967, when the then-Welland County Agricultural Society purchased it for $60,000.
Reached Monday, NRAS president Colin Bowman said he had no comment on the sale.
"There will be a press release from the buyer and ourselves, we're just not sure when."
He said the sale will not be discussed during the agricultural society's annual general meeting, slated for Sunday at the Welland Community Centre on Lincoln Street.
Society members will discuss minutes of previous general and special meetings, go over last year's activities, hear an auditor's financial review report, appoint a new auditor and elect new directors.
Following the general meeting, directors will hold a special meeting behind closed doors to discuss a replacement property purchase for the Niagara Regional Exhibition.
The Tribune learned of the sale by searching Ontario Land Registry Office records, which showed both the sale price of $39,939,750 and the property's new owner.
Mountainview Fairgrounds Developments Inc. was incorporated in mid-October 2024 with a registered head office at 3340 Schmon Pky., Unit 1, in Thorold. The address is the same as Mountainview Building Group, which has housing and condominium projects across Welland, the Niagara region and beyond.
The new company has two directors, Mark Basciano and Michael Memme. Basciano and Memme are also active officers, along with Jodi Lycett.
No one from Mountainview could be reached for comment Monday.
The fairgrounds feature a 3,715-square-metre exhibition building, which has been home to an indoor tennis club and the Bullettproof Baseball Academy. The site also hosted the World Electronic Music Festival in 2007, has been used for trade shows and, of course, the annual NRE.
The origins of the fair date back to 1832, connected with the history of the Three-County Society, comprising the communities of Haldimand, Lincoln and Welland.
It was formed to improve agricultural methods across Niagara and encourage domestic farm production.
With the society fracturing in 1853 due to the passing of a governmental law allowing division for large counties, a separate branch of the association called the Welland County Agricultural Society was formed in Port Robinson that same year.
From that point, it would be the organization responsible for continuing the tradition.
The fair moved to the Niagara Street site from Denistoun Street in 1975.
The north-end property, host to demolition derbies, dog shows, rib festivals, car shows and the fair, backs onto subdivisions in Thorold to the east, and is next door to Welland Sports Complex.
Talk of its sale began a year ago, following a special meeting of NRAS directors in December 2023, when a realtor was in attendance.
An annual general meeting scheduled for January 2024 was put off until March as society members, Niagara Centre NDP MPP Jeff Burch and Ontario Association of Agricultural Societies District 6 representatives questioned the sale and whether the agricultural society had enough members to carry out votes.
Just before the 2024 AGM, a news release from the society said the property had been sold to an undisclosed buyer at an undisclosed price with a potential October closing date.
At the time, Bowman said the fair would not shut down and would continue to operate no matter where it lands. He also said there were no plans to dissolve the society.
Society members had heard the exhibition had three-to-five years left at the Niagara Street fairgrounds.
On its Facebook page, the society has an event listed for June 7 called Mudbogz for ATVs and SXSs, which are side-by-side off-road vehicles also known as utility task vehicles (UTVs).
CAPTION: Welland's fairgrounds are now owned by Thorold company Mountainview Fairgrounds Developments Inc. after it purchased the Niagara Street property from Niagara Regional Agricultural Society for just under $40 million.Dave Johnson Welland Tribune

Document 52 of 249

Relations with U.S. 'tenuous': Redekop: Niagara mayors hope federal and provincial officials consult border community leaders

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/relations-with-u-s-tenuous-redekop/docview/3155061118/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Relations+with+U.S.+%27tenuous%27%3A+Redekop%3A+Niagara+mayors+hope+federal+and+provincial+officials+consult+border+community+leaders&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-01-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Tenuous.
That's how Fort Erie Mayor Wayne Redekop labelled the Canada-U.S. relationship in the midst of president-elect Donald Trump's ongoing tariff threat and remarks about absorbing Canada.
Niagara Falls Mayor Jim Diodati, meanwhile, said ties are "a little bit on the ropes."
Redekop said he agrees with Sarnia Mayor Mike Bradley that federal and provincial government leaders should speak to border communities about issues they could face from a second Trump administration.
"If you talk about Fort Erie, Windsor, Sarnia, you're talking about more than half of the trade that goes between the two countries," said Redekop. "Those are large numbers in terms of money and manufacturing and jobs."
Bradley spoke with CBC on the weekend about Trump's threats to Canada. He described the relationship between the two countries as "deteriorating," adding people are saying they're going to avoid heading to the U.S.
Sarnia shares a border with Port Huron, Mich. Fort Erie shares a border with Buffalo, N.Y.
Redekop said while he doesn't see any issues with the personal relationships between Canadians and Americans - it's not uncommon for many Niagara residents to cross into Buffalo to watch Sabres hockey games, Bills football games, shop or just enjoy pizza and wings - the situation is not the same for Ottawa and Washington.
"It's troubling because typically heads of state or incoming heads of state don't make comments about the internal affairs of other states. That, of course, is a rule that's among many rules Donald Trump doesn't recognize," he said.
"He just kind of speaks first and thinks later."
Redekop said the notion Canada could become America's 51st state "obviously doesn't make sense.
"There's a variety of things that you can point to, to indicate what is different about us and what we value and how those values are different," he said.
Redekop said he hopes federal and provincial leaders can "diffuse the likelihood" tariffs are imposed.
"One of the things that Fort Erie boasts in terms of economic development is that we're on the border of the greatest trading partner that we have in the world," he said.
"We have companies that manufacture goods and do export to the United States - some of them a large portion of the goods that they manufacture. (Tariffs would) have an impact on them because it will increase the cost of their goods to consumers in the States, and unless they've got virtually no competition for those goods, it will provide their competitors a leg up in terms of costs."
Diodati, whose city shares a border with Niagara Falls, N.Y., said he would like to see border-community mayors brought to the table to be part of the solution.
"I don't think one mercurial president is going to change centuries of relations," he said.
"I think sometimes this is (Trump's) strategy of shock and awe kind of negotiations where he'll say some far out comment just to get a reaction, and I think it's important that we don't overreact. We've got to just stay calm and cool."
At the same time, Diodati said Canada should also take a business approach to negotiating with Trump.
"I'm in favour of (Ontario) Premier (Doug) Ford's approach. He came out swinging and then he calmed down - he did the same kind of thing that president-elect Trump did, and I think that's how businesspeople negotiate," he said.
Diodati described the current Canada-U.S. political relationship as being "maybe a little bit on the ropes right now," adding the situation is not helped by Prime Minister Justin Trudeau announcing he will resign once a new federal Liberal is chosen.
"Our premiers of both Alberta and Ontario have been the two leaders right now, and I'm proud of them for stepping forward and building on that, and I think they're going about it the right way."
Niagara-on-the-Lake Lord Mayor Gary Zalepa said although Trump will be sworn in as president for a second term next Monday, "I don't see one set of opinions as something that's going to jeopardize such a long-standing relationship.
"I think our relationship with the United States goes much deeper than that. But it does worry me and I think Canadians and border residents in our community are worried about that kind of talk," he said.
"But I don't think we're at the point where our relationship is in jeopardy. I think, in fact, this is when our relationship is going to show its true value."
Zalepa, whose town shares a border with Lewiston, N.Y., credited federal and provincial leaders for taking a lead in addressing Trump's comments.
"I feel confident that that's going to work through a process and, hopefully, we can tone done the rhetoric and get to a more productive conversation on trade," he said.
"Sometimes these are a bit of negotiations and I think that's kind of exactly where we are right now, so I'm confident that we have some really good people that are on it."
CAPTION: The Peace Bridge connecting Fort Erie with Buffalo, N.Y., facilitates billions of dollars in annual trade between Canada and the United States.Niagara-on- the-Lake Lord Mayor Gary ZalepaNiagara Falls Mayor Jim Diodatimetroland file photo

Readers write Letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write-letters-editor/docview/3154670875/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write+Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-01-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Pierre's slogans
are not a plan
Trudeau is stepping down, but is Poilievre ready for the top job? Jan. 8
Obviously, Pierre Poilievre's attack dog antics will end now that Justin Trudeau is no longer leading the Liberals. Therefore, he presumably will stop acting like a petulant child and act like a leader.
However, to date, we have not heard specifics about his plans for Canada's future. Since the Conservatives are considered a lock to form the next federal government, it will be interesting to see details before we go to the polls.
So far I have heard "Axe the tax," "Build the homes," "Fix the budget" and "Stop the crime." Perhaps Mr. Poilievre has a magic wand to wave and these things happen, but I am somewhat of a skeptic and would prefer to see an action plan than hear empty slogans.
I do not consider Canada broken. We may need a few repairs, but we are in better shape than most countries, including our troubled southern neighbour.
I await Mr. Poilievre's vision for our future, as well as those of all other parties, before the next election.
David Rosier, St. Catharines
Better late than never
Niagara's Liberal MPs react to Trudeau's plan to resign, Jan. 6
To the end, Justin Trudeau is a phoney and goes out like a martyr to country and party.
If he was a martyr, he would have resigned years ago.
But late is better than never. I just hope we have a man with vision and true conviction who transcends politics and gets us back on track with Pierre Poilievre.
Wayne Borbey, Niagara Falls
Defending health care
Re: Ontario proposes requiring health staffing agencies to disclose their rates, Dec. 20
The Canada Health Act says health care must be publicly delivered without user fees or extra billing, but governments are encouraging private, for-profit surgical and other specialty clinics to open.
In Ontario, private for profit, yet publicly funded, MRI and CT scan clinics are expanding. This comes as part of the government's plan to expand the number of facilities offering services such as cataract surgeries, certain orthopedic surgeries and others.
This means additional costs and profits are covered by taxpayers, and less funding is available for hospitals. What this means for our public health system, a source of pride for Canadians, remains to be seen.
It can be a slippery slope to two-tiered care and unequal care, where those who pay get priority treatment, leaving others behind.
Our hospitals are running at 125 per cent capacity, and health-care workers are doing their best in a difficult situation. We should support them by advocating for sustainable funding, staffing and resources.
We need to defend our public health-care system. Government needs to reaffirm its commitment to the principles of the Canada Health Act - universality, comprehensiveness, portability, accessibility, public administration.
Robert Hicks, St. Catharines

Letters welcome
Send them to letters@niagaradailies.com or mail to PO Box 5031 Stn Main,
St. Catharines, Ont., L2R 7T4. Letters should be 250 words or less, and may be edited
for length and clarity. Please include your name, address and phone number for verification purposes.

Document 54 of 249

PM 'has done the right thing,' Liberal MPs say: Bittle and Badawey await new leader with fresh vision for Canadians

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pm-has-done-right-thing-liberal-mps-say/docview/3152068007/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=PM+%27has+done+the+right+thing%2C%27+Liberal+MPs+say%3A+Bittle+and+Badawey+await+new+leader+with+fresh+vision+for+Canadians&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-01-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Niagara's two Liberal members of Parliament were looking ahead Monday after Justin Trudeau announced he will be stepping aside for a new party leader and resigning as prime minister once one is chosen.
St. Catharines MP Chris Bittle said he felt mixed emotions because he was motivated to run for office after meeting Trudeau in 2013 when the now-PM was seeking leadership of the party.
"I'd really become disillusioned with politics after being involved in a few campaigns and he inspired me. He's a big reason why I ran in 2015," Bittle said, adding the party has had "incredible accomplishments since that time."
"He is a consequential prime minister and will be remembered fondly by history. At the same time, he's heard from caucus, he's heard from Canadians, we see the numbers where they sit in the polls, and I think he has done the right thing in stepping down."
Trudeau announced Monday outside Rideau Cottage he will resign as party leader and prime minister after a new Liberal leader is chosen, a move that came after growing calls for him to step aside within his own party. Parliament has been suspended until March 24.
During his remarks, he said he couldn't be the best option in the next election while fighting "internal battles." Removing himself from the position would "bring the temperature down."
Bittle agreed that Trudeau's stepping aside will be a good opportunity for Liberals to stand up and unite around a new leader and offer Canadians "a positive vision for a progressive future."
"We look to populist governments that are coming up, not only just in Canada but around the world, that speak to people's fears but don't offer anything but cuts, and we need to offer Canadians a better future," he said.
"We have to show them that we can continue to lead and that we deserve to lead. And so a new leader will have to step up and present that vision."
Niagara Centre MP Vance Badawey said while leadership transitions can create periods of uncertainty, he looks at them as an opportunity for fresh perspectives.
He said he'll be preparing for the upcoming shift in leadership as soon as candidates start announcing their intentions by advocating for Niagara's interests and ensuring projects progress without disruption.
"Having the prime minister make the announcement today on his resigning, there's no question that undoubtedly sparks significant political conversation," Badawey said.
"But from a local Niagara perspective, my focus remains squarely on continuing the important work that we've been doing for our communities. So I don't see a blip coming at all."
Badawey said whoever becomes the next leader will have a strong, supportive team.
"At the end of the day, when you look at who may be the next leader, the leader will draw his or her strength from the team. And that's the expectation I've always had."
Niagara's Conservative MPs, Niagara West's Dean Allison and Niagara Falls' Tony Baldinelli, did not respond to interview requests about the announcement Monday.
Baldinelli said in a previous interview he's prepared for the election this year, regardless of when it's called.
"It's got to happen this year, sooner or later, but it's coming," he said Saturday.
"The Liberals are the masters of their own misfortune, so we're seeing it play out. The economic conditions that we see, the 1,400 homeless encampments in Ontario, housing has doubled, rent has doubled."
Bittle pointed to local achievements during Trudeau's terms, including Asahi Kasei Corp. establishing a $1.56-billion electric vehicle battery separator plant in Port Colborne and its joint venture with Honda. He said the Liberal government's environmental policies helped attract the world-class companies.
"That will be transformative for the Niagara economy because we invested in environmental policy, because we took climate change seriously," Bittle said. "Those companies could have built a factory anywhere in the world, and they chose to build it here in Canada and to be at the forefront of that change as the global economy is changing."
He also pointed to the $28-million rehabilitation of the Port Dalhousie piers, which was a big issue in 2015 locally, and said Trudeau was incredibly supportive for the project that was outside the radar of the Department of Fisheries and Oceans.
But Bittle said Trudeau's biggest legacy will be as leader during the COVID-19 pandemic and getting the country through its biggest crisis since the Second World War.
He also pointed to the Canada Child Benefit, which he said lifted many kids out of poverty.
Still, it hasn't helped with the polls, but a new leader might.
"The polls have been stuck for a while, despite positive policy changes that I think, taken on their own, were very popular, but didn't do anything for our fortunes, especially looking at the last round of by elections," he said.
"It was clear that though Canadians may approve of the policies we've come forward with, they haven't come out to support the party and that's a problem."
With files from Allan Benner

Document 55 of 249

Trudeau stepping down, prorogues Parliament: PM said he asked Liberal party president Sachit Mehra Sunday night to immediately launch a leadership race ahead of the next election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-stepping-down-prorogues-parliament/docview/3152067688/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+stepping+down%2C+prorogues+Parliament%3A+PM+said+he+asked+Liberal+party+president+Sachit+Mehra+Sunday+night+to+immediately+launch+a+leadership+race+ahead+of+the+next+election&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-01-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As a tearful Justin Trudeau outlined his plans to resign as Liberal leader Monday, he put the country on track for an early election featuring a new party flag-bearer for the first time in a decade.
Once seen as the Liberal saviour who lifted a battered party brand up from the ashes, Trudeau came up against a groundswell of pressure from party rank-and-file to step aside as the public soured on his government and grew hungry for change.
The questions now are when exactly the election will come, and who will vie to become Trudeau's successor and the next to attempt the herculean feat of raising the party back up from the depths.
After more than a year of plummeting poll numbers and surging pressure from within his own caucus to step aside, Trudeau informed Canadians Monday he will resign as leader and prime minister as soon as a new leader is chosen.
"This country deserves a real choice in the next election and it has become clear to me that if I'm having to fight internal battles, I cannot be the best option in that election," Trudeau said outside his official residence in Ottawa Monday morning.
Trudeau said he reflected on his political future over the holidays and told his three kids about his decision over dinner Sunday.
He also said he met with Gov. Gen. Mary Simon on Monday morning and that she has agreed to his request to prorogue Parliament until March 24.
Trudeau had consistently signalled over the past year he intended to remain at the helm, but the decisive blow came when Chrystia Freeland suddenly resigned as minister of finance and deputy prime minister on Dec. 16, days after Trudeau had informed her he was going to move her out of the finance portfolio. Her departure, hours before she was to table the fall economic statement in the House of Commons, sent shock waves through the governing party.
Questions about Trudeau's future have swirled since support for his party began to tumble in 2023. The Liberals have trailed the Conservatives by more than 20 points for more than a year.
Trudeau said he asked for Parliament to be prorogued because the House of Commons has been paralyzed for months through obstruction and needs a reset. This move will shutter the House for two months, wipe clear the current slate of legislation and delay any opportunities for non-confidence votes that could trigger an election until it resumes in spring.
"It's time for the temperature to come down, for people to have a fresh start in Parliament to be able to navigate through these complex times domestically and internationally," he said.
"Removing me from the equation as the leader who will fight the next election for the Liberal party should also decrease the level of polarization we're seeing right now in the House and in Canadian politics."
Trudeau said he asked Liberal party president Sachit Mehra Sunday night to immediately launch a leadership race ahead of the next election.
Mehra could not be reached for an interview, but said in a statement he will call a national board meeting this week to begin the "nation-wide democratic process of selecting a new leader of the party." A concluding date for that race has not yet been set.
The spotlight now will be cast on long-suspected leadership aspirants such as Freeland, Finance Minister Dominic LeBlanc, former central banker Mark Carney, Foreign Minister Mélanie Joly, Industry Minister François-Philippe Champagne and a cast of others.
Candidates will have to scramble to launch speedy campaigns as they jostle under tight time constraints to organize and claim the mantle as the best to take on popular firebrand Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre.
Liberal MP Wayne Long, who had agitated for Trudeau to leave, said the prime minister could have figured out that he had passed his political expiration date a lot sooner. But his decision to finally step aside marks day-one of the party's rebuild and will give the party a fighting chance in the next election.
"This shouldn't be a Pierre Poilievre coronation," he said.
While the next election must be held by this October, spring or early summer are much more likely given the precarious minority Parliament that has all three main opposition parties pronouncing they're ready to bring the government down in a confidence vote.
Poilievre sought to cast Trudeau's move as a desperate political play by a sputtering Liberal party, whose MPs stood by their leader right up until he cratered in the polls and was no longer a viable candidate.
"Their only objection is that he is no longer popular enough to win an election and keep them in power," Poilievre said in a statement.
Trudeau's decision comes two weeks before Donald Trump is sworn back into office as president of the United States and Trudeau will remain at the helm during what is expected to be a rocky start to Trump's second term in the Oval Office. Trump has threatened to impose steep import tariffs on all Canadian goods the day he is inaugurated.
The Canadian Press

Reaction to Justin Trudeau's decision to step down
"Canadians desperate to turn the page on this dark chapter in our history might be relieved today that Justin Trudeau is finally leaving. But what has really changed? Every Liberal MP in power today and every potential Liberal leadership contender fighting for the top job helped Justin Trudeau break the country over the last nine years."
Conservative Leader
Pierre Poilievre
"The problem is not just Justin Trudeau. It's every minister that's been calling the shots. It's every Liberal MP that looked down their nose at Canadians who are worried about high costs or crumbling health care. The Liberals do not deserve another chance, no matter who is the leader."
NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh
"There is no significant difference between the Liberal party that we know and the Liberal party that will be presented to us after there's a new leader."
Bloc Québécois Leader
Yves-François Blanchet
"President-elect Trump continues to make real threats about imposing sweeping tariffs that would devastate Canada's economy. In two weeks, the president-elect will be sworn in as America's next president and will have every opportunity to make his threats real. Between now and then, the federal government needs to do everything humanly possible to avoid these tariffs, including by doing more to secure our border and offering a credible plan to invest more in Canada's military to meet and exceed our NATO spending commitments."
Ontario Premier Doug Ford
"I thank Justin Trudeau for his years of service to Canada and Canadians. I wish him and his family the very best."
Chrystia Freeland, former
Liberal deputy prime minister
The Canadian Press

Letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters-editor/docview/3150705543/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2025-01-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Simply fed up
with tax hikes
Re: Regional budget
doesn't hide the financial cost
of serving people, Dec. 27
I'm getting fed up with the continuous tax increases in Niagara.
I have no doubt seniors are a significant population in our region. We and the many workers who toil for minimum wage are being crushed by these tax increases.
If we had a seniors union, we'd go on strike.
We don't and the only people who would miss us are those places where we spend our fixed incomes.
Even those of us who have a pension aren't keeping up with inflation.
The further up the political ladder, the less concerned the politicians are, spending freely on pet projects and to buy our votes. Heck, the word debt seems to have no relevance for Premier Doug Ford or Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.
We have elections coming soon. Perhaps the various seniors clubs, interest groups, exercise groups, etc., could talk to each other and form a united front. It's worth trying.
Pete Morro, St. Catharines
Time to make campsites livable
Re: Niagara Falls councillors call for new approach to shelter sites, Dec. 27
I will no longer call people living in tents "homeless." It's demeaning and wrong.
In Canada, we go camping winter, spring, summer and fall. With climate change, it's tolerable to camp year round.
So, when experts say the campsites are needed, the municipalities, with provincial and federal help, should make them more livable so people around them have fewer complaints about filth and refuse.
A few items that could help include access to hot and cold potable water, a ride to the Y or similar facility for showers and hygiene or rent a shower trailer. How about a bus for laundry runs or, again, laundry trailers?
Russ Young, Fort Erie
Panda column
gets panned
Re: Pandas - the stuffed toys of the animal kingdom, Dec. 26
At a time when social media is spewing out the toxic ash of junk science, it's tragic the respected Niagara Dailies should publish William Thomas's polemic of hate against an endangered species, the Great Panda. Contrary to his rantings that it is not "a true bear," the Great Panda has been recognized by experts in taxonomy as such based on molecular studies since 1985.
The Great Panda is a vivid example of what conservation biologists recognize as an umbrella species. The formidable efforts to protect its habitat bring hope to other endangered wildlife species. These include the Golden Monkey and the Crested Ibis.
Thomas usually writes about harmless eccentricities of his neighbours in Wainfleet, a municipality in Canada that has some of our greatest treasures in biodiversity. It is to be hoped in the future he will return to addressing this, but in the context of protecting its natural habitats, which protect such rare species as the Fowler's toad, as a national park.
John Bacher, St. Catharines

St. Catharines, Ont., L2R 7T4. Letters should be 250 words or less, and may be edited for length and clarity. Please include your name, address and phone number for verification purposes.

Document 57 of 249

Eclipse, homeless among top stories of 2024: A look at some of the major issues that made headlines

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/eclipse-homeless-among-top-stories-2024/docview/3150264323/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Eclipse%2C+homeless+among+top+stories+of+2024%3A+A+look+at+some+of+the+major+issues+that+made+headlines&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-12-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A rare celestial event and a growing homelessness crisis.
Tying a record in homicides and record business investments.
The end of a political era and knockout sporting achievements.
It was a busy news year in Niagara.
Here were some of the biggest stories in various categories.
General news
The total solar eclipse brought tens of thousands of people to Niagara in April.
A report from Niagara Parks said traditional media ad value for coverage mentioning both the eclipse and Niagara Falls was $5.7 billion.
There were 33.2 billion impressions (54 per cent from television, 39 per cent from online news and seven per cent from radio) and more than 45,500 total mentions (55 per cent from TV, 30 per cent from radio and 15 per cent from online news).
Niagara Falls was highlighted as a top destination for people wanting to view the eclipse in the lead-up to the event. Various Niagara Parks attractions were set up as prime eclipse viewing spots, including Queen Victoria Park, Niagara Parks Power Station and Old Fort Erie.
Throughout the year, homelessness hit crisis levels.
So much so that St. Catharines Mayor Mat Siscoe, Niagara Falls Mayor Jim Diodati and Welland Mayor Frank Campion sent letters to Premier Doug Ford to support the use of the notwithstanding clause of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms to override court rulings limiting the ability of municipalities to remove encampments on public land.
About a dozen of the 29 mayors from the Ontario Big City Mayors caucus sent letters to Ford, requesting provincial action to help municipalities with issues related to mental health, addictions and homeless encampments - and "where necessary use the notwithstanding clause."
Local politicians, including Greg Miller, Caleb Ratzlaff and Haley Bateman of St. Catharines, and Lori Lococo of Niagara Falls, joined dozens of Ontario municipal councillors and mayors in signing a letter with concerns about using the clause.
In April, residents living around Niagara Region's Summer Street shelter had told Niagara Falls city council they feel like hostages in their own neighbourhood, affected by drug use, fights, public urination and stolen mail.
In December, similar concerns were raised by Buchanan Avenue residents about a supportive housing facility in their neighbourhood.
Health-care issues were also regularly in the news this year, whether it was in trying to address family doctor shortages across the region, protecting hospital services in Welland, Port Colborne and Fort Erie, or progress on Niagara Health's new hospital being constructed in south Niagara Falls.
Crime
Niagara continued to see an uptick in homicides in 2024.
The 15 broke a record set in 2005. There were six homicides in 2021, 10 in 2022 and 12 in 2023.
High-profile cases this year include 47-year-old Lance Cunningham, who was killed Oct. 2 while walking his dogs at John N Allan Park in Niagara Falls.
Two days later, police announced the arrest of Sabrina Kauldhar, 30, on a charge of second-degree murder. She was also charged with second-degree murder in connection with an Oct. 1 Toronto homicide and with first-degree murder for an Oct. 3 Hamilton homicide.
Randy Fader, 29, was killed in Niagara Falls April 1 following a shooting in the Epworth Circle and St. Lawrence Avenue area.
Three men, including a former Canadian Olympic snowboarder, were charged in connection to the shooting and are also accused of being part of a drug-trafficking operation involved in other deaths across Ontario.
During an Oct. 17 media announcement in Los Angeles, the FBI announced it had charged three people involved in an international cocaine smuggling operation.
Andrew Clark, 34, and Ryan James Wedding, 43, both Canadian citizens living in Mexico, and Malik Cunningham, 23, another Canadian, were charged in connection with Fader's death. Clark was arrested by Mexican authorities on Oct. 8, while Cunningham was arrested in Toronto on Oct. 16. The FBI issued an arrest warrant for Wedding, who competed for Canada in the 2002 Winter Olympics. He remains at large.
Johnathon Cronkwright, 32, was found dead in a rundown St. Catharines house in March.
Samantha Embert, 33, and Michael Patterson, 34, were charged with neglect of duty and committing indignity to a dead body.
Cronkwright went missing Feb. 29 after leaving a family member's home to go to a nearby store. His body was discovered inside a Raymond Street home March 9 following a civilian volunteer search.
Niagara Regional Police said foul play was not suspected in his death.
Patterson and Embert were arrested the following month.
Cronkwright's family members have said they feel his Indigenousness played a role in how police prioritized the case in comparison to other missing people in Niagara during the same period.
Niagara Regional Native Centre called for an investigation into the NRP on how the case was handled. As of press time, there are no updates on this call.
Politics
In Niagara Falls, Wayne Thomson, the longest-serving mayor in the city's history, stepped down from his council seat Sept. 10.
The 85-year-old was first elected in 1968 as an alderman. He served as mayor from 1978 to 1983 and again from 1991 to 2003. Prior to retirement, he'd been a city councillor since 2010.
As mayor, Thomson spearheaded changes in tourism and development that helped lead to two casinos and highrise hotels, changing the skyline of the city.
Thomson received the key to the city during a "thank you" event at the Niagara Falls History Museum Oct. 9.
In St. Catharines, Carlos Garcia, a city councillor known for advocating tirelessly for Port Dalhousie and working to keep taxes in check, died Dec. 9.
Garcia was elected to city council in 2014 and previously served as a regional councillor. He was an advocate for preserving heritage in Port Dalhousie before being elected to council. He consistently voted against plans he thought were not in keeping with the community and pushed for responsible urban planning.
As city budget chair, Garcia repeatedly brought up the impact of tax increases on residents struggling to pay their bills, particularly older adults, and emphasized keeping increases low. He played a role in establishing the city's new multi-year budget process.
In Welland, controversial Ward 4 Coun. Tony DiMarco was again at the centre of attention.
In August, city councillors refused to sanction DiMarco for publicly revealing confidential information in a February meeting and for a second breach of the city's code of conduct later in the month on the same subject.
DiMarco has been the subject of nine integrity commissioner reports since 2017, including two this year, and docked a combined 240 days' pay.
Business
South Niagara - in particular, Port Colborne - had quite an eventful year when it came to positive business news.
In May, Japanese manufacturer Asahi Kasei Corp. announced a $1.56-billion investment in the lakeside city to bring the first Hipore wet-process lithium-ion separator manufacturing facility to Canada. The company recently formed a partnership with Honda, which is investing $417 million in the operation.
The announcement was seen by dignitaries as setting the table for Niagara to become the leader in the electric vehicle industry, with the largest related investment made to date in the region.
The facility is being built on what was formerly known as the Nyon Lands on the west side of Highway 140, just south of Townline Tunnel Road.
In September, Jungbunzlauer (JBL) announced a $200-million investment to build Canada's first xanthan gum facility in Port Colborne, expected to create 50 permanent high-paying jobs, as well as 300 construction jobs, at 1555 Elm St.
The investment in the new facility is the first phase of up to three, representing a possible total investment of $500 million to $600 million in the city.
Founded in 1867, Switzerland-based JBL specializes in citric acid, xanthan gum, gluconates, lactics, special salts and sweeteners for the food, beverage, pharmaceutical and cosmetic industries, plus other industrial applications.
In November, it was announced Port Colborne will become home to a $33-million, eight-million-litre biofuel terminal that will serve more than 100 vessels annually.
It will sit on five hectares of land on the east pier of the Welland Canal and be managed by Hamilton-Oshawa Port Authority (HOPA) and operated in partnership with Canada Clean Fuels and Canada Steamship Lines.
The federal government is investing $13.8 million in the terminal through its green shipping corridor program, established in 2023 to fund technology, equipment and infrastructure that reduces emissions and creates a greener marine sector.
Sports
It was a statement year for Niagara Falls professional boxer Lucas Bahdi.
He lived up to his "Prince" nickname when he appeared in the lightweight undercard of the much-anticipated fight between Jake Paul, 27, and Mike Tyson, 58, Nov. 15.
Bahdi improved his record since turning pro to 18-0 with a 2-0 decision over Italy's Amando Casamonica in front of 72,000 fans at AT&T Stadium in Arlington, Texas.
Following the fight, it was announced Bahdi had signed with Paul's Most Valuable Promotions, with the 30-year-old graduate of A.N. Myer Secondary School becoming the first Canadian to sign with the company.
The Niagara River Lions won their first Canadian Elite Basketball League championship by beating the Vancouver Bandits, 97-95, in August.
Since joining the CEBL in 2019, the River Lions have qualified for the final four weekend five times. They lost to the Edmonton Stingers in the final in 2021 and fell short in the semifinals on three occasions: to the then-Hamilton Honey Badgers in 2019 and to the Scarborough Shooting Stars in 2022 and 2023.
The Fort Erie Meteors won their first junior hockey championship in 45 years this year. The Meteors scored four unanswered goals in the third period for a 5-3 victory over the host Caledonia Corvairs to take a best-of-seven Golden Horseshoe Conference final four games to three in April.
It was Fort Erie's first title of any kind since 1979, when the Meteors won it all in the former Niagara District Junior-B Hockey League's final season.
The win was especially emotional for general manager-head coach Nik Passero, who was born into a junior-B hockey family and has bled Meteors orange and black his entire life as the son of the late Tony Passero, the team's longtime owner.
With files from Niagara Falls Review, St. Catharines Standard and Welland Tribune reporters

Readers write Letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write-letters-editor/docview/3150093588/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write+Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-12-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Anti-Trudeau bandwagon full
Trudeau could stay or go. Either way, Canadians should brace for a spring election, Dec. 24
Election fever will make or break you.
Take the likely demise of our prime minister, Justin Trudeau. The prattle began several months ago and news anchors and reporters hopped on the wagon for a chance at some renown.
The question is: what did he do that was so bad? The answer: nothing. Familiarity breeds contempt.
Sick of the same old thing, cries for a "newbie," a different look, the suspense of an election. Like buying a lottery ticket: so much hope.
You can't tell a book by its cover. But in politics, the answer is, yes, you can. To wit, the recent presidential election in the U.S.
It's not hard to compete with outmoded fashion, especially when the opposition is all bluff and braggadocio. Canadians face what seems to be an imminent election and Yogi Berra's oft quoted statement, "You can observe a lot just by watching," is probably good advice.
The candidates are up front and in your face. What do you see?
James J. Antonio,
Niagara Falls
What have
we become?
Re: Niche dog sport
divided Doug Ford's caucus
as expansion plan killed,
then resurrected, Dec. 13
Why doesn't the SPCA speak up about this barbarism of penned hunting and get people to demand it be banned? How can we expect people to behave humanely to people if they can't do so to animals and the weak?
Why do we focus so much on technology and crow about it and leave our spiritual and moral characters to rot? Do we think technology will get us into heaven or teach us how to live and treat other beings and things?
Look around, for God's sake. We're producing dead-end people with no moral fibre and self-serving and satisfying goals. We were once a Judeo-Christian nation with considerable moral fibre compared to the present time.
What have we become? Is there any chance of reclamation? Not when we make excuses to serve ourselves and what we desire in doing so, such as being antisemitic instead of anti-Israeli policy in the Mideast and anti-Russian instead of anti-NATO policy pre-war in Europe.
Both issues have had truth and lie woven together seemingly inextricably but are easily separated if truth be served.
I am truly sick of repeating all this time after time to deaf ears, but what is one to do if one sees the truth and must speak it?
Wayne Borbey,
Niagara Falls

Document 59 of 249

Niagara's Liberal MPs at risk of losing jobs: Siegel: Trudeau's delayed departure could hurt re-election chances

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/niagaras-liberal-mps-at-risk-losing-jobs-siegel/docview/3149695915/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Niagara%27s+Liberal+MPs+at+risk+of+losing+jobs%3A+Siegel%3A+Trudeau%27s+delayed+departure+could+hurt+re-election+chances&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-12-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau should have resigned at least a year ago, says Niagara political pundit David Siegel.
As it is, the retired Brock University political science professor said Niagara's incumbent Liberal MPs should be worried about their jobs with a federal election in September or earlier on the horizon.
"The real problem is, (Trudeau) should have stepped down a year or two ago, because it's getting to be too late now," Siegel said.
"You need a kind of run-up to the (Liberal) convention where people like (Finance Minister) Dominic LeBlanc and (his predecessor) Chrystia Freeland and maybe (former housing minister) Sean Frazer and (Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Minister) Marc Miller can showcase what they can do.
"There's very little runway left to do that."
Siegel expects Trudeau's plans will be known by early January.
If the prime minister had resigned a year ago, he said, the Liberals "would be in a different position today," with a change of leadership maybe giving the party a stronger chance in the next election.
"Personally, I don't think I've spoken to anyone who is excited about (Conservative leader) Pierre Poilievre. When I hear people mention Pierre Poilievre, they say, 'We can't vote for the Liberals' - which is not exactly a ringing endorsement."
"If we were having this conversation a year ago, I would have said, 'Oh yeah, Poilievre is not really that popular and the Liberals have a lot of runway to build something.'"
Now, however, he said while Poilievre is still not that popular, the Liberals no longer have time to rebuild voter confidence in their party.
And if there's enough opposition to Trudeau, Siegel said Niagara Centre MP Vance Badawey and St. Catharines MP Chris Bittle could lose their seats.
Badawey's in a better position, he said, because of his years representing Port Colborne as mayor and as a regional councillor, "but even very popular local candidates can get swept up in a wave."
Neither of Niagara's Conservative MPs, Niagara Falls' MP Tony Baldinelli and Niagara West's Dean Allison, responded to interview requests for this story, nor did Bittle and Badawey when approached by the Toronto Star.
During an hour-long Zoom call of Ontario Liberal MPs on Saturday, none of the participants argued Trudeau should remain Liberal leader for the next election.
In a poll of all Liberal MPs published by the Toronto Star Friday, 102, including Bittle and Badawey, offered no comment, while 22 said Trudeau should resign. Eighteen said he should remain party leader and 10 did not take a definitive stance.
Siegel said he's not surprised by the silence among local MPs.
"Honestly, they're being wise in the sense that nobody knows what's going to happen," he said. "Academics can spout off any time they want because nobody cares, but for politicians who are accountable for what they say, they're being quite wise because they don't know what's happening."
Although a vote of non-confidence in Trudeau is possible when the federal legislature resumes in January, Siegel said it might not be enough to force an election earlier than the fall.
"September 2025 sounds a long way off, but in truth we're close enough maybe there's a way for the parties to stick this thing out," he said.
With files from the Toronto Star
CAPTION: St. Catharines Liberal MP Chris BittleNiagara Centre MP Vance BadaweyRetired Brock University professor and political pundit David Siegel

Readers write Letters to the editor

Full text: Singh has few alternatives
Ditch Justin Trudeau? What about Jagmeet Singh?, Dec. 19
Scott Stinson's opinion piece slamming NDP Leader, Jagmeet Singh is another blow against progressives, but also against all Canadians who want to maintain our society as distinct from other countries, and especially distinct from our neighbours to the south.
A Canada where fiscal conservatism and a social conscience formed our political foundation and allowed so many of us to thrive - yet, we are faced with a Conservative party that is led by a man who offers no solutions for Canadians, but taps into the rage so many feel in our post-pandemic world. Pierre Poilievre offers fear rather than hope and absolutely no path forward for Canada that is hopeful or framed in a positive, forward-thinking manner.
Yes, Justin Trudeau needs to leave and should have many months ago when the Liberal party had a chance to restructure in time for a federal election. He has broken too many promises and does not appear to heed the voice of citizens who clearly do not feel he is competent to lead.
So, the alternative for a progressive like Singh is to allow the Conservatives to take over, which is what we all know will occur if he ensures the government falls?
Rather, should he not continue to stumble along like all of us who want the current government to adapt to reality and hope the next election brings a minority government that will serve more of our needs than a majority led by a puppet of Stephen Harper and someone who uses the playbook of American extremists?
Nancy Vogel, St. Catharines
Breaking the
law and sense
On Friday, Dec. 20, I was driving along Glen Morris Drive in St. Catharines behind a school bus at 3:05 p.m. It stopped adjacent to the entry to the Denis Morris High School parking lot. The flashing lights were on, the stop sign was out and lit. Two vehicles passed the stopped bus. This is against the law as well as against plain common sense. I wish I had been able to get the licence plate numbers of those vehicles.
Pete Morro, St. Catharines
Stick to your lane
Doug Ford
Doug Ford says he got Donald Trump's attention with energy threat, Dec. 13
Premier Doug Ford needs to focus on Ontario issues, not federal ones. Ford recently closed the Ontario Science Centre, which needed a $200-million renovation over the next 10 years, so he could spend hundreds of millions more as part of the Ontario Place fiasco.
He has no detailed knowledge of the U.S.-Mexico-Canada trade agreement currently in force which needs to be updated.
Chrystia Freeland would be my pick to represent the interests of all Canadians as she led our delegation in negotiations over the last agreement signed during the first Trump presidency.
J. David Strathern,
Niagara Falls

Document 61 of 249

The 2024 News Quiz: How much do you remember from the last 12 months?

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/2024-news-quiz/docview/3149197401/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+2024+News+Quiz%3A+How+much+do+you+remember+from+the+last+12+months%3F&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-12-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's that time of the year when we like to rummage through the stacks of crumpled newspapers - or flick through the always shiny digital headlines - to look back on the year that was.
With 12 months filled to the brim with Niagara stories from across the region, there are a lot of whos, whats and whys to try to recall.
How many details do you remember from the past year? We invite you to test your memory and dive into our quiz.
1. Niagara Region declared a state of emergency "out of an abundance of caution" in April and Niagara's public and Catholic students were given the day off school. What was the issue?
a. Heavy snow
b. Total solar eclipse
c. Dense fog
d. The Eras Tour
2. St. Catharines installed 1,300 road signs citywide beginning in the spring that posted a new lower speed limit for all local residential streets. What is the new speed limit?
a. 15 km/h
b. 30 km/h
c. 40 km/h
d. 50 km/h
3. Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Ontario Premier Doug Ford were in Port Colborne in May for the announcement that Tokyo-based Asahi Kasei Corp. will build an electric vehicle battery separator plant. Called a "game-changer" for Niagara, how much is the company initially investing to build its factory in the city?
a. $1.56 billion
b. $1.56 million
c. $156,000
d. A figure hasn't been determined yet
4. Commisso's Fresh Foods threw a celebration in September to mark the 85th anniversary of a popular Post breakfast cereal made in Niagara Falls. What cereal is it?
a. Sugar Crisp
b. Shreddies
c. Life
d. Rice Krispies
5. St. Catharines hospital was given a new name almost 11 years after it opened its doors on Fourth Avenue, thanks to a $15-million donation announced in January. What is the name of the hospital?
a. St. Catharines Family Hospital
b. Marotta Family Hospital
c. SmartCentres Family Hospital
d. Moo Deng Family Hospital
6. Niagara Region was looking at the possibility of enhancing security measures after items were vandalized eight to 10 times in the first five months of the year. What was being damaged?
a. Speed camera poles
b. Parking meters
c. Police cars
d. Mailboxes
7. On Aug. 5, without warning, a storm destroyed a barn in Port Colborne, ripped a roof off a Rona store in Fort Erie and damaged other property. How many tornadoes were confirmed to hit the area that day according to experts?
a. Three
b. Two
c. One
d. No tornadoes were confirmed
8. Thorold history buffs gathered at the Allanburg Bridge on Nov. 30 to unveil a plaque and celebrate a milestone anniversary - the day construction started on the first Welland Canal. How many years ago did the first shovel go into the ground?
a. 250 years
b. 200 years
c. 175 years
d. 150 years
9. St. Catharines was selected as one of three locations in Ontario for a "no name" discount grocery store as part of a pilot project by Loblaw Cos. Ltd. What does the stripped-down grocery store not have that other Loblaw stores do?
a. Fresh meat
b. A dairy section
c. Flyers
d. All of the above
10. Niagara Parks launched a weeklong pilot project in June to target the removal of phragmites, an invasive species, from Gonder's Flats, a natural area being restored in Fort Erie. What low-impact, chemical-free initiative was used to target the biological pest?
a. Vinegar
b. Boiling water
c. Cornmeal
d. Goats
11. Niagara-on-the-Lake's landmark historic cenotaph and clock tower underwent exterior restoration work to improve its brick and parging and restore the clock face in time for Remembrance Day. What year was the clock tower originally unveiled?
a. 1901
b. 1922
c. 1945
d. 1966
12. The more than a century-old Lincoln and Welland Regiment Association Band was told to stop using the regiment's name and was evicted from the armoury in St. Catharines after a Canadian Army order affecting civilian bands. What is the band called now?
a. Lincoln and Welland Band
b. LincWell Reg Band
c. Niagara Region Marching Band
d. Niagara Community Band
13. Switzerland-based company Jungbunzlauer (JBL) held a groundbreaking ceremony in Port Colborne in September for a $200-million plant that will be Canada's first xanthan gum facility. What is xanthan gum?
a. A thickening and stabilizing agent used in foods and beverages
b. A grafting tool used in periodontal surgery for gum recession
c. A Swiss chewing gum with 31 flavours
d. An adhesive remover product similar to Goo Gone
14. In September, the Town of Lincoln warned residents not to consume the contents of boxes found at the side of the road, believed to have come from a stolen truck. What were residents finding?
a. Marijuana
b. Cases of Labatt two-fours
c. Hot chocolate bombs
d. Balzac's Coffee
15. Niagara saw a record-setting 28 new family physicians set up practices in 2024, its best year ever in terms of retaining and attracting doctors. How many family doctors does the region still require to meet the needs of the population, according to Niagara Region's physician recruitment specialist?
a. 200
b. 106
c. 54
d. 28
16. A new university in downtown Niagara Falls welcomed students for its inaugural spring term in April, with a second intake in July and a third in the fall. What is the school called?
a. University of Niagara Falls Canada
b. University of Niagara
c. Niagara University
d. Fallsview University
17. A new $200-million hospital is opening in Grimsby next year to replace the aging West Lincoln Memorial Hospital. After municipal debate about the name, what will the new hospital be called?
a. West Lincoln Memorial Hospital
b. West Niagara Memorial Hospital
c. West Niagara General Hospital
d. Grimsby and Area Memorial Hospital
18. A 40-year-old Welland man pleaded guilty to mischief over $5,000 and was sentenced to time served after using an angle-grinder on seven trees along the Welland Recreational Canal. What did he carve out?
a. Initials with hearts
b. Swear words
c. Faces
d. Firewood
19. In May, the 138-year-old controversial statue of Northwest Resistance soldier Pte. Alexander Watson was removed from the front lawn of St. Catharines city hall. Where was it relocated?
a. Canada Games Park
b. St. Catharines Museum and Welland Canals Centre
c. Victoria Lawn Cemetery
d. Walker Landfill
20. Niagara saw 172 convenience stores across the region ready to sell alcohol on Sept. 5, the first day Ontario rolled out alcohol sales in all corner stores with licences. What are the stores allowed to sell?
a. Beer and wine
b. Cider and ready-to-drink beverages
c. Vodka and gin
d. a and b
Answers
1-B, 2-C, 3-A, 4-B, 5-B, 6-A, 7-A, 8-B, 9-D, 10-D, 11-B, 12-A, 13-A, 14-D, 15-B, 16-A, 17-A, 18-C, 19-C, 20-D
CAPTION: 2024 NEWS: Clockwise from top left: The band formerly known as the Lincoln and Welland Regiment Band; a roof torn from a Fort Erie Rona store in August landed in a neighbour's yard; the no name pilot store in St. Catharines; the cenotaph and clock tower in Niagara-on- the-Lake; The Asahi Kasei Corp. announcement and inside the new university in Niagara Falls. Centre.St. Catharines Standard staff
