Document 62 of 249

Bradley guns for funding to offset rising police costs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/bradley-guns-funding-offset-rising-police-costs/docview/3147642373/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bradley+guns+for+funding+to+offset+rising+police+costs&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-12-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Niagara Region Chair Jim Bradley is joining law enforcement associations across Ontario in calling for provincial funding increases to help offset the cost of implementing Ontario's new Community Safety and Policing Act.
Last week, the province announced $77 million will be awarded to help municipalities served by Ontario Provincial Police cover increased costs tied to the legislation that came into effect April 1.
In February, the police board and regional council approved a $1.9-million transfer from an operating budget surplus to a contingency reserve to pay for expenditures related to the act, including $450,624 to cover the cost of increased staff within the training unit.
The training amount, because of a late start, was later reduced to $385,000 to temporarily add one sergeant and five constables to its training unit from August to December in relation to the legislation.
Additional one-time costs are anticipated next year, including for C8 rifles, rifle mounts for vehicles, ammunition for training, range facility rentals, replacement pay for extended training dates and training course registration fees.
Bradley is asking the province to do the same for communities served by municipal police services hit with similar budget hikes, as are Ontario Association of Chiefs of Police and Ontario Association of Police Service Boards.
Letters to the province from Bradley and the associations were presented during Thursday's Niagara police services board meeting.
In his letter sent Tuesday to Premier Doug Ford and Solicitor General Michael Kerzner, Bradley said implementing the legislation "has directly resulted in $5.2 million in increased costs for the police service, which must be borne by property taxpayers in Niagara."
He said regional council supports "positive changes" within the legislation; however, "the costs associated with these changes, and the subsequent property tax increases the Region must make to cover them, are making it even more difficult for Niagara's residents to pay their bills and are contributing to the unaffordability crisis affecting all our municipalities."
Bradley asked the province to provide "more clarity from the government on equitable funding models available to all police services across the province and other long-term, sustainable solutions to address the financial challenges municipalities across our province are facing due to policing costs."
Ontario Association of Chiefs of Police president Roger Wilkie and Ontario Association of Police Service Boards chair Patrick Weaver sent a similar letter to Kerzner dated April 7, calling for "additional and equitable funding."
The associations are also calling for sustainable long-term funding "to support the delivery of adequate and effective policing services, particularly in smaller and rural communities with limited resources."
"Given the concerns raised by police services in the wake of the recent announcement, we are looking for your ministry officials to help us better understand the equitable funding models available to all police services across the province," their letter said.
"We also believe that there is a need for clarity and greater awareness around the financial drivers, costs and budget impacts related to delivering adequate and effective policing in today's policing environment."
CAPTION: A Niagara Regional Police officer shoots at the training facility in Welland in 2014. Niagara Region Chair Jim Bradley wants the province- not local taxpayers - to cover costs associated with the newCommunity Safety and Policing Act.Welland Tribune file photo

CREDIT: Allan Benner reporter

Document 63 of 249

New Ontario Science Centre delayed to 2029, auditor general says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/new-ontario-science-centre-delayed-2029-auditor/docview/3147380547/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=New+Ontario+Science+Centre+delayed+to+2029%2C+auditor+general+says&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-12-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The timeline for opening a permanent new Ontario Science Centre appears to have been delayed, with infrastructure officials telling the auditor general's office the centre is expected to be up and running in 2029.
When Premier Doug Ford announced last year the planned move for the attraction, from its east Toronto location to a redeveloped Ontario Place on the city's waterfront, the science centre said the new facility would open in 2028.
In June, when the government announced the abrupt closure of the science centre over structural concerns with the roof, its press release said the Ontario Place facility would open "as early as" 2028.
Now, according to a report earlier this month from Ontario's auditor general Shelley Spence on the Ontario Place redevelopment, "the new building is expected to open in 2029."
That information was provided to the auditor general's office by Infrastructure Ontario, a spokesperson for the auditor said.
Jason Ash, co-chair of the group Save Ontario's Science Centre, said it's a shame that there will be no permanent Ontario Science Centre for at least an extra year.
"It's really a whole generation of Ontario children who are not going to have access to world-class science education that the Ontario Science Centre has provided for 55 years," he said.
"Simply put, the Ontario Science Centre was unique in that the majority of its business actually came from Ontarians, with tourism coming in second place. So Ontario kids aren't being served and tourists are not going to get a great experience either in the meantime."
Two requests for comment from Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma went unanswered, with a spokesperson ultimately replying to a third by saying she would not be responding.
"Due to the request submitted by the Leader of the Official Opposition to the Integrity Commissioner on October 16th, 2024, Minister Kinga Surma has been asked not to comment and will respect the process at this time," Ash Milton wrote.
Milton's reply came after Surma answered at least a dozen questions in question period on the auditor general's Ontario Place redevelopment findings.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles' integrity complaint focused largely on terms and negotiations that led to a 95-year lease for Therme to build and operate a spa and waterpark at Ontario Place, but also alleges "irregularities" with the plan to relocate the science centre.
Stiles suggests, based on information previously uncovered last year by the auditor general, that the government wanted to have the science centre at Ontario Place and integrate parking promised to Therme with the science centre building in order to dispel public concerns over the project.
"This is yet another expensive project that nobody asked for - with a plan that no one is buying," Stiles wrote in a statement this week. "We could have revitalized the existing Science Centre for a fraction the cost. Ford and Surma boasted about the new Science Centre opening in 2028, but that sounds like more false promises from a flailing government."
A spokesperson for Infrastructure Ontario said the procurement process for the new centre continues, with the request for proposals stage expected to wrap up next fall.
A firm date for the completion of construction will be posted once a contract is awarded, and the opening date will depend on the centre's timeline for moving into the new building, Karen Evans said in an email.
"The overall plan for delivering the vision remains unchanged," she said.
That audit found that it will now cost more for the government to build a new Ontario Science Centre at Ontario Place than it would have to maintain the site it abruptly closed earlier this year.
The cost estimate for building and maintaining a new science centre at Ontario Place has increased by nearly $400 million from the government's spring 2023 business case for relocating it, the auditor said, meaning it will cost approximately $1.4 billion - higher than the $1.3-billion estimate for maintaining the attraction at its east Toronto location.
The increase is due to higher design and construction costs, life cycle and maintenance costs, and ancillary costs that have added up because of changes to the scope of the planned building and about $61 million in cost escalations, Spence wrote.
Michael Lindsay, at the time the president and CEO of Infrastructure Ontario, said he didn't agree that building a new science centre will cost more than rehabilitating the old one, noting that project would also be facing inflationary price increases.
Lindsay provided some reasoning for possible delays in a briefing on the day the science centre was closed.
"The language ... (of "as early as" 2028) probably just reflects the realities of procurement and construction," he said.
"We're going to be talking to our counterparties through the procurement about what it would take to construct a new science centre, how long that might be, and so I think the plan remains the plan. That language probably just points at the reality that further conversation with our market is coming about what it's going to take to both procure and then build the new science centre."
Ontario is planning to have a temporary science centre open in the interim, but has not provided any public updates on that process since it issued a request for proposals in June.
That RFP showed the province is looking for retail or commercial space of about 50,000 to 100,000 square feet - much smaller than the original building's 568,000 square feet - with a start date of "no later than" Jan. 1, 2026.
The RFP also showed that the province wants a lease of up to five years for the temporary space, plus three options to extend the lease for one year each, which would allow the government to operate a science centre in a temporary home until 2034, Ash said.
The science centre is currently operating two pop-up exhibits in Toronto.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: A temporary pop-up Ontario Science Centre in Sherway Gardens mall in Toronto earlier this month.Nathan Denette The Canadian Press

CREDIT: Allison Jones The Canadian Press

Subject: Construction costs; Infrastructure; Science education; Redevelopment; Cost estimates

Business indexing term: Subject: Construction costs Infrastructure Cost estimates

Company / organization: Name: Ontario Science Centre; NAICS: 712110; Name: Infrastructure Ontario; NAICS: 926110, 926120

Title: New Ontario Science Centre delayed to 2029, auditor general says

Publication title: The Standard; St. Catharines, Ont.

First page: A.8

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Dec 20, 2024

Section: Local / News

Publisher: Torstar Syndication Services, a Division of Toronto Star Newspapers Limited

Place of publication: St. Catharines, Ont.

Country of publication: Canada

Full text: Trudeau's
terrible timing
Inside Chrystia Freeland's surprising resignation - and the fallout that has Justin Trudeau fighting for his political life, Dec. 16
I am writing about the political crisis we are witnessing this week after Prime Minister Justin Trudeau caused the resignation of Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland, who in my opinion was his most effective, knowledgeable and loyal minister.
I have never had a very high opinion of Trudeau as a politician or as a person. I still feel his only positive virtue was that he wasn't Stephen Harper. When I heard he had been chosen leader of the Liberal party and witnessed "Trudeaumania" explode across Canada, I was conflicted. Happy to see Harper go and concerned Justin was not up to the job. I thought that if his name was Justin Jones, he would never have had a political career. He had is father's looks, but not Pierre's political abilities.
Events have, unfortunately, shown my fears were not unfounded. He has shown, time after time, the unattractive qualities of hubris or overweening pride and arrogance, obviously feeling he is better and smarter than others. This is probably why he is not, like his father, immediately resigning.
We are in a crisis situation in Canada at a time Americans, again, have elected a person who also has these unfortunate flaws and more. Donald Trump who, like a dumb little kid in the schoolyard, says insulting and stupid things about a friend.
The timing could not be worse for our country.
Richard Murri, Niagara Falls
A disgusting
reversal by Ford
Niche dog sport divided Doug Ford's caucus as expansion plan killed, then resurrected, Dec. 13
It is with supreme disgust I react to the announcement that inhumane penned hunting by dogs will sadly continue after yet another reversal by Premier Doug Ford.
It was reported his caucus was divided and he initially decided to shut down this practice.
But wait, after talking to "hunters," he reversed the decision and applications for this un-Canadian sport will take place.
Wildlife penned in large open fields are abused to death by so-called hunters dogs. There is no escape for these terrified creatures. The dogs literally rip them apart.
Hard to hear but it must if there is ever going to be justice and, hopefully, the premier will grow a heart and a stick up his back to disagree with these hunters demanding such a low practice.
Is this how a Canadian politician puts Canada on the map for all to see?
Faye Suthons, Wainfleet

the answers
Here's a city that solved street homelessness - and how we can do it too, Dec. 8
Lots of noise but there's an issue that nobody does anything about.
Yes, I'm talking about people who are unhoused.
How about sending a delegation to Finland where it evidently has solved the problem?
Hank Bangild, Port Colborne

Full text: All governments
on spending spree
Is the municipal funding model broken? Niagara Region approves
9.6% tax increase for 2025, Dec. 13
How did these people get elected and why do we continue to vote for them?
Federally, the debt climbs and Justin Trudeau's Liberals believe it is not a problem. Why not give $250 to people earning $150,000, hire thousands more highly paid civil servants, work with the NDP to find more ways to spend. While Chrystia Freeland was never my favourite for finance, her resignation shows the government is falling apart.

In Ontario our government promises $200 to everyone. Why? Doug Ford cuts revenue sources but does nothing to cut spending. He also does nothing to improve health, education, affordable housing and much more. Opposition parties only know how to criticize and promise more spending.
Municipalities. Now here is where spending grows thanks in part to downloading from the province. In Niagara, regional and municipal governments find ways to expand salaries and increase taxes every year. In Welland, we are a small city with two councillors in each ward, neither of whom I ever see or hear from.
Has any politician found a way to get rid of old useless programs, reduce spending or taxes and perhaps eliminate some political positions? Toronto has more than three million people and Niagara 525,352. Toronto has 26 councillors; Niagara has 126.
Jack Hughes, Welland

With a massive 9.6 per cent property tax increase added together with last year's 7.02 per cent increase, the regional portion of Niagara homeowners' tax bills will have risen more than 16 per cent in just two years.
This is just the regional portion of the bill.
How is this sustainable? Yet, instead of looking for ways to mitigate tax increases it's business as usual. Put forth huge tax increases, lament we don't have enough revenue sources and don't acknowledge municipal governments in Niagara have a spending problem.
When will the province fix this?
Allen McKay, St. Catharines

This government is becoming unaffordable as it is without this almost 10 per cent tax increase.

This government is forcing people into poverty and no one is held accountable.

Full text: Gambling addicts help to fund
government
Sports gambling is a threat to public health, Dec. 11
Make no mistake regarding the intent surrounding the push for online gambling advertisements onto mainstream platforms.
It is the same reason behind the federal Liberals' obsession a few years ago to legalize marijuana.
It has nothing to do with some idealistic vision of providing broader choices and freedoms to the general public, or to eradicate the illegal drug and gambling markets. That was just a smokescreen.
It has everything to do with tax revenue, another source of public funds and nothing more. After all, someone has to pay for the huge deficits we've been running for the past decade.
The online gambling addiction we are seeing right now was completely predictable and our government and regulators knew it - they moved ahead regardless.
We don't need more "responsible gambling" ads to address this problem, what we need is "responsible government."
Alain Fournier, Lincoln
Provincial ads
are insulting
Ford government defends tripling spending on ads after searing auditor general's report, Dec. 4
I am writing about the appearance of advertisements from the Ford government, such as, "Ontario is helping you keep more of what's yours." The words "unmitigated gall" and "insulting BS" come to mind.
I must remind Premier Doug Ford the money he is using in a feeble attempt to bribe us is ours, not his. The Ontario logo at the bottom of these ads shows who is actually paying for this blatant political propaganda. All of us, not the Progressive Conservative Party of Ontario, which is, by the way, bloated with funds from developers, corporations, etc.
It is like rubbing salt in the wounds of those among us who are struggling in a world which has become less secure and more uncertain in many ways.
The supposed millions in savings is a case in point.
I would like to know where the numbers come from and where these supposed millions in savings have gone.
They have not gone to improve our health system, our schools, our roads, helping those with addictions or mental health problems, the homeless or the working poor. In fact, Ontario is lagging behind in regards to all of these issues compared to most other provinces.
Much of these savings have been achieved by the Conservatives' tried and true method of downloading costs to municipalities.
Have we forgotten the bad old days of Mike Harris and his accomplice in Alberta, Ralph Klein? We are still paying for many of Harris's schemes such as Highway 407.
Thanks for nothing, Doug. By the way, I am donating the provincial tax rebate to another party to help them get rid of you.
Richard Murri,
Niagara Falls

Document 67 of 249

Colleges face looming job action: Provincial bargainers 'committed to a solution without interrupting student learning'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/colleges-face-looming-job-action/docview/3144878806/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Colleges+face+looming+job+action%3A+Provincial+bargainers+%27committed+to+a+solution+without+interrupting+student+learning%27&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-12-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With little progress made toward a new contract, the union representing faculty at Ontario colleges - including Niagara College - has requested a no-board report from the provincial government, bringing job action a step closer.
Ontario Public Service Employees Union (OPSEU), which represents about 15,000 professors, instructors, librarians and counsellors at Ontario's 24 public colleges, made the request to the Ministry of Labour Thursday.
Once the report is issued, it will start a 16-day countdown, putting the union in legal strike position in the new year.
OPSEU and the College Employer Council (CEC) - the government-mandated bargaining agent for the province's publicly-funded colleges - met Dec. 6 to 8 but the faculty bargaining team said mediation was unsuccessful.
Darcie Hillier, vice-president of OPSEU Local 242 at Niagara College, said the CEC was unwilling to move on workload concerns and job security despite overwhelming evidence in a workload report conducted by both parties, which supported improvements to help faculty manage.
To the union it suggests a lack of "serious commitment to engage meaningfully in the bargaining process," said Hillier
"In the past, we've had to resort to arbitration, been legislated back from our lawful right to withhold labour during a labour dispute and been presented with forced offers that are full of concessions," said Hillier.
"Considering we're entering a season of uncertainty, it seems logical that the CEC would want to create an environment of stability, that they'd be motivated to work out a contract to ensure that there would be no more cracks in the college system, but they seem to have no interest."
In October, OPSEU announced its membership delivered a strike mandate, with 79 per cent of those who participated in the vote (about 76 per cent of its members) supporting a strike.
In a media release, the CEC said the move by the academic bargaining team is "deeply disappointing," and a potential strike is "wholly unnecessary and causes uncertainty, and disruption for students and faculty in a time of financial instability."
"Throughout this bargaining process our goal has remained the same: we want to keep our students learning and faculty in the classroom. That is why we initially proposed binding arbitration and continue to urge OPSEU to agree."
It added union demands ignore the reality facing Ontario colleges, which it said are projected to lose hundreds of millions of dollars in the coming years due to declining enrolment and higher costs.
"The CEC remains committed to a solution without interrupting student learning. We urge OPSEU to reconsider their approach in favour of an outcome that is fair and sustainable for everyone," it said.
In recent weeks, several institutions, including Sheridan College and Mohawk College, announced cuts to front-line staff and the suspension of academic programs following a cap on international students.
In a November campus update, Niagara College president Sean Kennedy said while the college expects to end the current fiscal year with a budget surplus, it is taking cost-containment measures, including only hiring positions essential to the "core operations of the college or (that) directly support our student learning, recruitment and retention priorities."
Hillier said while Niagara is not in as dire a position as other colleges, it doesn't know what the future holds and those facing greatest risk are partial-load faculty, with its union members seeing decreasing numbers of contracts for January.
While colleges are doing what they can to secure finances, it's "doing so at the cost of our most precarious faculty," and for colleges to be looking to reduce its bargaining unit's members "at a time when we are involved in active bargaining has incredibly serious implications."
"The system provincially has added almost 100,000 students in the past decade, 1,500 administrators and only 500 full-time faculty," she said.
"I believe my college administration is trying to do what it thinks is best and trying to do the best they can. This crisis has been created by our provincial government largely, so in some ways this outcome seems inevitable."
After decades of underfunding, including a tuition freeze which has been in place since 2019, Hillier said a substantial cash injection by the province is long overdue, and the Doug Ford government is playing a "large and looming role in the situation we're now in."
Instead of spending taxpayer money on "vanity projects like beer sales," it has a responsibility to financially support its post-secondary institutions, she said.
Hillier added that perhaps if college presidents bargained directly with elected faculty reps, where both parties have a direct interest in the well-being of students, faculty and community, the bargaining process would be more productive.
The two parties will meet for non-binding mediation in early January.
In a media release, Michelle Arbour, acting chair of the faculty bargaining team, said the college system is crumbling and it can't sit back and let its "failed stewards bulldoze education."
"Our basic demands are an antidote, aimed at protecting the core mandate of the colleges as places of education, not cash-grabs," said Arbour. "Record profits and runaway administrative bloat - that's the last decade of Ontario's colleges in a nutshell.
CAPTION: Niagara College faculty picket in front of the Niagara-On- the-Lake campus in October 2017 during a province-wide contract dispute.Ontario's college faculty, members of OPSEU, have requested a no-board report, setting the clock on potential job action.Metroland file photo

CREDIT: Victoria Nicolaou Reporter

Document 68 of 249

Bending the rules: How Canada opened its doors to Nazi war criminals: 'It would be rash to assume that significant numbers of war criminals and Nazi collaborators did not enter Canada'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/bending-rules-how-canada-opened-doors-nazi-war/docview/3144285099/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bending+the+rules%3A+How+Canada+opened+its+doors+to+Nazi+war+criminals%3A+%27It+would+be+rash+to+assume+that+significant+numbers+of+war+criminals+and+Nazi+collaborators+did+not+enter+Canada%27&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-12-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: An unknown number of Nazi war criminals and collaborators lived out their lives in peace in Canada, in places such as the Mennonite farms and villages of Waterloo Region.
They were among thousands of ethnic Germans brought into Canada after the Second World War - many of them members of the most reprehensible units of the Nazi war machine - by a group called the Canadian Christian Council for the Resettlement of Refugees (CCCRR), which included the Mennonite Central Committee (MCC).
How this happened is detailed in a Government of Canada report, "Nazi War Criminals in Canada: The Historical And Policy Setting From the 1940s to the Present." Known as The Rodal Report, it is a 600-page study prepared by Oxford-trained historian Alti Rodal for the Deschênes Commission on Nazi War Criminals in Canada, which released its findings in 1986.
For decades, Ottawa refused to release the entirety of the Rodal report. The Waterloo Region Record filed an Access to Information request for a complete copy six years ago.
The Deschênes Commission said there were 29 cases of suspected Nazi war criminals in Canada that warranted special consideration because of the seriousness of the allegations and the availability of evidence against the men.
Among those priority cases was Waterloo resident Helmut Oberlander. The Record filed an Access to information Act request for the files on the other 28 cases, but Library and Archives Canada has refused to release any of the documents.
Before he died in September 2021 Oberlander was front page news in Waterloo Region, fighting a deportation order on and off since 1995. That's when Ottawa announced it was going to strip Oberlander of his citizenship and deport him for failing to disclose his voluntary service with Einsatzgruppe D, one of the Nazi's mobile death squads that operated in Eastern Europe, Soviet Ukraine and the Caucuses.
Oberlander and his wife came to Canada in May 1954, sponsored by a German relative in Waterloo. He was 'Volksdeutsche' - a 'racial' term coined by the Nazis for ethnic Germans who didn't hold German citizenship - from one of the oldest Mennonite communities in southern Russia. Oberlander was not brought to Waterloo Region by the MCC, but others were.
The Rodal report makes it clear there were many more - in fact it was easier for Nazi war criminals to come to Canada after the Second World War than Jews who wanted to rebuild their lives in Canada following the Holocaust.
Refugee aid groups and immigration officials preferred Germans and Baltic people, deemed to be highly attractive and desirable immigrants, "who were likely to have included - and indeed have been shown to have included in their midst persons who were Nazi collaborators and alleged war criminals," says Rodal.
During 1947-1949, the MCC and the CCCRR helped bring 6,005 Mennonites to Canada, and 15,000 Volksdeutsche - primarily from the western and southwestern areas of the former Soviet Union that became Ukraine in 1991.
About 807 of them settled in Ontario, primarily Kitchener-Waterloo, St. Catharines and Leamington. Many who first settled in other parts of Canada later moved to this region, say Mennonite historians.
Some Mennonites who were former SS and Nazi collaborators came to this region, but not many, said Troy Osborne, a history professor and dean of Mennonite studies at Conrad Grebel University in Waterloo.
"I don't think there were a lot in this area," said Osborne.
But researchers are reluctant to dig into this chapter of Mennonite history in the region.
"Part of the reason I think it is frosted over around here, you are talking about people's grandparents, it is not just about the MCC, it is about people's own family histories too," said Osborne. "That's what I've discovered."
Library and Archives Canada released the most complete version of the Rodal report in February 2024 following an international scandal six months earlier. The House of Commons gave two standing ovations to a former member of the Ukrainian-SS in September 2023.
That's when Ukraine president Volodymyr Zelenskyy was a guest of honour in Ottawa and spoke in Parliament to thank Canada for help in the war against Russia.
In the audience was 98-year-old Yaroslav Hunka, a former member of the Ukrainian SS - part of the Waffen-SS largely made up of non-German volunteers.
Federal politicians and their guests gave Hunka two standing ovations after he was introduced as a true hero of Ukraine by the Speaker of the House, Anthony Rota, a Liberal MP from North Bay.
When Hunka's background became public, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau apologized, and Rota resigned as Speaker of the House. Immigration Minister Marc Miller acknowledged Canada has a dark history when it comes to Nazis, and said requests to release more classified information about it would be revisited.
In February 2024, Library and Archives Canada released almost everything from the Rodal Report, minus one page and a handful of redactions.
The report lays out in detail a seeming indifference to war crimes by officials, openly discriminatory immigration policies, antisemitism, and security screening that was lax to non-existent.
"It would be rash to assume that significant numbers of war criminals and Nazi collaborators did not enter Canada," Rodal says in her report.
The original SS were early, enthusiastic supporters of Adolf Hitler, pledging loyalty to him alone. Later, the Nazis easily found enough volunteers in the Baltics, Eastern Europe, the Balkans and Soviet Ukraine to form divisions called the Waffen-SS.
The SS and Waffen-SS ran the concentration camps and death camps. They were largely responsible for carrying out the Holocaust.
The Nuremberg War Crimes Tribunal ruled the SS as a whole, including Waffen-SS divisions, a criminal organization. From 1945-1950 they were officially banned from coming to Canada, along with members of the SD (the brutal SS intelligence agency that operated in tandem with the SS), the Brown Shirts, or Storm Troopers, known as the SA, and members of the Einsatzgruppen mobile death squads that killed 1.5 million people, mostly Jews, in 1941-1943.
After the war, the Allies suspected many men from Eastern Europe and Soviet Ukraine had volunteered for some of the Nazi's worst units, says Rodal. Among that group were the Volksdeutsche living in Eastern Europe and Soviet Ukraine.
By 1950 Canada had increased its immigration quotas and was relaxing its policies on the banned groups and favouring 'desirable' ethnic German immigrants.
Rodal ties the CCCRR - an umbrella organization that represented Baptist, Lutheran, Mennonite and Catholic church groups mainly of German origin - with the changes in government policy.
The group lobbied Ottawa tirelessly to drop the immigration bans on former SS members, and senior bureaucrats did, without telling House of Commons or the Canadian public, says Rodal, setting the stage for Germany to become the biggest source of immigrants to Canada in the 1950s.
The church group "is closely linked to changes in government policy with regard to admitting enemy aliens and former members of the Nazi party and the Waffen SS, and other Nazi organizations," says Rodal.
In fact, Helmut Rauca, a former member of Einsatzgruppe A, one of the Nazis' mobile death squads responsible for killing thousands of Jews in Latvia, came to Canada in the late 1940s as a CCCRR sponsored refugee, under his own name, settling in Toronto.
West Germany issued an arrest warrant for Rauca in 1963, and Canada deported him in 1983 to face charges of crimes against humanity. He died in a West German jail awaiting trial.
By an order-in-council, Ottawa provided funds to the CCCRR, says Rodal, and it enjoyed direct access to ministers of immigration, deputy-ministers of immigration, and the prime minister.
It also had access to a powerful group of senior bureaucrats that screened certain visa applications, the civil service and the military looking for communists, fellow travellers and gays. It was called the Security Panel, and it always met behind closed doors.
In 1950 the Security Panel ranked communists as the most dangerous security threat facing Canada, and former members of the SS, the Nazi Party or Nazi collaborators were ranked lower, says Rodal.
At the same time, Jews faced widespread discrimination when applying for visas to Canada and were often rejected by screening officials as communists or communist sympathizers, perpetuating Third Reich propaganda that equated Jews with communism, says Rodal.
The Rodal report is supported by many Mennonite historians.
The Mennonite Central Committee has released reports by 12 historians with access to MCC archives. Their research was summarized in a special, 68-page special edition of the MCC newsletter "Intersections" in the fall of 2021.
To help Mennonites get out of Europe after the war, the MCC publicly and systemically downplayed the collaboration of tens of thousands of Mennonites with the Nazis, says that special edition. Many of them were coached on what to say during interviews with immigration and security officers: You are not German, but Mennonite, maybe Dutch; sorry but all my ID was lost; and most importantly you were forced to join any Nazi military unit, you never volunteered.
Mennonites had enjoyed a privileged status under German occupation, notes the Intersections special edition.
"MCC partnered with or directly hired multiple former Nazi officials and even SS agents because of the expertise they had built promoting fascist schemes to resettle Mennonites during WWII," says the publication.
One of the former Nazi officials hired by MCC to help Mennonite refugees was Heinrich Wiebe. The Nazis had installed Wiebe as mayor of the Ukrainian city of Zaporizhzhia. He implemented Nazi racial policies, confiscated the property of Jews and moved them into a ghetto. Wiebe fled to the west when the Soviet Red Army began pushing the German military out of Russian territory.
With MCC help Wiebe and his wife Olga came to Canada, and settled in Vancouver after the war.
MCC Canada director Rick Cober Bauman supported the decision to bring in independent historians to review the archives. It was not a unanimous decision, and a minority was opposed to digging into old relationships with Nazis, he said.
"All things considered I am pleased that we did it," said Bauman, who grew up in a rural part of Waterloo Region, the son of a preacher. "I think it was a good decision."
Aileen Friesen is among the new generation of scholars asking questions about what Mennonites did before, during and after the Second World War. She wrote about Wiebe in the special edition of Intersections.
She is a University of Winnipeg historian, executive director of the Plett Foundation, which researches the history of Mennonites who came from Russia in the 1870s and settled in Western Canada, and co-director of the centre for transnational Mennonite studies. For years she's researched the Holocaust, Soviet Ukraine and Mennonites.
Friesen stresses it was a complicated time for millions of desperate and traumatized people on the move after the war, with thousands trying to hide their Nazi ties.
The MCC had about 100 people on the ground in postwar Europe to sort through everything and decide who to help.
The MCC was founded in 1920 to help Mennonites get out of the newly formed Soviet Union and come to Canada. In the early 1930s Soviet leader Josef Stalin engineered a famine in Ukraine that killed millions, including many Mennonites. Some Mennonite farmers were killed by the Soviets during anti-Kulak campaign that targeted prosperous peasants in Soviet Ukraine. Then Stalin had 750,000 people executed during a bloody purge of political opponents called "the Great Terror."
"You look at the 1930s in Ukraine, just indescribable, but then you look at what happened next in the Holocaust, also indescribable," said Friesen.
The MCC helped some Mennonites and Volksdeutsche come to Canada who were otherwise ineligible to come because of their war records, she said, but it is difficult to know how many. Some of the answers may be in archives in Ukraine that are now threatened by Russian drones and missiles.
"It is heartbreaking," said Friesen. "We are finally at the stage to tell the story more fully and these archives might not be there to do it."
In 1948 the MCC helped Jacob Loitjens emigrate to Paraguay from the Netherlands. In 1950 a Dutch court sentenced Loitjens to life in prison for war crimes. In 1962, Loitjens, his wife and three children came to Canada from Paraguay with MCC help and settled in Vancouver where he taught biology at UBC. He joined the First Mennonite Church in Vancouver and in 1972 he became a Canadian citizen.
In the early 1990s he was stripped of his citizenship and deported, but not before the MCC and the Mennonite church publicly defended Loitjens and spoke against his deportation.
This is not just Mennonite history but Canadian history and everyone should care about it because many different immigrant groups are implicated in the very difficult story of the Holocaust, said Friesen in an interview from Winnipeg.
"The antisemitism in the Christian churches within the broader society still needs to be addressed, we can't ignore that element of it," said Friesen. "Because it is important to be very honest about ways in which prejudice has encouraged people to take violent actions."
After the publication of the special newsletter, MCC leadership went through training and workshops on antisemitism.
"We take a firm and clear stand against antisemitism as an organization," said Bauman. "We have ongoing anti-racism work both internationally and, in the U.S., and Canada."
In the summer of 1949, the UN's International Refugee Organization ordered a pause in the MCC's immigration work, says Rodal. American intelligence had screened some Mennonite refugees preparing to board a ship in Germany bound for the U.S. and Canada.
The Americans found many former members of the Ukrainian-SS and former members of the Nazi's mobile death squads - the Einsatzgruppen - and German citizens among the Mennonite refugees selected by the MCC, says Rodal. The UN refugee agency passed on the warning to Ottawa that many Mennonites had volunteered for the Nazi's "most reprehensible units," says Rodal.
Cornelius Klassen, who played a key role as the European director of refugee and resettlement services for the MCC from 1945-1954, communicated directly with A.L. Jolliffe, the Director of the Immigration Branch and the federal Department of Mines and Resources, to explain.
"He presented the argument the Mennonites were tricked into German citizenship by being asked to sign blank forms when they were in the camps in Germany, forms which turned out to be applications for German citizenship," says Rodal.
Jolliffe was skeptical, but there was pressure to bring more immigrants to Canada as labour shortages were holding back economic growth, says Rodal.
The International Refugee Organization came up with a compromise - allow this ship load of Mennonites to sail, and those who were now barred from the U.S. could go to either Canada or Paraguay. The names of future immigrants would be screened with name checks at the Berlin Document Centre - a huge repository of all German military records.
They were allowed to come to Canada, and about a year later in April 1950, Prime Minister Louis St. Laurent, the Security Panel and the cabinet decided to drop the blanket ban on German nationals and Volksdeutsche, says Rodal.
The change was made quietly, by orders-in-council, says Rodal. They did that knowing it was impossible to get reliable information on war records for those from Eastern Europe, says Rodal. They were allowed to come if they had relatives of friends in Canada willing to sponsor them, and if they passed the medical examination, says Rodal.
When a Toronto couple sponsored their two sons, both former members of the Waffen-SS, the Security Panel cleared them because the parents were "reliable and respectable people," and the West German government had asked Canadian Immigration officials in Germany to approve the visa requests and reunite the family in Toronto, says Rodal.
The director of immigration in Ottawa cabled the head of the Canadian Immigration Mission in Germany about the approval of visas for the two brothers - "strictly secret information."
In the spring of 1950, the British wanted Canada to take in 8,500 Ukrainians as immigrants. British intelligence claimed the men were from the regular German army - the Wehrmacht. But they were in fact Ukrainian SS troops who fled west to escape the Soviet Red Army, says Rodal.
The plan was delayed when Samuel Bronfman, head of the Canadian Jewish Congress, warned Ottawa it was about to allow a division of Waffen-SS into the country.
The story of the Ukrainian-SS division is not well known in Canada, which is why a former member received two standing ovations in Parliament. Rodal is clear in her report - the SS did not have to force people in Soviet Ukraine to take up arms against their Jewish neighbours or the Soviet Red Army.
"That members joined the division voluntarily is an established fact," says Rodal. "The response to the call for volunteers was overwhelming."
According to German records cited by Rodal, 80,000 Ukrainian men volunteered for the SS, and 50,000 were accepted. "The quota set for the unit was over-filled many times over," says the Rodal report, "and tens of thousands of volunteers had to be rejected."
By the summer of 1943, the Nazi's formed a new Ukrainian Division with 13,000 men. Many of the other volunteers went into Nazi-organized police formations.
The Germans were astonished, says Rodal, even after so many Ukrainians helped the mobile Nazi death squads - the Einsatzgruppen units that massacred 1.5 million people, mostly Jews in 1941 and 1942 in Soviet Ukraine, Eastern Europe, and the Baltics.
The Ukrainian-SS Division fought against the Red Army at the Battle of Brody in July 1943 and 10,000 Waffen-SS were killed or captured, says Rodal. The Germans had no trouble in re-filling the ranks with Nazi police formations and concentration camp guards, says Rodal.
"It is likely that at least some persons who had served with Nazi sponsored Ukrainian police/militia units used in killing actions in 1941-1942 found their way into the ranks of the division either before or after the Battle of Brody," says the Rodal report.
The Nazis called it the 14th Galician Waffen Grenadier Division. It is sometimes called the Galician SS, the Ukrainian SS, the First Galician or the 14th Grenadiers. Different names for the same unit. Ukrainian-SS members massacred people in Delatyn, Nadvirna, Lawoczne, Obertyn and Kossów.
After the Battle of Brody, the Ukrainian-SS Division headed west and surrendered to the British. Less than two weeks before the end of the war, the Ukrainian-SS was rebranded as the Ukrainian Army of Liberation, or the OUN. The British were suspicious and kept 8,500 of them in prisoner of war camps for years, first in Italy and then in the U.K.
When they were finally allowed to come to Canada, the RCMP was supposed to screen them, but the Mounties relied on the British, who didn't look too deeply into the backgrounds of the disciplined troops with no badges on their uniforms and no identity papers in their pockets.
The outrage of the Canadian Jewish Congress slowed, but did not stop the immigration of the former Ukrainian-SS members to Canada, says Rodal.
The international embarrassment following the standing ovations for Hunka in the House of Commons is not the only time members of the Ukrainian-SS have given Canada an international black eye.
There is a cenotaph in an Oakville cemetery topped with the symbol of the Ukrainian-SS, and the 200 or so grave monuments around it also have the symbol - a stylized silhouette of a lion, standing on its hind legs and surround by three crowns. It is a Nazi symbol banned in Germany.
St. Volodymyr Ukrainian Cemetery at 1280 Dundas St. W. in Oakville is on land owned by St. Volodymyr's Ukrainian Orthodox Cathedral on Bathurst Street in Toronto.
In Edmonton there is a bust of Roman Shukhevych in front of a Ukrainian youth centre. Shukhevych was the commander of the Nachtigall Battalion, made up of 650 Ukrainian volunteers who perpetrated massacres of ethnic Poles in Galicia-Volhynia and Jews in Lviv.
In the spring of 1951, again by order-in-council, the blanket immigration ban on former SS and Waffen-SS members was relaxed and replaced with a case-by-case approach which focused on voluntary service versus forced conscription, says Rodal.
RCMP war crimes investigators found 15 members of the Ukrainian-SS had entered Canada before the immigration rules were relaxed, says Rodal.
In October 1955, the immigration restrictions put in place after the war were completely removed. Former members of the SS, the Waffen-SS and SA became admissible to Canada, says Rodal.
That decision was made by a subgroup of the Security Panel, and it did not seek the approval of the full Security Panel or the federal cabinet, says Rodal. It was treated as an administrative procedure within the discretionary authority of the responsible minister, says Rodal.
At that time the federal Minister of Immigration and Citizenship was Jack Pickersgill. As the immigration restrictions for former members of the SS were relaxed, he gave the opposite impression to a reporter.
In 1955 Pickersgill said in interview he said that German immigrants were carefully screened for communist sympathies, or membership in the Nazi Party and the SS.
"The best that can be said for this public stance is that it is untruthful," says Rodal, adding the government continued to imply that mere membership in the SS or collaboration with the enemy was an absolute bar to entering Canada.
The changes were kept quiet.
Millions of Europeans had fresh memories of SS atrocities, and Canada did not want its lax immigration policies for former SS members to become a public scandal among European allies, says Rodal.
Terry Pender is a Waterloo Region Record reporter. Reach him via: tpender@therecord.com

During the occupation of the Soviet Ukraine, Hamm collaborated with the Nazis, and after evacuating to Stutthof, Poland, in 1944 he was an administrator at a Mennonite-owned munitions factory that used slave labour from the Stutthof concentration camp. After the war, he organized a list of all known Mennonite refugees for the MCC and led efforts to free Mennonite POWs interned in Allied camps. 'They need action today': MPP worries about Niagara's family physician shortage

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/they-need-action-today/docview/3143664528/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27They+need+action+today%27%3A+MPP+worries+about+Niagara%27s+family+physician+shortage&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-12-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With there being more than 2.2 million people across Ontario without a family doctor and 106 physicians needed in Niagara, Wayne Gates is calling on the province to implement "real solutions" to resolve the shortage.
At Queen's Park Thursday, the New Democrat MPP for the Niagara Falls riding shared his concerns - a week after he tabled a private member's motion calling for immediate action.
He said Niagara Falls is the municipality with the largest shortage in the region, requiring 36 more family doctors to meet the needs of its growing population.
City councillors, he noted, are considering offering $100,000 incentives to lure new doctors, allowing Niagara Falls to compete with the recruitment efforts of neighbouring towns and cities.
But doing so means local taxpayers are "on the hook for this government's mistakes," he said.
Gates compared the competition for family doctors to an auction with increasing bids.
"It makes no sense to me," he said during question period, broadcast online at ola.org.
Gates said about 40 per cent of Niagara's population is comprised of older adults "who can't wait years for a solution."
"They need a doctor today," he said. "We have local health-care experts we have met with that have real solutions right now, but they need a government willing to take that action."
He asked Premier Doug Ford to commit "to take action today, or does he believe seniors and families don't deserve a family doctor in the province of Ontario?"
Health Minister Sylvia Jones said the province has recently invested in establishing 78 new primary health-care teams across the province, including in Niagara where "7,800 more people are now connected to primary care."
"That's the work we've been doing. When we make investments in our colleges and universities, expanding the number of seats available for young people who wish to train as physicians, as nurses, as nurse practitioners, that's when you start to see a difference in communities."
In September, the Ontario College of Family Physicians (OCFP) said the number of Niagarans without a family doctor had grown to more than 73,000 and expects that number to swell to more than 141,000 by 2026, as Niagara's population continues to grow and more physicians retire or close their medical practices.
Gates, in Toronto, also discussed concerns in Fort Erie, where residents have been relying on Niagara Health's urgent-care centre to meet primary health care needs.
"Urgent care centres play a crucial role as a primary care safety net, and can keep people out of emergency rooms while saving lives," Gates said.
Gates asked the government to commit to continued long-term operation of the UCC and other services at the Fort Erie hospital that is slated for closure after the opening of the new south Niagara Falls hospital, slated for 2028.
The private member's motion Gates tabled last week, developed in collaboration with physicians and family health teams, calls on the province to increase funding for primary health care and allied health professionals, reduce administrative requirements for doctors allowing them to devote more time to patient care.
Gates plans to bring the motion forward for debate early next year.
In the meantime, it is garnering support from health-care professionals.
Niagara North Family Health Team chair Marti Jurmain and executive director Stephen Szeplaki sent a letter to the provincial government on Tuesday in support of the motion, calling it "an important step toward ensuring that all Ontarians have timely access to primary care while improving workplace conditions for those who provide it."
"Niagara North Family Health Team has seen firsthand the growing challenges family physicians and health-care teams face," said the letter, provided to The Standard by Gates's office.
"Increased funding for allied health professionals is vital to the success of team-based care, which not only enhances patient outcomes but also eases the workload on physicians.
CAPTION: Niagara Falls MPP Wayne Gates.Wayne Gates photo

Document 70 of 249

Another winter in car becomes a 'vicious cycle': To find a job requires housing and housing requires job, says Welland man

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/another-winter-car-becomes-vicious-cycle/docview/3143664083/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Another+winter+in+car+becomes+a+%27vicious+cycle%27%3A+To+find+a+job+requires+housing+and+housing+requires+job%2C+says+Welland+man&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-12-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With no house to call home as temperatures plummet, Richard Tremblay and his dog are attempting to weather another winter without a home.
For the past two years, Tremblay, who lives with diabetes, and his dog, Fergus, an American Eskimo, have called his 2010 Hyundai Genesis Coupe home, and its back seat their bedroom.
"I'll take any chance I can to get into a place in just about any condition, but those don't exist, at least that I've found," said the 46-year-old former machinist from Welland, who is preparing to hunker down to eke out another season in the vehicle.
Niagara Centre MPP Jeff Burch took up Tremblay's case in a letter sent to Ontario Premier Doug Ford on Tuesday, two days before Ford's government introduced changes to give municipalities and police more money immediately and powers in legislation to help find shelter and services for homeless people - and punish scofflaws.
With homelessness rising and growing numbers of encampments, the province is earmarking an additional $75.5 million for shelters and affordable housing and toughening up trespassing laws.
"Enough is enough," Ford said Thursday. "This has to stop and it will stop. Families deserve to play in and enjoy their parks without fearing for the safety of their kids.
"These encampments are taking over public spaces with illegal drug use happening out in the open, creating huge safety risks for people and communities," said the premier, as critics warned he's seeking to criminalize homelessness that has grown worse under his watch.
Burch's letter outlined homelessness situations like Tremblay's, criticizing the premier for "giving mayors permission to tear down tents and move desperate people along," while offering no real solutions.
He chided Ford's approach to homelessness that suggested people "get an application and drop it off at one of these companies and start working."
Burch said that's not feasible for unhoused people, who lack the simplest necessities to get a job.
"He's (Tremblay) one of those people who shouldn't be homeless and if he needs financial assistance to get him through a rough period, it should be enough so he can house and feed himself while he finds a job," said Burch.
People like Tremblay, living in their cars, on friends' couches or in temporary accommodations without long-term housing stability are considered hidden homeless, and about 15 per cent of unhoused people in Canada are part of that population.
Tremblay has experienced ups and downs over the past few years, including multiple hospital stints due to ketoacidosis, complications of diabetes and homelessness.
His unhoused journey started in the early days of the COVID-19 pandemic. After losing his job, he quickly fell behind on rent, resulting in eviction.
Tremblay lost most of his possessions when that happened, including sentimental items left by his father.
"I lost 80 per cent of everything I owned including all my tax files, all the stuff my mom had given me from my dad when he passed away, things I can't get back," he said.
Since then, Tremblay has struggled to find accommodations, due to limited employment, help or money, resulting in him taking to his car to survive.
It's tough to find a job, as it requires housing and housing requires having a job, he said.
"Ultimately I have no income to support or be a feasible renter in a (landlord's) eyes," he said.
Tremblay said his 78-year-old mother has helped him, despite his protestations, as he only receives basic needs from Ontario Works of about $350 a month to survive on.
Living in his car has impacted his mental health, causing him to isolate from people he knows.
"If I've recoiled from friends, it's because they think I'm living in my car by choice because it's been so long, but that's not by choice," he said.
In his desperation, Tremblay considered going to a shelter, but was told he would have to leave his dog, as most shelters do not accommodate animals.
"There's no point in me calling any of them, because they will not accept someone with a pet, that's ridiculous," Tremblay said.
"Pets bring people comfort, half the time they mean more to people than other people, you can understand why they're called man's best friend."
Looking for help, Tremblay approached Burch's office.
As wages and social assistance rates stagnate, Burch said more people are having to take drastic measures just to survive.
People like Tremblay are falling into a "vicious cycle," said Burch.
"When someone is homeless through no fault of their own, it's impossible for them to find a job, like the premier seems to think, as they're spending all their time trying to survive and there's no time to devote to finding a job."
With files from the Toronto Star

Document 71 of 249

Province under fire for police funding: OPP communities get relief, Niagara taxpayers get bill

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/province-under-fire-police-funding/docview/3142492677/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+under+fire+for+police+funding%3A+OPP+communities+get+relief%2C+Niagara+taxpayers+get+bill&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-12-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Niagara is asking for its share of provincial funding to cover increasing costs tied to implementation of the Community Safety and Policing Act.
Although the province this month announced more than $77 million in financial relief for municipalities served by Ontario Provincial Police to help offset cost increases, those policed by local services have been left to hit up homeowners.
Fort Erie Mayor Wayne Redekop - chair of Niagara Region's budget committee - said the cost of implementing the legislation contributed to the 13.2 per cent budget hike for next year's Niagara Regional Police budget.
It is "absolutely" another example of provincial downloading, he said.
"This is another issue where the province mandates certain things to be done by municipalities and those have costs associated with them, but we never see the money needed to cover those additional mandates," Redekop said.
"This is a situation where the police have been told you have to start doing this to ensure you have safe communities, but there's no money coming from the province."
Jeff Burch, Niagara Centre MPP and the NDP's municipal affairs critic, said policing funds should be provided "across the board," including to areas served by municipal police services.
"There's an issue of fairness there," he said.
The legislation that came into effect on April 1, replacing the Police Services Act, includes sweeping changes for policing in Ontario - modernizing law enforcement while addressing governance and oversight, according to information about the legislation posted on NRP's website.
It lists more than 100 bylaws approved by Niagara's police services board members related to the act, making numerous changes related to how officers respond to various crimes, officer training and equipment.
Redekop said the province needs to help all municipalities foot the bill to implement those changes.
"If the province is going to help out all those municipalities that are served by the OPP, and they also helped out in Toronto, I don't see why they won't be helping out with other regions or municipalities that have their own police forces that are facing the same pressures," he said.
In an email, Ministry of the Solicitor General spokesperson Brent Ross said OPP-policed municipalities are billed by the province for their services, and the proposed financial relief will be applied to those bills.
"The ministry provides several grants that supply funding to police services to support additional costs and projects," Ross added.
Niagara Regional Police Chief Bill Fordy would not comment on the matter.
Police services board chair Jen Lawson did not respond to an interview request for this story.
But the issue of Queen's Park fairness isn't only about policing, Redekop said.
He listed several examples of costs that should be covered by the province, such as those tied to ambulance off-load delays, homelessness and mental illness.
"These are all issues that have a social aspect to them, and they are really issues that should be funded from upper levels of government," Redekop said.
Burch said the NDP's platform includes uploading many costs that were downloaded to municipalities, "such as heath care and housing costs and social services."
"That would, of course, free up more capacity for municipalities to deal with things like policing, which is under a lot of pressure because of homelessness and poverty issues as well," he said.
"Downloading is the opposite of what we should be doing when municipalities are under tremendous pressure, but this government has blamed all its troubles on municipalities from housing and homelessness to policing, and downloading more responsibilities is the last thing that should be happening."
Burch said the cost of implementing the Community Safety and Policing Act has been discussed several times in the legislature, with a particular focus on smaller communities facing "huge pressures" to cover double-digit increases.
"Downloading responsibilities and giving a little bit of money to a few smaller municipalities is not going to solve the problem," he said.
CAPTION: Niagara Centre New Democrat MPP Jeff Burch.metroland file photo

Readers write Letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write-letters-editor/docview/3142491989/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write+Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-12-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hitting
the trifecta
Saturday's opinion page, Dec. 7
Kudos on all three Dec. 7 opinion page articles.
Micheal P. Hallé nailed it when he responded to the story about Ontario Tourism Minister Stan Cho's visit to Niagara. Hallé suggested local consultation and environmental considerations must be integral in the development of the new strategy to double tourism numbers in Niagara.
Likewise, Doreen Nicoll is spot on in saying the foot-dragging by the Ontario government on the epidemic of violence against women is totally unacceptable. Premier Doug Ford's government should pass Bill 173, Intimate Partner Violence Epidemic Act (2024) without further delay.
And finally, Leila Sarangi and David MacDonald accurately outline the ineffectual nature of provincial and federal one-time payments and a GST holiday. They rightly focus on the most vulnerable - the ever-increasing "below the poverty line" population and the working poor.
Roger Demers, Welland
Astounding
sentence
Discharge for Toronto duo who defrauded Welland man in grandparent scam, Nov. 29
I read with disgust the article on the sentencing of two men involved in scamming an 80-year old man out of $9,000. When that money was received, the individuals realized they had an easy "mark" and tried for another $10,000 before police got involved.
I am astounded Judge Fergus ODonnell saw fit to grant both a conditional discharge because, in not doing so, they risked being deported.
Being afforded an opportunity to come to another country and pursue citizenship is a privilege that is earned and should not be taken for granted. These accused thought nothing of committing a crime in their new country.
For their lawyer to request adjournments to determine what impact a conviction would have is immaterial. Let the chips fall where they may.
Damian J. Parrent,
Niagara Falls
Remaining
upbeat
With all the grim news, it is hard to remain upbeat.
I'm no Pollyanna, but I find it encouraging in my daily newspaper the return of humour and hope. Stories about people helping others and William Thomas's droll Thursday column.
For me, there has been a fine upwards turn toward reporting on theatre, music, art, innovative restaurants and crafts. It used to seem sports reviews were the reigning recreation for readers, while many consider recreation to include the arts. John Law's coverage of the local Foster Festival, musical events, artistic events and much more is appreciated.
No longer is arts reporting only about the Shaw Festival. This pleases me and, I expect, also gives others relief to know they can take comfort in arts, dance, theatre, good food, good books and crafts.
Gail Benjafield,
St. Catharines

Letters welcome
Send them to letters@ni-
agaradailies.
com or mail to PO Box 5031 Stn Main, St. Catharines, Ont., L2R 7T4. Letters should be 250 words or less, and may be edited for length and clarity. Please include your name, address and phone number for verification purposes.

Document 73 of 249

Solve the Crisis campaign topic of neighbourhood alliance to end homelessness

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/solve-crisis-campaign-topic-neighbourhood/docview/3141356123/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Solve+the+Crisis+campaign+topic+of+neighbourhood+alliance+to+end+homelessness&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-12-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Several St. Catharines neighbourhood and church associations have signed onto a letter suggesting solutions to Ontario's homelessness crisis that do not entail use of the Canadian Constitution's controversial notwithstanding clause.
The letter is part of the Solve the Crisis campaign, which in August was launched by the Ontario's Big City Mayors caucus of 29 municipal council leaders.
Ontario should appoint a minister or ministry as a single point of contact to address housing, mental health and addictions, the letter said. It should also provide municipalities with the tools, resources and funding necessary to appropriately transition people in encampments to supportive housing and offer services such as counselling and treatment options through community hubs.
St. Catharines resident Jeff Hurst, the Haig Neighbours association chair, said finding permanent solutions to homelessness is needed in the city and Niagara.
"We're seeing folks in our parks and our community in distress or in trouble and they need help, so we're asking what's to be done and when will help come?" he said.
Hurst said any organization or neighbourhood association can sign onto the letter to show support.
The letter can be found at solvethecrisis.ca. In it, affiliated associations avoid talking about the notwithstanding clause as they believe it is unnecessary and only exacerbates the homelessness crisis.
"We're well aware of the current push by some Ontario mayors to use the notwithstanding clause to forcefully evict people from encampments and the decision to leave that out of our letter was intentional because we don't feel that's part of the solution," Hurst said.
"That's why we chose to stand with St. Catharines city council and Niagara regional council endorsing the Solve the Crisis campaign, as we believe that's a solution-oriented plan; as being good neighbours is about finding solutions and not necessarily finding differences," he said.
Debate over use of the constitutional clause to break up encampments started when Ontario Premier Doug Ford, who has been under pressure from cash-strapped municipalities for funding and resources to deal with issues pertaining to homelessness, dared mayors of the province's largest cities to ask him to use it.
Invoking the constitutional mechanism empowers the province to override a court ruling that found it is a violation of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms for a municipality to evict encampments when people have nowhere to go.
In response to Ford's challenge, 12 mayors from the Ontario Big City Mayors caucus, including St. Catharines' Mat Siscoe, signed a letter asking for the clause to be invoked if necessary.
The mayors of Ontario's two largest cities - Toronto and Hamilton - did not sign and have opposed use of the clause.
In days following, Welland Mayor Frank Campion and Niagara Falls Mayor Jim Diodati backed use of the clause and have since won support from their city councils.
An opposition group of more than 70 councillors and mayors, including four councillors from Niagara, responded by issuing its own letter opposing the move.
With winter temperatures fast approaching, Syl Roach, a member of the Queenston Neighbours association, called for warming centres and emergency overnight shelters to be opened to meet people where they are at, while more permanent solutions, such as the 80-unit affordable housing development at 320 Geneva St., are built.
"People are desperate, they're losing hope, which is the greatest thing to lose, because when you know nobody cares, what else do you have to lose?" she said. "In my view, we can't police our way out of this crisis and we have to speak up."
Roach said her neighbourhood now teeters on the "cusp of gentrification," when what is needed is affordable housing to support people rather than displace them.
"Let's bring some affordable housing for all ... affordable for people working in our neighbourhood, because it means more services to Queenston Street ... but we can't forget our neighbours in need, they are the most vulnerable," she said.
St. Catharines MPP Jennie Stevens said it's important to listen to people and experiences from the neighbourhood associations who have boots on the ground.
"I support what they're doing and they're the people on the ground; they're the people listening to what's going on within their communities and they bring that to me," she said.
Stevens said the province should be the level of government responsible for building housing infrastructure, not municipalities. "Municipalities deserve to spend their money on municipal needs, sidewalks, sewers, parks, swimming pools, that's where the municipalities should be focusing on," she said.
The St. Catharines Downtown Association joined the neighbourhood groups in the call for solutions, as businesses have been impacted by the fallout from the homelessness crisis.
"We've heard loud and clear, solutions are needed to ensure those in need have supports, because it is impacting our businesses," said Rachel Braithwaite, the downtown association's executive director.
"Systemic changes are needed, not just specifically around homelessness and encampments per se, but on a much broader level with regards to mental health." She said intergovernmental communication is needed in solving the crisis and supported creation of a ministry to bridge the gap on these issues.
"There's people falling through the cracks quite literally because the ministries don't talk to one another because programs and supports aren't funded," she said. "That's the biggest gap we need to have addressed to provide support."
St. Patrick's Coun. Caleb Ratzlaff emphasized the associations are focused on "what unites us rather than what divides us."
Ratzlaff said this is a humanitarian approach for solutions rather than the reactionary approach that has been tried in the past. "We want municipalities to work with the province to address the root cause of the problem," he said.
"Everyone recognizes cities, with all the other pressures already on our budget, don't have the tools necessary to address the root causes and we've just been simply putting Band-Aid after Band-Aid on an open wound."
Ratzlaff said the neighbourhood associations are asking for real and timely solutions that take people into consideration.
"(Things) will get worse the longer you wait to address the root cause of this situation, that's what's happened for years; we've failed to address the root causes of the housing and mental health crisis," he said.
CAPTION: From left: Syl Roach, of the Queenston Neighbours group; Jeff Hurst, of the Haig Neighbours group; and Rachel Braithwaite, of St. Catharines Downtown Association, want to solve the homelessness crisis without use of the notwithstanding clause.St. Patrick's Ward 4 Coun. Caleb Ratzlaff takes part in the Coldest Night of the Year walk in St. Catharines last February.St. Catharines MPP Jennie StevensJulie Jocsak St. Catharines StandardKarena Walter St. Catharines Standard file photo

CREDIT: Matthew P. Barker Reporter
