Document 74 of 249

Ford ready to override courts and 'dismantle' homeless encampments: Could give 'enhanced powers' to municipalities and police

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ford-ready-override-courts-dismantle-homeless/docview/3141355655/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ford+ready+to+override+courts+and+%27dismantle%27+homeless+encampments%3A+Could+give+%27enhanced+powers%27+to+municipalities+and+police&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-12-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford is prepared to override the courts by giving municipalities and police "enhanced legislative powers" to "dismantle" homeless encampments across Ontario.
"We are acting to put an end to the public disorder, drug use and trafficking and loss of public space that have resulted from the widespread growth in encampments," Ford said in a letter to 12 Ontario mayors.
His comments came in a response mayors who appealed to Ford to enact tougher laws to help them deal with encampments and "where necessary use the notwithstanding clause" to ensure any efforts aren't overturned by the courts.
"I am confident that the legislative authorities the province is preparing are common sense, practical and entirely aligned with the Charter of Rights and Freedoms, especially for the law-abiding residents who simply want to enjoy the benefits of their local public spaces," the premier told them.
"For that reason, I do not expect the legislation will need to proactively invoke Section 33 of the Charter of Rights and Freedoms," he said.
"However, should the courts interfere with our shared goal of effectively addressing and clearing out encampments using these enhanced tools, with your support, our government is fully prepared to use the notwithstanding clause."
His letter came after the mayors of Barrie, Brampton, Brantford, Cambridge, Chatham-Kent, Clarington, Oakville, Oshawa, Pickering, St. Catharines, Sudbury and Windsor appealed to him two weeks ago for help.
They urged his government to "become an intervener on any court case that restricts the ability of municipalities to regulate and prohibit encampments" and "strengthen the existing system of mandatory community-based and residential mental health care and to expand service to treat those who have severe and debilitating addictions."
While there are more than 1,000 people living in tents in Toronto parks throughout the city, Mayor Olivia Chow did not sign the letter.
The premier agreed there is "an urgent need to do more and I am thrilled that you have shown considerable leadership in putting forward concrete and actionable solutions."
"Ontario is finalizing the details of new initiatives, including enhanced legislative powers, that will be responsive to your requests," he wrote.
"The new legislation and supporting initiatives will achieve the outcomes all of us agree are necessary in order to protect public safety and dismantle encampments."
Legislation, which is expected before the house rises for the holiday break next Thursday, will include a funding boost "to create more capacity in our shelter systems, supported by the new accountability measures to ensure these funds support dismantling encampments."
As well, it will gives police "new tools and authorities" to further curb the use of illicit drugs in public.
Brampton Mayor Patrick Brown said he was "very pleased with the response from the premier."
"We don't permit illicit drugs in our Peel shelters and it's why we have seen encampments grow among those struggling with addictions," said Brown.
"We simply can't have dangerous drug use in our parks and public spaces. The commitment to clearly prohibit it and back it up legally is helpful," he said.
"I just hope the next shoe to fall is compassionate intervention for addictions."
In Waterloo last year, a judge ruled that evicting those living in homeless encampments would be a violation of their Charter rights if no shelter spaces are available. A similar judicial ruling was also handed down in Kingston.
Use of the notwithstanding clause - which Ford has invoked before, being the first Ontario premier to use the Constitution's nuclear option - allows a government to pass legislation overriding certain rights.
On Tuesday, the auditor general criticized him for his decision to close 10 of 17 Ontario's supervised consumption sites in favour of 19 Homelessness and Addiction Recovery Treatment hubs.
Auditor general Shelley Spence said shuttering the facilities near schools and daycares was made "without proper planning."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford says the government will put an end to the loss of public space regarding homeless encampments in Ontario.Tents are shown at an encampment in Toronto's Alexandra Park. Ontario Premier Doug Ford says new legislation is coming that will target homeless encampments across the province.Chris Young The Canadian PressChris Young The Canadian Press

Document 75 of 249

Petition calls for action on crisis: Condo owners support using notwithstanding clause to help deal with encampments

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/petition-calls-action-on-crisis/docview/3140792527/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Petition+calls+for+action+on+crisis%3A+Condo+owners+support+using+notwithstanding+clause+to+help+deal+with+encampments&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-12-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Rob McConnell had finally had enough.
His wife, Laura Rogers-McConnell, needed to walk their dog on some grass near their Gale Crescent condo in the late evening.
The dog, an "energetic and rambunctious" mini golden doodle still in the puppy stage, showed signs it needed to relieve itself, and her husband's back was bothering him.
Once outside, Rogers-McConnell noticed two women in the near distance arguing.
One was screaming like a "banshee." Without warning, the other, who had noticed Rogers-McConnell and her dog, abandoned the argument, sprinted down the St. Catharines street towards her, and began demanding money "aggressively."
Rogers-McConnell said she was "petrified" as she rushed to pull her fob out, get inside and push the door closed. The woman wasn't taking no for an answer.
They called police to report the incident, which also motivated McConnell to start a petition on Change.org that supports using the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms' notwithstanding clause to "end the crisis of homelessness in St. Catharines."
"I just figured something has to be done, and it seems that everything was lopsided towards the politicians who did not want the notwithstanding clause," McConnell said. "They don't live in the area. They don't know what goes on here day and night. If we don't have a police, fire or rescue down here three or four times a day, something's wrong."
The retirees moved to St. Catharines less than three years ago from their Crystal Beach home, which was increasingly difficult to maintain.
They knew a little about the homeless issues in the Gale Crescent area, but McConnell said the situation continues to deteriorate and nobody feels safe there.
On the afternoon of Nov. 22, perpetrators gunned down two men on Division Street. One victim died. The other is in an out-of-region hospital. One of the suspects is still at large, and police warned the public he is armed and dangerous.
"I have brought up concerns with politicians before, and they say 'We're doing the best we can,' " McConnell said. "They tell us they can't really do anything. Their hands are tied.
"I believe in the system if you take the appropriate steps, so I started the petition. I think most people in our building have had run-ins with homeless people, who are insulted if you don't give them money. They intimidate seniors."
At the end of October, 29 mayors from the Ontario Big City Mayors caucus, including St. Catharines Mayor Mat Siscoe, sent a letter to Premier Doug Ford requesting provincial action to help municipalities with issues related to mental health, addiction and homeless encampments - and "where necessary use the notwithstanding clause."
Using the notwithstanding clause would empower the province to override court rulings that found evicting people from encampments violates the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, if there weren't enough shelter beds to accommodate them.
Siscoe told The Standard he doesn't want to evict people experiencing homelessness from encampments. What he wants is the ability to move encampments away from schools and residential neighbourhoods.
Welland Mayor Frank Campion and Niagara Falls Mayor Jim Diodati also sent letters of support to the premier.
The use of the notwithstanding clause on the issue is already running into headwinds.
Locally, municipal politicians, including Greg Miller, Caleb Ratzlaff and Haley Bateman of St. Catharines, and Lori Lococo of Niagara Falls, joined dozens of Ontario municipal councillors and mayors in signing a letter with concerns about using the notwithstanding clause.
The letter, posted on social media, acknowledged the humanitarian crisis but maintained that no municipality should be above the law, and elected municipal representatives are sworn to uphold democracy and human rights.
The petition says the encampments and a temporary housing facility are feeding a growing crisis in the surrounding neighbourhood that includes syringes scattered on sidewalks, human feces, discarded underwear, increased crime rates, the open use and dealing of drugs, prostitution and carrying objects for use as weapons.
Rogers-McConnell questions how many people experiencing homelessness and addiction want help getting off the street.
She worked in a mental health ward for about a decade as a nurse.
"I was thrown up against a wall," Rogers-McConnell said. "I was raised by my neck with my feet dangling, the whole nine yards, but it was in a controlled environment.
"These people are out on the street, and we can't call a code white to get some assistance. We can't do anything."
McConnell hopes the petition brings some attention to the issues.
"You can't be stealing," Rogers-McConnell said. "You can't be walking around with a knife threatening people. You shouldn't shoot up drugs on the sidewalk. These things aren't hearsay. I've seen them.
"It's like there are no consequences for breaking the law. They are laughing at us as they party, do drugs, drink and carry on. They say they have their rights. What about the rights of the residents? Don't they count, too?"
CAPTION: Laura Rogers-McConnell's incident while walking her dog near her Gale Crescent residence inspired a petition supporting the use of the notwithstanding clause to deal with encampments.Bob Tymczyszyn St. Catharines Standard

CREDIT: Bill Sawchuk Reporter

Document 76 of 249

'We're very sound fiscally,' DSBN chair Sue Barnett says as she retains leadership of trustees

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/were-very-sound-fiscally-dsbn-chair-sue-barnett/docview/3139098113/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27We%27re+very+sound+fiscally%2C%27+DSBN+chair+Sue+Barnett+says+as+she+retains+leadership+of+trustees&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-12-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: District School Board of Niagara's leadership team remains intact as it enters the third year of its elected term, with both Sue Barnett and Alex Bradnam re-elected by trustees for another year as chair and vice-chair, respectively.
Barnett is in her 15th year representing Welland, and Bradnam is finishing his 11th year as trustee for St. Catharines/Niagara-on-the-Lake.
In an interview, Barnett said she had been contemplating whether to return to the position of chair, but several people reached out asking her to stay, and while it takes "time out of your life, it's worth it in the end."
"We are fiscally responsible, we're innovative - there's a lot of adjectives I could use to describe this group of trustees. We have a broad spectrum of knowledge. Each and every one of us comes from a different walk of life, and you need to play to the strengths of your board," she said.
"You have to cover all angles, and having a board of trustees, I think, is easier when we do have that."
Niagara trustees are entering the second half of a four-year term. Barnett said this board's strength has been its collaboration, focus on student achievement and making wise decisions with the use of its resources. As well, it continues to have a good working relationship with DSBN's senior management team.
Not all trustees agree, but "every viewpoint is listened to, the issue is looked at from all angles and everybody's voice is important."
"A board of trustees has to work as a unit, a collaborative unit. We have to have the focus where it needs to be and in our case, I'm very, very lucky because there are 11 trustees that are all super hyper focused on student achievement, safety for students and staff and moving forward in the education system to make it work for us," she said.
"We have become, as a school board, more open to public input. I think we've become more knowledgeable about the ins and outs of how to work and use the Ministry of Education directives and work to put that into our education system," said Barnett.
"Happy families mean trustees are doing it right and not getting phone calls about things that are not working well."
DSBN has continued to expand. At one time, Barnett said, it could determine kindergarten entry rate by the number of births, but that's no longer the case.
Now it's about looking at who is moving to the region, what they need to succeed and how the board can provide extra language courses as well as additional mental health supports.
"The other thing that we deal with at our board is a variety of different types of schools, and it's all very much area-dependent. What is suitable for one area may not be suitable for another, but you have to look at the big picture," she said.
"We need more school space, (we) need more buildings. The population of Niagara just exploded."
DSBN is the region's largest employer, Barnett said, and voters need to be educated, responsible and understand an elected trustee is going to be "making decisions about millions and millions of dollars" - while also trusting it is going to use those funds "properly."
"We're very sound fiscally, we're very, very good at staying on budget and doing what we have to do," said Barnett.
The public, however, may only pay attention to school boards when controversy arises.
In recent weeks, Premier Doug Ford ordered a review of all Ontario school boards' discretionary spending after a series of controversial decisions, including a $145,000 trip to Italy taken by four Brant Haldimand Norfolk Catholic District School Board trustees.
Barnett said that while other boards are facing troubles, "you really can't paint all school boards with the same paintbrush."
"Every school board is different. What I need down here is very, very different than what people in (other areas) need. I think it's the local people and knowing your community that makes a difference," she said.
"We're putting money into the classroom each and every time we can."
Barnett said DSBN wants to add more specialist high school majors, increase Indigenous curriculum across its schools and ensure more staff members take advanced qualification courses.
"Teaching is a hands-on job and with a number of students in a classroom, with a number of issues that our students face, we need the people there to do the job properly and that's where we have to focus our funding," she said.
CAPTION: Sue Barnett has been re-elected chair of District School Board of Niagara.Julie Jocsak St. Catharines Standard file photo

CREDIT: Victoria Nicolaou Reporter

Letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters-editor/docview/3139095714/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-12-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Profits over health
Province committed to 'big' and 'bold' tourism vision for Niagara: minister
It seems to me the Ontario government has done it again. It never gets citizens' wants or priorities straight.
Looking back over the years, tourism has always been on the front burner and many "improvements" have been made by the government. Yet it never seems to satisfy the tourism industry.
Now the government is coming up with a "big, bold tourism plan" to unleash tourism's "full potential."
I thought it had already done that once or twice.
How about it come out with a "big, bold health-care plan" instead? Goodness knows we could use one.
Improvements in health care in Ontario have always been put on the back burner.
As an ordinary citizen, even I can see any announcement regarding better health care in Ontario is normally smoke and mirrors and all the foot-dragging by the province points in the direction of privatization, a very dangerous approach.
I always thought our health care was the envy of many world citizens.
It's a shame our leaders care more about the profits of shareholders and CEOs and less about proper health care for Ontario's citizens.
Jim Hedden, Niagara Falls
Province, feds are both buying votes
A desperate Trudeau tries a GST relief pivot
With regards to the political cartoon, Ontario Premier Doug Ford is giving $200 cheques to all Ontario residents. That includes the multimillionaires, and those being paid six-figure Sunshine List salaries, to help them buy groceries.
Then there's Prime Minister Justin Trudeau giving out GST rebate cheques. But not to retired seniors living on pensions, who have worked their entire life and paid taxes. The federal government takes 15.5 per cent of my monthly pension cheque in taxes and then also adds to that the amount it takes off my CPP and OAS. Yet, according to Trudeau, I don't count.
This cheque giveaway is another blatant example of politicians trying to buy votes.
Jim Collacott, Chippawa
Trudeau raises Ford
The editorial cartoon points out the absurdity of thinking bribing people with their own money is likely to help those who need it most.
Premier Doug Ford is giving $200 to everyone. But not everyone needs this "gift." Why not double it for those who do? Receiving a $200 cheque from Ford is not going to get me to vote for him.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's gambit in buying votes is bizarre. If you are working and make less than $150,000, you get it. But seniors and the unemployed do not. One wonders which group is likely to need it more.
Then there is the amount. It is like Trudeau thinks he is at the poker table with Ford. I will see your $200 and raise you $50. It is no wonder so many despair for our province and country with this type of leadership.
John Rowan, Welland

Document 78 of 249

Letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters-editor/docview/3133958909/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-11-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Leave us alone
Province committed to 'big' and 'bold' tourism vision for Niagara: minister, Nov. 23
What a depressing front page story.
Tourism, Culture and Gaming Minister Stan Cho listed all that we have in the Niagara region: the falls, wineries, breweries, history, beautiful beaches and a port for cruise ships. But that's not good enough.
He and Ontario Premier Doug Ford want more casinos, more roads to replace those "single two-lane country roads" and an international airport. Their vision is to turn Niagara into another "Los Angeles/Las Vegas."
Many Niagarans want no part of your "vision," Mr. Cho.
We think we're doing quite fine with the 13 million visitors that come every year, and your plans to double that are distressing.
Please leave us alone.
David Fowler, Wainfleet
Failed potential
Province committed to 'big' and 'bold' tourism vision for Niagara: minister, Nov. 23
In this article Tourism Minister Stan Cho says Niagara "has not even come close to unleashing its full potential."
This is another example of lower-tier municipalities in Niagara working in silos and not together for the betterment of the larger Niagara area.
As Cho notes, tourism planning in Niagara has been siloed and it has had a negative impact on the region being able to achieve its full potential. This is very similar to how economic development officers compete with one another to bring business to their municipalities or lower-tier municipalities work in silos to attract doctors with promises of cash bonuses, whereas other municipalities are not doing this with it all having an impact on access to health care.
Just another example of how our governance system is broken in Niagara and the negative impact it is having on moving Niagara forward.
Allen McKay, St. Catharines
Story used incorrect terminology
SIU investigating after NRP fire anti-riot weapons during response to St. Catharines distress call, Nov. 25
Your article had referred to "non-lethal weapons" and described ARWEN (Anti-Riot Weapon Enfield) as "less-than-lethal." Both these terms are not only incorrect, but misguided.
They minimize the danger inherent in the use of alternative police weaponry and condition the public to be outraged if a death occurs. Fatalities can and have occurred from the use of rubber bullets, bean bag rounds and a host of other discharged projectiles, including the wadding from blank rounds.
Your terminology implies death will not occur from these weapons. Not so. The chances may be remote, and certainly nowhere near the odds of death from a bullet, but the risk still exists.
The police do everything possible to preserve life and using tools like the ARWEN rather than firearms are part of those efforts. The correct term is "less lethal." Please get it right so the public can understand no weapon is 100 per cent safe, including those alterative weapons but they're a lot less risky than just shooting people.
Insp. Bob Ciupa (ret.), Niagara Falls

Document 79 of 249

'It's mind-boggling and it's deeply worrying': Homeless advocates oppose possible use of notwithstanding clause

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/mind-boggling-deeply-worrying/docview/3133958897/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27It%27s+mind-boggling+and+it%27s+deeply+worrying%27%3A+Homeless+advocates+oppose+possible+use+of+notwithstanding+clause&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-11-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Confusing. And possibly deadly.
Calls from municipal leaders, including mayors of Niagara's three largest cities, for use of the Canadian Constitution's notwithstanding clause to break up encampments have upset advocates for the unhoused.
"It's very serious to try to use the notwithstanding clause in this way and, in fact, there's an irony because they want to avoid granting homeless people the right to life," said Leilani Farha, a Canadian human rights lawyer and, from 2014 to 2020, the United Nations' special rapporteur on the right to housing.
Debate over use of the constitutional clause to break up encampments started Oct. 31 when Ontario Premier Doug Ford, who has been under pressure from cash-strapped municipalities for funding and resources to deal with issues pertaining to homelessness, dared mayors of the province's largest cities to ask him to use it.
Invoking the constitutional mechanism would empower the province to override a ruling that found it is a violation of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms for a municipality to evict encampments when people have nowhere to go.
In response to Ford's challenge, 12 mayors from the Ontario Big City Mayors caucus, including St. Catharines' Mat Siscoe, signed a letter asking for the clause to be invoked if necessary.
The mayors of Ontario's two largest cities - Toronto and Hamilton - did not sign and have opposed use of the clause.
In days following, Welland Mayor Frank Campion and Niagara Falls Mayor Jim Diodati backed use of the clause and have since won support from their city councils.
An opposition group of more than 70 councillors and mayors, including four councillors from Niagara, responded by issuing its own letter opposing the move.
Farha said there's plenty of evidence supporting not using the clause, especially when there are "no appropriate places for people to go."
Farha said it's safer for people experiencing homelessness to live in encampments rather than be evicted and pushed further into wooded areas or off the beaten path, which could spell disaster.
"In so doing, they make people more susceptible to premature death, so it doesn't get more serious than this," she said. "Where laws, international human rights law or domestic human rights law, are concerned the right to life is the most foundational human right."
To Farha, the mayors supporting use of the clause are in effect suggesting "the right to life should not apply to people living in homeless encampments."
The stance taken by mayors and councillors supporting use of the clause is confusing, said Deanna Ernest, Beyond the Streets Welland vice-president.
"When we first started four years ago, people were under bridges, in bushes and now they're coming out and we always said this was going to happen, eventually it's going to come to the point where you can see it, that's when it's a problem nobody can ignore anymore," she said.
"That's exactly what's happened in Welland and I'm sure every other city before us. Now it's in your face and you can't ignore it anymore and many people are offended by it because there is that stigma."
Ernest said the volunteer Beyond the Streets group does not endorse use of the clause and is troubled Welland's mayor has done so.
St. Catharines regional Coun. Haley Bateman said there needs to be an understanding of how people became unhoused.
"Doug Ford encouraged mayors to ask him to use the notwithstanding clause and several have, it's disappointing that my mayor (Mat Siscoe) is on that list."
Siscoe defended signing the letter earlier this month, as municipalities need the ability to move encampments from vulnerable areas such as near schools or residential areas, but agreed encampments shouldn't be torn down.
"Politicians should not be above the law, they shouldn't be able to manoeuvre the law as it suits them," said Bateman.
"What we know is people are unhoused, we need supportive housing and that is a regional responsibility."
She said if people are unhoused it is a failure of every level of government.
"That is a failure of elected officials past, present and, if this continues to go in this manner, future," Bateman said.
Use of the constitutional clause is bad policy and will not improve the situation, said Christine Clark LeFleur, chief executive officer of Port Cares in Port Colborne.
"There's a level of frustration our communities and community leaders are experiencing," she said.
"That being said, if (Ontario) moves forward with this so-called idea the results are going to be even more difficult and unwieldy for communities. Just because you invoke a legal sanction doesn't mean the problem is going to go away."
Clark LeFleur likened it to a game of whack-a-mole, chasing people from one spot just to have them pop up in another and be whacked down again.
"Let's hit it (homelessness) over the head, make it go away," she said. "Well, you can hit it over the head a million times, it's not going to go away."
She said if the notwithstanding clause is used, the ensuing chaos will be hard to address, as people experiencing homelessness will shy away from those trying to help due to renewed lack of trust.
"Now we're going to disperse the people who are probably in the highest level of need, so it's going to take even more resources to work with them unless we want people to wither and die or live a subhuman existence," she said.
Farha said when the notwithstanding clause was created "it was intended to be used rarely, and it should not be used where there is not public support."
Farha said the number of councillors and mayors who signed the open letter opposing invocation of the clause is evidence of lack of public support for its use.
"There's petitions going around that thousands have signed, just everyday people who don't support the use of the notwithstanding clause," she said.
"To my mind, it's an inappropriate use of the notwithstanding clause in light of the interests at stake and the lack of public support."
Farha said there is an understanding in international law that governments need to do everything in their power to explore viable options before evicting people.
"Have these mayors done that? I don't think so ... rights are a fragile thing," she said.
"The idea governments would proactively undermine human rights is mind-boggling and it's deeply worrying ... it's the exact opposite of what governments are supposed to do.
"Governments are supposed to work very hard to ensure everyone enjoys human rights, especially the fundamental right to life and security of the person and these mayors haven't done their work."
CAPTION: Communities continue to debate using the Canadian Constitution's notwithstanding clause to break up encampments.Leilani Farha, human rights lawyer and former United Nations' special rapporteur on the right to housing.Tents line a wire fence at Montebello Park in St. Catharines.metroland file photoLeilani Farha photometroland file photo

CREDIT: Matthew P. Barker Reporter

Document 80 of 249

Ontario plan 'dangerous for our public hospitals': Unions, health coalition rally against privatization

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-plan-dangerous-our-public-hospitals/docview/3132764193/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+plan+%27dangerous+for+our+public+hospitals%27%3A+Unions%2C+health+coalition+rally+against+privatization&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-11-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A registered practical nurse in Niagara says health-care privatization is going to lead to more crowded hospitals and worsen problems for "overworked, underpaid and underappreciated" staff.
"We got into health care to take care of our patients," said Briar Moore, who works at St. Catharines hospital and is a member of Service Employees International Union.
"We got into health care to make sure that our families, our communities, such as Niagara, are taken care of to the best of our abilities and, unfortunately, the privatization and our government right now is making it impossible for us to do what we set out to do."
Moore said the government, based on its attitudes toward privatization, seems to care more about the profits of shareholders and CEOs than providing proper care in a public system.
As part of its provincewide tour, Canadian Union of Public Employees' Ontario Council of Hospital Unions (OCHU-CUPE) and Ontario Health Coalition brought a 4.6-metre-tall wooden Trojan horse to a rally in downtown Niagara Falls Monday. Officials said it was meant to symbolize the "duplicity" of the Ontario Progressive Conservative government's hospital services' privatization plan.
About a dozen attendees called for an end to ongoing privatization of hospital surgeries and diagnostic tests. They called for the government to invest in staffing capacity in public hospitals.
"The Trojan horse is the story of Greek methodology," said Kevin Cook, first vice-president of OCHU-CUPE. "The Greeks presented a giant horse to the city of Troy as a gift. It was meant to signal the end of war. But there were soldiers hiding inside who came out at night and destroyed the city.
"The Conservatives have presented privatization as a gift. They say private clinics will reduce wait times, they will alleviate the crisis in our hospitals and they will provide the care that the people need.
"Nothing can be further from the truth. Their plan is deceptive and it is dangerous for our public hospitals."
Cook said Ontario Health Coalition has documented "mounting evidence" of patients being billed extra for OHIP coverage services, people routinely having to pay out-of-pocket for cataract surgeries, MRIs and other tests.
"Private, for-profit clinics are price-gouging patients desperate for care. Patients have been threatened with longer wait times if they choose not to pay out-of-pocket," he said.
"Patients have been manipulated or coerced into paying out-of-pocket for unnecessary added on services."
Cook said there have been cases for which patients have been charged as much as $8,000 at private, for-profit clinics for services that would have been offered free of cost in public hospitals.
Sue Hotte, chair of Niagara Health Coalition and board member of Ontario Health Coalition, said last year urgent-care centres in Port Colborne and Fort Erie had 33,534 visits.
She said emergency departments in Welland, St. Catharines and Niagara Falls had 153,478 visits.
"They want to close the urgent care in Fort Erie and Port Colborne, so that means, just by last year's numbers, you're going to be at (nearly) 190,000 visits in already overcrowded emergency departments, in hospitals are that are operating at over 120 per cent capacity."
Plans for Niagara hospital sites backed by the provincial government include closing both Fort Erie and Port Colborne hospitals when the new south Niagara Falls hospital is completed in 2028.
"We have to make sure that our hospitals are well-funded, well-staffed, so that every single person in Niagara has access to care," said Hotte.
In an emailed statement, Hannah Jensen, a spokesperson for Health Minister Sylvia Jones, called Ontario Health Coalition "an out-of-touch, NDP-backed special interest group."
She said the coalition has spent the past decade "accomplishing nothing while standing ideologically opposed to innovation taking place in the health-care system."
Meanwhile, the government is taking "bold action" to connect more people to the care they need, when they need it, said Jensen.
"Ontario is proud to have one of the largest publicly funded health-care systems in the world, and under the leadership of Premier (Doug) Ford, our government has made record investments in our publicly funded health-care system."
Jensen said the government has increased its health-care budget by more than 31 per cent since 2018, investing more than $85 billion into the system this year, including a four per cent increase to the hospital sector.
Jensen also pointed to construction underway for a new Niagara Falls hospital, which will add 500 beds to the region and improve access to services such as diagnostic testing.
"Through our recently announced expansion, our government is adding 100,000 new, publicly funded scans and will help to reduce wait times for MRI and CT scans to 28 days in every region of the province to make it faster and easier for patients across the province to access the care they need, when they need it."
CAPTION: Briar Moore, who works at St. Catharines hospital as a registered practical nurse, told a health-care rally in Niagara Falls Monday that privatization will worsen problems such as overwork and underpaid staff.Bob Tymczyszyn St. Catharines Standard

CREDIT: Ray Spiteri Reporter

Subject: Health care access; Emergency medical care; Privatization

Business indexing term: Subject: Privatization

Location: Niagara Falls

Title: Ontario plan 'dangerous for our public hospitals':   Unions, health coalition rally against privatization

Document 81 of 249

Province committed to 'big, bold' tourism plan: Minister Stan Cho says region 'has not even come close to unleashing its full potential'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/province-committed-big-bold-tourism-plan/docview/3132764008/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+committed+to+%27big%2C+bold%27+tourism+plan%3A+Minister+Stan+Cho+says+region+%27has+not+even+come+close+to+unleashing+its+full+potential%27&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-11-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The provincial government has a "big" and "bold" vision that will unleash Niagara's vital tourism sector to its fullest potential, says Tourism Minister Stan Cho.
"When the premier sat me down in his office about six months ago, he stressed that to me that he thinks Niagara region - and I fully agree - has not even come close to unleashing its full potential," said Cho in a phone interview with the Niagara Falls Review during stops in Niagara Friday.
He called Niagara an important "gateway" into Canada from the United States.
"I'm talking about, yes, the falls and the beauty of that ... which, of course, is one of the natural wonders of the world, but ... you've got beautiful wineries throughout Niagara, and breweries, with some of the most luscious soils on the planet.
"You have the rich history of Niagara-on-the-Lake and a vibrant and thriving theatre culture and arts sector. You have the beautiful beaches of Crystal Beach and the history of Port Colborne and Nickel Beach. You have deep water with cruise ships that are docking there and a gateway into the largest freshwater lake system on the planet in the Great Lakes with tours operating as we speak."
Cho said the problem, however, is the "really long list of things that really, on its own, are special," have never been "tied in together as a vision" that can be marketed globally.
That's why, he said, the provincial government is working on a destination strategy for Niagara.
"I think Niagara has tapped out at something like 13 million visitors a year. I don't think it's unrealistic to think that we can double that figure."
Cho, whose ministry also deals with culture and gaming issues, said one of the first things he did when Premier Doug Ford tasked him with this plan was to make a "bunch of calls" to community leaders in Toronto and ask them "one very simply question."
"When was the last time you went to Niagara Falls for a vacation and not for work? I got one of two answers, exclusively, and that was not since I was really young or never," he said.
"I just can't think of if you were to call the business community leaders of Los Angeles and ask them the same question about Las Vegas that you would get close to the same answer. The challenge became, OK, what are we missing in the region?"
Cho said he concluded Niagara is missing attractions, dining and hotel options, and the connecting links.
"We're missing the transit options, the transportation options, the international airport and we're sort of missing that vision that ties in all of the region into one destination point where you have those options that a lot of other jurisdictions do," he said.
"It's given us a plan to execute ... and the next steps are sort of looking through the budget season, coming up with a plan on paper and saying, 'This is how we're going to execute it.'"
There have been recent media reports that Ford's government is trying to change a deal dictating how Niagara Falls casinos are run and potentially encourage more casinos to open in the city as part of a wider destination strategy for the region.
During a Niagara Falls city council meeting last month, Coun. Victor Pietrangelo said while reports about the strategy have focused on gaming, "from what I've heard anyway, it's not just specific to gaming - it's part of an overall master plan known as Destination Niagara and it has a whole number of factors included in it.
"It's the expansion of the highway, it's the expansion of our local airport, it's the vehicular transportation system, otherwise known as the people mover system, it's the expansion of gaming, it's the expansion of tourism."
Asked if expanding Niagara District Airport, gaming and GO train options is part of the plan, Cho said "literally every single aspect of the region is feeding into the vision of the future."
"The way we know we're succeeding is if we turn Niagara from what it is today, which is a lot to offer, but sadly it's just a one-night destination or maybe a four- or five-hour destination (into) a two- to three-night destination, or a four- to five-night destination, or a weeklong destination," he said.
"To have that, you have to look at what's missing and the government's role is investing into that legacy transit, is investing into that transportation, is incenting the private sector to get their capital here and to invest into those businesses - and devising a landscape of certainty for them to be confident that their investments will pay off and that those visitors will be there."
Cho said the plan can't all be implemented at once, but there can be a "reasonable time frame in which we think we can start to introduce those pieces to see a real difference in visitation numbers."
He said "far too long" tourism planning in Niagara has been "siloed" in that individual communities have been trying to capture the attention of tourists who visit for a few hours or just one night.
"The way I see it is a hub-and-spoke model with the falls being the hub and the spoke going out to Port Colborne, Niagara-on-the-Lake and you need connectivity for that - we cannot have these single, two-lane country roads that are leading from attraction to attraction," said Cho.
"There has to be a storytelling that you're doing within the region and I think regions have succeeded in that.
Cho said the government is at "various stages" of the plan.
"The airport stage is not as far along. We want to put pressure on the federal government - I've had conversations with some of our counterparts there that we would love to see an international airport near the Niagara area.
"The message for the locals is clear: you should be very excited because we're willing partners in getting this region to be what it can be," he added.
CAPTION: Tourism, Culture and Gaming MinisterStan ChoThere have been recent media reports that the provincial government is trying to change a deal dictating how Niagara Falls casinos are run and potentially encourage more to open.Metroland file photo

Readers write Letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write-letters-editor/docview/3132521051/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write+Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-11-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Improving
police service
in Niagara
Niagara Region councillors award 13.2% hike to police budget, Nov. 15
I wholeheartedly agree with Niagara Falls Coun. Joyce Morocco, who said, "It's a tough call and it does come down to affordability and safety."
I sincerely hope that with the 13.2 per cent hike, visible improvements can be made to the public's access to police services and public safety.
In 2023, I accompanied a victim of domestic violence to the police station on Valley Way, but, unfortunately, we couldn't get beyond the front door nor speak directly to a police officer. I was instructed to go into the lobby and make a telephone call to the St. Catharines dispatch.
In May 2018, someone fired a shot at my home, which has never been investigated to this day, despite letters that I have written to two police chiefs, the Office of the Independent Police Review Director, Ontario's Inspectorate of Policing and the solicitor general.
According to Morocco: "To me, it's about safety because what's the cost if we're not safe?"
I can easily answer that question. If the bullet had gone through my home, the cost could have been my life.
Rev. Derek Pedersen,
Niagara Falls
MP's shameful
deadlock

Government spending in limbo as Tories, Liberals continue game of chicken in House, Nov. 19
I am writing about the shameful deadlock in Parliament. This has not been widely reported, but the Liberals and the Conservatives have for the past two months virtually paralyzed Parliament.
The political quarrel is supposedly over millions of dollars the government is spending on the "Green Tech Fund."
Even though the government has handed over 29,000 pages of documents, which I am sure the Tories have read completely, it seems that the Conservatives are using systematic obstruction tactics by refusing to vote on anything, even their own motion.
Still following the U.S. Republican methods of delay and attack used to disrupt the Democrats' attempts to pass any legislation, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre is using the same tactics Donald Trump and his Republican majority Congress have used.
The victims of this are, of course, all Canadians.
With economic pressures building, with a world quickly becoming more dangerous by the day, these people who managed to get elected are acting like schoolchildren misbehaving in a classroom. One must question whether their loyalty lies with their parties or with the Canadian people, their real bosses.
To say that they should be ashamed of themselves is beyond question. I say they should have their generous pay and allowances stopped until they start to do their jobs: Work for the benefit of the people who put them in office.
Richard Murri,
Niagara Falls

Readers write Letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write-letters-editor/docview/3132035741/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write+Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-11-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Are Ontario
ads partisan?
Home care supply shortage due to mistakes by Ontario Health and Ontario Health atHome, sources say, Nov. 11
I mostly watch sports on TV and keep seeing a commercial that begins with the question, "Have you heard, there's a place where it's all happening?"
It goes on to extoll the virtues of connecting northern mines to southern businesses as well as stating more people are employed and making higher wages than ever. It finishes by telling viewers we already live there, and, of course, the place is Ontario.
Since it is a government of Ontario commercial it is paid for by taxpayers but, obviously, is a commercial for the ruling PC party to show how wonderful it is.
It fails to mention 10- to 12-hour waits at hospital emergency rooms; six- to nine-month waits for MRIs; the sudden shortage of medical supplies for individuals in need of home-care; the rush to pave wetlands and farmland to save a few people five minutes on daily commutes.
It also does not state the obvious that because Ontario's population is at its highest point in history, of course, more people than ever are working and pay cheques are higher because of inflation.
Television commercials are not cheap. Why is Premier Doug Ford wasting tax dollars to tell us what we already know? Surely he is not soliciting votes for the next provincial election using taxpayers' money? That couldn't be true, could it?
David Rosier, St. Catharines
Taxpayers pay more, get less
Welland taxes poised to climb 3.54 per cent, Nov. 13
The City of Welland is pondering a 3.54 per cent increase to taxes.
I am not sure what planet local politicians live on but these annual increases need a rest.
Life is tough for many residents. Older homeowners are looking at a future where they may need to leave homes due to taxation.
Maybe it's time to look at what moneys exist and how they are spent. Start with that.
Basically we live in a Welland that is busier, louder and more expensive, that lacks any environmental consideration to what green spaces we have left. Do we want to, can we afford to, pay more and more for less and less?
Douglas Todd, Welland
Environmental setback
Trump names former Rep. Lee Zeldin to lead EPA, Nov. 11
Donald Trump's election win is not good for Canada and Lee Zeldin as leader of the Environmental Protection Agency would set back progress on climate change.
Ironically or not, Elon Musk pushes EVs and Trump is a climate change denier, hypocrisy at its worst.
Trump cozies up to Vladimir Putin and says he can do what he wants in Ukraine.
Would he say the same about Canada if Pierre Poilievre or Justin Trudeau annoy him?
Russ Young, Fort Erie

Document 84 of 249

Welland won't apologize for MD recruiting success: Campion says city won't stop despite calls for collaborative approach

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/welland-wont-apologize-md-recruiting-success/docview/3132035666/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Welland+won%27t+apologize+for+MD+recruiting+success%3A+Campion+says+city+won%27t+stop+despite+calls+for+collaborative+approach&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-11-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles has likened physician recruitment efforts among municipalities to "The Hunger Games," a dystopian series by novelist Suzanne Collins in which the protagonists must compete for survival.
The current situation in Niagara over family doctors pits town against town, with competitors offering incentives in the form of cash bonuses to lure physicians to their underserviced communities.
And the competition has become fierce.
Welland Mayor Frank Campion said his city has no plans to change a physician recruitment strategy that is clearly working, despite increasing calls to eliminate competition between Niagara's municipalities.
After incentives of $100,000 each have so far brought 10 new family doctors to Welland in the past 12 months - far exceeding recruitment efforts in other parts of the region - Campion said the city's physician recruitment initiative was recently funded for a second year in the hope of bringing 10 more doctors to the city.
While other municipalities also provide incentives to physicians coming in from out of the region who agree to work in the municipality for at least five years, Welland offers top dollar. Fort Erie and Port Colborne offer incentives of $75,000 each per doctor, Niagara Falls offers $50,000, Niagara-on-the-Lake offers $25,000 and St. Catharines provides incentives of $5,000.
Meanwhile, neighbouring municipalities are now offering or considering new incentives in the hope of competing with the Rose City to bring new family physicians to other communities in a region that is short roughly 100 family doctors.
Lincoln council voted last week to offer $25,000 incentives to family physicians who set up practices in town for at least five years, after recently losing incoming doctors to communities that offer incentives. Thorold councillors, too, last week asked city staff to develop a report looking into strategies to recruit four family physicians to the city that may include financial incentives.
Despite concerns the incentives are creating competition between Niagara's municipalities, Campion said the city is "continuing on with our program."
"We approved another million dollars in funding for it this year to attempt to get 10 more doctors, getting us closer to the number that we need. It's certainly not all the way there, but we're hopeful we will be able to get more," he said.
Campion said the $100,000 incentives are "certainly a big part" of the city's success, "but it isn't the only reason we're successful."
He said ensuring incoming physicians have a variety of locations to set up their practices, as well as an abundance of patients to draw from has helped, as well as the assistance of Niagara Region physician recruiter Jill Croteau.
"Welland is a great place to set up an office. We have clientele for them and it's a growing community and that's attractive to physicians as well."
The city's success, however, has also led to concerns from political representatives in other communities, calling for a more collaborative approach to physician recruitment.
Niagara Falls Coun. Ruth-Ann Nieuwesteeg expressed concerns about competition and escalating incentives during a recent city council meeting.
"We've been hearing a lot about doctor recruitment because there's such a shortage and what we're seeing in our region is every different municipality upping the other one, trying to get a doctor or to recruit doctors for that municipality," she said during the Oct. 22 council meeting," she said.
"I'd like to put a (notice of) motion that we instruct maybe our CAO to work with the other municipalities to come up with a clever and creative collaborative approach to it instead of just, Welland does 50 or we do 75 and look at things that are a different way of attracting (physicians)."
She said there are probably alternatives to increasing financial incentives.
"I don't know how that would work, but I'd like to see if we can have our CAO meet with the other CAOs and figure out, instead of competing with one another, work together and then get people to come to Niagara region," she said.
"We go between cities all the time, so whatever we can do to get the doctors here - I think it's something that we need to look at collaboratively and creatively."
Thorold Mayor Terry Ugulini said Niagara Region is bringing lower-tier municipalities together next week for a meeting in the hope of addressing concerns about competition among municipalities offering incentives to bring doctors to their respective communities. Mayors and chief administrative officers from each of Niagara's municipalities were invited to meet with regional family physician recruiter Croteau on Tuesday, to discuss a collaborative approach to luring family physicians to the region rather than competing against one another.
Ugulini said the meeting is being held to "try and get everybody working together, because we don't want to start working against one another."
"I think everybody is willing to put skin in the game, it's just getting everyone on the same page," Ugulini said.
Campion said he looks forward to attending the meeting, but it won't change Welland's direction.
"People can make all the suggestions they want, but council approved the program and put funding to it, so that's not going to change," he said. "No matter what suggestions might come forward we're committed to this program for the year and we're not going to turn back on it."
Although Fort Erie offers incentives, Mayor Wayne Redekop said municipalities are being forced to do so because "successive provincial governments have failed to address" the shortage of family physicians across Ontario.
"Municipalities will do what they have to do, and they will continue to try to make sure their residents have access to primary care, which means doctors, nurse practitioners and maybe some physician assistants," he said. "There are a number of solutions, but it all really boils down to the provincial government."
He said the delivery of health care is a provincial responsibility, and "you can't be putting some of these expenses and responsibilities on the backs of municipalities and the property owners. It's not sustainable."
Redekop said the province has made efforts to address the shortage, such as increasing medical school enrolment, but it "nowhere near meets the need that we have."
"I know they've looked at other policies to try to take the pressure off of family physicians, but they really need to be encouraging more people to go into family medicine," he said. "They need to be providing better supports."
Redekop said the province also needs to develop a much more streamlined system to pay family physicians, because "right now there are about five separate funding models, all of which make it very confusing."
Discussing health care during a recent visit to Niagara, Stiles made "The Hunger Games" reference.
"It really does create this 'Hunger Games' environment, which is really unfortunate," Stiles said. "I don't think any municipality wants to be playing that game, but they feel they have no choice."
She said it also means smaller communities without the tax base to draw large incentives from get left behind.
"The saddest thing about it is there are communities that can't afford to play that game, to get involved at all, and they're just as deserving as anyone else," she said. "This is what has happened as a result of both Liberal and Conservative governments failing to plan forward for what we knew was coming, which was a retirement crisis among our family physicians, an aging population with more complex needs. And here we are."
Niagara Centre MPP Jeff Burch agreed.
"There are only so many doctors. All we're doing is pushing up how much incentives are being offered, and that's definitely not the solution. It puts a lot of pressure on municipalities that are already under a great deal of pressure," he said.
He said putting municipalities in a position where they need to offer incentives to recruit physicians is downloading a provincial responsibility.
"It's all like a downloading on municipalities that are already so overworked."
"We need to let municipalities do their work, and health care is a provincial responsibility," he said. "Making sure there are enough doctors so all municipalities can have an adequate number of doctors has to be a priority and it has to be a provincial responsibility, not a municipal one."
Minister of health spokesperson Hannah Jensen listed several of the investments in primary health care that have been made by the provincial government since 2018, including the recent expansion of a grant for medical school students "who commit to practising family medicine when they graduate. It is estimated that the total investment will enable the connection of an additional 1.36 million people to primary care."
Campion said physician recruitment is also an economic development issue, and that falls within the city's jurisdiction.
He said the city's initiative is being led by its economic development department, making the community more attractive to investors who are looking for communities that have the services their workers will need - including medical services.
"Those are all things that attract businesses and you're creating jobs," Campion said.
"Municipalities should be stepping up and trying to attract physicians because it's for the betterment of their own community. That's why we're doing it."
St. Catharines MPP Jennie Stevens said the family physician shortage has been "a real hot topic" discussed almost daily at Queen's Park, with estimates that as many as 2.5 million people across the province are without a family doctor.
"Every question period, we're hearing that number echo through the chambers," she said.
While calling recruitment efforts by local municipalities "great news," Stevens said they shouldn't be forced into that situation in the first place.
She said the province should be offering the incentives to recruit physicians, not municipalities.
"I think all cities should be commended for stepping up, but it's not the municipalities' job to be taking on the burden. It's a provincial matter."
Jensen, however, said the government is also offering thousands of dollars in incentives to help bring family physicians to some of the most underserviced areas in Ontario.
"Our government supports physician recruitment in communities across the province through programs like the Practice Ready Ontario program, the Northern Ontario Resident Streamlined Training and Reimbursement Program, and the Northern and Rural Recruitment and Retention Initiative," Jensen said.
With files by Ray Spiteri

Document 85 of 249

'Dangerous precedent': Four Niagara politicians oppose extra powers to deal with encampments

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/dangerous-precedent/docview/3130583392/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Dangerous+precedent%27%3A+Four+Niagara+politicians+oppose+extra+powers+to+deal+with+encampments&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-11-20&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Four Niagara politicians have signed an open letter opposing the use of the notwithstanding clause by the province to clear homeless encampments - a move that was requested by the mayors of St. Catharines, Welland and Niagara Falls.
St. Catharines city Couns. Greg Miller and Caleb Ratzlaff, Niagara Falls city Coun. Lori Lococo and St. Catharines regional Coun. Haley Bateman are among 50 municipal councillors and mayors across Ontario who signed the letter with concerns about use of the notwithstanding clause.
"As individual councillors in our respective municipalities, we know that Ontario municipalities are facing a humanitarian crisis that is affecting all of us," said the letter from the coalition that was posted on social media.
"We see it every day in our communities. But no municipality should be above the law, and we are sworn to uphold democracy and human rights as councillors."
The use of the notwithstanding clause would empower the province to override a court ruling that found it is a violation of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms for a municipality to evict encampments when people have nowhere to go.
The letter comes after 12 of the 29 mayors from the Ontario Big City Mayors caucus- including St. Catharines Mayor Mat Siscoe- sent a letter to Premier Doug Ford on Oct. 31 asking for provincial action to help municipalities with issues related to mental health, addiction and homeless encampments and "where necessary use the notwithstanding clause."
Ford had challenged the Big City Mayors to put the request in writing if they wanted the homeless situation to improve.
Since then, Welland Mayor Frank Campion sent his own letter to Ford supporting the call for the use of the notwithstanding clause and Niagara Falls Mayor Jim Diodati put forth a motion to his council, which was adopted, calling on Ford to use the clause to give authorities and police more power to break up encampments.
Siscoe said earlier this month he agrees encampments shouldn't be torn down if there's nowhere for unhoused people to go, but he wants municipalities to have the ability to move encampments out of sensitive areas, such as near schools or up against residential homes.
The responding letter from the coalition of councillors and mayors was published online Monday with 41 signatures and updated Tuesday with nine more.
The group, which came together from 26 municipalities, are calling on mayors who supported the use of the notwithstanding clause to rescind their letters to Ford.
It is also asking that municipal councils support "evidence-based solutions to homelessness and the charter" to ensure that the "democratic will" of their constituents and "human rights are respected."
"Any time you're talking about violating someone's charter rights, which is what the Big City Mayors are ultimately talking about, you've got to be very careful and it's going to be a great reason to do that,"said Miller, a Merritton ward councillor, in an interview Tuesday.
"I don't think this passes that test."
Miller said he and other councillors prefer a harm-reduction and compassionate approach.
Bateman wrote in an open letter to residents, posted on social media earlier this month, that the notwithstanding clause is "the most aggressive political tactic" at a provincial legislature's disposal.
"I believe that encampments are an expression of need for services (housing, food, community engagement), rather than an act that needs to be corrected through laws that would criminalize people living in extreme poverty," Bateman wrote.
Ratzlaff, a councillor for St. Patrick's ward, said in an interview the councillors want to find a way to deal with encampments that doesn't use the "blunt instrument" of the notwithstanding clause.
"It sets a dangerous precedent and it allows Doug Ford to shift the focus away from what I think is a failure on providing affordable housing for the people of Ontario. He was able to shift the focus from his failed policy to the mayors and blame the courts and say it's the court fault."
Ratzlaff said there are other ways to try to ensure safety around encampments in public spaces without using the notwithstanding clause. He pointed to Guelph, which has attempted to regulate encampments in sensitive areas such as schools through bylaws, something Siscoe has asked St. Catharines city staff to look into.
Lococo, the only Niagara Falls councillor to vote against Diodati's motion, said during the council meeting the use of the notwithstanding clause is a "dangerous, slippery slope" that "takes away" people's constitutional rights and could lead to "sweeping denial of freedoms."
"Those on the front line, closest to the problem, have asked for more resources and have been ignored. Now the province says it needs the notwithstanding clause to fix an issue that should have already been fixed with the tools at hand."
Several Niagara municipal councils and Niagara Region council have called on the province to provide more resources to deal with homelessness.
Ford has been on record as saying that no government has put more money in the homeless and homeless shelters, mental heath and addition than his government.
In St. Catharines in August, he said the government has invested $3.8 billion into mental health and addictions, referring to its Roadmap to Wellness strategy, and has put hundreds of millions of dollars into homelessness prevention.
with files from Ray Spiteri and The Toronto Star

Document 86 of 249

Catholic board welcomes audit: Taxpayers should be paying attention, ex-Brock prof says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/catholic-board-welcomes-audit/docview/3129466424/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Catholic+board+welcomes+audit%3A+Taxpayers+should+be+paying+attention%2C+ex-Brock+prof+says&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-11-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: School boards may not know what an upcoming provincial audit of their discretionary spending will entail, but Niagara's Catholic board says it will "welcome an outside audit" of its policies following reports of questionable international trips taken by staff members and trustees of other Ontario school boards.
"We have strict policies here that we follow," said Danny Di Lorenzo, chair of Niagara Catholic District School Board. "We're very transparent, all our policies align with ministry regulations and legislations.
"There should be that audit so that not everybody's flying on their own here. As a board, we hold the public trust in our decision-making and we're very prudent for financial decisions, especially when dealing with travel and trustee expenditures."
Premier Doug Ford ordered a review of all Ontario school boards' discretionary spending after a series of questionable practices, including a $145,000 trip to Italy taken by four Brant Haldimand Norfolk Catholic District School Board trustees.
On Thursday, Education Minister Jill Dunlop said it is important to immediately address the issue. She said parents expect school boards to be "responsible stewards of public education dollars to prioritize student achievement" and there is no room for "unnecessary or extravagant expenses that do not contribute to these core objectives."
In addition to Brantford Catholic's trip to Italy's South Tyrol region -which included purchasing $70,000-plus worth of statues and religious art for new schools -Lambton Kent District School Board spent $32,000 for three staff members to attend a conference in Hawaii.
Reports also emerged of a $16,000 staff retreat to St. Catharines by more than two dozen employees of London District Catholic School Board, as well as a $40,000 retreat for senior staff at Thames Valley District School Board.
While Di Lorenzo could not comment on the actions and policies of other boards, he said "we actually would welcome an outside audit to review our process and policies, to see if what we're doing is in line with ministry regulations."
"If you're doing things the way you should be doing (them), what the ministry is saying should not impact you at all."
He added Niagara Catholic understands the funding it receives is for students and there are people who may not agree with the ways in which it is spent, however, "we are in compliance with how we spend it."
"We're aware that we're funded by taxpayers, and we have a moral and legal obligation to be stewards of the funding we receive, so I think we're in compliance, but I think an audit would just reinforce that," he said.
District School Board of Niagara chair Sue Barnett said DSBN's student-focused budget demonstrates a commitment to maintaining a "strong and sustainable financial foundation but also our dedication to enhancing the quality of resources and supports."
"DSBN has guidelines, policies and procedures in place to support appropriate expenditures and the use of ministry funding for education," said Barnett in an emailed statement.
Conseil scolarie catholique MonAvenir said in an email it is awaiting additional information from the ministry before commenting on the audit.
Conseil scolarie Viamonde did not respond for comment by publication.
Former Brock University politics professor David Siegel said school boards are self-governing bodies within some "fairly broad outlines" set by the provincial government.
Although he said boards began with amateurs trying to figure out how to deliver education, they have since become professionally managed, with the vast majority understanding "problems that can come up with things like travel expenses."
"They are aware that you can't abuse these sorts of things and they don't want to bring either the provincial government or the local residents down on them by engaging in abuse," he said.
"I think you'll find 95 to 98 per cent of boards are right on top of this and have good control, (but) that poses problems for everybody when the small number of people take advantage of that."
While Siegel typically opts in favour of local autonomy, with school boards and municipalities making decisions based on local issues, it becomes a problem "when certain people abuse that."
As a result, the government "can't stand by and watch some boards abuse the system," he said.
"You've now caught the attention of the provincial government. What can happen when these decisions are made in a central location, like a provincial capital instead of locally, then things can happen that are not as sensitive as they should be to the local needs," said Siegel.
"I would like to see these decisions made as close to home as possible, but they have to be made in responsible and accountable manner."
In terms of management, school boards do tend to "fly under the radar" in part due to voting patterns during municipal elections, showing fewer people voting for trustees than mayor or councillors.
"The public is just not paying a lot of attention to what goes on in school boards either unless it's an argument of flying the Pride flag or something like that. That instantly increases the profile," said Siegel.
"They should because they're spending a significant amount of public money and educating our children is an incredibly important function, so more attention should be paid."
CAPTION: "We have strict policies here that we follow," said Niagara Catholic District School Board chair Danny Di Lorenzo.Julie Jocsak St. Catharines Standard file photo
