Document 87 of 249

Workers say their goodbyes to Ontario Science Centre: The 'magic is gone' from almost-empty building, leftover exhibits

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/workers-say-their-goodbyes-ontario-science-centre/docview/3129466146/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Workers+say+their+goodbyes+to+Ontario+Science+Centre%3A+The+%27magic+is+gone%27+from+almost-empty+building%2C+leftover+exhibits&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-11-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As the sun shone on an unusually warm day at the end of October, Emma Meadley Dunphy took one final, emotional stroll through the Ontario Science Centre.
The centre's volunteer co-ordinator wandered the cavernous spaces trying to soak it all in, before workers had to be out of the building by midday on Oct. 31.
"I kept running into lots of different people who were doing it themselves," she said. "Everybody was clearly having a moment in each of the spaces."
It had been a furious few months of packing up and sending countless truckloads of stuff to storage facilities in numerous locations.
A few things too difficult to move were still in the building. Questions hung in the air, too. Did it really need to be this way? Would they ever come back?
Ontario officials announced the abrupt and permanent closure of the science centre in late June, saying engineers found structural issues with the roof.
The province plans to move the science centre to a new location as part of a revamped Ontario Place.
The move sparked outrage among workers, nearby residents and visitors and has become a political hot potato for Premier Doug Ford at Queen's Park.
On her final day in the building, Meadley Dunphy visited a few places that had been meaningful to her.
She visited the camp room where she worked as a volunteer in high school some 20 years ago.
Then she stopped by the rainforest, or what remains of it.
The animals that lived there - turtles, snakes, fish and poisonous frogs - have been re-homed, while much of the vegetation was ripped out and transplanted at the Toronto Zoo.
Five large trees, too big to move, are still there and should survive, so long as the building's automated watering system continues working and the heat remains on.
On stressful work days, Meadley Dunphy said she would often find herself visiting the rainforest. The dewy, earthy smell relaxed her.
Despite the lack of vegetation, the smell remains. But it's not the same, she said.
"That magic is gone."
Meadley Dunphy spent the past few months winding up the centre's volunteer operations, helping with camps that still ran off-site.
Like most other workers, she also helped pack.
"It's not my normal job, but it's a very sad reminder about what's happening and there's no way to escape that," she said, tearing up.
Before they had to clear out that day, science centre workers had an ice cream party for 28 cleaning staff who were laid off by Dexterra, the company that managed them.
It was their final day on the job. While the pay hadn't been great at $16.50 per hour, the cleaners had union jobs with benefits and a full pension.
Ford pledged to help find them a job "within the system," but that never happened, said Martin Fischer, the president of Local 549 of Ontario Public Service Employees Union, which represents about 500 science centre workers. But they were buoyed by support from the public after news of the layoffs broke in September, he said.
He said he is still holding out hope that workers might someday return to the building.
He pointed to ongoing repair work as the source of that hope. There is scaffolding in the great hall and the auditorium that allows access to the roof, where engineers had identified panels in danger of collapsing. The heating in Building B, which housed many exhibits, theatres and the great hall, has been fixed.
"We do appreciate that the building is being repaired, but, of course, everybody wonders, what for? What is it going to be?" Fischer said. "So, I haven't given up hope that we can return."
The Canadian Press has reached out to the science centre with several questions about the state of affairs. Infrastructure Ontario and Infrastructure Minister Kinga Surma's office did not respond to requests for comment.
The province is looking for an interim location for the science centre with its permanent new space set to open in 2028 at the earliest.
The production team, which makes exhibits for science centres around the world, does not have a new home yet. The shop's machines and production materials are stored away in Huntsville, Ont., a three-hour drive north of Toronto, Fischer said.
Other items are stored in facilities in Guelph, northern Toronto and at Sherway Gardens, a mall in western Toronto.
Ward Kennedy can clearly remember his first visit to the science centre in 1970, when he was six years old.
He grew up in nearby Flemingdon Park, so his family visited the centre often.
"I remember the 25-cent submarine sandwiches and the old cafeteria, which was very, very tiny, and I remember the parabolic dishes at either end of one of the buildings where we could talk and our voice would travel across to the other side," Kennedy said. "It's a special place."
He served as a volunteer ham radio operator and helped run the science centre's amateur radio station.
As things wound down this October, Kennedy helped dismantle the radio equipment and send it off to storage.
"There are no plans in the future to install a new amateur radio station at a new Ontario Science Centre," he said.
He listed off more of the items that are gone now, sent away to places across the province.
The Toronto Bee Collective came and took the dozen or so bee hives that are now set up at Black Creek Pioneer Village, he said.
Gone too are the iconic Canadarm and the fin whale skeleton, which he said were especially difficult to take down.
But Kennedy said staff left a few big-ticket items behind, and he isn't sure why. There's a ship's propeller, and the cross-section of a massive Sitka spruce tree trunk.
He choked up as he recalled taking his daughter, years ago, to KidSpark - an interactive environment at the centre for younger children. This past spring, he brought her again, this time with his granddaughter in tow.
"It just meant so much," he said.
"I wish I could have taken them more often because it's a one-of-a-kind building."
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: A young visitor checks out aquariums at the Ontario Science Centre in 2018. The centre's animals have been re-homed.Steve Russell Toronto Star file photo

Document 88 of 249

Police budget hiked 13.2%: 'What's the cost if we're not safe?' councillor asks

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/police-budget-hiked-13-2/docview/3128943446/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Police+budget+hiked+13.2%25%3A+%27What%27s+the+cost+if+we%27re+not+safe%3F%27+councillor+asks&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-11-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With an 11th-hour cut, Niagara Region councillors passed a 2025 Niagara Regional Police Service operating budget of $213.3 million-a 13.18 per cent increase over this year's spending envelope - while endorsing a protest letter from the regional chair demanding the provincial government pay for its mandated changes to policing.
Police Chief Bill Fordy began Thursday's committee of the whole debate by asking for a 15.1 per cent increase that politicians later reduced by staggering the hiring of 33 new front-line recruits.
The change, which the NRP has used in the past to trim the budget, will temporarily save taxpayers about $3.65 million, though the police service will still fill all positions by year's end.
An estimated increase of 7.4 per cent is needed to maintain the existing service level.
Fordy said the police budget begins to account for Niagara's population projections, which the province expects to reach 700,000 residents by 2051.
Niagara's population growth rate is 6.8 per cent for the most recent five-year period for which data is available, growing from 453,817 in 2016 to 484,840 in 2022.
Calls for service increased by an average of 2.2 per cent annually, with the call complexity also rising. In 2023, police responded to an average of 381 calls for service per day, which is about 16 calls per hour or a new call every 3.75 minutes.
"It would be irresponsible of me to maintain the status quo and not look to the future and ensure that we are positioned to serve and protect communities that don't yet exist - when we know they are coming," Fordy said. "The status quo is not a sustainable model and is placed on the backs of our members taking on increasingly complex workloads."
Niagara Regional Police is not the only service in Ontario asking for significant operating budget increases.
Peel Regional Police requested a 21.3 per cent increase, with plans to hire 154 additional employees. Halton Regional Police asked for a 13.8 per cent increase, Waterloo Regional Police a 10.5 per cent increase and Hamilton Police a 10.24 per cent increase.
London, Ont., sets its police budget over a four-year term, starting with a 28 per cent increase in 2024, followed by 16 per cent increases spread over the remaining three years.
Said Fordy, "Our officers are already stretched thin, balancing responses to calls for service with ongoing investigations. There's no question that our people are working hard and doing their best.
"Increasing the number of officers will positively impact our ability to improve clearance rates. Our priority has to be bringing closure to victims of crime, which often involves persons coming from vulnerable communities.
"Many of our competitors are increasing the number of police officers and investigators. They are making significant investments this year that are even greater than what we are putting forward."
Regional Chair Jim Bradley said Fordy had made a compelling case, but asked council to request police find more efficiencies and reduce the budget because the public would be "enraged" at the increase.
"If you look at it in total dollars, it's by far the largest request of this council for an agency, board or commission, and the public has been alarmed to see that amount being asked for in this particular year," Bradley said.
"We also recognize that members of this council are held accountable by the public. You can't just say it's the police board or the police department. The public comes to us, expecting us to answer for the costs incurred. I think we're all mindful of that fact."
The police budget joins a series of regional increases that property taxpayers must absorb. Besides user fees, it's the only funding mechanism for municipalities to increase their revenue.
The water and wastewater budget increase for 2025 is 12.27 per cent. The special levy on property taxpayers for transit increased by 5.88 per cent.
Councillors couldn't complete Thursday's committee of the whole budget agenda. Council referred Niagara Peninsula Conservation Authority's operating budget - and its 9.51 per cent increase - to the Nov. 21 council meeting.
Council has yet to set the Region's operating budget. However, Beth Brens, the associate director of budgets planning and strategy, told elected officials it is looking at an increase in the 10 per cent range.
Council voted to direct Bradley to write a letter to Premier Doug Ford and Solicitor General Michael Kerzner requesting the province cover 5.7 per cent of the 2025 police increase, which equals $10.3 million and represents the cost of the changes mandated by the province in the Community Safety and Policing Act.
Much of the 5.7 per cent increase is due to additional police training costs necessary to comply with the act.
The budget also adds four new senior officers, including a new deputy chief of community services, a superintendent of emergency services and inspectors for emergency planning and investigative services.
Fort Erie Coun. Tom Insinna wanted the four new senior management hires delayed for a year.
"I understand things have to be done with regard to policing," said Insinna, a former RCMP officer. "We need more boots on the ground, but there must be some savings. There must be something we can put off until later.
"I think we have to look around and ask each other if we could personally afford this. Can each councillor here afford a 15 per cent hike? Some might, but the majority are going to say no, and I hate to say this, but in today's economy, the way people are struggling, we can't do this. We have to look for alternatives."
Fordy said it is not uncommon for officers to start their shift with a significant number of calls for service still waiting for a response. At shift change on Monday, Fordy said, there were 48 calls for service left in the queue. At shift change on Tuesday, there were 36.
Niagara Falls Coun. Joyce Morocco said she was struggling with whether to approve the budget increase.
"If I don't support this budget that's presented to us, those numbers waiting in the queue are only going to get worse, and I'm sure that one of those calls will be one of our family members, one of our friends, one of our neighbours, one of our colleagues. It's a tough call, and it does come down to affordability and safety.
"To me, it's about safety, because what's the cost if we're not safe?"
CAPTION: Niagara Regional Police Chief Bill Fordy told regional council the status quo is not a sustainable budget model.metroland file photo

Document 89 of 249

Niagara Falls pushes for help with encampments: Councillor warns of slippery slope if notwithstanding clause invoked

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/niagara-falls-pushes-help-with-encampments/docview/3128140245/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Niagara+Falls+pushes+for+help+with+encampments%3A+Councillor+warns+of+slippery+slope+if+notwithstanding+clause+invoked&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-11-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Niagara Falls Mayor Jim Diodati has won the support of his city council in calling on the provincial government for more tools to deal with homeless encampments.
Council on Tuesday approved a motion put forth by Diodati that asks Premier Doug Ford to use the Constitution's notwithstanding clause to give authorities and police more power to break up encampments.
The motion, which only Coun. Lori Lococo opposed, also calls for forcing more people into mandatory drug and mental-health treatment.
"Illegal activity and disruptive public behaviours take place openly, including drug use, solicitation, prostitution, human trafficking, and have been witnessed by and tolerated by residents as the authorities have no available recourse," said the motion.
"Discarded, used drug needles, defecating and urinating on public property, setting fires to private and public property, theft and vandalism have become common practice in neighbouring areas."
Diodati said vulnerable people need help to navigate drug addiction, mental health challenges and social supports.
"In the month of October alone, the City of Niagara Falls received 29 encampment complaints, which, if allowed to remain unchallenged, could cost millions of tax dollars to deal (with) with large scale cleanups, rather than addressing the concerns in real time to prevent this from happening."
Diodati said the city invested more than $500,000 on encampment cleanup and related vandalism costs in 2023.
"This is not a municipal responsibility, but as a city we continue to incur significant costs without the correct legislative tools and government support to solve the core issues," said the motion.
"The chronically homeless population in the Niagara region is approximately 647 people, just more than 0.1 per cent of the populus, causing a disproportionate burden on families in our community. Every neighbourhood in our community has been affected, many businesses have moved and residents face daily ongoing challenges, seeing no marked improvement in the situation."
People have a right to shelter under the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, which guarantees the right to life, liberty and security of the person. There is case law related to an encampment in Waterloo and a judge denied an application by Kingston to move one.
Diodati's motion also calls for trespass laws to be written to include jail time for "repetitive trespass" and to empower police to arrest repeat trespassers.
It calls for new legislation banning open drug use, similar to laws in place on alcohol, and to establish a new court diversion system that redirects offenders toward rehabilitation and more appropriate supports.
The motion requests the provincial government act as an intervener in court cases on encampments and to "advocate for the position that courts should not be dictating homelessness policy."
It said the province should look at using "every legal tool" available to clear encampments and "restore dignity and safety to public spaces."
The motion requests investment in 24-hour crisis centres to "relieve pressures" on emergency rooms and first responders and to consolidate appropriate funding with the federal government for the unhoused community where gaps exist.
It calls for the establishment of a ministry with appropriate funding and power as a single point of contact to deal with such matters and to create open lines of communication with relevant federal ministries, provincial areas of responsibility and municipalities.
Lococo said while she agrees "we're in crisis and that we need to be doing something, because what we're doing right now is not working," she does not support using the notwithstanding clause.
Lococo said the province is obligated to provide funding for resources to support mental health, addiction and homelessness.
"The province has not provided these resources and they have not put them in place," she said, adding not all people living in encampments have addiction or mental health issues - some are there for economic reasons.
"Those on the front line, closest to the problem, have asked for more resources and have been ignored. Now the province says it needs the notwithstanding clause to fix an issue that should have already been fixed with the tools at hand."
Lococo said using the notwithstanding clause is a "dangerous, slippery slope" that "takes away" people's constitutional rights and could lead to "sweeping denial of freedoms."
Lococo also expressed concerns with Diodati's calls for mandatory drug and mental health treatment and how that would affect current resources, "especially the long wait-times that we currently have."
"Our court, social service and corrections systems are overwhelmed as it is. With this motion, there will be more criminal charges, more court dates and more incarcerated individuals. Currently, we do not have the resources for this."
She said Niagara Region, Niagara Assertive Street Outreach and other teams have been successful in placing people in shelters.
"They've also been successful in moving encampments to less problematic areas. There are some great examples of the programs that work, such as RAFT, the youth shelter in St. Catharines that focuses on diversion before youth enter the shelter system, and mental health and drug treatment court. These are the resources that we need more of."
Coun. Victor Pietrangelo asked city staff whether Niagara Falls has enough shelter beds to accommodate people living in encampments in the city.
Chief administrative officer Jason Burgess said there is "adequate" space at Niagara Region's shelter on Summer Street.
"The Region has always said if someone needs housing in crisis, they have adequate space and resources to address that issue," he said.
"We've never come into a situation where someone has asked for shelter and the Region hasn't been able to provide shelter for that individual."
CAPTION: A homeless encampment at Bridge Street in Niagara Falls. That city is calling on the province for more powers to break up encampments and for more mandatory drug and mental-health treatments.Mayor Jim Diodati wants help with homeless encampments. Coun. Lori Lococo opposed the mayor's motion.metroland file photoMetroland file photoCity of Niagara Falls

Readers write Letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write-letters-editor/docview/3127514855/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write+Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-11-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ford to blame for long wait times
Prepare for delays: New Niagara Health, Shaver hospital information system goes live Saturday, Nov. 7
No, I cannot praise the wait time recently of more than 14 hours in the emergency room at Niagara Health's St. Catharines site before getting relief from a recent gallbladder attack.
With only one overnight emergency room doctor in attendance, the diligent staff cheerfully and thoroughly did their best to attend to myriad needs of a parade of individuals in numbers exceeding 50, according to the hospital wait time app.
I heard no complaints from my fellow cohorts as we were dealt with kindly and professionally in turn.
But shame on you Premier Doug Ford, for underfunding our health-care system so that people, including parents desperately trying to comfort children in respiratory distress as young as a newborn, had to endure their ordeal for such an unconscionable length of time.
After being admitted for surgery, I received the best of care and attention right down the line. All were cordial, responsive and thorough.
They were grace under fire.
Joan Hotson, St. Catharines
Apology needed over washroom
Dumped: St. Catharines' notorious self-cleaning washroom to be closed and removed, Nov. 5
What a complete waste of tax dollars.
Likely more than a million dollars has been spent on purchasing, installing, maintaining this project as well as the multiple attempts to keep it operational. Just another example of why St. Catharines has one of the highest property tax rates across Ontario.
This is for a project which has failed in other communities. How could this not have been predicted when city councillors supported the installation of this self-cleaning washroom in our downtown core?
The mismanagement of our tax dollars in St. Catharines is maddening.
As the current council considers yet another unsustainable property tax increase, there should be a formal apology to the taxpayers of St. Catharines for the wasted tax dollars for this failed project.
Allen McKay, St. Catharines
Election results not surprising
The story of how Trump went from diminished ex-president to a victor once again, Nov. 8
There's nothing surprising about what happened in the U.S. election, even thoughit's shocking to see the ghost of Donald Trump back as president.
Previous election results across the states can't be relied on as a guide, as the COVID-19 pandemic skewed so much.
That changes the picture and each year the older generation gradually dies off. Then there's first-time young people voting, so predictions about who is going to win is a greater mystery for pundits than ever.
Roscoe Reilly, Welland

com or mail to PO Box 5031 Stn Main, St. Catharines, Ont., L2R 7T4. Letters should be 250 words or less, and may be edited for length and clarity. Please include your name, address and phone number for verification purposes.

Document 91 of 249

Notwithstanding clause would give cities tools to deal with encampments: Campion: 'I need to do what I think is right' for the city, mayor says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/notwithstanding-clause-would-give-cities-tools/docview/3126871253/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Notwithstanding+clause+would+give+cities+tools+to+deal+with+encampments%3A+Campion%3A+%27I+need+to+do+what+I+think+is+right%27+for+the+city%2C+mayor+says&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-11-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Welland Mayor Frank Campion says he'll deal with whatever pushback comes from requesting stronger tools to handle homeless encampments.
On Wednesday, Campion issued a "mayor's message" that said he sent a letter to Premier Doug Ford to support use of the notwithstanding clause of the Charter of Rights and Freedoms to override court rulings that limit the ability of municipalities to remove encampments on public land.
"As mayor, I need to do what I think is right (for the city), and that is what I do all the time. If there is pushback, that's fine. I stand by it," said Campion in an interview Friday.
Niagara Falls Mayor Jim Diodati has also expressed support of using the constitutional mechanism and is bringing a motion to his council this week.
Campion's move came after a letter to Ford signed by 12 of 29 members of the Ontario Big City Mayors, including St. Catharines Mayor Mat Siscoe, asked the province to enact tougher laws to help them deal with encampments, homelessness and addiction issues and "where necessary use the notwithstanding clause" to ensure any efforts won't be overturned by the courts.
Among those who did not sign the Big City Mayors letter to Ford were Toronto's Olivia Chow and Hamilton's Andrea Horwath. Horwath said she won't sign the letter.
The province has "every ability" to invoke the charter without the urging of municipal leaders, Horwath said in the Hamilton Spectator.
"So this is more about politics, I think, than it is about actual solving of problems. And I'm here to solve problems."
Though Campion's letter was sent to the province, it will come as a notice of motion during Welland's Nov. 26 city council meeting.
"I want to get council involved. It's important they have their say. I want the support of council if I can get it. It sends a stronger message."
Asked why he sent the letter, Campion said there are rules and rights around encampments that prevent the municipality from going in and moving people without somewhere for them to go.
People have a right to shelter under theCanadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, which guarantees the right to life, liberty and security of the person. There iscase law related to an encampment inWaterloo and a judge denied an application by Kingston to move one.
Campion said Welland wants to have a compassionate eye regarding encampments and people who live in them, but added there are times when they need to be moved.
"It's to ensure everyone stays safe - the community and those in the encampments."
The mayor pointed to an encampment under Division Street Bridge last year as an example.He said living under the bridge was not safe, as evidenced by a later fire believed to be arson, where a person was injured.
"We were close to a tragedy there. There are certain cases and circumstances where we need to move people for public safety and the health and safety of the people in the encampments."
Having the clause in place would allow Welland to create bylaws and put restrictions in place as to where encampments could not be set up for safety reasons.
It would be a tool of last resort, Campion said.
But how that tool would be used, whether to use it all and where people forced to move would go if there was no available shelter space are among details and strategies to be worked out, he said.
Campion said the authority would not be used to force encampments to move just because people don't like the sight of them or to jail people living unhoused.
"It is all about safety, not convenience. We want everyone to be safe. I'd say people in encampments are probably in more danger than anyone else."
Welland's first goal is to find shelter space.
An encampment cleaned up recently behind Welland Civic Square alongside the Welland Recreational Canal and a smaller one at East Main Street and The Boardwalk resulted in at least four people being moved into shelter spaces.
A temporary 50-bed emergency homeless shelter to be built at 851 Ontario Rd. and operated by The Hope Centre on behalf of Niagara Region will fill a need for space in the city once open.
Residents around the shelter are opposed to it, fearing, among other things, that it will attract encampments of individuals waiting to get into the facility.
Campion said use of the notwithstanding clause could allow Welland to create bylaws and place a zone around the new shelter to ensure that doesn't happen.
He said the goal of a shelter is to get people into permanent housing.
"We want to do the right thing for everyone."
Campion isn't concerned about why Ford opened the door for Big City Mayors to ask him to invoke the notwithstanding clause to deal with encampments.
"The way I look at it, it makes sense to try and get it in. It opens the door to asking for more support. We need more money for social services and to build affordable housing," he said.
Meanwhile, Niagara Falls Mayor Jim Diodati said he's created a notice of motion that will be presented to city council .
"I'm asking for the premier to invoke the notwithstanding clause and a bunch of other things - it's a pretty big, all-encompassing motion that includes things from the Big City Mayors, things from other mayors, my own research," he said.
"What we're doing isn't working. It's failing miserably. We need a revamped, recharged approach. I think we know what's gone wrong and we need to change our path."
Diodati said many people living in encampments have mental health and addiction problems and that "a person in that state is not making good choices."
"We need to intervene. We can't live in a lawless society. We can't allow public drug use and people defecating and urinating in people's yards and leaving needles behind and people fearing for their safety," he said.
"We need to take some very aggressive, proactive approaches and get our hands wrapped around this problem and deal with it."
Diodati said he supports Ford's approach of asking municipal leaders for "buy-in."
"He doesn't want to make it look like it's a top-down approach. It's a smart approach. You deal with your people on the front lines - with the people that are right in the mix ... with the people with the lived experiences and come up with a solution and then get the province to come in and bring down the hammer," he said.
"He's saying, step up and speak up and I'm going to be one of those voices. It's a tough love approach. No more kid gloves, it doesn't work."
Asked what should happen to people living in encampments once broken up, Diodati said more facilities and spaces should be built.
"A lot of them refuse. We're spending a lot of money on cleaning these encampments and a lot of these people refuse to go and that's unacceptable," he said.
"You can't set up a tent next to a school, or a church or somebody's house and do your drugs and all the other illegal activities that are carried on there."
Diodati said he believes the courts have become "too soft" and, as a result, "police have thrown their hands in the air."
"They think, why even bother, they're just going to be out and we're going to be dealing with this again. We've got to empower police, we've got to stop letting the courts decide how we handle homeless policies.
"It's wrong - that's not their job, that's not their role and they're far from experts at it," he said.
"We're not throwing our hands up in the air and saying just toss them out, we're saying, you must get housing, you must get counselling, you must get the services you need, you can't live out on the streets like this.
CAPTION: Having the provinceinvoke the constitutional notwith- standing clause would give Welland the tools needed to deal with homeless encampments in unsafe and dangerous areas of the city, says Mayor Frank Campion.Metroland file photo

CREDIT: Dave Johnson and Ray Spiteri Reporters

Document 92 of 249

'I've yet to get through one ... without crying': Niagarans honour the people who served and continue to serve

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ive-yet-get-through-one-without-crying/docview/3126871227/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27I%27ve+yet+to+get+through+one+...+without+crying%27%3A+Niagarans+honour+the+people+who+served+and+continue+to+serve&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-11-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As wreaths were laid, some tears were shed as Niagarans honoured the men and women who fought for and serve to protect their freedoms.
"We're here to remember them and the sacrifices they made on our behalf to make our country the nation it has come to be- a nation that cares for all people and looks for peace in our world, in our time and in the years to come," Rev. Canon Jim Powell said during his Remembrance Day address at Fairview Cemetery in Niagara Falls.
Hundreds of people attended the city's ceremony, which featured music from Niagara Falls Concert Band; the singing of "O Canada," "Amazing Grace" and "God Save the King" by Reese Paterson; the reading of "In Flanders Fields" by Wayne Hardwick, and the placing of the wreaths by various community organizations.
St. Catharines Memorial Park also drew a large attendance as many people stood in a cold wind that blew over wreathes and swirled leaves at the city's cenotaph.
St. Catharines MPP JennieStevens, the NDP's critic for Veterans, Legions and Military Affairs, accompanied by party Leader Marit Stiles, attended the ceremony. They later joined veterans and their families at Royal Canadian Legion halls on George and Chestnut streets.
"It's important to get out and thank as many people as possible for their service and to remember not just those we've lost but those who have served and continue to serve today," said Stiles.
The legion plays a critical role in supporting veterans and their communities, Stevens said, as many branches struggle with dwindling membership.
"Our veterans are starting to look younger... and camaraderie is huge at our legions (branches) and it's up to us residents to go in and support our legions and become members," she said.
"We can honour our veterans of the past, present and the future, and we can go back for camaraderie into our legions and share that wealth of stories."
Other than the rustling of leaves on the ground and in the trees, Welland's Chippawa Park was quiet throughout its ceremony Monday.
At least 200 people attended the service that played a live feed of CBC Radio's Remembrance Day coverage from Ottawa.
Wreaths from Welland Museum and the City of Welland were laid ahead of time at Welland-Crowland War Memorial in the southwest corner of the park.
After the ceremony, people laid their poppies on the memorial and pinned them to the city's wreath.
At the restored Niagara-on-the-Lake cenotaph, more than 500 people attended a ceremony.
Members of the 809 Newark Royal Canadian Air Cadets stood watch as crowds gathered in front of the courthouse adorned with poppies.
Polish soldiers also took part in the ceremony. From 1917 to 1919, more than 22,000 soldiers of Polish descent came to Niagara-on-the-Lake to train at Camp Kosciuszko, now known as the Commons.
As a crowd of about 300 people filed in at the cenotaph in Grimsby, their attention was directed upward by the rumbling sound of an Avro Lancaster and B-25 from the Canadian Warplane Heritage Museum, the Second World War-era planes that flew across Niagara communities.
"I came home for Remembrance Day," said former town resident Damian Robertson who now lives in Alberta.
Robertson served with 1st Battalion of the Royal Canadian Regiment and was in Kosovo in 1999 and 2000.
"That was a NATO peacekeeping mission," he said. "I've been remembering my service all morning, and my friends, the camaraderie, and the work we did."
Retired Grimsby district fire chief Rick Boyd noted his father Norman Boyd is among the names listed on the Second World War memorial at the cenotaph. His father served on board HMCS Longueuil, a Royal Canadian Navy ship that did convoy protection work as part of the Battle of the Atlantic during the Second World War.
"A lot of the stories he would tell; he would be six weeks without seeing land," Boyd said. "They would pick up a convoy and drop them off in Derry, England, turn around and come back, pick up (a convoy) off Newfoundland, turn around and go back again."
Cathy-Lynn Hanson was at the service to lay a wreath in memory of her father Sgt. James Johnston, who fought in the Korean War.
"My entire family background for generations has been military and it's just something that's deep in my heart," said Hanson, who is secretary of the RCMP Golden Horseshoe veterans detachment and a former commanding officer with Grimsby Cadet Corps.
Colin Paddon served with the British troops in Northern Ireland and as a peacekeeper in Cyprus before joining the navy and then army of the Canadian Armed Forces.
"I lost a lot of friends in the engagements," said Paddon, who was pleased to see such a large turnout for the service.
"I've yet to get through one of these without crying."
CAPTION: Respect is paid to veterans as the St. Catharines' Remembrance Day service at the cenotaph on St. Paul Street drew a large crowd Monday morning.Colin Paddon, who served with the British troops in Northern Ireland and as a peacekeeper in Cyprus before joining Canada's navy and then army, at Monday's Remembrance Day service in Grimsby.Bob Tymczyszyn St. Catharines StandardMark Newman Metroland

Document 93 of 249

Readers write Letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write-letters-editor/docview/3126678847/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write+Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-11-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hope for the
future damaged
Canada ought to offer sanctuary to those threatened by Trump administration,
Nov. 7
It is a fact, in the case of movies, sequels are usually shoddy attempts to squeeze more money from the public.
Except for "The Godfather Part II" sequels are usually not very good and painful to endure.
I fear "Donald Trump Part 2" is going to be the worst sequel ever. I am not only amazed this person was elected for another try at president but at the size of his victory. My opinion of Americans has eroded as I grow older and hopefully wiser. I told friends and relatives I thought Trump would win and they laughed.
Look whose laughing now. Pierre Poilievre, that's who!
From the Roman to British empires, history has shown great powers were not defeated by external enemies, they rotted from within. The Americans seem well on their way.
Other governments, including Canada's, are putting on a brave face and declaring a willingness to work with a new Trump and Republican U.S. Good luck.
The day after the election, I watched a Buffalo TV news segment. One guy said he voted for Trump because gas was a lot cheaper when he was president. How does one argue with logic like that?
As a result of this election, my hope for the world, for the future, for my children and grandchildren, has been seriously damaged.
Richard Murri, Niagara Falls
Ford cheques
are nonsensical
Ford spending nearly half of Ontario's tax windfall on $200 'rebate' cheques,
Oct. 30
Premier Doug Ford has decided to return $3.2 billion to the citizens of the province, irrespective of how wealthy they are.
Does this make any sense? Why not fund projects that will reduce the province's operating costs?
To reduce the cost of police services, provide Apple tracking devices to the owner of every vehicle less than seven years old. This could make car theft far less attractive. If we reduce the car theft epidemic, we may be able to reduce policing costs and save taxpayers dollars.
Every hospital in the province has a charitable foundation seeking funds to upgrade equipment and enhance the quality of health care.
Reimburse users of Highway 407 through the annual tax filing to attract greater use of the toll highway during peak hours and reduce traffic on the 401 and Queen Elizabeth Way.
This could also take the form of a lottery-style draw that selects "winners" to receive rebates.
Promote cost-saving ideas that reduce operating costs of any taxpayer-funded activity by the creation of a panel of stakeholders that review proposals and recommend acceptance.
The premier's plan is a one-time event. Those listed above could produce savings for years.
J. David Strathern,
Niagara Falls

Document 94 of 249

Welland mayor joins call for power to deal with homeless encampments: Campion says city needs more tools 'to create an effective and compassionate response'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/welland-mayor-joins-call-power-deal-with-homeless/docview/3126340676/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Welland+mayor+joins+call+for+power+to+deal+with+homeless+encampments%3A+Campion+says+city+needs+more+tools+%27to+create+an+effective+and+compassionate+response%27&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-11-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Arguing the city has done everything in its power to deal with homeless encampments, Welland Mayor Frank Campion has joined other municipal leaders across the province asking Ontario's premier for stronger tools.
On Wednesday, Campion issued a "Mayor's Message," which stated he sent a letter to Premier Doug Ford to support the use of the notwithstanding clause of the Charter of Rights and Freedoms to override court rulings that limit the ability of municipalities to remove encampments on public land.
"Balancing the rights of those in need with the rights of the broader community regarding access to public spaces and overall community safety is a priority that requires more tools to create an effective and compassionate response," Campion said in the message.
The message explains the city has done everything in its jurisdiction to address the issue. It partnered with the region in a co-ordinated response that includes outreach to those in need and connected individuals with emergency shelter and other services. The region is currently building a new shelter in Welland, set to open by the end of year.
However, Campion said the city needs to be able to enact temporary bylaws that "prioritize the health and safety of all residents while ensuring responsible and dignified support for those experiencing homelessness." What such a bylaw would entail is not yet known.
The mayor also submitted a notice of motion at Tuesday's meeting. If passed, it would allow council as a whole to formally make the same request. The next meeting of council is on Nov. 26.
Campion's move follows after 12 of 29 members of the Big City Mayors Association, including St. Catharines Mayor Mat Siscoe, signed a letter to Ford asking the province to enact tougher laws to help them deal with encampments, homelessness and addiction issues and "where necessary use the notwithstanding clause" to ensure any efforts won't be overturned by the courts.
Among those who did not sign the Big Cities Mayors letter to Ford were Toronto's Olivia Chow and Hamilton's Andrea Horwath.
Horwath said last Friday she won't sign the letter.
The province has "every ability" to invoke the Charter without the urging of municipal leaders, she said in a story by the Hamilton Spectator.
"So this is more about politics, I think, than it is about actual solving of problems. And I'm here to solve problems."
A ruling last year in Waterloo found it was a violation of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms for the municipality to evict people from an encampment without adequate shelter. The Toronto Star reported there were similar rulings in Kingston and British Columbia.
Section 33, the notwithstanding clause, of the Charter of Rights and Freedoms gives the provincial government the ability to pass legislation that overrides certain portions of the Charter.
Earlier this week, city workers cleared the remnants of an encampment that sprang up beside the walls of the second Welland Canal aqueduct. Shelter space was found for at least three people living in the encampment and another person living in a parkette at East Main Street and The Boardwalk.
With files by Karena Walter, Dave Johnson and the
Hamilton Spectator
CAPTION: Tents were set up near the Welland Public Library and Civic Square earlier this fall. Mayor Frank Campion has joined some other Ontario municipal leaders in calls to Premier Doug Ford to give them more authority to address the issue of homelessness.Metrolandfile photo

Document 95 of 249

Readers write Letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write-letters-editor/docview/3125954386/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write+Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-11-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Remember them
Poppy campaign begins in
Niagara, Oct. 25
Every year, on the 11th of November, we pause from our daily activities to pay a special tribute to all who served our country in time of war.
Many served and fought, and returned to their homes, families, friends and normal life from the fields of war. Simply said, they did their duty and we remember their sense of service.
And, for it all, we are forever grateful and words cannot adequately express our thanks.
But there are some who did not return to home and family, and we feel that sense of loss. All we can do is remember them, offer a silent prayer of thanks, and even shed a tear. We can cry. And that is fine, for we should never forget but always remember.
Perhaps the most touching words on their day are the opening lines of Col. John McCrae's immortal poem, "In Flanders Fields."
In Flanders fields the poppies blow
Between the crosses, row on row,
That mark our place; and in the sky
The larks, still bravely singing, fly
Scarce heard amid the guns below.
We are the Dead. Short days ago
We lived, felt dawn, saw sunset glow,
Loved and were loved, and now we lie
In Flanders fields.
John Burtniak, St. Catharines

St. Catharines' notorious self-cleaning washroom to be closed and removed, Nov. 5

My advice to this naive city council is don't spend another nickel on this money draining toilet experiment to justify this inexplicable project.

Siscoe proposes budget with 2.71% St. Catharines tax hike, Oct. 29

Actions speak much louder than words and clearly this mayor does not care about taxpayer affordability.
Allen McKay, St. Catharines

Document 96 of 249

Siscoe wants encampments out of 'inappropriate' places: Mayor explains his support for province to use 'notwithstanding clause'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/siscoe-wants-encampments-out-inappropriate-places/docview/3125039721/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Siscoe+wants+encampments+out+of+%27inappropriate%27+places%3A+Mayor+explains+his+support+for+province+to+use+%27notwithstanding+clause%27&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-11-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: St. Catharines Mayor Mat Siscoe says he's been on record saying the city shouldn't tear down encampments if there's nowhere for unhoused people to go - and that hasn't changed since he signed a letter asking the province for tougher measures to deal with the issue.
What he wants, he says, is the ability to move encampments out of sensitive areas like near schools or up against residential homes.
"We know that there are drugs being consumed in some of these encampments. We know that human trafficking is taking place in some of these encampments. Those are public safety issues for the community," he said in an interview Monday.
"We have to have the ability to say, no, you can't set up there. That's a sensitive place."
Siscoe was one of 12 mayors who signed a letter Thursday to Ontario Premier Doug Ford requesting his government take action to help municipalities deal with issues related to mental health, addictions and homeless encampments, including use of the notwithstanding clause "where necessary" to ensure measures are implemented.
Use of the notwithstanding clause would empower the province to override a court ruling on the constitutionality of removing encampments when there's nowhere for people to go.
Siscoe explained his stance to council Monday and said the current situation is "untenable."
"The court rulings in Ontario- Waterloo and Kingston specifically- have made it clear that the court believes municipalities can't regulate where and when encampments can happen.
"I agree with the idea that we shouldn't tear down encampments if we can't offer shelter- but we should be able to have some power in regulating where and when they are set up."
A precedent-setting court ruling last year in Waterloo found it was a violation of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms for the municipality to evict people from an encampment without adequate shelter. The Toronto Star reported there were similar rulings in Kingston and British Columbia.
Section 33, the notwithstanding clause, of the Charter of Rights and Freedoms gives the provincial government the ability to pass legislation that overrides certain portions of the charter.
The letter sent to Ford on Oct. 31 was signed by Siscoe and the mayors of Barrie, Brampton, Brantford, Cambridge, Chatham-Kent, Clarington, Oakville, Oshawa, Pickering, Sudbury and Windsor. They requested the province become an intervener on any court case that restricts the ability of municipalities to regulate and prohibit encampments.
Earlier last week, Ford said he wanted Ontario's Big City Mayors, an association made up of 29 municipal leaders, to show "backbone" and support use of the notwithstanding clause option by putting a request in writing.
"I'm the first one to say, I recognize that using Section 33 of the constitution is something that shouldn't be done without a lot of forethought and we're not proposing that willy nilly," Siscoe said in an interview after Monday's meeting.
"The mayors who signed off on this, we've been having this discussion for many months trying to figure out a path forward."
Siscoe said he didn't know if invoking Section 33 would be necessary for the province, but at some point municipalities have to be given the ability to regulate where encampments are located.
"Right now, I can regulate where someone puts a driveway. I can regulate whether you get a shed in your backyard. I can regulate all kinds of things, but I can't regulate what situations are inappropriate for encampments."
He said in St. Catharines, the community may be able to bear an encampment in Richard Pierpoint Park, for instance, more than across the street from an elementary school or up against someone's backyard. Setting up on St. Paul Street in front of downtown businesses is another area he said is not appropriate and can't continue.
"My heart breaks for the person that's forced into that situation, but there's a business owner that's trying to keep, five, 10, 15 people employed. And if nobody's willing to walk through the door because there's a tent loaded in front with a shopping cart and all kinds of paraphernalia, that's a problem," he said.
Siscoe said the courts are putting an unreasonable demand on the city because the city has no capacity to offer shelter-that falls to Niagara Region. He said the city's hands are tied based on the actions of an entirely different level of government.
"From a city standpoint, we're in a very difficult place where residents are asking us repeatedly, 'What are you doing?' And the constraints that have been put on us, they're not tenable anymore," Siscoe said.
Deputy Mayor Bill Phillips, who attended a recent Ontario Big City Mayors meeting on behalf of Siscoe when he was overseas, said there was a consensus there that they need assistance from the province.
"Many, many of the mayors that were present expressed identical concerns of what you expressed today. Their cities are in the same condition that we are in and every mayor there, virtually, was asking for that assistance."
St. Patrick's Coun. Caleb Ratzlaff said the fact mayors are asking the premier to use the notwithstanding clause indicates Ontarians have come to a point where change is necessary when it comes to encampments and the need for housing for all.
He said rent control is important and the eviction rate has to be addressed if the problem is ever going to be solved.
"Many of the things that the letter asks for is just a Band-Aid because cities can't deal with the issues, as Coun. Phillips said, on our own," Ratzlaff said.
"It's important to continue to demand the province address the root causes that have led to the situation in our cities. It doesn't matter if you're in an encampment or if you're a homeowner- you're a resident and you deserve housing."
CAPTION: Parts of an encampment can be seen in Fairview Park in St. Catharines in October. St. Catharines Mayor Mat Siscoe wants the ability to move encampments out of sensitive areas like near schools.Mat Siscoe was one of 12 Ontario mayors who signed a letter asking Premier Doug Ford to use the constitutional notwithstanding clause if necessary to deal with homeless encampments.metroland file photometroland file photo

Document 97 of 249

Homeless encampment behind Welland city hall gone: Four people from two locations have moved into shelters and city staff has cleared what was left behind

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/homeless-encampment-behind-welland-city-hall-gone/docview/3125038358/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Homeless+encampment+behind+Welland+city+hall+gone%3A+Four+people+from+two+locations+have+moved+into+shelters+and+city+staff+has+cleared+what+was+left+behind&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-11-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A homeless encampment just 60 metres from the entrance to Welland Public Library and Welland Civic Square is no more.
Chief administrative officer Rob Axiak said city workers cleared out what remained of tents and other items left behind by those who set up in a small hollow beside the walls of thesecond Welland Canal's aqueduct.
He said shelter space was found for at least three people living in the encampment and a person at the parkette at the corner of East Main Street and The Boardwalk.
The encampment, alongside the Welland Recreational Canal, sprang up sometime in September with one or two tents on the south side of the former aqueduct.
It grew to five to six tents with bicycles, a beach umbrella and what appeared to be a green compost bin on site. It's unknown how many people were living at the site and Axiak said some may have moved out before shelter space was found.
"They took a lot of their personal belongings with them," said Axiak of those who were moved into shelters.
He said there was no damage to either city-owned property from the encampments.
When discovered, Niagara Assertive Street Outreach (NASO) workers visited the former aqueduct site, looking to help individuals living there.
NASO engages individuals across Niagara experiencing unsheltered homelessness "to promote, connect and co-ordinate access to the range of supports available across Niagara's Homeless-Serving System, including emergency shelter, housing and support services," said the agency's website.
Earlier this year, the city received advice indicating it has no legal authority to evict encampment residents based in city parks and other city-owned property unless there is sufficient alternative shelter space to accommodate individuals experiencing homelessness within the jurisdiction.
There'scase law that speaks to the issue stemming from decisions inWaterloo and Kingston, and people have a right to shelter under theCanadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, which guarantees the right to life, liberty and security of the person, council also heard.
Twelve Big City Mayors, including St. Catharines Mayor Mat Siscoe, recently signed a letter to Premier Doug Ford asking the province to enact tougher laws to help them deal with encampments, homelessness and addictions and "where necessary use the notwithstanding clause" to ensure any efforts won't be overturned by the courts.
Last week, Ford said he wanted Ontario's Big City Mayors, an association of 29 municipal leaders, to show "backbone" and support using the notwithstanding clause by putting it in writing "if they really want the homeless situation to improve."
Axiak said the removal of the homeless encampment in Welland had nothing to do with Ford and the Big City Mayors.
In a recent interview, Axiak said the city could legally move someone from an encampment if shelter space is available or find ways to get them back in their homes or with a relative.
Tuesday, he said it typically takes two weeks to find shelter space and that's what happened in this case with involvement from NASO and The Hope Centre, which will operate a temporary 50-bed emergency homeless shelter to be built at 851 Ontario Rd. on behalf of Niagara Region.
"Everyone was quite co-operative," he said of the encampment's residents.
Earlier this year, Welland formed an encampment task force that shares information, tracks, co-ordinates and provides support to people living in homeless encampments.
On it are city bylaw, community services and fire department staff, staff from Niagara Region,The Hope Centre,Gateway of Niagara,Niagara Assertive Street Outreach and Niagara Regional Police.
It meets weekly or every other week, depending on how things are in the community.
The group looks at encampment locations, as well as any relevant complaints or emergency calls, to ensure adequate supports are provided to move individuals into appropriate shelter opportunities.
It could ask that police deal with an encampment or for more caseworkers to help the homeless find shelter space.
If city staff notice an encampment on city-owned land, they can call 211, which starts the process with NASO conducting on-site reviews and assessments. Residents are also encouraged to call 211 if they spot an encampment.
Residents with concerns about encampments can view general information and see frequently asked questions about encampments and homelessness on the city's website atwelland.ca/Services/Encampments.asp.
With files from The
Toronto Star
CAPTION: Welland's Main Street Bridge is seen in the background ofa homeless encampmentin the city's downtown that has been cleared since its occupants were moved into a shelter.Metrolandfile photo

CREDIT: Dave Johnson Reporter With files from The Toronto Star

Document 98 of 249

Readers write Letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write-letters-editor/docview/3124325510/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write+Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-11-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Setting our
own priorities
Doug Ford spending nearly half of Ontario's tax windfall on $200 'rebate' cheques, Oct. 30.
To my neighbours in Ontario:
Come one sparkling day in January, apparently you and I will be jumping for joy as we retrieve from our mailboxes a cheque from Ontario Premier Doug Ford's government for at least $200.
Apparently, we can use this against our credit card debt from Christmas, gas for the car for holiday travel, daycare, etc. The critics say that this is a good thing for our January budget, but what about the other 11 months of the year?
My critique is far more solemn.
How is this even remotely responsible governing when our health-care system is in shambles? You can build all the new hospitals you want, but if you don't have doctors, nurses, and support staff maintaining them, then you just have one more closed emergency room and fewer beds for patients.
Simply stated, our nurses and support staff are at a breaking point.
So, let me offer you this idea. Our health system is in crisis. The provincial government doesn't seem willing to fix it. Maybe we have to do it for them.
Therefore, I would encourage all of you to take Ford's new year gift and donate it to your local hospital. You'll get a tax receipt and more importantly, together we may save a life.
If Ford won't put this money where it is needed, then let's do it for him and show where our priorities are in this province.
Kim Wright, Welland
Who's paying
for this? Look
in the mirror
Doug Ford announces details of $200 cheques for 15 million Ontarians, Oct. 30
My mind wanders when I'm shaving in the morning and today I found myself wondering what I would do with the $200 that Premier Doug Ford is going to send me.
A cartful of groceries or perhaps a couple of weeks of gasoline?
I thought, "Who is going to pay for this gift? It's not coming out of Ford's wallet."
Then I looked up into the mirror.
David Fowler, Wainfleet
Donating
the rebate
to Ford's foes
I will keep $50 of the bribery money Doug Ford is sending everyone. But the way l see it, the money could have gone a long way in funding health-care services or homelessness initiatives, but the Ford government thinks everyone is stupid.
I will give the provincial New Democrats $50, the federal Liberals $50 and the federal NDP $50. I will help any party defeat the farcical provincial Conservatives.
So thanks to Premier Ford for giving me my donation money for political parties that will address health care and the many other things the Ontario government is currently messing up.
Russ Young, Fort Erie

Document 99 of 249

Group wants answers on shortages of health supplies: Piecing together supplies like 'patchwork quilt,' nurse says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/group-wants-answers-on-shortages-health-supplies/docview/3123253781/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Group+wants+answers+on+shortages+of+health+supplies%3A+Piecing+together+supplies+like+%27patchwork+quilt%2C%27+nurse+says&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-11-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The president of a medical supply company went out on deliveries in Hamilton and Niagara last weekend to talk to home-care patients about a provincewide crisis that has left them without vital medical provisions.
Bayshore HealthCare confirmed that founder Stuart Cottrelle personally delivered supplies to patients in Hamilton on Oct. 26 and in Niagara on Oct. 27.
The visits come as doctors, care workers and patients have described utter chaos since the province changed what supplies are available to order on Sept. 24, as well as the contracts to procure the provisions, fulfil the requests and deliver them.
The province has not explained why the changes instantly plunged home care into desperate ongoing shortages of supplies needed to care for some of Ontario's most vulnerable patients including those with cancer, diabetes, babies, people on life support and the dying.
"It's shocking," said Natalie Mehra, executive director of the Ontario Health Coalition. "The supplies are out there. Emergency departments are getting them. Pharmacies have them."
Premier Doug Ford said Tuesday that his government is "fixing the problem" and he's "holding people accountable."
However, the offices of the premier and Health Minister Sylvia Jones refused to answer questions about who is accountable, what is being done to fix the shortages and why they have gone on for more than one month.
"Those are fair questions, and they should be answered," said Mehra. "The public has the right to know - this is public money and there should be public accountability."
Mehra said the former system was working well with the Ontario Health Coalition rarely getting complaints about the availability and quality of home-care supplies.
"We got mostly people complaining that they deliver too much stuff and there's a lot of waste," said Mehra.
"Supplies not showing up or not being available - including vital, urgent supplies - I don't think I've ever gotten a complaint like that in my 27 years of doing this."
Now the coalition is hearing from patients like Penny Moore, who says she ended up in the emergency room with an infection because of missing and poor-quality supplies.
The 61-year-old from London requires a medical device known as a Foley catheter to drain urine from the bladder into a collection bag because of medical issues.
For nearly nine years, Moore said she had no issue getting the supplies she needed to manage the catheter at home.
After the changes in September, Moore said some essential supplies were missing from the order and the ones she got were vastly different from what she had before.
"They're cheaper," Moore said. "They're thinner. The tubing is one quarter the size of tubing that normally is on a catheter bag."
As a result, Moore said the urine did not drain properly through the small tube.
"I was in so much pain," Moore said. "I felt like I was in labour having a baby and I couldn't even get out of bed. I couldn't walk."
She sought help at an emergency department where she says she was diagnosed with an infection, given antibiotics and had her Foley catheter changed to one that is higher quality.
Moore doesn't know what to do when the bag needs to be changed in about a week.
She says she's received no help from the emergency number Ontario Health atHome set up for patients at 1-866-377-7567.
Her care nurse has been trying to get better quality supplies to no avail, she says.
"It scares me and frustrates me," said Moore. "I always worry about getting another infection."
A Hamilton home-care nurse also expressed concern about the quality of some supplies this week.
Metroland is not naming the nurse because they are not authorized by their employer to speak.
The nurse described having to dress a bacterial skin infection like a "patchwork quilt," piecing together small absorbent pads with tape because the large, thick and super absorbent roll of padding that is normally used wasn't sent.
It's not clear who is responsible for the alleged changes in the quality of the supplies.
Sources say the formulary that lists what is available to order for home-care patients was overhauled in September.
It's unclear who made the changes, but Ontario Health atHome is the provincial agency that co-ordinates in-home and community-based care for over 650,000 Ontario patients a year.
It is a subsidiary of the province's superagency Ontario Health.
At the same time, new contracts started for procurement of supplies and fulfilment of the orders that gave a small number of companies responsibility for large parts of the province.
Orders in Hamilton, Niagara, Haldimand and Brant are filled by Bayshore Specialty Rx for supplies and Calea for infusion medicine. However, neither of the companies procure the items they deliver. Procurement is a separate contract.
Ontario Health atHome has not answered questions about the changes or the resulting shortages.
"We are doing everything we can to address this situation," Ontario Health atHome said in a statement on Oct. 22
Joanna Frketich is a reporter
at The Hamilton Spectator.
jfrketich@thespec.com
CAPTION: Natalie Mehra, executive director of the Ontario Health Coalition, says "the public has a right to know" how the home-care shortages happened.The Canadian Press file photo

CREDIT: Joanna Frketich Reporter

Document 100 of 249

letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters-editor/docview/3122483654/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-10-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ford driven
by driving
Premier Doug Ford seems to have quite a penchant for dabbling in areasthat relate to traffic.
His first dalliance was with changing our licence plates. The ones that couldn't be seen in the dark under traffic lights. It proved costly for the taxpayer. Isn't there an axiom - if it isn't broken why fix it? Did modesty prevail here?
Ford subsequently moved on to alter the speed limit on parts of 400-series highways to 110 kilometres per hour (km/h). That's how many cars are driven, illegally? The result was, in those speed limit areas drivers could go faster merely to catch up to the 100 km/h areas down the road. As speed kills, will insurance firms raise their rates to cover the likely more devastating crashes?
The next traffic issue in Ford's sights has been Garden City Skyway in St. Catharines. To ease congestion the number of lanes is to increase. Drivers will leave a bottleneck to enjoy 2,500 metres of extra lanes only to come back to the bottleneck after exiting the skyway. This doesn't seems like any solution to the problem, which isn't too much of a problem in reality.
The latest traffic issue Ford has strayed onto is tunnel building under Hwy. 401. A cost overrun extravaganza in the making if ever there is one. Again, where is the logic? More traffic, more environmental degradation. Getting vehicles off the road should be the government's ambition.
Such is Ford's tunnel vision, although if health care or education was at the end of the tunnel, one's point of view might be completely different.
Ross Smith, Niagara Falls
Use caution when reselling vehicle
There is a new scam, at least new to me, which involves anyone selling a vehicle.
The prospective buyer contacts the seller and expresses an interest in the vehicle. In my case, it is a fifth-wheel camping trailer. The person will ask if there are any maintenance records or a vehicle history report available for scrutiny before making the purchase. If everything checks out the buyer will make the purchase on the spot.
If you don't have a vehicle history report, the buyer will suggest a source, someone they have used before. They will provide a link where one can be obtained for a minimal fee and then ask it be forwarded to them before they view your vehicle.
The buyer said they are only being cautious as they have been fooled previously and bought something not as advertised.
It turns out, if you obtain the vehicle history report and forward it to the buyer, you will never hear from them again. Their job is to sell vehicle history reports.
It has happened to me twice so far. Two different contacts, two different vehicle report companies, with almost identical pitches. The first time, I couldn't see the harm. I now have a comprehensive vehicle history report I can offer the buyer when they want to view the trailer.
Mike Moore, Welland

Document 101 of 249

Readers write

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write/docview/3121512858/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-10-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Entangling
separate issues
Council pay hike more proof governance reform needed, Letters, Oct. 7
In the past month, there have been a number of letters to the editor that have sadly combined two distinctive issues. One is the number of elected municipal councillors. The other is the issue of a single city of Niagara.
Some of these letters have suggested municipal councillors are overpaid. These are unfortunate.
I had a conversation about pay with former St. Catharines city councillor Mark Elliott. He estimated that the pay for his duties was far below minimum wage.
Elliott served on all the committees that secured, with great difficulty, the combined arts centre of Brock University and the City of St. Catharines. This was a herculean feat and worthy of more than minimum wage.
A single city of Niagara is needed to ensure better land use planning to protect our threatened wildlife, farmland and water.
A comprehensive approach to these issues is needed, which is frustrated by municipal fragmentation.
A reduction in the number of councillors is a likely sad downside of a long-needed reform.
John Bacher, St. Catharines
A sad response
Weak argument, Letters, Oct. 25
I'm not surprised by the response from the former politician in supporting Niagara Catholic District School Board trustee Natalia Benoit.
He seems to believe Benoit's attitude is not hateful if her personal beliefs and her interpretation of her religion leads her to that conclusion.
His response and Benoit's actions are so sad.
David Pihach, Wainfleet
Full-service
hospital needed
in Welland
NDP leader promises full-service hospital in Welland if elected, Oct. 7
We need a full-service hospital in Welland.
The population is growing rapidly each year because of new housing being built all over the place.
Premier Doug Ford's government says it is short of money and yet they are building a brand new hospital in Niagara Falls, and calling it a south Niagara hospital.
There is a shortage of staff help, nurses included.
I was supposed to have a CT scan done but it was delayed until 2025 because they say the system is backlogged.
That's not good to hear, as there seems no shortage of cash for other priorities. Health-care spending should come first.
Seniors should not have to travel out of their own cities to get the help and care they need.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles and local MPP Jeff Burch are good leaders who are trying to keep our hospitals going straight forward because it seems Ford doesn't see the writing in front of him that tells him to save these hospitals and stop using public money unwisely.
Roscoe Reilly, Welland
