Document 102 of 249

Ontario's vision for Niagara may not include Marineland: 'This is going to put the Niagara region on the map in ways it's never been done before'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontarios-vision-niagara-may-not-include/docview/3121512838/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario%27s+vision+for+Niagara+may+not+include+Marineland%3A+%27This+is+going+to+put+the+Niagara+region+on+the+map+in+ways+it%27s+never+been+done+before%27&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-10-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A major amusement park is part of Ontario's grand vision to turn the Niagara region into Las Vegas north, but Marineland may not fit the bill, the provincial tourism minister says.
Stan Cho says his ministry has not spoken to the sprawling attraction, which is currently for sale and sits on 1,000 acres a short distance from Horseshoe Falls.
"I'll acknowledge that the site is well situated to the core," Cho said of the park's location, speaking with reporters recently at Queen's Park.
"There's no doubt about that, but it is privately held land and we are not currently in discussions with any of the private matters going around the sale of the land. But we know that there are other options."
The province would like to see an amusement park somewhere in the region to act as a marquee attraction as part of its "Niagara strategy," Cho said.
"There's a lot of land there that is available for things like amusement parks, increased shopping and all of that, five-star dining options," he said.
"Our goal isn't to pick exactly where and who, our goal is to create the conditions and provide the necessary sort of background with the foundation for those who want to invest capital to do it because there's going to be a return."
A new amusement park could be anywhere, and doesn't need to use the existing infrastructure of Marineland, he suggested.
The park said last year it was up for sale, but it has yet to announce a deal. Owner Marie Holer died last month, which set in motion a succession plan that has not been shared publicly.
This past summer, Marineland only opened for two months under significantly cheaper rates as it said it was "transitioning" to new owners. Much of the park, including its rides and many of the animals, was off-limits to visitors.
The park has been the site of a series of marine mammal deaths in recent years as the Ministry of the Solicitor General conducts a now four-year-long probe. Marineland has said the deaths are part of the cycle of life and defended its treatment of the animals.
"My main concern there is the animal welfare, just like the public," Cho said.
"We hope that they do the right thing and they listen to the public and look after the welfare of the animals."
Marineland did not respond to a request for comment about Cho's remarks and the government's plan for the region.
Doug Ford's government is looking to transform Niagara into a "Las Vegas of the north," Cho said at a recent committee meeting at Queen's Park.
Last week, he further explained the government's sprawling strategy for the area.
"I look at Niagara Falls as the hub of a wheel, but the spoke goes out 30 minutes in each direction to some of the most amazing places you've ever seen," he said.
The plans are grand, he explained, and they include a push for an international airport, more hotels, mass transit expansion within the region and more trains running to the area on GO Transit from Toronto.
"There is so much to offer within the region that we have not tapped, and what's been lacking is a cohesive vision to draw it together," Cho said.
"It's why people go to places like Las Vegas for four days or five nights, Niagara Falls for four hours or five hours or one night."
The province is also hoping to expand gaming and casino operations in the city.
The Trillium news outlet first reported on Ford's plan for casinos in the area.
Mohegan Gaming and Entertainment, which operates both casinos in Niagara Falls, signed a deal with Ontario Lottery and Gaming in 2018 to operate any casinos in the city until 2040, the outlet reported.
Ford wants to modify that deal, calling it a "monopoly" for Mohegan. He said there is a "tremendous opportunity" with 11 million tourists coming to the region.
"We just want to modernize it and clean it up, get more opportunities, more economic development, increase tourism," Ford said in early October.
"There's a whole market south of the border and we'd love to have them come up and spend a weekend, spend a week."
Cho said the vision is in its early days, but he's bullish on transforming the area. He wants more people experiencing wineries in Niagara-on-the-Lake, and more Great Lakes cruise ships docking in Lake Erie and Lake Ontario.
"This is going to put the Niagara region on the map in ways it's never been done before and we're super excited for it," he said.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: This past summer, Marineland only opened for two months under significantly cheaper rates as it said it was "transitioning" to new owners.Metroland file photo

CREDIT: Liam Casey The Canadian Press The Canadian Press

Full text: Tree planting
uprooted
NPCA, Region plant 1,000 trees by Decew water plant, Oct. 22
In a time where environmental concerns are rarely dealt with locally, it is wonderful to see members of the community and officials come together for such a cause.
However, there is some concern about the approach. By planting 1,000 trees and shrubs in an area that gets very little visitation, it raises the question as to the effectiveness of this initiative.
Instead these resources should be used to enhance green spaces within urban areas and centres so residents can actively benefit from these initiatives on a daily basis.
Planting trees in an isolated area does not tackle bigger issues of sustainability, urban development, pollution and habitat destruction.
If tree planting in remote areas is the only method of climate change reduction available, then the priority should be placed on holistic environmental policies that combine this type of tree planting with habitat restoration and pollution control so we can see real change within our community.
Keyanna Dolch, St. Catharines
Hitting mute
In new ad, Pierre Poilievre pivots from relentless critic to pedlar of hope, Oct. 10
Every day, my television mute button is getting a real workout as I must delete Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's ads, which constantly, desperately are trying to get him into the Prime Minister's Office.
Common sense is the main theme in these messages. Combining common sense and the Conservatives makes no sense. Ontario Premier Doug Ford consistently goes against the grain of anything that makes sense.
Poilievre says people will be able to afford food while at the same time Ford has no regard for our precious farmlands with Canada losing more than 300 acres per day. And Ford continues to pave over farmland with needless highways.
Ontario Place recently lost more than 800 mature trees - mowed and chipped at 2 a.m., under Ford's direction.
This week Ford is targeting bike lanes while apparently not realizing that taking a person off a bike and putting him in a car creates more gridlock.
Poilievre constantly points fingers at other parties. Well, I believe it is time "to bring it home" and take a look at Ford, Poilievre and the whole Conservative party - a common sense idea.
Faye Suthons, Wainfleet
Meaningless phrases
'Axe the Tax' - beware of false political catch phrases, Oct. 24
William Thomas's column is always intriguing. So droll. Even more so on Oct. 24 in which he squarely faces upcoming elections here and in the U.S.
How have we come to this? The endless comparison between Donald Trump and Pierre Poilievre is spot on. All catch phrases, no programs or party platforms to stand on and address either country's current problems. Just catchy, meaningless phrases. Empty-headed thoughtlessness.
It's not funny, except when Thomas draws it to our attention. Thank you, Mr. Thomas. Keep it up.
Gail Benjafield, St. Catharines

Readers write

Full text: Very lucky
to be Canadian
Harris speaks about
creating economic opportunity for Latino men; Trump airs familiar grievances, Oct. 22
I was born in Brooklyn, N.Y., a long time ago and brought to Canada at three years of age. With what's going on for our southern neighbour, I'm very thankful to be a Canadian.
Our nation surely has problems. We may have politicians we don't hold in high regard, federally and provincially, but we don't shoot them! We know election promises may be fairy tales, but we listen to the rhetoric and decide who we like - or in some cases who we dislike the least - and vote.

Weak argument for removal
Vote her out, Letters,
Oct. 18
Vote her out or vote her in - a very good point was made by the former Catholic high school principal in his letter on the motion from trustee Natalia Benoit to stop flying the Pride flag at Catholic schools.
However resorting to the "choose love or Benoit's hate" argument is regrettable and weak. Respectful rebuttal would be more helpful and juvenile speculation she "is so starved for attention, she is again spewing rhetoric of hate" is hateful in its own way.
It sounds a lot like hate for a different opinion. The notice of motion and supporting argument was on the Catholic school board website with them relying on both scripture and the Catechism of the Catholic Church. No hate involved.
It seems too easy to offer black-and-white, love-or-hate options. So the choice to vote for Benoit is not about choosing love or hate. It is about her arguments based on her beliefs in the Roman Catholic Church and the scriptures of her faith and her duty as a trustee of a Catholic school board.
Bruce Timms, St. Catharines

Russ Young, Fort Erie

Document 105 of 249

OLG makes gaming revenue payment to Niagara Falls: Since 1994, host communities have received more than $2.1 billion in non-tax gaming revenue

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/olg-makes-gaming-revenue-payment-niagara-falls/docview/3120340554/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=OLG+makes+gaming+revenue+payment+to+Niagara+Falls%3A+Since+1994%2C+host+communities+have+received+more+than+%242.1+billion+in+non-tax+gaming+revenue&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-10-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Lottery and Gaming Corp. has issued a second-quarter non-tax gaming revenue payment of $4.5 million to the City of Niagara Falls.
The payment, for the period July to September, was issued to the municipality for hosting Casino Niagara and Niagara Fallsview Casino Resort.
The second-quarter payment was lower than the first quarter payment of $5.8 million issued for April to June.
During the 2023 fiscal year, which ended March 31, OLG paid $19.6 million to Niagara Falls. In fiscal year 2022-23, the OLG made payments totalling $18.9 million to the city.
Payments to host communities are based on a formula applied across all gaming sites in Ontario using a graduated scale of gaming revenue at the hosted site.
"As it has for decades in Ontario, land-based gaming continues to serve as a source of good local jobs and generates vital economic activity in the communities that host gaming sites," said Stan Cho, minister of tourism, culture and gaming, in a news release.
"From helping build neighbourhood parks to supporting community programs, proceeds received through the Municipality Contribution Agreement with OLG help make municipalities all across the province better places to live and work."
Since 1994, host communities have received more than $2.1 billion in non-tax gaming revenue.
Niagara Falls has received more than $224 million.
Recent news reports indicate Ontario Premier Doug Ford's government is looking at making changes to the deal dictating how Niagara Falls casinos are run and potentially encourage more casinos to open in the city.
Currently, Mohegan operates as the service provider for Niagara Fallsview Casino Resort and Casino Niagara on OLG's behalf.
That deal, signed six years ago, expires in 2040. Prior to that, the two casinos were operated by OLG, which manages lotteries and casinos in Ontario.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Niagara Falls Review Staff

Document 106 of 249

Fort Erie eyes independent hospital: 'I think we can do it better ourselves,' says town councillor in support of mayor's motion

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fort-erie-eyes-independent-hospital/docview/3119503350/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Fort+Erie+eyes+independent+hospital%3A+%27I+think+we+can+do+it+better+ourselves%2C%27+says+town+councillor+in+support+of+mayor%27s+motion&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-10-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Laying out the litany of ways he considers health-care services have been degraded in Fort Erie, Mayor Wayne Redekop introduced a motion unanimously endorsed by town council Monday that calls on the province to commit to keeping funding used at Douglas Memorial Hospital within Fort Erie.
It also ponders a post-Niagara Health future for the hospital, calling for these services to be run by a qualified designated health-care services entity should Niagara Health choose not to do so.
Redekop's motion included a lengthy preamble detailing the history of Douglas Memorial and how the town got to the point it is at today - a former full-service hospital now run as a health-care site with chronic care beds, palliative care beds and diagnostics, and a 10-hous-a-day urgent care centre that is all slated to close when the new south Niagara Falls hospital opens in 2028.
The motion further called for the mayor and town staff to enter discussions with the Health Ministry, Niagara Health and other appropriate health-care providers about continuing to run primary care and other services at Douglas Memorial - with or without Niagara Health involvement.
In fortuitous timing, Redekop noted his motion came the same day Premier Doug Ford appointed former federal Liberal health minister Jane Philpott to head a primary care action team aimed at ensuring all Ontarians have a family doctor. He pointed to a statement by Philpott saying access to primary care should be as automatic as children being assigned to a public school in their neighbourhood.
"That really says it all," he said. "And that really says what those that have provided reports, commissions, investigators over the past 30 years across the country have been saying."
Ward 2 Coun. Nick Dubanow said it's long been time to address taking back control of health care, noting Fort Erie has a dedicated team of health-care professionals.
He also pointed to Haldimand War Memorial Hospital in Dunnville, a considerably smaller community than Fort Erie. The County of Haldimand itself has about 40,000 residents, he said.
"They're able to run a hospital in that community," he said, adding Fort Erie has the capacity to run one independently of a regional health-care administration.
"I'm of the opinion we don't necessarily need them at this point," he said. "I think we can do it better ourselves."
Ward 5 Coun. Tom Lewis again brought up a $3-million commitment the town made for the new hospital, saying it was the right decision at the time but was one made with a partner that has "changed the goalposts."
And never one to mince words, Ward 1 Coun. George McDermott said local politicians have continuously had to fight for the health care of their constituents. He said the people who make the decisions regarding health-care services are ignoring the fact Fort Erie is a swiftly growing community.
"They're not accounting for any of that when they make these crazy-ass decisions," he said.
CAPTION:

Full text: Ford spends,
Ontarians suffer
Doug Ford poised to send out pre-election cheques to 16 million Ontarians, Oct. 16
I have had the unfortunate and fortunate experience of visiting the Welland hospital emergency department three times in the past few weeks.
Unfortunate because I and my fellow visitors have been forced to wait for five to seven hours to see a physician. Old and very young, sitting and waiting.
Fortunate because the staff, the doctors and nurses, when we finally saw them, were professional, caring, helpful and sorry that it took so long.
I know every Canadian hospital is in the same situation.
I will reserve my comments for the mess in Ontario created by the current Progressive Conservative and previous Liberal governments.
Both have done their best to ensure we do not have enough money to pay for doctors and nurses and the proper hospital facilities.
Ontario's current government talks about all the money it spends, but restricts nurses' wages, doctors' incomes and closes emergency rooms. It encourages private clinics and sets up non-hospital operating facilities rather than ensuring hospitals can look after our population.
Premier Doug Ford says OHIP will cover visits to private clinics, but what he does not say is the amounts private clinics charge OHIP will be more than what a hospital charges.
There have been articles recently about wait times in emergencies and the weeks and months we have to wait for many medical procedures and simple procedures such as MRIs.
These facts are well known to anyone who has needs, but Ford would rather give us all - including many who do not need it - a $200 rebate cheque, free auto registration, close the Ontario Science Centre, build parking lots for private spas, dig billion-dollar vehicle tunnels and add more useless highways rather than fix health care, build and repair schools, improve social services and home care, build affordable housing and help fix climate change.
These are not surplus dollars - this is more debt.
Let's try spending our precious tax dollars wisely.
Jack Hughes, Welland
Rebate cheque
a disgrace
People are reacting after learning the Ontario government may offer $200 rebate cheques, Oct. 18
Premier Doug Ford removed the car registration fee just prior to the previous election and it worked out well for him.
The latest proposed gambit of a $200 bribe in the form of a rebate is a disgrace to the office of premier and is a failed attempt to distract the electorate from judging his government's agenda.
The Greenbelt fiasco, closure of the Ontario Science Centre, the Ontario Place spa, gridlocked traffic, the proposed Highway 401 tunnel, health-care service deterioration, homelessness all deserve attention.
J. David Strathern,
Niagara Falls

Full text: Dangerous
driving in Welland
Defective Memorial Park playground equipment being removed, July 23
I would like to express serious concern regarding safety on Memorial Park Drive in Welland.
There has been a noticeable increase in speeding along this road, with drivers frequently exceeding the posted speed limits.
This seemingly and potentially reckless behaviour puts children and seniors at potential risk as they cross the street in what is generally a safe area. Moreover, it may potentially endanger drivers on the road who are driving properly.
As example, I witnessed a driver speeding and nearly causing a collision with a slower-moving vehicle at around 10:30 p.m. Instead of slowing down, the driver executed a dangerous manoeuvre to overtake an SUV just before reaching the Lincoln/Wellington intersection.
And these speeders are constant. You hear them all hours of the day and night and it is obvious they are speeding.
It would be great if the City of Welland and/or police did something about this problem before someone is needlessly hurt, or worse, killed.
Ashley Mallenby, Welland
Time for Ford
to focus on
Niagara reform
Resident feedback sought as Welland ponders fewer wards, councillors, Oct. 16
Niagara has 12 separate lower-tier and one upper-tier municipality. We waste millions of dollars on administration of government services.
Reducing the number of politicians is something all lower tier municipalities in Niagara should be contemplating.
Niagara has 126 politicians, which supports duplication in government services and is costing local taxpayers millions more than it should. If saving tax money is the goal, then Niagara needs to eliminate duplication.
Niagara does not need 13 chief administrative officers, 13 finance departments, 13 planning departments, 11 fire services, two fire dispatch services and the countless number of economic development officers often working against each other to attract potential businesses to their municipality.
During previous elections, Premier Doug Ford criticized the size of government in Niagara.
"What we're trying to do is reduce the size and cost of government right across Ontario," Ford said in a June 2, 2020, article. "Our goal is to make sure that our government runs efficiently. And too many politicians is not a good thing. Too many politicians actually hurts communities."
The premier has since gone quiet on this issue. Instead of focusing on issues like expanded alcohol sales, building more highways or reducing bike lanes, Ford needs to refocus on issues he has identified as problematic in our communities, such as homelessness and better access to health care, and come back to his idea of reducing the number of politicians.
Allen McKay, St. Catharines

Document 109 of 249

MPPs lay out their priorities for fall session at Queen's Park: Health care, housing and affordability top issues for Niagara's New Democrat MPPs as provincial legislature resumes this week

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/mpps-lay-out-their-priorities-fall-session-at/docview/3118626670/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=MPPs+lay+out+their+priorities+for+fall+session+at+Queen%27s+Park%3A+Health+care%2C+housing+and+affordability+top+issues+for+Niagara%27s+New+Democrat+MPPs+as+provincial+legislature+resumes+this+week&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-10-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Health care, housing and affordability are some of the issues Niagara MPPs will be addressing when the provincial legislature resumes this week after an extended summer break.
Niagara Centre New Democrat MPP Jeff Burch said the five-month break was "an intentional ploy" by Premier Doug Ford and his Progressive Conservative government to "not face criticism and not allow the official opposition to do their job, which is to hold the government to account on provincial issues and issues in our riding."
"There's a reasonable schedule right now for the legislature sitting. You can't be there all the time ... and you have to be in your riding for a couple of months in the winter and a couple of months in the summer. But to be out of the legislature for almost half a year with no reasonable explanation really thwarts democracy and it hurts our ability to represent our constituents," he said.
Niagara Falls NDP MPP Wayne Gates said that with Queen's Park back in session, he will continue to raise several issues.
He has been seeking community support for a proposed motion that would compensate caregivers for the work they do, and legislation that would expand OHIP coverage for prostate cancer testing.
Gates said he will continue pushing for two-way, all-day GO train service between Toronto and Niagara Falls.
He will also advocate for the expansion of Niagara District Airport, a rebuild for Shaw Festival's Royal George Theatre, 24-7 care at Douglas Memorial Hospital and a reduction in MRI wait times, which Gates said can be more than 300 days for patients dealing with ailments deemed to be less serious than others.
"If your knees are so bad, your hips are so bad that you can't walk, you need to get an MRI done and you shouldn't have to wait 300 days or go to the States where you have to pay or go up to Mississauga where you've got to get a hotel room if you're going (for an MRI) at 2 a.m.," said Gates.
"I get calls every single day on that."
Burch said he will raise the issue of a medical supply shortage affecting home care workers across Ontario.
"Workers are calling my office concerned about not having things as basic as bandages for their patients, and those are things that need to be addressed," he said.
Also a priority: building a full-service hospital for Welland and restoring hospital services in Port Colborne.
Burch said there's also a "real problem" with people not being able to find housing.
"It's probably one of the bigger issues compared to other ridings, especially in places like Welland, where folks are having a real hard time finding housing or staying in the housing that they're in," he said.
St. Catharines NDP MPP Jennie Stevens said she's excited about going back to Queen's Park, adding she will prioritize issues that "matter most to residents."
She plans to bring forward a private member's bill to help residents heat their homes in the winter months.
Stevens began working on the proposed legislation last year after hearing from seniors and young families who were unable to pay their natural gas bills while already struggling with inflationary increases.
A similar relief program exists for electricity bills, she said, adding her home heating legislation would extend the electricity support program to gas bills.
Stevens said she will also continue calling for publicly funded, universal contraception, which she said would knock down barriers for women.
Niagara West PC MPP Sam Oosterhoff said the fall legislative session comes on the heels of a "very busy summer" for the government in Niagara.
He said the premier came to the region for three tours, including visits to Ontario Shipyards in St. Catharines to announce $10 million in funding through the Skills Development Fund, Airbus Helicopters Canada in Fort Erie to announce the purchase of three police helicopters and Jungbunzlauer Canada in Port Colborne to announce its groundbreaking of a new xanthan gum facility.
Oosterhoff said he also welcomed 13 provincial ministers and five parliamentary assistants to Niagara, and announced almost $100 million in provincial funding for the region. He said that included $6.1 million for Niagara Region through the Homelessness Prevention Program and more than $22 million and $20 million for Lincoln and Welland, respectively, through the Housing-Enabling Water Systems Fund.
Looking ahead, Oosterhoff said, he wants to ensure that industry and families have access to reliable and affordable energy while continuing to create good-paying jobs and positioning Ontario as a leader in the clean energy economy.
"In addition to securing one of the largest private capital investments in Niagara's history with the decision by Asahi Kasei to build in Niagara, we are well on the way to completing some of the largest public investments in our region, including the construction of the new $3.6-billion south Niagara hospital in Niagara Falls and plans to twin the Garden City Skyway in St. Catharines," he said.
"A local key priority in Niagara West, the new $224-million West Lincoln Memorial Hospital is scheduled for completion next year."
Oosterhoff said he will continue to advocate for the expansion of GO Transit in Niagara, including a new station in Grimsby, as well as widening the QEW from Hamilton to St. Catharines.
CAPTION: Niagara Falls MPP Wayne Gates is flanked by St. Catharines MPP Jennie Stevens and Niagara Centre MPP Jeff Burch. Gates and Burch say they plan to continue raising issues pertaining to health care, affordability and housing when the provincial legislature resumes this week after a five-month recess.Bob Tymczyszyn St. Catharines Standardfile photo

CREDIT: Ray Spiteri Reporter

Subject: Legislation; Legislatures; Community support

Location: Canada; Niagara Falls

Title: MPPs lay out their priorities for fall session at Queen's Park:   Health care, housing and affordability top issues for Niagara's New Democrat MPPs as provincial legislature resumes this week

Publication title: The Standard; St. Catharines, Ont.

First page: A.3

Publication year: 2024

Publication date: Oct 21, 2024

Document 110 of 249

Skyway twinning to be fast-tracked: 'Around the clock' construction of Garden City bridge planned

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/skyway-twinning-be-fast-tracked/docview/3117870325/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Skyway+twinning+to+be+fast-tracked%3A+%27Around+the+clock%27+construction+of+Garden+City+bridge+planned&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-10-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With an early provincial election looming, Premier Doug Ford's government wants to fast-track twinning of Garden City Skyway in St. Catharines and construction of Highway 413 and the Bradford Bypass.
Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria said legislation called the Building Highways Faster Act will be introduced after the legislature returns next week from an extended summer break to provide for "around-the-clock, 24-7 construction."
Garden City Skyway traverses the Welland Canal on a busy stretch of the Queen Elizabeth Way. Plans call for a new four-lane, 2.2-kilometre-long bridge to the north of the existing span in addition to a rehabilitation of the deck on the existing bridge.
The province issued a request for proposals this spring to five teams for the first phase of the work.
Following procurement, the design of the new bridge was expected to begin next year with construction starting in 2026, Sarkaria said during a visit to St. Catharines in April.
"This is one of the most critical corridors from an economic perspective in this province," he said in spring.
"In a year, we'll see about $1 trillion worth of goods travel over this piece of infrastructure that is behind us here today. So in order to ensure the safe travel of goods, as well as from an economical perspective, this is really needed."
Taking aim at potential protests against the two highway projects that have been heavily opposed by environmentalists and others for using up prime Greenbelt farmland and threatening endangered or rare species, the bill would set "new penalties for obstructing access for field investigations" or "damaging equipment," Sarkaria said Thursday in Caledon.
It would also "accelerate" the process for buyouts or expropriations of farmland or other properties needed for the projects and "streamline" relocations of utility lines.
There will also be legislation to establish an "accelerated environmental assessment process" for Highway 413, from Highway 401 at Milton to Highway 400 in Vaughan, allowing the province to proceed with early work on the controversial roadway rejected by a previous Liberal government.
"Projects like this need to get built," Sarkaria said, citing "unprecedented growth" in Ontario with projections the population will increase two million by 2031 and put existing 400-series highways at or over capacity within a decade.
"Gridlock suffocates our roadways."
Green MPP Aislinn Clancy (Kitchener Centre) said the proposed legislation is dangerous.
"Slashing environmental laws, paving over our Greenbelt, Ontarians have made it clear that they're not OK with this," she added in a statement, referring to the government's $8.28-billion Greenbelt land swap scandal under investigation by the RCMP.
"For the government to be trying it yet again with Highway 413 shows how they'll do anything to help their inner circle over the people of this province. We also need to build communities that don't require you to travel a great distance to get to work or school, by building more homes in existing neighbourhoods and especially around transit hubs."
The Bradford Bypass will stretch from Highway 400 in the Bradford area east to Highway 404 in a bid to ease congestion between there and Toronto.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie reiterated her opposition to Highway 413 earlier this week.
"I have never supported the 413."
However, the highway promise is credited with helping Ford's PCs win all the Brampton ridings in the 2022 provincial election, taking three seats from the New Democrats.
The premier has repeatedly refused to rule out a snap election as early as March, instead of waiting until the next provincewide vote slated for June 2026.
His government has also taken aim at bike lanes, pledging legislation that would require municipalities to get provincial approval before taking out any lanes of traffic on major roadways, and has stepped up feel-good government advertising about Ontario's economy.
The province is also planning to send cheques of at least $200 to 16 million Ontarians to offset the rising cost of living.
With files from The
Standard's Karena Walter
CAPTION: Proposed provincial legislation aims to have the Garden City Skyway twin bridge built faster than once planned by having 24-7 construction.Prabmeet Sarkaria, Ontario's minister of transportation, announces details of the Garden City Skyway twinning while in St. Catharines on April 5.Metroland file photoJulie Jocsak St. Catharines Standard file photo

Document 111 of 249

'Cyclists at greater risk': MPP says provincial officials should 'stay in their own lane'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/cyclists-at-greater-risk/docview/3117297363/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Cyclists+at+greater+risk%27%3A+MPP+says+provincial+officials+should+%27stay+in+their+own+lane%27&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-10-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When it comes to bike lanes, St. Catharines MPP Jennie Stevens says Ontario government decision-makers should "stay in their own lane."
That's because she fears new provincial legislation will undermine the plans of municipalities to add bicycle lanes to local streets as they work to promote active transportation and reduce traffic congestion.
Stevens said the province's proposed legislation, likely being considered when the legislature resumes sitting next week, will require municipalities to obtain the province's approval before bike lanes can be added to city streets if they replace any motorized vehicle lanes.
She called it another example of provincial overreach.
Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria said on Tuesday the Doug Ford government will not demand existing bike lanes be removed, but will compel local governments to pause any plans on new bike lanes that could affect vehicle traffic, and try to make the case for their existence with data.
Stevens said her office has been contacted by several cycling groups and individuals regarding the legislation and "they are worried."
"We have a lot of people within our city that are asking for this, and the province needs to really stay in their own lane," she said.
Stevens said municipalities, including St. Catharines, are making every effort to incorporate bike lanes, when safe to do so, as part of efforts to build safer and more sustainable transportation systems.
Restricting bike lanes, she added, will hinder that progress.
"During the years that I was a city councillor, we always looked into the future of making sure the city had walkable, rideable communities, and bike lanes were always at the forefront of our minds," said Stevens, who represented Merritton ward in St. Catharines for 15 years before being elected MPP in 2018.
Stevens said Hartzel Road was resurfaced with bike lanes during her time on council, and it is now "one of the safest roads for cyclists to ride on."
She said that decision was made by city council and staff, not the provincial government.
"The province's interference not only undermines local decision-making, but also puts cyclists at greater risk," Stevens added in a news release.
CAPTION: A vehicle passes by a bike lane on Vine Street in St. Catharines. Proposed provincial legislation would limit the addition of new bike lanes to municipal roads.Metroland file photo

Document 112 of 249

Economic development effort paying dividends: 'I think Niagara is being showcased in a very positive way': councillor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/economic-development-effort-paying-dividends/docview/3117295346/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Economic+development+effort+paying+dividends%3A+%27I+think+Niagara+is+being+showcased+in+a+very+positive+way%27%3A+councillor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-10-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Niagara Economic Development's continued focus on promoting the area as a prime location for business relocation and investment is paying off.
Katie Desharnais, Niagara Region's strategic marketing manager, told the municipality's planning and economic development committee last week that the targeted marketing efforts led directly to the successful relocation of international companies from Finland and China.
"I just wanted to highlight this because we don't normally see companies come through from our digital marketing efforts," Desharnais told councillors. "Our team was pretty excited to pick these up."
CBB International from Finland is a consulting firm specializing in international trade and business expansion and is incorporating in Ontario and setting up its Canadian subsidiary in Niagara, she said.
Chinese firm Vichnet, a manufacturer of cable trays and machine guards, will initially focus on warehousing before moving into manufacturing, with an estimated $5-million investment and the creation of 10 new jobs on a 1.2-hectare site.
"The level of sophistication and what I think you've done with the brand is impressive," said Pelham Coun. Diana Huson, committee chair.
"I think Niagara is being showcased in a very positive way. That was one of the motivations why I ran the first time. I think we've come a long way, and I'm proud of the team's work."
Both firms Desharnais highlighted to the commitee have plans to relocate in 2026.
She also referenced the international reaction to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's announcement on May 14 of a $1.6-billion investment by Asahi Kasei Corp. Niagara Economic Development hosted the event alongside staff from the City of Port Colborne.
The EV supply chain plant will produce advanced lithium-ion battery separators. Desharnais said it will position Niagara as a key player in Canada's growing EV supply chain.
"From our team's perspective, we think it was quite a success," Desharnais said. "We had the support of Port Colborne staff, and I have to thank them because they did a wonderful job facilitating and providing support at their location."
Desharnais said the event attracted significant international media attention, with coverage by Canadian outlets and major Japanese news organizations, and the project underscored Canada's commitment to strengthening its EV infrastructure, something emphasized by the prime minister.
Premier Doug Ford highlighted Ontario's leadership in the electric vehicle revolution, and local officials noted this investment is the largest industrial project in Port Colborne since 1914.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Ontario Premier Doug Ford in May speak about Asahi Kasei's $1.56-billion investment in Port Colborne.Metroland file photo

Readers write

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write/docview/3115312442/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-10-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Gratuitous comment
Re: Niagara Health officials who quit town committee say they were 'belittled,' 'bullied' and 'humiliated,' Oct. 7
Having once worked for a non-profit agency with a facility in Fort Erie, I have always respected Mayor Wayne Redekop as an honest and fair person and an effective politician.
In this case, his comment that people who work in the Niagara Health system are "allergic to criticism" seems gratuitous and below him.
It appears, in these often confusing and frightening times, some people have resorted to the worst tactics possible to get their point across. It is getting to the point where the people who work in agencies like Niagara Health and in government have become easy targets.
Mean-spirited and angry people now take to angry verbal abuse of anyone who disagrees with them to prove a point. This is causing people to reconsider running for public office, becoming a social worker, nurse, doctor or a police officer or paramedic.
I agree losing a hospital is unfair and frightening, but look deeper into the reasons. I think those celebrating the fact Premier Doug Ford gave up millions of dollars of revenue from auto licence fees while downloading costs for many services onto municipal governments are letting fear and anger cloud their judgment.
Using private nursing agencies to solve hospital staffing issues is just the beginning of a right-wing push toward two-tiered health care.
Where are the Ford and Pierre Poilievre signs?
Richard Murri, Niagara Falls
Protect habitat
Re: Oily substance discharging into Welland River in Niagara Falls, Oct. 3
Ray Spiteri is to be congratulated for revealing the pollution, which is impacting the water in what has come to be known as Thundering Waters Forest.
Mike Cushman's suggestion this waterway be renamed after local environmental activist Sarah Ward is a good one.
Prof. Mike Dickman and I walked the length of the damaged river a few years ago and were impressed by the considerable number of leopard frogs we encountered. The species now appears to have been killed off.
The forest as a refuge for declining wildlife species needs to be publicly acquired. It should be patrolled carefully to ensure wildlife have their habitat protected.
John Bacher, St. Catharines
Vote her out
Re: Pride flag ban again sought by Niagara Catholic trustee, Oct. 7
Could someone give Niagara Catholic school board trustee Natalia Benoit some milk and cookies? She is so starved for attention, she is again spewing divisive rhetoric of hate toward gay students. It can only be described as a pathetic need to be noticed.
If her motion to stop flying the Pride flag wasn't so serious, her behaviour would be laughable. The only way to stop politicians like her is to vote them out. Niagara-on-the-Lake Catholic board voters, it's up to you.
Do you choose love or Benoit's hate?
David Pihach, Wainfleet

Document 114 of 249

Beluga death is fourth in a year at Marineland: Ontario saying little about progress of probe into park

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/beluga-death-is-fourth-year-at-marineland/docview/3114754974/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Beluga+death+is+fourth+in+a+year+at+Marineland%3A+Ontario+saying+little+about+progress+of+probe+into+park&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-10-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Another beluga whale has died at Marineland and, four years into a provincial probe, Ontario's solicitor general is saying little about the investigation's progress.
The latest beluga death is the fourth in the past year, provincial records show. Since 2019, 16 belugas and one killer whale have died at the Niagara Falls tourist attraction, the only place in the country that still holds whales in captivity. And three out of five belugas that Marineland sold to Mystic Aquarium in Connecticut have died since being moved there in the spring of 2021.
Ontario's Animal Welfare Services, which is part of the Ministry of the Solicitor General, launched an investigation into Marineland in 2020. The next year, the province declared all marine mammals at Marineland in distress due to poor water quality and ordered the park to fix the issue - the park appealed while denying its animals were in distress, but later dropped that appeal.
The current status of the provincial probe is unclear, with little information available on action Ontario is taking, and the most recent beluga death has opposition politicians and animal welfare advocates questioning what they see as a lack of transparency from the province.
A spokesperson for Solicitor General Michael Kerzner, whose department is responsible for animal welfare across the province, said Animal Welfare Services has conducted more than 200 inspections at the park.
"As has been demonstrated in the past, Animal Welfare Services will not hesitate to issue orders or charges for animal welfare violations at Marineland," spokesperson Hunter Kell wrote in a statement.
Kerzner's office would not elaborate on what the inspections entailed, nor what orders have been issued, and to what effect.
That's not enough for the local member of provincial parliament.
"It's awful that these animals are dying at Marineland, but the public has a right to know what's going on there," said Wayne Gates, the New Democrat representative for Niagara Falls.
"We need the solicitor general to come to the table and let's have an intelligent discussion on how we're going to protect the animals."
The public is very concerned about the animals at Marineland, said John Fraser, parliamentary leader of the Ontario Liberal Party.
"The government is making claims that they're visiting frequently, but they're not telling anybody what they're doing and the whales keep dying," he said.
"What do they have to hide if they're doing their job?"
Marineland did not respond to multiple requests for comment.
The park has long maintained it treats its animals well. Its website says it has "a strong record" of providing for the welfare of its animals and "will continue to prioritize their health and well-being."
Concerning the latest death, the Ministry of the Solicitor General would only confirm that the beluga died in July. Spokesperson Brent Ross said two Magellanic penguins also died at Marineland in August.
Ross referred questions about the beluga's cause of death to Marineland, and other followup questions back to the minister's office. Neither Marineland nor the minister's office responded to the questions.
Ross also said the province's chief animal welfare inspector, Melanie Milczynski, declined to be interviewed. Milczynski is the fourth chief animal welfare inspector since Animal Welfare Services began operating in late 2019. Successive chiefs have declined multiple interview requests.
Efforts to learn more about Marineland through the Freedom-of-Information process have also hit multiple roadblocks.
In February 2023, The Canadian Press filed a request with the government seeking access to inspection reports and orders issued to Marineland, plus a copy of a 65-page government-commissioned report on the state of water at the park.
Two weeks later, the request was denied in full, citing exemptions that said the information could interfere with law enforcement and was subject to solicitor-client privilege. A third exemption said releasing the records would be an "unjustified invasion of personal privacy."
The Canadian Press appealed the case to the Information and Privacy Commissioner of Ontario. In September of last year, the government agreed to provide some of the information. Marineland appealed the decision, which meant that the records were not released.
In May, the Freedom-of-Information office agreed to release more than 500 pages of records. Among those were supposed to be copies of 28 orders issued to Marineland and 12 government reports about the park. Marineland again appealed.
Mediation in the case has failed and it will move to adjudication. It could be months or longer before a resolution allows the documents to be made public.
Marineland's owner, Marie Holer, died last month. At the time, the park said a succession plan had been put in place, but did not offer details.
The park is up for sale, and has been listed for more than a year. The vast property constitutes some 1,000 acres just a kilometre from Horseshoe Falls and the heart of the tourist district in Niagara Falls.
Marineland has not said what it will do with the animals once the park is sold.
This year, it opened to visitors for just two months, unlike its usual run from the May long weekend until Thanksgiving. There were no rides, and the vast majority of the animals were not on display.
A Canadian Press reporter and photographer visited Marineland in the summer of 2023, and staff said there were 37 beluga whales in the park. Shortly after that visit, the park banned the reporter from its property.
Drone footage shot by advocacy group UrgentSeas, co-founded by a former Marineland whale trainer, indicated 33 belugas at the park in mid-September. The group said more needs to be done to investigate animal deaths at the park.
"Marineland has normalized dead whales," said the group's co-founder Phil Demers, who has become an outspoken critic of the park.
"What concerns me is when you normalize things like this, people become apathetic, and the government is guilty of that, too."
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Since 2019, 16 belugas and one killer whale have died at Marineland amusement park in Niagara Falls.Chris Young The Canadian Press file photo

CREDIT: Liam Casey The Canadian Press

Readers write

Full text: Speed cameras needed
Re: A Niagara Falls driving distraction: Despite safety measures, Lundy's Lane remains 'tricky street,' Sept. 20
The need for better traffic control in Niagara is obvious. The police don't do enough when they're on patrol.
In Fort Erie we need more speed cameras, not less.
We have Highway 3 going through town and it is always dangerous, so anything that slows down the traffic is welcome.
Premier Doug Ford's Conservative government is raising speed limits on the 400-series highways. With the volume of traffic on those roads the move is not safe. It's easier to access alcohol which is also cheaper. Combined with faster roads, this is a formula for tragedy.
Will Ontario Transportation Minister Prabmeet Sarkaria be held accountable if there's a spike in traffic fatalities? I never feel safe on Ontario's highways and byways.
Repaving Highway 406 so vehicles can go faster seems good for the booming body shop business. And the crazy thing is these irresponsible people just won a byelection in the Bay of Quinte.
Health care is in disrepair, traffic deaths are hard to keep track of and they're closing safe consumption sites. What's next?
Russ Young, Fort Erie
Powerful storms
Re: Hurricane Milton is growing stronger, Oct. 6
Hurricanes developing in the Gulf of Mexico and the Atlantic Ocean are becoming very powerful because of the high temperatures of the ocean water that feeds them.
The future does not appear good unless scientists have a way to bring down water temperatures.
Roscoe Reilly, Welland
Subsidizing housing development through incentives
Re: Niagara's regional councillors compromise on housing incentive time frame, Oct. 2
Niagara Region council continues to debate whether we local taxpayers should pay a $16.2-million expenditure that could entail an estimated eight per cent tax increase.
This would pay for an extension of an expiring housing development incentive program.
Can taxpayers afford to subsidize luxury housing for the rich?
Pelham Coun. Diana Huson pointed out "the Region has limited resources and a responsibility to be accountable and fiscally responsible to taxpayers," according to the article written by Bill Sawchuk.
Huson quoted statistics from Canada Mortgage and Housing Corp. showing the number of rental units decreased in Niagara Region from 2020 to 2023, when the incentive programs were operating.
In her words: "If the incentives are going toward condos and single-home development, that is not where we need to build on the housing spectrum - we need a variety of all kinds of units, and we're not being strategic about where our focus is."
Perhaps some aspects of these incentive programs could be useful to the ordinary taxpayer, if and when they receive a major rewrite. Encourage your elected regional representatives to pay attention to the regional staff report which recommended against renewing this program package.
Joan Worthington, St. Catharines

Document 116 of 249

NDP leader promises full-service hospital with 24/7 ER in Welland: Marit Stiles also vowed to retain urgent care centres in Port Colborne, Fort Erie if elected

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ndp-leader-promises-full-service-hospital-with-24/docview/3114392426/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=NDP+leader+promises+full-service+hospital+with+24%2F7+ER+in+Welland%3A+Marit+Stiles+also+vowed+to+retain+urgent+care+centres+in+Port+Colborne%2C+Fort+Erie+if+elected&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-10-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A provincial NDP government would build a new full-service hospital in Welland and return services to Port Colborne and Fort Erie hospitals, says party Leader Marit Stiles.
"We're going to build a full-service Welland hospital with 24/7 emergency rooms, around the clock surgical care," Stiles told reporters outside Welland hospital Monday while announcing Niagara aspects of the party's election platform.
"And we're going to also restore services to the Niagara urgent care centres in Port Colborne and Fort Erie," she said.
The next provincial election is tentatively set for June 2026, but there have been numerous reports to suggest an election will be called much sooner.
The NDP's plans are in stark contrast to plans for the hospital sites backed by the Conservative government, which include closing both Fort Erie and Port Colborne hospitals when the new south Niagara Falls hospital is complete in 2028.
Although Niagara Health plans to build a new hospital in Welland, it would be limited to emergency services and outpatient programs - rather than a full emergency department and acute care services, including surgeries.
Stiles, who was in the Rose City to attend the Niagara Centre NDP provincial riding association nomination meeting Monday evening, said "the people of Niagara deserve health care they can rely on."
"That means care close to home, hospitals that are fully staffed, emergency rooms that are open, urgent care centres (UCC) that have the staff and the resources you need, and it is your government's basic responsibility to ensure that you, the people of Niagara - the people of Welland, Port Colborne, Fort Erie - have the health care you need," she said.
"But an NDP government is going to make sure you have it."
Referring to the premier's plans for Ontario Place and to dig underneath Highway 401, she said "Doug Ford is too busy building luxury spas and tunnels to nowhere in the GTA to focus on the needs of people in Niagara."
She said Niagara's New Democrat MPPs - Jeff Burch in Niagara Centre, Jennie Stevens in St. Catharines, Wayne Gates in Niagara Falls - are "people that fight for the communities, and know that government matters, politics matter and that you matter."
"They know that Niagara is growing, that people from all across our province and sometimes even from all around the world are coming to this region to build a life, to build a brighter future," Stiles said. "The region is home to good jobs, to a strong economy and deserves a government that invests in public health care, that is there when and where you need it. We need more services, not less."
Burch said Welland's hospital is "coming to the end of its days and needs to be replaced," but the new hospital should be more than the "ambulatory site" proposed.
"We're saying very clearly ... we are going to build a full-service hospital, and we're not going to leave the people of Port Colborne and Fort Erie without urgent care," Burch said.
Stiles said reducing access to health-care services at a time when Niagara's population is growing "is outrageous."
While reduced surgical services in Welland as well as the overnight closure of UCCs have been blamed on a shortage of medical staff, Stiles said addressing the shortages is also a priority for the NDP.
She said that includes the shortage of family doctors and primary care providers.
"This is a question of priorities for this government. We know there are now 2.7 million Ontarians without a family doctor or access to primary care. This has to be addressed urgently."
She said medical schools and physician training opportunities need to be dramatically increased, while more needs to be done to fast track the licensing of foreign-trained physicians and medical professionals.
"We know there are people qualified to provide that care in our communities right now," Stiles said. "The fact that the Conservatives and the Liberals before them failed to address that is just terrible. We know we can begin to address the crisis right now with people who are already here."
While she said current and previous governments have not been willing to invest, "how can we afford not to?"
Stiles' announcement was welcomed by Heather Kelley, who has been lobbying against the planned closure of the UCC in Fort Erie.
"It's lovely that this would be a consideration for the NDP," Kelley said. "Hopefully, things pan out that way."
Even if they don't, she said, other political parties should be paying attention to the concerns of residents of Ontario's rural communities.
"I think they have to," she said. "I think the small group as it is right now have a big voice and they're using it, finally ... We deserve health care as much as anyone else."
Hannah Jensen, spokesperson for Health Minister Sylvia Jones, in an email said "while the NDP continues to vote against our government's record investments in Niagara, our government will continue to take action to connect families in the Niagara region for years to come."
Jensen said the Tory government has increased health-care spending by 31 per cent since 2018, investing more than $85 billion into the system this year alone.
"In Niagara, our government is getting shovels in the ground for two new hospitals, the redevelopment of West Lincoln Memorial Hospital in Grimsby and the construction of the Niagara South Hospital in Niagara Falls, which will add a combined 600 new beds to the region, increasing capacity and improving access to critical services in the community," Jensen said.
"This is in addition to the over $2 million our government has invested in the Niagara region to connect 7,600 more people to primary care and the over $2.3 million to ensure children and youth in the Niagara region have the care they need, when they need it, right in their community."
CAPTION: Marit Stiles, leader of the provincial NDP, with Niagara Centre MPP Jeff Burch, visited Welland on Monday to announce the party's plans for hospital services in the region if elected to form the next government.Julie Jocsak St. Catharines Standard

CREDIT: Allan Benner Reporter

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-10-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: An argument
for change
Proposed pay hike for
St. Catharines city councillors calculated to cost $118,577
per year, Oct. 1
The proposal suggests each St. Catharines city councillor should be paid an additional $9,000 annually, a 32 per cent increase from $27,765 to $37,000.
No politician should have the ability to vote themselves a raise. This proposal should be deferred to the next council or, better yet, should be a referendum question for the electorate to answer during the next municipal election.
While I appreciate the time local councillors put in to perform this role, each councillor went into this role knowing the time they would need to perform their duties and how they would be remunerated. When did running for a municipal political position become more about self-service than civil service?
This proposed pay increase comes on the heels of the largest property tax increase in the city's history. Council also approved a new program that acknowledges property taxes are too high and allows seniors to use the equity in their homes to pay unsustainable property tax increases.
A vote in favour of a pay raise like this demonstrates how out of touch local politicians are with the plight of their own citizens.
Several years ago, Welland councillors gave themselves a 47 per cent pay increase while citizens and businesses were struggling though the pandemic.
Welland did it again and approved an additional 2.5 per cent increase in the past year.
It also happened in Thorold where the mayor and councillors got a raise in 2021.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford has previously noted that 126 politicians across Niagara is far too many and now citizens are watching these local politicians vote themselves big pay raises. Our governance system is broken and flawed in Niagara and local politicians are paralyzed to do anything about it.
Allen McKay, St. Catharines
Homeless
emergency
Homelessness issues 'conservatively' cost Niagara Falls $529,000 in 2023, Oct. 1
With the disclosure homelessness is costing huge sums of money to so many cities across Canada - one of the most blatant symptoms of a mismanaged society, along with its pathetic twin, fatal drug overdoses - should it not be clear to every last one of us that a state of emergency should be put into force and a solution come up with immediately and practically? And not by setting up some useless committee to look into these debacles, which seems to be the normal recourse of useless governments in disguising movement as action.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau should cease sending taxpayer money to Ukraine and Third World countries and apply it here in Canada where it is so desperately needed. Anything less, in my opinion, is a shirking of responsibility and should result in the ousting of his government.
Wayne Borbey, Niagara Falls

Readers write

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write/docview/3112397957/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-10-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A tale of
misdirection
Someone should tell Doug Ford this isn't 2012 and he isn't mayor of Toronto, Sept. 26
What is Ontario Premier Doug Ford trying to divert our attention from with his new folly?
First he recommended building a major highway through wetlands north of Toronto. Now he wants to build a super tunnel. It can't be to get MPPs to Queen's Park quicker because the legislature does not sit frequently enough to make that a priority.
An initial cost estimate is $60 billion, but as bridge builders in St. Catharines know, the final number will definitely be higher. Given the time it would take to build, expect a final cost to be triple or more of initial estimates. Oh, and by the way, it will not be just the City of Toronto that will pay for it. The money for this extravaganza comes out of your pocket.
This begs the question of whether the money could be better used elsewhere. I am thinking of things like shoring up our health system, which is in obvious distress, or improving schools, which are in need of major refurbishment, or putting money into our shameful long-term-care system.
That amount of money could make significant improvements to public transit across the province.
But Ford only cares for roads, and I doubt he even consulted his professional advisers regarding the tunnel.
It will never be built. So what is he trying to accomplish with this misdirection?
Maybe he is trying to give the appearance of being about to boost the economy with infrastructure projects given the Ontario economy is slowing and an election is near?
Or maybe his real goal is to finally get that highway built through wetlands when the tunnel idea is abandoned.
Bait and switch?
Bob Koruna, St. Catharines
A 1950s vision
Should Doug Ford buy the 407 instead of digging a tunnel to ease gridlock?, Sept. 27
With his typical penchant for tunnel vision, Premier Doug Ford has hatched a preposterous scheme to dig us out of some of Ontario's transportation woes.
He calls his antique 1950s approach to problem-solving visionary; delusional and embarrassing are more apt descriptors.
Douglas Melville,
St. Catharines
Following
the parade
Pair of parades highlight Grape and Wine Festival's supersized weekend in
St. Catharines, Sept. 29
Only in Niagara can you drive home behind a giant wine barrel.
We were returning home in the west end of the city and got behind one of the floats for the annual Grape and Wine Festival parade.
While we could have passed the float it was more fun to travel behind it and watch the surprised looks on peoples' faces.
Laurie Carter, St. Catharines

Document 119 of 249

Is Niagara ready for more casinos?: More competition will help grow the market, mayor says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/is-niagara-ready-more-casinos/docview/3111858015/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Is+Niagara+ready+for+more+casinos%3F%3A+More+competition+will+help+grow+the+market%2C+mayor+says&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-10-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It appears Niagara Falls' call to open the city's casino market up to competition has caught the attention of Queen's Park.
"We've been talking about this through multiple governments, multiple ministers," said Mayor Jim Diodati. "The good news is, finally, someone's listening.
"We've got 10 studies over 15 years requesting that we open up gaming and make it a competitive environment - not a monopoly. We even sued the OLG (Ontario Lottery and Gaming Corp.) a number of years ago over the last decision to not open it up. The outcome, we ended up getting a $130-million theatre. We were upset because monopolies don't serve the public - you need competition, it keeps everybody sharp."
Currently, Mohegan operates as the service provider for Niagara Fallsview Casino Resort and Casino Niagara on OLG's behalf.
That deal, signed six years ago, expires in 2040. Prior to that, the two casinos were operated by OLG, which manages lotteries and casinos in Ontario.
Since the mid-1990s, OLG has provided more than $200 million to the City of Niagara Falls for hosting two casinos.
An article last week, by online news publication The Trillium focused on the latest developments at Queen's Park, reported Premier Doug Ford's government is trying to change the deal dictating how Niagara Falls casinos are run and potentially encourage more casinos to open in the city.
Based on government records and a well-placed source, The Trillium reported one of the province's intentions is to help attract more tourists to Niagara Falls and the wider region.
The publication said the source was promised anonymity because the individual wasn't permitted to discuss what, until now, have been behind-the-scenes negotiations.
Information from the source is supported by various government records received in response to freedom of information requests by The Trillium.
Calendars of several senior Ford officials indicate the existing Niagara Falls casino-operations deal has been a topic of interest within the premier's office for more than the past year and a half, the publication reported.
In May, three separate "negotiations update(s)" about a "Niagara Destination Strategy" were prepared for the deputy finance minister's office, the ministry disclosed in response to a freedom of information request by The Trillium.
A spokesperson in the office of the premier did not respond to questions emailed by the Niagara Falls Review.
Anthony Vukojevic, a spokesperson for the Ministry of Tourism, Culture and Gaming and Ministry of Sport, said Niagara's $1.8-billion tourism industry plays a "key role" in creating jobs and supporting the local economy.
"Our government is considering several initiatives under a larger destination strategy aimed at unlocking the full potential of the Niagara region," he said in an emailed statement to the Review, which didn't address specific questions.
"Niagara is a major destination for global travellers, and this strategy will elevate it to the next level, strengthening Ontario's global reputation as a top-tier tourist destination."
Niagara Casinos referred questions to OLG.
OLG spokesperson Tony Bitonti said Niagara Falls' casinos are important to the success of OLG's land-based casino business.
"To respect commercial confidentiality, OLG does not comment on any discussions with any of its casino service providers," he said in an emailed statement.
But Diodati said he's happy the province is looking at the current model in Niagara Falls.
"We've got
some good opportunities if we do this right. We've got great insight from some gaming consultants from around the world who have made suggestions on there's ways to do it better and that's what we're suggesting to the government."
And it all starts with a competitive environment, said Diodati.
"We've got two casinos, but they're operated by the same group, so it's not competition. Even in the past, we said when you re-tender the casino operation, have the two casinos separated and competing with two different operators. Finally, after all these years of multiple resolutions, multiple discussions, we've finally got a government that's willing to listen to what we're asking for and to investigate it."
He said his comments are "no slight" to Mohegan officials.
"They're wonderful people and they're nice operators and we have a great relationship with them. This pre-dates Mohegan, this goes long before they ever came to the table," said Diodati.
Asked if he supports more casinos in Niagara Falls, Diodati said that would depend on what market studies suggest.
"The most important thing above all is competition," he said, adding the gaming world has changed since Casino Niagara and Niagara Fallsview Casino Resort opened in 1996 and 2004, respectively.
"There's a lot more competition out there than in the beginning. Fallsview Casino was the most successful casino in North America at the time. Casino revenues are significantly declining in Ontario. Online gaming is another factor that wasn't present when they first contemplated gaming."
Diodati said there should be a "completely new model and new regulations."
"With competition, that incentivizes the casino operator to reinvest in the product, because if you don't the product gets old. Our gem, the jewel in our gaming crown, is Fallsview Casino and it's almost 20 years old already. Casino Niagara is going on 30 years," he said.
"Yes, you can paint it and fix it up, but when you go to Las Vegas, you see what they do and it's a significantly different model."
While the city has seen hotel applications come before council, Diodati said there hasn't had a new hotel built in 17 years.
"Our inventory is getting stagnant and it's getting stale, so we could use some upgrades and some additions," he said, pointing to how opening gaming up to competition has benefited other jurisdictions around the world.
"Nashville has built more hotels in the last 10 years than we have ever. We've got the falls, one of the great natural wonders of the world - no excuse for our tourism to stagnate."
Diodati said Niagara Falls is missing the "nicer, high-end boutique" hotel market.
"Those kinds of people end up staying in Toronto and day trip to Niagara Falls. We want them to stay here and then day trip to Toronto."
Diodati said more competition will help grow the market, so the "high tide raises all boats."
"The pizza guy will make better money, the chambermaids will get more money, the T-shirt people will make more money, the operators, the hotels, the attractions - everybody benefits," he said.
With files from John Law
CAPTION: Nearly 30 years after Casino Niagara opened, the province is looking attract more tourists to Niagara Falls.metroland file photo

CREDIT: Ray Spiteri Reporter With files from John Law

Subject: Casinos; Monopolies; Tourism; Freedom of information; Hotels & motels

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-10-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Welland
tax deferral
Eligible St. Catharines seniors can now defer year-over-year city property tax increases, Sept. 27
St. Catharines has just instituted a tax deferral program for low-income seniors to help them remain in their homes and not be forced out by rising taxes.
Why can't Welland do the same?
There are a lot of seniors in Welland who moved here to enjoy the final stages of their lives, including us, and the taxes have risen considerably since we moved here 10 years ago.
My tax bill is by far my biggest monthly expense, way over what any of my utility bills or other carrying charges are.
Some kind of tax relief would be very welcome.
Richard Lilley, Welland
Ford suffering from Trumpian fantasies?
Doug Ford announces proposal for tunnel under Highway 401 to carry more traffic, Sept. 27
I'm starting to think Ontario Premier Doug Ford has Donald Trump fantasies.
So many of his ideas and plans make me think that he's secretly a fan.
A tunnel under Highway 401 from Brampton to Toronto? There's a great way to spend a few trillion dollars. Leave the digging to the gophers!
Allowing booze sales in every rinky-dink corner store in the province? There are areas of our province where this is worthwhile, like up north.
But does anyone believe that this won't cause more work for the police, everything from impaired driving, assaults - particularly domestic - and many more?
A highway through fertile farmland to save some drivers 20 minutes of driving time?
And then there was the plan to sell off some of the best parts of the Greenbelt.

Mr. Ford gained his throne due to the catastrophic downfall of his predecessor Kathleen Wynne. His prior experience was service for one term as a Toronto city councillor. Perhaps when Mr. Trump slinks back to Florida in defeat, our premier will settle down and find some common sense.
Pete Morro, St. Catharines
Gun control
badly needed
Societal illness, Letters, Sept. 20
Of course guns kill people.
Grandpa's shotgun, once used to put meat on the table, is now a potential suicide tool. There's the Quebec mosque shooting, the Polytechnique Montréal shooting and much more.
If a gun is not readily available, then there will be fewer murders or suicides.
And guns are being smuggled into Canada from the gun-crazy United States. We need mandatory minimum sentences of 10 years per gun.
Do Americans not realize how unhealthy their lust for guns is? From mass shootings in schools to crazy people trying to kill the Republican presidential candidate.
More gun control is long overdue.
Russ Young, Fort Erie
