Document 121 of 249

Readers write

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write/docview/3111421873/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-10-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Brock bravery
As Brock University
celebrates 60 years, a look back at the Niagara women and union workers who played a role in making 'something huge out of just a glimmer of an idea,' Sept. 20
As someone who benefitted from Brock University during its first two decades, I can offer perspectives of the heroic nature of its founders.
While a student at Brock, I was blessed to have as a role model the brave professor Dr. Robert Hoover.
What I found encouraging was his remarkable testimony to the Ontario Municipal Board, exposing the lack of need for urban sprawl on our unique fruit lands was supported by Brock's founding president, Prof. James Gibson.
During his OMB testimony, Hoover was pilloried in a horrific way. Accused of having a "Messiah complex." At one point, he expressed physical pain - the hearing was interrupted to allow for a cardiogram.
Concerned to give Hoover moral support after his ordeal, I visited him at his home. During my visit, I was pleasantly surprised that Gibson joined us to offer support.
Hoover later told me Gibson, when he was appointed, expressed hope his skill as a land-use planner would help protect the rare fruit-growing lands of Niagara. This prediction came true and as a result, these lands with their excellent agricultural microclimate are protected by the Greenbelt legislation.
It's to be hoped in future when we face critical threats of climate change and loss of biodiversity, Brock will live up to the legacy of its founders. There is need to speak truth to power, as Gibson properly encouraged his faculty to undertake.
John Bacher, St. Catharines
Ford's fumbles
This Ford only goes in reverse, Sept. 12
How is it Ontario Premier Doug Ford can bumble and stumble into every Ontario institution the public holds dear without consequence?
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau is being burned at the stake by media and pierced with childish rhymes like "Axe the Tax" by Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre.
Our health-care system is being destroyed by Ford, while hospital staffs suffer.
William Thomas wrote a humour column on Ford, bringing to light an issue that is not funny: permits for penned hunting. This cruel non-sport is alive in Ontario because of Ford's government.
Then there's Highway 413, which will destroy wetlands, threatening endangered species to shave minutes off people's commutes. Precious farmland will be lost at a time when food is so important.
Ford also has no shame in spending at least $223 million to seal a quicker Beer Store deal.
Remember, if it's convenient to buy booze at the corner store, do not take your empties because you still have to take them to the Beer Store. Convenient?
Even if you are not a Trudeau fan, it's apparent Ford's worse. Let's switch it up and concentrate on who is doing what.
Faye Suthons, Wainfleet

Document 122 of 249

After 40 years at the Star, it's finally time to say goodbye

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/after-40-years-at-star-finally-time-say-goodbye/docview/3111069195/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=After+40+years+at+the+Star%2C+it%27s+finally+time+to+say+goodbye&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-09-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: I remember when I was lying in bed as a child, listening to the radio and hearing the moving song, "Thanks for the Memories," sung by the late comedian Bob Hope.
Today, I'm feeling much deeper nostalgia for the remarkable 40 years of my lifetime career working for the Toronto Star.
Now, I'm retiring.
It's been fulsome and fantastic years of engaging with Toronto Star readers. It became a mutual relationship when I wrote my advice column for the last 23 of those years. Oddly enough, it was because the famous American-based advice columnist, Ann Landers, had died.
I'd read every column of hers in the Star since I was an early teen, fascinated by her readers' personal stories and Landers' insights.
After she passed on, I called the Star's then-publisher, John Honderich, and asked him, "What about a made-in-Canada relationship adviser?" He said "Lunch!" And the deal was done. I called it, simply, "Ellie." It was syndicated across Canada.
Honderich immediately launched a $1-million campaign to promote my new column. Billboards blasted my face on the sides and back of every bus in Toronto (like past billboard ads for the American TV Series "Sex in the City.")
I became the Star's relationship advice columnist, answering thousands of letters and emails over the years. I'm grateful for the loyalty and trust of every reader who sought my advice, even those who sometimes disagreed. I was learning all sides of people's personal situations.
So how did I land in the field of professional journalism?
Back when I was a young mother with two school-age children, I wrote freelance articles at home, sending them to magazines and newspapers. To my surprise, all were published.
Next came an unexpected phone call from the Star's then-managing editor, Mark Harrison, who invited me to join the newspaper staff as a reporter. It was the chance of a lifetime. I became a staff reporter writing daily news. Next, the Family Section editors put me in charge of the Food, Fashion and Home sections.
With the fashion season in full bloom, I was sent to cover the Paris "showings." While interviewing Karl Lagerfeld, the creative director for Chanel, about his latest fashion coup presenting "the maxi skirt," he called over an employee to deliver my skirt size.
I carefully said, "It's lovely." He said, "It's yours." I said, "I can't take it." He tried once more to give me the skirt, but I refused. That's when Lagerfeld, smiling, said, "The New York Times doesn't take freebies either."
It was a proud moment for me.
But Paris' excess was in full mode at the following after-party. The smoke-filled room boasted fashion models and celebrities alike. Suddenly, a woman pulled me to her lap. It was Iman, the Somalian-born beauty, married to the late singer-songwriter David Bowie for 24 years. We chatted briefly and I left.
During that same time, the Star boasted some fearless female reporters including the late, sadly missed Christie Blatchford, plus the remarkable Rosie DiManno, whose hard-hitting truths are still regularly delivered by her on page two, with the intellectual force of a wakeup punch!
Then I proudly became the Star's first female Sunday Editor. It was a big title for a very popular weekend paper. I remained as the editor throughout the astonishing baseball World Series of both 1992 and 1993!
And those were only the early years of my working for the Toronto Star.
I was next assigned to the Star's investigative unit, writing feature stories ... and everything changed.
I consider my biggest, most significant story was about the Dionne quintuplets and how I became involved with one of Canada's most fascinating yet shocking tales.
They were the first five identical newborn girls, hailed as worldwide wonders ever to survive beyond a few days, especially as they were so tiny, and then exploited for years. Ontario's then-Liberal government recognized the babies' enormous political and economic value. They were placed under guardianship of four provincial appointees with three acts granting control until the quintuplets were age 21.
All other power grabs and exploitations soon became highly possible, including a specially built "tourist attraction" named Quintland, where the very young girls were displayed like caged animals in a zoo. Money from countless visiting American and world-travelling tourists poured into the government's "guardians," but not to the Quintuplets themselves.
Years later, I followed closely the pursuit by the surviving three adult quintuplets (Cecile, Annette and Yvonne, after the earlier deaths of Emilie and Marie), for fair compensation from their trust funds.
Extravagant expenses and dubious billing practices had bled the women's trusts by half. Finally, the three sisters, urged on by Cecile's son Bertrand, connected with a Montreal public relations adviser, who told me, "I was meeting a part of Canadian history."
I felt the same way.
I wrote daily Toronto Star accounts about the women's determined efforts to receive fair compensation from the province. An avalanche of 6,000 faxes and letters of support arrived at my newsroom desk.
Readers expressed their great empathy for the women and outrage over years of their being exploited.
I packed boxes with the many letters delivered to me, got help carrying them to Premier Mike Harris' office and handed them over to him.
Finally, after negotiations between two high-powered Toronto lawyers, the women received a total of $4 million in compensation. It was a joyous highlight of my time at the Star.
Soon after, some remarkably big investigative stories followed with journalists pursuing them, across the globe.
Luckily, I'd found access through a Toronto woman to the story of Raoul Wallenberg, a Swedish diplomat based in Budapest, Hungary, during the Second World War amid Nazi terror. At the neutral Swedish legation, he was implored for help by Jewish residents living in Hungary and hiding for their lives. Wallenberg designed a self-styled "protection passport" providing Swedish immigration permits which he handed out.
He personally sheltered people for safety in his own apartment, including a baby born there (maternity hospitals were closed to Jews). He'd protected the delivering mother by remaining outside his own door, holding a gun.
He named the baby Yvonne after one of his relatives and considered her his goddaughter. She grew up to attend a ceremony years later in 1997, along with Her Majesty Queen Elizabeth II of England. I accompanied Yvonne Singer to the unveiling of the Wallenberg Monument by the Queen, at Western Marble Arch Synagogue. It honoured Wallenberg's selfless bravery.
Meanwhile, my Toronto Star work-related connections were expanding. A Canadian-based TV show called "Outlaw In-Laws," started in 2007, hired me to be "the talent," guiding warring in-laws towards some mutual understanding.
It was long gruelling work, travelling across Ontario in the middle of winter with a TV crew. But it was rewarding, fun and a new adventure for me. And throughout, those TV scripts were a basis for helping families heal their differences.
Several years ago, the #MeToo period began, with people of all ages and backgrounds speaking out about having experienced sexual abuse. I asked readers to send me, anonymously, their own stories. Most were heartbreaking.
Older women who'd been raped when young, said they'd never fully recovered from it. One man disclosed when, as a boy, he'd been tied to a tree and abused by older boys. Their letters committed me firmly to my work.
Now, years later and having reached retirement, I'm delighted that my daughter Lisi has also chosen to do the important work of offering relationship advice ... in the tradition of the daughters of Ann Landers and her twin sister, Dear Abby.
Thanks to all my readers for sharing these memories with me. I loved the varied jobs and learned enormously from special colleagues who'd accepted a "newbie."
Thanks especially for the past 26 years since I met and married a colleague there and, while loving the newspaper, I've loved him too.
To work at something deeply meaningful is a gift.
I hope all who've read this, have also achieved personal satisfaction in life. And that you still find and maintain it, whenever possible.
CAPTION: The Dionne siblings,five identical newborn girls, were the first quintuplet to survive beyond a few days. Hailed as worldwide wonders, the children were exploitedfor years.Ellie Tesher holds some of the hundreds of letters that arrived at the Star in support of the Dionne quintuplets, a story she worked on and considers the biggest of her career. Ellie's daughter Lisi has chosen to continue the advice column.EI Scan photo by Fred Davis from Helen DafoeSteve RussellToronto star file photo

Document 123 of 249

Loblaw no name store addresses bottom line: 'Hard discount' grocery store opens on Ontario Street in St. Catharines

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/loblaw-no-name-store-addresses-bottom-line/docview/3110234384/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Loblaw+no+name+store+addresses+bottom+line%3A+%27Hard+discount%27+grocery+store+opens+on+Ontario+Street+in+St.+Catharines&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-09-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Loblaw Companies Ltd., which already operates Zehrs and No Frills stores in Niagara region, on Thursday increased options for grocery shoppers by opening a "hard discount" no name store in St. Catharines.
The store name, no name, is exactly that: no name without capitals.
The St. Catharines location is the second to open in Canada and offers a limited selection of products costing as much as 20 per cent less than other discount grocers in the area.
But what exactly is "hard discount" and what does the new location on Ontario Street offer that sets it apart from the competition?
We went straight to the source - Loblaw hard discount division president Melanie Singh - to find out.
Following are her emailed responses to questions the Niagara Dailies asked ahead of Thursday's opening.
Niagara Dailies National grocery chains in Canada have been slammed for skyrocketing prices since the pandemic. To what extent has that perception among consumers sowed the seeds for the creation of no name?
Melanie Singh Since food inflation took off globally, we have been laser-focused on doing what we can to keep prices lower for customers, including opening more discount food locations in more parts of the country.
This new format pilot is in response to feedback from some customers who continue to struggle with the increased cost of living - it's a completely different shopping experience that allows us to pass on our savings as the lowest possible prices.
Will no name stores follow the lead of No Frills and offer price matching on items available in the same market?
No. Our commitment is to offer everyday low prices on the products in store, so at this point we will not be price matching.
What went in to deciding what products these stores would - and would not - stock?
Each store will carry around 1,300 SKUs (stock keeping units), offering frozen meals and proteins, pantry staples and household necessities. We also have shelf-stable bakery and produce items like bread, bagels, apples, bananas, peppers and carrots. To provide customers with what they need most during grocery shops, we've intentionally selected our bestselling categories and items in terms of flavour or format. This way, we can offer affordable essentials that are in high demand.
Any worries no name will take away business from Loblaw stores such as Zehrs and No Frills in the St. Catharines market?
No, all our formats provide a different shopping experience for customers. The no name store is focused on providing pantry staples and other essentials at the lowest price possible.
What is it about the St. Catharines market that placed it so high on your radar when the time came to select locations?
When selecting locations, we were looking for smaller urban markets and, wherever possible, areas in those cities that have less access to typical grocery chains.
We're excited to bring this new format to St. Catharines and deeper discounts to customers.
Can consumers collect PC Optimum points shopping at no name?
Customers can collect PC Optimum points when using a PC MasterCard or the PC Money debit card. Additionally, customers can redeem PC Optimum points at this store.
Will there be self-serve checkouts or does the absence of self-checkouts lower revenue lost from theft?
We have three self checkouts at this store.
Bernd.Franke
@niagaradailies.com
CAPTION: Loblaw Companies Ltd. hard discount division president Melanie Singh, centre, standswith managers Kelvin Schneider, left, and Jesse Bentolila as the new "no name" pilot store on Ontario Street in St. Catharines opened Thursday.Bob Tymczyszyn St. Catharines Standard

CREDIT: Bernd Franke Regional Sports Editor

Full text: Poilievre must condemn
harassment
The scary truth behind this viral video of Jagmeet Singh, Opinion, Sept. 20

As Brock University
celebrates 60 years, a look back at the Niagara women and union workers who played a role, Sept. 20

I am a proud and happy man.

Ford right to close injection sites
Doug Ford says safe injection sites are a 'failed policy', Aug. 21

If a loved one is an addict, treatment should be the No. 1 priority, not a lifetime of taxpayer-funded drugs and misery.
That is not a success story by any stretch of the imagination. Consumption sites are part of the problem, not the solution. B.C. learned its lesson. Let's prioritize treatment.
Brook Blanchfield,
Niagara Falls

Document 125 of 249

Jungbunzlauer investing $200M in new plant: Three hundred construction jobs expected and 50 new jobs planned

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/jungbunzlauer-investing-200m-new-plant/docview/3109025597/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Jungbunzlauer+investing+%24200M+in+new+plant%3A+Three+hundred+construction+jobs+expected+and+50+new+jobs+planned&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-09-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A $200-million investment to build Canada's first xanthan gum facility in Port Colborne will create 50 permanent high-paying jobs, as well as 300 construction jobs at the Jungbunzlauer plant.
The new facility will be built over two years in the southwest corner of the company's property at 1555 Elm St. and will employ 50 people in high-tech jobs once open.
Those employees represent a 30 per cent increase in the 165-person workforce at the Jungbunzlauer (JBL) installation.
It's part of a continuing investment in the lakeside city, said JBL chief executive officer Bruno Tremblay during a groundbreaking ceremony Tuesday.
The event was attended by Premier Doug Ford, Vic Fedeli, Minister of Economic Development, Job Creation and Trade, Sam Oosterhoff, Niagara West MPP and Associate Minister of Energy and Intensive Industries, Port Colborne Mayor Bill Steele, and site manager Ryan Doell.
"Over the last 20 years, we have invested more than a half billion into this site," said Tremblay, of the Port Colborne location.
He said the company, through jobs, services and local purchases from its facility, adds approximately $20 million to the city's economy annually.
Founded in 1867, Switzerland-based JBL specializes in citric acid, xanthan gum, gluconates, lactics, special salts and sweeteners for the food, beverage, pharmaceutical and cosmetic industry, plus other industrial applications. It operates manufacturing plants in Austria, France and Germany.
The Port Colborne facility was built in 2002 and has a wet milling plant for corn that produces raw material for fermentation.
Eight years ago, JBL purchased Ingredion, formerly Casco Inc., on Invertose Drive. Ingredion (and Casco) was the company's prime glucose supplier.
Tremblay said xanthan gum is critical as a thickening and stabilizing agent and is used in foods and beverages such as ice cream, desserts, instant food, sauces, dressings, and sports and energy drinks.
Using natural ingredients, it's also used in pharmaceutical, personal and home care products, such as deodorants, hair care, colour cosmetics and more.
"We spent 20 years training our current employees to be highly skilled in the specialization of fermentation. We'll train the 50 new employees for two years to get them to the right level of ability and qualifications to deliver what we expect for our North American customers. We invest in our people," said Tremblay.
The $200-million investment in the new facility is the first phase of up to three, representing a possible total investment of $500 to $600 million in the city.
That would make it the second largest investment in Port Colborne behind Asahi Kasei's $1.56-billion, 185,800-square-metre lithium-ion separator battery plant announced earlier this year.
Tremblay said the volume produced starting in spring 2026 won't be enough to serve the entire North American market and the second and third phases would add to capacity.
Though the plan is to increase the facility two to three times in size, if there's a slowdown or downturn in the market, the company would pause future expansions, he said.
Expansion in Port Colborne made sense for the company because of easy access to its main raw material, corn, and nearby highways and rail lines.
"We can serve a large part of our customers in Canada and the U.S. from Port Colborne. It's a key asset for us to be close to our customers."
In years past, Tremblay said the company used a dock on the Welland Canal, near the split between the former canal and working section at Dain City, to import corn from the U.S.
"It's not clear whether it makes sense to use the canal since we can get the corn we need in Ontario. We could use it to get other raw materials."
The canal provides JBL with water needed to manufacture its various other products, like citric acid, he said.
Tremblay said access to hydro was another factor in the decision to build in the city.
As Associate Minister of Energy and Intensive Industries, Oosterhoff said his role is to ensure a new hydro transmission line is built to allow for the 130 megawatts of power needed to run the facility.
"It's a massive amount of power for this project," he said.
The Niagara West MPP also said it was good to see 50 new jobs coming to the area, hit hard by losses over the past couple of decades in the industrial sector.
He said south Niagara is seeing jobs return with Tuesday's announcement, plus Linamar building its Giga casting facility in south Welland and Asahi Kasei's battery plant in Port Colborne's north end.
Ford said JBL's announcement was another huge investment in the city and noted the company has been a big part of the community and economy for two decades.
"The expansion will help (JBL) reach new markets, strengthen local supply chains and boost the economy," the premier said.
He said the provincial agency, Invest Ontario, supported the expansion with $4.8 million for the project.
According to its website, Invest Ontario pursues major investments across key industries, including the advanced manufacturing, life sciences and technology sectors.
Fedeli said when provincial officials met with JBL in Switzerland more than a year ago, they offered what Invest Ontario calls white glove service to help the company expand and grow.
He said the investment cements Port Colborne as a key part of Ontario's manufacturing ecosystem.
CAPTION: Jungbunzlauer chief executive officer Bruno Tremblay talks about the company's planned $200-million investment in Port Colborne to build a new xanthan gum facility to service North America.Ontario Premier Doug Ford speaks about Jungbunzlauer's planned $200-million investment to build a xanthan gum facility in Port Colborne.Julie Jocsak St. Catharines StandardJulie Jocsak St. Catharines Standard

Anti-gridlock act 'goes against everything,' cyclist says: Proposed law 'completely going backwards in almost every aspect you can think of'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/anti-gridlock-act-goes-against-everything-cyclist/docview/3108515676/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Anti-gridlock+act+%27goes+against+everything%2C%27+cyclist+says%3A+Proposed+law+%27completely+going+backwards+in+almost+every+aspect+you+can+think+of%27&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-09-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: After years of lobbying for changes to improve the safety of cyclists, Rita Dillon feels as though the provincial government is pedalling backwards.
Dillon, president of Niagara Freewheelers Bicycle Touring Club, said proposed provincial legislation from Premier Doug Ford's Progressive Conservative government that would restrict the construction of new bike lanes "goes against everything we've been trying to do."
"It took years of urging and pushing municipalities here to get to the point where bike lanes would be put on roads as they were approved, but it is completely going backwards in almost every aspect you can think of," she said of the legislation, which could be introduced as early as next month.
"The whole idea is we should be moving forward, with trying to have fewer cars on the road. But Mr. Ford seems to be content with highways and cars and pollution and anything that gets in the way of that, he just wants to crush immediately."
The Toronto Star reported on Friday that the province's proposed "Reducing Gridlock and Saving You Time Act" included plans to block the construction of new bike lanes if they result in the loss of other lanes to motorized vehicles, as well as plans to fast track the construction of new highway projects like Highway 413 from Milton to Highway 400.
Dillon said the proposed legislation is "astounding, but when looking at all of his other decisions, probably not surprising."
"Hopefully there are enough people around him to backtrack, divert this or come to a different conclusion," she said.
If the legislation is passed as currently proposed, Dillon said she hopes municipalities will find a way to continue including bike lanes regardless.
She said the Freewheelers, Niagara's largest cycling organization with more than 300 members, will be sending letters to Niagara's municipal governments asking them to "pay no attention" to the proposed legislation. "The rewards of cycling, they're so obvious and so researched and they were improving things. Every municipality here has been working on bike lanes and trails and trying to link these up so more and more cyclists can come out," she said. "This backward decision or discussion of reducing these lanes is foolish, and I'm hoping and I'm sure the politicians in Niagara are smarter than that."
St. Catharines city councillors were scheduled to consider final approval of the city's Active Transportation Master Plan on Monday night, including a commitment to expand the existing network of bike lanes in the city.
Dillon said most cyclists are also motorists, and "we see it both ways."
"The things that annoy any other motorist about cyclists who don't follow the rules of the road upset us just the same way," she said. "But we certainly are aware of our vulnerability on the road in a way that people who drive cars and don't ride bikes have no clue about the risks we face to be out there.
"But, every time you see a group of five or 15 cyclists coming down the road, that's an equal number of cars that are not on the road at that time. That removes a lot of exhaust pollution that we think about," Dillon added.
She said a bike also takes up a lot less pavement than a motorized vehicle, and the proposed legislation is "ludicrous when you come at it from that angle."
"I think it really promotes that element of drivers who really resent the fact that they might have to slowdown a little bit to pass a group of cyclists," she said. "But we (cyclists) certainly feel safer. We know we are safer to be out there in a group that we are on our own."
CAPTION: Niagara Freewheelers Bicycle Touring Club president Rita Dillon is concerned restricting new bike lanes will set backthe progress in enhancing the safetyof riders.metroland file photo

Full text: Don't take
Poilievre's bait
Food banks cannot be the permanent solution to food insecurity, Sept. 12
I never thought I would live to see the homeless sleeping on park benches and lining up at soup kitchens with their children for their only meal of the day.
As a youth, I listened to elders talking about the Great Depression of the 1930s. There were no federal programs that could deal with the devastation caused by a collapse of economies around the world.
With so-called socialist parties in Canada such as the CCF looked upon with suspicion, longtime traditional governments of Liberals and Conservatives seemed at a loss for answers.
Eventually, these parties accepted that the "Lefties" had the solution. People who had been honest and hard-working were left to beg and scramble. Right-wing governments reluctantly set up programs that allowed people to survive.
For many years governments in Canada were, through supporting reasonable social programs, an example to the world of how to provide for those in need. Even Conservative governments saw that attacking these supports was dangerous if they wanted to stay in power.
What happened? Mike Harris in Ontario, Ralph Klein in Alberta, Steven Harper in Ottawa happened. They slashed social programs and demonized the poor.
Pierre Poilievre's rise is the end result of all of this political trickery. Lose the glasses, find a better tailor and barber, and constantly oppose everything other parties propose. Never get pinned down on what your plans are if you do attain power.
Admittedly, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau is well past his best-before-date, but getting a mini-Trump in exchange is not a reasonable response!
Richard Murri, Niagara Falls
Our hockey
heritage
Future of Garden City Arena property still being decided, Feb. 10, 2023
History is important. Sports is important. The history of sports is of heritage importance to St. Catharines, especially through the evolution of junior hockey over the past decades.
A piece of this heritage was physically removed with the recent demolition of the Jack Gatecliffe and Rex Stimers arenas. Thankfully a measure of hockey and sports history has been preserved with establishment of a sports wall of fame at the south end of the Meridian Centre.
However, at the north end of the arena only one lonely banner exists, that of the late, great Stan Mikita of Teepees glory days.
There are many more players deserving of this recognition. Players such as Brian Cullen, Marcel Dionne, Pierre Pilotte, Pat Stapleton, Phil Esposito, the Hull brothers to name a few.
I would encourage City of St. Catharines councillors to consider supporting the addition of more banners to recognize these fine athletes.
Lets keep Stan in good company!
Ron Friesen, St. Catharines

Full text: A Conservative
filter needed
Carbon pricing to cause economic 'nuclear winter,' Poilievre tells his MPs, Sept. 15
Former U.S. president Donald Trump provided us with a good laugh last week with his comments about immigrants eating pets.
And then this morning we read Conservative party Leader Pierre Poilievre's predictions of a carbon tax producing a "nuclear winter," bringing mass hunger and malnutrition with people stuck in their homes, unable to drive anywhere.
Don't these conservative brains have some kind of filter that would stop such inane hysteria from exiting their mouths?
David Fowler, Wainfleet
Poilievre shows what's wrong with the world
Can Canadians trust Pierre
Poilievre with the economy? Here are some of the major changes we can expect, Sept. 14
The simple truth is Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre is a wealthy person whose sole motivation is acquiring and controlling more wealth for himself and other already wealthy people. Wealth is power. Power over others. Power over those who rely on the wealthy to earn their living. Unless you live 100 per cent off grid, this means you.
As privatization of public services and public lands continues to escalate; as the monetization of everything under the sun progresses, unrestrained in modern times, the control the folks at the top exert upon those below them continues to tighten into a stranglehold.
The wealthy become wealthier through the conduct of business and the vehicle of investment, primarily in large corporations. Not so much in small business, I expect.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and the party he leads are not perfect. It is often said the Liberals are cut from the same cloth as the Conservatives. The maxim was likely coined when Canada and our provinces featured the erstwhile Progressive Conservative party.
Canada's "right" was co-opted, then corrupted, by the movement conservatives from the U.S. via the Reform Party. They morphed into Preston Manning's Alliance, which took over the Progressive Conservative brand after Canadians buried Brian Mulroney's government. The "progressive" qualifier was summarily dropped.
Todd Benson, St. Catharines
Which is greener,
turbines or highways?
Ford government plans to boost electricity production to keep up with demand, Aug. 28
The news is so good at the moment.
Kamala Harris will win the U.S. election and Ontario Premier Doug Ford will get booted out.
Ford was elected because former premier Kathleen Wynne had wind turbines built across Ontario, which cost a lot of money. Today those turbines are producing a lot of green energy.
Ford thought the money would be better spent building new freeways across the province.
Which is more likely to help keep global warming below the limit set by the United Nations? Our very future depends on the answer.
Robin Guard, St. Catharines

Fort Erie plant gets deal to assemble OPP helicopters: Ford makes announcement as company celebrates 40 years in Canada

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fort-erie-plant-gets-deal-assemble-opp/docview/3104427870/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Fort+Erie+plant+gets+deal+to+assemble+OPP+helicopters%3A+Ford+makes+announcement+as+company+celebrates+40+years+in+Canada&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-09-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario government has ordered three Airbus helicopters for Ontario Provincial Police that will be assembled at Airbus's Fort Erie plant.
Premier Doug Ford announced the purchase of the H135 helicopters during an event Friday that also celebrated the company's 40th anniversary in Canada.
The helicopters will be assembled at Airbus's Gilmore Road facility.
"Earlier this summer, our government announced an investment of $134 million to purchase new police helicopters," said Ford, one of many dignitaries who attended Friday's event.
"Airbus will be supplying three of those new helicopters, which will be used to fight crime, including auto theft in Ottawa, Greater Toronto Area and the OPP northwest region."
The company has become a "key player" in the Canadian aerospace industry, providing helicopters that support communities, save lives and protect Canadians through law enforcement, emergency medical services, energy, firefighting, search and rescue, private and business transport and aerial work.
The company has grown to employ more than 4,500 employees across 10 facilities in the country - about 300 of those employees in Fort Erie.
Since 1984, Airbus has delivered nearly 600 helicopters in Canada. With more than 760 helicopters in service and more than 220 operators across the country, Airbus is the leader for civil and para-public helicopters, with eight types.
In 2023, Airbus helicopters in Canada accumulated more than 275,000 flight hours.
The Fort Erie facility completes, customizes and delivers helicopters for Canadian customers and is the centre of excellence for light single gearboxes and the single source for critical composite components supporting platforms worldwide.
Each year, Airbus ships 34,000 parts from Fort Erie to customers around the globe.
"For 40 years, Airbus has been an important part of the community here in Fort Erie, helping create good-paying manufacturing jobs for our world-class Ontario workers, with economic benefits that have stretched across the province," said Ford.
Economic Development Job Creation and Trade Minister Vic Fedeli was also at Friday's event.
He said Airbus is a "key player" in the province's aerospace industry, representing the "best of Ontario-made innovation on the world stage."
"You've got 45,000 employees here in Ontario across more than 200 aerospace companies - we are the second-largest industry in Canada," he said.
"And over the last two years, Ontario has attracted nearly $143 million in investment from just aerospace companies. That includes here at Airbus, who announced their investment at the Paris air show last year right here for Fort Erie to acquire new equipment and technology - they're going to increase capacity and they've hired 20 new jobs at this very facility."
Airbus Helicopters Canada president Dwayne Charette said the company's success has been "driven by a commitment to innovation, quality and close collaboration with our customers."
"As we celebrate this major milestone, it's especially meaningful to see our trusted partners in law enforcement continue to choose us to support their vital missions," he said.
"Thank you to the Ontario Provincial Police for their trust. This order is a testament to the strength of our relationships and our ongoing commitment to delivering solutions that meet the unique needs of those who safeguard our communities in Ontario."
Anna Pierce, vice-president of Niagara Helicopters, a year-round aerial attraction in Niagara Falls, spoke about her company's long-standing relationship with Airbus.
"There has never been an instance when our messages have gone unanswered, or our issues have been left unaddressed," she said.
"The team at Airbus has gone well above and beyond our expectations, providing us with exemplary customer service for close to a decade and we truly appreciate the partnership."
Pierce said as a "testament to our strong commitment to the Airbus product," Niagara Helicopters, in February at the Helicopter Association International Heli-Expo, announced it will replace its entire fleet and that six new H130 Airbus helicopters will be flying over Niagara Falls by fall 2025.
"We look forward to continuing our relationship with (Airbus) for many, many years to come."
CAPTION: Premier Doug Ford tours Airbus Helicopters in Fort Erie, where he announced on Friday the provincial government has ordered three Airbus H135 helicopters for Ontario Provincial Police - for Ottawa, the Greater Toronto Area and in the OPP's northwest Thunder Bay region.Julie Jocsak St. Catharines Standard

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-09-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Province abandoned amalgamations
Fire savings possible, Letters,
Sept. 9
The letter mentions how amalgamation could save money and the writer notes how the City of Hamilton identified inefficiencies prior to its transition.
However, it contains misleading phrases such as "fire savings possible," "a plan to reduce taxes," "amalgamation would save money" and "savings could be a million dollars annually."
In fact the Doug Ford government has already backtracked on forced municipal amalgamations.
This is because years back former premier Mike Harris and past provincial Conservative logic tried this tactic and it failed miserably. It was believed local services would be delivered more efficiently at a lower cost. It was also believed amalgamation would reduce duplication of services. It accomplished neither.
The Fraser Institute found these efficiencies did not materialize and in most cases taxes increased as did spending on many services. It is important to note the institute is an independent, non-partisan organization.
Thankfully, Ford and company walked away from forced amalgamations. For once they were willing to not repeat the mistakes of their predecessors.
Fred Audibert, Niagara Falls
Cut councillors
Welland politicians opt not to cut size of city council, want residents to speak first, Sept. 5
It's not often the public is asked for an opinion on a subject pertaining to council matters, so when asked, I feel obliged to respond.
Ward 4 Coun. Tony DiMarco is correct when saying council should be making this decision as councillors have more information than the public.
However, as a taxpayer, any decision that will save $250,000 of my taxes should be implemented at once. Council should be reduced to one councillor per ward without hesitation, especially after councillors received an almost 50 per cent pay raise in the prior term.
I was appalled when viewing a photo of regional council; it looked like the UN was in session. I hope Welland will show the Region what responsible government looks like and make cuts to reduce strain on constituents.
Paul D. Scott, Welland
Cyclists and
pedestrians
I am one of many seniors who walk dogs along the path on Prince Charles Drive between Fitch Street and Thorold Road in Welland.
Cyclists prefer to ride the path rather than bike lanes; I prefer they do also, for their safety.
But I would ask they warn pedestrians with their bell or voice as they approach from behind. I check for bikes regularly but still get surprised.
I emailed the city requesting a sign be placed at opposite ends of this stretch to remind cyclists to provide warning to pedestrians, to slow down as they pass.
No reply, no signs was the response.
Along the Beaches boardwalk in Toronto there are throngs of pedestrians mixed with serious cyclists, often with toddlers, a stroller and/or a dog.
I saw no accidents but heard lots of bells ringing and many thank-yous.
Todd Benson, Welland

Subject: Councils; Bicycling

Title: Readers write

Publication title: The Standard; St. Catharines, Ont.

Document 131 of 249

Readers write Letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write-letters-editor/docview/3101087144/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write+Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-09-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The right-wing politics of safe injection sites
Pierre Poilievre's attack on me is a symptom of a larger problem, Aug. 31
I read the column by Doris Grinspun, chef executive officer of Registered Nurses' Association of Ontario, in which she skilfully exposes the dangerous and Donald Trump-like attacks the Pierre Poilievre Conservatives are using against everyone and everything that does not fit their narrow-minded, scaremongering strategy.
Poilievre's support of Premier Doug Ford's dismantling of safe injection sites is just one example of following the ultra right-wing Christian Republican playbook.
Assisted by the Progressive Conservative government in Ontario, and right-wing leaders in Alberta and other provinces, Poilievre's Conservatives weave a false picture of Canada and use lies and half-truths eerily similar to Trump's Republicans. Their support of rioters in Ottawa and Alberta is just as offensive as Trump's encouragement of those attacking Congress.
Sadly, under a vulnerable and disliked Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, the Liberals have not effectively countered these sleazy tactics.
In the case of safe injection sites, by closing them Ford is, without question, causing the deaths of those among us who are caught up in the nightmare of addiction. Poilievre understands this but apparently does not care.
These seemingly heartless individuals are comfortable using every tactic to get into power and hold onto power. It is shocking and sad. Especially since Ford's and thousands of other families have lost and will continue to lose people they love to addiction.
Richard Murri, Niagara Falls
Flying the Canadian flag with pride
Shame on Welland, Letters,
Aug. 30
I cannot understand why persons who affix flags or decals of other nations on their vehicles and homes are not also flying a Canadian flag or decal.
It is disrespectful to Canada. Where do their greater loyalties lie, to this country where they live, earn their living, likely raised a family and have a secure existence, or to their ancestral homes?
Everyone living in Canada has ancestors who came here from somewhere else, presumably for a better life.
My family came from Scotland and northern Europe in the 19th century. My wife is from eastern Europe.
I ask: what public participation in July 1 Canada Day celebrations do they routinely partake?
Have they ever thanked this country publicly for assistance - financial or military - for their ancestral homes?
I praise a Canada that continues to assist democracies around the world time after time with either weapons, personnel or finances.
One final comment: many members of my family, including my father, fought in wars around the world and not all of them returned.
To paraphrase Mr. Cherwonogrodzky's comments: Glory to Canada's heroes! Shame on those who disrespect Canada!
William Connacher, St. Catharines

St. Catharines, Ont., L2R 7T4. Letters should be 250 words or less, and may be edited
for length and clarity. Please include your name, address and phone number
for verification purposes.

Document 132 of 249

'Turning grief into action': Niagara advocates vow to continue fighting on overdose awareness day

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/turning-grief-into-action/docview/3100438930/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Turning+grief+into+action%27%3A+Niagara+advocates+vow+to+continue+fighting+on+overdose+awareness+day&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-09-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: More than a hundred people gathered at St. Catharines city hall Saturday to remember loved ones lost to overdoses, but recent political events were top of mind for many.
Niagara residents expressed worry and anger about the future of the opioid crisis under the provincial government on International Overdose Awareness Day.
"Eleven days ago, with International Overdose Awareness Day fast approaching, the province of Ontario announced that it is set to cut funding to 10 consumption sites provincewide," Talia Storm, director of StreetWorks Services for Positive Living Niagara, said to boos in the crowd, "in what feels like a war against harm reduction and a war against people who use drugs."
While Niagara gets to keep its site on Queenston Street run by Positive Living, Storm said the decision to close others is "wilfully putting people in harm's way."
"We grieve for the communities affected by the closures and promise to advocate alongside them. This is not just about us, here in Niagara. This is so much bigger," she said.
Positive Living's consumption and treatment services site provides a supervised space to people using substances so if an overdose or drug poisoning occurs, a trained person can immediately administer life-saving naloxone.
Storm said the work is literally "life-giving." Since the site opened in December 2018, more than 1,300 drug poisonings have been reversed. She said infections have also been prevented by providing harm-reduction supplies and thousands of naloxone kits have been distributed into the community.
Event participants, spanning a wide range of ages, were met with rain and sun at city hall before they marched to Montebello Park. Some carried signs with a loved one's name or messages such as "Harm reduction saves lives," "End the stigma, educate the masses" and "No more dead friends."
"I completely believe in harm reduction. I'm terrified with what's happening right now with closing sites," said Sandi Tantardini, whose son Scott died from a fentanyl overdose at age 28 in Alberta after undergoing treatment for addiction and relapsing.
Tantardini was one of five mothers known as Moms Stop the Harm who addressed regional council in 2017 to plead with politicians to bring a safe injection site to Niagara.
She said she fears if Premier Doug Ford is re-elected the remaining seven centres will be gone, too.
"It's heartbreaking," she said. "People, they're dying. People are dying and people don't care. I find it very scary."
The province said it was closing sites within 200 metres of schools and daycares after hearing concerns from communities, parents and families about safety problems.
In the same announcement, it said it will introduce legislation this fall that if passed would prohibit municipalities or organizations from opening new consumption sites and participating in federal "safer supply" initiatives.
The province also said it will invest $378 million in 19 new homelessness and addiction recovery treatment hubs - HART - that will provide 375 "highly supportive" housing units, plus addiction recovery and treatment beds.
While additional treatment options and supports are welcomed by many, the HART hubs will not offer supervised drug consumption, needle exchange programs or "safer supply."
Deborah Richards said those harm-reduction services are critical for saving lives, preventing fatal overdoses and reducing the burden on hospitals.
"Doug Ford will have you believe that he cares about people with addictions," said Richards, whose son Jay died of an accidental fentanyl overdose in 2019.
"If he cares so much about addiction and thinks closing these much-needed sites (is) the answer, why is he continuing to expose people to substances like alcohol and putting it in corner stores?"
Richards, chair of the board of Positive Living Niagara, said she believes expanding alcohol access is being done for votes and as a way to make more money.
"Imagine if he were to figure out how to monetize harm-reduction programs - we would probably quadruple the number instead of eliminating two-thirds."
Alicia Marshall, who has lost more friends to addiction than she can count, said change has to start with people getting out to vote, noting the low turnout in the last municipal election.
"Get to know your politicians. Get to know what they think, where they want to go with this. We need people in there who are compassionate, empathetic and will make decisions using that lens," said Marshall, a founding member of NALE, Niagara Advocates with Lived and Living Experience.
She said people with lived experiences are needed in power to make policy changes.
"Often politicians will make these choices and decisions without consulting those who are going to access those services and (asking) them, 'What do you need?'"
Storm told the gathering at city hall that as harm-reduction work becomes increasingly political, people cannot "stand idly by."
"Now is the time to fight harder for what is right. Now is the time to scream from the rooftops," she said, adding they've done it before.
"Yet again, we will turn heartbreak into hope. We will turn rage into determination. We know how to turn grief into action."
CAPTION: A large contingent of people marched in St. Catharines to remember those who have died from opioid overdoses and to raise awareness of the issue on International Overdose Awareness Day. Peoplegathered for International Overdose Awareness Day Saturday in front of the Royal Canadian Legion Branch 24 cenotaph at St. Catharines city hall.Karena Walter photosSt. Catharines Standard

CREDIT: Karena Walter Reporter

Document 133 of 249

Reverse funding cut to Big Brothers Big Sisters, Niagara NDP MPPs tell province

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/reverse-funding-cut-big-brothers-sisters-niagara/docview/3100021216/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Reverse+funding+cut+to+Big+Brothers+Big+Sisters%2C+Niagara+NDP+MPPs+tell+province&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-09-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Niagara's NDP MPPs have issued a joint letter to the province's education minister calling for the reversal of a one-per-cent funding cut to Big Brothers Big Sisters of Niagara, and an expansion of funding to support programming for vulnerable children in the region.
"So many young people are helped by what Big Brothers Big Sisters of Niagara brings to our community," Niagara Falls MPP Wayne Gates said in a party media release.
"Their mentorship leads to better attendance in school, positive health outcomes and to these young people being happier and more confident. I can't imagine a more important investment than that."
He said the MPPs are calling on new Education Minister Jill Dunlop to "do the right thing, reverse the cut and increase funding" to Big Brothers Big Sisters across Ontario.
St. Catharines MPP Jennie Stevens said the agency has been a "cornerstone of support" for vulnerable youths across Niagara.
"Even a one-per-cent reduction will significantly impact the success rates we're seeing when youth participate in programming," she said in the release.
"Investing in our youth is not just a moral obligation; it's a necessity for the Niagara region."
Niagara Centre MPP Jeff Burch said Big Brothers Big Sisters of Niagara has been around for more than 85 years.
"We need to invest in this incredible support system for our children - not take it away when the demand for services is increasing."
Erin Graybiel, executive director for Big Brothers Big Sisters of Niagara, said the agency supported nearly 1,000 vulnerable children and youths last year across all 12 Niagara municipalities.
"Only seven per cent of the annual funding required to offer safe no-cost mentoring supports comes from the Ontario government," she said in the release.
"An investment in Big Brothers Big Sisters will have a direct impact on the lives and academic outcomes of children and youth who need support the most in our community."
Lee Ann Forsyth-Sells, superintendent of education for Niagara Catholic District School Board, said the board "values its long-standing partnership" with Big Brothers Big Sisters of Niagara.
"Niagara Catholic students have benefited from the opportunity to participate in the BBBS in-school mentoring program with their volunteer mentors in Niagara Catholic schools," she said in the release.
"We are pleased to continue this program with BBBS in the 2024-2025 school year."
Dunlop's office did not respond to an interview request prior to deadline.
In the joint letter to Dunlop, the MPPs said the Ministry of Education committed $1.5 million annually to support Big Brothers Big Sisters agencies across Ontario in 2008.
"This funding has remained stagnant for 16 years, despite the growing demand for their services, the rising challenge of mental health and addictions issues in Niagara and the significant impact of inflation," it said.
"We have recently learned that this funding is set to be reduced by one per cent - just as the new school year starts."
The letter said the impact of this reduction is "profound."
"Many agencies are already facing the difficult decision of scaling back their programs and services, which will inevitably lead to a decline in the number of vulnerable youth and families they can support. This will result in severe financial strain on Big Brothers Big Sisters, who have already been dealing with increased demands and fewer resources for years."
The letter said during the 2023-24 school year, Big Brothers Big Sisters provided mentorship to 9,957 youths across Ontario through its education-focused programs.
It said youths who are mentored show 80 per cent better school attendance, 17 per cent increased likelihood of gainful employment, earning 13 per cent more on average, have a 46 per cent reduced likelihood of initiating drug use compared to their peers, 47 per cent are in senior leadership positions, 87 per cent have strong social networks and 96 per cent report being happy.
CAPTION: Erin Graybiel, executive director of Big Brothers Big Sisters of Niagara, says the agency supported nearly 1,000 vulnerable children and youths last year across all 12 Niagara municipalities through its mentoring programs.

CREDIT: Ray Spiteri Reporter

Readers write Letters To The Editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write-letters-editor/docview/3098639705/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write+Letters+To+The+Editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-08-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Waiting for an honest government
Will the NDP or Liberals keep track of the deaths that Doug Ford's bumbling will cause?
Raised speed limits are causing a spike in biker deaths. His inaction with emergency rooms and hallway medicine. A buck a beer, and then buy helicopters to chase the people who drink them.
How many lives will be lost when the safe consumption sites are closed? Ford calls safe supply drug trafficking. Well, isn't that a far(ce)-right way of thinking.
When the next election comes around and the voters don't realize that the Conservatives are not interested in science then maybe we will have a minority government that is honest, because Ford is not.
Russ Young, Fort Erie
Memories of
Machu Picchu
Re: 'We couldn't be in a worse place': Niagara seniors may lose deposit after dream vacation grounded on advice of doctor, Aug. 24
The article about the dilemma of the four ladies trying to travel to Machu Picchu brought back memories for me.
Like them, I booked an eight-day trip to Lima, Cuzco and Machu Picchu in 2017 before consulting my family GP. In my case, he concluded that I was fit enough to do the trip so long as I visited the medical health unit then located in the Niagara Region headquarters building and specializing in medicine for travellers.
At the medical unit, they brought my Canadian vaccination certificate up to date (one injection was required) and discussed high-altitude travel. For travel to altitudes of 3,300 metres above sea level and higher, they offered a choice of a two- or eight-tablet regime to encourage red blood cell creation. I selected the two-tablet alternative.
They also advised about limiting physical activity at high altitudes, travelling light and avoiding the coca-leaf tea readily available that local residents use at high levels.
The trip to the Sacred Valley in the Andes was on my bucket list and was everything I hoped it would be. I was 75 years old when I did the trip. I travelled alone.
By the way, I completed my bucket list travel in 2018 with an 11-day trip to Antarctica, an adventure with a different set of medical challenges.
Kenn Moody, St. Davids
Shame on Welland
I could not believe that Welland city council refused to raise the Ukrainian flag at city hall in celebration of Ukrainian Independence Day (Aug. 24).
Oh, sure, you can make up reasons for refusing. But the main result is: Welland council ignored Ukraine.
Ukrainians are fighting and dying for liberty. And Welland council can't even raise the Ukrainian flag.
I praise Niagara Falls, Lincoln and Grimsby councils for celebrating Ukrainian Independence Day.
In the next city election, I will not be voting for Mayor Frank Campion and my Ward 6 councillors, Bonnie Fokkens and Jamie Lee. I urge Welland residents to do likewise.
Glory to Ukraine. Glory to her heroes. Shame on Welland council.
Mike Cherwonogrodzky, Welland

Document 135 of 249

Campaign calls for more crisis support: City council endorses Ontario's Big City Mayors resolution to to help address homelessness, mental health, addictions crisis

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/campaign-calls-more-crisis-support/docview/3098162550/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Campaign+calls+for+more+crisis+support%3A+City+council+endorses+Ontario%27s+Big+City+Mayors+resolution+to+to+help+address+homelessness%2C+mental+health%2C+addictions+crisis&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-08-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: St. Catharines residents have been lending their voices to the Solve the Crisis campaign, the mayor reports, and now city councillors are, too.
Councillors unanimously called on the provincial and federal governments on Monday to take action to address the growing mental health, addictions and homelessness crisis by supporting the campaign launched this month by Ontario's Big City Mayors.
Mayor Mat Siscoe said St. Catharines got a special shout-out by the organizers of Ontario's Big City Mayors at the Association of Municipalities of Ontario conference last week for the "amazing response" from the community to the campaign.
"St. Catharines has definitely stepped up and tried to get the point across to the provincial and federal governments exactly what is going on our streets and in our parks here in the community," Siscoe said during Monday's council meeting.
"This is a humanitarian crisis. And it's now a matter of us expressing the same sense of urgency to upper levels of government that we here at the municipal and the regional level feel. It's a crisis that's going to require a lot of changes."
Siscoe had asked residents to provide input at solvethecrisis.ca during council's Aug. 12 meeting.
The campaign specifically calls on the provincial government to appoint a minister with appropriate funding and powers to be a single point of contact to address housing needs, mental health, addictions and wraparound supports.
Siscoe said there are now 16 ministries involved with issues pertaining to homelessness, mental health and addictions.
The campaign is also asking the province to strike a task force to develop an action plan with representatives from municipalities, the health-care sector, first responders, community services, business and the tourism industry.
And it's asking the province to provide "adequate, sufficient and sustainable funding" to ensure municipalities have tools and resources to support individuals suffering from mental health, addictions and homelessness.
"I'm fully in support of this and it can't happen quick enough for us," said Grantham Coun. Dawn Dodge, who just completed four years representing the city on AMO's large urban caucus.
During an announcement in St. Catharines last week involving the shipbuilding industry, the Standard asked Premier Doug Ford if he would support Ontario's Big City Mayors' call for a single ministry, task force and funding.
Ford said the minister of health is doing a great job with Ontario's first ever minister of mental health and addictions. He said the government is putting $3.8 billion into mental health and additions, referring to the province's Roadmap to Wellness strategy, and has also put hundreds of millions of dollars into homelessness prevention.
"We're always willing to sit down and talk to the mayors and see their situation," Ford said.
"We're there to support them and there's no one, no government - think of this - there's no government that's put more money in the homeless and homeless shelters, mental health and addiction than this government has."
Niagara West MPP Sam Oosterhoff, at the same event, said Niagara received an 86 per cent increase in its homelessness prevention program funding allocation last year, an increase to $21 million a year from just shy of $11 million.
"Those funds are directly going into homelessness prevention services. They're the reason we're seeing new shelter beds opening up across the region with the support of local municipalities as well," Oosterhoff said.
"So this government has almost doubled the amount of local funding going to address those issues. And, I would say, that this is a premier that's led the charge on making that happen."
The increase in Community Homelessness Prevention Initiative funding came after years of Niagara politicians arguing that the province's funding formula was shortchanging Niagara, going back to 2013 and the Liberal government.
Siscoe said on Monday that the provincial government announced some positive changes last week related to the funding of homelessness and addiction recovery treatment hubs, but there is a lot more that still needs to be done.
His motion said municipalities are working on community-specific solutions to the crisis, but "lack the expertise, capacity and resources" to address "increasingly complex health-care and housing issues" alone.
"Any municipality that is facing the challenges similar to what we're facing is going to be passing this motion in the coming weeks and months," Siscoe said, adding that includes Welland and Niagara Region councils.
"I think residents in St. Catharines are currently leading the charge, and I think it's up to our council as well to do this."
CAPTION: The temporary shelter for individuals experiencing homelessness is seen at 29 Riordon St. in St. Catharines.Bob Tymczyszyn St. Catharines Standard file photo

CREDIT: Karena Walter Reporter

'Star Wars Outlaws' heavy with Canadian talent: Game's featured voice artist honed her craft studying theatre while growing up in Alberta

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/star-wars-outlaws-heavy-with-canadian-talent/docview/3098162539/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Star+Wars+Outlaws%27+heavy+with+Canadian+talent%3A+Game%27s+featured+voice+artist+honed+her+craft+studying+theatre+while+growing+up+in+Alberta&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-08-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: "Star Wars Outlaws" would have had a distinctly Canadian touch if the game's narrative director had his way.
Early in the development process for the upcoming video game, Navid Khavari suggested an idea that ultimately never made it out of the writers' room.
"I wanted to write a scene that was just two smugglers apologizing to each other the entire time," said Khavari, who is based in Toronto along with lead writer Nikki Foy and lead voice actor Humberly Gonzalez.
Developed by Ubisoft, "Outlaws" is the first open-world "Star Wars" game and is set between the events depicted in the movies "The Empire Strikes Back" and "Return of the Jedi."
While Sweden-based Massive Entertainment led the development, support provided by other Ubisoft studios included motion capture work that Khavari says was done in Toronto and Montreal.
As its name suggests, "Outlaws" explores the criminal underbelly of the galaxy, a side of "Star Wars" that has rarely been seen.
Players will have the opportunity to explore several planets, including the classic Tatooine, but also lesser-known and even new locales.
Foy says it was a fun challenge to create a brand new story in a well-established universe.
"Getting to work on this project, in this galaxy, at this time period, it was like a cheat code that got all of us on the same page right away," Foy said.
"You didn't have to worry about what's the world going to be like, what's the tone, what's the vibe - that stuff we already know."
Khavari, who was also the narrative director for "Far Cry 6" and has worked on a number of other Ubisoft titles, says Massive contacted him early on about joining the "Star Wars" project.
"We have a huge abundance of writing talent in Toronto," he said. "The idea really became, let's build a story team in Toronto. This was years and years ago, and Massive was up for it."
In a departure from recent "Star Wars" video games, the main character in "Outlaws" is not a lightsaber-wielding, Force-pushing Jedi but a blaster-packing scoundrel.
Gonzalez voices protagonist Kay Vess, a charming and witty rogue trying to find her way in the galaxy.
Gonzalez was born in Venezuela and learned English in the nearby Caribbean island nation of Aruba before moving with her family to Fort McMurray, Alta., where she spent nearly 10 years and studied theatre.
"Being in a small city, I think it really allowed me to home in on my art," said Gonzalez, who later lived in Montreal and is now based in Toronto.
"Moving to Canada changed everything for me," she added.
"Much like Kay - she left everything behind, she had big dreams, she went to an unknown place where there were a lot of opportunities, and you just have to find them and seize them."
"Star Wars Outlaws" will be released Friday for Xbox Series S and X, PS5 and PC.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Humberly Gonzalez, the main voice actor who plays the character Kay Vess in the upcoming video game "Star Wars Outlaws," at Ubisoft's Toronto offices.Chris YoungTHE CANADIAN PRESS File Photo

Ontario parties spend summer preparing for possibility of an early election

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-parties-spend-summer-preparing/docview/3096852903/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+parties+spend+summer+preparing+for+possibility+of+an+early+election&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-08-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario's major political parties have been spending the summer nominating candidates, running "campaign schools," and canvassing after remarks from the premier this spring fuelled speculation he will call an early election.
The province's next fixed election date isn't until June 2026, but Doug Ford set off alarm bells in political circles this past May when he repeatedly refused to commit to that timeline during a news conference, saying only that he wants to make sure he fulfils his agenda and keeps the promises his majority Progressive Conservative government has made.
Liberal co-campaign directors Genevieve Tomney and Chad Walsh - officially named to their positions soon - looked at each other and said, "now it's time to go," Tomney said in an interview.
"We've kind of been treating this as a campaign summer," she said.
"What I would say about campaigns is that I sometimes kind of compare it to an iceberg, where so much of the work that's being done is happening below the surface, but we're gearing up for a pretty big end of summer into the fall."
Some of that below-the-surface work entails putting calls out to potential campaign staff and volunteers. Gone are the majority Liberal government days when the party had a campaign army at the ready - most of them have moved into different roles and into the private sector.
"Pulling all of those people back together again is kind of like a huge volunteer-slash-HR effort as well," Tomney said.
Some of the public-facing election preparations include nominating candidates. The Liberals are starting by nominating their incumbents, but that will only fill nine out of 124 ridings, so the task ahead is daunting. It's unclear how much time they will have - elections tend to happen in the fall or spring, and Tomney said the Liberals are focusing on three main scenarios: fall 2024, spring 2025 and the as-scheduled spring 2026.
Ford told Ottawa radio station CFRA that Ontario would "100 per cent" not have an election this summer or fall.
But parties like the NDP are not banking on that.
"We're going to prepare as such," Leader Marit Stiles said in an interview. "If it's not this fall and it's, say, next spring we will be just even more ready."
Laryssa Waler, a former director of communications for Ford and founder of Walman Strategies, said a spring 2025 election seems more likely than the fall.
"You saw how the last election got called, there was essentially a referendum on the budget that they presented and they won more seats," she said in an interview. "So I wouldn't be surprised if they did something similar, because it worked so well last time."
The NDP was starting some early election campaign planning this year, but Ford's comments "kicked it up a notch," Stiles said. Summers are always a good time for members of provincial parliament to connect with their constituents, and this year the politicians and volunteers are out knocking on doors, Stiles said.
"Our MPPs are on the road and some of them have been out across the province and will continue to be to help engage with our activists, connect with potential voters, help us to identify candidates, which of course is a big push right now.
"In a lot of ridings, where we don't have incumbents and we don't even have a candidate yet (organizers) are canvassing this summer. They're trying to cover as much of the riding as they can. Many of them are trying to get through the entire riding ... before the fall."
The NDP are also running campaign schools this summer to help teach people in local riding associations how to run campaigns, so the party is ready on the ground locally, not just centrally, Stiles said.
Both opposition parties are also working on some ads to help introduce their leaders to voters, since it will be the first provincial general election each of them fight.
The Green Party of Ontario, which now has two seats in the legislature, has also been busy over the past couple of months.
"We've spent the summer assembling localized mobilizing teams and boosting our ground game capacity in target ridings across the province, with our leader and deputy leaders participating in regular riding canvasses," executive director Jaymini Bhikha wrote in a statement. "We are also preparing to open nominations in the very near future."
The Progressive Conservatives currently hold 78 seats, so they have a significant advantage when it comes to getting 124 candidates in place for an election.
The party is also ahead in polling and is raising more money than the others, Waler said, which are big factors in considering election readiness. As well, they are constantly polling and conducting research, she said.
Tory caucus members have been out door knocking too this summer, but Ford encourages them to do so year-round, no matter the time in the election cycle, Waler said.
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: The province's next fixed election date isn't until June 2026, butDoug Ford set off alarm bells in political circles this past May when he repeatedly refused to commit to that timeline.The Canadian Press
