Full text: Ironic advice from St. Catharines
not needed
Potential savings ignored, Letters, Aug. 22
I find it ironic a resident of St. Catharines seems to know what is best for Niagara Falls. The writer states local politicians "will start being vocal about what they think the public wants to hear."
Does he believe our local councillors do not talk to constituents regularly to know what their residents want? As a resident of St. Catharines, he sure seems to know what's best for residents of Niagara Falls.
He states governance reform is needed and only provincial intervention will make it so.
As I have said before, one only need look at the long list of failures by our present provincial government to realize (Premier) Doug Ford and his incapable group of minions are not welcome to get involved in our local governance structure.
Ford's continued broken promises and incompetence in dealing with mental health, homelessness, education, long-term care and, of course, our broken health-care system are but a few of his disastrous decisions.
We can add to the list the handling of the COVID-19 pandemic, Greenbelt scandal, Ontario Science Centre fiasco and, most recently, awarding lucrative health-care contracts to companies such as that owned by Laura Harris, wife of former Conservative premier Mike Harris.
I disagree governance reform in Niagara is needed, especially when helped by our Ontario government.
Fred Audibert,
Niagara Falls
Glad we could help with
the shelter
Welland leasing land
for 50-room temporary
emergency shelter, Aug. 15
Congratulations to Welland city councillors from wards 1, 2, 3, 4 and 6 for keeping the new emergency shelter out of their wards and placing it in Ward 5.
It was a pleasure to read how councillors knew homelessness is a problem. Some went on to say they didn't agree with the location but voted in favour of it anyway.
Congratulations to everyone involved from the city for streamlining this problem and moving it through quickly with no public consultation, no news or updates regarding what's on its way. We don't need to waste time with all that. Also staff should be rewarded for negotiating a whopping $2 a year lease for five years.
We can now sleep at night knowing this site in Ward 5 was identified by the city, Niagara Region and other stakeholders as the "best spot" for the shelter. I guess no one drove to 570 King St. and looked at the Hope Centre property.
Please don't get us wrong here in Ward 5.
We think it's a great idea. When parents from the other side of the tracks drive kids to Memorial Park, they can tell the kids as they go by the new shelter what a great job their ward councillors did keeping it out of where they and their councillors live.
Mark Dzugan, Welland

Full text: Too many
school buses?
Niagara parents alarmed by earlier start times at some secondary schools, Aug. 20
After reading statements from parents and others interested in the welfare of kids attending schools, my observation is the school bus program is wagging the tail of good and decent education and reasonable learning hours.
It is obvious there are too many buses in our community running around virtually empty. Is there a possibility you have investigated taking all the school buses in the city and committing them for rural use as they were originally intended?
Our young people might benefit from a brisk walk in the morning and mid-afternoon. For those who are unable, city bus passes could be offered.
I am sure more money will be saved; the kids will be healthier and our local bus lines will benefit as well. Schoolchildren will get more sleep, more exercise and be awake to learn through decent waking hours.
Budget money being used for empty buses can now be used to benefit better education.
Paul D. Scott, Welland

sites are a 'failed policy' that harms communities and people with

This move will kill many and people will find discarded syringes everywhere.
If the opioid crisis hasn't touched you yet, be patient. It will.
Russ Young, Fort Erie
Small-town living
I'd like to tip my hat to the gentleman in Welland who came to my rescue.
On Aug. 14, my truck warned me the oil level was low. Of course, I pulled over to check. That's when a friendly man with a pleasant English accent came out of his home and asked if I needed assistance.
After a brief explanation of my problem, he asked if 5W-30 is what I needed. I replied that would be perfect. Kinda restores my faith in humanity. Don't you just love living in small-town Ontario?
Drew Bell, Welland
Seniors guide
I read an article about a seniors guide available from MPP Jennie Stevens' office and picked one up. I was impressed with the amount of information. There was much that applied to me that I didn't know.
Some examples of subjects include health care, long-term care, end of life resources, power of attorney, wills, veteran supports, cultural support info, transportation, seniors centres, accessibility, mobility devices, legal help, elder abuse, fraud and government income supports.
The booklet has so much information every household with a senior should have a copy.
Pete Morro, St. Catharines

Document 140 of 249

Province announces funding for supportive housing project: 'An investment in homelessness prevention'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/province-announces-funding-supportive-housing/docview/3096520281/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+announces+funding+for+supportive+housing+project%3A+%27An+investment+in+homelessness+prevention%27&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-08-24&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario government has answered the call to support a bridge and supportive housing project planned for 320 Geneva St. in St. Catharines.
Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra made a $6.1-million funding announcement on Thursday in St. Catharines that supports individuals experiencing homelessness.
"Our government has made the largest investment in homelessness prevention in Ontario's history because every Ontarian deserves a home that meets their needs," Calandra said.
"Today, we continue to build on that investment to ensure our partners have the tools to get more community, affordable and supportive housing built across the province."
With the announcement, the province joined Niagara Region and the City of St. Catharines on a three-phase project at 320 Geneva St., including a four-storey building with dozens of units.
The property once housed the city's parks and recreation staff in a building the city demolished before turning the lot over to the Region.
The site will eventually house a mixture of bridge housing, Niagara Regional Housing affordable townhouses and land for private market development.
"This project represents a significant step forward for our city, and I'm excited about the positive impact that it will have," Mayor Mat Siscoe said.
"This ambitious project, which will unfold in three distinct phases, is set to transform a former city-owned property into a residential development to help support residents and families here in the Garden City.
"St. Catharines council decided to transfer this site rather than sell it on the open market because we understood the need and the demand here in St. Catharines for this form of housing."
Calandra's funding announcement includes $2.6 million for the emergency shelter with modular trailer units at 29 Riordan St. downtown.
St. Catharines NDP MPP Jennie Stevens had been after Calandra in the legislature to help fund the Geneva Street project, upbraiding him during the May 16 session for ignoring the pleas from Niagara.
After Calandra playfully included her in his opening remarks Thursday, calling her "his favourite opposition colleague," Stevens said she wanted to see more of him in St. Catharines.
Quips aside, Stevens said the project will help take people off the streets.
"It takes them out of encampments," Stevens said.
"It gives them a roof over their head, which is a human right. I think that this is going to be a wonderful project moving forward for the city of St. Catharines, and I want to thank the unsung heroes, most of all the staff at the Niagara Region, who have worked behind the scenes to make sure that this project gets up off the ground for our community."
Region Chair Jim Bradley said the unveiling of Phase 1 marks a significant milestone in the plans for 320 Geneva St., which include bridge housing and health-based programming, two initiatives that have proven successful at other regional facilities.
"The site will also feature a teaching kitchen and private meeting space for community agencies to support their clients," Bradley said. "This location will be staffed 24 hours a day, seven days a week, ensuring that client's needs are met regardless of the time or day of the week."
Bradley said when the regional council term started in 2022, the housing crisis was a top priority for councillors.
"This project clearly demonstrates our unwavering commitment to tackle this challenge across the entire region, particularly in this case with the city of St. Catharines," Bradley said.
"While the causes behind the housing crisis are complex and nuanced, we are aligned with our partners in the provincial government and their desire to see more people have a safe and affordable place to call home.
"This project is a positive step in the right direction."
Niagara West Tory MPP Sam Oosterhoff said his government is working hard to ensure every person in Ontario can access safe and affordable housing.
"Today's announcement builds on our government's efforts to build more homes faster and make life more affordable for Ontario families," he said.
CAPTION: Paul Calandra, minister of municipal affairs and housing, announces the Ontario governmentis providing more than$6.1 million in support of individuals experiencing homelessness in St. Catharines.Architect's drawing shows what the supportive housing project planned for 320 Geneva St. in St. Catharines will look like.Bob Tymczyszyn St. Catharines Standard metroland

Document 141 of 249

Shipyard gets piece of $10M for skills training: Ontario Shipyards gets a boost from fund that cuts risk for employers

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/shipyard-gets-piece-10m-skills-training/docview/3095396195/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Shipyard+gets+piece+of+%2410M+for+skills+training%3A+Ontario+Shipyards+gets+a+boost+from+fund+that+cuts+risk+for+employers&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-08-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Shipyards is getting a $10-million skills training boost for new and current employees at its trio of locations in St. Catharines, Hamilton and Thunder Bay.
Premier Doug Ford made the announcement Wednesday in St. Catharines at the company's Port Weller facility with employees, city officials and business people on hand.
"This investment will help reskill and upskill existing workers for careers in the millwright, welding, electrical and painting trades and many more," said Ford, accompanied by Labour, Immigration, Training and Skills Development Minister David Piccini and Niagara West MPP Sam Oosterhoff.
"It will give new workers hands-on experience in shipbuilding and ship repair, and it will help recruit and retain the next generation of workers and leaders in our shipbuilding industry."
The $10 million is being provided through the province's Skills Development Fund training stream.
Ontario Shipyards, which changed its name from Heddle earlier this year, has about 250 employees across the three sites, with 130 at the St. Catharines shipyard.
The company previously received $3.7 million from the Skills Development Fund in July 2023 for training and modernization in Hamilton and Port Weller.
Shaun Padulo, president and chief executive officer of Ontario Shipyards, said the Ford government has played a pivotal role in the revitalization of the shipyards, allowing the company to advance multiple phases of the Ontario Shipyard Modernization Project.
"On a personal note, the Skills Development Fund has been crucial during times when work was scarce, allowing us to keep our workforce intact through various training initiatives," he said.
"This support has directly benefited over 250 families, ensuring their stability, and many of those families are represented by the fine folks right behind me here."
Ted Kirkpatrick, director of Ontario Shipyards business development and government relations, said afterwards the funds really are helping to bring shipbuilding back to Ontario, explaining the impact of having to train a workforce.
The company signed a long-term lease at the Port Weller dry docks in 2017 when the site was dormant and had been through years of instability.
"At one point when we took these facilities over, we took them over out of bankruptcy. So it's a tremendous amount of not just capital in terms of what you need to build and to buy equipment, but to train the people that are no longer there," he said.
"Today's announcement, it's hugely important to rebuilding this industry in Ontario."
Kirkpatrick said the fund will be used to train current employees who want to increase their skills, as well as for people who may have no experience in shipbuilding to bring them into apprenticeship programs through the various trades at the shipyard.
"We employ a vast array of tradespeople. And that's why this money is so effective - we can deploy that across all layers of skills."
Jordan Sherlock, executive director of Niagara Industrial Association, was at the announcement and said afterwards the government's fund takes the risk out of employer-led training.
"It makes it a lot easier for large and small businesses to train and bring in employees that they need to do what they have to do without having to incur the cost and the risk," he said.
"There's a lot of risk associated with workforce development today so de-risking that is is a huge advantage for our industrial folks."
Mayor Mat Siscoe told people gathered that shipbuilding and ship repair have played a vital role in St. Catharines for generations and has become a cornerstone of the local economy and a key contributor to the broader national economic landscape.
He said he was excited to see another investment in "upskilling" workers in St. Catharines and the Niagara region.
"We know that our industrial base is capable of meeting the demands of the 21st century with the right training opportunities," he said during the announcement.
"The provincial government's investment is a welcome step for the workers of our community, and it's a clear demonstration of their dedication to empowering individuals to thrive in the modern workforce by providing them with the necessary tools and the knowledge that they need to succeed."
The province launched its Skills Development Fund in 2021.
Applications are open for the next round of funding from the SDF training stream.
CAPTION: Doug Ford, with St. Catharines Mayor Mat Siscoe, meets with workers at Ontario Shipyards' Port Weller facility while announcing the Ontario government is investing $10 million to train and upskill workers in the shipbuilding and repair sector.Premier Doug Ford, with Labour, Immigration, Training and Skills Development Minister David Piccini, announces the Ontario government is investing $10 million through its Skills Development Fund with Ontario Shipyards Inc. to train and upskill workers.Bob Tymczyszyn The St. Catharines Standard Bob Tymczyszyn St. Catharines Standard

CREDIT: Karena Walter Reporter

Readers write Letters to the editor

Full text: Home-care contracts disturbing
The entire process of choosing home-care contractors needs an independent sober second look, Aug. 13
This column raises many concerns for the elderly and Ontario citizens at large. As Ontario ages, home care is becoming a vital source of assistance to many.
We as taxpayers should demand more of our provincial government in regards to transparency and accountability.
Once again, Premier Doug Ford and his Progressive Conservative government are back to their old tactics. As proven during COVID-19, our for-profit long-term-care homes are in great need of better regulation and frankly a complete overhaul to better protect our aging population.
It seems the provincial government is now going after the home-care system. As many elders are choosing to stay at home as long as possible, there is an urgent need for these services.
Just released for 2024, the newly introduced pre-qualified home-care providers list proves, again, the questionable actions of our provincial government. Instead of issuing these lucrative contracts to qualified organizations, especially non-profits, it appears the PCs wish to continue taking care of their friends.
One might ask the government to explain how recently a big home-care contract was awarded to Care Company Inc., owned by none other than Laura Harris, wife of former Ontario PC premier Mike Harris?
Taxpayers of our province should be enraged that this continues. There are other examples, as the column illustrates.
The people of Ontario deserve much better in regards to long-term care and home care.
Fred Audibert, Niagara Falls
Niagara Filipinos
celebrate gold
Filipino gymnast who won 2 Olympic golds in Paris gets hero's welcome and free buffets for life, Aug. 13
Thank you for spotlighting Philippines participation in the Paris Olympics.
Imagine how proud the whole country of 7,000-plus islands (population 119 million), the almost 2 million expatriates and residents of Canada and the over 2 million overseas Filipino workers in 100 countries are about the two-gold performance of gymnast Carlos Yulo and the bronze medals of Nesthy Petecio and Aira Villegas in women's boxing.
No wonder these remarkable athletes from humble beginnings are rewarded and feted as national heroes by both the Philippine government and their compatriots, much like they did to Hidilyn Diaz, who won gold in women's weightlifting at the 2020 Tokyo Olympics.
Filipinos have also excelled in sports like boxing (Manny Pacquiao, Pancho Villa et al.), 10-pin bowling and billiards, to name a few. However, basketball and volleyball are still the most popular sports in the islands.
Besides world-famous tourist beaches and mega-malls, the Philippines also has the distinction of sending abroad high-calibre nurses, musicians, singers and foreign workers. Let's hope for a brighter future for the Republic of the Philippines, a bastion of democracy and Christianity in Southeast Asia.
Renato F. Aoanan, Niagara Filipino-Canadian Seniors Social Club

Readers write Letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write-letters-editor/docview/3091535719/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write+Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-08-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Voting against
hallway medicine
Len King could walk and talk when he waved goodbye to his family from the ambulance. He ended up bedridden, incoherent and died in hospital, Aug. 7.
I wept when I read the story describing the catastrophic care Len King endured at Brantford General Hospital, strapped to a hallway gurney, drugged out of his mind by medications contraindicated by his condition, while desperately waiting 33 hours for a treatment as simple as IV fluids, given so he would just calm the heck down, instead of treating the UTI and dehydration he presented with.
He must have been terrified.
When my mother, who had Alzheimer's disease for many years, reached the end of her life and chose to stop eating, I was offered the opportunity to transfer her to the ER, but knowing Doug Ford's hallway medicine awaited her, I refused.
Reading Mr. King's tragic tale only affirmed my belief I made the right decision, and the registered nurses, licensed practical nurses, personal support workers and others (most of whom were new Canadians, just to give them a little recognition for doing a difficult job) of Extendicare gave my mom professional, loving care, so she spent not one second in terror or delirium like Mr. King, for which I will be forever grateful.
His story should alarm all of us, but mostly those who plan to vote Progressive Conservative in the next provincial election, for this is the fate that awaits you or a senior you love.
Kay Holly, St. Catharines
Remembering
Rev. Burns
Zion Baptist Church plaque to honour legacy serving Black freedom seekers in Niagara, Aug. 1
Reporter Victoria Nicolaou is to be commended for her informative and engaging article about Zion Baptist Church and Rev. Anthony Burns. It coincided appropriately, as July 27 was the anniversary date of his death in 1862.
Rev. Burns and his church should not be forgotten but rather, remembered as an integral chapter in our Black history. It is welcome news to read that the city is planning to prepare and place a heritage plaque, temporarily, at space kindly provided by the nearby venerable BME Salem Chapel on Geneva Street, until a permanent location is selected at the original site.
May the story of the Zion Baptist Church and Rev. Anthony Burns become better known.
John Burtniak, St. Catharines
Our weather
is worsening
Researchers confirm south Niagara tornado, Aug. 7
We have seen a lot of very bad storms lately.
Unfortunately, it is not going to get better with ongoing hurricanes, tornadoes and very heavy rains caused by rising water temperatures in oceans, the Gulf of Mexico and the Great Lakes. The warm water feeds the storms.
There should be a method to reduce high water temperatures or it's just going to keep getting worse.
Roscoe Reilly, Welland

Document 144 of 249

One year later, Ford's tone has shifted: Premier may be ready to move on, but political opponents aren't and an investigation continues

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/one-year-later-fords-tone-has-shifted/docview/3091093830/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=One+year+later%2C+Ford%27s+tone+has+shifted%3A+Premier+may+be+ready+to+move+on%2C+but+political+opponents+aren%27t+and+an+investigation+continues&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-08-10&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Contrition may not quite be what it once was.
With Friday marking the first anniversary of the explosive auditor general's report into the Greenbelt land swap, Premier Doug Ford was asked about the lessons learned from a scandal that still looms over his Progressive Conservative government.
Ford shrugged at the reporter's question.
"We made that decision not to move forward with it - and we're going keep moving forward and building homes everywhere other than the Greenbelt," he said Wednesday in Mississauga.
"So that's it."
While the premier conceded his government was still in the process of implementing all 15 recommendations from then-auditor general Bonnie Lysyk's report, his tone was a far cry from the plaintive plea when he cancelled the land deal at the end of last summer.
"It was a mistake to open the Greenbelt. I'm very, very sorry. I made a promise to you that I wouldn't touch the Greenbelt. I broke that promise," Ford said on Sept. 21, 2023.
That was when he announced 7,400 acres of environmentally sensitive land would no longer be removed from the two-million-acre Greenbelt around the Greater Toronto and Hamilton Area.
"As a first step to earning back your trust, I'll be reversing the changes. We moved too quickly and we made the wrong decision ... it caused people to question our motives," he said at the time.
It was Lysyk's report - and a separate review by integrity commissioner J. David Wake released on Aug. 30, 2023 - that set in motion events that dog Ford's government to this day.
The RCMP "O" Division's Sensitive and International Investigations unit, the branch that investigates corruption and political crimes, has an ongoing probe of the affair.
Lysyk, who retired last September after a 10-year term as the financial watchdog, told the Star this week she has not been contacted by the RCMP.
The reports by her and Wake are widely viewed as potential road maps for police investigators because the independent legislative watchdogs had access to people who do not necessarily have to play ball with the Mounties.
She found developers with ties to Ford's Tories were "favoured" in the process led by then-housing minister Steve Clark's chief of staff, Ryan Amato, and said land they owned that was selected to be opened for housing construction could have soared in value to $8.28 billion as a result.
Amato, who resigned last August, has always denied any wrongdoing and his supporters at Queen's Park - including some past and present PC cabinet ministers - believe he has been unfairly scapegoated.
Clark, who has also insisted he did nothing wrong and has promised to co-operate with the Mounties, stepped down from cabinet last September, languishing as a backbencher for nine months until Ford promoted him to government house leader in the June 6 shuffle.
"He's one of the most experienced and qualified members we have - he knows Queen's Park inside and out. A very credible individual and I support him 1,000 per cent," the unrepentant premier said earlier this week.
Others seen as collateral damage remain politically sidelined.
Kaleed Rasheed resigned as Ford's business minister last September.
Rasheed no longer sits as a Tory MPP after misleading Wake about a 2020 Las Vegas trip with Shakir Rehmatullah, a developer who attended Ford's daughter's wedding and who had a stake in about 23 acres of land removed from the Greenbelt near Markham and Whitchurch-Stouffville.
The affable Mississauga East-Cooksville Independent MPP maintains he made "an honest mistake" on the date of the Vegas jaunt and is trying to return to the PC fold.
Jae Truesdell, who was also on that trip, quit as Ford's housing adviser after Rasheed's defenestration.
The imbroglio has remained in the headlines - eight weeks ago, the Star and the Narwhal shared the Michener Award, Canada's most prestigious journalism prize, for their work examining the Greenbelt affair.
A joint Star and Narwhal investigation found eight of the 15 parcels of land affected had been bought up by developers after Ford's election in 2018 - when the properties were still protected.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles said Thursday that "people power, investigative journalism and a strong NDP opposition helped thwart Ford's plan to carve up the Greenbelt, but he hasn't learned a thing."
"A year later, the minister who took the fall is back ... and Ford is still making backroom deals that benefit wealthy developers over everyone else," she said.
Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie said, "Doug Ford isn't sorry for trying to give the Greenbelt away to his rich friends, he's only sorry he got caught."
"He even brought his friend, Steve Clark, the Greenbelt fall guy, back," said Crombie.
Green Leader Mike Schreiner expressed concern Thursday that "the premier has not learned from the Greenbelt scandal."
"He is still refusing to legalize gentle density to build homes people can afford in the communities they love," said Schreiner.
"And he is destroying farmland by imposing urban boundary expansions in places like Waterloo region that threaten the land that feeds us."
CAPTION: Those implicated in last year's auditor general and integrity commissioner reports into the Greenbelt land swap have been politically sidelined, including then minister of housing Steve Clark, shown with Premier Doug Ford last August.The Canadian Press file photo

CREDIT: Robert Benzie Toronto Star

Subject: Criminal investigations; Scandals

Document 145 of 249

Poilievre pitches his 'common-sense' plan in Niagara: Conservative party leader tours sites across region touting promises if elected

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/poilievre-pitches-his-common-sense-plan-niagara/docview/3090836224/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Poilievre+pitches+his+%27common-sense%27+plan+in+Niagara%3A+Conservative+party+leader+tours+sites+across+region+touting+promises+if+elected&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-08-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Pierre Poilievre is pointing to a significant housing cost difference on both sides of the international border in Niagara as an example of the housing crisis in Canada.
"Housing costs have literally doubled - in this region closer to tripled," the federal Conservative leader said after a tour of Ontario Shipyards in St. Catharines on Thursday.
"If you go across the border from Niagara, Ont., to Niagara, N.Y., you find the housing on this side of the border is three times as expensive as it is in the U.S.A."
Poilievre did not provide a source for his housing statistics.
He said "life was not like this" before the Liberal party came into power nine years ago.
"Right now, we have the fewest homes per capita of any country in the G7, even though we have the most land to build on. Why is that? Because we have the worst bureaucracy. It takes three times longer to get a building permit in Canada than in the U.S. and in the U.K."
Although Niagara Falls, N.Y., and Ontario are only minutes and a river apart, there are various socio-economic factors at play that make comparisons difficult.
Poilievre said "fees, charges, taxes, delays, consultants, lawyers and other paper costs" add more to the cost of a home than anything else.
"You spend more on bureaucrats when you buy a home than you do on carpenters, electricians and plumbers who actually build it."
Poilievre said a Conservative government would require municipalities to speed up permits, free up land and cut building taxes to allow for the building of 15 per cent more housing per year as a condition of getting federal funds.
"We'll sell off 6,000 federal buildings, thousands of acres of federal land to build, build, build and we'll back the trades because we need boots and not suits to build the homes," he said.
"We'll put more red-seal materials in our high schools so kids can start readying for an apprenticeship before they even graduate. We'll make sure that the lion's share of federal education and training dollars goes to training centres, polytechnics, career colleges - not just to universities."
Poilievre said his government would also bring in "tax fairness."
"CEOs can write off the cost of their private jet; you should be able to write off the cost of going from one job site to the next," he said.
"If you're a pipe fitter from Timmins and you're going to Fort Mac, Alberta, you should be able to write that off because you did that trip to earn a salary."
Poilievre described his "axe the (carbon) tax, build the homes, fix the budget and stop the crime" agenda as a "common-sense" plan.
He said removing the carbon tax will bring down the cost of gas, heat and grocery bills, but also "bring home" production.
"High taxes have sent production of our steel and manufacturing to other countries around the world. Half a trillion Canadian investment dollars have gone to the States, more than have come back. That's pipelines, shipyards, factories, warehouses, business centres, all being built with Canadian dollars to pay American wages. I want to bring that money back to Canada and we can by axing the carbon tax, lowering tax on work, investment and making stuff in Canada."
Beyond cutting the carbon tax, Poilievre did not discuss what the Conservative policy is for climate change and the environment.
Poilievre said to pay for tax relief, Ottawa needs to get its spending "under control."
"The debt has doubled in the last nine years and that means we're spending more on interest than we are on health care," he said.
"My common-sense plan will cap government spending and cut government waste. We'll bring in a dollar-for-dollar law that requires politicians find $1 of savings every time they bring in a new dollar of spending - just like single moms, small businesses and seniors manage their budgets. We'll cut back on the waste and we'll use the savings to balance budgets, bring down interest rates, inflation and income tax, so that you bring home more."
Poilievre made several stops in Niagara Thursday to hear from workers about challenges they're facing and to speak to them about his plan.
In addition to Ontario Shipyards on Lakeshore Road in St. Catharines, Poilievre made stops in Niagara Falls, Welland, Stevensville and Niagara-on-the-Lake.
Asked about Niagara-specific issues during an interview, Poilievre said as prime minister he would "look" at Niagara Region's $400-million south Niagara wastewater treatment plant project in Niagara Falls, which regional officials hope will be a partnership between Niagara, the provincial and federal governments.
Earlier this year, Region Chair Jim Bradley said provincial officials are "very interested in having a three-way conversation" on the project, adding the Environment Ministry was "very supportive" and would send a letter on its behalf to the federal government.
Asked about how the federal government can help promote tourism, Poilievre said his plan to cut taxes would help to "revitalize" the sector.
" We need to lower taxes for the workers so that they can earn a more powerful paycheque as waitresses, as reception-desk workers at hotels, and in all the other tourism-related jobs and we need to unleash more investment in Canada with a bring-it-home tax cut."
In an interview this week, Niagara Parks chief executive officer David Adames said this summer's tourism season is "exceeding expectations," with his agency seeing as much as a 12 per cent increase in visitors and revenue.
CAPTION: Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, centre, at a tour of Ontario Shipyards on Lakeshore Road in St. Catharines.Julie Jocsak St. Catharines Standard

CREDIT: Ray Spiteri Reporter

Subject: Taxes; Government waste; Cost control; Budgets; Tourism; Carbon; Climate change; Environmental tax

Document 146 of 249

Readers write Letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write-letters-editor/docview/3089767768/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write+Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-08-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Health system
failing in Ontario
Doctor shortage contributing to Niagara's high amputation rate, July 29
We did not have to come to this.
Imagine the world you want to live in. We had a health-care system that placed people ahead of ability to pay. We had a system with community doctors, hospitals and medical clinics anyone could reach.
Now? Hospitals, emergency rooms, urgent-care facilities are closing and we are being sent to the pharmacy to access medical care.
We do not have enough family doctors, general practitioners, nurses, nurse practitioners, diagnostic staff, personal support workers, hospital staff in general to keep local hospitals running. What happened?
This is 2024 and people are losing limbs due to chronic conditions because they cannot be seen by doctors. People are dying alone and afraid to speak out.
We cannot wait for our government to prioritize health care, not just the building of new hospitals but the actual care we need.
We are being left behind and younger generations have no idea what is in store. Our community hospitals can help reduce wait times and the hallway medicine being practised in large emergency rooms.
Open our doors, open beds, finance training and education for our medical needs now and into the future.
Heather Kelley, Ridgeway
Service duplication choking us
Region's 2025 budget process starts at 8.72% tax hike, Aug. 2
Here we go again, a preliminary municipal property tax proposal, going up at a rate of over two times the rate of inflation, and that's only the regional portion.
This doesn't fit with Ontario Premier Doug Ford's plan of making buying and renting housing more affordable.
I don't know why people don't get it, but the public sector is growing faster than the private sector with many public sector workers getting the biggest raises, benefit packages with decent pensions. The public sector is making life unaffordable for seniors and those trying to eke out a living in the private sector.
I'm a homeowner and I'm mortgage-free and it still costs me at least $1,000 a month to keep the lights on, and that does not include home repairs.
Maybe we will finally wake up to the notion that duplication of services in Niagara, under four levels of government, is choking us.
Peter Voss, Welland
Keep publishing
I want to thank the newspaper for allowing letters to the editor from the public.
If you have something to say put your thoughts out there. With so much division in Canada and the U.S. we need people to put forward fact-based opinions.
If a politician refuses to be interviewed by the mainstream media, why vote for someone with no answers?
Social media can be manipulated far easier than the Toronto Star, Globe and Mail or the local Standard, Tribune and Review.
Keep the presses running.
Russ Young, Fort Erie

Document 147 of 249

Niagarans travelling to GTA, U.S. for more timely MRIs

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/niagarans-travelling-gta-u-s-more-timely-mris/docview/3086771754/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Niagarans+travelling+to+GTA%2C+U.S.+for+more+timely+MRIs&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-08-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: St. Catharines senior Colleen Krasij had a choice to make.
Wait about a year for an MRI scan in Niagara to look into the lower back pain she's been experiencing.
Or travel much sooner to Mississauga and get a scan at 1:30 a.m.
Although less convenient, and more costly, Krasij chose to leave the region for the diagnostic test a couple of weeks ago so that she could see her doctor for a followup appointment in August.
"They say they're all backlogged (in Niagara)," said her husband, John, adding while the issue his wife has been dealing with may not be considered an emergency from an MRI priority standpoint, his wife's pain has been difficult to live with.
"We had to stay overnight. Now you've got to pay for a hotel. We've got a hospital here in St. Catharines that they built (11 years ago). Niagara Falls, they're building a (larger) hospital. Why do we have to go out (of the region) at different hours of the day when we have facilities in the Niagara area?"
John said while he and his wife were waiting in Mississauga, they heard from another St. Catharines resident who also had an early morning MRI scheduled and had to be back in Niagara for work at 8 a.m.
Niagara Falls MPP Wayne Gates said he has heard from several constituents who have similar stories.
In some cases, he has heard from Niagara residents travelling to the United States to get "timely access" to an MRI.
"They would be forced to wait upwards of a year to get an MRI appointment in Niagara. I have heard directly from seniors who have had to book hotel rooms in the GTA so they could get an MRI appointment as soon as possible, because they couldn't access this service close to home in Niagara," said the New Democrat.
Gates reiterated his call for the provincial government to provide additional funding and resources to address wait times.
"The provincial government must ensure residents across Ontario, including in Niagara, have 24/7 access to MRI and CT services and must address the shortage of MRI technologists," he said.
"This is a problem that must be addressed through investment in front-line public health care - not more privatization."
In a letter sent earlier this month to Premier Doug Ford and Health Minister Sylvia Jones, Gates said some of his constituents have paid out of pocket to get the care they need outside of Niagara.
He said according to Ontario Health's publicly available data, the provincial target for a Priority 4 patient is within 28 days, but the Ontario provincial average as of May was a wait of 92 days.
Gates said at Greater Niagara General Hospital in Niagara Falls, the average wait time for Priority 4 patients was 277 days, or 213 days at St. Catharines hospital.
All MRI requests are assigned a priority level between one and four.
Priority 1 means emergency, so those patients are seen immediately and are not captured in wait-time data.
Priority levels 2, 3 and 4 are determined by surgeons, specialists and other health-care providers to guide treatment decisions and improve patient access and outcomes.
In an emailed statement, Hannah Jensen, a spokesperson for Jones, said Ontario is "proud" to have one of the largest publicly funded health-care systems in the world, and under the current government Ontario has made "record" investments.
"Since 2018, we have increased the health-care budget by over 31 per cent, investing over $85 billion into the system this year alone," said Jensen.
"This includes a four-per-cent increase to the hospital sector."
She said the government is increasing publicly funded diagnostic imaging capacity by 97,767 MRI and 116,443 CT operating hours, adding 32,000 OHIP-covered cataract surgeries, eliminating a 180,000-person Pap test backlog and is "achieving some of the shortest wait times of any province in Canada, with nearly 80 per cent of people receiving their procedure within clinically recommended target times."
"In Niagara, our government is getting shovels in the ground for the new Niagara south hospital, adding 500 new beds to the region and improving access to services like diagnostic testing."
Jensen said the government is adding 100,000 publicly funded scans and will help to reduce wait times for MRI and CT scans to 28 days in every region of Ontario to "make it faster and easier for patients across the province to access the care they need, when they need it."
Niagara West Progressive Conservative MPP Sam Oosterhoff said the government also increased MRI capacity by 50 per cent in the region by providing operating funding to Niagara Health for a third MRI machine.
Last month, when the province announced plans to nearly double the number of publicly funded MRI and CT scans through private clinics, Oosterhoff said the government will "always ensure that people in Ontario and Niagara can access health care with their OHIP card, never their credit card."
Right now, there are seven such clinics in the province that provide one, or both, of the diagnostic imaging services.
"Calls for community surgical and diagnostic applications opened this past June for new licences expected to be issued starting in fall 2024 that will connect nearly 100,000 more people to scans each year," said Oosterhoff.
"I am confident these bold provincial initiatives will increase capacity in Niagara in the months and years to come and make it faster and easier for patients to access the care they need, when they need it."
CAPTION: Niagara Falls NDP MPP Wayne Gates says his office has heard from several constituents who have had to travel as far as Mississauga, and in some cases the United States, to get "timely access" to an MRI scan.Dreamstime photo

CREDIT: Ray Spiteri Reporter

Letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters-editor/docview/3086243620/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Full text: All quiet on the reform front
Re: Seven months ago, a
governance reform hearing was held in Niagara.
Since then, silence, July 26
As has been the case many times in the past, all discussions regarding any type of governance reform have again gone quiet.
Let's not forget many of thosepeople who presented at the public hearings in January were politicians who noted the current governance system was not working and wanted to see changes.
Although local politicians have the capacity to make governance changes, there doesn't appear to be any motivation by local politicians to do so.
Let's not forget, Niagara Region Chair Jim Bradley and his council had the opportunity to hear from citizens, but council pumped the brakes on any motion to discuss a directly elected regional chair and rejected the motion to develop a citizen group to solicit input on governance structure.
There was also a group of small businesses who presented to regional council about being taxed in an unsustainable manner, asking for some assistance. Instead of debating the issue, regional council deferred it.
We have acknowledgment from the current government of Premier Doug Ford that we have way too many politicians in Niagara. We also have local politicians who have indicated Niagara has too many politicians and the current governance structure is not working.
Niagara regional council is not interested in hearing about any changes and would prefer to drag its feet and do nothing. The province needs to step in.
Allen McKay, St. Catharines
We're still waiting
Re: Understanding surgical wait times in Niagara, July 26
I read with interest the opinion piece regarding surgery wait times in Niagara. That as patients we need to wait for Ontario's health system to sort out its dilemma and its problem.
Just stop it.
It seems we have done nothing but wait for you to straighten out the need for more anesthetists, the need for more doctors, the need for more technologists. Now we are waiting for staff to be trained.
Did we not see this coming, did we not plan and prepare?
I get worried when boards and our public leadership tell me they are working out a plan. In one instance, it seems, public administrators may have finally heard the patients screaming for timely care - but at what cost to our health?
Instead of funding our community hospitals and the staff (where we had one-stop shopping), we are now selling out delivery of care to pharmacies or private clinics and closing viable sites and still have no quicker access to care.
Shame on all of you for not properly funding and delivering the care we have paid for. Come talk to the people that matter, the people waiting for care.
Heather Kelley, Ridgeway

Document 149 of 249

Hudak leaving provincial real estate association

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/hudak-leaving-provincial-real-estate-association/docview/3086241875/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Hudak+leaving+provincial+real+estate+association&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-07-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: After seven years advocating for homeowners and affordable housing as chief executive officer of Ontario Real Estate Association (OREA), Tim Hudak announced this week he is leaving the organization Friday.
Although Hudak, 56, would not reveal his future plans, he said in a statement he will be "tackling new adventures in the fall."
A former leader of the provincial Progressive Conservatives (2009-14), Hudak said he was recruited to lead OREA at a time when he was leaving public office and planned to spend about five years with the organization "delivering high impact advocacy, greater profile for the organization and refreshed, practical products to support realtors as they connect people with a great place to call home."
During his time with OREA, Hudak said the organization became a "leading champion for homeowners and those who aspire to join their ranks."
He said the province's Trust in Real Estate Services Act also made Ontario the North American leader in professional standards and consumer protection with new business tools.
The organization also worked to convince governments to pass legislation to get more affordable homes built faster, developed a program to provide health and insurance benefits to realtors, and streamlined the organization by reducing middle management while focusing on "doing fewer things better."
"With these successes in mind, earlier this year I advised the board of directors of my intention to leave OREA this summer," Hudak said in the statement.
In an email, OREA spokesperson Jean-Adrien Delicano said Hudak, who grew up in Fort Erie and was first elected as the MPP for the former Niagara South riding in 1995 at the age of 27, was not available for an interview.
In the statement, Hudak said he will be "forever a champion of the Canadian dream of home ownership and the extraordinary work Ontario realtors do in helping people get the keys to a great home."
"I look forward to spending August with my family and tackling new adventures in the fall," he said.
CAPTION: Tim Hudak is stepping down as the Ontario Real Estate Associa-tion's chief executive officer.

Letters to the editor

Full text: Province's alcohol sales policy a long-term loser
Re: 36 Niagara convenience stores, 28 in Niagara Falls, set to sell alcohol in September, July 16
I find it disturbing that our provincial government, led by Premier Doug Ford, is willing to pay $225 million to get out of a contract with the Beer Store. The government is not just paying this sum. The cost of breaking this deal will come close to $1 billion when direct payouts to the Beer Store, grocery chains and convenience store owners, as well as lost revenue for LCBO, are added in.
My elderly mother spent the night, along with three other patients, on a stretcher in the hallway of the Greater Niagara General Hospital emergency room last week.
No beds were available.
Those funds spent breaking that Beer Store contract would be better spent on our struggling health-care system.
Excuse me if I don't bemoan the "inconvenience" of going to the LCBO store for your beverages. And, yes, I drink.
I am happy to live with having to go to the LCBO, but spending another full night in the glaring light of the emergency room hallway is unacceptable.
As a side note, Ford is making it impossible for any government to be successful in the future without increasing taxes significantly. Eliminating the income from LCBO, as well as the licence renewal fees, etc., will leave the provincial coffers a lot emptier.
D. White, Niagara Falls
It's the same Pierre Poilievre, despite the
silky makeover
Re: Poilievre says Ontario teenager's killing shows Liberal, NDP policies have failed, July 25
Ignoring the fact that federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre's inflammatory remarks are hurting the family of the victim and are inflaming those in the community who are prone to taking extreme measures, he is typically seizing the opportunity to score political points.
There is an old saying that "You can't make a silk purse out of a sow's ear" - meaning in, Poilievre's case, that if you are born a right-wing ideologue and get a better haircut, a better tailor and contact lenses, this changes nothing.
Using tactics learned from those developed by the Trump machine, the Conservatives have no interest in helping Canadians by finding common ground to solve the major problems facing the people today.
They would rather blame their opponents for anything that goes wrong, not even attempting to try to understand the other side's perspective.
Forget the makeover.
This is the same Pierre Poilievre, this is the same person that was perched on the bumper of a tractor trailer in Ottawa, sharing a drink with those breaking the law.
He is still Stephen Harper's "attack poodle."
Richard Murri, Niagara Falls

Document 151 of 249

A look at one year of strong mayor powers: Siscoe says reaction mostly positive

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/look-at-one-year-strong-mayor-powers/docview/3083464586/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+look+at+one+year+of+strong+mayor+powers%3A+Siscoe+says+reaction+mostly+positive&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-07-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In the year since so-called strong mayor powers were granted to the heads of council in a swath of Ontario municipalities, most mayors have used them sparingly - if at all - though in some corners a sense of unease with the sweeping authority remains.
St. Catharines Mayor Mat Siscoe, who has used the powers a few times including directing staff to prioritize development applications, said the reception to the powers has been largely positive, though he understands some people's hesitation.
"When the powers came in there was a lot of confusion as to what exactly they could be," he said.
"I had folks in the development industry coming forward and saying, 'Well, you know, I'm running into this problem. I need you to use your strong mayor powers to get me past this point.' It's like, 'No, your issue, sir, (is) building code related, and you actually have to meet the building code. I can't waive that.' "
As of this month, nearly 30 mayors have had the ability for a year or more to propose bylaws and pass them with the support of one-third of councillors, veto bylaws and hire and fire department heads, among other powers.
Premier Doug Ford's government later doled out the powers to many more mayors, even when they were not interested in receiving them, and Ontario now has a total of 46 strong mayors.
Many of them are in the province's largest cities, and the chair of the Ontario Big City Mayors group said by and large the mayors have "exercised enormous restraint and responsibility" in exercising the powers.
"Where they've used some of those discretionary tools it's been after careful thought and consideration of the best interest, long term, of the community," said Marianne Meed Ward, mayor of Burlington.
Some of the higher-profile uses include Hamilton Mayor Andrea Horwath using the powers to advance an affordable housing development on two municipal parking lots, and then-Mississauga mayor Bonnie Crombie using them in favour of fourplexes.
In Caledon, Mayor Annette Groves recently rescinded her use of strong mayor powers to push forward 12 rezoning applications for 35,000 homes after the move caused pushback in her community. She said she will instead have the issue go through the regular council process, but defended the usage.
"I am not abusing the use of strong mayor powers," she said. "I believe that I've used it only where it's necessary to carry on ... the priorities of the province to get housing built."
The provincial government framed the powers as a set of tools in service of reaching the goal of building 1.5 million homes in Ontario by 2031. Time is ticking by, Groves said, and municipalities like Caledon need to meet current and future housing needs.
"If we don't start planning, and we don't start getting ahead of this growth, the growth is going to get ahead of us," she said.
The strong mayor powers are broad, and only a few of them directly relate to housing, in law. The powers to propose and pass a bylaw with one-third of council support and to veto a bylaw must relate to building housing or related infrastructure.
The law also allows mayors to direct staff to conduct research and write reports, as well as appoint the chief administrative officer, department heads, chairs and vice-chairs of local boards, and establish and dissolve committees, though they can delegate those powers to council.
The majority of items on municipalities' websites listing uses of the powers are the mayor "approving" a bylaw - in other words, indicating they will not veto it.
Mayors are required under the law to prepare and propose a municipal budget, though some say they are in effect still working collaboratively with their council. Other mayors have used the strong mayor budgetary powers to impose a cap on property tax increases, institute a property tax deferral for seniors, reopen the document to add millions in new spending to revitalize a downtown, and add funding to put "Aurora" in capital letters outside that community's town hall, similar to what is seen in Toronto.
Rachel Gilliland, a councillor in Aurora, said there is too much grey area in what the strong mayor powers can be used for.
"Strong mayor powers to me, really (do) attack democracy," she said. "It certainly has not, in my opinion, done what it's supposed to be doing, at least in Aurora."
Aurora's council received a legal opinion that the decisions are not reviewable or appealable, and the Ministry of Municipal Affairs and Housing has said it is up to the municipalities to determine if the uses of the powers are in accordance with the law.
"(That) just really opens up Pandora's box," Gilliland, said. "It doesn't matter what the mayor vetoes or what the mayor decides. The mayor has the sole discretion and control to do whatever they so choose."
Ajax Mayor Shaun Collier, who has used the powers for housing projects involving more than 4,000 units, suggested the accountability mechanism for the strong mayor powers is the election cycle.
"People ask me, 'Did you consult on certain things?'" he said. "Well, our consultation is every four years. It's called an election. And if you don't like what we're doing, then that's your opportunity to change."
Collier said the powers have been "incredibly helpful" in speeding up the creation of housing in Ajax. In one case he used them to approve up to 62 storeys for two residential towers near a GO Transit station even though the town's official plan has 25 storeys as the maximum. In another case a multi-residential development made design changes that resulted in the loss of some parking spaces and Collier used the powers to ensure the project didn't have to go back through a committee of adjustment.
Leanne Caron, a councillor in Guelph, is among those uneasy with the process. Guelph Mayor Cam Guthrie has used the powers a number of times, including to direct staff to research establishing a structured encampments site - which would involve tiny homes - and Caron said she supports what he has used them for, just not the actual use of the powers.
"Nothing that the mayor has done using strong mayor powers, in my opinion, is anything that wouldn't have happened with the full support of council," she said.
"We were all elected to have a voice in the direction our community goes and that's what the Municipal Act was designed to do, was to put the power in the collective, and not the power of one."
Guthrie said in the case of the encampments report, his directive does bypass the step of council debating whether they want the report to be done, but at the end of the day the council and community still debate the issue after the report is submitted.
"If we wanted to look at getting tiny homes, there was, in my mind, a very big issue of timing," he said. "We needed to order them and/or build them much prior to the winter coming."
Guthrie also noted that he delegated the personnel-related powers, saying it's important to have a divide between the administrative and political sides of city hall.
In Welland, Mayor Frank Campion used them to hire the city's new chief administrative officer.
The Association of Municipal Managers, Clerks and Treasurers of Ontario, which represents municipal professionals, said a little more than half of the strong mayors have retained the power to appoint the CAO and about 35 per cent of them retained the power to appoint department heads.
"Right from the outset, we were very concerned with the opportunity to essentially politicize the municipal public service and we remain concerned," said executive director Dave Arbuckle.
"Say a CAO is hired directly by a mayor. That staff is wondering where potentially that individual's loyalties lie. Is it to the municipality as a whole? Is it to council? Is it to just the mayor?"
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: St. Catharines Mayor Mat Siscoe has used strong mayor powers to help staff to prioritize development applications.Metroland file photo

CREDIT: Allison Jones The Canadian Press

Full text: Grocery code
of conduct a 'joke'
Government to provide $1.2 million to establish office of grocery code of conduct, July 19
The grocery code of conduct is a joke, too little too late. The damage has been done, people are suffering and many are not able to afford the nutrition they need to be healthy.
The government's language gives it away: "This is a positive step toward bringing more fairness, transparency and predictability to Canada's grocery supply chain and for consumers."
More fairness, transparency and predictability. What does that mean? Why not, simply, fairness, transparency and predictability, period? And it is a "positive step" toward bringing it but not actually bringing it.
I have previously suggested a comprehensive investigation be made of the grocery supply chain. Now, they're saying it but not saying it.
Aren't they even aware of the vagaries and mendacious character of their language? What a bunch.
There are only two conclusions to draw from, not just this little effort but for the vast majority of their efforts: Stupidity or complicit intention. In my opinion sometimes it's the one, sometimes the other.
Wayne Borbey,
Niagara Falls

36 Niagara convenience stores set to sell alcohol in September, July 16
I think Premier Doug Ford is marching to his own drummer, as he's certainly out of step with a majority of Ontarians.
His latest decisions are ill advised, hasty and thoughtless.
Most of us, if asked "what do you think Ontario needs" would answer hospitals, doctors, nurses, MRIs, CT scanners. Corner stores selling booze would be much further down the list.
I saw a sign the other day held by one of the LCBO folks that said "Doug Ford gives out maps to booze, but not to doctors."
Preacher Doug seems like a train that goes off the rails from time to time, and no one is able to get him back on track.
He obviously fails to realize what we, the voters, expect from a premier.
Pete Morro, St. Catharines

Instead of booze, could province spend on health care?, July 19
Booze over health care, an obsession with speed limits and a subway that you will need scuba gear for. Let's break down the Ford government's many mistakes.
Raising speed limits when aggressive driving is at an all-time high. Building a subway extension when sea-levels are rising is such a bad idea I will never take a subway anywhere again.
In Fort Erie we have 32,000 people who should rate an emergency room 24/7, but everything this bungling party does is just nonsensical.
Kiss our social safety net goodbye.
The list is much longer than the letter to the editor word count limit.
Russ Young, Fort Erie

Full text: Niagara's expensive transit boondoggle
continues
Dropping the word 'region' as too negative, Niagara Transit getting a fresh new look, July 17
At what cost is all this, to not only all Niagara taxpayers, but also those taxpayers in some of the 12 municipalities who receive absolutely no direct benefit from this latest regionally inspired boondoggle?
I've read stories talking of millions of dollars needed to replace the current fleet of buses, and this story talks of the many consultants engaged, because the newly employed senior and administrative staff for the Niagara (Regional?) Transit are unable to administer it without help.
And just how much will the simple matter of changing the logo cost us taxpayers?
The taxpayers within Niagara Region still have no idea of just what the bill will be for this bright new Niagara Transit system and no one in regional government seems to care.
You'd think all this new staff, along with their consultants, all employed and paid for by Niagara taxpayers, could assure all of us that Niagara Transit is, and will continue to be, financially self-sustaining, without demanding more from already overwhelmed and overtaxed residents.
The only honest comment in this whole "tale" is agreeing that the word "Region" is too negative.
How can absolutely anyone believe that this time, Niagara Region has got it right and it will cost Niagara taxpayers nothing, when they have never, in over 40 years, ever succeeded before?
Andrew Watts, Wainfleet
Let's get our
priorities straight
in Ontario
Internal poll for Doug Ford
finds strong support for booze
liberalization plan, July 16
As the province speeds up alcohol expansion, Premier Doug Ford insists it is keeping its promise to the people of Ontario for better access to alcohol.
How about the promise of governance reform in Niagara? During his election campaign, the premier was baffled by the number of politicians, 126 here, and encouraged local politicians to make changes.
What has happened since? Nothing, other than a steering committee meeting which took place earlier this year and heard from local politicians, many of whom agreed the current structure is not working. Nothing since.
More concerning, this governance structure supports a duplication in the administration of government services in Niagara costing local taxpayers millions more than it should.
Niagara Region does not need 13 CAOs or 13 finance departments, 13 planning departments and so on.
In the meantime, while a new report by Zoocasa has shown Niagara's property taxes are among the highest in Canada, the province has spent millions so residents can have better access to beer and ... is in the middle of an LCBO strike in order to do the same with liquor.
Let's get our priorities in order.
Allen McKay, St. Catharines

Full text: What's with Ford's
alcohol attraction
Doug Ford fast-tracks 'ready-to-drink' cocktails to grocery stores as LCBO strike continues, July 15

MRIs overused
'Disgusting' MRI wait,
Letters, July 12
Going back a few years, as far as I know MRIs were rarely requested by orthopedic surgeons.
My late wife had both hips replaced, one in 2014 and the other a few months later. No MRI was done.
I had an X-ray and ultrasound done for a shoulder concern, then was informed I needed an MRI. I was shocked when I got a call from a Mississauga clinic to confirm an appointment.
I declined as I feel MRIs should be reserved for more serious concerns. Also I think it's strange private operators can afford the equipment but local hospitals can't.
I have another shoulder concern and an MRI has been requested, after X-rays and ultrasound have been done.
I feel it's an abuse of the system and contributing to backlogs.
Don McIntee, Niagara Falls

Canadian leaders say democracy must prevail following Trump rally shooting, July 13
It is sad to see this in the country much of the world relies on for protection. One can't help wonder if Donald Trump and the Republicans will toughen their stance on guns, but his strong support from the NRA means that's not likely.
It is hard to comprehend how a 20-year-old can walk into a store and purchase 50 rounds of high-powered rifle ammunition. One would think the store clerk would be suspicious, notify authorities or refuse the sale.
This event is just the start of what is going to be six months of chaos in America.
Gerry MacCarthy, Niagara Falls

Full text: LCBO workers disrespected
by Ford
Doug Ford slams union over LCBO strike, says workers 'want a deal,' July 12
It is disgusting to see how Doug Ford disrespects hard- working Ontario workers.
His recent comment in regards to the striking workers at LCBO, "that ship has set sail," is a slap in the face to the workers who are trying to save their jobs.
This is not the first time Ford has used this comment as Ontario workers faced job losses.
Back in December of 2018, General Motors announced the closure of its Oshawa plant by December of 2019. Throughout 2019, we fought to save the plant and avoid thousands of job losses, not only at that plant, but also the loss of jobs at feeder plants and the trickle down effect to other Ontario workers.
This was an extremely stressful and pressure-filled time that affected the mental health of workers and their families. We had reached out to Doug Ford to assist with our fight to keep the plant open for the thousands of Ontario workers who were losing their jobs. His comments to those thousands of workers was, "That ship has set sail."
His recent recycled comment is a slap in the face to the hard- working LCBO employees who are currently on strike and to their families.
Doug Ford is definitely not for the little guy on the front lines, but he is for his buddies lining their pockets on the backs of the little guy.
Brent Roberts, Niagara Falls

priorities

What priorities this government has!
John Rowan, Welland

Document 156 of 249

Politicians urge calm following Trump shooting: Rhetoric all across Canada has become too inflamed, leaders of all parties say

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/politicians-urge-calm-following-trump-shooting/docview/3080953401/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Politicians+urge+calm+following+Trump+shooting%3A+Rhetoric+all+across+Canada+has+become+too+inflamed%2C+leaders+of+all+parties+say&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-07-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canadian politicians on opposite ends of the ideological spectrum stressed a need Monday to avoid inflaming discussion to the point of inciting violence against public figures.
An attempted assassination of former U.S. president Donald Trump at a weekend rally in Pennsylvania prompted concern and reflection north of the border, where politicians have seen a steady increase in threatening behaviour.
The shooting in Butler, Pa., left one person dead and two others critically injured. Officials said the gunman was killed by Secret Service personnel.
Trump, who was not seriously injured, is in Milwaukee for the Republican National Convention.
U.S. President Joe Biden said he had ordered an independent security review of the events leading up to the attack on his political rival.
The Prime Minister's Office said Sunday that Justin Trudeau spoke with Trump, saying there's no place for political violence and expressing condolences for the victims.
Public Safety Minister Dominic LeBlanc told reporters Monday he had been briefed by the RCMP and Canada's spy service.
"They went through the threat landscape," he said. "They talked to me about the additional vigilance that they've put in place since the assassination attempt."
LeBlanc said the government would not discuss specific security measures but said he is "confident that the RCMP will do what's necessary to protect elected leaders in Canada."
RCMP spokesperson Marie-Eve Breton said the force is in regular contact with domestic and international partners regarding protective matters.
In addition to the prime minister and Governor General, the RCMP is mandated to protect federal ministers and those designated by the public safety minister to receive protection on a case-by-case basis, she noted.
"Protective measures are intelligence-led and based on the latest risk and threat assessments, ongoing security considerations and a number of other factors."
LeBlanc said he exchanged text messages with Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre on Sunday to assure him that measures were in place.
In a radio interview Monday with Newstalk 1010, Poilievre said he had requested protection in response to menacing statements.
"Some of them are threats, some of them are implied threats and some of them are vitriol that you can tell lead to violence. And a lot of them have been targeted at my family," he said.
"There are whack jobs out there that could do something. We take all the precautions possible," he said, quickly adding: "I have to talk to people, and I'm not going to stop doing that."
NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh said it is wrong to inflame public debate to the point it ignites harassment or violence.
"I'm worried about the increase of that in the political forum in general. I'm worried about that polarization," he said Monday in Toronto.
"You should be able to raise concerns, you should be able to say that you don't agree with a policy and that you're really upset at it. But it should not be that we treat political opponents as enemies."
Alberta Premier Danielle Smith said Monday the way in which conservative politicians have been characterized is outrageous, alleging it has led to the current atmosphere in the U.S.
"I certainly hope that progressive politicians here are careful of their language, because they've been talking about conservative politicians in the same way and they need to dial it down."
Finger-pointing, name-calling and violent episodes have made elected officials of all political stripes wary in Canada.
A Manitoba man drove a truck onto the grounds of the Governor General's official residence in July 2020 and crashed through the gate. He intended to arrest Trudeau to make a statement about the government's COVID-19 restrictions and its ban on assault-style firearms. He ultimately pleaded guilty to seven weapons-related charges.
A St. Thomas, Ont., man pleaded guilty to common assault for throwing gravel at Trudeau during an election campaign stop in September 2021.
MPs have also been followed on the street and subjected to death threats in recent years.
Ontario Liberal MP Pam Damoff announced she would not run in the next election, saying the threats and misogyny she has experienced made her fearful of going out in public.
The federal Integrated Terrorism Assessment Centre routinely analyzes the threat landscape, including possible dangers to public figures.
RCMP commissioner Mike Duheme said last spring the Mounties were seeing an increase in invective directed at politicians, including comments from the same individuals on multiple occasions.
Duheme said he wanted the government to look at drafting a new law that would make it easier for police to pursue charges against people who threaten elected officials.
However, Justice Minister Arif Virani subsequently suggested existing Criminal Code provisions and police resources were sufficient.
An intelligence report released in March said threats against politicians had become "increasingly normalized" due to extremist narratives prompted by personal grievances and fuelled by misinformation or deliberate lies.
The June 2023 report, prepared by a federal task force that aims to safeguard elections, said baseless theories, disinformation and misinformation had spread to larger audiences, exposing online users to narratives that undermine science, systems of government and traditional figures of authority.
"Violent rhetoric routinely fixates on elected officials - with particular hostility towards high-profile women," the report said.
LeBlanc said Monday that ensuring the safety of politicians is an important consideration for attracting people to public life.
"If you see discussions about threats of violence, about intimidation, about attacks on social media, it's going to be harder to get good people to come forward and offer themselves to the service of their communities."
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: A person watches news coverage of the Donald Trump shooting in a local bar near the Fiserv Forum ahead of the 2024 Republican National Convention.Minister of Public Safety, Democratic Institutions and Intergovernmental Affairs Dominic LeBlanc is "confident that the RCMP will do what's necessary to protect elected leaders."Matt Rourke THE ASSOCIATED PRESS Adrian Wyld The Canadian Press
