Document 157 of 249

Letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters-editor/docview/3080953136/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-07-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Vigilance
required
Brock criticized for silence on professor's conduct, July 10
I am a retired faculty member of Brock University, after 40 some years of service. I am also a Zionist, advocating for the national freedom of the Indigenous Jewish community.
As a professional academic, I strongly favour academic freedom. But like any good thing, too much of a good thing is not a good thing.
In the 1930s German universities were replete with antisemitism and Nazi drivel. Universities are not immune from illogic, sloppy work and hatred. Many research works in reputed universities were illogical, sloppy and, yes, hateful. Genocide was the consequence.
Fortunately, we are located in a relatively free nation, but we must be vigilant. Our university must be at the forefront of preserving a society in which prejudice and racism, emphatically including antisemitism, cannot be concealed under a cloak of academic freedom. I hope leaders and administrators recognize their responsibilities.
Your front page article on Prof. Tamari Kitossa was thoughtful to all who value Brock University. I was pleased you let him be quoted at length, where he showed a mixture of pseudo-intellectual abuse, sloganeering and self-pity. Evidently academic freedom is good for him but not so good for his critics.
Please continue your good work and I hope my university follows suit.
Stan Sadava, Professor
Emeritus, Brock University
Who are the
real victims?
Some 38,200 innocent civilians, mostly women and children, have been murdered by the Israeli government's bombing of Gaza.
But Brock University should censure Prof. Tamari Kitossa for protesting this because Jewish supporters believe this is hate speech. Thanks for redirecting and pointing out who the real victims are.
Rick Strosberg, Fonthill
PremierFord takes the cake
Doug Ford reveals new road map around the LCBO strike, July 8
Lately, Ontario Premier Doug Ford has acted like Donald Trump. He's following his own agenda, never mind what we, the people who live here, think.
His closing of the Ontario Science Centre, despite the fact repairs could have been done, is irresponsible.
The LCBO workers are exercising their legal right to strike. But Ford is telling us we really don't need the LCBO. "We'll give you a map."
LCBO workers are knowledgeable, polite and taxpayers. The LCBO stores return $2.5 billion to our province annually.
Should the premier start acting as a leader, maybe he'd pay more attention to health care and education. We may then start to respect him.
I can recall dumb moves by premiers, plenty of them, but this one, with his majority and arrogance, surely takes the cake!
Pete Morro, St. Catharines

Letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters-editor/docview/3078937398/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-07-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: 'Disgusting' MRI wait
Re: MRI machine needed at Welland hospital, Letters, July 8
I was referred to an orthopedic specialist in Welland in October 2023 and got an appointment on Feb. 14, 2024. At that appointment, the specialist said he wanted me to have an MRI before proceeding with any surgery because he wanted to see "what was happening with my hip."
He asked if I was willing to go to Mississauga for one because the Niagara area is so far behind.
I agreed I would be happy to go anywhere for the MRI rather than being in pain as I was.
I recently received a letter from Niagara Health, informing me my MRI appointment at St. Catharines hospital is on July 21. I thought February to July, six months, was quite a wait since nothing could be done until the doctor had this information, but accepted that this was how bad the Niagara system was.
A few days later, I looked again at the letter to enter the date in my calendar and was shocked to find it was July 21, 2025.
This is what Ontario health care has become. Disgusting.
Please elect someone else to run Ontario.
Barbara Berrie, Port Colborne
Booze and votes
Re: Convenience stores can now apply for alcohol licences. Here's how easy it is, June 17
It is truly distressing that the provincial government has legislated the expansion of the sale of alcohol into large and small grocery/convenience stores.
The easy and quick availability of beer, wine, spirits and pre-mixed drinks will lead to further drunk driving, highway deaths, store robberies and increased family violence.
Is this another way for the Conservative government under Doug Ford to garner votes?
Judith MacCarthy, Niagara Falls
Divisive column
Re: With Labour leader Keir Starmer's landslide win, Britons might finally see their luck turn, July 8
The subheading says: "After they voted in a Ronald Reagan-style government that favoured the well-born and the greasy, the economy tanked."
Well, if Heather Malick is truly an "Anglophile," then with friends like her who needs enemies?
Here in Canada, I guess she viewed the Dalton McGuinty and Kathleen Wynne years as a complete and total economic and societal success.
If she bothered to study actual voting figures, she may understand much of it was to do with an appalling voter turnout and other factors, although nothing would have prevented a Labour win.
Rishi Sunak's Tories deserved defeat, but a supposed serious political commentator embarking on such partisan invective does little to offer any hope of us ever reaching any sort of dialogue between those on the left and those on the far left.
There are no longer any truly responsible fiscally conservative politicians on the "right," just a bunch of folks who manage to get elected only to adopt tax-and-spend policies that do nothing for their citizens.
Andrew Watts, Wainfleet

Document 159 of 249

Five years after historic tobacco ruling, 'nothing has changed'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/five-years-after-historic-tobacco-ruling-nothing/docview/3076528172/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Five+years+after+historic+tobacco+ruling%2C+%27nothing+has+changed%27&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-07-08&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: In the span of a few short years, Jean-Luc Duval lost two of the most important people in his life to lung cancer.
His wife Monique was diagnosed on her birthday and died on July 3, 2005 - the couple's 40th wedding anniversary. Months of debilitating chemotherapy treatments couldn't stop the disease, which had quietly spread and taken root in her digestive system.
Duval then reconnected with a former co-worker, and as they grew closer they decided to live together as companions in his home in Repentigny, a suburb of Montreal. One night, she started coughing violently and he took her to the emergency room. Doctors found she had cancer in both lungs, and she died within five months.
Both women had been smokers, though they had quit years earlier. Duval, too, had smoked for several decades, but managed to kick the habit years before his wife.
Duval joined a lengthy legal battle against three major tobacco companies, and in a historic ruling in 2019, Quebec's highest court confirmed he and roughly 100,000 other Quebecers were entitled to billions in compensation for the harm they or their loved ones had experienced.
But five years later, none of them have seen even a fraction of that money - and recent court filings suggest hundreds have died in the interim.
"Not only have we not received a cent, but absolutely nothing has changed," Duval, 80, wrote in French in a recent open letter to the Quebec government.
"Cigarettes from these same manufacturers are sold across the province and in every corner of the country.
"I'm not interested in the money, but I want justice," he added. "I want this industry to cease to exist."
Several health advocacy groups sounded the alarm about the lack of movement and transparency in the case, warning Canada could miss out on what they call a historic opportunity to reduce tobacco use and regulate the industry.
"We're never going to have a better opportunity than we do right now and, you know, it cannot be business as usual for tobacco companies after (a) settlement," said Rob Cunningham, a lawyer for the Canadian Cancer Society.
The lawsuits involved smokers who took up the habit between 1950 and 1998 and either fell ill or were addicted, or involved their heirs.
A Quebec Superior Court judge ordered the payment in 2015 after finding the three companies - Imperial Tobacco, JTI-Macdonald and Rothmans-Benson & Hedges - had chosen profits over the health of their customers.
The province's Appeal Court then upheld the landmark decision, prompting the companies to seek creditor protection in Ontario. That protection also suspended legal proceedings against them, which includes lawsuits filed by provincial governments to recoup health-care costs related to smoking.
The initial stay of proceedings lasted a few months, but it has since been renewed roughly a dozen times - most recently in March, when it was extended to September.
The purpose of the stay is to maintain the status quo as the companies negotiate a global settlement with all those who have claims against them, including the class-action members and the provinces.
The talks are confidential. In court filings last September, however, one of the lawyers for the class action members said a settlement was "not currently in sight."
Philippe Trudel said in his affidavit that about 700 of the class action members have died of tobacco-related illnesses since the first stay was granted, and "many more are becoming increasingly frail." Some "could wait no longer" and have opted for medically assisted suicide, he said.
It's not just the delays that are problematic, but the entire creditor protection process and the secrecy it entails, said Flory Doucas, spokesperson and co-director of the Quebec Coalition for Tobacco Control.
By nature, the process centres on industry viability, sidelining the public health and justice elements of the case, she said. It essentially allows the companies to continue operating as usual as they restructure - and get more people addicted in the process, she said.
The coalition is among several groups calling on the provinces to insist on significant public-health measures to reduce tobacco use as part of a settlement, warning them that focusing on financial compensation would only cause more harm down the line.
"It means you rely on future sales (to fund the payments)," she said. "It means governments have an interest in keeping those companies in business ... and their business model is based on addiction and harmful products."
Until recently, no provincial government had publicly indicated what it was seeking or expecting to receive as part of a settlement.
Some details emerged in May, when Manitoba Premier Wab Kinew told the provincial NDP convention that the province expects to receive an initial payment in the hundreds of millions of dollars soon.
The premier's comments confirmed the coalition's "worst fears" in terms of what a settlement could look like, Doucas said.
"It does look like it's primarily financial and it's based on instalments," she said, which means "having future and current victims use products to compensate past victims and provinces."
Last year, the Canadian Cancer Society, the Canadian Lung Association and the Heart and Stroke Foundation compiled a series of measures they believe should be in the settlement.
These include: putting at least 10 per cent of the money received in a fund to reduce tobacco use; banning all remaining tobacco promotion; requiring the companies to make extra payments if tobacco-reduction targets aren't met; and publicly disclosing millions of pages of internal company documents.
The companies have declined to comment on the negotiations or the concerns raised by smoking-reduction and health groups, nor would they say whether they would seek another stay extension come fall.
Duval said he's skeptical the matter will be resolved soon.
"I'm not someone who throws in the towel easily," he said.
"I'll do everything I can until the day I die. With all the hardships I've had, with all that tobacco has done to me, to my kids and others ... (I want) to see it through to the end."
The Canadian Press
CAPTION: Jean-Luc Duval stands next to a picture of his late wife, Monique, in his home last month. Monique died of lung cancer in 2005.Ryan Remiorz The Canadian Press

CREDIT: Paola Loriggio The Canadian Press

Document 160 of 249

letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters-editor/docview/3076229297/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-07-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Bumpy ride in
a Ford term
Premier Doug Ford's government seems to relish being on a roller-coaster.
The Progressive Conservatives are quite willing to sacrifice $225 million to break a contract with LCBO to terminate it one year earlier. That money - our money - to be sacrificed for one year of maintaining the status quo?
Meanwhile, the government has been scant in addressing the issue of autistic children being integrated into regular classrooms. That amount of money spent on a needy cause would be a vote-earner.
Ford, though, has offered an olive branch of a curious sort to soften the blow. LCBO can apply to sell lottery tickets. Profits from LCBO come back to us. Profits from corner store outlets are lost to voters.
Mind you, this is a further example of failing to look at all the implications of whims.
The failed licence plate redesign was another cost to the taxpayer. The plates were seen to be difficult to identify with night driving. One would have expected that flaw to be identified in the planning stages. One would hope. Too much to expect?
The Greenbelt travesty continues to be a reminder of Ford's management, such as it is. Emails sent to personal addresses rather than through government links showed more than a lack of short-sightedness. Ford in reaction to his reversal indicated the public wouldn't be interested in an inquiry as they had other issues to worry about. Housing for one. Scant consideration for the many individuals and organizations that were motivated in various ways to oppose the nightmare.
Ford's latest is the Ontario Science Centre, with the bombshell announcement to close it which came on a Friday afternoon, a typical time for politicians for such announcements. Announce it then close the door. The "agent orange" approach seems too drastic when the report on which this announcement was made focused on more than the five per cent where its roof is in a dangerous state.
Perhaps Ford is playing a strategy. When reversals on outlandish projects are made members of the public exhale "Phew, we made a difference," and somehow feel they are on Ford's team now.
Ross Smith, Niagara Falls
Nice if we had say in U.S. election
With the amount of impact Donald Trump had on Canada during his time as president - from ripping up Great Lakes fresh water protection to withdrawing from the Paris Climate Accord - he hurts everything he touches.
Asked what he would do to address climate change, he was silent. But he did mention doing away with red tape that protects the environment.
Now on to Joe Biden. If he just shows up and does nothing for four years it will make the world a safer place. So, l propose that for any Canadian who wants to help cover our Canadian butts, we get half a vote in any federal American election.
Russ Young, Fort Erie

Document 161 of 249

On the lam, an international pursuit as justice closes in: A sinister double-cross in Hamilton. A panicked getaway across Ontario. An international pursuit over three European countries. A shooting victim must face her boyfriend's accused killers. In this three-part series, The Hamilton Spectator's Nicole O'Reilly brings us a story of betrayal, greed and murder

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/on-lam-international-pursuit-as-justice-closes/docview/3076228046/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=On+the+lam%2C+an+international+pursuit+as+justice+closes+in%3A+A+sinister+double-cross+in+Hamilton.+A+panicked+getaway+across+Ontario.+An+international+pursuit+over+three+European+countries.+A+shooting+victim+must+face+her+boyfriend%27s+accused+killers.+In+this+three-part+series%2C+The+Hamilton+Spectator%27s+Nicole+O%27Reilly+brings+us+a+story+of+betrayal%2C+greed+and+murder&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-07-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Tyler Pratt and Jordyn Romano expected to meet their friends for a routine real-estate inspection in Hamilton, and a dinner together afterward at a nice restaurant in Oakville.
Their friends, Oliver Karafa and Lucy Li, planned something more sinister.
When Pratt and Romano arrived in their usual white Range Rover, Karafa and Li were already there in a car Pratt and Romano didn't recognize, a white Audi.
The two vehicles parked facing each other at the back of an industrial building at 347 Arvin Ave. in Stoney Creek.
Where's the realtor? Pratt asked.
They were expecting a man named Michael to show them around, but he was nowhere to be seen. What's more, they were locked out of the building.
Li was sent to look for a lockbox; she didn't find one - that's because no one had actually made plans to view the property.
Romano, who was 13 weeks pregnant and cold, returned to their Range Rover to warm up. Li was directed to join her while the guys talked.
Romano was dressed up, in a tan sweater and thigh-high Christian Louboutin boots. She believed the foursome was going out for dinner at the restaurant Soto Soto in Oakville afterward. It was strange, she thought, that Li - usually impeccably dressed - was in sweats. She "showed up in rags," Romano said.
Romano and Pratt had known Karafa about four months, since they started investing in one of his businesses. Over the preceding months, they met often. Pratt and Karafa spoke almost daily. Romano believed the two couples had become good friends.
Indeed, Pratt and Romano bought Karafa a diamond chain as a gift for his birthday a week earlier. And two days earlier, Karafa messaged them about taking a trip to Italy in June. Li spoke to Romano about her upcoming baby shower.
But inside the car that day, something seemed off.
Li was "nervously texting," Romano said. "She comes in the car and couldn't even look at me."
When Li got out of the car, Romano slid over into the driver's seat.
Treachery revealed
Her window was down when she looked up and froze. Karafa, his face shaking, was pointing a gun at her.
"Get out of here, Jo," is all Romano could remember him saying before she was shot. Panicked, she reversed her car and crashed into something. She heard Pratt saying, "What the f--k, bro," before more gunshots.
More than an hour later, about 7:20 p.m., a passing motorist - Annette Bright - found Romano crawling on the road in front of 347 Arvin Ave. Police later found Pratt's body lying on the cold ground behind the property.
Both cars, damaged in the chaos, were gone. The Audi was found abandoned nearby parked at Green Road and South Service Road.
Li and Karafa fled in Romano's Range Rover. Karafa - who was prohibited from driving after being convicted of impaired driving causing death years earlier - had to hold the door closed as they drove on the highway.
They dumped the vehicle near Mimico GO Station in Etobicoke. Li says Karafa later told her he also dumped the gun somewhere near there, but it was never found.
Karafa arranged for a guy he met through a Kijiji ad, named Mohamad Morad, to buy both vehicles for $2,000, strip them for parts and ship them out of the country. But police found the vehicles first.
As Romano fought for her life, Karafa and Li took a taxi back to their condo on Erskine Avenue, near Yonge Street and Eglinton Avenue in Toronto.
Before the shooting, they had switched cellphone SIM cards on one of their phones with Phi Chinh - an unsuspecting friend of Karafa's - and then switched them back later that evening.
When data from cell towers was inspected later, it appeared as if Li and Karafa stayed home, while Chinh travelled to the shooting scene. Indeed, Chinh was later arrested for murder. When he explained to police what actually happened, he was let go.
Back at their condo, Karafa and Li tried to get some rest, but early the next morning they saw an article about the shooting in The Hamilton Spectator: one male was dead and one female critically injured.
Now they had a problem. Romano could identify them.
They thought their careful planning would allow them to continue their lives without paying Pratt and Romano back. Pratt and Romano had invested nearly $500,000 in a business of Karafa's selling medical supplies in Europe. Karafa had promised big profits but the couple had not been repaid a penny.
Now, with a witness, they had to run.
On the lam
Surveillance video shows Karafa, carrying a garbage bag, and Li, carrying a UPS envelope, leaving their condo shortly before 3 a.m. on March 1. They drove Li's Mercedes north to Muskoka, stopping at the Glen Orchard General Store near Port Carling, which also includes a gas station, restaurant, tackle shop and other properties off Butterfly Lake.
When the store's general manager, Charlene Smith, arrived at work around 6:30 a.m. on March 1, she noticed the vehicle in the parking lot. She opened the store at 7 a.m. and, about an hour later, saw an attractive young couple come in to use the washroom.
They bought some drinks and left in a taxi.
Chris Whittingham, who owns the Glen Orchard store, didn't think much of the abandoned Mercedes until his daughter found a garbage bag in the recycling bin filled with 40 nine-millimetre bullets, a blond wig, clothing and other suspicious items. He called police.
Ontario Provincial Police documented the evidence found in the bag, including a pair of Li's pants with blood drops and a phone charger with blood on it. The key fob to an Audi was also found in the bag.
Inside the abandoned Mercedes, police found more clothing and items, including the UPS folder that contained documents from the life-insurance scheme. Before the shooting, Romano and Pratt believed Li was finalizing a $1.5 million life-insurance policy that would help them transfer some of the money they were owed from Europe; Li later admitted she never intended to file the application and only pretended to go along with it to buy Karafa more time.
By the time police found the evidence in Muskoka, Li and Karafa were long gone.
Sandy Sethi, the taxi driver, was going to drop them off at the bus station in Barrie, but the couple changed their plans and she drove them to Toronto. She dropped them off at a subway station, earning $400 for the trip.
Li and Karafa then travelled to Union Station, where their movements could be traced on surveillance video buying tickets and then boarding a train to Montreal. In Montreal, they sat silently on a shuttle bus that took them to Montreal-Trudeau International Airport. They arrived at 4:40 p.m., and checked in for a flight to Germany and a connecting flight to Prague.
Li carried nothing. Karafa had a bag filled with cash: between $16,000 and $17,000. Because of this, Li said they were stopped at the gate until she showed them her banking records for the money she had withdrawn to buy the Audi from Karafa's friend the day before.
The prosecution would later argue the money was likely Pratt's, which he brought back from Vancouver the day of the shooting. That money was never recovered.
In messages between Karafa and Li just two days before the shooting, Karafa said, "I hope these retards have money."
By March 4, Hamilton police had announced warrants for Karafa and Li for first-degree murder and attempted murder. Later that month, they said the fugitives had fled to eastern Europe and were now wanted on international warrants.
Jim Callender, then a detective sergeant with Hamilton police and the case manager in charge of the investigation, vowed they would find them. It was a promise Callender, now an inspector, and his team, including Det. David Tsuchida, who was the lead detective, made good on several months later.
An international pursuit
What happened between the time Li and Karafa landed in Prague and were arrested in Budapest, Hungary, months later has been the subject of speculation. The details that are known largely come from Li, who testified about their run from the law.
Karafa was an entrepreneur with multiple businesses. Most were in Europe. Karafa's business partner, Martin Macek, was based in Prague.
After landing there, they took an Uber to Macek's condo and then spent a few days at an Airbnb.
Business records obtained by The Spectator show their partnership in at least one business was dissolved a few days after Karafa's arrival. In 2021, Macek declined to speak with The Spectator and said he found out about the murder only after Karafa left Prague.
After a few days in Prague, Li and Karafa took a train to Slovakia, where his dad picked them up and drove to their family home, Li said. It was just Karafa's parents and their cat in the house.
Karafa and Li were both exhausted; they showered and fell asleep.
But Li awoke around midnight when her phone rang. It was one of her sisters telling her warrants for their arrests had been reported in the media.
Li woke her husband, who woke his dad. The Karafa family home would be the first place authorities would look for the fugitive couple, so they would have to leave.
Karafa's father didn't want to lose his son again to the justice system in Canada - Karafa had already served a prison sentence for the drunk-driving death.
In 2021, there were strict pandemic lockdown rules in Eastern Europe. And, as a Chinese woman, Li said she stood out. She was made to crouch in the back of a truck while Karafa's dad drove them to a friend's house.
Li said she called her mom, Hong Wei 'Winnie' Liao, who hysterically yelled at her daughter while Li sobbed. Her mom was telling her to come home.
It was then that Liao, a high-profile businessperson with political ties, released a statement in Chinese-language media.
In the statement, which the Spectator translated from Mandarin, the family said they were "shocked, disturbed and puzzled by Lucy's involvement in the tragic incident" and offered condolences to the victims and their families.
Addressing Li directly, they said they were sure she would not be involved in such an incident voluntarily and would not do "such a ridiculous thing."
"We can hardly imagine the shock and anxiety you experienced after the incident."
The family implored Li to co-operate.
Li's mom arranged for her to speak with a lawyer on the phone while they were in Slovakia. Karafa told her he would not turn himself in.
That night, she awoke to hear Karafa speaking Slovak in the kitchen with men who claimed to be Europol police officers. The men, who called themselves Paul and Milo, drove vehicles with diplomatic plates and claimed they could help make the warrants go away.
Li said Karafa's father paid 250,000 euros that night - and more money later - for their help. She also said her mom paid about 200,000 euros through a lawyer after another man, Sasa (pronounced Sasha), who appeared to be a higher-ranked officer, became involved.
They were first moved to an apartment that belonged to Paul, where their phones were taken from them and fake passports issued.
Li's fake Slovak passport listed her name as Ying Feng Nichta, born in Cebu, Philippines.
Karafa's said his name was Jakub Nichta born in Bratislava.
With their fake passports, Li said they were driven in military-style vehicles across the border into Hungary, where they spent April and half of May in a safe house overseen by a family from Syria in the city of Gyor. Li called it the "pink house" due to its colour.
She said they were told on May 15 that the international warrants were gone, but that Hamilton police were slow to update. They were given their phones back and travelled to Budapest, where Li said they wrongly believed they were free.
Justice closes in
The couple stayed in an Airbnb and Li - who wanted to come home - started looking into getting new Canadian passports. She said they spent a lot of time just walking around the city and visiting a church.
It was outside a church where the pair were arrested June 12.
Police in Hungary released photos of the pair, hands cuffed and heads bowed to avoid cameras.
Li immediately waived extradition and agreed to come back to Canada. Hamilton police travelled to Budapest and escorted her back. She landed in Toronto a month after her arrest on July 13 and was charged.
In Hamilton, she was interviewed for hours by Det. Troy Ashbaugh, an expert interrogator who is now retired. Li, used to flying first class, told him she was "not used to sitting in tight spaces" while being escorted home in economy. Her mom, she said, had offered to fly them all back privately.
Throughout the interview, Li was polite but refused to answer many questions. She repeatedly said there was more to the story, but her lawyer and mother had told her not to talk.
She admitted to being at the scene of the shooting, but claimed to be far away when shots were fired.
Back in Budapest, Karafa had perks in jail, including a contraband cellphone. He fought extradition and instead wanted to go home to Slovakia.
According to court records there, Karafa argued that corruption within Hamilton police and the Hamilton-Wentworth Detention Centre, where Karafa was expected to be held, made it unsafe for him to return to Canada.
His lawyers also argued that they had hired a private investigator to look into Pratt and his "extensive criminal connections."
However, the court there - including the Budapest Court of Appeal - dismissed those concerns. Canadian authorities provided detailed information about Karafa's rights and security plans.
Just over a week after the Appeal Court upheld Karafa's extradition and while Canadian authorities were working out the plan to transport him back to Hamilton, Li was granted bail.
Many people charged with murder in Canada are not granted bail. But granting bail to someone who was an international fugitive is exceptionally rare.
In Li's case, her mother and other sureties promised strict control, including bars on her windows at the family's North York property. Li was released on bail with a $2.7-million bond on Jan. 20, 2022.
Only four months later, Li was again in custody after allegedly breaching her bail conditions by going out to a party and being in contact with someone she was prohibited from communicating with.
By March 30, Karafa - escorted by Hamilton police homicide detectives - arrived back in Canada and was transported to jail. He has been behind bars ever since.
Nicole O'Reilly is a crime and justice reporter at The Spectator. noreilly@thespec.com

Title: On the lam, an international pursuit as justice closes in:   A sinister double-cross in Hamilton. A panicked getaway across Ontario. An international pursuit over three European countries. A shooting victim must face her boyfriend's accused killers. In this three-part series, The Hamilton Spectator's Nicole O'Reilly brings us a story of betrayal, greed and murder

The double-cross, the shooting and the surviving witness: A sinister double-cross. A panicked getaway across Ontario and an international pursuit over three European countries. A shooting victim faces her boyfriend's accused killers. In a three-part series, The Hamilton Spectator's Nicole O'Reilly brings us a story of betrayal, greed and murder

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/double-cross-shooting-surviving-witness/docview/3075886646/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=The+double-cross%2C+the+shooting+and+the+surviving+witness%3A+A+sinister+double-cross.+A+panicked+getaway+across+Ontario+and+an+international+pursuit+over+three+European+countries.+A+shooting+victim+faces+her+boyfriend%27s+accused+killers.+In+a+three-part+series%2C+The+Hamilton+Spectator%27s+Nicole+O%27Reilly+brings+us+a+story+of+betrayal%2C+greed+and+murder&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-07-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It was late on a cold Sunday afternoon, the last day of February 2021, when the Audi Q5 pulled into 347 Arvin Ave. and stopped in front of the gate.
Like most businesses in the industrial area of Stoney Creek on the eastern edge of Hamilton, Colonial Pillars was closed for the weekend. The street was quiet, save for the odd passing car. The property was secured, the gate locked.
Oliver Karafa left his wife, Yun (Lucy) Lu Li, in the passenger seat, and approached the gate, returning once when someone walked by. Then it opened - with the padlock now cut - and the Audi disappeared around back.
There were no cameras to capture what happened next.
Just that morning, Karafa and Li had bought the white Audi. They paid Karafa's best friend Jaime Gutierrez about $18,000, using about $4,000 from Li's line of credit.
Karafa had already arranged for someone in Montreal to buy it, along with another vehicle: a Range Rover that didn't belong to them and was also destined for 347 Arvin that day.
From the surveillance cameras of businesses on Arvin, the Audi was unrecognizable, its rims crudely painted black, and untraceable to Karafa or Li.
Colonial Pillars had a for-sale sign out front. No arrangements had been made with a realtor to look at the property, but that is what Tyler Pratt and his pregnant girlfriend, Jordyn Romano, had been led to believe.
The couple thought they were meeting their friends to look at the property for a legal marijuana growing operation. They had already invested nearly a half a million dollars in another of Karafa's businesses and were anxious to see it returned.
"We considered them really good friends," Romano said.
But this Feb. 28 meeting was a ruse.
Karafa did not have the money to pay them back, and he did not want to set up a grow-op on Arvin or do any more business with the couple.
At 5:21 p.m. Karafa messaged Pratt and Romano on the encrypted messaging app Signal to say they would be at the property in 10 minutes, even though they were already there.
Just two minutes after receiving the message, Pratt and Romano arrived in their white Range Rover and drove back to meet their friends.
The shooting
For 31 minutes the two couples remained at the back of the property, unseen by anyone.
What exactly happened may never be known.
What is known is that Karafa shot at Romano three times as she sat inside her Range Rover, one of the bullets lodging in her heart and the other two missing her and hitting the vehicle.
Then he shot Pratt five or six times, with bullets hitting his arm, going through the back of his neck and a graze to his chin that may have been caused by the same bullet that hit his chest. This gunshot to the chest is what killed him, but his death would not have been immediate. He bled out on the cold ground as chaos ensued around him.
Both the Audi and Range Rover were damaged in the melee, either crashing into each other or into one of the warehouses at the back of the large property.
It is "difficult to imagine a more planned murder," said the judge who presided over Karafa's and Li's trials, after a jury found the pair guilty. He described it as an "outright execution."
It's not just that they travelled in an unknown car - Li's identifiable Mercedes was safely parked at their Toronto condo - but there were other steps taken to make it look like they never left their Erskine Avenue residence in the Yonge Street and Eglinton Avenue area of Toronto.
Karafa took the stairs where there are no cameras. Li wore a blond wig in the elevator. They switched cellphone SIM cards with a friend to give themselves a "digital alibi" that made it look like their phones never left the city.
The survivor
There was only one problem: Romano didn't die.
As Karafa and Li fled to Europe, Romano underwent life-saving surgery and spent days in a coma before she was able to tell police the identity of her killers.
Her memory of that day is foggy and, in some spots, unreliable because of the trauma she went through. But there are crucial moments she remembers in flashes.
Karafa staring at her with an odd look, his face shaking, he raised a gun and pointed it at her. Romano froze.
"Get out of here, Jo," was all she could remember Karafa saying before she was shot. Panicked, she put the car in reverse and crashed.
She doesn't remember seeing Pratt, who was standing outside, being shot. But she heard him scream, "What the f--k, bro."
Another flash is Karafa dragging her out of her car. Police would later find one of her thigh-high Christian Louboutin boots, the top folded over as if pulled down while she was dragged out, on the ground in the parking lot.
Karafa and Li took both vehicles from the scene, leaving Pratt and Romano for dead.
Video from neighbouring properties shows the Audi, and then Pratt and Romano's Range Rover, coming and going several times during the hour after the shooting. The Audi was left nearby on Green Road. Then the Range Rover returned to Arvin twice.
The prosecution would later argue that was Karafa - and possibly Li - returning to the scene looking for Romano to finish the job. They finally left for good around 6:52 p.m. in the Range Rover.
The getaway
Eleven minutes later, Karafa messaged his car buyer in Montreal, Mohamad Morad.
"Both cars are f--ked," and would need to be towed, Karafa wrote. He offered both the Audi and Pratt and Romano's Range Rover for just $2,000, but only if they were sent out of the country in a shipping container with no trace to Karafa. With Li in the passenger seat, Karafa drove the Range Rover to Etobicoke, and left it parked on Audley Street near the Mimico GO Station.
Back on Arvin Avenue in Hamilton, about 25 minutes after the last time the Range Rover passed, Annette Bright drove by with her boyfriend in the passenger seat.
She saw something on the road in the dark. At first, she thought it was an animal. But something nagged at her, so she turned around. That's when she saw a woman crawling on the road.
Romano was wearing one boot. Her sweater bloody. She said she was cold.
"Help me, I've been shot," she pleaded.
Police arrived by 7:22 p.m., soon followed by paramedics, who took Romano to hospital, where she would live, but lose her unborn baby. She was about 13 weeks pregnant; a fact Li and Karafa knew.
After discovering Romano on the road, police found Pratt's body on the pavement behind the warehouse, his arms outstretched, bullets, shell casings and other debris around him.
The ensuing investigation by Hamilton police followed a complex web of shady business investments and false friendships. The tale spans from Vancouver to Toronto to Hamilton to Europe.
It is a story about money, desperation and betrayal. Perhaps more than anything it is about greed.
The killers
Yun Lu Li was born in Shenzhen, China, a city north of Hong Kong, in 1995. She is a triplet, and the sisters are the oldest children in the family.
Like the rest of her siblings, Li's English name is adapted from her Chinese name Yun Lu, with the Lu becoming Lucy.
When the girls were little, their parents moved to Toronto, and the triplets stayed with family while their parents established themselves in Canada, spending years apart until the girls joined their parents when they were seven. In Canada, the family grew, with a younger sister and brother.
At first, their dad ran a driving school for Chinese immigrants, where their mom also worked. But soon their mom's wealth management business took off. Respon International grew to one of the top financial institutions working with Chinese-Canadians, with Hong Wei (Winnie) Liao becoming a prominent businessperson, photographed rubbing shoulders with political elites, including Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.
According to Lucy, the bread and butter of her mom's business is selling 20-year life insurance policies that allow clients, after an initial premium paid in Canada, to transfer funds from overseas without being taxed.
This type of insurance policy later plays a role in the murder plot.
Li said she initially struggled at school and was behind her sisters in learning English. Her first language is Mandarin. The family moved to North York, and it was at Earl Haig Secondary School where she met her future husband.
"He was older and cool from the beginning," Li said. "I was pretty nerdy with braces and bangs."
Karafa was born in Slovakia and immigrated to Canada in 2001 with his family, first living in Windsor and then Toronto. Li said she liked Karafa right away.
They went on dates, but Li claimed the short-lived relationship ended when Karafa wanted to have sex and Li wasn't ready. She also claimed he slept with a friend of hers at a party.
In April 2012, when Karafa was 19, he crashed his Range Rover on Mount Pleasant Road in Toronto.
He was only a G2 driver at the time. Karafa walked away, but his passenger - a friend and personal trainer - was killed.
Karafa was sentenced to five years in prison in 2014 and prohibited from driving for 15 years.
When Karafa first applied for parole in January 2016, he was denied because of two incidents in Beaver Creek Institution, where he was involuntarily transferred from minimum to medium security.
"The board suggested your behaviours since being incarcerated is indicative of a young man with an abnormal sense of entitlement; you agreed," the parole decision says. "You present as a person who believes rules made for society are for everyone else to live by and not you."
By December 2016, Karafa was granted day parole, but denied full parole.
Karafa appealed the decision to deny his full parole, and that was rejected. But six months later, he was released on full parole in August 2017. He was 24 and the parole board believed he was motivated to get his life on track. He had a job as a manager at a café and was supported by his sister, her husband and his unnamed girlfriend, the document says.
His parents had already returned to Europe, where they lived in the family home in Levice, Slovakia - a location that would play a role later when Karafa and Li were on the run.
Karafa's first prison sentence for the fatal drunk-driving offence officially expired on May 4, 2019, while the driving ban continued.
Li said her upbringing was strict. Her mother was a "tiger mom" with resolute plans for her children.
All were encouraged to study business. Li's mom offered a reward of a car to the first triplet to acquire accreditation to sell life insurance. Li was the first to do this, focusing on that more than her studies at Western University in London, Ont. Eventually she transferred Ryerson University (now Toronto Metropolitan University) to finish school, while working with insurance companies.
Li, working with her mother, struggled to attract bigger clients herself and realized that she needed more life experience. She, along with her triplet sisters, asked for a break from work at Christmas 2018. The family often had annual meetings around the holidays where they would discuss what they accomplished that year and plans for the future. Li said she was given two years of freedom, with her mom offering a $5,000 a month allowance.
The triplets had an account on Instagram together where they attracted sponsors who paid for the sisters to model their products. Li said they made about $500 to $1,000 per sponsored post, but that the money was split three ways and was more for fun and not enough to live on. One sponsor gave her the blond wig.
It was during this time, when Li was about 23, that Karafa came back into her life. They messaged on Instagram and met for a date.
The relationship moved fast, with Li moving in with Karafa after their second date. Li's mom stopped giving her an allowance because Karafa paid for most things.
Out of prison, Karafa worked as an entrepreneur and seemed to be making good money. According to Li, his businesses included everything from exporting cars, renting out a studio to film social-media ads, to selling weighted blankets. But much of his business was in Europe, where he had a partner in Prague.
The couple got a two-story penthouse condo at 18 Erskine Ave. in Toronto. Because Karafa was a prohibited driver, Li drove him everywhere, including to multiple business meetings.
This included frequent meetings hosted at the Mississauga offices of Karafa's longtime friend Alex (also known as Sasha), where Karafa would later meet Pratt and Romano.
Karafa and Li travelled often, including to Europe to see his family and conduct business. In spring 2020, about two months into lockdowns at the start of the COVID-19 pandemic, the couple were going a little "stir crazy," and Karafa wanted to return to Europe.
They bought tickets to Zurich for May 16. But when they got to the airport, they had a problem. Karafa, who had dual citizenship in Canada and Slovakia, was allowed to return to Europe, but Li was not.
At the suggestion of someone working at the airline they decided to get married, Li said. Karafa was going to leave with or without her.
"We were not planning on marrying, but I was very in love with him and due to travel restrictions I made the irresponsible choice and decided to ... marry him on the spot," Li said.
They stepped out of line and married with an officiant presiding over Zoom. They spent months in Europe before returning to Toronto that summer.
The victims
Tyler Pratt was raised in a large and loving family in British Columbia, where he coached kids' hockey and, like Karafa, was also an aspiring entrepreneur.
Pratt grew up in Taylor, B.C., then, as a young man, he lived in Kelowna before relocating to Vancouver, where his two children were born.
Described by family as hardworking, his businesses included a diamond company called Hard as Diamonds and a wellness supplement company, Elite Organic Nutrition.
But he made most of his money selling cocaine. He wasn't a street-level dealer - he arranged shipments by phone. He once made $1 million in a day, Romano said, but that was rare.
By 2020, he had cash and was looking to invest and move away from cocaine.
"He wanted to just become a more legitimate businessman," Romano later said in court. "He wanted to get out of the cocaine life; we wanted to have a family and be normal and escape that negativity."
She grew up in Ontario, with family in Hamilton. In her early 20s, she went back and forth to B.C. for a few years before moving there when she was about 24.
Romano worked as an exotic dancer and met Pratt about six months before they started dating. She stopped working and he paid for their lifestyle, which included luxury goods and travel. Their Toronto condo, photographed by police after the murder, was filled with boxes of designer shoes, bags and other items.
Romano agreed that Pratt was careful to live off the radar because of his cocaine business, but she said he was kind and had an even temper. Pratt's former wife - the mother of his two children - also said she never had issues with Pratt and that he always put his children first.
Romano and Pratt were together for about a year before he was killed, first living in Vancouver and then moving to Toronto not long before the shooting. They wanted to start a new life together.
In 2020, the couple travelled back and forth between Toronto and Vancouver often, both for work and so Pratt could see his kids. On the day he was killed, he had just returned from a trip for his daughter's birthday.
In July 2020, Romano took a solo trip to Toronto and messaged a girlfriend she hadn't seen in a while. This friend was dating a guy named Alex (Sasha) and the group went out for dinner.
The swindle
Later, Romano introduced Sasha, described in court as the owner of a Mississauga construction company, and Pratt. The two men hit it off. Pratt was looking for a business to invest in and Sasha helped arrange meetings.
Among those Sasha introduced was his friend, Oliver Karafa, who he had known for a decade and vouched for.
At that first meeting, Karafa told them: "Yo, I have something that can make us a lot of money."
Soon after, Pratt and Romano invested $170,000 in Karafa's business, selling truckloads of personal protective equipment (PPE) in Europe. Selling everything from hospital gowns to syringes promised to be lucrative, especially during the pandemic. Soon after, they invested more cash and, by late fall, they'd given Karafa roughly $470,000.
"I really liked Oliver," Romano said. "He was just always nice and easygoing. Tyler loved Oliver, they had a great friendship. He adored him."
And they trusted him. The couple never signed a formal contract.
There were a lot of meetings with Karafa and Pratt, often at Sasha's office. It was there they first met Li. By late 2020, Sasha was dating one of Li's sisters, Jane, one of the triplets, and Li would visit with her sister after driving Karafa to the meetings.
Li later testified that she knew Pratt had invested money in the PPE business, but didn't know the particulars. She wasn't involved in the PPE business meetings.
Karafa promised big profits, but claimed the money was stuck in Europe. Romano said he showed them a European account with millions in it. He promised $3 million was their share.
But by January, Karafa had failed to pay Romano and Pratt and was desperately trying to find ways to delay.
His answer: an insurance scheme. This is where Li really enters the story.
Li had let her licence lapse, but she was toying with getting back into insurance and knew the industry well. Karafa sold Pratt on one of the 20-year life insurance policies and set up meetings with the four of them.
The held several meetings, including at the North York home of Li's parents - which the family continued to own despite her parents primarily living in Vancouver - as well as at Pratt and Romano's new Cumberland Street condo in Toronto, where they moved in February.
Romano said Li initially suggested a $2.8-million policy, but she and Pratt couldn't afford that so it was reduced to $1.5 million. Romano believed if they paid the first premium in cash from Canada, their money stuck in Europe could be added later, allowing them to transfer the money without paying tax or risk the attention of authorities, including Canada's national financial intelligence agency, FINTRAC (Financial Transactions and Reports Analysis Centre of Canada). Romano also said she was told they could borrow against the policy, allowing them to get a loan in Canada to expand business.
The truth is that insurance premiums cannot be paid in cash. But Pratt and Romano didn't understand how it worked.
Li later admitted the insurance scheme was a ruse to buy Karafa more time to come up the money owed. She said she initially was sincere in trying to sell them a policy, but that after learning the premium would need to be in cash, she played along to help Karafa. She never intended to submit the application.
Li, who said she never like Romano and Pratt, claimed she offered to pretend to pay the first premium. Police would later find a $1.5-million cheque in an envelope of insurance documents. But Romano said she never saw that cheque and instead believed Li was going to loan them $1 million - taken from her mom's account where she "wouldn't notice" it missing.
Pratt needed the loan because he was having cash-flow problems, including losing a cocaine shipment that was seized at the border, court heard.
Romano said the two couples were set to meet at Li's mom's offices on March 1 to finalize the insurance application. Karafa was going to be paying them back the initial $500,000 investment, with the $1-million loan from Li that would make up the $1.5 million required. Then they were going to sign paperwork finalizing their positions with the European PPE business at Karafa's lawyer's office afterward.
Instead, by March 1, Li and Karafa were on a flight to Europe, Pratt was dead and Romano in a coma, all while Hamilton police worked to put together what happened the day before at 347 Arvin Ave.
Nicole O'Reilly is a crime and justice reporter at The Spectator. noreilly@thespec.com

A shooting victim faces her boyfriend's accused killers. In a three-part series, The Hamilton Spectator's Nicole O'Reilly brings us a story of betrayal, greed and murder

Document 163 of 249

Readers write Letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write-letters-editor/docview/3073539183/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write+Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-06-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A housing
solution
Waiving development charges: Is province's definition of affordable housing realistic? June 25
I am going to tackle a very sticky wicket, but perhaps like every complicated sticky wicket, this too has a simple solution. And the sticky wicket is housing.
Since the price of what is considered affordable housing begins at $316,000 or so in Niagara and that figure is unrealistic and even arbitrary, a deep dive into the cost of everything to do with building a house must be undertaken to determine how prices for materials have increased as well as all other costs and services and whether these increases are fair and legal or just another gouge as in the food industry.
If it turns out the expenses are justified, which I doubt, then our economic system must be adjusted so building of houses is subsidized by increased taxes on business and high incomes. Period.
As for rental properties, newly built, the same parameters must apply.
With both, a set profit margin must be fixed in place. Costs for the necessities for living must not be allowed to be blown about by what regularly rear up as typhoons of the free market.
Obviously, the same deep dive must be conducted in the food industry and profit margins set. Period.
First the costs and the reasons for their increase. Then the practical solution applied.
Though it would be a big alteration, it would be one badly needed and be of long-term benefit to all and sundry. Then a sharp eye must be levelled at our shifty representatives in the slippery halls of power to make sure they walk the straight and narrow and do our bidding.
Wayne Borbey, Niagara Falls
Circus is back
Ontario Science Centre: What the report that prompted the surprise shutdown actually says, June 26
The circus is in town. Last Friday, Ontario Premier Doug Ford decided to close the world-renowned Ontario Science Centre.
The "problem" is the roof is unsafe. The building has 152,000 square feet of roof. The report by Rimkus Consulting apparently identified panels that need attention, six of them, totalling 90 square feet. The company did not recommend closure. Outside groups have offered to pay repair costs.
Ford wants to move the centre to Ontario Place, where Minister Kinga Surma's spokesperson says there will be 15 per cent more exhibition space. Strange, in that the new plans call for a building with 225,000 fewer square feet.
When they visit the new centre, the thousands of schoolchildren who have visited in the past can also pay to use the new waterpark and maybe even visit the luxury spa to see how the other half live.
I am guessing Ford figured a 4 p.m. Friday afternoon news release would not get much attention. People wouldn't be watching.
Wrong. People love watching a circus.
Rob Hay, Niagara Falls

Readers write Letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write-letters-editor/docview/3073048345/se-2?accountid=15090

Full text: Health-care
inefficiency
It's not inevitable that our health-care system is broken, June 22
In response to Leila Attar's column, I concur. Recently my doctor advised that I see a specialist due to an ongoing issue.
I was advised by my family doctor it was my responsibility to call the specialist's office to book an appointment. When I called the specialist's office I was asked when the referral was faxed (who faxes these days?).
I was emphatically told there could be no appointment until I could tell the specialist's office the exact day the referral fax came from my family doctor. In calling back my doctor's office, I had to leave a voice mail to request the date of the fax referral then call the specialist's office and leave another voice mail saying I left a voice mail at my doctor's office requesting the referral fax date in order to make the appointment.
When I do hear back from my doctor's office as to the date of the referral fax I will need to call back the specialist's office and probably leave another voice mail then pray there is a response and an appointment date is booked.
Does this make sense?
In contrast, I ordered a pair of shoes online from New Balance. I was advised by different emails my ordered was accepted, the order was processed, the order was shipped and arrival time of my purchase.
Perhaps Premier Doug Ford's staff could contact New Balance for advice on implementing efficiency and customer service within our health-care system.
David Pihach, Wainfleet
Affordable
electric vehicles
Freeland calls China's electric vehicles a threat to Canada, June 24
As a news junkie I'm amazed at the flawed approach to doing the right thing for the planet and people. I have no problem with the carbon tax but do have a problem with protectionism over electric vehicles made for less in China.
For the sake of planet, the more affordable the car, the more people will buy them.
My financial situation excludes me from EVs that are grossly over priced.
An EV from China is within my budget, why keep me from saving the planet?
Russ Young, Fort Erie
An easy
EV choice
The federal government has to have a 30-day consultation to decide to put restrictions on Chinese EVs? The U.S. has already placed restrictions on Chinese EVs, so what is the next closest country to the U.S. with no restrictions, Liberals?
I know these Liberals are the worst financial managers in Canadian political history, but surely even they must remember they have committed billions of taxpayer dollars to supporting EV batteries made in Canada.
Now, would cheap Chinese EVs dumped into Canada help or jeopardize a Canadian EV industry?
Chris Robertson,
Stony Plain, Alta.

Document 165 of 249

What's the price of affordable housing?: Builders and agencies say Ontario housing incentive will not achieve its purpose

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/whats-price-affordable-housing/docview/3072196584/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=What%27s+the+price+of+affordable+housing%3F%3A+Builders+and+agencies+say+Ontario+housing+incentive+will+not+achieve+its+purpose&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-06-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: What does the term "affordable housing" mean in Niagara?
The Ontario government has tried to answer that question through legislative amendments intended to encourage more affordable housing construction.
An online portal maintained by the province lets people search for what it defines as the cost of affordable residential units within their communities.
The purpose of the site revolves around development charges, the fees municipalities charge developers to cover costs of infrastructure needed to support housing projects, such as water and sewer services.
If developers build units that sell or rent at or below the defined amount on the site, they can have development charges waived by the municipality. The applicable units must remain affordable for 25 years, according to provincial legislation.
The site, ontario.ca (then search municipal development charges), has some strange anomalies. There is no difference between prices for an affordable house or condo, for example.
To be considered affordable housing, a purchase price in Niagara for a detached, semi-detached, townhouse or condo would have to be between $305,000 and about $320,000, depending where in the region the home is.
There also doesn't seem to be as big a difference in numbers between Niagara communities and Toronto as one might expect. An affordable house in Niagara Falls is defined as $319,800, while in Toronto it's $366,500.
The question is whether or not the amounts listed on the site are "affordable," and whether the provincial policy will succeed in encouraging developers to build affordable units.
And it seems people in the building industry and homeless advocates agree: for reasons at opposite ends of the debate, the province's numbers are unrealistic.
Removing development charges is a start in reducing the price of housing, said Chuck McShane, president of Niagara Home Builders' Association (NHBA). But getting homes built that sell for around $300,000 is going to be a "tough number to come by and get into," he said.
"Like any business, you can't ask the builder to build them and not make money," he said. "Nobody's going to put money into something that doesn't make money."
Niagara builders are not opposed to building affordable and attainable housing units, but truly affordable rental units and homes are usually built by non-profits and not-for-profit agencies, he said.
"There's a difference between affordable housing and housing attainability," he said.
To McShane, attainable homes are places the average person can afford based on average salary, while affordable housing is based on 30 per cent of monthly income for mortgage or rent. "And affordable housing is subsidized by the taxpayer," he said.
To build affordable housing there "has to be an appetite for it," McShane said.
At the other end of the spectrum, advocates in Niagara said $310,000 for a house is not affordable and the province's definition of affordable rental units is also unrealistic.
Laura Dumas, Start Me Up Niagara's executive director, said the affordable rental prices do not reflect what people can afford. "Affordability needs to be across the board. The rental costs have skyrocketed," she said.
People receiving Ontario Works would barely be able afford a bachelor apartment based on the province's affordable price in Niagara Falls, where to get development charges waived an apartment would have to rent for no more than $732 per month, leaving $1 of the $733 monthly cheque for the recipient to live on.
People receiving Ontario Disability Support Program payments would be able to afford higher priced bachelor apartments, but it would still not be considered affordable, said Dumas.
According to provincial legislation, a rental unit is deemed affordable if it is at or below income-based rent, which equals 30 per cent of a person's or family's gross annual income.
Jon Braithwaite, chief executive officer of The Hope Centre in Welland, said the term "affordable housing" has been bastardized to fit whatever scenario it needs to accommodate.
"The building next door to The Hope Centre had some affordable housing and they helped people who qualified for grants because it was 'affordable,' but it was only 10 per cent below market (rates)," he said.
Braithwaite said in Welland there are no apartments, bachelor or otherwise, under $1,000 a month.
The Hope Centre keeps a listing of available housing on its website, he said.
A majority of the listings "are one-bedrooms for $1,100 plus hydro and $1,200 plus hydro."
Realistically, when it comes to people receiving some kind of assistance, most places are out of reach, he said.
"The challenge is everybody talks about median rent or average monthly rents, and we're dealing with people unhoused right now," Braithwaite said.
"They don't get the luxury of average cost or median cost, they're paying in today's market, which is really high."
Habitat for Humanity Niagara's leader also feels the housing price listed by the province to be unrealistic.
Executive director Mark Carl said to sell a home at the provincially approved price of $310,000 would be challenging for the not-for-profit due to many added costs, particularly land fees and HST.
Habitat for Humanity relies on trade workers and businesses to donate material, labour and other forms of support to get housing built as cheaply as possible.
On average, Carl said, Habitat's expenses for typical homes in the 1,000- to 1,200-square-foot size are between $350,000 and $650,000, depending on donations.
"If you look at (building) lots they're anywhere between $150,000 to $300,000, then construction price on top of that," he said.
"A lot of waiving of fees and support from the community could get us to a feasible level," he said.
"The one thing that would help is the government currently charges HST when we sell the homes to families, so we're building affordable housing but paying HST. Habitat would like to see that waived," he said.
"Things that have to happen to make it achievable to make these houses work at that price are more affordable land to build on."
McShane said more than 20 per cent of a home's price is tied up in land transfer taxes, development charges and other taxes before a shovel even breaks ground.
Building single-family homes that meet the province's price threshold would be tough, but McShane said a more reasonable approach would be to focus efforts on condos and highrises.
"Because the cost of land is reduced, the more people (and units) you put on a piece of property the cheaper that is."
"There's got to be movement on both sides, $10,000 in a development charge is certainly going to help, but there's many other charges involved in trying to bring that $310,000 unit on board," he said.
St. Catharines NDP MPP Jennie Stevens is critical of the Doug Ford government's plan for affordable housing and said it only benefits a select few people within the province - the developers - and even they are saying it cannot be done.
"It's time for this government to be bold and lead solutions. We should listen to the experts, not just those that are in the inner circle of the wealthy speculators," she said.
"We need to bring nonmarket housing back so everyone can afford a place to live, especially, people on OW and ODSP," she said.
The Standard reached out to Niagara West Conservative MPP Sam Oosterhoff, but did not receive a response before deadline.
CAPTION: Chuck McShane, president of Niagara Home Builders' Association, believes affordable housing needs to be built, but he is unsure it is economical to build and sell homes for what the Ontario government is saying is affordable.Jon Braithwaite is executive director of The Hope Centre in WellandBob Tymczyszyn St. Catharines Standard

CREDIT: Matthew P. Barker Reporter

Document 166 of 249

Sports Hall inductee Hominuk opts for life on the streets: Family insists amputee needs spot in long-term-care facility

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/sports-hall-inductee-hominuk-opts-life-on-streets/docview/3071773924/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Sports+Hall+inductee+Hominuk+opts+for+life+on+the+streets%3A+Family+insists+amputee+needs+spot+in+long-term-care+facility&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-06-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: After losing both his legs and then losing his home while recovering in hospital, a 68-year-old Welland man has chosen to become homeless rather than stay in a homeless shelter.
His nephew is furious, and worried about his safety.
"It's absolutely disgusting," said Mike Konderka.
He said his uncle Mike Hominuk was rushed to hospital after he fell during a walk in February.
The Rose City business owner said his uncle "kind of neglected his diabetes and it got bad," leading to the amputation of both his legs.
At the time, he said, Hominuk was living in an apartment he could afford with his pension income, after retiring from a career as a steelworker.
However, after the amputations, Konderka said the family gave up the apartment, certain his uncle would spend the rest of his life in a long-term-care home.
Konderka said his uncle, too, had no intention of returning to his apartment.
"He said, 'Yeah, get rid of the apartment. I'm going to need help now that I have no legs,' " Konderka recalled.
But that changed when Niagara Health staff informed Hominuk he did not quality for long-term care. Eligibility for set out by Ontario's Ministry of Long-Term Care.
Instead, Konderka said his uncle was given a pile of papers with Kijiji ads printed on them, and told to "find yourself something, or you have to get out of here to a homeless shelter."
Konderka said he only learned his uncle would be discharged from the Welland hospital into a homeless shelter less than a week ago. He said his uncle said he was afraid of being placed in the shelter.
"He said, 'They won't place me in long-term care because they don't think I need help,' " he said.
Konderka said he would have renovated his uncle's apartment to make it wheelchair accessible had he known how things would unfold.
"I would have made it work for him."
But the short notice gave the family no time to make alternative arrangements for Hominuk, he added. When his uncle was scheduled to move into the homeless shelter on Friday morning, he refused.
Konderka said his uncle instead left the hospital in his wheelchair and returned to his old apartment, where someone else now resides.
He said he found his uncle in the neighbourhood in the afternoon and stopped to talk to him. He had no idea where his uncle would be sleeping Friday night.
Niagara Health in a statement said it could not comment on Hominuk's case due to privacy legislation.
"In general, the care team at Niagara Health works with the patient and their family to find the best discharge options. However, it is always up to patients to decide if they accept the options available to them in the community. It's also important to note there are eligibility requirements for long-term-care placements, and not everyone qualifies," the statement said.
"Niagara Health, like all hospitals in Ontario, is facing capacity pressures that make it impossible for us to keep people in hospital beds when they no longer require medical care. We need our beds for patients who require hospital care.
"This issue reinforces the need for more home and community-based supports, outside of the hospital."
Konderka contacted Niagara Centre NDP MPP Jeff Burch, who sent an "urgent" letter to Niagara Health president and chief executive officer Lynn Guerriero, asking her to intervene.
"Mr. Hominuk has no legs, nowhere to go and no one to help him with his activities of daily living," Burch wrote. "He needs long-term care."
In an interview, Burch said he is "not going to stop pushing the (Health) Ministry and the hospital, and we're going to keep track of where he is until he is properly taken care of in a place that can meet the needs of someone who is a double amputee."
Konderka said only a few weeks ago he proudly accompanied his uncle to Seaway Mall, where he was inducted into the Welland Sports Wall of Fame.
Hominuk was an assistant manager of the 1973 Atlas Steels tyke baseball team - the city's first team to win a provincial title since 1949.
"Now he's out on the street. He's out on his own."
Konderka said he cannot accommodate his uncle because of his young children and a wife struggling with her own medical issues in a home "that's nowhere near accessible."
CAPTION: Mike Hominuk seen at his induction into the Welland Sports Wall of Fame in May. The double amputee has refused to stay at a homeless shelter after being discharged from hospital.Metroland file photo

Readers write Letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write-letters-editor/docview/3070997507/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write+Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-06-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Attacking journalists
is misguided
I am very concerned about the state of our democracy should Conservative party Leader Pierre Poilievre be victorious in the next federal election.
Historically, leaders such as Brian Mulroney and Joe Clark sought to work with other party leaders to introduce legislation which would have a positive benefit for the electorate in general.
Instead, Mr. Poilievre, in his public utterances, employs incessant attack language in which he seeks to belittle his opponents with disrespectful language and meaningless phrases devoid of any policy statements.
His most grievous misbehaviour is reserved for journalists who pose meaningful questions and are often met with disregard for the truth and a lack of any clarification or explanation. Mr. Poilievre seeks to deride and taunt these journalists - YouTube is rife with examples of Mr. Poilievre mocking and ridiculing journalists.
Mr. Poilievre seems to forget that every time he disrespects a journalist, he is disrespecting the voters who rely on the journalists who are acting as our proxies to pose the questions we need answered - to maintain our democracy, we need Mr. Poilievre to act respectfully.
Jeff Bondett, Ridgeway
Feeling
disenfranchised
Learn from the past, Letters,
June 18
The author obviously detests anyone from what he dismisses as the "right."
So, I guess he is clearly describing himself as from the "left."
I find it curious he is OK with disenfranchising half of the U.S. voting population. Funny that, but hardly democratic.
And then he goes on to imply other countries should consider taking the same action, including Canada.
I'm no fan of Donald Trump, but if the author is serious about the future of our neighbour, the U.S., then perhaps he can answer this question: What has Joe Biden's administration done in the past three years to support and protect those who voted for him in the last presidential election?
For myself, I feel disenfranchised in Canada. There is no longer any political party concerned with anything else but holding onto power, by raising taxes to promote policies destined to fail financially.
They are all "leftish" agendas, with not a single fiscally responsible, conservative agenda in sight.
Getting hysterical about Trump will do nothing to solve the problems we face in Canada.
Andrew Watts, Wainfleet
Cramming them in
Re: 106 townhomes coming to Ontario Road in Welland, June 12, 2023
Anyone who lives in south Welland can't miss the housing complex being built along Ontario Road, between Plymouth and King Street.
Was this the best place for what one might call affordable housing?
The buildings are jammed in like sardines, and I can't see adequate green space for children.
They are also erected on a busy thoroughfare, facing an industrial complex. Is this the best we can do to build adequate housing in Welland?
In my opinion, this property was best suited for a small strip mall.
Peter Voss, Welland
