Document 168 of 249

Letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters-editor/docview/3070411867/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-06-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Road to ruin in
Niagara-on-the-Lake
Re: Niagara-on-the-Lake councillors approve proposed hotel for Parliament Oak property, June 17
If Niagara-on-the-Lake town council approves this hotel, it's important to understand that it's not a single project council is approving.
The municipality won't be able to deny any similar seven-storey building on any residentially-zoned land anywhere in town. If this one is given approval, it changes from a town where people want to live, to a place where people won't even want to visit.
Please do not take this first step to the destruction of our town.
Wayne Murray, Niagara-on-the-Lake
Ignoring the climate put live at risk
Re: Wildfire that forced thousands out of Fort McMurray, Alta., officially under control, May 29
In Alberta, they have Danielle Smith; in Ontario, we have Doug Ford. Both have ignored climate change.
In Calgary, the water system is a mess, the province is in flames, yet Smith pushes petroleum products.
The science is clear: climate change is real, so why vote in a climate change denier? She is an environmental tragedy waiting to happen.
In Ontario, Ford is on a mission to destroy animal habitat regardless that the houses he approves for development are not affordable - otherwise there would be fewer mortgage failures.
These clueless politicians are putting lives at risk. If you vote against action in climate change, you will be sending us to disaster.
Russ Young, Fort Erie
Keep your dogs cool
Re: Ontario is facing 'dangerously high' heat and humidity this week, June 17
Ontario is expecting dangerously hot and humid weather conditions for most of the week, and police have responded to more than 10 calls this month about animals left in hot cars. Leaving an animal in a vehicle in this heat, even for a few minutes, can have deadly consequences.
While humans sweat to cool down, dogs must rely on panting to expel heat from their bodies. Contrary to popular belief, constant panting isn't normal or comfortable behaviour.
If a dog's tongue is hanging out and their mouth is wide open while they're panting heavily, they are likely hot and trying to cool themselves. It's even worse for breathing-impaired breeds such as bulldogs and pugs, whose flattened faces and distorted airways make them particularly vulnerable to overheating.
I urge all dog guardians, especially those with breeds prone to breathing issues, to be vigilant. Recognize signs of heat-related stress, which include heavy panting, excessive drooling, restlessness, vomiting, diarrhea, impaired balance or co-ordination, lethargy, seizures, and collapse. Never leave an animal in a car, an enclosed space without ventilation or air conditioning, or unattended outdoors.
Keep your animals safe at home, inside, with the air conditioning and/or fans running. Call police immediately if you see an animal in distress. There's no time to spare - heatstroke can kill in minutes.
Melissa Rae Sanger,
the PETA Foundation

Document 169 of 249

Letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters-editor/docview/3068362011/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-06-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Housing on command
Re: Three-storey infill apartment approved on narrow lot near downtown St. Catharines, June 11
What is St. Catharines council doing to our city? I grew up in a city that was beautiful, clean, friendly and prosperous, and I came back from serving in Canada's military in 1985.
Our city had changed, but the world had too.
Now it seems council is panicking. Ontario's premier has spoken - build more houses, hurry, find space, build more and more.
The latest move has me wondering what the inhabitants in city hall are thinking. Planning seems to be a dirty word; just squeeze houses, condos, apartments in on any handy piece of grass.
The 500-plus new residents at Geneva and Scott streets will snarl traffic for thousands who drive the area. Not to mention the folks who won't be able to get out of their driveways. The value of homes nearby will no doubt nosedive.
Has anyone from planning taken a look at the vacant grasslands along Vine Street south and Eastchester? There's plenty of empty space.
All this to follow the command of Doug Ford. Why are we turning our city into a hodgepodge of misplaced people and buildings?
Who cares? I do and four councillors. Do you?
Pete Morro, St. Catharines
An impossible reality
Re: Niagara-on-the-Lake councillors approve proposed hotel for Parliament Oak property, June 12
My next door neighbour in Niagara-on-the-Lake told me he's going to build his house 40 feet higher than my two-storey house. He then said it wouldn't be a house but a hotel accommodating more than 250 different strangers every night.
I said impossible. The town would never allow it.
I told him the town's bylaws and official plan and the rules for building heights wouldn't allow it in an established single-family residential zone.
Yet the staff and council of the sophisticated little town of Niagara-on-the-Lake just approved it. It's in the middle of heritage homes in Old Town.
It would also remove housing units for which the land is zoned, contrary to the province's instructions to create more housing.
This town has some of the most educated, talented, wise, experienced, knowledgeable residents. How did we end up with a council and staff who share none of those attributes?
Wayne Murray, Niagara-on-the-Lake
Mismanagement
of money
Re: Bank of Canada cuts interest rates, June 5
Nobody helped me buy my first home from someone else's investment income.
In 1973, my first mortgage was at 8.75 per cent while making $4.50 an hour.
Meagre interest rates have caused huge debt problems for ordinary Canadians and interest on the federal deficit has ballooned.
Why do you think redistributing wealth is fair when someone went out and bought a million-dollar house with a low down payment?
It's not my fault some people can't handle money.
Why don't MPs admit they mishandled the economy instead of trying to penalize "Joe Taxpayer"?
Peter Voss, Welland

Document 170 of 249

Niagara MPPs weigh in on largest cabinet in province's history: Retired political science prof says cabinet size could be sign early election on horizon

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/niagara-mpps-weigh-on-largest-cabinet-provinces/docview/3067797147/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Niagara+MPPs+weigh+in+on+largest+cabinet+in+province%27s+history%3A+Retired+political+science+prof+says+cabinet+size+could+be+sign+early+election+on+horizon&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-06-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Niagara West MPP Sam Oosterhoff said the new 36-member provincial cabinet - the largest in Ontario's history - will serve the region well.
But opposition party members from Niagara say it's nothing more than keeping members of government happy and a distraction from issues Ontarians are concerned about.
Oosterhoff said it's "good news" Niagara will now have an MPP at the cabinet table, "working to deliver for the region, as well as the good of the whole province."
The 26-year-old Progressive Conservative was one of eight MPPs named associate ministers in Premier Doug Ford's cabinet shuffle last week.
The MPP now serves as associate minister of energy-intensive industries under new energy minister Stephen Lecce, who previously served as education minister.
Oosterhoff is the only PC MPP in Niagara, with the other three ridings represented by New Democrats.
"The premier has tasked me with ensuring the energy needs of our growing industries from automotive to steel making and technology innovation to mining are met, so that we can grow our economy and provide good jobs for families," he said.
"I will have that focus, while working with minister Lecce on broader energy and electrification projects."
While in opposition a decade ago, Progressive Conservatives promised to cut the Ontario cabinet from 27 down to 16 ministers if they won government. Instead, Ford has exceeded that target by 20 ministers.
This, after Ford slashed the size of Toronto city hall from 47 to 25 councillors in efforts to find greater efficiency.
Also in the past, he criticized Niagara for having too many politicians. Although his government has so far not slashed numbers, itis reviewing governance in regions across Ontario, including Niagara.
Niagara Centre NDP MPP Jeff Burch said Ford just appointed a "$10-million cabinet and took off on a 19-week vacation."
"He's giving more of his MPPs a raise and has created the biggest cabinet in Ontario's history, all while people struggle to pay their bills, find a home and find a family doctor."
The cabinet shuffle came as the legislature rose for an extended summer break. It won't resume sitting until Oct. 21.
"It is ludicrous for a government that claims to be all about cutting red tape to now have the most bloated cabinet in Ontario's history," said Burch.
"We've literally got a minister of red tape reduction in a cabinet that has ballooned to 36 ministers. What if they started by reducing the red tape in their own cabinet?"
He also decried the creation of "silly ministerial files plus associate ministers to staff them."
"Associate minister of energy-intensive industries? A separate minister of sport? This is a government that is out of touch."
Quite the opposite, said Oosterhoff.
"This cabinet is focused on creating good jobs with bigger paycheques, keeping taxes low to put more money back in the people's pockets, investing in the infrastructure our communities need, and building a stronger economy for Ontario workers and families," he said.
"At the end of the day, we will be judged on how we deliver on these priorities, rather than how many people it takes to get the job done."
St. Catharines NDP MPP Jennie Stevens, however, said Ford is sending the "wrong" message by giving his MPPs a "19-week vacation with pay raises."
"Quite frankly, that is not putting your community first and they should all turn it down," she said.
"It is deeply concerning for any MPP to accept a made-up cabinet position with a large pay raise while regular families are struggling to pay their bills, find a home, and endure long waits in the ER."
Niagara Falls NDP MPP Wayne Gates said the government should be "spending less time making up new titles for people" and focusing more on the issues Ontarians are "worried about."
"They're worried about affordability, they're worried about being able to pay their rent, or how they're going to pay their mortgage, how they're going to buy their groceries," he said.
Retired Brock University political science professor David Siegel said the size of cabinet is something that "jumps around" from the time to time.
"It's not so unusual that a party would at the beginning of their term ... take great pride in having a small cabinet and then over time it increases - that's sort of the norm," he said.
"Frequently there's a big increase in the size of cabinet just before an election because you want to give a cabinet seat or some kind of associate or parliamentary secretary seat to as many people as you can because it increases their prestige when they're running for office."
Although the next provincial election isn't scheduled for another two years, there have been rumblings Ford may call an early election.
"This might be some of the tea leaves that can give you an idea that an early election might be coming up," said Siegel.
CAPTION: Niagara West MPP Sam Oosterhoff, now associate minister of energy-intensive industries, says it's "good news" Niagara will have a local MPP at the cabinet table.Niagara Centre NDP MPP Jeff Burch says "it is ludicrous for a government that claims to be all about cutting red tape now has the most bloated cabinet in Ontario's history."Retired Brock University political science professor David Siegel says it's not unusual to see the size of cabinet change from time to time.Mark Newman Metroland file photo

Document 171 of 249

Stevens takes shot at Oosterhoff for 'lack of investment' in local tourism

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/stevens-takes-shot-at-oosterhoff-lack-investment/docview/3067262558/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Stevens+takes+shot+at+Oosterhoff+for+%27lack+of+investment%27+in+local+tourism&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-06-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: St. Catharines MPP Jennie Stevens has taken a shot at Niagara West MPP Sam Oosterhoff's tenure as parliamentary assistant to the former minister of tourism.
In a letter to new Tourism Minister Stan Cho congratulating him on his new role after Premier Doug Ford's cabinet shuffle last week, Stevens said it's "critical that we acknowledge the considerable gaps left by the previous minister and the parliamentary assistant for the Ministry of Tourism, which have hindered the growth and sustainability of tourism in our province."
Former minister of tourism, culture and sport Neil Lumsden is now solely responsible for sport, as Cho becomes new minister of tourism, culture and gaming, including responsibility for the Ontario Lottery and Gaming Corp.
Oosterhoff was Lumsden's parliamentary assistant. He's now in cabinet as associate minister of energy-intensive industries.
"Despite representing regions close to Niagara, a corridor renowned for being a driving force of tourism's economic impact, the minister had failed to address key issues," said Stevens. "Their tenure was marked by a lack of investment to critical requests from the tourism sector, which has resulted in missed opportunities for development and growth."
In an emailed statement to the Niagara Falls Review, Oosterhoff defended his government's investment in regional tourism initiatives.
"This spring, the Ontario government announced it is reinvesting in expanded GO Transit service to Niagara 365 days a year with year-round weekend service from Toronto's Union Station to Niagara Falls. The government is giving a major boost to the region by unlocking access to Niagara Falls, a world-class tourist destination and economic driver for Ontario," he said.
"In 2022-23, the ministry and its agencies provided over $12 million in support to the Niagara region. In 2022-23, $75,000 was provided to Niagara for the 2022 Canada Summer Games, $5,392 for the 2021 Canadian Junior Squash Open, and $2,000 for the 2022 MJT Golf National Team Challenge."
Stevens said as tourism businesses gear up for the summer, they have important requests that have been "ignored."
"This negligence has left many businesses with a stunted and suboptimal overall growth potential, despite being a part of some of the most vibrant and historically rich regions," wrote Stevens.
While there are many recommendations to "better support" Ontario tourism, she outlined three priorities.
"Despite having the largest tourism industry in Canada, Ontario currently has the lowest per capita spend on destination marketing in Canada," said Stevens. "Stakeholders will confirm that future tourism growth lies in cultivating greater inbound visitation. It is disappointing that provinces like Nova Scotia spend 800 per cent more per capita than Ontario on its destination marketing budgets. "
She said an auditor general's report in December pointed out a provincial tourism strategy has not been developed since the Progressive Conservative party formed government in 2018.
"Despite the ministry acknowledging the need in 2022 State of Ontario's Tourism report, we urgently need a comprehensive strategy to guide our actions and investments, ensuring long-term and short-term growth for the sector," said Stevens.
She said Shaw Festival, a "crown jewel" for Niagara tourism, requires capital investment support to revitalize the Royal George Theatre.
"While they have successfully raised most of the funds privately, the Shaw still needs approval for provincial support to complete the project and create 850 jobs in Niagara," said Stevens.
Oosterhoff said Ontario's marketing agency, Destination Ontario, continues to work on campaigns that promote Niagara and position the region as a "signature" destination, including to the United States and through domestic summer and fall market drive campaigns.
"In April 2023, the government invested $19.5 million in one-time funding through the Experience Ontario 2023 and Marquee Event programs to provide support to in-person festivals and events across the province. In previous years, these programs supported several events in Niagara."
He said 10 events will be held in Niagara this year.
"That is a total of $603,172 in funding allocated from Experience Ontario to the Niagara region," said Oosterhoff.
"In addition, $1 million was provided to support the 2023 Grey Cup Festival held in Niagara. The province supported the Tourism Partnership of Niagara with $3.2 million to develop industry led strategies for product development, investment attraction, workforce development, marketing and brand positioning, and industry partnerships."
He said the government continues to explore opportunities in Niagara, including engaging with Niagara Parks Commission on its ongoing work to develop "innovative" new attractions, as well as ministry programs to support and provide funding to promote festivals and events, wine and culinary tourism, Indigenous and francophone experiences and Niagara as a "live music hub."
CAPTION: St. Catharines NDP MPP Jennie Stevens, right, has taken aim at Niagara West Progressive Conservative MPP Sam Oosterhoff's record as parliamentary assistant to former minister of tourism Neil Lumsden, saying their tenure was marked by a "lack of investment" in the local tourism sector.Metroland file photo

Readers write Letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write-letters-editor/docview/3067262539/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write+Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-06-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: More garbage cans needed
Littering concerns lead Niagara Falls to review cleanup programs, June 5
I have adopted two parks in Niagara Falls through my business, Evergreen Thrift Store. They are Grey Robinson Park in 2019 and Don W. Johnson Park in 2020. I have picked up garbage at least three times a year ever since.
The first winter I did a cleanup at Grey Robinson Park, I noticed there was one less garbage can, only one at the entrance. There was no longer a can near the playground, and there was a lot of garbage strewn about that area.
Thinking it was stolen or vandalized, I emailed my contact with the city. I was informed it in fact wasn't stolen. It turns out a lot of parks in the city have garbage cans removed in winter time due to staffing shortages, with staff redirected to other winter-maintenance responsibilities. This was discouraging to say the least.
I understand the reasons, but it is still disappointing each winter to have much more garbage to pick up because of this.
Nick Cupolo, Niagara Falls
Filling the
money tree
Do we get the governments we deserve?, Letters, June 7
I can't fault David Fowler's letter and agree with him completely. Sadly, as he so rightly says, there seems no reason to believe anything will change any time soon.
An even bigger problem is while I also agree another Doug Ford government will make matters even worse, there is no political party in Canada, federally or provincially, who would do anything but claim a majority mandate from voters based on the reality of a desperately small minority of votes.
We no longer have any party of any political persuasion even interested in any fiscal responsibility with tax dollars.
It's government money, so theirs to spend, and when it runs out they have all those nice voters who they can tax to fill up their money tree once again.
Andrew Watts, Wainfleet
Keep kids
away from
lawn mowers
As we are in the season of lawn maintenance, I would like to remind everyone about potential hazards associated with machinery such as lawn mowers.
I was two when an accident altered my life. Playing outside, I ventured too close to a moving mower, unaware of the danger. The operator was unaware of my presence and backed up, not realizing I had stumbled and fallen. I was rushed to hospital where doctors amputated my right arm above the elbow and part of my left hand.
As a graduate of The War Amps Child Amputee (CHAMP) Program, I advocate for safety measures around lawn mowers. I accept who I am, but wouldn't want another child to go through what I did. It's important to recognize accidents can occur in the blink of an eye, with lifelong consequences.
Denise Swedlo, Brooklin, Ont.

Readers write Letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write-letters-editor/docview/3065224020/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write+Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-06-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Helplessly
watching Ford government
'Terrible deal': Doug Ford attacked over possible $1 billion price tag for liberalized booze sales, June 3
Two years ago on June 2, 2022, Ontarians went to the polls.
Progressive Conservative Leader Doug Ford won a majority of seats in the election, allowing him to crow about having the support of an overwhelming majority of Ontarians.
Well, wait a minute. About 40 per cent of eligible voters bothered to go to the polls and about 40 per cent of them voted Conservative, i.e. about 16 per cent of eligible voters.
Can we fix this election system that allows a small minority to run the show? Probably not.
First, many people are completely disinterested in this problem - they don't care. So first-past-the-post elections are here to stay. The Conservatives and the Liberals benefit most from the current system, so don't expect change from them.
We watch helplessly as Ford throws billions of tax dollars down black holes such as the privatization of alcohol sales, while our hospitals and schools beg for more funding. And now he is talking about calling an early election ... a complete waste of time, energy and money.
And we already know the likely outcome will be a reinstatement of the PC government with less than 20 per cent support of the eligible voters.
When 60 per cent of the electorate doesn't vote, do we deserve what we get?
David Fowler, Wainfleet
A small step
in right direction
Let developers pay: Large hikes to development charges approved by Niagara Falls city council, May 31
Passing the costs on to the developers instead of the taxpayers in housing construction is a wonderful addition to a sector of cost changes that are overdue and just.
When we have the wisdom and courage to get to the bottom of unjust charges for much more than only this we may have what Pierre Trudeau called a "just society."
The question is, will the notoriously mendacious groups involved in this chicanery admit the unfairness and greed and even dishonesty involved in their organizational practices? Or will they use their influence and power in the political and economic spheres of society to discredit their questioners and investigators and intimidate them with the so-called "law"?
If that be the case then things will be ramped up considerably and truly our democracy being under attack will no longer be questioned by those who disbelieve we could have such powers at the top of the deck and scattered throughout basically honest people as a corrupting influence.
If we can't fix such piddling things as these then what chance do we have of putting an end to such hells as the death and horror in Gaza and Ukraine and other catastrophes yet to come?
Wayne Borbey,
Niagara Falls

Document 174 of 249

Inquest called into Heather Winterstein's hospital death: Country needs to get to 'the bottom of how this happened'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/inquest-called-into-heather-wintersteins-hospital/docview/3064875095/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Inquest+called+into+Heather+Winterstein%27s+hospital+death%3A+Country+needs+to+get+to+%27the+bottom+of+how+this+happened%27&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-06-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: An Ontario coroner's inquest has been called into the death of Heather Winterstein, two and a half years after the 24-year-old Indigenous woman died in the St. Catharines hospital's emergency department.
The announcement on Wednesday by the regional supervising coroner in Hamilton was welcome news to the Niagara Indigenous community and others who have been advocating for an inquest alongside Winterstein's family.
Jennifer Dockstader, executive director of Fort Erie Native Friendship Centre, said it is pleased an inquest has been called so it can start getting some answers and direction.
"This is an issue that's happening across the country to Indigenous people in health care. How we can prevent this from happening ever again?" she said.
"How do we improve a health-care system that insists on making medical decisions based on personal bias of whatever health-care professional is triaging?"
Dockstader said it's about keeping all people safe by using science to make medical decisions.
"We believe that this is in the interests of all Canadians that we get to the bottom of how this happened to a 24-year-old young Indigenous woman."
Winterstein died in hospital on Dec. 10, 2021, of necrotizing fasciitis, a type of flesh-eating disease that is preventable if treated properly.
She went to Niagara Health's St. Catharines hospital the day before feeling unwell with severe back pain and was given Tylenol and sent home.
The next day, the back pain became more severe and she was taken back to the hospital by ambulance. She died while waiting for two and a half hours in the emergency department.
The coroner's office said the inquest will examine the circumstances surrounding Winterstein's death. The jury may make recommendations aimed at preventing further deaths.
A date and location for the inquest is to be announced.
"Niagara Health recognizes that the family of Heather Winterstein has long been requesting an inquest into her death and we will fully participate in the coroner's work," Niagara Health president and chief executive officer Lynn Guerriero said in a statement.
"We welcome any recommendations that will help ensure we continue to improve the care for Indigenous patients and their families."
Niagara Health undertook a third-party review with a panel of external experts after Winterstein's death. The panel found "unconscious bias may have played a role" in the care she received and made recommendations.
Guerriero said the hospital system is actively implementing all the recommendations made by the panel.
"As a hospital, we have made significant investments in improving care for Indigenous patients and their families, including the development of the Indigenous health services and reconciliation team," she said.
"This six-person team directly supports Indigenous patients and their families who are receiving care in the hospital."
She said Niagara Health also has its first Indigenous health service plan, which identified areas of focus and priorities as identified by the Indigenous community.
Jim Bradley, chair of Niagara Region, which oversees Niagara Emergency Medical Services paramedics, said he wanted to assure the public the municipality will provide any information or assistance asked of it by the Coroner's Office as part of the inquest.
"We are always keen to identify ways that our processes and practices can be strengthened," he said in a statement.
"To this end, we welcome this fulsome review, and we will consider any recommendations that may be identified as part of this inquest."
Calls for an inquest have been happening since Winterstein's death.
Saleh Waziruddin of Niagara Region Anti-Racism Association, which endorsed Niagara Chapter Native Women Inc.'s call for an inquest, said it was a long time coming.
"We hope that this will lead to justice for Heather Winterstein and all the many Indigenous people who've been suffering under inequality and mistreatment," he said.
Waziruddin said it's hoped that inquest recommendations lead to changes in the way Indigenous people are treated and to the policies and practices of government agencies like hospitals.
St. Catharines MPP Jennie Stevens said in a statement that she stands with Winterstein's family and the Indigenous community in their pursuit of justice.
"Heather Winterstein's passing was a devastating loss for our community, putting a heartbreaking spotlight on the considerable biases and gaps in health equity provided to Indigenous individuals," Stevens said.
"The news today is a credit to the Fort Erie Native Friendship Centre, Métis community and the advocacy of Heather's family to correct the systemic failures that deserve the attention of the province."
Stevens said that while the calling of an inquest is positive news, it had taken too long. She said she worked with the family to push for the inquest and is committed to pushing for accountability and transparency.
That was echoed by fellow NDP MPP Wayne Gates of Niagara Falls.
"I'd like to commend the work the Indigenous communities in Niagara have done to continue advocating for truth and accountability in this tragedy. However, now isn't a time to stand down or become complacent in the important work of ensuring equity, fairness and respect in our health-care system.
"I'm hoping this inquest will be a positive first step and that we can continue to work together to ensure our health-care services provide equitable treatment across the board," he said.
CAPTION: Heather Winterstein, 24, was sent home from the hospital in St. Catharines and died the next day in the emergency room.Francine Shimizu photo

CREDIT: Karena Walter Reporter

Readers write Letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write-letters-editor/docview/3064514803/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write+Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-06-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Adding to the
family history
Several long-running Welland businesses operated from
232 East Main St., June 1
This article was a delight to read and one I will add to my family history record.
My mother was born in the upper apartment of that building in 1918. Her mother died there from Spanish flu in 1920. My mother was the daughter of Norval Lynn and Asenath Hamilton, sister to James Hamilton. I have many memories of the Hamilton & Ferguson store during the 1950s and '60s.
In addition, my father was trained in the shoe business by Harry Holcomb, and for a couple of years when I was in high school I worked for Harry myself in that building.
Please allow me to make a correction. It was James Hamilton, not Tom Ferguson, who was born near Dresden, Ont., in 1900 and came with his family to Welland in, or about, 1909.
James's parents and all of his siblings, including my grandparents, are buried in Woodlawn Cemetery. James Hamilton's grandfather came to Canada from County Armagh in the mid 1840s.
Thank you for this article that honours my family and their role in Welland's history.
Jim McQuiggin, Welland
Give us train service
How will bridge twinning improve traffic?, Letters, May 15
I second Mr. Frugier's opinion regarding twinning Garden City Skyway. It will not relieve the traffic on the QEW, which is getting worse every day. More frequent and reliable GO/Via trains would definitely ease the traffic nightmare, considering that the vast majority of Niagara visitors come by car.
I hope Premier Doug Ford and others reconsider this project and invest, instead, in a reliable 21st-century train service from Niagara to Toronto.
George Haber, Niagara Falls
'Bernstein Mass'
astounding
I anticipated opening Saturday's newspaper to read a review on a production I attended Friday night. Nothing there but perhaps there wasn't time to make deadline. On Monday I rushed through the paper looking but was disappointed again.
I was privileged to be in the audience at our magnificent performing arts centre for the Canadian premier of "Bernstein Mass."
At the request of Jacqueline Kennedy, it premiered at the opening of the Kennedy Center on Sept. 8, 1971, honouring the late president John F. Kennedy. We are fortunate to have had Robert Cooper bring this production to our stage.
We were entertained and amazed by the talented Chorus Niagara Chamber Orchestra, Chorus Niagara Children's Choir and Laura Secord High School Chamber Singers.
Nathan Keoughan stole the show as celebrant/priest.
"Mass" is indeed a celebration of human faith. The performers and presenters were given a long and rousing standing ovation. The audience fully embraced the closing remarks of the celebrant/priest: "The mass is ended. Go in peace."
We all had goosebumps. It was an innovative and astounding performance.
Nancy Reimer, St. Catharines

Full text: Sad end for Walker YMCA site
Owners of former Y hope St. Catharines will reconsider partnership, Dec. 13, 2023
I regularly drive along YMCA Drive in St. Catharines and look at the condition of what was once a great facility. What a shame that this has happened!
The Walker family donated much of the funding to create the Walker Family YMCA facility in St. Catharines, and now it's awaiting its turn for the wrecking ball.
I'm told and it has been reported by this newspaper that the developer offered to lease the building to the city, but by then it would have cost too much for taxpayers to rehabilitate.
There was a time when I, a swimmer, applied to join the Y. I was surprised at the cost.
I explained that I only wanted to swim ... the clerk told me that once I'm in, I could use the other facilities with only having paid for the pool.
Obviously, the place catered to a younger set than me, and not enough of that set were interested in joining.
So, here we are anticipating yet another condominium on this site. What a waste!
Now that the grounds are filled with weeds, we hopefully will see our bylaw officers filling out tickets. A sad ending to a once-promising endeavour.
Pete Morro, St. Catharines
Hospital project
a mistake
Niagara contingent to join health-care rally at Queen's Park, May 29
Leaders of our health-care system are saying we are short of doctors and nurses, that there's no money. What does Niagara Health do? Build a brand new hospital in Niagara Falls.
What is wrong with the old Niagara Falls hospital? It can be updated in certain areas, or added to; the same is true for the Welland hospital.
The new site is not a south Niagara hospital because you have to travel to the northeast from Welland. I think every city should have its own hospital to serve public demand and that includes Fort Erie and Port Colborne. There is money, taxpayers' money.
If Premier Doug Ford wants to privatize health care he is not thinking about people in need, of their health care since it will be a runaround for many to get to the new hospital. Look at all those townhouses that also increase population.
So Niagara Health cuts back services in some communities and is building this new hospital and not following the map of Niagara.
I'm thankful for the efforts of the Niagara Health Coalition, but can they get ahead of this huge mistake?
Niagara Health is supposed to keep the Welland hospital and maybe add on to it, but to eliminate urgent care centres and force people to go out of their communities for health care is a huge mistake.
Roscoe Reilly, Welland

Readers write Letters to the editor

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write+Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-06-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Voices deserve
to be heard
Niagara contingent to join health-care rally, May 28
This article speaks about the health coalition and volunteers and supporters who went to Queen's Park Thursday to oppose closures and partial closures of hospitals, emergency departments and urgent care centres. We were also there to oppose privatization of health care.
Hannah Jansen, spokesperson for Health Minister Sylvia Jones, called Ontario Health Coalition "an out of touch, NDP-backed special interest group."
It was a cheap shot thrown out to deter people from speaking out. I will not stop. We have spoken to tens of thousands of Ontario residents frightened about the future and if they will be able to receive the care they need, when they need it.
If the government is not there to represent every constituent, they have been misinformed about their obligation.
Our urgent care centre is shut down overnight due to lack of staffing. I'd like to know where the 17,000 new nurses and 2,400 new physicians are. Not in Fort Erie. This government has yet to properly staff this facility and this is not the only one in Ontario.
I'm sure people receiving a new hospital are happy, but I also think of the loss people in Fort Erie will experience when they have to try to fit into Niagara Falls. It will be almost impossible for many to visit or stay with a loved one.
This is not the care we paid for. Open the rural ERs and urgent cares, stop the closures.
Heather Kelley, Fort Erie
Blame the
trash tossers
Downtown St. Catharines trash, Letters, May 9, May 13
It's easy to criticize St. Catharines for the trash throughout downtown.
But there's been many hands on board to address the never-ending problem. The new vacuum cleaner St. Catharines is purchasing with my tax dollar is yet another attempt to get a handle on this senseless mess.
It's time to put the blame on the perpetrators. They just drop garbage anywhere and everywhere, despite being feet away from a city trash receptacle. Even donated clothing is tossed hither and yon. This is sheer laziness and disregard on the part of the perpetrators. How can you blame the city for the behaviour of ungrateful others?
Brenda Fox, St. Catharines

skyway twin
How will bridge twinning improve traffic?, Letters,
May 15
We would like to endorse Bernard Frugier's letter proposing that funds for twinning of the Garden City Skyway be spent instead on a decent rail connection with Toronto. We would like to add to his suggestion a serious ferry service. This would be more beneficial for commuters as well as people like us who find it increasingly difficult to visit family in Toronto because of heavy traffic.
Mike and Jacquie Aston, St. Catharines

Document 178 of 249

'The government has got to start to listen': Niagara health-care advocate at rally says we're 'all fighting absolutely for the same thing'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/government-has-got-start-listen/docview/3062844608/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27The+government+has+got+to+start+to+listen%27%3A+Niagara+health-care+advocate+at+rally+says+we%27re+%27all+fighting+absolutely+for+the+same+thing%27&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-06-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Heather Kelley says she sometimes feels isolated when fighting to preserve hospital services.
The Fort Erie woman doesn't feel that way any longer.
The founder of Fort Erie Healthcare SOS joined thousands of people from throughout Ontario on Thursday who rallied outside the provincial legislature, "all fighting absolutely for the same thing."
"It was fantastic," she said.
Although she didn't want to estimate the size of the crowd at the Queen's Park rally organized by the Ontario Health Coalition, she said it was "far more people than I anticipated."
"It really was quite a show of solidarity among all the different political leaders, minus, of course, the government in power ... It was so nice to see so many people out, because sometimes you feel very isolated."
She said many demonstrators were "extremely angry" about the state of health-care services in their communities - such as West Grey Mayor Kevin Eccles, who recently declared a state of emergency after the hospital system serving his community announced plans to remove 10 patient beds from Durham hospital due to a shortage of nurses.
"He is really looking to make connections right across Ontario and, of course, that is what we've been trying to do as a small group," Kelley said.
Eccles publicly apologized earlier this month after telling the president of South Bruce Grey Health Centre "to keep her hands off the Durham hospital and I'll promise to keep my hands off of your throat."
Kelley said that "there's such a feeling of hopelessness so often, and you don't feel you can do anything but sign a letter or a petition, or go to a rally."
"You need sometimes to be able to say exactly what's going on inside you and he (Eccles) certainly doesn't hold back," she said. "The people today at the rally, they weren't holding back. They're angry. It's not about giving up, it's about moving forward and trying to change the minds of the government."
Kelley said she had an opportunity to speak from the podium during the rally, and shared the struggles Niagarans have faced for more than a decade as they fought to preserve hospital services.
"Now, we're still fighting for urgent care," she said.
She said the outcry from communities across Ontario have buoyed her.
"I've always been hopeful, but to see so many people now really speaking out and not being afraid," Kelley said, "the government has got to start to listen to the people."
Niagara Centre NDP MPP Jeff Burch brought their message inside the legislature, asking Health Minister Sylvia Jones when she will "finally listen to the citizens of Niagara and step in to restore full urgent- care services in south Niagara."
He said residents of Port Colborne and Fort Erie were among the thousands of people at the rally, "protesting this government's disastrous health-care policies and privatization scheme."
Niagara Health was forced to close the urgent-care centres on evenings and weekends last summer in order to preserve services at emergency departments while coping with staff shortages.
Burch said residents of the communities "have now gone 330 days without after-hours services at their local urgent care centres."
Nolan Quinn, parliamentary assistant to Jones, responded by referring to the government's $3.1-billion investment in the south Niagara hospital under construction in Niagara Falls, as well as expanded interprofessional primary health teams.
"In Ontario, we have a plan and it's working," Quinn said.
Burch also said it has been "a staggering 457 days" since Niagara's hospital system was forced to temporarily transfer after-hours emergency surgeries from Niagara Health's Welland hospital to other sites "as a result of this government's failed health-care policies and mistreatment of health-care workers."
He said that cut, too, was due to staffing shortages.
"Welland's population is surging and housing targets were surpassed by 276 per cent last year alone. We should be seeing a historic expansion of services in south Niagara," Burch said.
Quinn again referred to the new south Niagara hospital investment, adding that it was necessary to replace "outdated infrastructure that the NDP allowed to crumble under the Liberal government."
He said the health minister recently met with Port Colborne Mayor Bill Steele "about the creative ideas they're exploring locally."
"We will continue to work with all of our health-care partners across the province to ensure that we have the best publicly funded health-care system in the north, south, east and west," Quinn said.
CAPTION: Fort Erie Healthcare SOS founder Heather Kelley says she feels hopeful about efforts to preserve hospital services after joining thousands of people at a rally at Queen's Park on Thursday.Alison Langley Metroland file photo

CREDIT: Allan Benner Reporter

Subject: Emergency medical care; Health care; Hospital systems; Labor shortages

Document 179 of 249

DSBN suing social media giants: 'We want our kids to have positive mental health'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/dsbn-suing-social-media-giants/docview/3061629389/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=DSBN+suing+social+media+giants%3A+%27We+want+our+kids+to+have+positive+mental+health%27&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-05-30&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: District School Board of Niagara has added its name to the growing list of Ontario school boards suing the giants behind Facebook, Instagram, Snapchat and TikTok, citing the negative impacts of social media on students in the classroom.
On Wednesday, the region's largest school board said its job is to educate students and provide them with opportunities to grow skills in a safe, inclusive space, but over the past decade, that has become increasingly difficult as it's getting harder for students to maintain their focus on learning because of the pressures and negative impacts of social media.
Knowing there is a problem and seeing the evidence of it every day, it felt "compelled" to join the multibillion-dollar legal fight "so we could work toward a solution."
"We felt that we needed to stand up and become a part of this to say it's unacceptable," Marian Reimer Friesen, superintendent of curriculum and student achievement, said in an interview.
"We want to be part of the movement that helps to impact positive change because we care. We want our kids to have positive mental health, to be able to focus and (pay) attention to their learning, and it's really important to us that we're part of that."
In March, four of Ontario's largest school boards - Toronto District School Board, Peel District School Board, Toronto Catholic District School Board and Ottawa-Carleton District School Board - announced they would pursue legal action against Meta Platforms (Facebook and Instagram), Snap Inc. (Snapchat) and ByteDance Ltd. (TikTok).
Their lawsuits, which total $4.5 billion in damages, allege that the addictive nature of social media platforms is impacting students and rewiring the ways children think, behave and learn, "leaving educators and schools to manage the fallout."
Five additional school boards - DSBN; Catholic boards in Ottawa, Dufferin-Peel and York; and Trillium Lakelands District School Board - and two private schools - Holy Name of Mary College School, a Catholic girl's school in Mississauga, and the Jewish Eitz Chaim Schools - filed lawsuits this week.
The latest lawsuits total about $2.6 billion in additional damages.
Reimer Friesen said conversations took place internally following the March announcement, and considered input from educators, students, student-leaders and parents, and it became clear that while social media is a reality, they were "very concerned about the impact it is having on students."
"We're seeing the increase in stress and anxiety, depression, lack of sleep, and also acknowledging that it's bringing with it these conflicts and social circumstances into the classroom that are taking away from instruction," she said.
"When teachers need to navigate and investigate, when administrators need to spend their time focusing on unpacking a circumstance that evolved on social media over the night, they're not able to attend to the things that are priorities."
The legal action targets the addictive properties of social media, which the initial filing claimed is putting "massive strains" on resources, forcing boards to divert resources to personnel, including the increasing need for in-school mental health programming and IT costs, hours and funds.
Reimer Friesen said the DSBN is adding mental-health professionals, social workers and youth councillors, with its IT department doing its due diligence.
Education Minister Stephen Lecce has said the court action is a waste of staff resources and time, with Premier Doug Ford wanting boards to focus on the "core values of education."
In response, Reimer Friesen said DSBN's goal is to educate students and ensure they have a safe, inclusive space to learn and achieve success, which is why the public board joined the lawsuits.
"We do believe that we're doing what's right for our students," she said. "Then we're going to be in a better position to be able to focus on teaching and learning, which is our primary reason for being, and we can do so with the confidence that our kids are safe, that they're healthy and they're well and able to focus on the learning."
A spokesperson for Snapchat said the platform was "intentionally designed to be different from traditional social media, with a focus on helping Snapchatters communicate with their close friends" and while it has work to do, it feels good about the role Snapchat "plays in helping close friends feel connected, happy and prepared as they face the many challenges of adolescence."
A Meta spokesperson said the company has teens' "interests at heart," with more than "30 tools to support teens and their families," including parent controls, age verification technology and notifications to take regular breaks.
The boards have retained Neinstein LLP, a Toronto-based litigation firm, to represent them, under the name Schools for Social Media Change.
None of the allegations have been tested in court.
Asked about whether Niagara Catholic District School Board is considering taking a similar step, education director Camillo Cipriano said in a statement, "Our board will be discussing this at a June meeting with some feedback from our legal counsel on whether we will be following this process as well in Niagara Catholic."
With files from the Toronto Star
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Victoria Nicolaou Reporter With files from the Toronto Star

Niagara contingent to attend health-care rally at Queen's Park: Minister's spokesperson calls rally organizers 'an out-of-touch, NDP-backed special interest group'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/niagara-contingent-attend-health-care-rally-at/docview/3060987417/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Niagara+contingent+to+attend+health-care+rally+at+Queen%27s+Park%3A+Minister%27s+spokesperson+calls+rally+organizers+%27an+out-of-touch%2C+NDP-backed+special+interest+group%27&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-05-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Niagarans will be among as many as 10,000 people expected at a health-care rally at Queen's Park on Thursday, during which demonstrators will demand a moratorium on hospital closures and privatization of services.
The local chapter of the Ontario Health Coalition will be providing a bus for Niagara residents wanting to participate in the rally, while health care-related labour groups also plan to ensure Niagara is represented during the event protesting hospital cuts across the province, as well as provincial government plans to expand private health-care services.
While health care in Niagara is coping with significant pressures such as staff shortages, Niagara Health Coalition chair Sue Hotte said the issues are not unique to the region.
"It's everywhere," she said. "It really is horrific, the number of hospitals that are closing, the number of hospitals that have lost services."
An Ontario Health Coalition report published last December said 867 emergency departments were temporarily closed last year, and one was permanently shuttered.
Other closures included 316 urgent care centres, two outpatient laboratories, 11 obstetrics units, one intensive care unit and one labour and delivery unit.
In Niagara, Hotte said, the shortage of medical professionals has led to reduced hours of service at urgent care centres in Port Colborne and Fort Erie, while emergency surgeries are being transferred from Welland hospital to other sites on evenings and weekends.
The local hospital system is also facing a budget deficit while operating at 115 per cent over capacity.
"The hospitals and health systems are in real trouble. They have staffing and funding issues," Hotte said. "It's hard."
Brenda Allan, president of Ontario Public Service Employees Union Local 215, said the union representing workers at Niagara Health facilities and at Hotel Dieu Shaver Health and Rehabilitation Centre has also organized a bus to bring workers, as well as their family members, to the rally. It will stop in Hamilton and Mississauga to pick up additional passengers.
Allan said she also expects a large group of Canadian Union of Public Employees representatives at the rally because many of them will already be in Toronto attending that union's convention.
In an email, Hannah Jansen, a spokesperson for Health Minister Sylvia Jones, called the Ontario Health Coalition "an out-of-touch, NDP-backed special interest group."
She said the coalition "has spent the last decade accomplishing nothing while standing ideologically opposed to innovation taking place in the health-care system, (while) our government is taking bold action to connect more people to the care they need, when they need it."
"Our government has made record investments in our health-care system, are getting shovels in the ground for over 50 hospital developments, achieving the shortest wait times in Canada and hiring a record number of 17,000 new nurses and 2,400 new physicians this year alone," Jensen added.
Hotte said the buses from Niagara will be among about 60 carrying passengers from throughout Ontario to the rally.
She said the health coalition's bus leaves the Fort Erie Leisureplex at 8:15 a.m., and it will not wait for any late arrivals.
"We're telling people to be there for 8 a.m.," she said.
It will then stop at the St. Catharines GO bus station on YMCA Drive at 9 a.m. to pick up additional passengers before travelling to Toronto.
Thursday's rally begins at noon at Nathan Phillips Square at Queen and Bay streets.
From there, the crowd will walk along University Avenue to Queen's Park, joining the demonstration outside the main legislative building.
CAPTION: Niagara Health Coalition chair Sue Hotte speaks during a recent rally at Niagara Health's Welland hospital, with Niagara Centre MPP Jeff Burch. The organization is participating in a large rally planned for Queen's Park on Thursday.metroland file photo

Document 181 of 249

OLT hearing on Rand Estate development takes break until July: Testimony heard from planning, transportation, heritage experts

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/olt-hearing-on-rand-estate-development-takes/docview/3060709786/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=OLT+hearing+on+Rand+Estate+development+takes+break+until+July%3A+Testimony+heard+from+planning%2C+transportation%2C+heritage+experts&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-05-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Ontario Land Tribunal hearing on the controversial development of the historic Rand Estate in Niagara-on-the-Lake has taken a break after more than a month of virtual testimony, cross-examinations and expert presentations.
More than 20 planning, transportation and heritage experts, called by Solmar Development and Save Our Rand Estate (SORE), were heard over a month of virtual hearings beginning April 9 and ending May 17, with cross-examination conducted by Niagara-on-the-Lake lawyer Nancy Smith; Catherine Lyons, a lawyer for SORE, a grassroots organization that has been fighting to save the property; and Mark Flowers, co-counsel for Solmar.
Solmar has seven appeals before the tribunal involving the proposed subdivision for the Rand Estate at 200 John St. and 588 Charlotte St. Those appeals include an official plan amendment, a zoning planning amendment and a draft plan subdivision that proposes as many as 202 residential units. The other four appeals are under the Heritage Act.
The development would involve a mix of semis and townhouses, along with single-family dwellings, totalling 158 units. But the revised subdivision proposal includes 44 additional units that could be divided, resulting in a possible final total of 202 units.
The revised plan includes a new access at John Street, green space, a mix of housing types and stormwater access.
SORE, which is participating in the hearing, will complete presenting its evidence when the hearing returns July 29. The town will then make its case to the hearing.
Flowers said on the first day of the hearing the developer is focused on the original plan to use the so-called "panhandle" at 200 John St. for the development's access. Smith said Solmar's plan will have a "direct" impact on the heritage buildings and the natural features on the property, including the watercourse that runs through the land.
"Development is premature," said Smith.
Lyons said development can occur on the property, but not at the expense of the natural features and heritage buildings.
Lyons said the Two Sisters Resort, a property adjacent to the Rand Estate, and owned by Benny Marotta, who also operates Solmar, could provide the access needed to the proposed subdivision.
Both the town and SORE have said the main issue is protecting the heritage buildings and landscape of the property. Other issues, which number about 60, include engineering, grading, tree preservation, the provincial growth plan, hydrogeological impacts, preserving wetlands and infrastructure.
Also taking part in the hearing is Tom Halinski, a lawyer for Blair and Brenda McArthur, who own Brunswick Place at 210 John St. The McArthurs received party status for the hearing to provide evidence on how the development will impact their property, said Halinski.
Former Niagara-on-the-Lake heritage planner Denise Horne, who left the municipality late in 2023 and now works for a private planning firm, presented about two days of evidence based on her 2023 heritage report on the property.
Horne reviewed items from a lengthy report she presented to council that suggested several recommendations, including the demolition of the Calvin Rand summer house and the main residence on Charlotte Street, a collection of former stables and barns.
Horne's report recommended rejecting 15 other proposals from Solmar, including demolition of the carriage house on John Street East, and two smaller sheds on Charlotte Street. The report also opposed removing the Mound Garden and the Dunnington-Grubb-designed swimming pool garden, which Solmar proposes to relocate and incorporate into its development.
Initially, the municipal heritage committee and council approved the report with some conditions. But at a followup meeting councillors voted against any demolition, removal or relocation of any buildings on the property.
Horne told the tribunal that her focus was preserving as much as possible on the property.
Tim McCormick, an associate principle senior landscape architect and certified arborist with Arcadis, said under questioning from Solmar lawyer Flowers, the panhandle and whistle-stop could not be proven to be designed by Howard and Lorrie Dunnington-Grubb. Throughout the early- to mid-1900s, the Dunnington-Grubbs were responsible for landscape designs in Canada, such as Oakes Garden Theatre in Niagara Falls.
During his testimony, transportation consultant Stewart Elkins supported Solmar's residential proposal, but acknowledged under questioning from town lawyer Smith that a "plan B" should be created for the emergency access proposed for Charlotte Street if it is found to be inappropriate. The access is also near where the Upper Canada Heritage Trail intersects with Charlotte Street. The access requires the removal of two historic stone pillars and a nearly 50-year-old chestnut tree.
Elkins agreed with Flowers that an alternative access could be constructed for emergency responders at the main road entrance.
Late last year, the town approved a motion to offer Solmar a portion of the Upper Canada Heritage Trail to use as an access from Charlotte Street to the development. Solmar lawyers agreed to the offer, but council in January 2024 withdrew it.
CAPTION: The Randwood estate property in Niagara-on-the-Lake.Metroland file photo

Document 182 of 249

NPC relaunches major marina development proposal: Planned $19-million enhancement is on hold as a result

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/npc-relaunches-major-marina-development-proposal/docview/3060709462/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=NPC+relaunches+major+marina+development+proposal%3A+Planned+%2419-million+enhancement+is+on+hold+as+a+result&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-05-28&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If it seems like you've heard this one before, you're not imagining it.
Niagara Parks Commission has put on hold its plans for a $19-million enhancement project for the marina at Miller's Creek and is instead going back to its much grander plans to engage the private sector to redevelop the site, including lands on the west side of Niagara Parkway, which the agency has declared surplus and would house a resort/condominium development.
Estimates for previous proposals submitted to NPC were in the $120-million range and higher.
The decision was made during NPC's closed-door board meeting on May 16. In a letter for Fort Erie council, Bob Gale, who Premier Doug Ford recently appointed board chair of the provincial agency, communicated that the board voted to put the current redevelopment plan into abeyance and to "immediately prepare a scoped RFP (request for proposals) to seek interest from the private sector in this redevelopment opportunity."
This means Niagara Parks will not be moving on its plan to redevelop the site in-house, which included increasing the number of dock slips to more than 200, while also expanding public use of the marina through amenities such as a restaurant and outdoor patio space, retail store and a new public boat launch.
Work on the project was to start this year, resulting in the closure of the marina this fall and into 2025 for reopening in May 2026.
Current slipholders have been told the marina will remain open next year, the letter said.
Gale said he initiated the move as part of his review of the project since his appointment to the board in April. Upon learning the land to the west of the marina was surplus, Gale said he realized there was an opportunity.
"I couldn't understand why wouldn't we sell the land when we could have a deal there for a large tender on it, and the board agreed," he said, adding the premier told him to have a large vision for NPC and the province and to use his business sense.
Gale said previous respondents have been sent back the scoped RFP, though it is open to other proponents.
"We'd like to see what the respondents may send to us on a scoped RFP on this because that was too good for the area and for the people to shy away from," he said.
"It's a significant priority, the marina there, for the parks (commission). And we are the environmental and cultural stewards of the Niagara River corridor, but we also see that that could generate a better opportunity for new, expanded tourism and help for the Fort Erie area."
Gale stressed the marina itself will not be put up for sale.
The idea of redeveloping the marina has a history going back nearly 20 years.
In 2006, NPC announced plans to close the marina entirely, as it was losing money.
But at the request of Fort Erie town council and the town's economic development corporation, the marina was kept open and plans for a $120-million redesign were announced by a development company.
That plan ended up being abandoned, and Niagara Parks embarked on another attempt to forge ahead with the marina and development, retaining ownership of the land and seeking a developer to build and operate the facility through a long-term lease. But no proposals were submitted by developers by 2017.
In May 2020, then-Fort Erie chief executive officer Tom Kuchyt told Fort Erie council talks with NPC and Niagara Region representatives had him hopeful the marina project could actually become a reality.
Last December, NPC announced the current, less ambitious plan.
In an interview, Fort Erie Mayor Wayne Redekop, who is the town representative on the NPC board, said he couldn't disclose what was discussed in the meeting but wanted to make sure residents were kept abreast of the development - or delay of it, which is why the letter was included in the agenda for Monday's meeting.
"It's kind of back to the future," he said.
He said the plan has the potential to be a great benefit to the town and province, though a number of hurdles need to be overcome, as the lands west of the marina are not serviced and would require official plan and zoning amendments. NPC would also require provincial approval to engage in a long-term lease or sale.
There's also a question of whether the private sector interest is still there.
"I will be monitoring it closely as a member of the commission and as the mayor of Fort Erie," Redekop said. "I'll be monitoring this closely because we don't want this to fall prey to what has happened in the past."
With files by Paul Forsyth and Alison Langley
CAPTION: Niagara Parks Commission is again seeking private sector interest for a large-scale redevelopment of its marina at Miller's Creek.Niagara Parks photo

Document 183 of 249

Niagara priorities are on province's 'radar': Bradley: Regional chair confident meetings will yield positive results in the future

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/niagara-priorities-are-on-provinces-radar-bradley/docview/3058532092/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Niagara+priorities+are+on+province%27s+%27radar%27%3A+Bradley%3A+Regional+chair+confident+meetings+will+yield+positive+results+in+the+future&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-05-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Niagara Region officials didn't leave Queen's Park with any firm commitments, but a sense the provincial government fully understands the peninsula's needs - and how Ontario can help fulfil them, says Chair Jim Bradley.
"We weren't going there expecting that they're going to be signing agreements or flat out coming out publicly in favour of anything we're asking for," he said in an interview, recapping a week's worth of meetings with key provincial officials.
"But we wanted to put it on their radar, and I think that was very successful."
Starting last Monday, the Region took part in Niagara Week hosted at Queen's Park, which allowed councillors and senior staff to meet one-on-one with provincial leaders.
Participants showcased core regional priorities and identified specific projects ready for provincial partnerships and co-investment.
Among the priorities regional officials focused on were the need for provincial funding to support the $400-million south Niagara wastewater treatment plant, advocating for increased GO train service, additional assistance to build more housing and the Region's ongoing efforts to integrate more services and reduce duplication.
Bradley said Premier Doug Ford has "certainly been aware" of the wastewater treatment plant to be built in Niagara Falls, and that provincial officials are "very interested in having a three-way conversation on this."
"What we're looking for is the federal government, the provincial government and the local government, that being the Region, being involved in the financing of this project," said Bradley.
He said the Ministry of Environment was "very supportive" and will send a letter on its behalf to the federal government.
"We're certainly willing to lobby the feds on getting involved in it as well."
Bradley said other ministries, including those dealing with housing, infrastructure and economic development, also understand the importance of the plant.
"We look at it from a number of angles - first of all, housing is a high priority for the government and we indicated to get to the number of housing units we'd like to get built in that portion of Niagara Falls, that it would be essential to have this wastewater treatment plant in progress and in operation."
He said regional officials met with Vic Fedeli, minister of economic development, job creation and trade, to talk about the plant's potential in supporting businesses and industries in the future growth area.
"Of course, the hospital is another entity that could be using the wastewater treatment plant," said Bradley.
"Ministry of Environment understood that it's very positive for the environment because it reduces the number of bypasses of existing water treatment plants because this impacts not only Niagara Falls, but also Thorold, Niagara-on-the-Lake and St. Catharines because it alleviates some of the loads on those particular plants."
Bradley said there are "logistical challenges" when it comes to significantly expanding GO train service in Niagara, but he believes all sides will eventually "overcome" them.
"We have to deal with CN owning the tracks and certain works have to be done, but we indicated where we were in establishing a regionwide transit system which will be a feeder for the GO trains and also investments we've made in the train stations," he said.
"We indicated our willingness to continue funding that aspect of it and showing that we've got a stake in the game - a financial stake and I think that boded well in terms of their reception. Like everybody else within 100 miles of Toronto, we would all like to have all-day commuter service."
Bradley said regional officials also expressed how "we're true partners" in providing more housing in Niagara.
"We indicated we are moving quickly to ensure that approvals are given in a timely fashion and that we already have a lot of approvals that have been granted already and that everything we could do to assist in that regard we would be doing," he said.
"We shared with them some of the potential projects we have here and the funding that would be very helpful in terms of affordable housing for people. But there's all kinds of housing that's required and we're prepared to be part of that."
Bradley said the idea of amalgamating Niagara municipalities "did not come up" during meetings.
"No one raised that issue at all, but we did talk about the fact that we are involved in several shared services initiatives here in Niagara," he said.
"They were very interested in what we're doing down here and the progress we've made, and I indicated we're continuing to make that kind of progress. The (chief administrative officers) are meeting frequently to look at any area where adjacent municipalities or local municipalities can share services with the Region. I think they were very happy to hear that we were doing that."
CAPTION: An important issue discussed during Niagara Week at Queen's Park was increased commuter GO train service to Niagara.Metroland file photo

CREDIT: Ray Spiteri Reporter
