Document 184 of 249

Gates pressing for caregiver compensation

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/gates-pressing-caregiver-compensation/docview/3057618549/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Gates+pressing+for+caregiver+compensation&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-05-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Wayne Gates is hoping to drum up community support as he continues fighting for "common-sense motions" that were defeated by the provincial government earlier this year.
The Niagara Falls New Democratic MPP launched petitions during opening day of the Niagara Falls market on Saturday.
He wants community support for a proposed motion that would compensate caregivers for the work they do, and legislation that would expand OHIP coverage for prostate cancer testing.
"We put forward two common-sense motions that are non-partisan," Gates said. "When we saw the caregiver bill and the prostate bill supported by the Liberals, supported by the Greens, supported by the independents, it made no sense that they weren't supported by the Conservatives."
The caregiver's motion, defeated earlier this month, would have developed a program to provide compensation for caregivers who dedicate hours of their time caring for people.
Gates, the party's long-term-care critic, said he feels strongly the caregiver motion is something Ontario needs with as many as 3.3 million people identified as caregivers, including many who experienced financial hardship because of their responsibilities.
While Nova Scotia is the only province that has a caregivers benefit program, he said Prince Edward Island and Manitoba are considering similar legislation, while the federal government is considering a national program. Gates said he has been trying to get the province to extend OHIP coverage for prostate-specific antigen (PSA) testing since 2019.
It would have covered the cost of the tests for patients referred by a health-care provider, saving them the roughly $35 each private lab in Ontario charges. Although earlier readings of the bill won all party support, the majority Progressive Conservative government defeated the bill on March 6.
Gates said his petitions have garnered nearly 500 signatures after just a few days of circulating them, and he plans to continue to collect names throughout the summer months before reintroducing motions in the fall. "We're going to knock on doors over the course of the summer, we're going to go to grocery stores to see if we can set up out front. We're trying to put pressure on the province during the summer," he said. "The big thing is heightened awareness of the motions."
Gates plans to bring the petitions with him to any community event he attends in the months to come. They can also be signed at his constituency office, 6746 Morrison St., Unit 1, Niagara Falls, weekdays from 10 a.m. to 4 p.m. His office staff will provide copies of the petition to community groups that would like to help circulate them, too. An online version of the caregiver petition is available.
"As we've seen, if you put pressure on (Premier) Doug Ford he will backtrack, so that's what we're hoping he does on these two important issues for Ontarians," Gates said.
CAPTION: Niagara Falls MPP Wayne Gates.Wayne Gates photo

CREDIT: Allan Benner Reporter

Document 185 of 249

Stevens 'frustrated' by ramp closures: Shutdowns will take place nightly on QEW between Seventh and Niagara streets

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/stevens-frustrated-ramp-closures/docview/3057038196/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Stevens+%27frustrated%27+by+ramp+closures%3A+Shutdowns+will+take+place+nightly+on+QEW+between+Seventh+and+Niagara+streets&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-05-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: St. Catharines politicians are calling on the Ministry of Transportation to improve communications after multiple QEW ramp closure signs went up to the surprise of residents and, apparently, city officials.
The signs say QEW access ramps throughout the city will be closed for five months starting on May 27, prompting anxious calls to city hall and NDP MPP Jennie Stevens' office.
"I have major concerns and am incredibly frustrated at what amounts, once again, to a lack of co-ordination by MTO with the city regarding these closures," said Mayor Mat Siscoe.
"Our staff found out the same way everyone in the city did - signs up indicating the closures after they had happened."
It turns out all the ramps won't be shut down at the same time and work will only happen overnight - information Stevens said her office found out by reaching out to contractor Rankin Construction when the ministry didn't immediately respond to inquiries.
Stevens said the city knew work would happen, but wasn't given a final schedule.
"(The MTO) really dropped the ball on this issue," she said on Friday. "To suddenly see an appearance of multiple QEW on-ramp closures without any explanation or real responses to the mayor's office, Region's office, my office, stakeholders. You can imagine the panic."
Ministry spokesperson Tanya Blazina told the Standard on Friday that the city and Niagara Region have been aware of the project for nearly nine months.
She said the ministry works closely with the local municipality and contractor on all of its projects.
"Information including closures, detours, paving and project details have been discussed at multiple subsequent meetings, following the initial discussion in August 2023," she said in an email.
The ministry is having the QEW and interchanges between Niagara and Seventh streets resurfaced. That would affect all St. Catharines QEW exits - Niagara Street, Lake Street, Ontario Street, Martindale Road, Third Street and Seventh Street.
No two adjacent ramps will be closed at the same time and ramp closures will only take place at night to minimize traffic impacts.
Blazina said there won't be any closures during the World Rowing Championships taking place in St. Catharines from Aug. 17 to 25. She said the "ministry worked closely with the city" to ensure access during that time.
Closures during the event were one of the big concerns of callers to Stevens' office, as they would have a major impact on getting in and out of Port Dalhousie to the venue.
Stevens said if all the ramps were closed down at the same time, it would "shut down St. Catharines," which explains residents' frustration.
She said she'll be raising the communications gap with the transportation minister later this month.
Siscoe said he'll also be following up with the minister about "continued lack of proper communication" with the city.
Going forward, Stevens said, there has to be clear communication from the ministry to residents.
She's heard from commuters concerned about the upheaval to routes.
"Their minimal requirement is to provide clear and accurate timing for the commuters so they can plan ahead."
CAPTION:

Document 186 of 249

Port Colborne battery plant property has storied history: $1.56B facility proposed for property subject of an MZO

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/port-colborne-battery-plant-property-has-storied/docview/3055843620/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Port+Colborne+battery+plant+property+has+storied+history%3A+%241.56B+facility+proposed+for+property+subject+of+an+MZO&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-05-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The site of Asahi Kasei Corp.'s planned $1.56-billion lithium-ion separator battery plant in Port Colborne is the subject of a minister's zoning order (MZO), the Welland Tribune discovered Wednesday.
The order was found on the Environmental Registry of Ontario (ERO) website when researching the history of the land on the west side of Highway 140, just south of Townline Tunnel Road.
On Tuesday, Tokyo-based Asahi Kasei announced its investment in the lakeside city to bring the first Hipore wet-process lithium-ion separator manufacturing facility to Canada.
Among people present for the announcement made at the city's engineering and operations centre on Killaly Street West were Asahi Kasei, president, and representative director Koshiro Kudo, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford.
Job numbers at the new factory will be released later this year.
Construction over three phases is to be completed in 2027.
The MZO, which regulates the use of land and the erection, location and use of any building or structure on it, was requested by the Ontario Ministry of Economic Development, Job Creation and Trade and went to the Ministry of Municipal Affairs and Housing for consideration.
The ERO website lists the property as approximately 78.9 hectares in size, but a release from BMI Group, which operates the Thorold Multimodal Hub on the Welland Canal, said Asahi Kasei Corp.'s facility will sit on nearly 81 hectares.
Now vacant, the property - for which an access road was recently built - is zoned for heavy industrial use under Port Colborne's zoning bylaws.
The requested MZO would permit a battery separator manufacturing facility building approximately 185,806 square metres in size (two million square feet), parking lots, stormwater management ponds, private electric substations, a private wastewater treatment plant and tank yards.
The Municipal Affairs Ministry said it's seeking feedback on whether the request should be considered and for any additional information that may help inform a decision.
Residents have until June 13 to provide feedback on the ERO website - ero.ontario.ca/notice/019-8690#proposal-details.
Mayor Bill Steele said there's a simple explanation for the MZO.
"Our (urban) boundary stops at Third Concession, and from Third to the (Asahi Kasei) property is considered rural and we can't run water and sewer (lines) through a rural area."
Steele said the city had to get permission, sometime in the 1990s, to run a 200-millimetre water line along Second Concession from Highway 140 to Pinty's (formerly Port Colborne Poultry), just east of Miller Road.
In the city's north end, bordering Welland, the land was once proposed as a potential landfill site and later, a petrochemical storage facility.
In the early 1990s, Port Colborne and the Town of Fort Erie were developing a waste management master plan and part of the land was proposed for a joint landfill.
The landfill never came to fruition, and in 2005, a company calling itself Nyon Oil, proposed using the property as a 56-tank petrochemical farm for diesel, petrol and oil, placing a pipeline to the Welland Canal for vessels, with secondary transport through rail about once a day, followed by trucks as a third transport option.
A Canadian Pacific rail line runs on the north side of the property alongside Townline Tunnel Road. The canal is to the west.
Later called Nyon Marine Fuelling Corp., the group also proposed a solar farm on site and ended up before the then-Ontario Municipal Board in 2013, after the city turned down the proposal.
It was last before Port Colborne city council in 2020, when the city hosted a special public hearing meeting to allow the developers to make a presentation to councillors and the public. Nyon wanted to change aspects of how the project was to be completed, but it never moved forward.
Fast forward to 2024, 81 hectares of 162 owned by BMI Group were purchased by Asahi Kasei for an undisclosed price.
BMI Group operates Thorold Multimodal Hub with Hamilton-Oshawa Port Authority and Niagara Ports, which stretches from Thorold to Port Colborne along the Welland Canal.
Justus Veldman, the company's market and business development officer, said between the 200-plus-hectare Thorold property - it has marine, rail and highway access - and the Port Colborne land, there are 364 hectares open for development.
In a brief interview, Veldman said the goal is to drive more vessel traffic on the canal.
"The more ships stop with products, it adds value and is better for the region," said Veldman, adding that can create jobs and more opportunities.
As for the remaining 81 hectares in Port Colborne, Veldman said the company is waiting for the dust to settle to see if any of Asahi Kasei's suppliers require land to be closer to its facility.
In a release, the company said when the 162 hectares of Niagara Ports land was shortlisted as a contender, Veldman co-ordinated a multi-sector team to meet all infrastructural requirements for Asahi Kasei's facility, reinforcing the region's role as an emerging EV supply chain hub.
"This collaborative initiative involving 19 stakeholders - from Port Colborne, Niagara Region to the St. Lawrence Seaway Management Corp. and Invest Ontario - culminated in the city being selected as the site for Asahi Kasei's new facility," the release said.
BMI Group chief executive officer Paul Veldman said with major investments across Ontario, including GM in St. Catharines, Linamar in Welland and Asahi Kasei in Port Colborne, Niagara is rapidly emerging as an EV supply chain powerhouse.
CAPTION: Asahi Kasei Corp.'s $1.56-billion plant will sit on about 81 hectares of land in north Port Colborne, west of Highway 140.Dave Johnson Welland Tribune

CREDIT: Dave Johnson Reporter

Document 187 of 249

Battery plant a 'game-changer': $1.5B facility will bring thousands of jobs to Port Colborne

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/battery-plant-game-changer/docview/3055354718/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Battery+plant+a+%27game-changer%27%3A+%241.5B+facility+will+bring+thousands+of+jobs+to+Port+Colborne&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-05-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Game-changer. Massive. Transformational.
To say the excitement was palpable at a gathering of municipal, regional, provincial and federal dignitaries for the announcement a $1.56-billion electric vehicle battery separator plant will be built in Port Colborne would be an understatement.
"This is a game-changer for Niagara," said Mishka Balsom, chief executive officer of the Greater Niagara Chamber of Commerce.
"We will look back on this particular day and remember the announcement that has been made here. What's good for Port Colborne is good for all of Niagara."
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford were joined Tuesday at Port Colborne's engineering and operations centre by several key ministers, along with officials from across Niagara, to welcome Asahi Kasei Corporation's massive investment that attendees said will lead to significant spinoff benefits for the entire peninsula.
With medium- to long-term growth forecasted in the North American electric vehicle market, the Japanese company expects to make additional multibillion-dollar investments through multiple phases, said the federal government, adding details about direct jobs are being finalized and will be announced at a later date.
Both Trudeau and Ford said Asahi's decision to build its facility in Port Colborne is a testament to Canada and Ontario's competitive business environment, highly skilled workforce and dependable supply chains.
The project will benefit from federal support through the Clean Technology Manufacturing Investment tax credit. Ontario will support the project with both direct and indirect incentives.
"This is probably the most important announcement made in the last 25 years in the Niagara region," said Niagara Region Chair Jim Bradley.
"For Port Colborne, for the Niagara region, this really paints a good picture of the future. It puts us in the right place for the green economy. It means that we're a key player here."
He said Port Colborne is attractive, in part, due to its large and suitable available land.
"There's going to be a lot of spinoffs because this company is going to have to be serviced by local people when it's up and established, so this will produce a lot of good jobs, a lot of economic activity."
Port Colborne Mayor Bill Steele said it was a "Team Niagara" approach that helped bring the investment to fruition.
"We've been meeting for nine months. Every week, we have a call that has approximately 45 people on it. We talk about the project, we talk about infrastructure, we talk about what we can do for this company to come to Niagara - the incentives that we have, both at the regional level and the city level. This is what's made them come here."
He said Niagara is on the "cusp" of bringing "lots of new businesses, lots of new people here."
"You're going to see the resurgence of all kinds of new industry that will tie in not just with the EV battery trade but, quite frankly, our marine trade."
Welland Mayor Frank Campion said Niagara is "starting to turn into a bit of an EV centre," pointing to the Linamar manufacturing facility expected to start producing parts for electric vehicles in his city next year.
"It's massive for Niagara. We don't really care where the jobs are in Niagara, as long as the jobs are being created and (there is) prosperity and economic spinoffs. It's good for every municipality in Niagara."
Niagara Falls Mayor Jim Diodati used the analogy of a "high tide is going to bring up all the boats of Niagara."
"This is going to be as impactful as General Motors was when they came to Niagara a long time ago," he said.
"Even though it was in St. Catharines, so many people in Niagara Falls and Welland and around the region worked at General Motors and this is going to have the same impact, except it's forward-leaning into a new industry."
Wainfleet Mayor Brian Grant said the new investment will benefit his township, which is next door to Port Colborne.
"I think this is an amazing opportunity for Port Colborne and Niagara, especially southern Niagara because generally we see a lot more development like this happening more in the north end - Niagara Falls, St. Catharines areas," he said.
"There's going to be other opportunities that are going to come up. It's going to bring more investors and more business that's ultimately good for all around Niagara."
Niagara West MPP Sam Oosterhoff said the investment is "great news" for local jobs.
"This is going to be transformational for our local workers," he said.
"It's really about having that anchor corporation ... that's going to be a facility hundreds of acres in sizes, with potential for thousands of jobs ... and we know that there are a lot of suppliers that are going to be feeding into not just building this site, but then a lot of the transformational manufacturing that's going to be happening around this site."
While the mood inside the centre garage bay on Killaly Street West was positive, the same couldn't be said about a group of protesters gathered on the other side of a fence.
People waving Canadian flags and signs, including one calling for an end to the carbon tax, chanted against Trudeau throughout Tuesday's announcement.
With one of the chants being "Trudeau must go," the protesters blamed the prime minister for a plethora of issues, such as cost of living and homelessness, while calling for him to come out and address their concerns.
CAPTION: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford at Tuesday's announcement. Wainfleet Mayor Brian Grant speaks to the media at Japanese manufacturer Asahi Kasei Corp.'s announcement of a $1.56-billion investment in Port Colborne.St. Catharines StandardWelland Tribune

Readers write Letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write-letters-editor/docview/3054756244/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write+Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-05-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Cowardice displayed by antisemitic flyers
Police investigating after antisemitic flyers distributed across
Niagara Falls, May 9
As a Jew, I would like to personally thank whoever it was who left those flyers on people's doorsteps in Niagara Falls.
In doing so, you've awakened people to the dangers of hatred.
The public's overwhelming rejection of your message fills me with both joy and hope. In distributing your propaganda surreptitiously and anonymously, you demonstrated your cowardice and insecurity.
A person of conviction would proudly put their name to their beliefs, as I do here.
Please continue to spread your ridiculous message, so that the intelligent and rational majority never forgets the threat presented by insecure racists and xenophobes like you.
Perry Schlanger, St. Catharines
How will bridge
twinning help traffic?
Garden City Skyway twinning now targeted for 2026 as province issues RFPs, April 5
After reading the latest government update regarding the proposed twinning of the St. Catharines skyway bridge, am I the only one wondering how that project will actually improve traffic flow on the QEW?
When that project is completed the QEW will have eight lanes between Welland and Glendale avenues. Three lanes for cars plus the rightmost one for trucks in each direction.
Could our provincial leader, his government or their transportation experts explain how adding a dedicated lane for trucks for a few kilometres will reduce the traffic congestion on the QEW?
Premier Doug Ford, how about thinking "out of the box" for a change? Why not consider allocating that half-billion - plus the billions you plan to spend on more highways - toward a reliable 21st century high-speed train service linking Niagara and Toronto?
Rest assured, a large number of commuters would love to leave their vehicles at home if they had a faster alternative. It would guarantee fewer cars on the QEW, less traffic congestion. These are side benefits the bridge twinning project fails to offer.
Bernard Frugier, St. Catharines
Getting rid of Region
is not the answer
Shared concern, different
conclusion, Letters, May 2
I agree with the writer's concern regarding too much bureaucracy in Niagara. I don't agree with the contention that getting rid of Niagara Region is the answer.
This is the very reason the province reversed its decision regarding the Region of Peel dissolution: It was due to costs.
If the Region was gone, things such as public health, emergency medical services and policing would need to run by the individual lower-tier municipalities. This administration would end up costing taxpayers way more money.
As baffling as it is to have 126 politicians, more concerning is the inefficient governance structure which supports a duplication in the administration of government services in Niagara costing local taxpayers millions of dollars more than it should.
It's time for the province to end this madness and change Niagara's governance structure.
Allen McKay, St. Catharines

Document 189 of 249

Gates' plan to help caregivers shot down: Niagara Falls New Democrat MPP vows to continue fight: 'Caregivers are struggling'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/gates-plan-help-caregivers-shot-down/docview/3054756222/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Gates%27+plan+to+help+caregivers+shot+down%3A+Niagara+Falls+New+Democrat+MPP+vows+to+continue+fight%3A+%27Caregivers+are+struggling%27&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-05-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Though disappointed his efforts to develop legislation to compensate caregivers across Ontario was shot down by the provincial government Monday, Niagara Falls MPP Wayne Gates says he's not surprised.
The New Democrat, however, said he's not giving up.
"This isn't going to stop. It's not going to go away. We're going to continue to do everything we can to convince this government to support (my) motion."
Gates said he reached out to other MPPs across the province of all political affiliations to gauge their support, prior to introducing his private member's motion last week to create a caregivers benefit that would provide compensation to roughly 3.3 million Ontarians who spend about 10 to 20 hours a week caring for others.
He said he also called Premier Doug Ford to discuss the need for the program, telling him: "It's not just the caregiver that it's affecting, it's entire families."
"I said, 'Listen, you can become a caregiver overnight, and I believe this is something we need. We know caregivers are struggling.'"
Although his motion was supported by his fellow New Democrats, Liberals and Green party members, he said the majority Progressive Conservatives voted against it.
"I was disappointed ... But after our debate and the fact that Sam (Niagara West MPP Sam Oosterhoff) didn't show up on Thursday, I kind of figured where they were going to go," he said. "To me, this was something that should have been supported."
Ultimately, Gates said, it's less expensive to compensate caregivers than it is to provide that care in an institution.
Gates said the program has been implemented elsewhere in Canada.
"There's home care, retirement homes, nursing homes, it's all savings," Gates said.
"In Nova Scotia where they have this program, just with $400 a month in a direct payment, they end up saving 7.8 per cent in long-term care. That works out to about half a billion dollars."
Nova Scotia's Progressive Conservative government expanded eligibility for its caregiver program in 2018.
"There's an incredible savings to the system," Gates said.
He vowed to keep fighting for a caregiver benefit.
He said past motions he introduced that were initially defeated were eventually implemented, such as 2016 motion to reduce MRI wait times, and a motion he introduced in 2019 to scrap a 6.1 per cent tax on small- and medium-sized wineries.
"I don't give up. I think this is a motion that all parties can support," he said.
Even if the PC government won't support his motion, he said he hopes it will develop its own.
"I don't really care if it's the Conservatives or the Liberals or whoever gets it passed. Let's just get it passed. It's the right thing to do," he said.
CAPTION:

Document 190 of 249

Battery maker plans $1.5B Niagara plant: Asahi Kasei facility to be built near Hwy. 140 in Port Colborne

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/battery-maker-plans-1-5b-niagara-plant/docview/3054754786/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Battery+maker+plans+%241.5B+Niagara+plant%3A+Asahi+Kasei+facility+to+be+built+near+Hwy.+140+in+Port+Colborne&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-05-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Niagara is setting the table for being the leader in the electric vehicle industry with the largest related investment made to date in the region, says Port Colborne Mayor Bill Steele.
Steele made the comment after Japanese manufacturer Asahi Kasei Corp. announced a $1.56-billion investment in the lakeside city to bring the first Hipore wet-process lithium-ion separator manufacturing facility to Canada.
Tuesday's announcement was made inside the city's engineering and operations building on Killaly Street West, with Asahi Kasei president and representative director Koshiro Kudo, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau, Premier Doug Ford, federal Innovation Minister François-Philippe Champagne and Ontario Economic Development Minister Vic Fedeli, Steele and others.
Separators are an essential component of the lithium-ion batteries used in electric vehicles, and make them more sustainable and durable.
"I believe this is a resurgence not just for Port Colborne, but for Niagara, Ontario and Canada, quite frankly," said Steele. "The prime minister and premier hit on this, that other countries are looking at Canada as a safe haven to come to. We have stable governments, a great workforce and it's not expensive to invest in Canada, particularly Port Colborne."
Before the facility is built - it's on what was formerly known as the Nyon Lands on the west side of Highway 140, just south of Townline Tunnel Road - the development will bring jobs for site servicing, including water and sewer.
Steele said there will be at least 500 construction jobs to start at the plant, which will be built in three phases beginning later this year and is set to be complete by 2027.
"Every one of these jobs really creates five outside jobs. This is thousands of jobs, not just at the plant but all across Port Colborne, Niagara and Ontario."
Steele said it should draw Asahi Kasei's suppliers closer to the plant and bring more business.
"This is a catalyst for more EV manufacturers to come to Canada," the mayor said.
Asahi Kasei's Koshiro Kudo said the Port Colborne facility marks the first Hipore wet-process outside of Japan and the largest greenfield plant to date for the company.
He said a reliable supply of lithium battery components, including battery separators, will be needed to meet and supply the EV industry as it grows.
The separator is a microporous polyolefin sheet that prevents a battery's anode and cathode from contacting one another and causing a short circuit, enabling lithium ions to pass back and forth during battery charging and discharging.
Kudo said they will be sold in the North American market.
The company's website it said it had a basic agreement with Honda Motor Co., Ltd. (Honda) and the two parties are studying a joint investment.
Honda announced recently that it is building an electric vehicle battery plant next to its Alliston, Ont., assembly plant, which it is retooling to produce fully electric vehicles as part of a $15-billion project to create a supply chain in the province for the automaker.
Asahi Kasei's planning for a new facility outside Japan began two to three years ago, with the company first looking to Europe, the U.S. and, lastly, Canada.
"So, frankly speaking, Canada was the third country to investigate opportunities for investment. As we progressed in our investigation, we learned about huge (investment) infusions from the federal, provincial and local governments," Kudo said through a translator.
"We found plenty of great quality human resources available here ... and reasonable costs."
Kudo said the company is bringing the world's best technology to Canada and called it a monumental project.
He said the company is excited to be the city's new neighbour and looks forward to growing with the community.
Both Trudeau and Ford said the announcement is good news for Canada and Ontario, adding the project will bring economic growth and good-paying jobs.
Trudeau said Asahi Kasei's investment is another vote of confidence in the nation's auto industry and autoworkers.
Ford said it shows confidence in Ontario's ongoing work to build a domestic EV supply chain that benefits workers across the province.
Said Niagara Region economic development director George Spezza in a release, "This historic investment by Asahi Kasei is a testament to Niagara's attractive and competitive business environment. Today's announcement shows that Niagara can attract global investment and is strategically positioned for growth."
Niagara Centre Liberal MP Vance Badawey said the investment will increase Canada's productivity and will strengthen the EV supply chain and secure the future of Canada's automotive industry and encourage new investment.
Innovation Minister François-Philippe Champagne said Canada has seen $46 billion invested in the EV industry since 2020 and the Port Colborne investment is another step in ensuring there is a complete supply chain in the country.
"This is a key piece needed in the equation."
Champagne said earlier this year, Bloomberg ranked Canada ahead of China in the battery supply chain ecosystem and expected the country to be a major player in the EV industry.
CAPTION: Asahi Kasei Corp. president and representative director Koshiro Kudo announces a $1.56-billion investment to bring a lithium-ion separator manufacturing facility to Port Colborne as Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Ontario Premier Doug Ford listen.Federal Innovation Minister François-Philippe Champagne speaks with the media ahead of Asahi Kasei Corp.'s announcement of a $1.56-billion investment to bring the first Hipore wet-process lithium-ion separator manufacturing facility to Port Colborne.Bob Tymczyszyn St. Catharines StandardBob Tymczyszyn St. Catharines Standard

Document 191 of 249

GO train service among priorities for Niagara: Regional officials to meet with provincial leaders to highlight concerns

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/go-train-service-among-priorities-niagara/docview/3053439012/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=GO+train+service+among+priorities+for+Niagara%3A+Regional+officials+to+meet+with+provincial+leaders+to+highlight+concerns&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-05-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There are 400 million reasons why the south Niagara wastewater treatment plant will be a top priority for regional politicians and staff when they meet with key provincial officials at Queen's Park next week.
Niagara officials are hopeful the provincial government will allocate funding to the $400-million project in Niagara Falls, the largest and most expensive infrastructure undertaking in Niagara Region's 54-year history.
Starting Monday, Niagara Region will be hosting Niagara Week at Queen's Park, which allows councillors and senior staff to meet one-on-one with provincial leaders.
During meetings, participants showcase core regional priorities and identify specific projects ready for provincial partnerships and co-investment.
Last year's event resulted in a commitment from the provincial government about the proposed south Niagara wastewater treatment plant.
Niagara Falls MPP Wayne Gates said in the legislature last month that while a commitment was made, funding was "only guaranteed if the federal government introduced an infrastructure program that would support the cost-sharing structure."
He said the provincial and federal governments "must each do their part," but the Region stated it "must move forward with this critical project now."
In an interview with the Niagara Falls Review, Niagara Region Chair Jim Bradley, whose office is responsible for organizing Niagara Week, said a list of about four issues has been finalized, with the proposed treatment plant being "No. 1."
He said the project is "exceedingly important" for a couple reasons.
"Obviously, one is housing. There are targets for housing being set and if we are going to meet those targets, we want to make sure that we have the infrastructure capacity, in this case the wastewater treatment capacity, for that," said Bradley, adding the funding for that is "key" from the provincial and federal governments.
"It will also be of help eventually to the (new south Niagara) hospital," which is under construction on the outskirts of Niagara Falls.
He said another issue will be to advocate for increased commuter GO train service to Niagara.
"We are pleased that we have seen some expansion of that and we'd like to see even further expansion with more commuter trains being involved," said Bradley.
"We have invested in the train stations and we'll continue to do so. We developed a regional transit system which would have the impact of bringing people to the GO station. We think there's room for some additional service there ... and we know that the government is very interested in expanding its GO services around the province."
He said officials will also be looking for any additional assistance they can receive for housing.
"We have some proposed new housing projects that would be able to handle people who are confronted with homelessness and people who need affordable housing, and we certainly want to make a pitch for that," said Bradley. "Again, we've had progress there. We're looking for further progress."
He said regional officials will also talk about their efforts to bring together some of the services between regional and local governments, leading to less red tape and more efficient governance.
Bradley said the hope is to receive funding that will "help us ease into the integration of these services."
He said meetings have been scheduled with several key ministers, deputy ministers, parliamentary assistants and other senior civil service staff. A meeting has also been scheduled with Premier Doug Ford.
He said it's important to focus on some critical requests rather than a "scattered" approach with countless asks.
"Obviously, when you're in conversations with people, different issues come up," said Bradley.
"We're not going to overwhelm the meetings with large numbers, but we're going to have those who have been specifically interested in certain subjects to be present for those meetings. We'll have our senior staff there as well as members of regional council."
St. Catharines Mayor Mat Siscoe said he won't be attending Niagara Week as he will be in Montreal for a Great Lakes conference.
Welland Mayor Frank Campion said he's unable to attend, while Niagara Falls Mayor Jim Diodati said he's awaiting confirmation on who exactly Niagara officials will be meeting with before deciding whether he will attend.
Diodati said he's confident regional officials will hammer home key issues of importance for his community, including the wastewater treatment plant and GO train expansion.
CAPTION: Niagara Region Chair Jim Bradley said another important issue to be discussed during Niagara Week at Queen's Park next week will be to advocate for increased commuter GO train service to Niagara.Bob Tymczyszyn St. Catharines Standard file photo

Readers write Letters to the editor

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write+Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-05-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Garbage remains a problem
Niagara letters: What is happening in downtown St. Catharines?, Feb. 1
I wrote an email earlier this year on behalf of my partner and I regarding the lack of care for St. Catharines downtown, particularly with respect to the trash.
We met with representatives from the City of St. Catharines and the downtown business association. They assured us there were already plans in place with additional future considerations to address our concerns. They expressed appreciation with a promise of keeping us connected and informed. Since then, nothing.
For a time, there seemed to be an improvement in the cleanliness of downtown, but the last couple of weeks are showing that attention to the appeal of downtown is once again falling off the radar. There was so much trash on the streets, it was more than simply picking up a piece here and there to place in a waste or recycle receptacle.
We are extremely disappointed. Except for a few we have spoken to while picking up garbage and to businesses we continue to patronize, it just seems few others care.
David Hall, St. Catharines
Uncovering
the truth
Animal activists thrilled after parts of agriculture law unconstitutional, April 30
Thanks for publishing the article regarding Ontario's ag-gag law.
If it was not for undercover investigators and whistleblowers uncovering the truth of what's going on in the inhumane and secretive animal agriculture industry, who would?
Instead of trying to improve the welfare of animals, the government would rather cover up cruelty.
Denis LaRoche,
St. Catharines
Ask non-union workers
Niagara letters: Workers' gains are due to unions,
April 30
Richard Murri shouldn't ignore my letter. He has every right to question opinions he disagrees with and it's a shame so many folks won't engage in serious debate.
I don't believe I stated I was "outraged" because Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre was not invited. Trade unions, like any other group, can invite whoever they wish.
It just seemed more than a little partisan for Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh, who were invited, to be encouraged to criticize Poilievre to gain applause without being part of any debate.
As for those claims about democracy and transparency within the election process?
I have no problem with any union representation that protects the best interests of its members, but any successful negotiation affects another sector of society which has no say in how those successful negotiations are paid for.
I can't recall a single instance when any union has ever asked those taxpayers how they feel as their own earnings fall further and further behind those who they pay for with their taxes.
Andrew Watts, Wainfleet

Document 193 of 249

MPP wants financial help for province's caregivers: More than 3.3 million providing unpaid care for family, friends

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/mpp-wants-financial-help-provinces-caregivers/docview/3052470316/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=MPP+wants+financial+help+for+province%27s+caregivers%3A+More+than+3.3+million+providing+unpaid+care+for+family%2C+friends&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-05-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Saying they play a vital role in health care and enhance quality of life for those they care for, Niagara Falls MPP Wayne Gates is calling on the province to implement an Ontario caregiver support benefit to provide direct financial supports to unpaid caregivers.
The New Democrat said more than 3.3 million Ontarians self-report as having provided unpaid care for family and friends.
From basic medical tasks to administrative assistance, caregivers provide an "endless stream of support," said Gates, adding there's often a real financial cost as caregivers spend on average 10 to 20 hours per week on care.
"That's a part-time job and should be compensated like one," he said, noting Ontario Caregiver Coalition has done research highlighting the financial impact caregiving takes and has recommended a direct financial benefit to Ontario caregivers as a "vital" policy solution.
He pointed to a 2021 survey from the Canadian Association of Retired Persons that shows 93 per cent of caregivers feel caregiving is a source of stress, with 40 per cent saying it caused significant stress in their lives.
Sixty-six per cent of caregivers said they felt overwhelmed, while nearly 300,000 Ontario caregivers reported financial hardship because of their responsibilities.
Lauren Bates, chair of Ontario Caregiver Coalition, said the issue of financial hardship caused by caregiving is one the top issues the grassroots, volunteer-driven, non-profit organization hears from its members.
"It's something that caregivers often find as a surprise to them. They don't realize how little support, financially, caregivers get when they come to take on this important role and just how expensive caregiving is going to be," she said.
Gates said research by the coalition has suggested a two-phase implementation for an Ontario Caregiver Support Benefit program.
The first phase would provide direct financial support for those providing 20 or more care hours weekly - a monthly payment of $1,000.
Eligibility would mimic existing caregiver tax credits and programs for ease of implementation.
The second phase would improve, expand and invest in services, while providing direct monthly payment based on hours of care, including for those who provide less than 20 hours.
Gates is scheduled to introduce his private member's motion Thursday.
He has received support from several stakeholders, some of whom are expected to be present at the news conference.
They include Ontario Caregiver Coalition, Canadian Association of Retired Persons, Young Caregivers Association, MS Canada, Canadian Cancer Society, Alzheimer Society of Ontario, Ontario Caregiver Organization, AdvantAge, Community Living Ontario, Ontario Community Support Association and Canadian Centre for Caregiving Excellence.
Gates said he has support from Green Party Leader Mike Schreiner and former interim Liberal leader John Fraser.
He said he's also provided documentation to Niagara West Progressive Conservative MPP Sam Oosterhoff, the only Niagara MPP in the majority PC government who, Gates said, will take the motion to his caucus.
"I'm hoping that they (the government) understand that there's a need. I'm hoping they understand that we're an aging population that is relying more on caregivers to take care of our loved ones."
Gates said he's had many conversations with stakeholders about the issue, but also has a personal reason for bringing the motion forward.
"This is exactly what happened to my wife, Rita," he said.
"She ended up retiring a year early from her job to take care of her dad and then her dad passed and then she ended up taking care of mom, too, until she passed. She quit her job. She gave up a year of her pension."
The federal government offers some tax credits, including the Canadian Caregiver Credit and credits for disability and medical expenses. However, as these credits - and similar ones offered by the provinces - are non-refundable, they only benefit caregivers with sufficient income to take advantage of them.
The Canadian Centre for Caregiving Excellence reported that in 2018, only eight per cent of caregivers received tax credits, and therefore advises making all caregiver-related credits refundable.
In its most recent budget, the federal government said it intends to hold consultations on creation of what it calls a National Caregiving Strategy.
"I think MPP Gates' motion is really important as an acknowledgment of the importance of the issue and a way to bring the conversation forward and get a bipartisan movement toward figuring out how we can effectively address this issue," said Bates.
"Other provinces are doing things. This is something that can be done. It's something that would make a huge difference for caregivers. It would also be a really practical thing to do in terms of supporting our health and long-term-care systems."
CAPTION: Niagara Falls MPP Wayne Gates said he's had many conversations with stakeholders about the caregiver issue, but also has a personal reason for bringing his motion forward.Ontario Legislature livestream screen shot

CREDIT: Ray Spiteri Reporter

Document 194 of 249

Health minister says province will keep testing drinking water

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/health-minister-says-province-will-keep-testing/docview/3050053551/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Health+minister+says+province+will+keep+testing+drinking+water&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-05-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario Minister of Health Sylvia Jones is assuring the public the province has no intention of discontinuing private drinking-water testing by public health staff, as recommended by the Office of the Auditor General's report.
"To be clear, there are no changes where the people of Ontario can get their well water tested for free in the province of Ontario," said Jones April 21 in the legislature. "There are no changes to well-water testing."
In a 2023 value-for-money audit of Public Health Ontario, the auditor general recommended gradually discontinuing free, private drinking-water testing. The proposal, the fifth of 10 recommendations made to the province, would help to save the government about $6 million that included closing down six of the 11 public health laboratory operations in an effort to "mitigate rising costs of repairs and upgrades."
In a 2017 auditor general report about public health, a similar recommendation to discontinue free, private drinking-water testing was made. But the plan to reorganize laboratory operations in the province was upended with the onset of COVID-19 in 2020.
Hamilton is the closest laboratory that provides testing services, with others located in London, Orillia, Kingston and the largest in Toronto.
The auditor general's report revealed that between nine per cent and 80 per cent of the laboratory tests - which also include testing for COVID-19, and H. pylori - were completed in-house, with the rest sent out to private laboratories for testing.
The Ministry of Public Health said in its response that it "will commence the phased recommendations of the plan, including the regional consolidation of the laboratories" but it provides no timeline for starting or completing the reorganization.
An April 10 letter from Bill Hodgson, chair of the Niagara Peninsula Source Protection Committee, and approved by the committee at its April 19 meeting, urged the province to reject the auditor general's recommendation.
The organization is "concerned that the discontinuation of the free, drinking-water testing program provided by PHO will ultimately have a negative impact on the residents that rely on it in Ontario," said Hodgson in the letter.
Hodgson said the free service "has been a valuable resource to those that do not benefit from the protections and safeguards offered by the province's clean drinking water acts" of 2002 and 2006, "particularly in rural areas."
The letter said information from the testing has also been critical to researchers as well that has revealed instances of bacterial contamination in private drinking water samples, including in the Niagara region.
Jones said in the legislature that Public Health Ontario has assessed and reviewed the auditor general's recommendation and "perhaps, perhaps not (will) make a recommendation to the minister of health."
"There has been no recommendation from public health to the minister of health. We will continue to test the well water in the province of Ontario. We've had it for decades."
Hannah Jenson, deputy director of communications for the health minister, reiterated Jones' statement, saying "what is in the auditor general's report does not direct government action nor is it reflective of a government decision."
Niagara's NDP MPPs also urged the province to reject the idea of eliminating what they call a "life-saving" service.
In an April 24 letter to Jones, Jennie Stevens, St. Catharines MPP, Jeff Burch, Niagara Centre MPP, and Wayne Gates, Niagara Falls MPP, said the free, drinking water-testing program is a "preventive measure against health crises like the tragedy in Walkerton where government cost-cutting on essential services like water testing led to disastrous outcomes."
The former Progressive Conservative premier Mike Harris had to address an outbreak of E. coli in the Walkerton drinking water supply in 2000. It became contaminated because of improper water treatment following heavy rainfall which allowed manure from nearby cattle to flow into a shallow aquifer of a nearby well.
It was the worst E. coli outbreak in Canadian history, causing more than 2,000 cases of sickness and seven deaths.
It led to the Walkerton Inquiry, which among other revelations found the privatization of water testing by the provincial government was partly to blame for the disaster. Stricter water-treatment guidelines were established soon after the completion of the Walkerton Inquiry.
The MPPs said the province should talk to Niagara experts "to discuss alternative proposals for lab-testing efficiencies."
In an interview, Gates said when he saw the source protection committee's letter to the province about the possible discontinuation of the free, private drinking-water testing, it "really bothered me."
"We should have learned from Walkerton," said Gates. "It doesn't make sense. We have to continue to do the testing. If we don't, we will continue to have problems with our water. If we don't have water, none of us will be here."
CAPTION: Ontario Minister of Health Sylvia Jones says the province has no intention of discontinuing private drinking-water testing by public health staff.Graeme Frisque Metroland file photo

CREDIT: Kevin Werner Reporter

Document 195 of 249

Effectiveness of student cellphone ban questioned: Major issues such as classroom violence mental health ignored, says teachers' union boss

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/effectiveness-student-cellphone-ban-questioned/docview/3050053492/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Effectiveness+of+student+cellphone+ban+questioned%3A+Major+issues+such+as+classroom+violence+mental+health+ignored%2C+says+teachers%27+union+boss&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-05-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With policies regulating the use of technology already in place at Niagara's two English-speaking school boards, the provincial government's forthcoming cellphone restrictions are expected to result in minimal changes in local classrooms.
But that hasn't stopped students, parents and teachers from expressing confusion and worry in the aftermath of this week's announcement.
Simona Berardocco, a Grade 11 District School Board of Niagara (DSBN) student, said she was "a little surprised" by the direction from Queen's Park.
"Phones definitely play a role of distraction within a classroom setting, but I didn't think that it would be something that would start to be controlled by the government," she said.
In her school, some students won't see any change, she said, noting some teachers are stricter than others when it comes to cellphone use.
But Berardocco said she is uncertain whether removing phones altogether "will fully solve the problem" when it comes to classroom attentiveness.
"There are definitely people (who) will just find another thing to do," she said.
"There is probably going to be an increase on the focus level of the class as a result (of banning cellphones), I just am unsure if that level of gained attention is going to be as significant as the government is anticipating."
Over the weekend, the provincial government announced its Ontario-wide ban on cellphones in classrooms is an effort to improve student concentration. Beginning next academic year, all phones must remain on silent mode and out of view during school hours.
Students in kindergarten through Grade 6 will see those rules apply for the entire school day. Those in Grade 7 and above, however, will be allowed to use the devices between classes or during lunch.
Exceptions can be made for students who ask permission.
To set an example, teachers and classroom assistants will be asked to also not use their phones.
The province also said access to social media sites during the school day on board-owned devices and anyone using school networks will be prohibited.
For Amber Hughes, DSBN's parent involvement committee chair, the announcement appears to be less of a ban and more about enforcement.
She is interested in seeing how it rolls out from a government "famous for making an announcement without providing a ton of detail."
"We know that phones can be a valuable resource in schools when they're used properly, but I definitely see the risks in terms of distraction and bullying and all the things that go along with having a phone," said Hughes.
Removing access to social media sites, she said, "I don't think that's terribly inappropriate."
While she allows her 15-year-old son to take his phone to school, she said she does not allow her fifth-grade daughter to do the same, so the new rules will not apply to her.
"It's a little bit different in the high school level because they are leaving the school property and so to bring that phone with them makes sense," she said.
She said it's her job to educate her children about technology and let them know teachers are at school to educate, not monitor cellphones.
"Technology is not going anywhere. We are depending on it more and more and so we need to ensure that going forward, our kids understand when it is and isn't appropriate to be using their phone and what the suitable content looks like," said Hughes.
"Most adults that I know still don't manage their cellphones appropriately."
Jennifer McArthur, president of the Niagara elementary unit of Ontario English Catholic Teachers' Association, said teachers have raised concerns about cellphone misuse for years, but despite the province's announcement, it's unclear how the guidelines will improve conditions.
"It may actually result in teachers taking more time to police cellphone activity, which means less time for teaching," she said.
McArthur said the province is deflecting and ignoring more challenging topics on the minds of teachers, such as increased violence in the classroom and an urgent need for student mental-health supports.
To her, officials in Premier Doug Ford's government are "completely out-of-touch."
"They ignore teachers' expertise and push items onto teachers without genuine dialogue about the learning environment," said McArthur.
While the new rules will streamline school board procedures across Ontario, both DSBN and the Niagara Catholic District School Board have policies in place which address the use of mobile phones by students and teachers.
Niagara Catholic said its internet network blocks mainstream social media sites such as Snapchat, TikTok and Instagram, sites containing adult and extreme content and streaming services such as Netflix.
"We believe that social media has a time and a place, and class time - with very, very few exceptions, such as learning how to be discerning consumers of information - is not the appropriate place," said Jennifer Pellegrini, communications officer for the Catholic school board.
"We also have systems in place that prevent students from accessing these sites on board devices or networks using a VPN."
With changes not taking effect until September, DSBN communications officer Milica Petkovic said the public board has time to be "intentional and thoughtful" in its approach, address blocking access to social media sites and further build upon procedures and polices already in place.
CAPTION: Ontario's coming cellphone bans at schools will be accompanied by a requirement that school boards block access to social media sites.Shutterstock photo

CREDIT: Victoria Nicolaou Reporter

Full text: Tourism silos
not working,
alliance needed
2 for 1: St. Catharines and Lincoln take tourism to another level, April 15

I congratulate St. Catharines and Lincoln municipal councils for deciding to partner in tourism promotion. But this is a mere drop in the bucket in what should be taking place.
Tourism in Niagara is a David and Goliath situation, with Niagara Falls and Niagara-on-the-Lake overshadowing what I like to refer to as the "Other Niagara," that being the remaining 10 municipalities, who each are individually vying to promote their small piece of Niagara in a silo type of environment.
Are tourists able to comprehend or care whether they are in West Lincoln or Grimsby, etc.?
A logical solution would be for Niagara Region to resurrect the tourism department that once existed and again promote the whole region. Sadly, that is not likely to happen.
The alternative is for the 10 outsiders to form a "The Other Niagara" tourism alliance and publish along with a web page a magazine-type publication that would highlight the many attributes of each domain. It would be similar to what the region used to publish and it would be a bonus if the two Goliaths joined the alliance. This publication was a hot item in years past when Tourism Niagara had a booth at the Toronto Bike Show.
At present, if I were considering a vacation in the Niagara region, I would need to study a map and research 12 individual municipalities.
It is time Niagara breaks away from the silo mentality and promotes the whole region as one vacation area.
Paul Pattison, St. Catharines
Shared concern, different
conclusion
Municipal job duplication found right at the top, Letters, April 24

I do agree with the author's concerns with the inflationary increases for senior bureaucrats, but I'm not quite sure how, or why, that somehow "proves" a One Niagara, administered by a regional government and its growing army of managers and administrators, is the only solution?
And to demand that the Doug Ford provincial government should be the one to make this happen is, quite frankly, frightening.
It is proving to be totally unsuited to govern, although, sadly, I don't see any other party offering any better solutions.
Here in Niagara, I would like to see some significant reductions in the number of senior administrators at both the local and regional levels instead of the continued march toward even more overpaid senior bureaucrats.
How about comparing the total cost of regional government and the total cost of the 12 municipalities? I believe Niagara Region costs us way more than local government, even with their 12 chief administrative officers, and at least they have a direct interest in the municipalities they "serve" - far more than any regional chair or CAO.
Andrew Watts, Wainfleet

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-04-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Just a tempest
in a teapot
Firefighters make up more than half of Sunshine List earners across Niagara's 3 big cities, April 23

The Ontario Sunshine List was developed in 1996, when the dollar was worth double what it is today - $1.99 to be exact. The threshold for making the list was and still is $100,000 per year.
Why?
In 1996, there were 4,494 public employees on the list. Today the figure is 300,526. If this threshold was adjusted by the rate of inflation, as it is in Alberta, it would then become relevant.
Who cares about who makes $100,000? It is certainly better than the living wage ($40,000), but it is no longer that far out of line. Who doesn't change such a threshold in 27 years?
To my surprise, while researching figures for this letter, the most significant statistic I have come up with is that if the threshold were adjusted for inflation, there would have been 13,253 public-sector employees earning more than $200,000 per year. That's nearly three times as many as in 1996.
Is that good governance? All three levels of government (in Niagara's case four) have a hand in this boondoggle and this is the story that should be told, not the fact a few firefighters are making $120,000 or $130,000.
Bob Lessard, Welland
Street cleaning job not done
The street cleaners have been and gone, alas, without finishing my street. The winter mess is still there and the sweeper machines nowhere to be seen.
I have contacted the City of Niagara Falls' service department, but not surprisingly, without remedial action. What makes this especially galling is reading the article about the Sunshine List and the revelation some of its members are a group of Niagara Falls city managers.
Now l don't begrudge people making a good wage if they deserve it, but is there anybody up there who can justify their generous paycheques by actually having something done right? Cleaning my street would be a good start.
Dan Wheeler,
Niagara Falls
There should
be no surprise
Justin Trudeau veers to the left, so where does that leave Jagmeet Singh?, April 19

Thomas Walkom has written many opinion articles for this newspaper. As you would expect from his age and expertise, the question he presented, "As the Liberals veer left, what's next for the NDP?" would be a no-brainer for him.
The Liberals have always veered left when their poll numbers get low or an election is in the offing, then when they win they run
to the right, over and over again.
I'm surprised when people like Walkom and the public don't see this when it happens all the time.
If you want the things the NDP proposes, then vote for them.
Dana Harvey, Fort Erie

Full text: Premier Ford
panders to
his voting base
Premier Doug Ford's
rush to judgment, editorial cartoon, April 24

As the editorial cartoon points out, Premier Doug Ford's pretrial comments on this case were beyond belief.
Does he not know our system of justice is based on the idea that the accused is innocent until proven guilty? Is he unaware that an accused cannot be denied reasonable bail without just cause?
Reasonable restrictions to bail are available. Umar Zameer could have been ordered to surrender his passport or wear an ankle bracelet had he been considered a flight risk.
If the premier of Ontario was unaware of any of this, his ignorance, in the true sense of the word, would be worrisome.
The conclusion one is left with is that the premier's comments were made to pander to his law-and-order constituency.
Strange how so often politicians will not comment on cases as they are before the court. Unless, of course, it serves a political purpose.
John Rowan, Welland
Union gathering political quackery
Pierre Poilievre slammed by Canada's top labour leader: 'Have you ever, ever, anywhere in Canada seen him walk the picket line?'

The heartbeat of politically biased quackery was on blatant display at the recent "national meeting of union heads" in Ottawa.
If that's our best choice of a top labour leader, it's not surprising that Canada's economy continues to decline.
If any Conservative politician chose to appear to walk on the picket line in support of any union, public or private, he or she would be likely to suffer personal insults at best and physical harm at worst.
And this from a top labour leader who did not invite Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre to attend, while celebrating the presence of Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and NDP Leader Jagmeet Singh, both of whom were applauded for attacking Poilievre at the event.
It's a funny world we are living in. Union leaders appear to me to look pretty well done by when seen in public. They aren't elected in any democratic sense of the word, yet have tenure that lasts for years.
They show no interest in any transparency, but merely choose to hold the dwindling number of private taxpayers to ransom, many of whom are not union members, whenever they decide to ignore the wishes of those same taxpayers and create chaos in the economy whenever they choose to strike, which continues to become their first weapon of choice.
Andrew Watts, Wainfleet

Document 199 of 249

Bob Gale returning to parks board: Councillor credited with breaking up an 'old boys club' and saving millions

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/bob-gale-returning-parks-board/docview/3046381447/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Bob+Gale+returning+to+parks+board%3A+Councillor+credited+with+breaking+up+an+%27old+boys+club%27+and+saving+millions&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-04-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The maverick who left the Niagara Parks Commission board as a whistleblower is returning, this time at the head of the table.
The province has appointed Bob Gale, a Niagara Region councillor for Niagara Falls, as the chair of NPC's board of commissioners.
"I'm a Niagara Falls boy," Gale said. "I was raised here, and there are parts of the Niagara Glen that I know like the back of my hand.
"I'm happy the premier thought enough of me to put me here. His comment was, 'Bob, run it like a business. I know you can do that.'"
The commission's role is maintaining, protecting and showcasing the falls and Niagara River corridor. The self-funded agency was established in 1885 and is responsible for 1,325 hectares of parkland, stretching 56 kilometres along the Niagara River corridor between lakes Erie and Ontario.
"They called me and asked if I would be interested in being on the board," Gale said. "I told them I've been there and done that - unless you're interested in me as chair. I said I would definitely be interested in that."
The province appointed Gale's daughter, Jessica Friesen, to serve on the NPC board in 2020. Gale said she has stepped down.
"She had to resign because of the conflict of interest," Gale said. "She did it voluntarily for her dad. I don't believe anybody ever resigned for reasons other than health, and I'm proud she would do that for me.
"She knew what I went through and what it means for me to be here as chair."
Gale called his previous term on the board from 2006 to 2009 the "toughest time" of his life.
Gale is credited with breaking up an "old boys club" on the commission board and saving NPC hundreds of millions of dollars by forcing an overhaul of procurement procedures after it secretly approved a 25-year renewal of the lease that would have allowed the Maid of the Mist to ferry visitors through the Niagara Gorge to the falls without any competitive bids in 2008.
Gale's appointment to the commission wasn't renewed after he went public with his concerns and filed a complaint with Ontario's integrity commissioner.
"My family saw what it did to me," Gale said. "I took them on alone for two years, but I was right, and I just kept going."
The complaint led to two government audits, an overhaul of the board and a competitive procurement won by Hornblower to ferry tourists that guaranteed NPC more than $60 million during the first five years of a 30-year lease worth more than $500 million.
Gale's term ends on April 10, 2025. He takes over as chair from former Wainfleet mayor April Jeffs, who was appointed to the board in January 2019. Jeffs became chair of the 12-person commission after Sandie Bellows' death in October 2021.
Neil Lumsden, the province's minister of tourism, culture and sport, confirmed that Jeffs is leaving NPC to serve on the Niagara Falls Bridge Commission board.
"April Jeffs has done an amazing job as chair of the Niagara Parks Commission," Lumsden said in an email to the Standard. "We're grateful for her accomplishments in strengthening Niagara's tourism industry and supporting jobs and economic growth.
"She will be a terrific asset to the Niagara bridge commission as she continues working to keep our economy moving."
Gale shrugged off a question about patronage appointments. Both Gale and Jeffs have run unsuccessfully for the governing Progressive Conservatives.
"They know my record," Gale said. "They know I'll ask questions. I am all about accountability, transparency and fairness."
Niagara Falls NDP MPP Wayne Gates said Jeffs worked closely with his office, and he is disappointed she has been replaced.
"I've known about this for a while, and I tried to find out why from the tourism minister, but we're not getting any of that information from the government," Gates said.
"Sometimes the government makes appointments, and as the opposition, we say, 'What are you doing? You only appoint your conservative buddies.'"
Gates said Jeffs earned the respect of his office and most organizations with which he is familiar.
"She did a good job. David Adames (NPC's chief executive officer) does a great job. They were a good team. I understand they had one of the most successful years in parks commission history."
According to the province's website, board members are appointed to terms of no more than three years.
CAPTION: Bob Gale is the new chair of the Niagara Parks CommissionOak Hall, off Portage Road in Niagara Falls, houses the administrative offices of the Niagara Parks Commission.Niagara Parks photo
