Document 220 of 249

Explaining Ontario's big bucks housing rollout: The Building Faster Fund rewards municipalities based on overall targets, but it's complicated

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/explaining-ontarios-big-bucks-housing-rollout/docview/2957705364/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Explaining+Ontario%27s+big+bucks+housing+rollout%3A+The+Building+Faster+Fund+rewards+municipalities+based+on+overall+targets%2C+but+it%27s+complicated&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-03-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario government officials have been on a big cheque tour across the province in recent weeks, including stops in St. Catharines and Welland, to reward communities that hit provincial housing targets.
Over the past three weeks, Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra, Premier Doug Ford and associates have made funding announcements in at least 16 municipalities with rewards ranging from $400,000 to $114 million.
"Obviously, you know why we're here," Calandra said at St. Catharines City Hall on Monday, standing next to a large novelty cheque from the Building Faster Fund.
"It's a big, huge cheque - $2.3 million. That is a very, very good sum and it recognizes the fact that St. Catharines is doing what it takes to get shovels in the ground to build more homes for the people in your community."
That cheque for St. Catharines was distinctly larger than the one handed out at Welland Civic Square 10 days earlier, despite Welland seeing more housing starts in 2023.
St. Catharines had 648 units begin construction last year, leading to the reward money for meeting 80 per cent of the province's 2023 target for the city.
Welland, which surpassed its housing target by 276 per cent with 868 home starts, received $1.72 million.
St. Catharines wasn't the only city that built fewer units than Welland but got more money.
Peterborough, which had 506 housing starts, received $1.88 million, while Caledon, which started 778 homes, was rewarded with $2.8 million.
Brantford got $3.06 million for 788 units under construction in 2023.
But what may seem like a random reward system is actually done through very specific calculations, according to the ministry.
A pot of money - $400 million a year for three years - is being distributed based on a municipality's targets and overall contribution to the province's goal of 1.5 million new homes by 2031.
Fifty municipalities were given yearly and overall targets and are eligible for reward money if they achieve at least 80 per cent of their target and bonus money if they exceed it.
If a municipality was given a target of 150,000 homes by 2031, for instance, it would represent 10 per cent of the 1.5-million target. That municipality would be eligible for 10 per cent of the $400 million in annual funding, a $40-million sum.
The province's 2023 goal was to see 110,000 units built.
St. Catharines' share of that was supposed to be 807 units, representing 0.73 per cent of the 110,000 goal.
If St. Catharines had seen all of those units built, it would have received 0.73 per cent of the $400 million in funding - about $2.9 million. Because it reached 80 per cent of the target it received 80 per cent of the $2.9 million, for a $2.35-million cheque.
Welland, meanwhile, had a target of 315 units which represented 0.29 per cent of the provincial goal of 110,000. That made Welland eligible for 0.29 per cent of the $400 million in funding, or approximately $1.15 million.
Because Welland exceeded its target by 276 per cent and built 868 homes, it got a bonus of $573,333 based on further calculations, which was the maximum amount it could receive.
Niagara Falls, which hasn't had a cheque presentation, was given a target of 587 units and built 401 in 2023, falling short of the 80 per cent threshold at 68 per cent.
Some politicians in Ontario have raised concerns about the target reward system because it distributes money based on actual construction starts of units, rather than the number of approvals given by cities. Approvals are something cities have control of - a developer not proceeding with a project because of financing or other reasons is out of a city's hands.
According to the provincial housing tracker online, about half of the 50 municipalities given targets for 2023 haven't met them.
Nineteen municipalities, including Welland, met or exceeded their targets and seven, including St. Catharines, are on track. Another 24, including Niagara Falls, were below the 80 per cent target.
Niagara municipalities' targets by 2031 are 11,000 units in St. Catharines, 8,000 in Niagara Falls and 4,300 in Welland.
CAPTION: Municipal Affairs and Housing Minister Paul Calandra announces more than $2.35 million for the City of St. Catharines on Monday for meeting 80 per cent of its housing-build target last year.Matthew Rae, parliamentary assistant to the minister of municipal affairs and housing, announces $1.7 million in provincial funding for the City of Welland for exceeding its 2023 housing goal set by the province.Julie Jocsak St. Catharines Standard file photos

CREDIT: Karena Walter Reporter

Subject: Funding; Housing starts; Cities; Municipalities

Business indexing term: Subject: Housing starts

Title: Explaining Ontario's big bucks housing rollout:   The Building Faster Fund rewards municipalities based on overall targets, but it's complicated

Document 221 of 249

Readers write Letters to the editor

Abstract: None available.

Full text: Climate action
is needed
Privately and publicly, Doug Ford warns Justin Trudeau's Liberals they'll be 'annihilated' if they raise the carbon price, March 13

I am disgusted with the political leaders of both our major parties, federal and provincial.
The federal Conservatives carry on with the catchy little phrase "axe the tax;" Ontario Premier Doug Ford screams threats about not getting re-elected due to the carbon tax to Prime Minister Justin Trudeau; meanwhile, our prime minister talks around the issue of additional money being raised by this climate change policy, but avoids the reason for the tax.
The Conservatives need to understand that our children and grandchildren will have a massive climate battle on their hands if we, in 2024, don't take initiatives to combat it now.
Until there are changes in the leadership of both parties, I'm afraid my generation of Canadians will go down in history as the "could have, would have, should have" people, who lived in comfort when they might have provided leadership on climate change.

Philip O. Main, Fonthill
Council's decision was 'prudent'
Dispatch decision still doesn't make sense, letters to the editor, March 12

Niagara Falls city council's decision not to amalgamate fire dispatch services with St. Catharines makes perfect sense. In making this difficult decision, council should be given credit for considering all perspectives, and not just trying to save precious tax dollars.
They understood Niagara Falls is a unique community with its varying population and vast special circumstances. They appreciated the importance of having a consolidated and more controlled dispatch centre.
Niagara Falls city council was prudent to acknowledge the viewpoints of experts, and not those only concerned with lowering taxes, cutting services and furthering personal agendas.

Fred Audibert,
Niagara Falls

Re: Urgent care centre
'fundamental part' of Fort Erie's primary care: Redekop, March 10
When I moved to Fort Erie in 2004, we had a hospital. Since then, the town's population has grown, taxes have doubled and our services have disappeared.

We want a 24-7 emergency room, nothing less.

Mental-health services in south Niagara are a sad joke.
I have no confidence in our mayor or provincial government to do anything useful. Premier Doug Ford is preparing for privatized health care. We are at the mercy of incompetent politicians and bureaucrats.

Russ Young, Fort Erie

Document 222 of 249

Canada's energy minister says carbon pricing benefits families, environment: Rebate rebranding good messaging, Wilkinson says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/canadas-energy-minister-says-carbon-pricing/docview/2957097451/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Canada%27s+energy+minister+says+carbon+pricing+benefits+families%2C+environment%3A+Rebate+rebranding+good+messaging%2C+Wilkinson+says&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-03-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Canada Energy and Natural Resources Minister Jonathan Wilkinson says the government must do a better job at selling its carbon pricing plan to the public.
As Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre continues to hammer the Liberal government's policy with his cross-country travelling "Axe the Tax" campaign, promising to remove it if elected, Wilkinson said despite the benefits of the carbon tax few people know what it's about.
He said one study by Trevor Tombe, an economics professor at University of Calgary and the university's school of public policy, revealed if the carbon tax and rebate were removed, it would harm lower- and middle-income households more than high-income people "so it is an affordability measure and it helps to reduce carbon emissions."
"But Pierre Poilievre has been effective in somehow convincing people it doesn't do that," said Wilkinson in an interview. "The government needs to do better in communication. We have to make sure it is much more visible to people."
But Niagara Falls MP Tony Baldinelli said rebranding "a harmful tax policy will not change Canadians' views on it," arguing most oppose the pricing, especially as the cost of groceries and other critical items continue to rise.
"But (Prime Minister Justin) Trudeau doesn't care that this will make everything more expensive," Baldinelli said.
Wilkinson, who was in Thorold March 8 to announce $15 million in funding to assist three Niagara-based companies as they build clean-energy facilities, said if the Conservatives and Poilievre win government in 2025, "he will likely eliminate it."
"People should be concerned."
That means, said Wilkinson, the former federal environment minister, not only will the government's "most economically efficient instrument" to reduce greenhouse gas emissions end, but it will also impact the most vulnerable individuals in society.
"The people who benefit the most from the rebates live on modest incomes," said Wilkinson.
The Canada carbon rebate, formerly known as the climate action incentive payment, has been in effect since 2019. The price on carbon is designed to change people's behaviour to reduce using fossil fuels. It consists of a fuel charge collected by the federal government and a rebate is issued on a quarterly basis that returns 90 per cent of that revenue to Canadian households. The other 10 per cent is sent to businesses, farmers and Indigenous communities.
People who use less fuel will pay less tax, but still receive the same rebate as someone who burns more fuel.
The government changed the name of the rebate for 2024-25 to "clarify its function, and make its meaning and relationship to the carbon pricing system more intuitive for Canadians," a switch Wilkinson applauds. He said in the past Canadians were receiving a cheque through direct deposit from the federal government without even knowing what it was for.
"The rebranding of the carbon rebate was a step," he said. "We have to make it much more visible to people. The government needs to do better communicating with Canadians and showing them they are benefiting. That is something we are thinking about a lot."
The government last October opted to exempt home heating oil from the carbon pricing for the next three years, as well as double the rural supplement in the tax rebate program and added more incentives to help rural Canadians switch to electric heat pumps.
The hike is to begin on April 1, and it will increase fuel costs by about 3.5 cents a litre for regular gasoline and four cents a litre for diesel. The government's plan is to increase the cost each year until 2030 from about 14.31 cents a litre to about 37.43 cents a litre. As of April, the tax in Ontario will be 17.71 cents.
"Trudeau's decision to quadruple the carbon tax is the last thing Canadians need," said Baldinelli. "A common-sense Conservative government under Pierre Poilievre will axe the carbon tax on everything for everyone, everywhere."
Alberta has the highest total rebate for a family of four with about $1,800 annually, followed by Saskatchewan at $1,504, Manitoba at $1,200, Newfoundland and Labrador at $1,192 and Ontario at $1,120.
Saskatchewan Premier Scott Moe is eliminating the carbon pricing, which Wilkinson said will result in "making poor people poorer."
"And that is what Pierre Poilievre will do," added Wilkinson.
The federal government has set reducing the country's greenhouse gas emissions by up to 38 per cent below 2019 levels by 2030, which is below what Canada committed to at the 2015 Paris Agreement of up to 45 per cent below 2005 levels by 2030.
Globally, burning fossil fuels account for more than 75 per cent of the greenhouse gas emissions that are driving climate change, according to the United Nations.
Despite not meeting the Paris targets, Wilkinson said Canada continues to invest in clean energy, including funding electric battery plants in Ontario, in an effort to transition to a decarbonized society.
"Anybody who thinks you can go back to the day where you are simply going to pump more oil and gas as an economic strategy, that's just living in the past," said Wilkinson.
Meanwhile, the Liberals are keenly interested in what happens in the United States presidential election this fall, which is pitting former president Donald Trump against President Joe Biden.
"If Mr. Trump comes to power, he will be very similar as before. There will be no action on climate change," said Wilkinson.
But even when Trump was in power from 2016 to 2020, the U.S. government managed to establish climate change agreements with various state governments, such as with California.
Still, most of the countries in the world "are very much of one mind in driving forward with the energy transition" from fossil fuels to clean energy, said Wilkinson.
Regardless of who is president of the United States, Wilkinson said the "momentum will continue" in adopting clean energy.
"It's like a boulder running downhill, even if Mr. Trump decides not to be part of the transition."
CAPTION: Jonathan Wilkinson, Canada's minister of energy and natural resources, announced funding for clean fuel projects while at the Thorold Multimodal Hub last week.

CREDIT: Kevin Werner Reporter

Full text: Ford's vote-losing declaration
Ford promises to build
as many jails as needed to keep criminals behind bars, March 8

I have never been a member of any political party. Provincially, I have voted for every "major" one.
Between elections, I try to stay informed; at election time, I assess platforms and evaluate the performance of the incumbent.
For the first time in memory, I have reduced my choices by one. Had Premier Doug Ford said he would build as many hospitals or long-term- care homes as we need, he may well have had my vote.
Building jails over these facilities demonstrates a lack of priorities that would make a difference. Locking more people up does not, as the experience of our neighbours to the south clearly shows.
John Rowan, Welland
Captured by
the bamboozle
It is curious the route taken by populist, egomaniacal blowhards with questionable histories who develop into "tough on crime" politicians.
Even more curious is the level of support they are able to garner to themselves.
Carl Sagan's words have never rung more true:
"One of the saddest lessons of history is this: If we've been bamboozled long enough, we tend to reject any evidence of the bamboozle. We're no longer interested in finding out the truth. The bamboozle has captured us. It's simply too painful to acknowledge, even to ourselves, that we've been taken. Once you give a charlatan power over you, you almost never get it back."
An enlightened populace is an empowered people.
Roger Demers, Welland
Changes needed
to Niagara council
Re: Port Dalhousie pedestrian mall gets thumbs down from city council, March 5
Another reason for a double duty city-regional councillor model like Oakville has is made clear by the Port Dalhousie pedestrian mall debate.
Under the Oakville model, one of the Port Dalhousie ward councillors would have been sitting at Niagara Region council at the very beginning of this debate and would have had the opportunity and the duty to share the local concerns a year ago.
We need to modernize Niagara Region with a change to the Halton Region model of governance. These local regional road issues would be dealt with sooner and more democratically than the current model allows.
Bruce Timms, St. Catharines
Dispatch decision makes no sense
Re: Niagara Falls has demanding fire dispatch requirements, March 8
While I appreciate the perspective of the writer, the non-amalgamation of fire dispatch still does not make sense.
How do other emergency services address dispatch issues for a world-class city such as Niagara Falls? It's not by duplicating the service.
Allen McKay, St. Catharines

Full text: Drive to build homes will cost taxpayers
Re: Developers should pay
for infrastructure, Letters,
March 6
An interesting letter that, unfortunately, failed to emphasize some important points.
While he is certainly not alone, Welland Mayor Frank Campion is solely interested in finding ways to "make the ratepayer pay for growth" and many other things, just as other current mayors and councils do.
Their only income is from ratepayers/taxpayers to "fund" increasingly failing initiatives. Without increased taxes they would quickly bankrupt their constituencies.
And to dismiss their pride in getting regional, provincial, and federal "fundin'" as no more than a "let us keep in mind" thought, is a complete let-off for all our current politicians!
They all ignore where this "funding" comes from and instead, frequently claim they have been granted some magical "free" money to do with as they will all because of their own hard work.
How many times have we read of politicians "investing" our tax dollars, without one of them asking their constituents what they think about it? I can't think of a single politician of any political party who I would ever ask to take my money to invest.
And with Premier Doug Ford wanting to reduce development fees then it is inevitable this drive to build more and more homes, a vast majority not close to being seen as affordable, will have to be funded by even more raised taxes.
Andrew Watts, Wainfleet
Months-long wait times for MRIs
is too long
Her migraines turned out to be a tumour, but she waited a year for an MRI to find out, March 6
Reading the article about the woman who waited a year for a magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) because of ongoing migraines only to discover she had a brain tumour raises the issue of wait times we Canadians face.
If your medical issue is not determined to be life-threatening, you wait for your MRI, sometimes for weeks or many months.
I was having medical issues with my ankles/feet.
Not life-threatening, but to get an accurate diagnosis through an MRI in Niagara, my approximate wait time would be months, maybe a year.
While recently visiting Florida, I began researching the potential of obtaining an MRI in that state. I was able to purchase a doctor's referral, book an MRI, obtain the MRI report, and schedule an appointment with a surgeon to analyze my MRI all in four days for less than $600 (U.S.). With an exact diagnosis of my problems, I was able to proceed with the appropriate treatment.
This is not meant as criticism of our provincial health-care system or that we should be like Florida; but is there not an alternative to how we provide MRIs in our province?
David Pihach, Wainfleet

Document 225 of 249

Mulroney had a personal touch: Ex-PM 'fearless' with tough choices

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/mulroney-had-personal-touch/docview/2934178233/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Mulroney+had+a+personal+touch%3A+Ex-PM+%27fearless%27+with+tough+choices&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-03-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Former prime minister Brian Mulroney is remembered in local conservative circles as a passionate leader with a personal touch and the courage to make groundbreaking decisions.
Rob Nicholson first won a seat in the House of Commons representing Niagara Falls in the 1984 election that brought Mulroney to power. He joined Mulroney's cabinet as attorney general from 1989 to 1993.
Nicholson said it was "easy" to work with Mulroney.
"It was terrific to be with him," Nicholson said. "He was always so fair and open and honest. He was always on the right track, I used to believe, and it was easy for me to support him."
Mulroney was 84. A family spokesperson said he died Thursday at a hospital in Palm Beach, Fla., where he was being treated after a recent fall.
Nicholson said he appreciated the leadership role Mulroney played for Canada on the world stage.
"It didn't matter whether it was the United States or Britain or NATO, or any of these others, he always had a leadership role to play, and it was very impressive - in particular with respect to the question of apartheid.
"He took the leadership role on this and put pressure on South Africa to stop their apartheid regimes. It was one of the things that impressed me about him."
Nicholson also said Mulroney was fearless in tackling what could be controversial issues if he believed it was right for Canada.
"With the free trade agreement that we have with the United States, there was some controversy about that, but he truly believed, and he made the point to us that it was the right thing to do for Canada and we would prosper in it," he said, adding Mulroney had a way of connecting with people on a personal level.
"He would reach out to people and be supportive of people, even those in different political parties, and I always agreed with that. When one of my kids was born or something, he made a point of pointing that out to everybody that I had a new baby child."
Ken Atkinson, a lawyer and former St. Catharines city councillor who won a seat representing the city in Mulroney's government from 1988 to 1993, also remembered Mulroney's personal touch.
"During my time in office, my father, Harold Atkinson, passed away suddenly," Atkinson said. "One of the first calls I received was from Brian Mulroney to offer his sympathy to my mother and our family. He had lost his father and was very empathetic to how I was feeling, and that call provided me with much comfort.
"I feel very fortunate and honoured to have served in his government and to support his leadership on such matters as the Canada-U.S. Free Trade Agreement, acid rain and the support of Nelson Mandela against the apartheid regime of South Africa."
Atkinson said the former prime minister cared about his MPs and the issues concerning their ridings.
"In particular, he wanted to know more about Ontario Paper since his father had worked for the company in Baie Comeau," Atkinson said.
"Whether it was the wine industry, improving the piers at Port Dalhousie, or our auto industry, he was always aware and supportive of our Niagara area."
Rob Welch, a lawyer and Niagara Region councillor from 1985 to 1994, has deep ties to the Progressive Conservative Party of Canada. Welch's father, Bob, represented St. Catharines at Queen's Park from 1963 to 1985 and made a run for the provincial party leadership in 1971 at the convention Bill Davis won.
Welch met Mulroney in 1975 when Welch was home for a reading weekend at Queen's University.
Mulroney was invited to a gathering of party faithful at the Parkway Complex on Ontario Street in St. Catharines as he made the rounds during his first run at party leadership.
"He certainly impressed me," Welch said. "He was probably 36, and I would have been 21. I decided to support him in that leadership race and ran and won a delegate position with the Queen's Youth PC club."
Welch observed Mulroney during his time on the political stage and said one of his strengths was bringing people together and moving forward on big issues such as free trade and changes in the tax system that initiated the GST.
"We tried a couple of times to get the constitution back home, and that obviously didn't work, but he tried," Welch said. "I think he made mistakes, which are well documented, and there were times when he had a bigger personality than others would have liked, but on the other hand the results were there.
"In later years, he was certainly seen with much more respect as those mistakes were well in the rear-view mirror."
With files from Ray Spiteri
CAPTION: Brian Mulroney meets the local media in this 1983 photo at the Henley Tavern in St. Catharines. Former colleagues say the former PM was always supportive of Niagara.St. Catharines Standard file photo

CREDIT: Bill Sawchuk Reporter With files from Ray Spiteri

Document 226 of 249

Councillor DiMarco docked about $4,600 for verbal attack on Axiak: City staffer blocked emails from Welland Ward 4 representative

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/councillor-dimarco-docked-about-4-600-verbal/docview/2933743204/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Councillor+DiMarco+docked+about+%244%2C600+for+verbal+attack+on+Axiak%3A+City+staffer+blocked+emails+from+Welland+Ward+4+representative&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-03-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: An apology email from Tony DiMarco to a senior city staffer following an outburst by the controversial Welland councillor last year never reached its intended recipient, the city's integrity commissioner says.
In a presentation Tuesday, Deborah Anschell said then-community services director, now chief administrative officer, Rob Axiak had blocked the Ward 4 councillor's emails and didn't see the apology.
DiMarco tried to apologize for an outburst on Sept. 5 when, with the city's livestream off and as he was being kicked out of council chambers, he said, "Axiak, you've got to go, buddy. You have screwed up every project you have touched in this city. Everything you touch, you have screwed up in the past two years. Get out of here."
The councillor apologized to Mayor Frank Campion, another target of his outburst that evening, but was unable to get through by phone to Axiak.
Anschell's report is the result of a code of conduct complaint against DiMarco by then-acting CAO Sherri-Marie Millar, the only person to approach the councillor in chambers in an effort to get him to leave before security arrived.
"In the matter that I investigated, the facts were very simple and undisputed in the sense the Coun. DiMarco made some comments to Rob Axiak at a council meeting that were the subject of a complaint," said Anschell.
Her investigation and report was the second on DiMarco before city council Tuesday night. The first reprimanded him for breaches of the Municipal Conflict of Interest Act and city code of conduct.
DiMarco did not treat Axiak with respect, professionalism or courtesy, used abusive commentary and insulting words toward a staff member and showed a lack of decorum, said Anschell.
In the report, she said the councillor's only explanation for his comments was he has a lengthy history with Axiak, and had a number of issues with his performance, identifying a list of 17 items that were not named.
Anschell recommended the councillor face a formal reprimand from his colleagues and a 60-day pay suspension.
DiMarco is now the subject of eight integrity commissioner reports since 2017 and has been docked 240 days' pay in that time.
Ward 3 Coun. John Chiocchio said though he may agree with Anschell's report, he didn't agree with taking away someone's pay.
"That's just the way I feel. I don't like the system because it doesn't make sense to me."
Chiocchio said withholding DiMarco's pay still leaves the city in a hole with the cost of the reports.
Like Chiocchio, Ward 5 Coun. Claudette Richard wasn't in favour of stripping DiMarco of his pay.
"That's not to say I agree with the conduct of Coun. DiMarco. It's sad it's the only tool we have. The process needs to change," she said.
Last year, council supported Bill 5, the Stopping Harassment and Abuse by Local Leaders Act, introduced by Stephen Blais, MPP for Orléans and the Liberal critic for municipal affairs and housing.
The bill, which did not pass, would have permitted municipalities to direct their integrity commissioners to apply to the courts to vacate a member's seat for failing to comply with workplace violence and harassment policies, as well as restrict officials whose seat has been vacated from seeking immediate subsequent re-election, reappointment to council or local boards.
Before the vote on whether to suspend DiMarco's pay, Campion said taking action is part of council's job.
"If we don't do something, it's sending a really, really bad message to our staff. We're sending a message to a staff that says, 'Guess what? You can get yelled at, you can get harassed in council chambers, and it's OK.'
"We all say, 'I don't support what he's done,' but I'm not going to suspend his pay either. Those are contradictory. You have to take a strong stand, and we need to tell our staff that you can work here safely without worrying because we're going to do something when these things happen," said Campion.
Council voted to reprimand DiMarco and suspend his pay for 60 days. Based on this year's annual base pay for councillors, which is $28,041, the suspension represents a loss of about $4,600.
In mid-February Ontario Premier Doug Ford's government said new legislation that could see local politicians thrown out of office for workplace harassment should be introduced before the summer.
CAPTION: Ward 4 Coun. Tony DiMarco was reprimanded twice on Tuesday. City council heard Rob Axiak blocked emails from Ward 4 Coun. Tony DiMarco.

CREDIT: Dave Johnson Reporter

Full text: Protests won't
accomplish
anything
Niagara Region council seeks integrity commissioner's help after Gaza motion uproar, Feb. 23

Regional councillors voted 26-2 to quash St. Catharines Coun. Haley Bateman's call for a ceasefire in Gaza. I wholeheartedly agree this matter should not have been on the agenda. This protest has nothing to do with our residents' daily lives.
Protesters may have good intentions holding a candlelight vigil at Niagara Region headquarters, but what did it accomplish? Nothing.
This protest, like many others around the world, will not ease the conflict between Israel and Hamas.
Our world traveller, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has met with leaders from various countries, and he, too, has accomplished nothing to ease the tensions in Gaza. The destruction, injuries and death will continue until the Israeli government and Hamas agree to some kind of solution.
In the meantime, local protesters at regional headquarters and Coun. Bateman are just dreamers if they think their actions will end this.
I would suggest that Bateman and the protesters pack their bags and take a trip to Gaza. Let your voices be heard in person by the Israeli government and Hamas leaders and see if they will listen to you.
The residents of Gaza cry in anguish on a daily basis as their lives are being destroyed. Are they being listened to by Israel's or Hamas's leaders?
What good are protests at regional headquarters?
Lou Cesar, St. Catharines
Ontario should further expand alcohol sales
Niagara industry says moving alcohol sales to convenience stores 'good first step', Dec. 16

The only difference between Liquor Control Board of Ontario (LCBO) and say warehouse stores like Costco or Home Depot is the LCBO generally provides less service.
Ontario Premier Doug Ford is going to put beer and wine into corner and grocery stores to provide more convenience, and create the opportunity to produce more tax revenue from more sales.
Everything these new outlets sell has to come through the LCBO system.
Why not go a step further and allow larger corporations to sell alcohol products not necessarily sold through the LCBO. Both Costco and Walmart are large distributors of alcohol products in the U.S. and could easily do so in Ontario.
I would add a caveat, which would be that these stores would be required to provide say 25 per cent of shelf space to Canadian-made products in all categories of beer, wine and spirits, and that these be wholly Canadian and not foreign blends.
The end result would be the consumer would be better serviced. Ford's government would have its tax revenue increased, and he would have no excuse for not properly funding health care.
Bob Koruna, St. Catharines

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write+Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-02-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Pharmacare
a non-starter
Liberals and NDP reach pharmacare deal that will cover birth control, diabetes drugs, Feb. 23
The federal Liberals have finally reached a level of desperation I thought was out of reach.
The numbers for free pharmacare were never sustainable and, in my mind, a non-starter. But here we are, a government hanging by a thread kowtowing to the lowly NDP in some shallow attempt to salvage votes and hang on to power.
I weep for our children and our grandchildren who will be burdened with this debt for generations to come. This will be Justin Trudeau's legacy, budgets don't balance themselves after all.
Alain Fournier, Beamsville
Bring back Charles
Flyers a nuisance, Feb. 22
The letter regarding delivery of flyer advertising hits a chord. My daily dog-walking activities takes us by numerous unclaimed parcels along north end streets. Throwing the items through the back window of a car is haphazard. Many land on boulevards, not driveways, and others so carelessly placed one wonders for which home the delivery was destined.
Charles was our local mailbox to mailbox deliverer of the weekly grocery ads. No speculative throwing. Nothing was left loose. Charles was reliable as he walked over several days to complete his deliveries.
No matter the weather, with his cart holding his stock protected from the weather, he would complete his job.
Bring back Charles.
Ross Smith, Niagara Falls
Approvals
continue
They just keep coming, don't they? And not just in Niagara, although I doubt there is a single municipality in the region where the last folk who find out they have been fooled by both their elected councils and municipal staff they employ and pay for are the citizens, property owners and taxpayers, while developers are free to build what they want to maximize profits.
When is someone going to ask the developers' biggest supporters why?
That would be municipal planners. Every planning application lands on their desks first.
A planner's primary role, supposedly, is to approve applications that comply with existing planning policies, official plans and zoning bylaws. Instead they work with the developer to concentrate on producing a planning application to recommend to council for approval. They usually include a couple of amendments to make it sound plausible while ignoring many fairly important and basic policies already in place.
And despite concerns coming from residents, or some on council or committees of adjustment, the planner and developer know they always have the Ontario Land Tribunal to rely on, which will almost in every case rule in favour of the developer.
Andrew Watts, Wainfleet

Document 229 of 249

School boards face struggle putting teachers in classes: Province's 'blatant disrespect' taking toll on staff and pupils

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/school-boards-face-struggle-putting-teachers/docview/2931930900/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=School+boards+face+struggle+putting+teachers+in+classes%3A+Province%27s+%27blatant+disrespect%27+taking+toll+on+staff+and+pupils&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-02-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With the provincial government looking at "every option available" to ease a shortage of teachers, Niagara's English-speaking school boards are dealing with varying degrees of pressures, each maximizing programs and recruiting efforts to limit staffing challenges.
Last week Education Minister Stephen Lecce said the province is looking at a multitude of actions to increase the supply of qualified educators, which he called "a real challenge" for Ontario school boards.
Niagara Catholic District School Board is not exempt to the staffing pressures and while it's "certainly interested and looking forward" to what the ministry puts forward, its focus is on ensuring it has qualified educators in its classrooms, said Julia Tiessen, its executive officer of human resources.
Stemming back to the pandemic, Tiessen said the board has had trouble finding substitute teachers. There has also been "a lot of retirements," with teachers deciding no longer choosing to work past their retirement dates.
And as a growing board, Niagara Catholic has looked to hire more contracted employees in recent years than it had previously.
"(That) is exciting for us but that comes with ensuring we have qualified teachers in front of our students, so keeping the healthy numbers within our occasional teacher supply-list roster is very important for us," said Tiessen.
For Niagara Catholic, the difficulty has been in attracting and retaining educators with specialized qualifications, such as in technology and French, with French-qualified teachers a known challenge across the province.
Tiessen said the board has participated in French-focused job fairs and has put together "strong recruiting strategies" to make Niagara Catholic and the region an "employer of choice and a board of choice for young students."
District School Board of Niagara communications officer Milica Petkovic said the public system is seeing minimal teacher and education assistant shortages on days with high absence rates - illnesses around cold and flu season combined with activities that take teachers out of the classroom.
She said it has a "fulsome supply list and limited shortages" and is hiring teachers to meet its increasing enrolment numbers.
"While we have some unfilled positions, we have an ongoing plan for our internal events, an occasional staff roster, a recruitment strategy, and we review and predict our needs throughout the year," said Petkovic in an email.
"We are fortunate to not be in the position that other boards may find themselves in with barriers that are beyond their control."
When it comes to filling daily absences, the Catholic board said it first looks to its occasional teachers list. Tiessen said Niagara Catholic has a 22 per cent threshold of occasional to permanent teachers that it works within, as part of its collective agreement.
It would then look to retired teachers to fill gaps, followed by bringing in student-teachers - the province has given permission for teacher-educational students to fill in - and, when necessary, relying on emergency adult instructors to "ensure that we have safety at the forefront of our students" while it finds replacements.
Niagara Catholic has also used letters of permission, which allow the board to hire non-certified teachers for specified subjects and courses through a school year.
To combat its few shortages, DSBN said it calls upon retired teachers and occasionally takes advantage of the province's certified teachers practicum students. While it is not a new strategy for the public board, it also employs education assistant students during their practicums to fill vacancies.
"Limited availability of supply staff was high the past two years following the pandemic, however, it has begun to level out this school year resulting in very few shortages," said Petkovic.
For Niagara's teachers unions, challenges teachers and boards across the province are facing - with classes being covered by unqualified and uncertified adults - have been "exacerbated" in recent years, and are all signs of a lack of planning and "chronic underfunding" of public education by the current provincial government.
Shannon Smith, Ontario Secondary School Teachers' Federation District 22 president, agreed that while DBSN does not have as many challenges as other boards, there are myriad issues teachers are dealing with.
Those include larger class sizes, leaving students with less support, mandatory e-learning with no direct support, fewer education assistants to help students with more complex needs and increased violent incidents in schools.
"These are all issues that are causing recruitment and retention issues in education across the province," said Smith.
Jennifer McArthur, president of the Niagara elementary unit for Ontario English Catholic Teachers' Association, said the province's refusal to engage with teachers and other education experts to address the crisis has made teaching a less attractive profession.
McArthur said she spoke with a new teacher last spring who was contemplating leaving education, following in the footsteps of other classmates who found "satisfying work in other fields."
"Veteran teachers are reporting that they have never felt so squeezed by the demands and additional burdens being placed on them as they juggle to meet the needs of students," said McArthur.
She has also heard concerns of threats and increased violence, and said the government's "blatant disrespect for educators is taking its toll on teachers and, by extension, the students they serve."
Ontario College of Teachers said during the 2021-22 school year, one per cent of five-year teacher graduates reported they could not find work and 18 per cent of those who were educated outside Canada were not able to secure a job.
CAPTION: Ontario English Catholic Teachers' AssociationNiagara elementary unit president Jennifer McArthur says the Doug Ford government's lack of adequate investment in education has exacerbated problems.Bob Tymczyszyn St. Catharines Standard file photo

CREDIT: Victoria Nicolaou Reporter

Letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/letters-editor/docview/2929386843/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Vaccination
déjà vu
Re: 5,500 unvaccinated
Niagara students facing
suspension, Feb. 15
This was another case of déjà vu all over again, with apologies to Yogi Berra.
In 1982, in my role as a school trustee in Scarborough and a member of the board of health, I undertook a project to improve the level of vaccinations against common childhood diseases in the student population. At the time only about 60 per cent of students in Scarborough schools had their vaccination records on file at the schools.
After obtaining support for my proposal from every school board and municipality in Ontario, the government adopted my concept and passed the first legislation making vaccinations mandatory (with a few exceptions) in Ontario schools on July 7, 1982. In the 1982-83 school year, compliance with the act jumped to 94 per cent.
Then, as I suspect is still the case, it is not that the children are not vaccinated, it is instead that we don't know which children are at risk. I see, as noted in the story, that starting with only 59 per cent of pupils having records updated, that number increased from 83 per cent last fall to 92 per cent in November 2023, indicating that Niagara schools are moving towards the required "herd immunity."
I wish to express my thanks for the challenging work the schools and public health folks are doing and to the parents who are helping achieve these very positive results.
Bert Dandy, Niagara Falls
Expecting more
from Crombie
Re: Ford uses announcement of carbon tax referendum bill to take digs at Bonnie Crombie, Feb. 15
Unfortunately, this story failed to report Bonnie Crombie's reaction, in which she referred to Premier Doug Ford as "Desperate Doug," thus lowering herself to the childish tactic of name-calling, so commonly used by a certain ex-president.
I expected better of her, but perhaps I was mistaken.
Anne Bell, Fort Erie
Flyers a nuisance
I am writing about what some may think is a minor problem. That is the eyesore caused by advertising flyers thrown from vehicles on lawns, sidewalks and edges of streets.
I have seen multiple blue plastic bags over a period of weeks accumulating on both occupied and obviously vacant properties. They end up in piles of plowed snow as dirty messes when the snow melts.
Several winters ago I managed to destroy a perfectly good snowblower I had used for years when it caught one of these blue plastic packages hidden under four inches of snow, causing irreparable damage. The flyer package had been thrown from a vehicle onto a private sidewalk before a snowfall.
If the businesses using this tactic to advertise realized the nuisance it causes, they would inform those responsible for circulating these flyers to be more careful when they distribute them.
Richard Murri, Niagara Falls

Document 231 of 249

Province blamed for health-care woes: Niagara residents are feeling impact 'privatization scheme,' say local coalition members

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/province-blamed-health-care-woes/docview/2929385118/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Province+blamed+for+health-care+woes%3A+Niagara+residents+are+feeling+impact+%27privatization+scheme%2C%27+say+local+coalition+members&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-02-22&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: While hospital systems across Ontario are facing budget deficits and struggling with staff vacancies, including Niagara Health, a new report by Ontario Health Coalition says funding public health-care facilities desperately need is instead being handed to private for-profit clinics.
Niagara Health Coalition chair Sue Hotte said the report released Wednesday - called "Robbing the public to build the private: The Ford government's hospital privatization scheme" - accuses the provincial government of intentionally creating a crisis in public health care.
"This has been very well-planned from the get-go," Hotte said at a media conference at Welland Civic Square.
She said the report, based on a year-long study looking at hospitals across Ontario, determined the province created that crisis by underfunding public health-care services at less than the inflation rate, and by bringing in Bill 124 - legislation that is now being repealed after twice being deemed unconstitutional - that worsened staffing shortages.
Hotte said the government then brought in legislation focused on for-profit clinics and made "sure that they're funded well over what hospitals are getting and at the same time."
As a result, she said, hospitals are coping with budget deficits, including Niagara Health, which expects to be about $12 million in the red by the end of its fiscal year, and with about 1,600 unfilled staff vacancies.
"And then we have a government that says we have a huge crisis, and the solution is privatize. Have for-profit clinics, make sure that pharmacists are able to give injections and get paid for it more than the hospitals and doctors are getting."
The report said private clinics are seeing provincial funding increases of as much as 300 per cent, while public hospitals are funded at the lowest rate of all provinces.
In response to the report, Hannah Jensen, a spokesperson for Health Minister Sylvia Jones, said the provincial government under the leadership of Premier Doug Ford "has made record investments in our publicly funded health-care system."
"While the Ontario Health Coalition continues to be ideologically opposed to any action our government is taking to build a more connected health-care system, we will continue our work that is providing you with better access to care, closer to home," she said.
Hotte said Niagara has been feeling the impact of the "privatization scheme," such as nightly closures of urgent-care centres in Fort Erie and Port Colborne, while surgeries are no longer performed in evenings and on weekends at Welland hospital.
"Our area has been under the gun since the mid-1990s," Hotte said.
Heather Kelley, from Fort Erie Healthcare SOS, said "people are so disgruntled by the system and the lack of availability to get the help they need."
She said the impact is aggravated for people who don't have transportation and "can't afford to call an Uber or a taxi to take them to the hospital, let alone bring them back home again."
Hotte said Ontarians are feeling the impact of increased privatization, being asked to pay thousands of dollars in fees.
St. Catharines resident Margaret Unruh said her mother-in-law required surgery to remove cataracts from both eyes in 2020, and was referred to a private clinic. She said her mother-in-law was told to write a cheque for $570, on top of charges of $110 per eye for special lenses.
Unruh said a few years later, her nephew required the care of ophthalmologists at a private clinic for an emergency procedure. She said as soon as she walked in the door, she was told it would cost $185 for an emergency appointment.
Although she said the doctor told her the fee would be covered by OHIP, Unruh remained worried about others who might not have someone advocating for them.
Patients, Hotte said, should have the right to access care without user fees or extra billing.
"Our taxes fund our public health system and it should not be used to fund for-profit clinics," Hotte said.
Jansen, however, listed numerous investments in public health care the Progressive Conservatives have made.
She said health-care budgets have been increased by more than $18 billion since 2018, "$80 billion into the system this year alone."
"This includes a four per cent increase to the hospital sector and is the total health-care budget of almost every other province and territory combined," she said, referencing data from Canadian Institute of Healthcare Information.
She said the government is continuing to take action "to build on the progress we have achieved including having a record-breaking year in 2023," such as adding 17,000 new nurses and 2,400 new physicians, achieving the shortest wait times for surgeries in Canada.
She said the province also eliminated a 180,000-person backlog for PAP (cervical screening) tests, "while returning the surgical backlog to pre-pandemic levels and making historic investments in pediatric and primary care."
"This is on top of the $1-billion investment we have made through the Surgical Recovery Fund to allow hospitals to utilize operating rooms on weekends and weekdays to complete even more surgeries," Jensen added.
CAPTION: Sue Hotte of Niagara Health Coalition, right, and Margaret Unruh of St. Catharines speak about a new report to be released by Ontario Health Coalition.Bob Tymczyszyn St. Catharines Standard

CREDIT: Allan Benner Reporter

Move of ServiceOntario slap in the face, MPP says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/move-serviceontario-slap-face-mpp-says/docview/2928800343/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Move+of+ServiceOntario+slap+in+the+face%2C+MPP+says&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Niagara Centre MPP Jeff Burch is blasting the provincial government for stripping small businesses of their ServiceOntario contracts so that they could be awarded to Staples Canada.
The ServiceOntario location in Welland owned by Niagara Falls resident Klaudia Savona was among the first six standalone locations across Ontario to be shuttered.
Savona operated in a plaza at Thorold Road and Niagara Street. The province's move put Welland's Service Ontario site inside the Staples store further north at Seaway Mall.
Additional locations are to be opened in Staples stores across the province later this year.
"Their ServiceOntario contracts were ripped away without a tendering process and handed to Staples, a big-box American corporation that is in the process of laying off staff," Burch told Public and Business Service Delivery Minister Todd McCarthy during question period Tuesday as the provincial legislature resumed after a 10-week winter break.
"When will this government stop pandering to big-box corporations and apologize to small business owners, who feel they have been slapped in the face?" Burch asked.
McCarthy responded, calling the service delivery model with Staples "good news for Ontarians."
He said it offers extended hours, more accessibility, more parking and the convenience of online booking.
McCarthy said contracts with previous service providers were due to expire, and "every single one of those employees is eligible for employment at any one of the retail partnerships, including that of Staples Canada."
"That is interaction with government that puts the customer first. The people of Ontario come first every time with ServiceOntario," he said.
Burch said Savona was notified by email that her contract wouldn't be renewed. She had invested thousands of dollars of her own money to operate for 23 years and recently employed four people.
"Now this government will pay Staples to renovate their big-box store, leaving Klaudia out in the cold."
He said Savona was also sent a threatening email warning her not to speak to the media about the situation.
In a Niagara This Week in story published Jan. 16, Savona said the province gave her 70 days' notice about the change, "and we are not being compensated in any way."
"We are being treated like crap," she said.
Burch asked McCarthy if Premier Doug Ford had "prior knowledge that small business owners like Klaudia were going to be used and abused in this manner, or is this yet another example of incompetence in the offices of the premier and his cabinet?"
McCarthy said "the small business model is a key part of the service delivery model for all ServiceOntario locations, but not one size fits all."
CAPTION: Welland's ServiceOntario location moved after the provider contract was "ripped away" from a small business owner, says MPP Jeff Burch.St. Catharines Standard

CREDIT: Allan Benner Reporter

Full text: Downtown
frustrations
What is happening in downtown St. Catharines?, Letters, Feb. 1
Thank you to David Hall for sharing his concerns.
It is residents like him who are our city's biggest assets and I cannot thank him enough for his dedication to downtown and the small business community.
We share his frustrations and concerns. Some specific items we have been working on with the city include:
a proposal for new garbage receptacles (with lids to prevent debris blowing out and people dumping household waste in them);
a pilot allowing downtown residents to be eligible for large item pickup;
requirement for boarding to be covered with art or paint rather than left as plain plywood.
We have a dedicated and passionate streetscape and sustainability committee that meets monthly to create plans to enhance beautification of downtown. This is led by a director who personally oversees several community clean-ups each year when business members come out and participate in picking up garbage downtown. She also sits on the city's clean city committee and participates in a number of cigarette cleanups.
We have applied for Canada summer students to fill some community cleanup ambassador positions this summer.
We are also looking to do a larger community clean-up in the spring with the intent of having a bit of a "deep clean" after the winter.
We are grateful that last year the city implemented a vacant building registry. This has been helpful to encourage landlords to utilize/maintain their buildings. I believe they may be looking to add additional staff to support this as there are quite a number of buildings to be registered.
It is unfortunate we have seen a larger than normal number of business closures due to a variety of challenges. We are hopeful the influx of additional residential units coming to downtown will provide more customer stability for businesses.
Rachel Braithwaite, executive director, St. Catharines
Downtown Association
Finger-pointing on health care
Niagara Health faces $12M deficit, Feb. 1
This article states Ontario Health Minister Sylvia Jones blames previous Liberal governments and the NDP for financial pressures facing our hospitals.
Her spokesperson states "the Liberals supported by the NDP, underfunded the health-care system, closing hospitals and hospital beds, firing nurses," etc.
Two points occur to me.
The first is the government prior to the Liberals was a Conservative one, led by Mike Harris. Under his government, nurses lost their jobs. Asked to comment on this, he famously referred to nurses as being like hula hoop makers that were not needed and should find other work.
Secondly, even if there is blame to share, is it not the job of the current government to deal with the problem?
John Rowan, Welland

Full text: Stop the Trump bashing
Liberals think Donald Trump comparisons hurt Pierre Poilievre. Canadians aren't so sure they're the same, poll suggests, Jan. 27
"Keep your friends close and your enemies closer." Truer words have never been spoken in the game of politics.
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau has been in power since 2015 and still chooses to virtue signal, which is now hurting Canada in a profound way. We've seen numerous super powers around the world dismissing Canada as a fringe player and he's about to add the U.S. to that list.
His recent response to a possible Donald Trump presidency was way off the mark. Yes, Trump is a maniacal, egotistical narcissist; however, there is a real possibility we'll be dealing with him over the next four years.
The U.S. is our No. 1 trading partner, representing more than 75 per cent of all exports made from this country. And by the way, Trump is not shy to remind us our national defence comes at a huge discount as we contribute only 1.2 per cent of our GDP toward defence spending while the U.S. does most of the heavy lifting where Norad and our national defence is concerned.
Based on our PM's latest comments regarding the upcoming U.S. election, you can be certain Trump now has us in his crosshairs, which will cost us dearly in trade and defence.
We're going to need strong leadership in the very near future.
I suggest our PM take up golfing and start building relationships down south; our economy depends on it!
Alain Fournier, Beamsville
Province
to blame
Ontario government holds some blame for
Emergency Act overreaction, Jan. 26
The judge's ruling that use of the federal Emergencies Act violated charter rights was technically correct. Sadly, It may have the negative effect of encouraging further actions of this type.
Public roads and streets were cut off, virtually holding local residents hostage. These were the jurisdictions of Ontario and local governments. Roads leading to international border crossing were provincial roads.
The conservative governments of Ontario, Alberta and Saskatchewan gladly turned the blame on the federal government. Conservative Party of Canada Leader Pierre Poilievre shared a beverage with protesters sitting on the bumper of a tractor trailer.
A few of those protesters, mainly the organizers, have been charged. A few others were handed tickets by local police for trespassing on private property or publicly relieving themselves. Others disturbed the peace by constantly chanting and blowing air horns from their trucks.
Laws were being broken, and local governments and the Doug Ford government stood by, gladly handing over the messy task to the federal authorities.
I would advise the fed bashers to take a good look in the mirror when assigning blame in this matter.
Richard Murri,
Niagara Falls

Readers write Letters to the editor

Full text: College response 'laughable'
International cap hits Niagara post-secondary
institutions, Jan. 27
It's laughable Niagara College is pleading innocence for its role in the housing crisis, because it's not the main nor only cause.
There are many factors, but it surely knew of them and invited 6,000 international students to Niagara anyway.
There are less than 500 on-campus housing spots for all 10,500 (domestic plus international) students at Niagara College.
It's building 1,000 more, and that's good; but that's 10 per cent of the student body. When you add in the additional 9,300 students in its programs at the Toronto School of Management, a public-private partnership which operates for profit and doesn't offer any on-campus housing, it means Niagara College is responsible for more than 15,400 international students coming into Ontario's housing market in 2022 and relied on the private market to absorb them.
Niagara College, plus its Toronto campus, totals the fourth highest international student visa acceptances, ahead of every university and nearly every college in the province.
I sympathize with international students' struggles. They're important to the future of our country.
But it is Niagara College which dropped them into Ontario's housing market, and prior to this cap planned to bring thousands more - $41 million in profits last year; no wonder it wanted to double down.
Don't blame students or the federal government after the province did nothing - blame colleges like Niagara that abused the system.
Erik Johnston, Niagara Falls
Staples decision was 'special'
New Staples locations part of Service Ontario
review, Jan. 28
How special for seniors like me who needed not only a new health card but also to renew a driver's licence this past year.
In St. Catharines there are two ServiceOntario locations, one on St. Paul Street and another on Scott Street. There is no phone for the downtown location, so one must either call a provincial 1-800 number or Scott Street. Workers there are unfailingly polite, but you cannot make an appointment time.
You must work through the system. I received my health card. The driver's licence was a problem, as over-stressed workers at the 1-800 line were curt, telling you when you could have your appointment at Russell Avenue Community Centre. It worked out in the end.
Now we have Premier Doug Ford deciding Staples, an American retailer, will have workers at kiosks in its stores to get people their cards. And while stressed out elders and others just want their cards we are told we could "shop while waiting for your virtual place in line."
It appears it really is all about retail, certainly not Ontario service. One wonders if the Beatties store in the west end will qualify as a Staples, as it was bought by Staples. Tell us please.
Gail Benjafield,
St. Catharines

Document 236 of 249

Niagara puts its best foot forward

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/niagara-puts-best-foot-forward/docview/2921976632/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Niagara+puts+its+best+foot+forward&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-02-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: With 444 municipalities in Ontario and another 3,573 in Canada, gaining the ear of federal and provincial governments is challenging and can't be left to chance.
The upper-tier levels of government at Queen's Park in Toronto and Parliament Hill in Ottawa have the financial and legislative resources Niagara needs to deal with issues such as homelessness and ambulance off-loading delays.
"It's important that we have a seat at the table, and without us practising proactive government relations," Daryl Barnhart, the Niagara Region chair's chief of staff, told council recently. "No one will tell our story if we're not there."
It took a multi-year regional effort to get Queen's Park to fix the Community Homelessness Prevention Initiative funding formula in 2023 after staff estimated it had shortchanged Niagara by $28 million since being introduced in 2013.
That meant the Region had to invest more local taxpayer dollars in homelessness programs per capita than any other location in Ontario.
Barnhart said having effective inter-government relations is critical in ensuring the Region's perspective is considered in provincial and federal policy and funding decisions.
"We're looking to create a positive relationship with those levels of government with a consistent approach during our annual and ad hoc opportunities as well as day-to-day opportunities with MPs and MPPs and their staff.
"There are times where it's incumbent upon us to ensure the government understands the local implications of policy decisions."
The year also saw the relaunch of Niagara Week, a three-day blitz of provincial politicians, cabinet members and ministry staff at Queen's Park in May that opened with a social reception for all 124 MPPs featuring some of the Region's best culinary offerings - catering from Niagara-based Ricky+Olivia, beer and wine from Niagara College and 13 Street Winery and takeaway treats from Monks Chocolates.
The top advocacy priorities were narrowed down and included:
South Niagara Wastewater Solutions Project: Request for one-third funding on the estimated $400M project;
Consolidated housing master plan: The need for long-term capital funding to increase the supply of community housing units;
Shared services: The reintroduction of the Municipal Modernization Program with a focus on shared service implementation between Niagara's two-tier municipalities;
Emergency medical services off-loading delays: Seeking reimbursement for emergency EMS funding and identifying long-term solutions for off-load delay challenges.
Region staff and key council members advanced Niagara's priorities through 30-minute meetings with the appropriate ministers and ministry officials.
"We have been received well by those to whom we made representation, including the premier, who took a keen interest and indicated some support for some of the initiatives we're bringing forward," Region Chair Jim Bradley said.
Barnhart said people often think government relations are only about money, and that's usually true, but there are exceptions.
The Ministry of Transportation's decision to close the Thorold tunnel both ways during construction in 2019 effectively cut Niagara in half.
Emergency advocacy by local MPPs, Region staff and Thorold Mayor Terry Ugulini convinced the ministry to reconfigure its plans.
"It wasn't necessarily a money issue," Barnhart said. "It was more of a 'You don't understand. To you, it's just a tunnel. To us, it's a critical piece of infrastructure.'"
Bradley said it helps to have regional Couns. Rob Foster (Lincoln) and Diana Huson (Pelham) on the boards at Association of Municipalities of Ontario (AMO) and Federation of Canadian Municipalities, respectively.
Rachael Ball-Condron, a government relations specialist at the Region, said the effort always works better when Niagara speaks with a unified voice.
"We participated in the AMO conference last summer in London, and we were able to meet with seven delegations of MPPs; three were independent, and four were in partnership with our local communities," Ball said.
Bill Sawchuk is a reporter at the St. Catharines Standard. He can be reached at william.sawchuk@niagaradailies.com
CAPTION: The Niagara delegation, led by Regional Chair Jim Bradley, centre left, meets with Premier Doug Ford and then minister of municipal affairs and housing Steve Clark during Niagara Week at Queen's Park in May 2023.Michael Swiep Special to the St. Catharines Standard File Photo

CREDIT: Bill Sawchuk Reporter Bill Sawchuk is a reporter at the St. Catharines Standard. He can be reached at william.sawchuk@niagaradailies.com

Subject: Funding

Document 237 of 249

Hundreds rally outside PC policy conference: Rural health care really does matter, says Heather Kelley of the Save Fort Erie Hospital SOS campaign

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/hundreds-rally-outside-pc-policy-conference/docview/2921975793/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Hundreds+rally+outside+PC+policy+conference%3A+Rural+health+care+really+does+matter%2C+says+Heather+Kelley+of+the+Save+Fort+Erie+Hospital+SOS+campaign&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-02-05&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As Premier Doug Ford and his caucus met behind closed doors at the Ontario Progressive Conservative Party policy convention inside Niagara Falls Convention Centre on Saturday, hundreds of people lined up outside to denounce the current state of the provincial government.
Heather Kelley, of the Save Fort Erie Hospital SOS campaign, said the province's hospitals are on life-support and placed blame squarely at the feet of the PC party.
"Rural health care really does matter," she told the crowd gathered along Stanley Avenue. "It does stand between life and death for so many people."
In July, staff shortages forced nightly closures of the hospital system's urgent care centres in Fort Erie and Port Colborne that were previously open 24-7.
Both hospitals are slated for closure in about 2027, when the new south Niagara hospital opens in Niagara Falls.
"Anger does not even express what the people in Fort Erie, Ridgeway, Crystal Beach, Stevensville and Black Creek are feeling right now," Kelley said. "But, it's not just my community, there are so many hospitals that are closing their doors or creating part-time hospitals, part-time urgent care."
Niagara Falls New Democratic MPP Wayne Gates recently presented a petition signed by more than 4,000 people to the provincial government demanding restoration of 24-7 urgent care centre services in Fort Erie and Port Colborne.
"Can you imagine going to an emergency room after you had a heart attack and you get there and it's closed and you have to go on to the next place and the next place?" he said Saturday.
"This is not right. We all deserve to live with respect and dignity in one of the richest provinces in the world."
Lily Chang, secretary treasurer of Canadian Labour Congress, said families should not have to struggle to make ends meet or scramble to find health-care services.
"It doesn't have to be this way, it shouldn't be this way," she said.
"We need to remind our elected representatives they work for us and it's high time they start putting our interests first."
The Progressive Conservatives have led the province since 2018.
"If ever there was a best before date for governments, this one is well past it," said Jackie Taylor, executive vice-president of Ontario Federation of Labour (OFL), which organized the rally. "The sad reality is, for working-class people and their families, life in Ontario today is more difficult, more expensive, than it was six years ago."
OFL is the largest provincial labour federation in Canada, representing more than 50 unions and one million workers across Ontario.
Niagara West PC MPP Sam Oosterhoff, co-chair of the policy conference, could not be reached for comment Saturday.
CAPTION: Heather KelleyPeople unite to denounce the current state of the provincial government as the Ontario Progressive Conservative Party policy convention takes place inside Niagara Falls Convention Centre.Alison Langley Niagara Falls Review
