Labour leaders will take concerns 'to the doorstep' of Ford: 'We're going to tell the truth about what's really happening in our communities'

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/labour-leaders-will-take-concerns-doorstep-ford/docview/2921461031/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Labour+leaders+will+take+concerns+%27to+the+doorstep%27+of+Ford%3A+%27We%27re+going+to+tell+the+truth+about+what%27s+really+happening+in+our+communities%27&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Life in Ontario is more difficult and more expensive for workers and their families under Premier Doug Ford and his Progressive Conservative government, said labour leaders during a news conference at a Niagara Falls hotel Friday.
"In just a few hours, Doug Ford and his caucus will be gathering not far from here for their so-called 'get it done' policy convention and for the next two days we can expect to hear more of the same 'get it done' rhetoric about what this government thinks it has achieved for the people of Ontario," said Laura Walton, president of Ontario Federation of Labour, inside a conference room at Embassy Suites Hotel, surrounded by workers carrying signs reading: "Don't Throw Workers Under The Bus."
"But when we hear them say those words 'get it done,' let's be clear about what exactly this government has got done. Corruption, closures, and a cost-of-living crisis."
Walton said the government is "still reeling" from last year's Greenbelt scandal and that "while Ford can find tax dollars for his corporate buddies, he apparently can't find the resources to keep our hospitals open, or to properly fund education, or other public services."
Instead of helping workers earn more money, Walton said the government has "held down wages."
"Ford still refuses to introduce paid sick days in Ontario, which means workers lose money when they have to stay home sick and instead of being able to afford housing and easing the housing crisis and building those houses, Ford tried to prop up his developer buddies and we ended up with a Greenbelt scandal."
The Ontario PC party is holding its 2024 policy conference at Niagara Falls Convention Centre Friday and Saturday.
Walton said the union has organized a rally outside the convention centre at noon Saturday.
"Ontario workers are not going to sit back and give Ford a free pass this weekend," she said.
"Instead, we're going to challenge his 'get it done' rhetoric and we're going to tell the truth about what's really happening in our communities in Doug Ford's Ontario in 2024."
Heather Kelley, chairperson for the Save Fort Erie Hospital SOS campaign, said "anger doesn't even begin to cover the range of emotions" town residents are feeling "when it comes to watching the complete dismantling of Douglas Memorial."
She said after downgrading Douglas Memorial from a hospital to an urgent-care centre in 2009, the plan is to close that when the new south Niagara hospital opens in Niagara Falls in 2028.
"This is not acceptable. The rural hospitals are the difference between life and death."
Allison Cillis, an educator and member of Ontario Secondary School Teachers' Federation District 21, said she has watched the government "refuse to invest" in public education and choose to "shortchange" students.
"We're seeing overflowing classrooms that impact the one-on-one supports that our students need. We're seeing decreased access to course options that provide students the opportunities for their futures and a lack of specialized professional supports for our most vulnerable and special-needs students," she said.
Cillis said she's seeing more students relying on school breakfast and lunch programs.
"This weekend, Ford and his party are coming to our community, and he should be prepared because we're going to be out in full force and we're going to send a message to Ford that enough is enough," she said.
"Hamilton and Niagara do not want you here. We've had it. Stop shortchangingvices and stop choosing profits over people."
JP Hornick, president of Ontario Public Service Employees Union, said the government should be investing in the public services that people "rely on for a decent life."
"We need to support the workers, the people who deliver these services. We need to reverse the cuts in services, the reductions in spending on public services, and the caps on workers' wages and salaries. We also need to stop the billion-dollar giveaways to your rich friends, CEOs, and corporations," they said.
"This is Ontario labour rising. This is workers rising. We will take it to the doorstep of their convention, and we will take it to the doorstep of every community where there is a Conservative MPP until workers get it done."
In an email statement, Niagara West PC MPP Sam Oosterhoff, co-chair of this weekend's policy conference, said "our Ontario PC team is working for workers, having added over 700,000 jobs since 2018, and raising the minimum wage to $16.55/hour."
"Our series of Working for Workers legislation has improved working conditions, increased pay transparency, and expanded access to health and safety benefits. PC party members are united - we cannot go back to the Liberal-NDP policies that put workers out of jobs and drove industry out of our province, including here in Niagara."

Readers write Letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write-letters-editor/docview/2921084326/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write+Letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-02-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Emergencies Act warranted
Ontario government holds some blame for Emergency Act overreaction, Jan. 26
I agree with Andrew Phillips' opinion that inaction by the province of Ontario (can we say Doug Ford) led to the federal government using emergency powers.
Considering the protesters were "occupying" our nation's capital and disrupting many lives and businesses, and border occupations were creating major economic hardship, in my opinion use of the Emergencies Act was warranted, notwithstanding Judge Richard Mosley's opinion.
My question to Phillips, who believes use of the act was wrong, is "What would your solution have been?"
Marvin Aulthouse, Welland
A bitter lesson
Re: Public board holds firm with grad dates, Jan. 26
District School Board of Niagara has taught Eden students Manuela Jarrin and Olivia Gillespie a bitter lesson. The people most affected by a bureaucratic decision often have the least input.
They have come up against an intransigent bureaucracy, which responds to genuine concerns with newspeak. Decades of tradition that worked well are swept aside by the perception of the administrators that we need to "redefine graduation." Why? If it ain't broke, don't fix it.
Instead of providing students with pleasant memories, DSBN will give them disappointment and frustration.
David Fowler, Wainfleet
Listen to
future grads
As a former principal who initiated the first St. Catharines school to move graduation from the fall to after June examinations (1988), I sympathize with the concerns of the students. The direction DSBN is initiating for high school graduations is hollow and bland.
DSBN needs to listen to future graduating students. Please provide high schools with the necessary framework to make legitimate June graduations happen. It's simple to do.
David Pihach, Wainfleet
We haven't learned
Re: Woman hidden from Nazis appeals for Palestinian children, Jan. 2
As someone who visited the Holocaust Memorial in Berlin and has been taught repeatedly about the Second World War and the Holocaust, I cannot fathom how these events came to happen.
Even so, the ICJ ruled Israel has to prevent and punish genocide incitement, take all measures to prevent genocide and allow more humanitarian aid into Gaza. It fell short of asking for a ceasefire, which the world community has called for for months.
While history is important to learn so we don't repeat it, one cannot help but think we haven't learned from it, especially since the genocide convention, the basis for South Africa's case at the ICJ, was conceived in response to the Holocaust.
Fatima Haidar, Canadians for Justice and Peace in the Middle East

Letters welcome
We welcome letters from our readers. Send them to letters@niagaradailies.com. Letters should be 250 words or less, and may be edited for length and clarity. Please include your name, address and phone number for verification purposes.

Document 240 of 249

Niagara hospitals facing $12M deficit: Past year 'presented challenging set of circumstances,' Niagara Health says

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/niagara-hospitals-facing-12m-deficit/docview/2921084215/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Niagara+hospitals+facing+%2412M+deficit%3A+Past+year+%27presented+challenging+set+of+circumstances%2C%27+Niagara+Health+says&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-02-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Niagara Health is facing the same financial pressures as other hospital systems across the province, with a projected year-end deficit of millions of dollars.
In a statement, Niagara Health blamed its shortfall on high labour costs, inflation and an increase in demand for hospital services as a result of the region's large aging population.
The hospital system said the deficit could climb to $12 million by the time the fiscal year ends on March 31.
"The past year has presented a challenging set of circumstances for Ontario hospitals, intensifying pressures and increasing costs," the statement said.
"However, the projections are still preliminary as we won't know our funding from the government until the spring budget is announced. Our projections are likely to change quite significantly as we work to finalize our year-end numbers."
Niagara Health said it has been relying on its commercial operational line of credit for recent purchases - such as $150,000 for a health information system.
Meanwhile, Hamilton Health Sciences, which operates West Lincoln Memorial Hospital in Grimsby, is expecting a $57.4-million deficit. St. Joseph's Healthcare Hamilton has projected a deficit of $46.4 million.
Both Brantford Community Healthcare System and Joseph Brant Hospital in Burlington are each forecasting deficits of $15 million.
Niagara Health said it is "working with government partners to better understand what funding may be available to address these financial pressures."
"We recognize this is not unique to us and appreciate the Ontario Hospital Association and the government working together to find a path forward," its statement said.
The provincial government has indicated it will assist hospitals facing financial pressures, informing Ontario Hospital Association it will provide financial support to hospitals under financial restraint.
Niagara Centre MPP Jeff Burch, however, said he fears a provincial bailout will be too little, too late.
"It's not surprising they have deficits, because the Ontario government - despite what they tell people - is not supporting our health-care system," the New Democrat said.
Doris Grinspun, chief executive officer of Registered Nurses' Association of Ontario (RNAO), told the Hamilton Spectator, "Nurses don't want the government to come and bail out the hospitals.
"Nurses want the government to come and work with hospitals, primary care, nurses, doctors, everybody to solve our collective challenge. That requires immediate action ... It is solvable."
All 141 public hospitals in the province are in an "untenable situation," the Ontario Hospital Association warned the province's health minister, the president of the treasury board and the finance minister in a letter on Dec. 20.
"We're spending quite a lot of time working ... to understand the fundamental underlying reasons for the situation that we're in," said CEO Anthony Dale. "There's a real appetite to drive change and innovate in order to meet the needs of the people of Ontario into the future."
Health Minister Sylvia Jones blames the previous Liberal government and the NDP for the strain on hospitals today.
"For a decade the Liberals, supported by the NDP, underfunded the health-care system, closing hospitals and hospitals beds, firing nurses and cutting medical school residency spots," the minister's spokesperson, Hannah Jensen, said in a statement to The Hamilton Spectator. "Our government inherited a health-care system under pressure."
But Dale and Grinspun said a wide range of factors - some new and some long term - have come together to leave hospitals cash-strapped.
Burch said the health-care funding shortfall is not limited to hospitals.
He said he recently met with representatives of an Ontario health team who are concerned about increasing costs after three years without a base funding increase from the province.
"They're in bad shape," Burch said. "They're having a really hard time retaining staff. It's getting to the point where it's going to be a crisis."
The province announced $110 million in funding on Thursday to support inter-professional health teams, including funding nurse practitioner-led clinics across Ontario.
RNAO president Dr. Claudette Holloway said members of her organization "welcome this news with open arms."
But the Ontario Medical Association dismissed it as "another small Band-Aid solution that doesn't do anything to get to the root of the problem," said Dr. David Barber, chair of OMA's section on general and family practice.
With files by Joanna Frketich
CAPTION: Niagara Health's budget deficit could reach $12 million, although that could change before the fiscal year end on March 31.Niagara Centre MPP Jeff Burch said the government "is not supporting our health-care system."Bob Tymczyszyn St. Catharines StandardDave Johnson Welland Tribune file photo

Ontario PC policy conference on its way to Niagara Falls

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-pc-policy-conference-on-way-niagara-falls/docview/2919312109/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+PC+policy+conference+on+its+way+to+Niagara+Falls&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Progressive Conservatives from across Ontario will gather for a policy conference in Niagara Falls Friday and Saturday.
"As a local MPP who advocated for the policy conference to be held in Niagara, this event will support hundreds of local businesses and contribute to the bottom line of our hospitality sector during a critical time of the off-season," said Niagara West MPP Sam Oosterhoff, co-chair of the conference to be held at Niagara Falls Convention Centre.
Oosterhoff, who's the lone Niagara Conservative MPP, said the party's four Niagara riding associations have "joined together to highlight the people and products of our unique region at the convention to share the best of what Niagara has to offer to the rest of the province."
"The convention is an especially important opportunity for grassroots members to engage with the PC caucus and party leadership on key policy issues that reflect the concerns of everyday people and help get things done for Ontario."
Premier Doug Ford is scheduled to deliver the keynote address Saturday evening.
Niagara Falls Mayor Jim Diodati said having key provincial government officials in the city will give municipal officials an opportunity to discuss important local issues with them in the region, as opposed to having to visit Queen's Park.
"I'm already co-ordinating some one-on-ones with some different ministries," he said.
Diodati said he plans to talk about the city's housing plan and new south Niagara Falls wastewater treatment plant.
"We want to build more houses quickly and we need support from the provincial and federal government. It takes all three levels and we're all saying the same thing, but we need to all be doing the same thing," he said.
"Our wastewater treatment plant that will be in Chippawa will take huge pressure off of the current system. It will remove flooding issues in Chippawa, and it will open up for more houses in Thorold and Niagara Falls."
Diodati said he plans to discuss what impact the federal government's recent decision to cap international students will have on Ontario's universities, with the new private University of Niagara Falls Canada set to welcome its first cohort of students in April and planning to build housing for students.
He said he also hopes to gather "some insight" into the direction the province may take with governance in Niagara.
"We had the standing committee meetings (recently in Niagara). They're still having them around the province," said Diodati.
"People were under the misunderstanding that the province walked away from amalgamation in Peel, but that's simply not the case. They're just going to be taking a different angle and they will be, as I understand, making some substantial changes to allow Peel to be more efficient."

Ford will be in the city for an Ontario Progressive Conservative Party policy conference at Niagara Falls Convention Centre Friday and Saturday.Tara Walton The Canadian Press file photo

Document 242 of 249

Fairground secrecy 'quite bizarre': Possible sale of property hidden from public ahead of planned Friday board meeting

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/fairground-secrecy-quite-bizarre/docview/2918481446/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Fairground+secrecy+%27quite+bizarre%27%3A+Possible+sale+of+property+hidden+from+public+ahead+of+planned+Friday+board+meeting&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-01-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Niagara Regional Agricultural Society (NRAS) directors themselves cannot make a decision to sell the Niagara Regional Exhibition fairgrounds, says Michael Philpott, Ontario Association of Agricultural Societies (OAAS) District 6 president.
The 21-hectare property at 1100 Niagara St. in Welland, owned and controlled by NRAS since 1967 and bordering southwest Thorold, is purportedly for sale, with a letter of intent to purchase from an unknown buyer said to expire Friday.
"They must have support from the membership (to sell)," said Philpott, who acts as an adviser for 12 agricultural societies from Niagara through to Norfolk County.
"Membership should be involved with all big transactions that happen on the grounds," he said.
Caledonia Fair general manager Ian Thompson, OAAS District 6 secretary-treasurer, agreed with Philpott and said the board can't unilaterally sell the land.
Agricultural societies are public entities, provincially legislated by the Ontario Ministry of Agriculture, Food and Rural Affairs (OMAFRA) and the Agricultural and Horticultural Organizations Act.
"There are certain requirements that we all have to meet in order to be in compliance with that act and remain an agricultural society," said Thompson. "One of those is that there has to be a certain number of members of the organization and the other is you have to have transparent accounting processes documented in your constitution and bylaws."
OAAS is the provincial association representing more than 210 agricultural societies across the province, and its mission is to provide leadership, guidance, education and resources for its member societies.
Thompson said NRAS's constitution echoes that provincial legislation, outlining the responsibilities and limitations of board members.
"One of the arguments they've tried to make is that there's a provision in their constitution that says the board of directors can act on behalf of the organization."
While that provision might allow the board to run day-to-day operations of the organization, Thompson said, "any kind of significant decision that would involve changing the constitution or any kind of clause in the constitution requires a vote of the entire membership."
"Any major financial transaction such as dissolving the organization or selling the fairgrounds property or deciding to relocate the fairgrounds property ... ultimately would require a vote of the membership to support a transaction of that type."
Philpott said he was informed of a meeting to take place Friday at 7:30 p.m. on the fairground property and was waiting to see if he would be invited by one of the members.
NRAS president Colin Bowman said there is no public meeting Friday, adding the public can attend annual general meetings, such as the one that was to be held this past Sunday but did not have a quorum to be considered an official meeting.
He said there are also meetings for NRAS members only. The society's constitution allows for directors meetings to deal with monthly business, such as paying bills.
Another source said a realtor would be at meeting Friday, with information provided to reporters that Royal LePage's Roman Grocholsky, of the Grocholsky Real Estate Group, is involved in the process. Grocholsky would neither confirm nor deny his involvement, citing confidentiality.
A realtor was at a special meeting of the NRAS board of directors in December according to sources.
Philpott said he was working with OMAFRA to determine whether a meeting Friday is open or closed to him, adding he has attended other agricultural societies' directors meetings.
"I'd go as an adviser. I would not be there to dictate."
Niagara Centre NDP MPP Jeff Burch remains concerned about a lack of transparency provided by NRAS regarding any possible sale.
"It's really kind of shocking that anyone would think they could move forward with a multimillion-dollar property deal in the middle of Welland and not talk to anyone, including their own members about it," he said.
Burch was referring to comments made during the failed attempt to hold Sunday's AGM - only six board members showed up - when treasurer Mary-Louise Ravazzolo said she was not privy to information about an offer being concerned to sell the property.
"I deal with a lot of inquiries about transparency but this one really is quite bizarre," Burch said.
"Usually, organizations should be public and transparent with information until they have a legal or ethical reason not to be. But a lot of organizations are not transparent until they have reason to be, and that's the opposite way of looking at it," he said.
"In this case, they're not even sharing information with their own members who could be financially on the hook, because if you're a member of an organization you have a responsibility for that organization as well."
Burch said his office is continuing to monitor the situation, to ensure everything that happens "is the way it should happen."
Philpott attended Sunday's chaotic AGM at Welland Civic Square and said at least two directors - Elizabeth Gill and David Gill - seemingly verbally resigned.
Asked if he had a list of directors, the District 6 president said he's had trouble finding one. He knows Bowman is president, Mary-Louise Ravazzolo is treasurer and others include Michael Gill, Kerby Bentley and Ray Ravazzolo.
Under its constitution, NRAS allows for 12 directors with four junior directors who do not have voting rights.
Philpott said Sunday's meeting showed discord and dysfunction that he's not seen before. "District 6 societies are looking at what's going on here. It puts what they do into question."
Part of the purported land sale includes 3.2 hectares District School Board of Niagara was interested in last September.
Philpott sent along a template of how to sell off agricultural society land and pointed to when the non-profit Lincoln Agricultural and Smithville Agricultural societies, each operating for more than 150 years in Lincoln and West Lincoln, amalgamated and formed West Niagara Agricultural Society.
Though it's unclear what became of the DSBN proposal, Philpott thought it would have been a win-win for it and NRAS, promoting agriculture to children who may not been exposed to that kind of life and the importance of the industry.
He was told the school board was offering $750,000 per acre.
"Personally, I wish they had started working with that. They (the NRAS) could have stayed on the remaining property and had money to put on a good fair.
"I don't want to see 171 years go down the toilet. It's a frustrating situation. The only place the society could really make a comeback is on the property," Philpott said.
CAPTION:

CREDIT: Dave Johnson Reporter Allan Benner Reporter

Readers write letters to the editor

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/readers-write-letters-editor/docview/2915913572/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Readers+write+letters+to+the+editor&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-01-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Bradley correct on amalgamation
Amalgamation 'not a silver bullet' for Niagara,
Jan. 10
Bravo to Niagara Region Chair Jim Bradley for finally steering this amalgamation debate in the right direction.
It's been painful watching local politicians spin their wheels on the future of governance in this region. Amalgamation might sound like a great idea, but in reality it's like trying to boil the ocean. Any attempt to untangle the spaghetti that is unique to each municipality and consolidate all those needs into a cohesive and manageable construct is doomed to fail.
Lean business models have been around since the 1970s-'80s and they work. It applies the basic principle that all business organizations contain five to 10 per cent waste within their operations, low hanging fruit. Using the Region's $483-million operating budget, that represents an opportunity of $24 million to $48 million annually in cost reductions.
With a bit of hard work and political courage, as sure as Wayne Gates's moustache, that would bring a smile to our taxpayers.
Alain Fournier, Beamsville
A challenging land mass
Megacity, four-city model supported at Niagara governance hearing, Jan. 12
Have the people who advocate for one city thought of the size of the area that would need to be governed? Niagara region is three times the size of Toronto in land mass.
There would still be a need for satellite offices and staff. The four-city idea would eliminate this and be more efficient.
Brian White, St. Catharines
Privatization of ServiceOntario started years ago
Welland ServiceOntario relocation worries residents: Burch, Jan. 14
Why does MPP Jeff Burch have "deep frustration" over the planned closing of the Welland ServiceOntario office and its relocation to the Staples store at Seaway Mall? Is it because he fears losing future votes of people who work at this location?
Kevin Werner reported "Burch and other NDP MPPs have identified Staples looking for a regional services manager "responsible for monetization of senior Ontario traffic and drive sales and profit results.'"
Imagine that - a business that wants to make a profit so it can stay in business.
Presently there are close to 300 ServiceOntario locations and almost three-quarters of them operate through private delivery. One must also remember it was the Liberal McGuinty/Wynne government in 2012 that expanded private sector involvement in ServiceOntario locations. If so many of these locations are already privately run, why does it make a difference to privatize only 11 more locations?
Perhaps Staples will be more efficient at running these locations. Let's give it a chance.
Leonard Groeneveld,
St. Catharines

dailies.com. Letters should be 250 words or less, and may be edited for length and clarity. Please include your name, address and phone number for verification purposes.

Document 244 of 249

'You need decent staff to serve the public': ServiceOntario contractor Klaudia Savona warns Staples may not be able to handle 'complicated' licence issues

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/you-need-decent-staff-serve-public/docview/2915521371/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27You+need+decent+staff+to+serve+the+public%27%3A+ServiceOntario+contractor+Klaudia+Savona+warns+Staples+may+not+be+able+to+handle+%27complicated%27+licence+issues&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-01-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Klaudia Savona will be out of a job at the end of the month.
The Welland native, who lives in Niagara Falls, has operated the ServiceOntario site at 440 Niagara St. in Welland for the past 18 years. But on Nov. 20 she was told by the provincial government her contract would end on Jan. 31.
"It was a shock," said Savona, who oversees four employees. "They gave us 70 days' notice and we are not being compensated in any way. We are being treated like crap."
Savona was talking about the province's decision to close the ServiceOntario location, with some caution. She had recently been sent by the province a communique warning her not to speak to the media or in public about the government decision to close the ServiceOntario site at the southeast corner of Niagara Street and Thorold Road and relocate it to the Staples store at Seaway Mall.
Still, despite being "quite concerned" about what the province may do to her, she agreed to speak to a reporter about the loss of her ServiceOntario operation.
"The information is already public," she said. "I don't know what they are trying to hide. But I know other operators are fearful about what could happen if they talk."
The government announced in a Dec. 7 release it then would be closing an unknown number of ServiceOntario locations and moving them to Staples Canada sites as kiosks.
The government has described the decision as part of a pilot project to "reduce the overall cost to deliver government services to the public."
But last week it was revealed Welland is one of 11 ServiceOntario locations across the province to move. Most of them are operated by contractors such as Savona.
At the Welland Staples store there are signs up directing customers to an under-construction ServiceOntario kiosk inside. The province, said Savona, is providing the funding to Staples to construct these kiosks.
At these kiosks, the public will be able to renew driver's licences and health cards.
A Welland store employee said its kiosk is projected to be open within a month.
"Staples doesn't know what they are getting involved in," said Savona, who has 41 years in the business.
"You need decent staff to serve the public and they are hard to find. And renewing licences and issuing health cards can be difficult.
"It's not the kind of thing Staples knows how to do. It's complicated."
Of her four employees, Savona said only one is moving to the store.
"I'm glad she is going over," she said.
A request for comment from Staples Canada was referred to Ministry of Public and Business Service Delivery communications staff.
Doug Allingham, a spokesperson for the ministry, said in an emailed response to questions that Staples was selected to host ServiceOntario centres because it met the province's criteria, including store size, parking availability and willingness to participate.
He said these ServiceOntario centres are not "kiosks," and will have about 30 per cent more hours available to customers.
He said further information will be provided in the future.
In an email from the City of Welland, communications manager Marc MacDonald told the St. Catharines Standard the city does not know why the ServiceOntario location is moving from Niagara Street.
"This was not something that was discussed with the city prior to the decision being made by the province," he said.
Niagara Centre New Democrat MPP Jeff Burch has said Welland residents who have contacted his staff "are worried that these changes will lead to additional delays and longer wait times for basic government services."
Burch, in a letter to Premier Doug Ford dated Jan. 12, wrote to express the "deep frustration" residents have felt after learning about the change.
"As with so many decisions made by the (Progressive) Conservatives, the lack of transparency and community consultation for this important decision is deeply troubling," he said.
As a contractor, Savona invested her own money to improve resources at the ServiceOntario site in Chelwood Gate Plaza. During the COVID-19 pandemic, for instance, the province offered some financial help to install plastic barriers for her staff. Savona, though, invested in tempered glass to better protect her employees.
"Any time there was a need to improve the location, I had to do it out of my own pocket," she said, including training staff. "I didn't mind because I was protecting my staff. But we still saved (the province) a crapload of money over the years."
Savona said she has heard residents express outrage about the move.
"We had a great relationship with residents, and with (auto) dealers," she said. "That's gone now."
After Jan. 31, Savona said she will relax and decide what her next move will be. She is helped that her husband is employed. Still, Savona will begin searching for another job.
"I do need to do something to pay the bills," she said.
CAPTION: The Welland ServiceOntario office will be moving to the Staples store at Seaway Mall on Niagara Street.Kevin Werner Metroland

CREDIT: Kevin Werner Reporter

Provincial governance review team to make second Niagara visit: Oosterhoff: Diodati wants detailed report to follow hearings

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/provincial-governance-review-team-make-second/docview/2915059443/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Provincial+governance+review+team+to+make+second+Niagara+visit%3A+Oosterhoff%3A+Diodati+wants+detailed+report+to+follow+hearings&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It appears as though a second hearing by the province's standing committee on heritage, infrastructure and cultural policy about governance in Niagara will be held, but where and when has yet to be determined.
A representative of the committee said the initial goal was to visit at least two cities in the regions where governance is being reviewed, which includes Niagara, Durham, Halton, Waterloo and York regions and Simcoe County.
In an interview, Niagara West Progressive Conservative MPP Sam Oosterhoff said he's not sure on the exact timing, but he has been told the committee will return to Niagara "at least one more time, likely by spring."
"As to where things go from here, the standing committee will publish a report with recommendations on the various meetings in the assigned regions and then it is up to the minister of municipal affairs and housing as to whether or not he will introduce legislation/regulations moving on those recommendations. I do not know what the timeline on that will be."
Members of the committee, who are studying regional governance as overseen by the Ministry of Municipal Affairs and Housing, heard dozens of presentations about Niagara's governance structure at Holiday Inn & Suites in St. Catharines last Wednesday.
Representatives from municipalities and business organizations, as well as individuals, presented their vision for what the future of governance in Niagara should look like, which ranged from maintaining the status quo to various forms of restructuring.
In addition to St. Catharines, the committee held a hearing in Burlington last Thursday, in Brampton Friday and will hold sessions Tuesday in Ajax, Wednesday in Vaughan and Thursday in Kitchener.
Niagara's four MPPs joined the standing committee members for the St. Catharines hearing who included Progressive Conservative members - Brampton-East MPP Hardeep Singh Grewal, who chaired the meeting; Whitby MPP Lorne Coe; Mississauga Centre MPP Natalia Kusendova-Bashta and Perth-Wellington MPP Matthew Rae - and Beaches-East York Liberal MPP Mary-Margaret McMahon.
Niagara Falls Mayor Jim Diodati, who favours "dramatic reorganization" in Niagara government, said whatever the province decides, he hopes a fulsome report is publicly released.
"I absolutely hope that they do because I don't want to leave people hanging and wondering."
The province held a review of Niagara's governance in 2019 and sent special advisers Michael Fenn and Ken Seiling to the region to hear feedback and come up with recommendations.
Their report was never released publicly by the Doug Ford government, which said it fell under cabinet secrecy provisions, and no changes were made.
CAPTION: A provincial committee looking at the future of governance in Niagara met last Wednesday atHoliday Inn & Suites in St. Catharines.Bob Tymczyszyn Metroland file photo

Office relocation worries residents, MPP says: Burch 'frustrated' by move of ServiceOntario in Welland

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/office-relocation-worries-residents-mpp-says/docview/2914626449/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Office+relocation+worries+residents%2C+MPP+says%3A+Burch+%27frustrated%27+by+move+of+ServiceOntario+in+Welland&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-01-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Niagara Centre New Democratic MPP Jeff Burch has "deep frustration" over the planned closing of the Welland ServiceOntario office and its relocation to the Staples store at Seaway Mall.
"This sudden change raises serious concerns about residents' access to government services," said Burch in a Friday letter that was sent to Premier Doug Ford.
"This deal with Staples is yet another example of your government's sole-sourced contracts that appear to benefit wealthy insiders with connections to the PC Party."
The Welland Staples store on Saturday displayed ServiceOntario signs inside pointing to an under-construction kiosk area. An employee who did not provide his name said it is expected the kiosk will be operational within a month.
The Welland ServiceOntario office at 440 Niagara St., in the Chelwood Gate Plaza at Thorold Road, did not have any signs this weekend indicating it is to be closed.
On social media, Burch said Welland residents have reached out to his office "worried that these changes will lead to additional delays and longer wait times for basic government services."
Burch and other New Democrat MPPs have identified Staples looking for a regional services manager "responsible for monetization of senior Ontario traffic and drive sales and profit results."
The Ontario government announced it is closing a number of ServiceOntario Centres and adding new locations in select Staples stores. Locations also include Stoney Creek, London and Windsor.
The government said in December it would be opening new ServiceOntario centres in Staples stores in early 2024, but would not say at the time that existing sites would close. The move, said the government in December, is to "reduce the overall cost to deliver government services to the public."

Document 247 of 249

Health coalition holding discussion on fate of Fort Erie hospital

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/health-coalition-holding-discussion-on-fate-fort/docview/2911757088/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Health+coalition+holding+discussion+on+fate+of+Fort+Erie+hospital&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-01-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.1&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: There's increasing interest in a public meeting on the state of health care in Fort Erie, after a previously scheduled meeting was cancelled due to a medical emergency that resulted in death.
Organizer Sue Hotte from Niagara Health Coalition said the scope of Thursday's meeting has grown since it was cancelled on Nov. 30, when a volunteer collapsed as the meeting was starting. Despite the efforts of three nurses and paramedics who performed CPR, the man later died at Niagara Falls hospital.
"We're obviously very concerned about what's going on with health care, not only in Fort Erie but in Niagara and across Ontario," Hotte said.
While concerns about health care brought more than 150 people to the last meeting before it was cancelled, Hotte said they're "even more concerned now."
Thursday's meeting starts at 6:30 p.m. at Fort Erie Leisureplex. Eight panellists are scheduled to participate - Mayor Wayne Redekop, Niagara Falls MPP Wayne Gates, Fort Erie Native Centre executive director Jennifer Dockstader, Heather Kelley from Fort Erie Healthcare SOS, Natalie Mehra from Ontario Health Coalition, Ontario Public Service Employees Union Local 215 president Brenda Allan, Ontario Nurses' Association Local 26 president Loretta Tirabassi, and Hotte.
Hotte said organizers are expecting an audience of hundreds of residents. She said Niagara Health representatives were invited to participate.
In a statement, Niagara Health said it "supports open dialogue and discussion about improving the health-care system."
"We are open to hearing solutions to address pressing issues such as recruiting doctors and nurses, and ensuring patients have access to family doctors," it said. "Like most hospitals across the country and province, our top priority continues to be timely access to life-saving emergency care throughout the Niagara region by focusing our emergency resources on the emergency departments in Welland, Niagara Falls and St. Catharines. We value the opinions and insights of the community and in the coming months we will be hosting engagement sessions about the future of the Niagara hospital system."
In July, staff shortages forced nightly closures of the hospital system's urgent care centres in Fort Erie and Port Colborne that were previously open 24-7. The two hospitals are slated for closure in about 2027, when the new south Niagara hospital opens in Niagara Falls.
Hotte said each of Thursday's panellists have five minutes to share their thoughts and concerns about plans for the Fort Erie hospital "and then we're going to open it up to the audience for questions and concerns."
Organizers plan to launch a letter-writing campaign during the meeting. People will be asked to send correspondence to Premier Doug Ford and Health Minister Sylvia Jones, urging them to maintain hospital services in the community.
Hotte said each of the letters will be delivered to Queen's Park in individual sealed envelopes, "and they're going to have to open them because we'll find out if they don't."
She said they're hoping roughly 4,000 letters are sent to the provincial government.
Hotte said similar campaigns have proven to be "very effective," leading to government commitments to preserving hospital services in other communities.
Hotte said the group is planning similar meetings for Port Colborne and Welland, where for the latter its hospital services including its full-service emergency department are to be downgraded.
"This campaign is going to go on for a few months," she said. "It gives people a chance to get their concerns known."
CAPTION: A group protests in front of Douglas Memorial Hospital in Fort Erie last September. Concerns about the facility's future will be discussed at a public meeting on Thursday.Julie Jocsak Metroland file photo

Document 248 of 249

Successes and challenges at Region headquarters defined 2023: Bradley: Chair reflects on housing crisis, budget battles, an industrial 'renaissance' in year-end interview

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/successes-challenges-at-region-headquarters/docview/2911756417/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Successes+and+challenges+at+Region+headquarters+defined+2023%3A+Bradley%3A+Chair+reflects+on+housing+crisis%2C+budget+battles%2C+an+industrial+%27renaissance%27+in+year-end+interview&amp;title=The+Standard&amp;issn=08373434&amp;date=2024-01-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Standard&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As the curtain fell on 2023, Niagara Region Chair Jim Bradley took time last week to reflect on the year's achievements and difficulties at regional headquarters in Thorold.
"With the start of a new term, regional council ended up passing two budgets in the same calendar year, and both were challenging," Bradley said, as he concluded his fifth year as regional chair on the heels of a political career that spanned decades as an MPP at Queen's Park.
"We had to look at everything in the context of inflation, which impacts municipalities as it does every other entity and person," Bradley said. "We came forward with a budget with a pretty good consensus about where we should be going - even though some councillors would like to have seen more funds allocated in certain areas, and others might have liked to see fewer funds allocated. I think we found a good balance there."
On the plus side of the ledger, Bradley outlined more than $225 million in new investment for Niagara's long-suffering industrial sector. The highlight is in Fort Erie with a new $100-million, 19,500-square-metre manufacturing facility for Siltech Corp., which makes specialty silicone used across multiple industries, including personal care, automotive, agriculture, pulp and paper, and oil and gas.
There is also the construction of the 27,870-square-metre Linamar gigacasting plant in Welland and a groundbreaking by Atura Power for a new hydrogen power centre at the Sir Adam Beck plant in Niagara Falls. In West Lincoln, Stanpac Inc. used government grants to help scale up the manufacturing of compostable and recyclable packaging.
"We're experiencing an industrial renaissance with significant investments that will eventually create hundreds of new jobs," Bradley said. "I'm confident Niagara will continue to grow in the coming years, and I'm excited about the possibilities on our doorstep."
Bradley pointed out that Standard & Poor's Global Ratings reaffirmed Niagara Region's credit rating as AA+ in a report released Oct. 19. The agency praised the Region's financial management practices and solid institutional framework as key to the rating.
Standard & Poor's noted Niagara's economy would continue to face challenges from inflation and high interest rates and will need to manage growth-related debt as it continues to build up services and infrastructure to meet community needs. It also highlights the amalgamation of regional transit and the corresponding upload under a new transit commission as a success for Niagara.
Regional council approved the 2024 general levy budget with a 7.02 per cent property tax increase, the second hefty tax hike in as many years.
"A budget is always extremely difficult," Bradley said. "In an ideal world, most people would love to have more and better services and lower taxes. Of course, that's not possible, and we have to find that appropriate balance and work with the local municipalities as well."
Bradley said the budget increases were driven primarily by external forces, including global inflation and revenue losses because of the province's Bill 23, which limited the Region's ability to generate revenue for infrastructure through development charges.
"The province has indicated there's a possibility they will make us whole due to Bill 23, but that remains to be seen," Bradley said. "They have challenges as well."
Bradley was scheduled to spend part of the upcoming year working with a provincial facilitator to find the best mix of responsibilities between upper- and lower-tier municipalities to implement plans to build new homes, including 1.5 million in Niagara, during the next decade.
As one of six regional governments selected to work with the facilitator, the Ministry of Municipal Affairs insisted that regional councillors elect Bradley, a known entity with whom they were confident they could work, as chair by promising to reject the appointment of anyone else.
However, the province abandoned the process after then-minister of municipal affairs Steve Clark resigned as part of the Premier Doug Ford government climbdown in the Greenbelt scandal.
A legislative committee is now conducting the review by consulting the public. It has a scheduled stop at the Holiday Inn in St. Catharines on Wednesday.
"That was their decision, and I support that decision if they felt that was appropriate," Bradley said.
Addressing the critical housing issue, Bradley said the Region is "laser-focused" on it, with a particular emphasis on attainable housing.
Bradley said the Region's office of strategic transformation - a dedicated team focused on attainable housing and expediting shared services among the Region and 12 local municipalities - is at the forefront of the effort to deal with the crisis, which has also reduced vacancy rates in Niagara to near zero and cause the rents and home prices to soar. However, Bradley cautioned it's not something the Region can solve on its own.
"We have seen a greater degree of co-operation than ever before, but in terms of approvals with thousands in place," he said. "The development community is not moving on them. They have their reasons. It could be the financing and interest rates, a shortage of skilled trades or a lack of available building materials.
"The point I'm making is that municipalities are not roadblocks. We've provided all approvals. They're ready to go when the development community decides it's in their interest."
As the Region emerges from the shadows of the COVID-19 pandemic, Bradley acknowledged its impact on all levels of government.
However, he underscored the lessons learned and changes made during "challenging times."
Vaccination efforts and adherence to precautions have positioned the Region to where it can go back and focus on essential services with more human resources available to address ongoing issues like homelessness and challenges brought on by opioid addictions.
Bradley also highlighted the Region's commitment to environmental and social issues - including holding its second annual climate change summit and efforts to strengthen ties with Indigenous leaders.
"I'm optimistic about the future," Bradley said.
"I like working with this particular council. I think the members are there, by and large, for the right reasons - and that's to provide the best services to the people of our area at as low a price as possible with the quality they expect."
CAPTION: Niagara Region chair Jim Bradley says he is optimistic about the current council and its ability to make progress on the many issues facing the Region.Julie Jocsak Metroland file photo

Full text: Reinstate licence sticker fees
Don't count Ford out yet, Jan. 3
I would like to suggest to the Ontario government and the MTO that annual renewal of licence plate stickers be reintroduced.
Although the elimination of the annual fee is good political PR, it makes no sense. Going online to register is great, but many will not bother. This necessitates that law enforcement must check every vehicle in question before pulling the driver over. This takes unnecessary time for the officer.
In addition to the sticker reinstatement, the government should use that revenue to permit drivers to replace illegible plates for free. Far too many drivers have plates that no longer have paint on them.
My understanding is this is against the law. People would replace their plates if it was not necessary to pay for them.
All those drivers who have darkened licence plate covers should be fined. Many have done so not to protect the plate, but rather to avoid identification by photo cameras.
Ron Oliver, St. Catharines
Double standards
What really gets me are the double standards which people use to judge the same things in different guises. For example, terrorism is given legitimacy if committed by a government with uniforms, tanks, planes, artillery and helicopters, thereby "officially" sanctioned. When you see a body ripped to shreds, does it make any difference if it was done by a motley crew with limited means or by powerful nations?
It's amazing so many believe it, or say they do. Personally, I think people cling to the lie and convince themselves of its truth by the consensus of the many. Same as those folks exposed in the Pentagon Papers in their self delusion about their official policy on the Vietnam War. As Josef Goebbels once said, repeat a lie often enough and it becomes the truth.
Wayne Borbey, Niagara Falls
Ford has dismal health record
If we're going to name anything after Rob Ford,
why not a football stadium?,
Dec. 15, 2023
Now that a park has been named after Rob Ford, perhaps a property should be named after Premier Doug Ford. I would recommend a cemetery.
This would honour his government's incredibly dismal record on handling COVID-19 which led to the needless deaths of thousands, particularly in long-term care.
But the property should have room to grow because his experiment in underfunding the health-care system, in spite of having funds available as the Ontario Auditor pointed out in the last report, is going to lead to more needless suffering and death.
Expect a new commercial from the Ford government suggesting you have a civic duty to not become ill or old or require emergency medical services.
Bob Koruna, St Catharines

dailies.com. Letters should be 250 words or less, and may be edited for length and clarity. Please include your name, address and phone number for verification purposes.
